1:1.text: Dhritarashtra said: O Sanjaya, assembled on the sacred field of Kurukshetra, eager to fight — what did my sons and the sons of Pandu do?
1:1.transliteration:
dhṛtarāṣṭra uvāca |
dharmakṣetre kurukṣetre samavetā yuyutsavaḥ |
māmakāḥ pāṇḍavāś caiva kim akurvata sañjaya ||1||
1:1.devanagari:
धृतराष्ट्र उवाच ।
धर्मक्षेत्रे कुरुक्षेत्रे समवेता युयुत्सवः ।
मामकाः पाण्डवाश्चैव किमकुर्वत सञ्जय ॥ १ ॥
1:1.speaker: Dhṛtarāṣṭra
1:1.explanation: The blind king Dhritarashtra, seated at Hastinapura, opens the Gita with a question to his divinely-sighted charioteer Sanjaya. The word 'dharmakṣetre' (field of dharma) is deeply significant — Kurukshetra was sacred ground, the chosen site for the restoration of cosmic order. The phrase 'māmakāḥ' (mine) versus 'pāṇḍavāḥ' (Pandu's sons) reveals Dhritarashtra's bias and inner division. Despite knowing that dharma was not on his side, he hopes his sons will prevail. This opening question sets the stage for the entire Bhagavad Gita.

1:2.text: Sanjaya said: Then King Duryodhana, seeing the Pandava army arrayed in battle formation, approached his teacher Drona and spoke these words.
1:2.transliteration:
sañjaya uvāca |
dṛṣṭvā tu pāṇḍavānīkaṃ vyūḍhaṃ duryodhanas tadā |
ācāryam upasaṅgamya rājā vacanam abravīt ||2||
1:2.devanagari:
सञ्जय उवाच ।
दृष्ट्वा तु पाण्डवानीकं व्यूढं दुर्योधनस्तदा ।
आचार्यमुपसङ्गम्य राजा वचनमब्रवीत् ॥ २ ॥
1:2.speaker: Sañjaya
1:2.explanation: Sanjaya, endowed with divine sight by the sage Vyasa, begins narrating the battlefield events. Duryodhana's act of approaching his teacher Drona — rather than rallying his own troops — reveals his inner anxiety. Despite commanding a larger army, the sight of the well-arrayed Pandava forces unnerved him. This sets up Duryodhana's speech in the following verses, where he attempts to both survey and encourage his own side.

1:3.text: O Teacher, behold this great army of Pandu's sons, arrayed for battle by your wise disciple, the son of Drupada.
1:3.transliteration:
paśyaitāṃ pāṇḍuputrāṇām ācārya mahatīṃ camūm |
vyūḍhāṃ drupadaputreṇa tava śiṣyeṇa dhīmatā ||3||
1:3.devanagari:
पश्यैतां पाण्डुपुत्राणामाचार्य महतीं चमूम् ।
व्यूढां द्रुपदपुत्रेण तव शिष्येण धीमता ॥ ३ ॥
1:3.speaker: Duryodhana
1:3.explanation: Duryodhana draws Drona's attention to the Pandava army's formation. The phrase 'your disciple' (tava śiṣyeṇa) is a subtle provocation — Dhrishtadyumna, who arranged the Pandava forces, was Drona's own student, born specifically to kill Drona. Duryodhana simultaneously acknowledges the threat and attempts to stir Drona's resolve, perhaps testing whether his loyalty might waver. This shows Duryodhana's political cunning even in the midst of impending battle.

1:4.text: Here are great archers equal to Bhima and Arjuna in battle: Yuyudhana, Virata, and the great warrior Drupada.
1:4.transliteration:
atra śūrā maheṣvāsā bhīmārjunasamā yudhi |
yuyudhāno virāṭaś ca drupadaś ca mahārathaḥ ||4||
1:4.devanagari:
अत्र शूरा महेष्वासा भीमार्जुनसमा युधि ।
युयुधानो विराटश्च द्रुपदश्च महारथः ॥ ४ ॥
1:4.speaker: Duryodhana
1:4.explanation: Duryodhana catalogs the foremost heroes on the Pandava side. By measuring others against Bhima and Arjuna, he establishes them as the gold standard of martial prowess — which itself reveals his anxiety about the opposing forces. Yuyudhana (Satyaki) was Krishna's kinsman and ally; Virata was the king of Matsya, who sheltered the Pandavas during their incognito year; Drupada was Dhrishtadyumna's father and the father-in-law of all five Pandavas.

1:5.text: Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, and the valiant king of Kashi; Purujit, Kuntibhoja, and Shaibya, the best of men —
1:5.transliteration:
dhṛṣṭaketuś cekitānaḥ kāśirājaś ca vīryavān |
purujit kuntibhojaś ca śaibyaś ca narapuṅgavaḥ ||5||
1:5.devanagari:
धृष्टकेतुश्चेकितानः काशिराजश्च वीर्यवान् ।
पुरुजित्कुन्तिभोजश्च शैब्यश्च नरपुङ्गवः ॥ ५ ॥
1:5.speaker: Duryodhana
1:5.explanation: Duryodhana continues listing the Pandava allied warriors. Dhrishtaketu was king of Chedi; Chekitana was a Yadava hero; the King of Kashi (Varanasi) was a powerful ally; Purujit and Kuntibhoja were relatives of Kunti; Shaibya was a noble king allied with the Pandavas. This exhaustive enumeration reveals the breadth and diversity of the coalition against Duryodhana, deepening the sense of alarm he is communicating to Drona.

1:6.text: The valiant Yudhamanyu, the powerful Uttamauja, the son of Subhadra (Abhimanyu), and the five sons of Draupadi — all of these are great warriors (maharathas).
1:6.transliteration:
yudhāmanyuś ca vikrānta uttamaujāś ca vīryavān |
saubhadro draupadeyāś ca sarva eva mahārathāḥ ||6||
1:6.devanagari:
युधामन्युश्च विक्रान्त उत्तमौजाश्च वीर्यवान् ।
सौभद्रो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्व एव महारथाः ॥ ६ ॥
1:6.speaker: Duryodhana
1:6.explanation: This verse concludes the enumeration of the Pandava allied forces. 'Saubhadra' refers to Abhimanyu, the son of Arjuna and Subhadra (Krishna's sister), one of the most formidable warriors of the younger generation. The five sons of Draupadi — each born of one of the five Pandavas — were also accomplished fighters. A maharatha (great chariot-warrior) is one capable of fighting ten thousand ordinary soldiers single-handedly. The sheer number of such warriors on the Pandava side forms the core of Duryodhana's concern.

1:7.text: O best of brahmanas (Drona), know also those who are distinguished on our side. I name the commanders of my army for your information.
1:7.transliteration:
asmākaṃ tu viśiṣṭā ye tān nibodha dvijottama |
nāyakā mama sainyasya saṃjñārthaṃ tān bravīmi te ||7||
1:7.devanagari:
अस्माकं तु विशिष्टा ये तान्निबोध द्विजोत्तम ।
नायका मम सैन्यस्य संज्ञार्थं तान्ब्रवीमि ते ॥ ७ ॥
1:7.speaker: Duryodhana
1:7.explanation: Duryodhana now turns to enumerate his own side's strengths. The title 'dvijottama' (best among the twice-born, i.e., brahmanas) is an honorific for Drona. After cataloging the formidable Pandava forces, Duryodhana shifts to reassure himself and Drona by listing the Kaurava champions. The phrase 'for your information' (saṃjñārtham) hints that Duryodhana wants Drona to know exactly who he must protect and who must be strategically relied upon in the coming battle.

1:8.text: Yourself (Drona), Bhishma, Karna, and Kripa, ever victorious in battle; Ashvatthama, Vikarna, and the son of Somadatta (Bhurishrava) —
1:8.transliteration:
bhavān bhīṣmaś ca karṇaś ca kṛpaś ca samitiñjayaḥ |
aśvatthāmā vikarṇaś ca saumadattis tathaiva ca ||8||
1:8.devanagari:
भवान्भीष्मश्च कर्णश्च कृपश्च समितिञ्जयः ।
अश्वत्थामा विकर्णश्च सौमदत्तिस्तथैव च ॥ ८ ॥
1:8.speaker: Duryodhana
1:8.explanation: This verse lists the foremost champions of the Kaurava side. Bhishma was the invincible grandsire and supreme commander; Karna was Arjuna's great rival, son of the sun-god; Kripa was an immortal warrior-teacher; Ashvatthama, Drona's son, was fierce and powerful; Vikarna was one of Duryodhana's brothers who had notably defended Draupadi's honor; Bhurishrava (son of Somadatta) was a renowned Kuru warrior. Duryodhana lists his own side's champions partly to reassure Drona and partly to bolster his own courage.

1:9.text: And many other heroes who are ready to give up their lives for my sake — equipped with various weapons, all skilled in warfare.
1:9.transliteration:
anye ca bahavaḥ śūrā mad-arthe tyakta-jīvitāḥ |
nānāśastrapraharaṇāḥ sarve yuddha-viśāradāḥ ||9||
1:9.devanagari:
अन्ये च बहवः शूरा मदर्थे त्यक्तजीवितान् ।
नानाशस्त्रप्रहरणाः सर्वे युद्धविशारदाः ॥ ९ ॥
1:9.speaker: Duryodhana
1:9.explanation: Duryodhana concludes his survey of the Kaurava forces with a collective reference to the remaining warriors. The phrase 'tyakta-jīvitāḥ' (those who have renounced their lives for my sake) reveals Duryodhana's ego — he sees his soldiers as personal devotees ready to die for him rather than for a righteous cause. The phrase 'various weapons' reflects the diverse armament of ancient Indian warfare: bows, swords, maces, spears, and more. This verse closes his military assessment.

1:10.text: Our army, protected by Bhishma, is unlimited (invincible); whereas their army, protected by Bhima, is limited (conquerable).
1:10.transliteration:
aparyāptaṃ tad asmākaṃ balaṃ bhīṣmābhirakṣitam |
paryāptaṃ tv idam eteṣāṃ balaṃ bhīmābhirakṣitam ||10||
1:10.devanagari:
अपर्याप्तं तदस्माकं बलं भीष्माभिरक्षितम् ।
पर्याप्तं त्विदमेतेषां बलं भीमाभिरक्षितम् ॥ १० ॥
1:10.speaker: Duryodhana
1:10.explanation: This is one of the Gita's grammatically debated verses. The words 'aparyāptam' (unlimited/inexhaustible) and 'paryāptam' (limited/sufficient) can be read in two ways. The standard Gita Press reading: Duryodhana boasts that his forces, led by Bhishma, are unlimited and invincible, while the Pandava forces, led by Bhima, are limited and beatable. An alternate reading suggests the opposite — Duryodhana secretly fears his forces are insufficient. Most commentators follow the first reading, viewing this as Duryodhana's attempt at false bravado.

1:11.text: Therefore, stationed in your respective positions at all the fronts, all of you must surely protect Bhishma alone.
1:11.transliteration:
ayaneṣu ca sarveṣu yathābhāgam avasthitāḥ |
bhīṣmam evābhirakṣantu bhavantaḥ sarva eva hi ||11||
1:11.devanagari:
अयनेषु च सर्वेषु यथाभागमवस्थिताः ।
भीष्ममेवाभिरक्षन्तु भवन्तः सर्व एव हि ॥ ११ ॥
1:11.speaker: Duryodhana
1:11.explanation: This is Duryodhana's final strategic instruction. He directs all commanders to protect Bhishma from all sides — indicating that Bhishma was the linchpin of the Kaurava strategy. Should Bhishma fall, the Kaurava morale would collapse. This instruction also inadvertently reveals Duryodhana's anxiety: he is already thinking defensively, focused on protecting a key general rather than projecting offensive confidence. The Gita's opening drama is built on this atmosphere of tension and uncertainty.

1:12.text: Then, to hearten him (Duryodhana), the aged grandsire of the Kurus — the mighty Bhishma — roared like a lion and blew his conch with great power.
1:12.transliteration:
tasya sañjanayan harṣaṃ kuruvṛddhaḥ pitāmahaḥ |
siṃhanādaṃ vinadyoccaiḥ śaṅkhaṃ dadhmau pratāpavān ||12||
1:12.devanagari:
तस्य सञ्जनयन्हर्षं कुरुवृद्धः पितामहः ।
सिंहनादं विनद्योच्चैः शङ्खं दध्मौ प्रतापवान् ॥ १२ ॥
1:12.speaker: Sañjaya
1:12.explanation: Bhishma, sensing Duryodhana's anxiety, acts to boost his morale. The lion's roar (siṃhanāda) was a warrior's battle cry, symbolizing courage and dominance. The epithets 'Kuruvṛddhaḥ' (elder of the Kurus) and 'pitāmahaḥ' (grandsire) emphasize Bhishma's age, experience, and the deep respect he commanded. The blowing of his conch was the formal signal that battle was beginning — and Bhishma chose to be the one to initiate it.

1:13.text: Then conches, kettledrums, tabors, trumpets, and horns — all suddenly sounded together, and that noise became tumultuous.
1:13.transliteration:
tataḥ śaṅkhāś ca bheryaś ca paṇavānakagomukāḥ |
sahasaivābhyahanyanta sa śabdas tumulo 'bhavat ||13||
1:13.devanagari:
ततः शङ्खाश्च भेर्यश्च पणवानकगोमुखाः ।
सहसैवाभ्यहन्यन्त स शब्दस्तुमुलोऽभवत् ॥ १३ ॥
1:13.speaker: Sañjaya
1:13.explanation: Following Bhishma's conch, the entire Kaurava war orchestra erupts simultaneously. Ancient Indian battles began with a ceremonial sounding of instruments to signal commencement and intimidate the enemy. The paṇava was a small drum; the ānaka was a large war drum; the gomukha was a trumpet shaped like a cow's mouth. The tumultuous sound (tumula śabda) would have been psychologically overwhelming, a wall of sound designed to terrify the opposing army before a single arrow was shot.

1:14.text: Then Krishna (Madhava) and Arjuna (the Pandava), standing in their great chariot yoked with white horses, blew their divine conches.
1:14.transliteration:
tataḥ śvetair hayair yukte mahati syandane sthitau |
mādhavaḥ pāṇḍavaś caiva divyau śaṅkhau pradadhmatuḥ ||14||
1:14.devanagari:
ततः श्वेतैर्हयैर्युक्ते महति स्यन्दने स्थितौ ।
माधवः पाण्डवश्चैव दिव्यौ शङ्खौ प्रदध्मतुः ॥ १४ ॥
1:14.speaker: Sañjaya
1:14.explanation: In response to the Kaurava orchestra, Krishna and Arjuna blow their own divine conches. The white horses symbolize purity, divinity, and auspiciousness. 'Mādhava' is an epithet of Krishna meaning 'lord of Lakshmi' or 'of the Madhu dynasty.' The pairing of Krishna and Arjuna — divine charioteer and human warrior — is emblematic of the Gita's central relationship: the eternal and the temporal united in a single chariot.

1:15.text: Krishna (Hrishikesha) blew his conch Panchajanya; Arjuna (Dhananjaya) blew his conch Devadatta; and the great-bellied Bhima (Vrikodara), of terrible deeds, blew his mighty conch Paundra.
1:15.transliteration:
pāñcajanyaṃ hṛṣīkeśo devadattaṃ dhanañjayaḥ |
pauṇḍraṃ dadhmau mahāśaṅkhaṃ bhīmakarmā vṛkodaraḥ ||15||
1:15.devanagari:
पाञ्चजन्यं हृषीकेशो देवदत्तं धनञ्जयः ।
पौण्ड्रं दध्मौ महाशङ्खं भीमकर्मा वृकोदरः ॥ १५ ॥
1:15.speaker: Sañjaya
1:15.explanation: Each great warrior possessed a named divine conch, a symbol of his identity and cosmic status. 'Hṛṣīkeśa' means 'lord of the senses,' an epithet of Krishna. Panchajanya was obtained by Krishna from the demon Panchajana. 'Dhanañjaya' means 'winner of wealth,' an epithet of Arjuna. Devadatta was given to Arjuna by the gods. 'Vṛkodara' means 'wolf-bellied,' referring to Bhima's enormous appetite and physical power. These named weapons were believed to carry divine energy and their sound was auspicious.

1:16.text: King Yudhishthira, son of Kunti, blew his conch Anantavijaya; while Nakula and Sahadeva blew their conches Sughosha and Manipushpaka respectively.
1:16.transliteration:
anantavijayaṃ rājā kuntīputro yudhiṣṭhiraḥ |
nakulaḥ sahadevaś ca sughoṣamaṇipuṣpakau ||16||
1:16.devanagari:
अनन्तविजयं राजा कुन्तीपुत्रो युधिष्ठिरः ।
नकुलः सहदेवश्च सुघोषमणिपुष्पकौ ॥ १६ ॥
1:16.speaker: Sañjaya
1:16.explanation: The remaining three Pandavas are now named with their conches. Yudhishthira, the eldest Pandava and rightful king, bore the conch Anantavijaya ('boundless victory'). Nakula and Sahadeva, the twin sons of Madri, blew Sughosha ('beautiful sound') and Manipushpaka ('jewel-flower'). With this verse, all five Pandavas and Krishna have been identified by their conches, establishing each warrior's individual identity and divine backing before the battle begins.

1:17.text: The great archer of Kashi, the great warrior Shikhandi, Dhrishtadyumna, Virata, and the unconquered Satyaki —
1:17.transliteration:
kāśyaś ca parameṣvāsaḥ śikhaṇḍī ca mahārathaḥ |
dhṛṣṭadyumno virāṭaś ca sātyakiś cāparājitaḥ ||17||
1:17.devanagari:
काश्यश्च परमेष्वासः शिखण्डी च महारथः ।
धृष्टद्युम्नो विराटश्च सात्यकिश्चापराजितः ॥ १७ ॥
1:17.speaker: Sañjaya
1:17.explanation: More Pandava allies blow their conches. Shikhandi had a special destiny — born as a woman in a previous life, she/he was the instrument by which Bhishma would eventually fall, since Bhishma had vowed never to strike a woman. Dhrishtadyumna was the Pandava commander-in-chief and son of Drupada. Virata was the king who sheltered the Pandavas during their year of concealment. Satyaki (Yuyudhana) was Krishna's kinsman, trained by Arjuna, and one of the fiercest Pandava warriors.

1:18.text: O lord of the earth (Dhritarashtra), Drupada, the sons of Draupadi, and the mighty-armed son of Subhadra (Abhimanyu) — all of these blew their conches separately, each one after another.
1:18.transliteration:
drupado draupadeyāś ca sarvaśaḥ pṛthivīpate |
saubhadraś ca mahābāhuḥ śaṅkhān dadhmur pṛthak pṛthak ||18||
1:18.devanagari:
द्रुपदो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्वशः पृथिवीपते ।
सौभद्रश्च महाबाहुः शङ्खान्दध्मुः पृथक्पृथक् ॥ १८ ॥
1:18.speaker: Sañjaya
1:18.explanation: This verse completes the Pandava roll-call of conch-blowers. Sanjaya addresses Dhritarashtra as 'pṛthivīpate' (lord of the earth), a reminder that the blind king is hearing all this from afar. The sons of Draupadi — one each from the five Pandavas — and Abhimanyu (Arjuna's son by Subhadra) were all elite warriors. The phrase 'pṛthak pṛthak' (separately, one by one) suggests the thunderous cumulative effect of each warrior adding his conch's voice to the battle-call.

1:19.text: That tumultuous sound, resonating through heaven and earth, tore asunder the hearts of Dhritarashtra's sons (the Kauravas).
1:19.transliteration:
sa ghoṣo dhārtarāṣṭrāṇāṃ hṛdayāni vyadārayat |
nabhaś ca pṛthivīṃ caiva tumulo 'bhyanunādayan ||19||
1:19.devanagari:
स घोषो धार्तराष्ट्राणां हृदयानि व्यदारयत् ।
नभश्च पृथिवीं चैव तुमुलोऽभ्यनुनादयन् ॥ १९ ॥
1:19.speaker: Sañjaya
1:19.explanation: This is a pivotal verse. The divine conch-calls of the Pandava side were so overwhelming that they reverberated through both sky and earth. 'Vyadārayat' (split, tore asunder) is a powerful word — the Kauravas' hearts were psychologically shattered even before the first arrow was shot. This psychological victory for the Pandavas signals that dharma carries a resonance that strikes fear in the hearts of adharma. The cosmic nature of this battle is already being established.

1:20.text: Then, seeing Dhritarashtra's sons arrayed for battle, as weapons were about to clash, the Pandava (Arjuna) — whose flag bore the emblem of a monkey — raised his bow.
1:20.transliteration:
atha vyavasthitān dṛṣṭvā dhārtarāṣṭrān kapidhvajaḥ |
pravṛtte śastrasampāte dhanur udyamya pāṇḍavaḥ ||20||
1:20.devanagari:
अथ व्यवस्थितान्दृष्ट्वा धार्तराष्ट्रान्कपिध्वजः ।
प्रवृत्ते शस्त्रसम्पाते धनुरुद्यम्य पाण्डवः ॥ २० ॥
1:20.speaker: Sañjaya
1:20.explanation: The moment of battle has arrived. 'Kapidhvajaḥ' (monkey-bannered) is Arjuna's distinguishing epithet — Hanuman, the great devotee of Rama, had blessed Arjuna by taking residence on his battle flag, lending it divine protection. As both armies stood ready and weapons were raised, Arjuna lifted his bow — the moment of no return. The next verse reveals what Arjuna then says to Krishna, initiating the Gita's great dialogue.

1:21.text: Arjuna said: O Achyuta (infallible one), place my chariot between the two armies.
1:21.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
hṛṣīkeśaṃ tadā vākyam idamāha mahīpate |
senayor ubhayor madhye rathaṃ sthāpaya me 'cyuta ||21||
1:21.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
हृषीकेशं तदा वाक्यमिदमाह महीपते ।
सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये रथं स्थापय मेऽच्युत ॥ २१ ॥
1:21.speaker: Arjuna
1:21.explanation: Arjuna addresses Krishna as 'Acyuta' — the Infallible, the Immovable, one who never falls from his divine state. This epithet is profound: Arjuna, who is about to fall into confusion and grief, calls upon the one who never falls. His request to position the chariot between both armies reflects the warrior's impulse to survey the battlefield before engaging — a standard military practice. However, what Arjuna sees when the chariot is placed there will change everything.

1:22.text: So that I may observe those who stand here ready for battle, eager to fight — with whom must I contend in this military venture?
1:22.transliteration:
yāvad etān nirīkṣe 'haṃ yoddhukāmān avasthitān |
kair mayā saha yoddhavyam asmin raṇasamudyame ||22||
1:22.speaker: Arjuna
1:22.explanation: Arjuna explains his reason for wanting the chariot repositioned: he wishes to survey the opposing forces before engaging. This is a rational, strategic thought. Yet what he is about to see — his own grandsire, teachers, uncles, cousins, and friends standing across from him — will shatter his warrior resolve. The seed of Arjuna's grief is planted in this simple, practical request. The Gita's transformative dialogue is about to begin.

1:23.text: I wish to see those who have assembled here, ready to fight, wishing to please the evil-minded Duryodhana in battle.
1:23.transliteration:
yotsyamānān avekṣe 'haṃ ya ete 'tra samāgatāḥ |
dhārtarāṣṭrasya durbuddher yuddhe priyacikīrṣavaḥ ||23||
1:23.devanagari:
योत्स्यमानानवेक्षेऽहं य एतेऽत्र समागताः ।
धार्तराष्ट्रस्य दुर्बुद्धेर्युद्धे प्रियचिकीर्षवः ॥ २३ ॥
1:23.speaker: Arjuna
1:23.explanation: Arjuna's final words before seeing the assembled armies show him still in his warrior mindset. He calls Duryodhana 'durbuddheḥ' — evil-minded or corrupt-intellect — a moral judgment he makes clearly and without hesitation at this point. He views the Kaurava army as supporters of adharma. Ironically, the very act of observing these 'enemies' will dissolve his warrior resolve, not because he changes his moral assessment, but because he cannot bear to harm those he loves.

1:24.text: Sanjaya said: O Bharata (Dhritarashtra), thus addressed by Gudakesha (Arjuna), Hrishikesha positioned the excellent chariot in the middle of the two armies.
1:24.transliteration:
sañjaya uvāca |
evaṃ ukto hṛṣīkeśo guḍākeśena bhārata |
senayor ubhayor madhye sthāpayitvā rathottamam ||24||
1:24.devanagari:
सञ्जय उवाच ।
एवमुक्तो हृषीकेशो गुडाकेशेन भारत ।
सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये स्थापयित्वा रथोत्तमम् ॥ २४ ॥
1:24.speaker: Sañjaya
1:24.explanation: 'Guḍākeśa' is an epithet for Arjuna meaning 'conqueror of sleep' — one who has mastered sloth and unconsciousness, denoting his spiritual discipline and alertness. Hrishikesha (lord of the senses, i.e., Krishna) immediately and without question fulfills Arjuna's request — demonstrating the humility of the divine playing the role of a charioteer-servant. However, this positioning between the two armies is exactly what leads to Arjuna's collapse, as he is about to see his beloved relatives face to face.

1:25.text: In the presence of Bhishma, Drona, and all the kings of the earth, (Krishna) said: O Partha, behold these assembled Kurus.
1:25.transliteration:
bhīṣmadroṇapramukhataḥ sarveṣāṃ ca mahīkṣitām |
uvāca pārtha paśyaitān samavetān kurūn iti ||25||
1:25.devanagari:
भीष्मद्रोणप्रमुखतः सर्वेषां च महीक्षिताम् ।
उवाच पार्थ पश्यैतान्समवेतान्कुरूनिति ॥ २५ ॥
1:25.speaker: Sañjaya
1:25.explanation: 'Pārtha' (son of Pṛthā/Kunti) is Krishna's affectionate name for Arjuna, invoking his noble lineage. Krishna positions the chariot directly before Bhishma and Drona — the two elders Arjuna revered most as grandfather-figure and teacher. The simple command 'behold these assembled Kurus' carries profound irony: Arjuna had wanted to see the enemy, but what he sees instead are the faces of his own family. This moment triggers everything that follows.

1:26.text: There, Partha saw standing in both armies: fathers and grandfathers, teachers and maternal uncles, brothers and sons, grandsons and companions.
1:26.transliteration:
tatrāpaśyat sthitān pārthaḥ pitṝn atha pitāmahān |
ācāryān mātulān bhrātṝn putrān pautrān sakhīṃs tathā ||26||
1:26.speaker: Sañjaya
1:26.explanation: This is the core of Arjuna's tragedy — not that he faces enemies, but that the battlefield is populated with his own family. Every category of human relationship is represented: elders to be respected (pitṝn, pitāmahān), revered teachers (ācāryān), maternal relatives (mātulān), brothers (bhrātṝn), and friends (sakhīn). The Mahābhārata's civil war was precisely this — a war within the family of Bharata. Arjuna sees this now, perhaps for the first time, with full personal and emotional clarity.

1:27.text: And fathers-in-law and dear friends in both armies — seeing all these kinsmen thus assembled, that son of Kunti (Arjuna) —
1:27.transliteration:
śvaśurān suhṛdaś caiva senayor ubhayor api |
tān samīkṣya sa kaunteyaḥ sarvān bandhūn avasthitān ||27||
1:27.devanagari:
श्वशुरान्सुहृदश्चैव सेनयोरुभयोरपि ।
तान्समीक्ष्य स कौन्तेयः सर्वान्बन्धूनवस्थितान् ॥ २७ ॥
1:27.speaker: Sañjaya
1:27.explanation: This verse is grammatically connected to verse 28. 'Śvaśurān' (fathers-in-law) refers to Arjuna's in-laws including Drupada. 'Suhṛdaḥ' (well-wishers, dear friends) were present on both sides, since this was a war within a single extended family. The epithet 'Kaunteya' (son of Kunti) invokes Arjuna's gentle, compassionate side — the nature he inherited from his mother. The verse breaks off mid-sentence, creating narrative suspense before Arjuna's reaction is described.

1:28.text: Overcome with utmost compassion, sorrowful, he (Arjuna) said: O Krishna, seeing my own people assembled here, eager for battle —
1:28.transliteration:
kṛpayā parayāviṣṭo viṣīdann idam abravīt |
arjuna uvāca |
dṛṣṭvemaṃ svajanaṃ kṛṣṇa yuyutsuṃ samupasthitam ||28||
1:28.devanagari:
कृपया परयाविष्टो विषीदन्निदमब्रवीत् ।
अर्जुन उवाच ।
दृष्ट्वेमं स्वजनं कृष्ण युयुत्सुं समुपस्थितम् ॥ २८ ॥
1:28.speaker: Sañjaya / Arjuna
1:28.explanation: Here begins Arjuna's Viṣāda — the 'yoga of grief' that forms the subject of this entire first chapter. 'Kṛpayā parayā' means overwhelmed with the deepest compassion. This compassion (kṛpā) is human and genuine — it is not wrong to feel grief at the prospect of killing beloved ones. But when compassion is entangled with personal attachment and ego ('my people,' 'my family'), it creates delusion about dharma. The Gita does not condemn Arjuna's grief; it transforms its foundation from ego to wisdom.

1:29.text: My limbs are giving way, my mouth is drying up, my body is trembling, and my hair is standing on end.
1:29.transliteration:
sīdanti mama gātrāṇi mukhaṃ ca pariśuṣyati |
vepathuś ca śarīre me romaharṣaś ca jāyate ||29||
1:29.devanagari:
सीदन्ति मम गात्राणि मुखं च परिशुष्यति ।
वेपथुश्च शरीरे मे रोमहर्षश्च जायते ॥ २९ ॥
1:29.speaker: Arjuna
1:29.explanation: Arjuna describes the physical symptoms of his psychological and emotional crisis — a classic psychosomatic response to extreme grief. 'Sīdanti' (sinking, giving way) describes muscular weakness; 'pariśuṣyati' (drying up) indicates anxiety-induced dry mouth; 'vepathu' (trembling) is involuntary shaking; 'romaharṣa' (hair standing on end) is a visceral fear response. These physical symptoms establish that Arjuna's crisis is total — body and mind together are overwhelmed. The Gita's wisdom is addressed to exactly this state.

1:30.text: The Gandiva (bow) slips from my hand, my skin is burning, I am unable to stand, and my mind seems to whirl.
1:30.transliteration:
gāṇḍīvaṃ sraṃsate hastāt tvak caiva paridahyate |
na ca śaknomy avasthātuṃ bhramatīva ca me manaḥ ||30||
1:30.devanagari:
गाण्डीवं स्रंसते हस्तात्त्वक्चैव परिदह्यते ।
न च शक्नोम्यवस्थातुं भ्रमतीव च मे मनः ॥ ३० ॥
1:30.speaker: Arjuna
1:30.explanation: Gandiva, Arjuna's divine bow — gifted by Agni and Indra, never before known to slip from his grasp — now falls from his hands. This is deeply symbolic: the weapon that represents Arjuna's warrior identity is slipping away under the force of his grief. The burning skin suggests the physical sensation of extreme emotional distress. The whirling mind (bhramatīva) indicates loss of the mental clarity that Arjuna is famous for — his greatest asset as a warrior is dissolving before the battle has even begun.

1:31.text: O Keshava, I see ill omens and I perceive no good (shreya) in killing my own people in battle.
1:31.transliteration:
nimittāni ca paśyāmi viparītāni keśava |
na ca śreyo 'nupaśyāmi hatvā svajanaṃ āhave ||31||
1:31.devanagari:
निमित्तानि च पश्यामि विपरीतानि केशव ।
न च श्रेयोऽनुपश्यामि हत्वा स्वजनमाहवे ॥ ३१ ॥
1:31.speaker: Arjuna
1:31.explanation: 'Keśava' (beautiful-haired, or slayer of the demon Keśī) is an epithet of Krishna. Arjuna now begins to rationalize his grief — he sees bad omens (a traditional Indian belief that battle outcomes could be foretold) and makes a philosophical argument: killing one's own people cannot lead to 'śreya' (ultimate good, lasting well-being). This is Arjuna's first intellectual challenge to the necessity of war, and 'śreya' vs 'preya' (immediate pleasure vs lasting good) becomes a central Gita theme.

1:32.text: O Krishna, I desire neither victory nor kingdom nor pleasures. O Govinda, what is a kingdom to us? What are enjoyments? What even is life itself?
1:32.transliteration:
na kāṅkṣe vijayaṃ kṛṣṇa na ca rājyaṃ sukhāni ca |
kiṃ no rājyena govinda kiṃ bhogair jīvitena vā ||32||
1:32.devanagari:
न काङ्क्षे विजयं कृष्ण न च राज्यं सुखानि च ।
किं नो राज्येन गोविन्द किं भोगैर्जीवितेन वा ॥ ३२ ॥
1:32.speaker: Arjuna
1:32.explanation: 'Govinda' is an epithet of Krishna meaning 'protector of cows and knowledge.' Arjuna's renunciation here — rejecting victory, kingdom, and pleasure — may superficially resemble spiritual dispassion (vairāgya), but as Krishna will later clarify, it arises from moha (attachment-based confusion) rather than genuine wisdom. True vairāgya is grounded in understanding the nature of the Self; Arjuna's comes from grief and personal attachment. This is the crucial distinction the Gita will systematically address.

1:33.text: Those for whose sake we desire kingdom, enjoyments, and pleasures — they themselves stand here in battle, having renounced their lives and wealth.
1:33.transliteration:
yeṣām arthe kāṅkṣitaṃ no rājyaṃ bhogāḥ sukhāni ca |
ta ime 'vasthitā yuddhe prāṇāṃs tyaktvā dhanāni ca ||33||
1:33.devanagari:
येषामर्थे काङ्क्षितं नो राज्यं भोगाः सुखानि च ।
त इमेऽवस्थिता युद्धे प्राणांस्त्यक्त्वा धनानि च ॥ ३३ ॥
1:33.speaker: Arjuna
1:33.explanation: Arjuna makes a poignant and humanly understandable argument: the very people for whom he had wanted to win a kingdom — his teachers, grandfathers, sons, and friends — are the ones standing across from him, willing to die. So what purpose would a kingdom serve? This argument reveals Arjuna's deep human sensitivity and his genuine love for his kinsmen. However, as the Gita will show, duty (dharma) cannot be subordinated to personal affection — this is precisely the confusion Krishna must resolve.

1:34.text: Teachers, fathers, sons, grandfathers, maternal uncles, fathers-in-law, grandsons, brothers-in-law, and other kinsmen —
1:34.transliteration:
ācāryāḥ pitaraḥ putrās tathaiva ca pitāmahāḥ |
mātulāḥ śvaśurāḥ pautrāḥ śyālāḥ saṃbandhinas tathā ||34||
1:34.devanagari:
आचार्याः पितरः पुत्रास्तथैव च पितामहाः ।
मातुलाः श्वशुराः पौत्राः श्यालाः सम्बन्धिनस्तथा ॥ ३४ ॥
1:34.speaker: Arjuna
1:34.explanation: Arjuna again enumerates his network of relationships, listing every type of kinship bond. This repetition is psychologically revealing — his mind keeps returning to these connections, unable to move past them. The comprehensive list — from respected teachers (ācāryāḥ) to in-laws (śvaśurāḥ) to brothers-in-law (śyālāḥ) — represents the full web of human social bonds. Arjuna's crisis is not just personal but social: it is the crisis of a man who sees his entire world of relationships about to be destroyed.

1:35.text: O Madhusudana, I do not wish to kill these men even if they kill me — not for lordship over the three worlds, let alone for the sake of this earth.
1:35.transliteration:
etān na hantum icchāmi ghnato 'pi madhusūdana |
api trailokyarājyasya hetoḥ kiṃ nu mahīkṛte ||35||
1:35.devanagari:
एतान्न हन्तुमिच्छामि घ्नतोऽपि मधुसूदन ।
अपि त्रैलोक्यराज्यस्य हेतोः किं नु महीकृते ॥ ३५ ॥
1:35.speaker: Arjuna
1:35.explanation: 'Madhusūdana' (slayer of the demon Madhu) is an ironically pointed epithet here — Krishna destroys demons, yet Arjuna cannot bring himself to oppose his own kinsmen. Arjuna's emotional argument reaches its extreme: even at the cost of his own life, he refuses to fight. He extends this even to sovereignty over the three worlds (trailokya) — the full extent of the cosmos. While this appears to be magnanimity, Krishna will later show that it is moha (attachment-generated confusion) masquerading as compassion, not genuine renunciation rooted in self-knowledge.

1:36.text: O Janardana, what joy would come to us from killing Dhritarashtra's sons? Only sin would cling to us from slaying these aggressors.
1:36.transliteration:
nihatya dhārtarāṣṭrān naḥ kā prītiḥ syāj janārdana |
pāpam evāśrayed asmān hatvaitān ātatāyinaḥ ||36||
1:36.devanagari:
निहत्य धार्तराष्ट्रान्नः का प्रीतिः स्याज्जनार्दन ।
पापमेवाश्रयेदस्मान्हत्वैतानाततायिनः ॥ ३६ ॥
1:36.speaker: Arjuna
1:36.explanation: 'Janārdana' (one who is sought by all people, or who punishes the wicked) is a name that subtly echoes the very dharmic principle Arjuna is violating. The word 'ātatāyī' (aggressor) is legally significant in dharmaśāstra: one who attacks with fire, poison, or weapons, steals property or land, or violates a woman is an ātatāyī, and killing such a person incurs no sin. The Kauravas had committed all of these. Yet Arjuna, overwhelmed by grief and attachment, inverts this principle and fears sin from what the scriptures would consider justified action — a perfect illustration of the delusion (moha) the Gita diagnoses and resolves.

1:37.text: Therefore, O Madhava, we are not justified in killing our own kinsmen, Dhritarashtra's sons. How indeed could we be happy after slaying our own people?
1:37.transliteration:
tasmān nārhā vayaṃ hantuṃ dhārtarāṣṭrān svabāndhavān |
svajanam hi kathaṃ hatvā sukhinaḥ syāma mādhava ||37||
1:37.devanagari:
तस्मान्नार्हा वयं हन्तुं धार्तराष्ट्रान्स्वबान्धवान् ।
स्वजनं हि कथं हत्वा सुखिनः स्याम माधव ॥ ३७ ॥
1:37.speaker: Arjuna
1:37.explanation: 'Mādhava' (consort of Mā, i.e., Lakshmi — lord of auspiciousness) invokes Krishna's aspect as the giver of prosperity; yet Arjuna argues no prosperity is worth this price. 'Nārhāḥ' (not worthy, not entitled) frames the issue as one of moral desert rather than mere emotion. The question 'how could we be happy?' (sukhinaḥ syāma) reveals Arjuna's understanding of happiness as dependent on the welfare of loved ones. Krishna will systematically redefine happiness: true sukha is rooted in the unchanging ātman, not in transient relationships. Arjuna is asking the right question from the wrong foundation.

1:38.text: Even if these men — their minds overcome by greed — do not perceive the wrong incurred by destroying a family and the sin of betraying friends —
1:38.transliteration:
yady apy ete na paśyanti lobhopahata-cetasaḥ |
kula-kṣaya-kṛtaṃ doṣaṃ mitra-drohe ca pātakam ||38||
1:38.devanagari:
यद्यप्येते न पश्यन्ति लोभोपहतचेतसः ।
कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं मित्रद्रोहे च पातकम् ॥ ३८ ॥
1:38.speaker: Arjuna
1:38.explanation: This verse is grammatically incomplete and continues directly into verse 39. 'Lobhopahata-cetasaḥ' (those whose consciousness is overpowered by greed) diagnoses the Kauravas' moral failure: lust for power has blinded them to both the social sin of kula-kṣaya (family destruction) and the personal sin of mitra-droha (betraying those who trusted them as friends and allies). Arjuna's diagnosis of the Kauravas' failing is accurate — but he will use it to argue for inaction rather than right action, which is where his own reasoning goes astray.

1:39.text: O Janardana, how should we — who clearly see the wrong in the destruction of a family — not know to turn back from this sin?
1:39.transliteration:
kathaṃ na jñeyam asmābhiḥ pāpād asmān nivartitum |
kula-kṣaya-kṛtaṃ doṣaṃ prapaśyadbhir janārdana ||39||
1:39.devanagari:
कथं न ज्ञेयमस्माभिः पापादस्मान्निवर्तितुम् ।
कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं प्रपश्यद्भिर्जनार्दन ॥ ३९ ॥
1:39.speaker: Arjuna
1:39.explanation: Verse 39 completes the argument begun in verse 38: since the Kauravas cannot see the evil they commit (being blinded by greed), and since we (Arjuna) can see it clearly (prapaśyadbhiḥ — those who perceive directly), we who see should withdraw. This is Arjuna's most logically structured argument. Its flaw, which the Gita systematically addresses, is that it conflates moral perception with the right response to that perception. Seeing a problem does not always mean withdrawing — sometimes dharma demands engagement. The Gita's answer spans: kṣatriya-dharma, ātma-jñāna, and niṣkāma-karma.

1:40.text: When a family is destroyed, its eternal family traditions perish; when tradition perishes, adharma overtakes the entire family.
1:40.transliteration:
kula-kṣaye praṇaśyanti kula-dharmāḥ sanātanāḥ |
dharme naṣṭe kulaṃ kṛtsnam adharmo 'bhibhavaty uta ||40||
1:40.devanagari:
कुलक्षये प्रणश्यन्ति कुलधर्माः सनातनाः ।
धर्मे नष्टे कुलं कृत्स्नमधर्मोऽभिभवत्युत ॥ ४० ॥
1:40.speaker: Arjuna
1:40.explanation: 'Kula-dharmāḥ sanātanāḥ' — the time-honored codes of a family: ancestral rites, daily worship, hospitality, and ethical conduct carried across generations. Arjuna argues that war destroys not only people but the spiritual and cultural fabric they sustain. This is sociologically sound and morally serious. However, it carries an implicit contradiction: the Kauravas — who cheated at dice, violated Draupadi, usurped a kingdom, and betrayed every covenant — had already destroyed the family's dharma. What Arjuna wants to preserve has already been desecrated by those he refuses to fight. The Gita's response is that dharma must sometimes be restored through action, not preserved through inaction.

1:41.text: O Krishna, when adharma prevails, the women of the family become corrupted; O Vārṣṇeya, when women are corrupted, there arises the intermixing of social orders (varṇa-saṃkara).
1:41.transliteration:
adharmābhibhavāt kṛṣṇa praduṣyanti kula-striyaḥ |
strīṣu duṣṭāsu vārṣṇeya jāyate varṇa-saṃkaraḥ ||41||
1:41.devanagari:
अधर्माभिभवात्कृष्ण प्रदुष्यन्ति कुलस्त्रियः ।
स्त्रीषु दुष्टासु वार्ष्णेय जायते वर्णसङ्करः ॥ ४१ ॥
1:41.speaker: Arjuna
1:41.explanation: 'Vārṣṇeya' (of the Vṛṣṇi lineage) is Krishna's clan epithet. In the ancient Indian social understanding, women were the preservers of lineage, dharmic customs, and family continuity. 'Varṇa-saṃkara' — literally 'mixing of varṇas' — refers in its broader sense to the collapse of structured social roles, duties, and identities that hold a civilization together, not merely to genealogical mixing. Modern readers should understand Arjuna as arguing from within his social and historical context: the dissolution of a family's women's protected status was seen as the beginning of total social disintegration.

1:42.text: This intermixing leads to hell for the destroyers of the family and for the family itself. Indeed, their ancestors fall, deprived of the offerings of rice-balls and water.
1:42.transliteration:
saṃkaro narakāyaiva kula-ghnānāṃ kulasya ca |
patanti pitaro hy eṣāṃ lupta-piṇḍodaka-kriyāḥ ||42||
1:42.devanagari:
सङ्करो नरकायैव कुलघ्नानां कुलस्य च ।
पतन्ति पितरो ह्येषां लुप्तपिण्डोदकक्रियाः ॥ ४२ ॥
1:42.speaker: Arjuna
1:42.explanation: 'Lupta-piṇḍodaka-kriyāḥ' — piṇḍa are balls of cooked rice offered in the śrāddha ceremony to nourish deceased ancestors; udaka is the ritual offering of water (tarpana). In the Vedic worldview, these rites performed by male descendants are essential to maintaining the welfare of the pitṛs (ancestors) in their realm. Without living descendants to perform these rites, the ancestors are believed to fall from their state. Arjuna thus argues that war brings harm not only to the living but to the dead — extending his moral concern across three dimensions: social, spiritual, and cosmic.

1:43.text: By these wrongs of the family-destroyers — wrongs that cause the intermixing of social orders — the eternal traditions of social duty (jāti-dharma) and family duty (kula-dharma) are uprooted.
1:43.transliteration:
doṣair etaiḥ kula-ghnānāṃ varṇa-saṃkara-kārakaiḥ |
utsādyante jāti-dharmāḥ kula-dharmāś ca śāśvatāḥ ||43||
1:43.devanagari:
दोषैरेतैः कुलघ्नानां वर्णसङ्करकारकैः ।
उत्साद्यन्ते जातिधर्माः कुलधर्माश्च शाश्वताः ॥ ४३ ॥
1:43.speaker: Arjuna
1:43.explanation: 'Jāti-dharma' (the duties of one's social order) and 'kula-dharma' (the particular traditions of one's family lineage) are both called 'śāśvatāḥ' (eternal, time-honoured). 'Utsādyante' (uprooted, obliterated) is a strong word — not just weakened but destroyed at the root. This verse forms the conclusion of Arjuna's social-ethical argument: war's ultimate consequence is not merely death but the total dismantling of the civilizational framework — the interlocking duties, rites, and traditions that sustain human society across generations.

1:44.text: O Janardana, we have heard that men whose family traditions are destroyed must dwell in hell for an indeterminate time.
1:44.transliteration:
utsanna-kula-dharmāṇāṃ manuṣyāṇāṃ janārdana |
narake 'niyataṃ vāso bhavatīty anuśuśruma ||44||
1:44.devanagari:
उत्सन्नकुलधर्माणां मनुष्याणां जनार्दन ।
नरकेऽनियतं वासो भवतीत्यनुशुश्रुम ॥ ४४ ॥
1:44.speaker: Arjuna
1:44.explanation: 'Anuśuśruma' (we have heard, we have been taught by tradition) is a significant epistemic marker. Arjuna is not merely expressing personal emotion — he appeals to received scriptural and traditional knowledge (śruti-paramparā) to ground his argument. This shows Arjuna as a man of dharmic education: he has heard and remembered the teachings. 'Aniyataṃ vāsaḥ' (indeterminate dwelling) in naraka suggests an indefinite period of consequence. Arjuna's concern now spans this world (social order) and the next (pitra-loka and naraka) — a complete ethical framework, but applied in the wrong direction.

1:45.text: Alas, what a great sin we are resolved to commit — that out of greed for the pleasures of kingship, we are prepared to kill our own people.
1:45.transliteration:
aho bata mahat pāpaṃ kartuṃ vyavasitā vayam |
yad rājya-sukha-lobhena hantuṃ svajanaṃ udyatāḥ ||45||
1:45.devanagari:
अहो बत महत्पापं कर्तुं व्यवसिता वयम् ।
यद्राज्यसुखलोभेन हन्तुं स्वजनमुद्यताः ॥ ४५ ॥
1:45.speaker: Arjuna
1:45.explanation: 'Aho bata' is a Sanskrit exclamation of lamentation and dismay — 'how terrible!' or 'alas!' This verse marks a crucial shift: Arjuna turns his moral scrutiny inward, condemning himself and the Pandavas alongside the Kauravas. The phrase 'rājya-sukha-lobhena' (out of greed for the pleasures of kingship) is Arjuna's surprising self-indictment — he now applies the charge of lobha (greed) to his own side. This moment of self-examination is genuine, but it is still grounded in confusion: the Pandavas' cause was just (reclaiming a rightful kingdom), whereas Arjuna is framing justice itself as selfishness.

1:46.text: It would be more beneficial for me if the armed sons of Dhritarashtra were to kill me in battle, unresisting and unarmed.
1:46.transliteration:
yadi mām apratīkāram aśastraṃ śastra-pāṇayaḥ |
dhārtarāṣṭrā raṇe hanyus tan me kṣemataraṃ bhavet ||46||
1:46.devanagari:
यदि मामप्रतीकारमशस्त्रं शस्त्रपाणयः ।
धार्तराष्ट्रा रणे हन्युस्तन्मे क्षेमतरं भवेत् ॥ ४६ ॥
1:46.speaker: Arjuna
1:46.explanation: This is the absolute nadir of Arjuna's crisis. He does not merely want to avoid killing — he actively wishes to be killed without resistance. 'Apratīkāram' (without retaliation) and 'aśastram' (unarmed) describe the stance of a man who has surrendered his warrior identity entirely. 'Kṣemataraṃ' (more beneficial, more conducive to welfare) shows he has intellectually concluded that passive death is better than righteous combat. A kṣatriya dying weaponless on the battlefield is the antithesis of his dharma. This is where Krishna's response in Chapter 2 must begin — with the question of who Arjuna actually is, and what constitutes his highest duty.

1:47.text: Sanjaya said: Having spoken thus in the midst of battle, Arjuna sank down on the seat of the chariot, his mind overwhelmed with grief, casting aside his bow and arrows.
1:47.transliteration:
sañjaya uvāca |
evam uktvārjunaḥ saṃkhye rathopastha upāviśat |
visṛjya saśaraṃ cāpaṃ śoka-saṃvigna-mānasaḥ ||47||
1:47.devanagari:
सञ्जय उवाच ।
एवमुक्त्वार्जुनः सङ्ख्ये रथोपस्थ उपाविशत् ।
विसृज्य सशरं चापं शोकसंविग्नमानसः ॥ ४७ ॥
1:47.speaker: Sañjaya
1:47.explanation: This closing verse of Chapter 1 is the pivotal image that occasions the entire Bhagavad Gita. Sanjaya reports to Dhritarashtra what he sees: Arjuna — the greatest archer of his age — has laid down his Gandiva bow and arrows and collapsed onto the chariot seat. 'Śoka-saṃvigna-mānasaḥ' (his mind shattered and overwhelmed by grief) describes a total crisis of identity and purpose. The warrior who rode into battle has become a man paralyzed by love and moral confusion. This image — of a capable human being unable to act rightly despite knowing duty — is the perennial human condition the Gita addresses. Chapter 2 begins with Krishna's response.

2:1.text: Sanjaya said: To him who was thus overcome with compassion, whose eyes were filled with tears and in distress, Madhusudana spoke these words.
2:1.transliteration:
sañjaya uvāca |
taṃ tathā kṛpayāviṣṭam aśrupūrṇākulekṣaṇam |
viṣīdantam idaṃ vākyam uvāca madhusūdanaḥ ||1||
2:1.devanagari:
सञ्जय उवाच ।
तं तथा कृपयाविष्टमश्रुपूर्णाकुलेक्षणम् ।
विषीदन्तमिदं वाक्यमुवाच मधुसूदनः ॥ १ ॥
2:1.speaker: Sañjaya
2:1.explanation: 'Kṛpayāviṣṭam' (completely overcome with compassion) and 'aśrupūrṇākulekṣaṇam' (eyes filled with tears and agitated) give Dhritarashtra — and the reader — a precise image of Arjuna's collapsed state. The epithet 'Madhusūdana' (slayer of the demon Madhu) is thematically resonant: Krishna, who destroys demonic forces, is about to destroy the inner demon of Arjuna's delusion (moha). This verse is the threshold of the Gita's actual teaching — everything in Chapter 1 was the preparation; now the Bhagavad Gita begins in earnest.

2:2.text: The Blessed Lord said: O Arjuna, from where has this faint-heartedness (kaśmala) come upon you at this critical moment? It is unbecoming of an Ārya, does not lead to heaven, and brings infamy.
2:2.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
kutas tvā kaśmalam idaṃ viṣame samupasthitam |
ānārya-juṣṭam asvargyam akīrti-karam arjuna ||2||
2:2.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
कुतस्त्वा कश्मलमिदं विषमे समुपस्थितम् ।
अनार्यजुष्टमस्वर्ग्यमकीर्तिकरमर्जुन ॥ २ ॥
2:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:2.explanation: 'Kaśmala' (impurity, faint-heartedness, mental contamination) is a sharp diagnostic word — Krishna frames Arjuna's grief not as noble sensitivity but as a psychological impurity to be cleansed. 'Viṣame' (at a crisis, in difficulty) acknowledges the gravity of the situation even as Krishna refuses to validate the response. The three-fold condemnation — unworthy of noble people, not conducive to heaven, productive of infamy — is Krishna's opening treatment: not comfort, but a direct confrontation with the nature of the problem. The teaching will follow; first comes the diagnosis.

2:3.text: O Partha, do not yield to unmanliness — it does not befit you. O scorcher of foes (Parantapa), shake off this faint-heartedness and rise.
2:3.transliteration:
klaibyaṃ mā sma gamaḥ pārtha naitat tvayy upapadyate |
kṣudraṃ hṛdaya-daurbalyaṃ tyaktvottiṣṭha paraṃtapa ||3||
2:3.devanagari:
क्लैब्यं मा स्म गमः पार्थ नैतत्त्वय्युपपद्यते ।
क्षुद्रं हृदयदौर्बल्यं त्यक्त्वोत्तिष्ठ परन्तप ॥ ३ ॥
2:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:3.explanation: 'Klaibyam' (unmanliness, impotence) is a deliberately strong word — Krishna does not soften the diagnosis. The two epithets in this verse create a powerful tension: 'Pārtha' (son of the noble Pṛthā) invokes Arjuna's royal lineage and ancestry, while 'Paraṃtapa' (scorcher of enemies) invokes his warrior identity — both contradict the cowardice he is displaying. 'Uttiṣṭha' (rise up) is the Gita's first direct command. The entire Gita is, in one sense, the elaboration of this single imperative: the philosophical, spiritual, and practical grounds for rising and acting rightly.

2:4.text: Arjuna said: O Madhusudana, O destroyer of enemies, how shall I fight in battle against Bhishma and Drona — who are worthy of my reverence — with arrows?
2:4.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
kathaṃ bhīṣmam ahaṃ saṃkhye droṇaṃ ca madhusūdana |
iṣubhiḥ pratiyotsyāmi pūjārhāv arisūdana ||4||
2:4.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
कथं भीष्ममहं सङ्ख्ये द्रोणं च मधुसूदन ।
इषुभिः प्रतियोत्स्यामि पूजार्हावरिसूदन ॥ ४ ॥
2:4.speaker: Arjuna
2:4.explanation: In response to Krishna's forceful command, Arjuna raises a concrete, specific objection. 'Pūjārhau' (worthy of reverence) is the crux: Bhishma was Arjuna's great-grandfather and the most revered elder in the Kuru dynasty; Drona was his beloved teacher who had shaped him into the world's finest archer. The word 'iṣubhiḥ' (with arrows) makes the action viscerally real — not just 'fight' but 'shoot arrows at.' Arjuna calls Krishna by two names meaning 'slayer of enemies' — implicitly asking: how can these people be my enemies?

2:5.text: It is better in this world to beg for sustenance than to kill these great and noble teachers. If I slay them, all the pleasures I would enjoy here would be stained with blood.
2:5.transliteration:
gurūn ahatvā hi mahānubhāvān
śreyo bhoktuṃ bhaikṣyam apīha loke |
hatvārtha-kāmāṃs tu gurūn ihaiva
bhuñjīya bhogān rudhira-pradigdhān ||5||
2:5.devanagari:
गुरूनहत्वा हि महानुभावान्
श्रेयो भोक्तुं भैक्ष्यमपीह लोके ।
हत्वार्थकामांस्तु गुरूनिहैव
भुञ्जीय भोगान् रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् ॥ ५ ॥
2:5.speaker: Arjuna
2:5.explanation: 'Mahānubhāvān' (great-souled, magnanimous ones) — Arjuna accords Bhishma and Drona the highest moral dignity even as he prepares his argument. For a kṣatriya, begging (bhaikṣyam) was the ultimate degradation — lower than death in battle — yet Arjuna says even that is preferable to killing his teachers. 'Rudhira-pradigdhān' (smeared with blood) is a visceral poetic image: the food, pleasures, and kingdom gained through this war would taste of blood. 'Arthakāmāṃs tu gurūn' — Arjuna acknowledges that his teachers are fighting for wealth and desire (on Duryodhana's side), yet still cannot bring himself to oppose them.

2:6.text: We do not know which is better for us — that we should conquer them or that they should conquer us. The sons of Dhritarashtra stand before us; having slain them, we would not wish to live.
2:6.transliteration:
na caitad vidmaḥ kataran no garīyo
yad vā jayema yadi vā no jayeyuḥ |
yān eva hatvā na jijīviṣāmaḥ
te 'vasthitāḥ pramukhe dhārtarāṣṭrāḥ ||6||
2:6.devanagari:
न चैतद्विद्मः कतरन्नो गरीयो
यद्वा जयेम यदि वा नो जयेयुः ।
यानेव हत्वा न जिजीविषामः
तेऽवस्थिताः प्रमुखे धार्तराष्ट्राः ॥ ६ ॥
2:6.speaker: Arjuna
2:6.explanation: This is Arjuna's most honest admission: 'na vidmaḥ' — we do not know. He acknowledges his own fundamental confusion about what is truly better. Victory or defeat — he cannot determine which is preferable. 'Jijīviṣāmaḥ' (we would wish to live) — having killed those for whom life has meaning, living would itself be meaningless. This acknowledgment of not-knowing is the most important step: it is the condition of genuine discipleship. A person who thinks he knows does not ask. Arjuna's uncertainty opens the door for the Gita's teaching.

2:7.text: My nature is overcome by the fault of faint-heartedness; my mind is confused about dharma. I ask you: tell me with certainty what is truly better for me. I am your disciple; teach me, who has taken refuge in you.
2:7.transliteration:
kārpaṇya-doṣopahata-svabhāvaḥ
pṛcchāmi tvāṃ dharma-sammūḍha-cetāḥ |
yac chreyaḥ syān niścitaṃ brūhi tan me
śiṣyas te 'haṃ śādhi māṃ tvāṃ prapannam ||7||
2:7.devanagari:
कार्पण्यदोषोपहतस्वभावः
पृच्छामि त्वां धर्मसम्मूढचेताः ।
यच्छ्रेयः स्यान्निश्चितं ब्रूहि तन्मे
शिष्यस्तेऽहं शाधि मां त्वां प्रपन्नम् ॥ ७ ॥
2:7.speaker: Arjuna
2:7.explanation: This is the pivotal verse of the entire Gita. 'Kārpaṇya-doṣopahata-svabhāvaḥ' — Arjuna now accepts Krishna's diagnosis: his true nature (svabhāva) has been corrupted by the flaw of kārpaṇya (miserliness of spirit, faint-heartedness). 'Dharma-sammūḍha-cetāḥ' — his mind is utterly confused about what dharma requires. The declaration 'śiṣyas te 'ham' (I am your disciple) and 'prapannam' (having taken refuge) are the formal acts of submission that make the guru-śiṣya relationship possible — and make the Gita's teaching possible. The teaching that follows is given not to a debater but to a surrendered student.

2:8.text: I do not see what could remove this grief that dries up my senses — even if I were to attain unrivalled, prosperous dominion on earth, or even lordship over the gods.
2:8.transliteration:
na hi prapaśyāmi mamāpanudyād
yac chokam ucchoṣaṇam indriyāṇām |
avāpya bhūmāv asapatnam ṛddhaṃ
rājyaṃ surāṇām api cādhipatyam ||8||
2:8.devanagari:
न हि प्रपश्यामि ममापनुद्याद्
यच्छोकमुच्छोषणमिन्द्रियाणाम् ।
अवाप्य भूमावसपत्नमृद्धं
राज्यं सुराणामपि चाधिपत्यम् ॥ ८ ॥
2:8.speaker: Arjuna
2:8.explanation: 'Ucchoṣaṇam indriyāṇām' (that which dries up the senses) is a precise description: Arjuna's grief is not surface-level; it has sapped his vital energy and deadened his perceptive faculties. Crucially, Arjuna recognizes that no worldly achievement — not even undisputed rule over the whole earth or sovereign power over the gods — can resolve this grief. This self-awareness is significant: he knows the problem is existential, not circumstantial. It is precisely this recognition that makes him a fit recipient for the profound ātma-jñāna (knowledge of the Self) that Krishna is about to impart.

2:9.text: Sanjaya said: Having spoken thus to Hrishikesha, Gudakesha — the scorcher of enemies — said to Govinda 'I will not fight,' and fell silent.
2:9.transliteration:
sañjaya uvāca |
evam uktvā hṛṣīkeśaṃ guḍākeśaḥ paraṃtapaḥ |
na yotsya iti govindam uktvā tūṣṇīṃ babhūva ha ||9||
2:9.devanagari:
सञ्जय उवाच ।
एवमुक्त्वा हृषीकेशं गुडाकेशः परन्तपः ।
न योत्स्य इति गोविन्दमुक्त्वा तूष्णीं बभूव ह ॥ ९ ॥
2:9.speaker: Sañjaya
2:9.explanation: 'Guḍākeśa' (conqueror of sleep) is a rich epithet here — the man who has mastered sleep and sloth is now paralyzed by the sleep of delusion (moha-nidrā). The finality of 'na yotsye' (I will not fight) — a complete refusal — followed immediately by silence ('tūṣṇīṃ babhūva ha') represents the nadir of Arjuna's crisis. He has exhausted his arguments, made his declaration, and gone mute. It is from this silence that Krishna's teaching emerges — the Gita arises in the space created by human helplessness.

2:10.text: O Bharata (Dhritarashtra), Hrishikesha, as if smiling, spoke these words to him who was grief-stricken between the two armies.
2:10.transliteration:
tam uvāca hṛṣīkeśaḥ prahasann iva bhārata |
senayor ubhayor madhye viṣīdantam idaṃ vacaḥ ||10||
2:10.devanagari:
तमुवाच हृषीकेशः प्रहसन्निव भारत ।
सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये विषीदन्तमिदं वचः ॥ १० ॥
2:10.speaker: Sañjaya
2:10.explanation: 'Prahasann iva' (as if smiling) — the word 'iva' (as if) qualifies the smile: it is not amusement at Arjuna's suffering but a divine serenity, the composure of one who sees the situation from a higher vantage point and knows that the moment of true teaching has arrived. A doctor smiles when the patient finally describes the real symptom. 'Hṛṣīkeśa' (lord of the senses) is a fitting epithet: the master of all sense-faculties is about to address Arjuna's crisis of sensory and mental overwhelm. This gentle, knowing smile precedes the most transformative discourse in Indian literature.

2:11.text: The Blessed Lord said: You grieve for those who should not be grieved for, yet you speak words of wisdom. The wise grieve neither for the living nor for the dead.
2:11.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
aśocyān anvaśocas tvaṃ prajñā-vādāṃś ca bhāṣase |
gatāsūn agatāsūṃś ca nānuśocanti paṇḍitāḥ ||11||
2:11.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
अशोच्यानन्वशोचस्त्वं प्रज्ञावादांश्च भाषसे ।
गतासूनगतासूंश्च नानुशोचन्ति पण्डिताः ॥ ११ ॥
2:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:11.explanation: This is the opening statement of the Gita's philosophical teaching. 'Aśocyān anvaśocaḥ' — you grieve for those not worthy of grief. The reason will unfold across the next twenty verses: the ātman is eternal and cannot be destroyed, therefore grief over apparent death is a misunderstanding of reality. 'Prajñā-vādāṃś ca bhāṣase' — yet you use the language of wisdom (dharma, śreya, pāpa, kula-dharma): the gap between wise words and confused understanding is Arjuna's precise problem. 'Paṇḍitāḥ' (the truly wise) grieve neither for the living nor the dead because they know the ātman transcends both states.

2:12.text: There was never a time when I did not exist, nor you, nor these kings; nor in the future shall any of us cease to be.
2:12.transliteration:
na tv evāhaṃ jātu nāsaṃ na tvaṃ neme janādhipāḥ |
na caiva na bhaviṣyāmaḥ sarve vayam ataḥ param ||12||
2:12.devanagari:
न त्वेवाहं जातु नासं न त्वं नेमे जनाधिपाः ।
न चैव न भविष्यामः सर्वे वयमतः परम् ॥ १२ ॥
2:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:12.explanation: This may be the most philosophically foundational verse of the entire Gita. Krishna speaks in the language of plural persons (I, you, these kings) — which reflects the conventional reality of multiple souls (jīvas). The assertion is absolute: through all three times — past, present, and future — the existence of each individual ātman is continuous and unbroken. This is the metaphysical premise on which all of Chapter 2's ethics rests: if no one truly dies, the grief of anticipated loss is based on a fundamental misapprehension of reality. The teaching of ātman's immortality begins here.

2:13.text: Just as in this body the embodied soul passes through childhood, youth, and old age, so too it passes into another body. The wise person is not deluded by this.
2:13.transliteration:
dehino 'smin yathā dehe kaumāraṃ yauvanaṃ jarā |
tathā dehāntara-prāptir dhīras tatra na muhyati ||13||
2:13.devanagari:
देहिनोऽस्मिन्यथा देहे कौमारं यौवनं जरा ।
तथा देहान्तरप्राप्तिर्धीरस्तत्र न मुह्यति ॥ १३ ॥
2:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:13.explanation: Krishna offers a brilliant and accessible analogy. Within a single lifetime, the soul passes through radically different bodily states — the child's body becomes the adolescent's, then the adult's, then the elder's — yet we do not say the soul 'dies' at each transition; it simply inhabits a changed form. Death and rebirth are the same kind of transition, only more dramatic. The continuity of the 'witness' — the ātman that experiences all these states — is constant. The 'dhīra' (the steady, wise person) understands this and is therefore not deluded (na muhyati) by the apparent tragedy of death.

2:14.text: O Kaunteya, the contacts between the senses and their objects give rise to cold and heat, pleasure and pain. They come and go, they are impermanent — endure them, O Bharata.
2:14.transliteration:
mātrā-sparśās tu kaunteya śītoṣṇa-sukha-duḥkha-dāḥ |
āgamāpāyino 'nityās tāṃs titikṣasva bhārata ||14||
2:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:14.explanation: 'Mātrā-sparśāḥ' (the contacts of the senses with their objects) — Krishna locates the origin of suffering precisely: it arises not from reality itself but from the interaction between the sense organs and their objects, producing sensations of heat/cold, pleasure/pain. All these sensory experiences share one characteristic: 'āgamāpāyinaḥ' (they come and go). What comes must go; what goes came. Their impermanence means they cannot form the ground of lasting well-being. 'Titikṣasva' (endure them) is not a call for emotional suppression but for discriminative understanding: since they are transient, they do not warrant disruption of one's equanimity.

2:15.text: O best of men (Puruṣarṣabha), the wise person whom these (sense contacts) do not disturb — steady in pain and pleasure — becomes fit for immortality (amṛtatva).
2:15.transliteration:
yaṃ hi na vyathayanty ete puruṣaṃ puruṣarṣabha |
sama-duḥkha-sukhaṃ dhīraṃ so 'mṛtatvāya kalpate ||15||
2:15.devanagari:
यं हि न व्यथयन्त्येते पुरुषं पुरुषर्षभ ।
समदुःखसुखं धीरं सोऽमृतत्वाय कल्पते ॥ १५ ॥
2:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:15.explanation: 'Puruṣarṣabha' (bull among men — best among humans) is an epithet that holds Arjuna to a high standard. The verse defines the fruit of titikṣā (endurance): 'sama-duḥkha-sukham' (equanimous in pain and pleasure) is not emotional flatness but unshakeable inner steadiness. 'So 'mṛtatvāya kalpate' — becomes fit for immortality. 'Amṛtatva' here means liberation (mokṣa) — freedom from the cycle of birth and death. The path is clear: inner equanimity → fitness for liberation. This equanimity is only possible when one's identity is rooted in the unchanging ātman rather than the fluctuating body-mind.

2:16.text: The unreal has no being; the real never ceases to be. The conclusion about both these has been perceived by the seers of truth.
2:16.transliteration:
nāsato vidyate bhāvo nābhāvo vidyate sataḥ |
ubhayor api dṛṣṭo 'ntas tv anayos tattva-darśibhiḥ ||16||
2:16.devanagari:
नासतो विद्यते भावो नाभावो विद्यते सतः ।
उभयोरपि दृष्टोऽन्तस्त्वनयोस्तत्त्वदर्शिभिः ॥ १६ ॥
2:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:16.explanation: This is perhaps the most philosophically concentrated verse in the Gita — a statement of Vedāntic ontology in two lines. 'Asat' (the unreal): the body, sensations, emotions — all that changes, appears, and disappears — has no independent, permanent being. 'Sat' (the real): the ātman, Brahman — that which is always present, which does not come and go — has no non-being; it cannot be destroyed. 'Tattva-darśibhiḥ' (those who see the truth) — not through inference alone but through direct contemplative vision. This sat/asat distinction is the epistemological foundation of the entire Sānkhya teaching in Chapter 2: grief over the body's destruction misidentifies the sat (ātman) with the asat (body).

2:17.text: Know that by which this entire universe is pervaded to be indestructible. No one is capable of destroying this imperishable one.
2:17.transliteration:
avināśi tu tad viddhi yena sarvam idaṃ tatam |
vināśam avyayasyāsya na kaścit kartum arhati ||17||
2:17.devanagari:
अविनाशि तु तद्विद्धि येन सर्वमिदं ततम् ।
विनाशमव्ययस्यास्य न कश्चित्कर्तुमर्हति ॥ १७ ॥
2:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:17.explanation: 'Yena sarvam idaṃ tatam' (by which all this is pervaded) hints at the ātman's relationship to Brahman — the universal Self that pervades everything. 'Avināśi' (indestructible) and 'avyaya' (imperishable, inexhaustible) are absolute attributes: this reality cannot be diminished, depleted, or destroyed by any force. No weapon, no natural force, no temporal power can reach it. When this is understood, the concept of 'killing' undergoes a radical transformation: one cannot kill the ātman; one can only cause the body — the asat — to dissolve. Arjuna's fundamental premise of 'I will kill my teachers' is based on a misidentification of ātman with body.

2:18.text: These bodies of the eternal, indestructible, immeasurable embodied Self are said to have an end. Therefore, O Bharata, fight.
2:18.transliteration:
antavanta ime dehā nityasyoktāḥ śarīriṇaḥ |
anāśino 'prameyasya tasmād yudhyasva bhārata ||18||
2:18.devanagari:
अन्तवन्त इमे देहा नित्यस्योक्ताः शरीरिणः ।
अनाशिनोऽप्रमेयस्य तस्माद्युध्यस्व भारत ॥ १८ ॥
2:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:18.explanation: 'Antavantaḥ' (having an end, perishable) describes the bodies; 'anāśinaḥ' (indestructible) and 'aprameyasya' (immeasurable, beyond the reach of the instruments of knowledge) describe the ātman. This verse draws the practical conclusion from the preceding Sānkhya teaching: since the body is perishable and the Self is eternal, the act of 'killing' in battle is ontologically limited to the body alone — the Self remains untouched. 'Tasmād yudhyasva' (therefore fight) is Krishna's first direct command of the Gita, grounded in metaphysical truth.

2:19.text: He who thinks this (Self) to be the slayer and he who thinks it to be slain — both of these do not know. It neither slays nor is it slain.
2:19.transliteration:
ya enaṃ vetti hantāraṃ yaś cainaṃ manyate hatam |
ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyaṃ hanti na hanyate ||19||
2:19.devanagari:
य एनं वेत्ति हन्तारं यश्चैनं मन्यते हतम् ।
उभौ तौ न विजानीतो नायं हन्ति न हन्यते ॥ १९ ॥
2:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:19.explanation: This verse is a near-direct citation from the Kaṭhopaniṣad (1.2.19), one of the principal Upaniṣads. The twin errors are identified: thinking the Self is an agent of killing ('hantāraṃ vetti') or a recipient of death ('hatam manyate'). Both rest on the confusion of Self with body. 'Na vijānītaḥ' (do not truly know) is the verdict on both positions. The ātman, being pure consciousness beyond causality, neither acts nor is acted upon. This directly dissolves Arjuna's primary moral concern — the fear of incurring the sin of killing — by showing that the very category of 'killing' does not apply to the Self.

2:20.text: This (Self) is never born, nor does it ever die; nor having once been, does it cease to be. It is unborn, eternal, ever-existing, and ancient. It is not slain when the body is slain.
2:20.transliteration:
na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin nāyaṃ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ |
ajo nityaḥ śāśvato 'yaṃ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre ||20||
2:20.devanagari:
न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचिन्नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः ।
अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे ॥ २० ॥
2:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:20.explanation: This is one of the Gita's most celebrated verses, echoing the Kaṭhopaniṣad (1.2.18). The ātman is described with seven attributes: (1) na jāyate — unborn; (2) na mriyate — undying; (3) na bhūtvā bhavitā — not newly come into being; (4) ajaḥ — birthless; (5) nityaḥ — eternal; (6) śāśvataḥ — unchanging; (7) purāṇaḥ — ancient, primordial. The culminating phrase 'na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre' (not slain when the body is slain) is the axial declaration of the Gita's metaphysics: the true Self exists beyond the reach of any physical event, including death.

2:21.text: O Partha, he who knows this Self to be indestructible, eternal, unborn, and inexhaustible — how can that person kill anyone or cause anyone to be killed?
2:21.transliteration:
vedāvināśinaṃ nityaṃ ya enam ajam avyayam |
kathaṃ sa puruṣaḥ pārtha kaṃ ghātayati hanti kam ||21||
2:21.devanagari:
वेदाविनाशिनं नित्यं य एनमजमव्ययम् ।
कथं स पुरुषः पार्थ कं घातयति हन्ति कम् ॥ २१ ॥
2:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:21.explanation: 'Veda' here means 'truly knows' (not merely intellectually believes). 'Avināśinam' (indestructible), 'nityam' (eternal), 'ajam' (unborn), 'avyayam' (inexhaustible) reiterate the four essential qualities. The verse poses a logical question: if one truly knows the nature of the ātman, the categories of 'killing' and 'being killed' become inapplicable. True knowledge destroys the doer-sense (kartṛtva). The one who knows the Self as beyond causality cannot coherently think 'I am the killer' or 'I cause killing.' This is the first step toward the teaching of niṣkāma-karma (desireless action) in verse 47.

2:22.text: Just as a person puts on new garments after discarding the worn-out ones, similarly the embodied Self (dehī) casts off worn-out bodies and takes on other new ones.
2:22.transliteration:
vāsāṃsi jīrṇāni yathā vihāya navāni gṛhṇāti naro 'parāṇi |
tathā śarīrāṇi vihāya jīrṇāny anyāni saṃyāti navāni dehī ||22||
2:22.devanagari:
वासांसि जीर्णानि यथा विहाय नवानि गृह्णाति नरोऽपराणि ।
तथा शरीराणि विहाय जीर्णान्यन्यानि संयाति नवानि देही ॥ २२ ॥
2:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:22.explanation: This is the Gita's most celebrated metaphor — the comparison of bodies to garments. As a person remains continuous through changes of clothing, the ātman remains unchanged through the succession of bodies. 'Jīrṇāni' (worn out, decayed) applies to both old garments and old bodies. 'Dehī' (the embodied one, the dweller in the body) is the Self that persists across incarnations while the body changes. This analogy makes the abstract metaphysics of the preceding verses accessible and concrete, and transforms the terror of death into a natural, cyclical event.

2:23.text: Weapons cannot cut it, fire cannot burn it, water cannot wet it, and wind cannot dry it.
2:23.transliteration:
nainaṃ chindanti śastrāṇi nainaṃ dahati pāvakaḥ |
na cainaṃ kledayanty āpo na śoṣayati mārutaḥ ||23||
2:23.devanagari:
नैनं छिन्दन्ति शस्त्राणि नैनं दहति पावकः ।
न चैनं क्लेदयन्त्यापो न शोषयति मारुतः ॥ २३ ॥
2:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:23.explanation: This is one of the most quoted verses of the entire Gita — the ātman's absolute imperviousness to the four destructive agents corresponding to the four classical elements: weapons (earth/solid), fire, water, and wind (air). 'Nainam' (not this — emphatically) is repeated, making each statement absolute. The ātman is accheda (uncuttable), adāhya (unburnable), akledya (unwettable), and aśoṣya (undryable). Since the instruments of war (weapons and fire) are specifically invoked, this verse directly answers Arjuna's fear: what he proposes to do on the battlefield cannot touch the true Self of those he faces.

2:24.text: This Self is uncleavable, incombustible, neither to be wetted nor dried. It is eternal, all-pervading, stable, immovable, and everlasting.
2:24.transliteration:
acchedyo 'yam adāhyo 'yam akledyo 'śoṣya eva ca |
nityaḥ sarva-gataḥ sthāṇur acalo 'yaṃ sanātanaḥ ||24||
2:24.devanagari:
अच्छेद्योऽयमदाह्योऽयमक्लेद्योऽशोष्य एव च ।
नित्यः सर्वगतः स्थाणुरचलोऽयं सनातनः ॥ २४ ॥
2:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:24.explanation: This verse is the affirmative counterpart to verse 23 — where verse 23 states what cannot be done to the Self, verse 24 states what the Self positively is. 'Sarva-gataḥ' (all-pervading) indicates the ātman is not localized; 'sthāṇuḥ' (pillar-like, immovable) suggests unshakeable solidity; 'acalaḥ' (motionless, not moved by anything) reinforces absolute unchangeability; 'sanātanaḥ' (everlasting, primordial) echoes the 'purāṇa' of verse 20. Together these attributes describe a Self that is simultaneously omnipresent and utterly stable — the exact opposite of the shifting, perishing body.

2:25.text: This Self is said to be unmanifest, unthinkable, and unchangeable. Therefore, knowing it as such, you should not grieve.
2:25.transliteration:
avyakto 'yam acintyo 'yam avikāryo 'yam ucyate |
tasmād evaṃ viditvainaṃ nānuśocitum arhasi ||25||
2:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:25.explanation: 'Avyaktaḥ' (unmanifest, not perceivable by the senses), 'acintyaḥ' (unthinkable, beyond the reach of the mind and intellect), and 'avikāryaḥ' (unchangeable, without modification) are the three final qualifiers of the ātman. The conclusion 'nānuśocitum arhasi' (you should not grieve) is the direct practical application: grief over the death of bodies makes no sense once one understands that the Self underlying all beings is unmanifest, ungraspable, and immutable. This verse marks the end of the core Sānkhya argument about the ātman and transitions toward the question of duty.

2:26.text: But even if you think of this Self as perpetually born and perpetually dying, O mighty-armed one, even then you should not grieve like this.
2:26.transliteration:
atha cainaṃ nitya-jātaṃ nityaṃ vā manyase mṛtam |
tathāpi tvaṃ mahābāho naivaṃ śocitum arhasi ||26||
2:26.devanagari:
अथ चैनं नित्यजातं नित्यं वा मन्यसे मृतम् ।
तथापि त्वं महाबाहो नैवं शोचितुमर्हसि ॥ २६ ॥
2:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:26.explanation: Krishna now presents a conditional argument for those who may not accept the Sānkhya position on the ātman's eternity. He essentially says: 'even granting the materialist view that consciousness is born with each body and dies with it, grief is still irrational.' This is the nyāya method of addressing the opponent's position (pūrvapakṣa) on its own terms and showing it still yields the same conclusion. 'Mahābāho' (mighty-armed) is an epithet that quietly reminds Arjuna of his warrior identity even in the midst of philosophical argument.

2:27.text: For the born, death is certain; and for the dead, birth is certain. Therefore you should not grieve over what is inevitable.
2:27.transliteration:
jātasya hi dhruvo mṛtyur dhruvaṃ janma mṛtasya ca |
tasmād aparihārye 'rthe na tvaṃ śocitum arhasi ||27||
2:27.devanagari:
जातस्य हि ध्रुवो मृत्युर्ध्रुवं जन्म मृतस्य च ।
तस्मादपरिहार्येऽर्थे न त्वं शोचितुमर्हसि ॥ २७ ॥
2:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:27.explanation: 'Dhruvaḥ' (certain, inevitable) applied to both death and rebirth makes grief logically incoherent — one does not grieve over the necessity of sunrise. 'Aparihārye 'rthe' (in a matter that is unavoidable, in an inevitable circumstance) is the key phrase: if death is an inescapable law of embodied existence, then grieving over it serves no purpose and changes nothing. Whether one accepts the Sānkhya position (eternal ātman) or the materialist position (consciousness is temporary), the conclusion is identical: grieve not. Krishna has now closed both logical escape routes for Arjuna's grief.

2:28.text: O Bharata, beings are unmanifest before birth, manifest in the middle (during life), and unmanifest again after death — what lamentation is there in this?
2:28.transliteration:
avyaktādīni bhūtāni vyakta-madhyāni bhārata |
avyakta-nidhanāny eva tatra kā paridevanā ||28||
2:28.devanagari:
अव्यक्तादीनि भूतानि व्यक्तमध्यानि भारत ।
अव्यक्तनिधनान्येव तत्र का परिदेवना ॥ २८ ॥
2:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:28.explanation: This verse presents a temporal philosophy of existence: the unmanifest (avyakta) state before birth, the manifest (vyakta) state during life, and the return to unmanifest after death. The manifest life is sandwiched between two eternities of the unmanifest. 'Paridevanā' (lamentation, wailing) is the target word: when viewed against the full arc of existence, grief over the death of a being that was unmanifest before and will be unmanifest again is without rational foundation. The lifespan is a brief illumination between two stretches of darkness — beautiful and transient, but not ultimately tragic.

2:29.text: Someone sees this Self as a wonder; another speaks of it as a wonder; another hears of it as a wonder — yet even after hearing, no one truly knows it.
2:29.transliteration:
āścaryavat paśyati kaścid enam āścaryavad vadati tathaiva cānyaḥ |
āścaryavac cainam anyaḥ śṛṇoti śrutvāpy enaṃ veda na caiva kaścit ||29||
2:29.devanagari:
आश्चर्यवत्पश्यति कश्चिदेन-माश्चर्यवद्वदति तथैव चान्यः ।
आश्चर्यवच्चैनमन्यः शृणोति श्रुत्वाप्येनं वेद न चैव कश्चित् ॥ २९ ॥
2:29.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:29.explanation: This verse closely echoes the Kaṭhopaniṣad (1.2.7). The threefold wonder — of seeing, speaking of, and hearing about the ātman — corresponds to the three valid means of knowledge: direct perception, inference/scripture, and guru-instruction. Yet 'veda na caiva kaścit' (no one truly knows it) asserts that the Self surpasses all of these as objects of intellectual comprehension. The Self cannot be known as an object, because it is the subject — the very knowing awareness. This is not pessimism but precision: the ātman can only be realized (anubhava), not merely learned.

2:30.text: O Bharata, this embodied Self (dehī) dwelling in the bodies of all is eternally indestructible. Therefore you should not grieve for any being.
2:30.transliteration:
dehī nityam avadhyo 'yaṃ dehe sarvasya bhārata |
tasmāt sarvāṇi bhūtāni na tvaṃ śocitum arhasi ||30||
2:30.devanagari:
देही नित्यमवध्योऽयं देहे सर्वस्य भारत ।
तस्मात्सर्वाणि भूतानि न त्वं शोचितुमर्हसि ॥ ३० ॥
2:30.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:30.explanation: This is the concluding verse of the Sānkhya section. 'Avadhyaḥ' (incapable of being killed) and 'nityam' (eternally) together make the point absolute. 'Dehe sarvasya' (in the body of all) is universalized: this eternal Self is not special to Bhishma or Drona — it is the nature of every being. This universalization transforms Arjuna's personal grief into a philosophical category error: if the Self of all beings is indestructible, grieving for any of them is based on a false premise. With this conclusion, Krishna pivots in verse 31 to the duty-based argument (kṣatriya-dharma).

2:31.text: Furthermore, looking at your own dharma, you should not waver; for a kṣatriya, there is nothing more auspicious than a righteous war.
2:31.transliteration:
svadharmam api cāvekṣya na vikampitum arhasi |
dharmyāddhi yuddhāc chreyo 'nyat kṣatriyasya na vidyate ||31||
2:31.devanagari:
स्वधर्ममपि चावेक्ष्य न विकम्पितुमर्हसि ।
धर्म्याद्धि युद्धाच्छ्रेयोऽन्यत्क्षत्रियस्य न विद्यते ॥ ३१ ॥
2:31.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:31.explanation: Having concluded the Sānkhya argument, Krishna shifts to the argument from svadharma (one's own inherent duty). 'Vikampitum' (to tremble, to waver) is the behavior to be avoided. Every human being belongs to a functional order in society; for Arjuna as a kṣatriya (warrior-protector), the righteous battle (dharmyāt yuddhāt) is not merely permitted but is the highest expression of his nature and social role. 'Śreyas' (the supreme good, lasting welfare) is claimed to reside, for a kṣatriya specifically, in righteous combat. The philosophical grounding of verse 30 is now being grounded in practical duty.

2:32.text: O Partha, fortunate are the kṣatriyas who encounter such a battle, which has come of itself, opening the very gates of heaven.
2:32.transliteration:
yadṛcchayā copapannaṃ svarga-dvāram apāvṛtam |
sukhinaḥ kṣatriyāḥ pārtha labhante yuddham īdṛśam ||32||
2:32.devanagari:
यदृच्छया चोपापन्नं स्वर्गद्वारमपावृतम् ।
सुखिनः क्षत्रियाः पार्थ लभन्ते युद्धमीदृशम् ॥ ३२ ॥
2:32.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:32.explanation: 'Yadṛcchayā' (of itself, without being sought) and 'upapannam' (having arrived, having presented itself) describe this battle as a rare, unsought opportunity. 'Svarga-dvāram apāvṛtam' (with the gates of heaven thrown open) refers to the ancient warrior's code: a kṣatriya who dies righteously in battle attains the highest celestial states. 'Sukhinaḥ' (fortunate, happy) flips Arjuna's entire perspective: he is grieving over an opportunity that any true kṣatriya would consider the ultimate grace. Far from a tragedy, this battle is a gift.

2:33.text: But if you do not fight this righteous battle, then having abandoned your own dharma and glory, you will incur sin.
2:33.transliteration:
atha cet tvam imaṃ dharmyaṃ saṃgrāmaṃ na kariṣyasi |
tataḥ svadharmam kīrtiṃ ca hitvā pāpam avāpsyasi ||33||
2:33.devanagari:
अथ चेत्त्वमिमं धर्म्यं संग्रामं न करिष्यसि ।
ततः स्वधर्मं कीर्तिं च हित्वा पापमवाप्स्यसि ॥ ३३ ॥
2:33.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:33.explanation: Krishna now presents the negative case: the consequences of refusal. Three losses are enumerated: (1) svadharma — the abandonment of one's inherent duty; (2) kīrti — the loss of the noble reputation that Arjuna has earned as the greatest archer; (3) pāpa — the accrual of sin from failing to perform what dharma demands. 'Dharmyam saṃgrāmam' (righteous battle) underscores that this is not merely any war — it is a war ordained by dharma. Refusing it is therefore not virtue but a violation of one's deepest dharmic nature.

2:34.text: People will speak of your imperishable infamy; and for one who has been honoured, disgrace is worse than death.
2:34.transliteration:
akīrtiṃ cāpi bhūtāni kathayiṣyanti te 'vyayām |
sambhāvitasya cākīrtir maraṇād atiricyate ||34||
2:34.devanagari:
अकीर्तिं चापि भूतानि कथयिष्यन्ति तेऽव्ययाम् ।
सम्भावितस्य चाकीर्तिर्मरणादतिरिच्यते ॥ ३४ ॥
2:34.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:34.explanation: 'Avyayām' (imperishable, never diminishing) qualifies 'akīrti' (infamy) — disgrace, once earned through cowardice, does not fade. 'Sambhāvitasya' (for one who is highly respected) makes the wound deeper: Arjuna's reputation as the greatest archer of his age makes his potential disgrace all the more catastrophic. Krishna appeals here to the kṣatriya's deepest value — yasha (honour and renown). In the warrior code, an honourable death is supremely preferable to a living dishonour. This verse transitions the teaching from Sānkhya metaphysics toward the practical motivation for action.

2:35.text: The great warriors will think that you have withdrawn from battle out of fear; and you will fall from esteem among those who have held you in high regard.
2:35.transliteration:
bhayād raṇād uparataṃ maṃsyante tvāṃ mahārathāḥ |
yeṣāṃ ca tvaṃ bahumato bhūtvā yāsyasi lāghavam ||35||
2:35.devanagari:
भयाद्रणादुपरतं मंस्यन्ते त्वां महारथाः ।
येषां च त्वं बहुमतो भूत्वा यास्यसि लाघवम् ॥ ३५ ॥
2:35.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:35.explanation: 'Mahārathāḥ' (great chariot-warriors of the highest order) — Bhishma, Drona, Karna — will interpret Arjuna's withdrawal not as compassion but as cowardice. 'Bhayād raṇād uparataṃ' (withdrawn from battle out of fear) is the verdict they will render. 'Lāghavam' (smallness, diminishment) — from one who was 'bahumataḥ' (held in great esteem). Krishna deliberately invokes Arjuna's sense of honour and peer regard, which is the kṣatriya's most visceral motivator. This is not flattery — it is a precise psychological lever aimed at the right point.

2:36.text: Your enemies will speak many unspeakable words, deriding your ability. What could be more painful than that?
2:36.transliteration:
avācya-vādāṃś ca bahūn vadiṣyanti tavāhitāḥ |
