nindantas tava sāmarthyaṃ tato duḥkhataraṃ nu kim ||36||
2:36.devanagari:
अवाच्यवादांश्च बहून्वदिष्यन्ति तवाहिताः ।
निन्दन्तस्तव सामर्थ्यं ततो दुःखतरं नु किम् ॥ ३६ ॥
2:36.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:36.explanation: 'Avācya-vādān' (words that should not be said, unspeakable slanders) — Arjuna's enemies, who had trembled before him, will now mock and ridicule him freely. 'Tava sāmarthyam nindantaḥ' (deriding your capability) targets precisely the warrior's competence and courage, which formed the foundation of his identity. 'Tato duḥkhataraṃ nu kim' (what could be more painful than that?) is a rhetorical device — Krishna places the pain of dishonour before Arjuna as a vivid, inescapable reality that is far worse than the pain of battle.

2:37.text: If slain, you will attain heaven; if victorious, you will enjoy the earth. Therefore, O son of Kunti, rise up with firm resolve for battle.
2:37.transliteration:
hato vā prāpsyasi svargaṃ jitvā vā bhoḳṣyase mahīm |
tasmād uttiṣṭha kaunteya yuddhāya kṛtaniścayaḥ ||37||
2:37.devanagari:
हतो वा प्राप्स्यसि स्वर्गं जित्वा वा भोक्ष्यसे महीम् ।
तस्मादुत्तिष्ठ कौन्तेय युद्धाय कृतनिश्चयः ॥ ३७ ॥
2:37.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:37.explanation: This is the Gita's most direct pragmatic argument — a complete logical dilemma that eliminates all bad outcomes. Death in righteous battle (dharmika yuddha) leads to heaven (svarga); victory leads to enjoyment of the earth (mahī). There is no losing scenario for the warrior who fights with dharmic purpose. 'Kṛtaniścayaḥ' (with firm resolve, with decided mind) is the antidote to Arjuna's paralysis of indecision. 'Uttiṣṭha' (arise, stand up) is the command — the same word used when awakening someone from sleep. Krishna is calling Arjuna back from the sleep of delusion.

2:38.text: Having made pleasure and pain, gain and loss, victory and defeat equal, engage in battle. In this way you will not incur sin.
2:38.transliteration:
sukha-duḥkhe same kṛtvā lābhālābhau jayājayau |
tato yuddhāya yujyasva naivaṃ pāpam avāpsyasi ||38||
2:38.devanagari:
सुखदुःखे समे कृत्वा लाभालाभौ जयाजयौ ।
ततो युद्धाय युज्यस्व नैवं पापमवाप्स्यसि ॥ ३८ ॥
2:38.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:38.explanation: This verse introduces the principle of samatva (equanimity) — the mental posture of treating all dualities as equal: sukha-duḥkha (pleasure-pain), lābha-alābha (gain-loss), jaya-ajaya (victory-defeat). 'Same kṛtvā' (having made equal) refers to an inner orientation, not a denial of reality. When action is performed without attachment to its fruits — whether pleasant or painful — it loses its karmic binding power. This is the seed of Karma-yoga, the practical philosophy Krishna will develop fully in verse 47: the most famous verse of the entire Gita.

2:39.text: O Partha, this understanding has been declared to you in terms of Sānkhya; now hear it in terms of Yoga — endowed with which understanding, you shall completely break free from the bondage of karma.
2:39.transliteration:
eṣā te 'bhihitā sāṃkhye buddhir yoge tv imāṃ śṛṇu |
buddhyā yukto yayā pārtha karma-bandhaṃ prahāsyasi ||39||
2:39.devanagari:
एषा तेऽभिहिता सांख्ये बुद्धिर्योगे त्विमां शृणु ।
बुद्ध्या युक्तो यया पार्थ कर्मबन्धं प्रहास्यसि ॥ ३९ ॥
2:39.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:39.explanation: This verse is the structural pivot of Chapter 2. 'Sāṃkhye' refers to the metaphysical teaching just given (verses 11–38): the eternal ātman, the nature of the body, and the philosophical case for action. 'Yoge' introduces Buddhi-yoga (the yoga of discriminative wisdom applied to action) — the practical framework Krishna will now elaborate. 'Karma-bandham' (bondage of karma) is the chain of action and reaction that perpetuates the cycle of birth and death. The promise is total liberation: 'prahāsyasi' (you will completely shed). Sānkhya gives the knowledge; Yoga gives the method.

2:40.text: In this (Yoga) there is no loss of the initiative, nor is there any adverse result. Even a little practice of this dharma protects one from great fear.
2:40.transliteration:
nehābhikrama-nāśo 'sti pratyavāyo na vidyate |
svalpam apy asya dharmasya trāyate mahato bhayāt ||40||
2:40.devanagari:
नेहाभिक्रमनाशोऽस्ति प्रत्यवायो न विद्यते ।
स्वल्पमप्यस्य धर्मस्य त्रायते महतो भयात् ॥ ४० ॥
2:40.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:40.explanation: 'Abhikrama-nāśa' (loss of effort already made) — unlike worldly endeavors where partial effort can be wasted, in Yoga whatever is accomplished is permanently banked in the soul and carries forward even across births. 'Pratyavāya' (adverse result, counter-effect) — this path has no downside; even incomplete practice brings net benefit. 'Svalpam api' (even a little) — the Gita makes the path accessible to all by declaring that even modest practice provides protection from 'mahato bhayāt' (great fear) — the existential fear of the endless cycle of birth, death, and suffering.

2:41.text: O joy of the Kurus, in this path the resolute understanding is single and one-pointed; but the thoughts of the irresolute are many-branched and endless.
2:41.transliteration:
vyavasāyātmikā buddhir ekā iha kurunandana |
bahu-śākhā hy anantāś ca buddhayo 'vyavasāyinām ||41||
2:41.devanagari:
व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिरेकेह कुरुनन्दन ।
बहुशाखा ह्यनन्ताश्च बुद्धयोऽव्यवसायिनाम् ॥ ४१ ॥
2:41.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:41.explanation: 'Vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ' (the resolute, determined intellect) — the mind of the Karma-yogi is focused on a single goal (the Self, duty, liberation) and does not scatter. 'Bahu-śākhā' (many-branched) describes the restless mind of one without resolve: pulled in countless directions by desires, doubts, and competing priorities. 'Anantāḥ' (endless, without limit) — the unresolved mind multiplies its distractions infinitely. This is a profound psychological observation: clarity of purpose unifies the mind, while indecision fragments it. The Gita consistently presents one-pointedness (ekāgratā) as the foundation of all yoga.

2:42.text: O Partha, those who are undiscerning, who are devoted to the letter of the Vedas and declare that there is nothing else — they proclaim this flowery speech.
2:42.transliteration:
yām imāṃ puṣpitāṃ vācaṃ pravadanty avipaścitaḥ |
veda-vāda-ratāḥ pārtha nānyad astīti vādinaḥ ||42||
2:42.devanagari:
यामिमां पुष्पितां वाचं प्रवदन्त्यविपश्चितः ।
वेदवादरताः पार्थ नान्यदस्तीति वादिनः ॥ ४२ ॥
2:42.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:42.explanation: 'Puṣpitāṃ vācam' (flowery speech) — attractive, ornate, but without the deeper fruit of Self-knowledge. 'Avipaścitaḥ' (those without true discernment) — these are people who have learned the Vedic rituals but missed the Vedic vision. 'Veda-vāda-ratāḥ' (attached to the literal word of the Vedas) — they treat the means (ritual and heavenly reward) as the end. 'Nānyad astīti vādinaḥ' (declaring there is nothing beyond this) — they have no awareness of ātman, Brahman, or mokṣa. Krishna does not reject the Vedas — he restores their ultimate purpose: liberation, not mere heavenly enjoyment.

2:43.text: Desirous of pleasures and heavenly reward, proclaiming rebirths as the fruit of actions, filled with various special rites aimed at attaining enjoyment and lordship —
2:43.transliteration:
kāmātmānaḥ svarga-parā janma-karma-phala-pradām |
kriyā-viśeṣa-bahulāṃ bhogaiśvarya-gatiṃ prati ||43||
2:43.devanagari:
कामात्मानः स्वर्गपरा जन्मकर्मफलप्रदाम् ।
क्रियाविशेषबहुलां भोगैश्वर्यगतिं प्रति ॥ ४३ ॥
2:43.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:43.explanation: This verse continues the description from verse 42 and is completed in verse 44. 'Kāmātmānaḥ' (those whose selves are made of desire) — their motivation is entirely want-based. 'Svarga-parāḥ' (those for whom heaven is the supreme goal) — they do not seek liberation but only a higher, pleasurable realm, which is itself perishable. 'Janma-karma-phala-pradām' (yielding births as the fruit of actions) — rituals earn heaven, but even heavenly merit is exhausted, leading to another birth. 'Kriyā-viśeṣa-bahulāṃ' (filled with many specific ritual acts) — complexity of ritual is mistaken for depth of wisdom.

2:44.text: For those who are deeply attached to pleasure and power, whose minds have been carried away by such (flowery) speech — for them, the resolute, one-pointed understanding does not arise in meditation.
2:44.transliteration:
bhogaiśvarya-prasaktānāṃ tayāpahṛta-cetasām |
vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ samādhau na vidhīyate ||44||
2:44.devanagari:
भोगैश्वर्यप्रसक्तानां तयापहृतचेतसाम् ।
व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिः समाधौ न विधीयते ॥ ४४ ॥
2:44.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:44.explanation: 'Apahṛta-cetasām' (those whose consciousness has been stolen away) — a powerful phrase: the mind, captured by desire for pleasure (bhoga) and power (aiśvarya), loses its freedom and cannot turn inward. 'Samādhau na vidhīyate' (is not established in samādhi) — the focused, settled understanding that is the gateway to liberation cannot take root in a mind scattered by sensory desire. This completes the three-verse description (42–44): such people hear beautiful teachings but cannot absorb or apply them because their minds are already occupied. The Gita's teaching requires a prepared mind.

2:45.text: The Vedas deal with the three guṇas (qualities of nature). Be free of the three guṇas, O Arjuna — free from the pairs of opposites, ever established in sattva, unconcerned with getting and keeping, and established in the Self.
2:45.transliteration:
traiguṇya-viṣayā vedā nistraiguṇyo bhavārjuna |
nirdvandvo nitya-sattva-stho niryoga-kṣema ātmavān ||45||
2:45.devanagari:
त्रैगुण्यविषया वेदा निस्त्रैगुण्यो भवार्जुन ।
निर्द्वन्द्वो नित्यसत्त्वस्थो निर्योगक्षेम आत्मवान् ॥ ४५ ॥
2:45.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:45.explanation: 'Traiguṇya-viṣayāḥ Vedāḥ' — the Vedic ritualistic system operates within the domain of the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) and their interactions. Krishna urges Arjuna to transcend this domain. 'Nirdvandvaḥ' (free from dualities) — beyond pleasure-pain, heat-cold, honour-dishonour. 'Nitya-sattva-sthaḥ' (ever abiding in sattva) — cultivating the quality of clarity and purity. 'Niryoga-kṣema' (unconcerned with acquisition and preservation) — the anxious human preoccupation with getting what one doesn't have and keeping what one has. 'Ātmavān' (one who possesses the Self, i.e., Self-abiding) — the culminating state.

2:46.text: As much use as there is in a small well when there is a flood of water on all sides — so much is the use of all the Vedas for a brāhmaṇa who has gained true knowledge.
2:46.transliteration:
yāvān artha udapāne sarvataḥ samplutodake |
tāvān sarveṣu vedeṣu brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ ||46||
2:46.devanagari:
यावानर्थ उदपाने सर्वतः सम्प्लुतोदके ।
तावान्सर्वेषु वेदेषु ब्राह्मणस्य विजानतः ॥ ४६ ॥
2:46.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:46.explanation: One of the Gita's most elegant analogies. The 'udapāna' (small well) is the Vedic ritual system with its specific rites and limited heavenly rewards; the 'sarvataḥ samplutodaka' (flood of water on all sides) is the vast, boundless knowledge of Brahman. When one has access to the infinite, the finite serves only a partial purpose. This does not invalidate the Vedas — the well is real and useful — but it establishes their instrumental nature. For one established in Brahma-vidyā (knowledge of ultimate reality), the Vedas' ritual section is already subsumed and transcended. Knowing the ocean, one needs not count the wells.

2:47.text: Your right is to action alone; never to its fruits at any time. Do not be motivated by the fruits of action; nor let there be attachment to inaction.
2:47.transliteration:
karmaṇy evādhikāras te mā phaleṣu kadācana |
mā karma-phala-hetur bhūr mā te saṅgo 'stv akarmaṇi ||47||
2:47.devanagari:
कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन ।
मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूर्मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि ॥ ४७ ॥
2:47.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:47.explanation: The most celebrated verse of the entire Bhagavad Gita — the foundational statement of Karma-yoga. Four precise injunctions: (1) 'Karmaṇy eva adhikāraḥ te' — your domain, your jurisdiction, is action alone. (2) 'Mā phaleṣu kadācana' — never, at any time, in the fruits. (3) 'Mā karma-phala-hetuḥ bhūḥ' — do not be the one who acts for the sake of results (this would make the fruit the motivator, corrupting the action). (4) 'Mā te saṅgo 'stv akarmaṇi' — but neither should there be attachment to inaction (a third option that misuses the teaching). The teaching is absolute: act fully, attach to nothing, and do not use non-attachment as an excuse for passivity.

2:48.text: O Dhananjaya, perform actions while established in Yoga, abandoning attachment, being equal in success and failure. Equanimity (samatva) is called Yoga.
2:48.transliteration:
yoga-sthaḥ kuru karmāṇi saṅgaṃ tyaktvā dhanañjaya |
siddhy-asiddhyoḥ samo bhūtvā samatvaṃ yoga ucyate ||48||
2:48.devanagari:
योगस्थः कुरु कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा धनञ्जय ।
सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योः समो भूत्वा समत्वं योग उच्यते ॥ ४८ ॥
2:48.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:48.explanation: 'Yoga-sthaḥ' (established in Yoga) — acting from a state of inner integration and balance, not from agitation or craving. 'Saṅgaṃ tyaktvā' (having renounced attachment) — releasing the inner grip on outcomes before the action begins. 'Siddhy-asiddhyoḥ samaḥ' (equal in success and failure) — receiving both outcomes with the same undisturbed awareness. Then comes the Gita's concise definition of Yoga: 'Samatvaṃ yoga ucyate' — Yoga is equanimity. Not escapism, not emotional flatness, but the stable inner poise that allows full, wholehearted engagement with life without being enslaved by its results.

2:49.text: Far inferior to Buddhi-yoga is action motivated by results, O Dhananjaya. Seek refuge in the buddhi. Those who are motivated by fruits are miserly.
2:49.transliteration:
dūreṇa hy avaraṃ karma buddhi-yogād dhanañjaya |
buddhau śaraṇam anviccha kṛpaṇāḥ phala-hetavaḥ ||49||
2:49.devanagari:
दूरेण ह्यवरं कर्म बुद्धियोगाद्धनञ्जय ।
बुद्धौ शरणमन्विच्छ कृपणाः फलहेतवः ॥ ४९ ॥
2:49.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:49.explanation: 'Avaraṃ karma' (inferior action) — result-motivated action is fundamentally lower because it keeps the actor bound to the wheel of karma. 'Buddhau śaraṇam anviccha' (seek refuge in the discriminative intellect) — the buddhi, when purified and aligned with the Self, becomes the vehicle of liberation rather than bondage. 'Kṛpaṇāḥ phala-hetavaḥ' (miserly are those who act for fruits) — a striking judgment. The Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad calls those who do not know the Imperishable 'kṛpaṇāḥ' (the impoverished). One who is unaware of one's true wealth — the ātman — grasps at petty external fruits. This is the poverty of the unawakened.

2:50.text: One endowed with Buddhi-yoga casts off both good and evil deeds here itself. Therefore, yoke yourself to Yoga. Yoga is skill in actions.
2:50.transliteration:
buddhi-yukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛta-duṣkṛte |
tasmād yogāya yujyasva yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam ||50||
2:50.devanagari:
बुद्धियुक्तो जहातीह उभे सुकृतदुष्कृते ।
तस्माद्योगाय युज्यस्व योगः कर्मसु कौशलम् ॥ ५० ॥
2:50.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:50.explanation: 'Ubhe sukṛta-duṣkṛte jahāti' (casts off both good and bad deeds) — the Buddhi-yukta (one united with discriminative wisdom) transcends the entire domain of merit and demerit. Good karma (sukṛta) still binds — it binds to pleasant rebirths; only action performed with non-attachment and equanimity leaves no residue. 'Yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam' — this is one of the Gita's most quoted definitions: Yoga is skill (kauśala) in action. The skill referred to is not mere technical efficiency but the inner wisdom of acting without creating bondage — the art of engaged non-attachment.

2:51.text: The wise endowed with Buddhi-yoga, abandoning the fruits born of action, are freed from the bondage of birth and attain the state that is free from all affliction.
2:51.transliteration:
karma-jaṃ buddhi-yuktā hi phalaṃ tyaktvā manīṣiṇaḥ |
janma-bandha-vinirmuktāḥ padaṃ gacchanty anāmayam ||51||
2:51.devanagari:
कर्मजं बुद्धियुक्ता हि फलं त्यक्त्वा मनीषिणः ।
जन्मबन्धविनिर्मुक्ताः पदं गच्छन्त्यनामयम् ॥ ५१ ॥
2:51.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:51.explanation: 'Manīṣiṇaḥ' (the wise, those of refined mind) — the Buddhi-yuktas who have absorbed the teaching and live it. 'Karma-jaṃ phalaṃ tyaktvā' (abandoning the fruit born of action) — not the action itself but the inner claim to its results. 'Janma-bandha-vinirmuktāḥ' (freed from the bondage of birth) — birth-and-death is the fundamental bondage; liberation is liberation from this cycle. 'Padam anāmayam' (the state free from all affliction, disease, or blemish) — mokṣa described through negation of all limitation: no grief, no bondage, no impermanence. This is the ultimate destination of Karma-yoga practiced with wisdom.

2:52.text: When your intellect will cross the thicket of delusion, then you will attain dispassion (nirveda) toward what has been heard and what is yet to be heard.
2:52.transliteration:
yadā te moha-kalilaṃ buddhir vyatitariṣyati |
tadā gantāsi nirvedaṃ śrotavyasya śrutasya ca ||52||
2:52.devanagari:
यदा ते मोहकलिलं बुद्धिर्व्यतितरिष्यति ।
तदा गन्तासि निर्वेदं श्रोतव्यस्य श्रुतस्य च ॥ ५२ ॥
2:52.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:52.explanation: 'Moha-kalilaṃ' (the dense thicket or swamp of delusion) — moha (confusion-generating attachment) is not merely error but a viscous, clinging medium that traps the intellect. 'Vyatitariṣyati' (will cross over) — the crossing is a dynamic event, not gradual erosion. 'Nirvedam' (dispassion, satiation) — not depression or disgust, but the natural turning away that occurs when one has tasted something greater. 'Śrotavyasya śrutasya ca' (of what has been heard and what is to be heard) — all scriptural injunctions, including the Vedic ones just critiqued, become automatically subsumed when direct realization dawns.

2:53.text: When your intellect — scattered by the conflicting declarations of the scriptures — will stand immovable and unwavering in samādhi, then you will attain Yoga.
2:53.transliteration:
śruti-vipratipannā te yadā sthāsyati niścalā |
samādhāv acalā buddhis tadā yogam avāpsyasi ||53||
2:53.devanagari:
श्रुतिविप्रतिपन्ना ते यदा स्थास्यति निश्चला ।
समाधावचला बुद्धिस्तदा योगमवाप्स्यसि ॥ ५३ ॥
2:53.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:53.explanation: 'Śruti-vipratipannā' (confused, pulled in different directions by scriptural declarations) — different Vedic texts prescribe different paths for different stages and purposes; when heard without integration, they create bewilderment. 'Niścalā samādhāv acalā' (immovable and unwavering in samādhi) — when the intellect settles into the silence that underlies all scriptural instruction, the confusion dissolves. 'Yogam avāpsyasi' (you will attain Yoga) — Yoga here is its ultimate meaning: the complete union of the individual with the universal, the state of liberation. This verse naturally prompts Arjuna's famous question in verse 54: what does such a person look and speak like?

2:54.text: Arjuna said: O Keshava, what is the description of one of steady wisdom established in samādhi? How does the sthita-dhī speak? How does he sit? How does he walk?
2:54.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
sthita-prajñasya kā bhāṣā samādhi-sthasya keśava |
sthita-dhīḥ kiṃ prabhāṣeta kim āsīta vrajeta kim ||54||
2:54.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
स्थितप्रज्ञस्य का भाषा समाधिस्थस्य केशव ।
स्थितधीः किं प्रभाषेत किमासीत व्रजेत किम् ॥ ५४ ॥
2:54.speaker: Arjuna
2:54.explanation: This is the Gita's single most consequential question. 'Sthita-prajña' (one of steady wisdom) is the Gita's central practical ideal — the person in whom Self-knowledge has become permanently established. 'Samādhi-sthasya' (established in samādhi) — not the temporary state of formal meditation but the ongoing condition of inner equanimity. Arjuna asks three questions covering all dimensions of expression: speech (prabhāṣeta), seated comportment (āsīta), and movement (vrajeta). This question bridges metaphysics and daily life: what does enlightenment look like when it walks and speaks? The answer (verses 55–72) is the Gita's portrait of the liberated human being.

2:55.text: The Blessed Lord said: O Partha, when one completely abandons all desires dwelling in the mind and is satisfied in the Self alone by the Self — then one is called sthita-prajña (one of steady wisdom).
2:55.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
prajahāti yadā kāmān sarvān pārtha mano-gatān |
ātmany evātmanā tuṣṭaḥ sthita-prajñas tadocyate ||55||
2:55.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
प्रजहाति यदा कामान्सर्वान्पार्थ मनोगतान् ।
आत्मन्येवात्मना तुष्टः स्थितप्रज्ञस्तदोच्यते ॥ ५५ ॥
2:55.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:55.explanation: 'Prajahāti' (completely abandons) — not suppression but genuine release, the natural falling away of desire when the Self is found to be self-sufficient. 'Mano-gatān sarvān kāmān' (all desires residing in the mind) — desires live in the mind, not in the objects. The objects are external; the craving is internal. 'Ātmany evātmanā tuṣṭaḥ' (satisfied in the Self alone, by the Self) — this is the revolutionary claim: the ātman is self-luminous and self-complete; no external object is required for its fullness. When this is realized experientially, desire does not need to be renounced — it simply has no more ground to stand on.

2:56.text: One whose mind is not agitated in adversity, who has no craving in prosperity, who is free from attachment, fear, and anger — such a one is called a muni of steady wisdom.
2:56.transliteration:
duḥkheṣv anudvigna-manāḥ sukheṣu vigata-spṛhaḥ |
vīta-rāga-bhaya-krodhaḥ sthita-dhīr munir ucyate ||56||
2:56.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:56.explanation: 'Anudvigna-manāḥ' (mind not agitated) in the presence of duḥkha (pain, grief, difficulty) — the sthita-prajña is not immune to suffering; rather, the mind does not lose its ground when suffering arises. 'Vigata-spṛhaḥ' (craving absent) in the presence of sukha (pleasure, good fortune) — pleasure is received without grasping, so it does not create a dependency. 'Vīta-rāga-bhaya-krodhaḥ' — free from the triad that drives most human behavior: rāga (desire-attachment), bhaya (fear), and krodha (anger, the response to obstruction). The 'muni' here is not a forest renunciate but any person of realized wisdom.

2:57.text: One who is without attachment everywhere, who neither rejoices on obtaining good nor feels aversion on getting evil — his wisdom is firmly established.
2:57.transliteration:
yaḥ sarvatrānabhisnehas tat-tat prāpya śubhāśubham |
nābhinandati na dveṣṭi tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā ||57||
2:57.devanagari:
यः सर्वत्रानभिस्नेहस्तत्तत्प्राप्य शुभाशुभम् ।
नाभिनन्दति न द्वेष्टि तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ॥ ५७ ॥
2:57.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:57.explanation: 'Anabhisnehaḥ' (without deep clinging-affection) — 'sneha' here means not love in itself but possessive attachment, the kind that creates dependency. This is not the teaching of cold indifference but of love without grasping. 'Nābhinandati' (does not over-rejoice) at good fortune — not suppressing joy, but not being pulled off-center by it. 'Na dveṣṭi' (does not feel hatred/aversion) at misfortune — not pretending it is pleasant, but not being destabilized by it. 'Tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā' (his wisdom is established) — this refrain appears repeatedly in the sthita-prajña section, anchoring each quality back to the core attribute of settled understanding.

2:58.text: When, like a tortoise withdrawing its limbs on all sides, one withdraws the senses from the sense-objects — then his wisdom is firmly established.
2:58.transliteration:
yadā saṃharate cāyaṃ kūrmo 'ṅgānīva sarvaśaḥ |
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyas tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā ||58||
2:58.devanagari:
यदा संहरते चायं कूर्मोऽङ्गानीव सर्वशः ।
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ॥ ५८ ॥
2:58.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:58.explanation: One of the Gita's most beloved and memorable similes. The kūrma (tortoise) withdraws its limbs completely within its shell when threatened, and extends them again at will — it does not destroy its limbs, it masters them. The sthita-prajña similarly possesses the ability to withdraw the senses from their objects (indriyārthebhyaḥ) voluntarily and completely. This is pratyāhāra — sense-withdrawal — the fifth limb of Patañjali's Yoga, here expressed through a single luminous image. The key point is sovereignty over the senses, not their elimination.

2:59.text: For one who restrains the senses from objects, the objects turn away, but the taste (rasa) for them remains. Even this taste departs for one who has seen the Supreme.
2:59.transliteration:
viṣayā vinivartante nirāhārasya dehinaḥ |
rasa-varjaṃ raso 'py asya paraṃ dṛṣṭvā nivartate ||59||
2:59.devanagari:
विषया विनिवर्तन्ते निराहारस्य देहिनः ।
रसवर्जं रसोऽप्यस्य परं दृष्ट्वा निवर्तते ॥ ५९ ॥
2:59.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:59.explanation: 'Nirāhārasya dehinaḥ' (the embodied one who abstains from sense-objects) — mere external restraint, without inner transformation, removes the objects but not the craving. 'Rasa' (taste, subtle relish, inner attraction) persists even in formal renunciates who have not yet experienced the Supreme. 'Paraṃ dṛṣṭvā' (having seen the Supreme) — when direct, experiential knowledge of the Self/Brahman dawns, the taste for lesser things does not need to be forcibly removed — it simply evaporates in the greater light. This is the Gita's distinction between will-power asceticism and wisdom-based liberation: the former suppresses, the latter transcends.

2:60.text: O son of Kunti, even for the wise person who strives, the turbulent senses forcibly carry away the mind.
2:60.transliteration:
yatato hy api kaunteya puruṣasya vipaścitaḥ |
indriyāṇi pramāthīni haranti prasabhaṃ manaḥ ||60||
2:60.devanagari:
यततो ह्यपि कौन्तेय पुरुषस्य विपश्चितः ।
इन्द्रियाणि प्रमाथीनि हरन्ति प्रसभं मनः ॥ ६० ॥
2:60.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:60.explanation: 'Vipaścitaḥ' (the discerning, learned person) — Krishna is honest: even one who has knowledge and makes sincere effort (yatataḥ) is not automatically immune to the power of the senses. 'Pramāthīni' (those that agitate, churn, violently disturb) — a vivid word for the raw, churning force of the sense impulses. 'Prasabhaṃ haranti manaḥ' (forcibly carry away the mind) — 'prasabham' means by sheer force, without asking permission. This frank acknowledgment of the senses' power is not defeatism — it sets up the solution in the next verse: surrender to the Supreme as the ultimate safeguard.

2:61.text: Having restrained all of them (the senses), one should sit with Me as the Supreme (mat-paraḥ). For whose senses are under control, his wisdom is firmly established.
2:61.transliteration:
tāni sarvāṇi saṃyamya yukta āsīta mat-paraḥ |
vaśe hi yasyendriyāṇi tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā ||61||
2:61.devanagari:
तानि सर्वाणि संयम्य युक्त आसीत मत्परः ।
वशे हि यस्येन्द्रियाणि तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ॥ ६१ ॥
2:61.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:61.explanation: 'Tāni sarvāṇi saṃyamya' (restraining all of them) — all the senses, not selectively. 'Mat-paraḥ' (with Me as the Supreme, devoted to Me) — the key insight: the antidote to the senses' power is not will-power alone but devotion (bhakti). When the mind is oriented toward the Supreme, it naturally withdraws from lesser pulls. This is the confluence of Karma-yoga, Jñāna-yoga, and Bhakti-yoga: discipline, wisdom, and love united. 'Vaśe hi yasyendriyāṇi' (whose senses are under his control) — the test of sthita-prajñatā is not the absence of senses but their subordination to the awakened will.

2:62.text: In one who dwells on sense-objects, attachment to them arises; from attachment, desire is born; from desire, anger is born.
2:62.transliteration:
dhyāyato viṣayān puṃsaḥ saṅgas teṣūpajāyate |
saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho 'bhijāyate ||62||
2:62.devanagari:
ध्यायतो विषयान्पुंसः सङ्गस्तेषूपजायते ।
सङ्गात्सञ्जायते कामः कामात्क्रोधोऽभिजायते ॥ ६२ ॥
2:62.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:62.explanation: This and the following verse describe the Gita's famous 'chain of downfall' — a precise psychological sequence. 'Dhyāyataḥ viṣayān' (one who dwells upon sense-objects in the mind) — the problem begins not with the senses but with sustained mental attention on objects of desire. From contemplation → saṅga (attachment, the sense that 'I need this') → kāma (desire, active craving) → krodha (anger, when desire is frustrated or blocked). This sequence has striking parallels to modern psychological models of craving, cognitive fixation, and emotional dysregulation. The Gita pinpoints the starting point: unguarded mental attention to objects of craving.

2:63.text: From anger comes delusion; from delusion, loss of memory; from loss of memory, destruction of the intellect; from destruction of the intellect, one is completely ruined.
2:63.transliteration:
krodhād bhavati sammohaḥ sammohāt smṛti-vibhramaḥ |
smṛti-bhraṃśād buddhi-nāśo buddhi-nāśāt praṇaśyati ||63||
2:63.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:63.explanation: Verses 62–63 together describe the complete chain of psychological collapse. From anger (krodha) → sammoha (delusion, total confusion of judgment) → smṛti-vibhrama (loss of memory — here meaning the obliteration of one's accumulated wisdom, values, and guiding principles) → buddhi-nāśa (destruction of the discriminative intellect — the loss of the capacity to tell right from wrong) → praṇaśyati (complete ruin of the person). This is not hyperbole but a precise map of how one moment of unguarded desire, left to escalate, can destroy a person's entire moral and psychological infrastructure. Arjuna's own crisis in Chapter 1 is a living example of this chain in early stages.

2:64.text: But one who moves among sense-objects with senses freed from attraction and aversion, self-controlled, with the self mastered — attains inner serenity (prasāda).
2:64.transliteration:
rāga-dveṣa-viyuktais tu viṣayān indriyaiś caran |
ātma-vaśyair vidheyātmā prasādam adhigacchati ||64||
2:64.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:64.explanation: 'Rāga-dveṣa-viyuktaiḥ' (senses freed from attraction and aversion) — the senses are not destroyed but purified of their habitual biases: the 'toward' of rāga and the 'away from' of dveṣa. 'Indriyaiś caran' (moving among sense-objects) — this is not renunciation of the world but engagement with it from freedom. 'Vidheyātmā' (one whose self is disciplined, under governance of the higher Self) — the lower self is subordinated to the higher. 'Prasādam adhigacchati' (attains prasāda) — prasāda is a luminous, translucent serenity of consciousness — not mere calmness but an inwardly radiant clarity. This is the alternative path to the ruin described in verses 62–63.

2:65.text: Upon attaining serenity (prasāda), all sorrow disappears for this person. Indeed, the intellect of one with a serene mind quickly becomes well-established.
2:65.transliteration:
prasāde sarva-duḥkhānāṃ hānir asyopajāyate |
prasanna-cetaso hy āśu buddhiḥ paryavatiṣṭhate ||65||
2:65.devanagari:
प्रसादे सर्वदुःखानां हानिरस्योपजायते ।
प्रसन्नचेतसो ह्याशु बुद्धिः पर्यवतिष्ठते ॥ ६५ ॥
2:65.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:65.explanation: 'Sarva-duḥkhānāṃ hāniḥ' (the cessation of all sorrows) — a sweeping claim: inner serenity is not merely one of many solutions to suffering but the foundational one. When the inner ground is stable, no external event can constitute total ruin. 'Prasanna-cetasaḥ' (of one with a bright, clear mind) — prasanna carries connotations of luminosity and purity, not merely contentment. 'Āśu' (swiftly) — once the foundation of inner peace is found, wisdom establishes itself rapidly; the two reinforce each other. Prasāda is both the fruit of wisdom and the soil in which further wisdom grows.

2:66.text: For the undisciplined (ayukta), there is no wisdom; for the undisciplined, there is no contemplative power (bhāvanā); for one without contemplation, there is no peace; and for the peaceless, how can there be happiness?
2:66.transliteration:
nāsti buddhir ayuktasya na cāyuktasya bhāvanā |
na cābhāvayataḥ śāntir aśāntasya kutaḥ sukham ||66||
2:66.devanagari:
नास्ति बुद्धिरयुक्तस्य न चायुक्तस्य भावना ।
न चाभावयतः शान्तिरशान्तस्य कुतः सुखम् ॥ ६६ ॥
2:66.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:66.explanation: A flawless logical chain of negation. 'Ayukta' (unintegrated, undisciplined, not yoked to the Self) — the foundational condition of most unexamined lives. Without discipline: no buddhi (discriminative wisdom); without buddhi: no bhāvanā (sustained meditative attention, the ability to dwell on truth); without bhāvanā: no śānti (peace); without śānti: 'kutaḥ sukham?' (from where could happiness possibly come?). This verse implicitly answers Arjuna's anguished question from Chapter 1 (verse 37: 'how could we be happy?') — happiness has a precise prerequisite structure, and none of it comes from external achievement.

2:67.text: For when the mind follows any one of the roaming senses, it carries away his wisdom — just as the wind carries away a boat on water.
2:67.transliteration:
indriyāṇāṃ hi caratāṃ yan mano 'nuvidhīyate |
tad asya harati prajñāṃ vāyur nāvam ivāmbhasi ||67||
2:67.devanagari:
इन्द्रियाणां हि चरतां यन्मनोऽनुविधीयते ।
तदस्य हरति प्रज्ञां वायुर्नावमिवाम्भसि ॥ ६७ ॥
2:67.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:67.explanation: Another luminous Gita simile. 'Vāyur nāvam ivāmbhasi' (as the wind carries away a boat on water) — a large boat on open water, however seaworthy, is helpless before a powerful sidewind that drives it off course. Similarly, the entire structure of one's wisdom can be capsized by a single unguarded sense-indulgence. 'Yat manaḥ anuvidhīyate' (whichever sense the mind follows) — even one sense — not all of them need to be simultaneously active. This explains why the previous verse emphasized all-encompassing sense-restraint: one breach is sufficient for the loss of course.

2:68.text: Therefore, O mighty-armed one, the wisdom (prajñā) of one who has fully withdrawn the senses from their objects in every way is firmly established.
2:68.transliteration:
tasmād yasya mahābāho nigṛhītāni sarvaśaḥ |
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyas tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā ||68||
2:68.devanagari:
तस्माद्यस्य महाबाहो निगृहीतानि सर्वशः ।
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ॥ ६८ ॥
2:68.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:68.explanation: 'Tasmāt' (therefore) marks this as the direct conclusion of the preceding argument: as wind carries away a boat on water (2.67), so uncontrolled senses carry away the mind and destroy discernment. 'Nigṛhītāni sarvaśaḥ' (withdrawn completely, in every direction) emphasizes total and consistent restraint, not partial or occasional control. 'Mahābāho' (mighty-armed) is a deliberate contrast — Krishna addresses Arjuna's external physical power while speaking of the far greater internal power of sense-mastery. 'Prajñā pratiṣṭhitā' (wisdom firmly established) is the culminating phrase: only when the senses are mastered does the intellect achieve its natural, undistracted stability. This verse closes the practical portrait of the sthita-prajña begun at verse 55.

2:69.text: What is night for all beings, in that the self-controlled one (saṃyamī) is awake; what all beings are awake to, that is night for the seeing sage (muni).
2:69.transliteration:
yā niśā sarvabhūtānāṃ tasyāṃ jāgarti saṃyamī |
yasyāṃ jāgrati bhūtāni sā niśā paśyato muneḥ ||69||
2:69.devanagari:
या निशा सर्वभूतानां तस्यां जागर्ति संयमी ।
यस्यां जाग्रति भूतानि सा निशा पश्यतो मुनेः ॥ ६९ ॥
2:69.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:69.explanation: This is among the most celebrated and poetically compressed verses of the Gita — a perfect paradox. The verse describes two inverted states of consciousness. What is 'day' for ordinary beings — sense pleasures, worldly success, material experience — is 'night' (darkness, sleep, ignorance) for the wise: the sage is utterly indifferent to it, as one asleep. Conversely, what the sage is 'awake' to — the ātman, the eternal reality, Brahman — is total darkness (niśā) for ordinary beings: they cannot perceive it at all, being absorbed in external phenomena. 'Saṃyamī' (the disciplined one) and 'paśyato muneḥ' (the seeing sage) describe the same realized person from two angles: one who has mastered the senses and thereby gained true vision.

2:70.text: Just as waters flow into the ocean, which though ever being filled remains unmoved and within its bounds — so too the one into whom all desires enter (and are absorbed) attains peace; not the one who keeps desiring desires.
2:70.transliteration:
āpūryamāṇam acala-pratiṣṭhaṃ
samudram āpaḥ praviśanti yadvat |
tadvat kāmā yaṃ praviśanti sarve
sa śāntim āpnoti na kāma-kāmī ||70||
2:70.devanagari:
आपूर्यमाणमचलप्रतिष्ठं
समुद्रमापः प्रविशन्ति यद्वत् ।
तद्वत्कामा यं प्रविशन्ति सर्वे
स शान्तिमाप्नोति न कामकामी ॥ ७० ॥
2:70.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:70.explanation: One of the Gita's most luminous similes. The ocean (samudra) receives rivers from all directions continuously (āpūryamāṇam) yet remains perfectly still and contained within its banks (acala-pratiṣṭham) — it is not disturbed, does not overflow, does not lose its nature. This is the precise image of the sthita-prajña's inner life: desires, sense-impressions, and experiences flow in continuously, but the sage, whose identity is rooted in the ātman rather than in the desires themselves, is not moved by them. The desires dissolve into his vast equanimity. 'Na kāma-kāmī' (not the one who lusts after desires) is the sharp contrast: the person who actively seeks and chases desires can never find peace, for desires multiply as they are fed. Fulfillment lies in transcending the desirer, not in satisfying the desires.

2:71.text: The person who, having abandoned all desires, moves through life free of longing (niḥspṛha), free of the sense of 'mine' (nirmama), and free of ego (nirahaṃkāra) — that person attains peace.
2:71.transliteration:
vihāya kāmān yaḥ sarvān pumāṃś carati niḥspṛhaḥ |
nirmamo nirahaṃkāraḥ sa śāntim adhigacchati ||71||
2:71.devanagari:
विहाय कामान्यः सर्वान्पुमांश्चरति निःस्पृहः ।
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः स शान्तिमधिगच्छति ॥ ७१ ॥
2:71.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:71.explanation: This verse presents the positive portrait of the liberated person through three 'nir-' (without) compounds, each removing a root of suffering. 'Niḥspṛhaḥ' (without longing, without craving) is the absence of tṛṣṇā — the thirst that drives all grasping. 'Nirmamaḥ' (without the sense of 'mine') dissolves the possessive identification with body, relations, wealth, and name that binds the self to the perishable. 'Nirahaṃkāraḥ' (without ego, without the false 'I') removes the root cause: the mistaken identification of the infinite ātman with the limited individual ego. These three are causally connected — ego generates 'mine-ness,' and 'mine-ness' generates craving. When ego is uprooted, peace is the natural result. 'Carati' (moves, lives) confirms this peace is lived in the world, not in isolation.

2:72.text: O Pārtha, this is the Brāhmī state (the state of Brahman). Having attained it, one is never again deluded. Being established in it even at the moment of death, one reaches Brahma-nirvāṇa — absorption in Brahman.
2:72.transliteration:
eṣā brāhmī sthitiḥ pārtha naināṃ prāpya vimuhyati |
sthitvāsyām anta-kāle 'pi brahma-nirvāṇam ṛcchati ||72||
2:72.devanagari:
एषा ब्राह्मी स्थितिः पार्थ नैनां प्राप्य विमुह्यति ।
स्थित्वास्यामन्तकालेऽपि ब्रह्मनिर्वाणमृच्छति ॥ ७२ ॥
2:72.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
2:72.explanation: 'Brāhmī sthitiḥ' (the state of Brahman) is the culminating phrase of Chapter 2 — the name given to the totality of what has been described since verse 55: the steady wisdom, sense-mastery, ocean-like equanimity, freedom from ego and desire, and full wakefulness to the ātman. This is not a temporary mental state but an irreversible transformation: 'naināṃ prāpya vimuhyati' — having attained it, one is never again subject to moha (delusion). 'Anta-kāle 'pi' (even at the moment of death) confirms that this is a living realization, not a posthumous reward; one established in it carries it into death itself. 'Brahma-nirvāṇam' (absorption in Brahman) is the Gita's term for liberation — not annihilation, but the dissolution of the false individual self into the infinite reality it always was. This verse is both the conclusion of Chapter 2 and the opening statement of the Gita's entire spiritual program.

3:1.text: Arjuna said: O Janardana, if You consider knowledge (buddhi) to be superior to action, then why do You urge me to this terrible action (war), O Keshava?
3:1.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
jyāyasī cet karmaṇas te matā buddhir janārdana |
tat kiṃ karmaṇi ghore māṃ niyojayasi keśava ||1||
3:1.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
ज्यायसी चेत्कर्मणस्ते मता बुद्धिर्जनार्दन ।
तत्किं कर्मणि घोरे मां नियोजयसि केशव ॥ १ ॥
3:1.speaker: Arjuna
3:1.explanation: Arjuna's question is sharp, sincere, and logically rigorous. In Chapter 2 Krishna had praised the path of buddhi (discriminative wisdom) and the characteristics of the sthita-prajña. Arjuna now draws what seems the obvious conclusion: if knowledge is superior, why fight? 'Ghore karmaṇi' (terrible action) — Arjuna's word 'ghora' (terrible, violent) reveals he still sees war through the lens of his grief. The pairing of 'Janārdana' (lord of all people) and 'Keśava' (beautiful-haired) in a single verse gives the question a slightly challenging tone. This question is the perfect opening for the doctrine of Karma-yoga: the resolution of the apparent tension between knowledge and action.

3:2.text: With seemingly mixed words, You confuse my intellect. Therefore, tell me decisively that one thing by which I may attain the highest good (śreya).
3:2.transliteration:
vyāmiśreṇeva vākyena buddhiṃ mohayasīva me |
tad ekaṃ vada niścitya yena śreyo 'ham āpnuyām ||2||
3:2.devanagari:
व्यामिश्रेणेव वाक्येन बुद्धिं मोहयसीव मे ।
तदेकं वद निश्चित्य येन श्रेयोऽहमाप्नुयाम् ॥ २ ॥
3:2.speaker: Arjuna
3:2.explanation: 'Vyāmiśreṇeva vākyena' (with seemingly confusing, mixed words) — Arjuna's use of 'iva' (seemingly, as if) is tactful: he does not directly accuse Krishna of contradiction but registers his confusion. The apparent contradiction he perceives is between Chapter 2's praise of jñāna and the present command to act. 'Tad ekaṃ vada' (tell me that one thing) — Arjuna craves a single, clear, actionable directive. 'Śreya' (the highest good, lasting welfare) — the word echoes Chapter 1's central concern. This honest confession of confusion is the perfect invitation for Krishna to systematically expound Karma-yoga.

3:3.text: The Blessed Lord said: O sinless one (Anagha), two paths of devotion have been expounded by Me from ancient times in this world — the path of knowledge (jñāna-yoga) for the contemplative (sāṃkhya), and the path of action (karma-yoga) for the active (yogin).
3:3.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
loke 'smin dvividhā niṣṭhā purā proktā mayānagha |
jñāna-yogena sāṃkhyānāṃ karma-yogena yoginām ||3||
3:3.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
लोकेऽस्मिन्द्विविधा निष्ठा पुरा प्रोक्ता मयानघ ।
ज्ञानयोगेन सांख्यानां कर्मयोगेन योगिनाम् ॥ ३ ॥
3:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:3.explanation: 'Anagha' (sinless, blameless) — Krishna's address acknowledges Arjuna's purity even as he is confused. 'Dvividhā niṣṭhā' (two kinds of steadfast devotion, two paths) resolves the apparent contradiction Arjuna perceived: Krishna had not contradicted himself — he had presented two valid and complete paths suited to different temperaments. Jñāna-yoga is for the sāṃkhya — the predominantly contemplative, discriminative person who approaches the truth through direct philosophical inquiry. Karma-yoga is for the yogin — the active, engaged person who realizes the same truth through disciplined, desireless action. Neither is inferior; both lead to the same goal.

3:4.text: A person does not attain freedom from the bondage of action (naiṣkarmya) by merely refraining from action; nor does he attain perfection (siddhi) merely by renouncing action (sannyāsa).
3:4.transliteration:
na karmaṇām anārambhān naiṣkarmyaṃ puruṣo 'śnute |
na ca sannyasanād eva siddhiṃ samadhigacchati ||4||
3:4.devanagari:
न कर्मणामनारम्भान्नैष्कर्म्यं पुरुषोऽश्नुते ।
न च सन्न्यसनादेव सिद्धिं समधिगच्छति ॥ ४ ॥
3:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:4.explanation: 'Naiṣkarmya' is not the cessation of action but the state of being free from karma's binding effect. Krishna demolishes a common misunderstanding: that liberation is achieved by simply doing nothing. The first line addresses passive inaction (not starting action); the second addresses formal renunciation (sannyāsa) taken as an external ritual without inner transformation. Both miss the point. True naiṣkarmya arises not from the absence of action but from action performed without ego and desire. This verse, together with 3.5, forms the logical foundation of the entire Karma-yoga: the impossibility of inaction makes desireless action the only intelligent path.

3:5.text: Indeed, no one can remain even for a moment without performing action; for all beings are helplessly impelled to action by the guṇas born of prakṛti.
3:5.transliteration:
na hi kaścit kṣaṇam api jātu tiṣṭhaty akarma-kṛt |
kāryate hy avaśaḥ karma sarvaḥ prakṛtijair guṇaiḥ ||5||
3:5.devanagari:
न हि कश्चित्क्षणमपि जातु तिष्ठत्यकर्मकृत् ।
कार्यते ह्यवशः कर्म सर्वः प्रकृतिजैर्गुणैः ॥ ५ ॥
3:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:5.explanation: 'Kaścit kṣaṇam api' (even for a moment, anyone at all) — this is an absolute statement. Every biological, psychological, and volitional activity is karmically speaking an action. Even the decision to 'do nothing' is itself an action. The three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) born of prakṛti (nature) are the cosmic forces that drive all activity without exception. 'Avaśaḥ' (helplessly, without agency) shows that resistance to action is futile — the question is never whether to act, but how. This verse closes the door on escapism and makes the Karma-yoga imperative: since action is unavoidable, make it wise, desireless, and God-oriented.

3:6.text: One who outwardly restrains the organs of action while inwardly dwelling on sense-objects with the mind — that deluded person is called a hypocrite (mithyācāra).
3:6.transliteration:
karmendriyāṇi saṃyamya ya āste manasā smaran |
indriyārthān vimūḍhātmā mithyācāraḥ sa ucyate ||6||
3:6.devanagari:
कर्मेन्द्रियाणि संयम्य य आस्ते मनसा स्मरन् ।
इन्द्रियार्थान्विमूढात्मा मिथ्याचारः स उच्यते ॥ ६ ॥
3:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:6.explanation: 'Karmendriyāṇi' (the organs of action — hands, feet, speech, and the lower organs) can be physically restrained while the mind continues to roam freely among sense pleasures. Krishna condemns this as 'mithyācāra' (false conduct, hypocrisy) — outward renunciation masking inner craving. 'Vimūḍhātmā' (greatly deluded in soul) identifies this as not just moral failure but a fundamental cognitive error: the person mistakes the external form of renunciation for its substance. True renunciation is the mind's withdrawal from objects, not the body's. This verse directly addresses those who mistake ascetic posture for spiritual practice.

3:7.text: But one who, controlling the senses through the mind, O Arjuna, engages the organs of action in Karma-yoga without attachment — that person is superior.
3:7.transliteration:
yas tv indriyāṇi manasā niyamyārabhate 'rjuna |
karmendriyaiḥ karma-yogam asaktaḥ sa viśiṣyate ||7||
3:7.devanagari:
यस्त्विन्द्रियाणि मनसा नियम्यारभतेऽर्जुन ।
कर्मेन्द्रियैः कर्मयोगमसक्तः स विशिष्यते ॥ ७ ॥
3:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:7.explanation: The contrast with verse 6 is precise. The hypocrite controls the body but not the mind; the true karma-yogī first controls the mind ('manasā niyamya' — restraining through the mind) and then engages the body in righteous action. The sequence matters: inner before outer. 'Asaktaḥ' (without attachment) is the defining quality — action proceeds, but the actor is not bound to results. 'Viśiṣyate' (is distinguished, is superior) — this person excels not only over the hypocrite of verse 6 but over anyone who mistakes outward posture for genuine yoga. This verse is the practical definition of a karma-yogī.

3:8.text: Perform your prescribed duty (niyata karma); action is superior to inaction. Even the maintenance of your body cannot be accomplished without action.
3:8.transliteration:
niyataṃ kuru karma tvaṃ karma jyāyo hy akarmaṇaḥ |
śarīra-yātrāpi ca te na prasiddhyed akarmaṇaḥ ||8||
3:8.devanagari:
नियतं कुरु कर्म त्वं कर्म ज्यायो ह्यकर्मणः ।
शरीरयात्रापि च ते न प्रसिद्ध्येदकर्मणः ॥ ८ ॥
3:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:8.explanation: 'Niyataṃ karma' (prescribed, regulated duty) refers to action aligned with one's svabhāva (own nature) and dharma — for Arjuna, this is the duty of a warrior. 'Jyāyo hy akarmaṇaḥ' (superior to inaction) — Krishna offers a layered argument: philosophically, regulated action is superior to aimless inaction; practically, even biological survival requires continuous activity. 'Śarīra-yātrā' (the journey of the body, i.e., sustaining life) cannot happen without action — the most basic rebuttal to the idea of renouncing all activity. This verse marks the practical turning point: enough philosophy, now act.

3:9.text: This world is bound by action except when action is performed as sacrifice (yajña). O Kaunteya, perform action for that purpose (yajña), free from attachment.
3:9.transliteration:
yajñārthāt karmaṇo 'nyatra loko 'yaṃ karma-bandhanaḥ |
tad-arthaṃ karma kaunteya mukta-saṅgaḥ samācara ||9||
3:9.devanagari:
यज्ञार्थात्कर्मणोऽन्यत्र लोकोऽयं कर्मबन्धनः ।
तदर्थं कर्म कौन्तेय मुक्तसङ्गः समाचर ॥ ९ ॥
3:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:9.explanation: This verse introduces the yajña principle — the key that unlocks Karma-yoga. 'Yajñārthāt karmaṇaḥ' means action performed as an offering, as sacrifice, as a sacred act of service to a higher purpose. All other action — performed for personal gain, ego-satisfaction, or sense-gratification — binds the doer to further karma and further rebirth. 'Mukta-saṅgaḥ' (free from attachment) is the interior quality. Yajña here is not limited to Vedic fire-rituals; it encompasses any action performed as an offering to the divine, to society, or to the cosmic order — the spirit of giving rather than getting. This verse is the practical formula: purpose + non-attachment = liberation through action.

3:10.text: In ancient times, the Lord of Creatures (Prajāpati) created humankind together with sacrifice (yajña) and said: 'By this shall you prosper; let this be the fulfiller of all your desired wishes (iṣṭa-kāma-dhuk).'
3:10.transliteration:
saha-yajñāḥ prajāḥ sṛṣṭvā purovāca prajāpatiḥ |
anena prasaviṣyadhvam eṣa vo 'stv iṣṭa-kāma-dhuk ||10||
3:10.devanagari:
सहयज्ञाः प्रजाः सृष्ट्वा पुरोवाच प्रजापतिः ।
अनेन प्रसविष्यध्वमेष वोऽस्त्विष्टकामधुक् ॥ १० ॥
3:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:10.explanation: 'Prajāpati' (Lord of Creatures, Brahmā the creator) established the yajña principle at the very foundation of creation — it is a cosmic law, not a human convention. 'Saha-yajñāḥ prajāḥ sṛṣṭvā' (having created beings together with sacrifice) means that the spirit of giving, offering, and interdependence is built into the very structure of conscious existence. 'Iṣṭa-kāma-dhuk' (the fulfiller of all desired wishes, the wish-granting cow) — the promise is profound: those who live in the spirit of sacrifice find that their genuine needs are met through the cosmic reciprocity they participate in. This verse opens the Gita's cosmological understanding of karma.

3:11.text: Nourish the devas (divine powers) with this sacrifice; let those devas nourish you. Nourishing one another, you shall attain the highest good (paraṃ śreya).
3:11.transliteration:
devān bhāvayatānena te devā bhāvayantu vaḥ |
parasparaṃ bhāvayantaḥ śreyaḥ param avāpsyatha ||11||
3:11.devanagari:
देवान्भावयतानेन ते देवा भावयन्तु वः ।
परस्परं भावयन्तः श्रेयः परमवाप्स्यथ ॥ ११ ॥
3:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:11.explanation: 'Devān bhāvayata' — 'devas' here encompasses not only mythological deities but the cosmic powers that sustain natural order: rain, sun, earth, and all forces that make life possible. The yajña principle is an ecology of reciprocity: humans sustain the cosmic forces through gratitude and offering; the cosmic forces sustain humans through rain, food, and vitality. 'Parasparaṃ bhāvayantaḥ' (nourishing one another mutually) is the Gita's articulation of interdependence as a spiritual and natural law. Those who participate in this sacred reciprocity rather than only extracting from it attain the highest good — a phrase that points to both social and spiritual flourishing.

3:12.text: Nourished by sacrifice, the devas will indeed give you your desired enjoyments. One who enjoys their gifts without offering back to them is verily a thief.
3:12.transliteration:
iṣṭān bhogān hi vo devā dāsyante yajña-bhāvitāḥ |
tair dattān apradāyaibhyo yo bhuṅkte stena eva saḥ ||12||
3:12.devanagari:
इष्टान्भोगान्हि वो देवा दास्यन्ते यज्ञभाविताः ।
तैर्दत्तानप्रदायैभ्यो यो भुङ्क्ते स्तेन एव सः ॥ १२ ॥
3:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:12.explanation: 'Stena eva saḥ' (that person is truly a thief) — one of the Gita's most striking moral declarations. The logic is cosmic: all that humans enjoy — food, water, oxygen, social organization, knowledge — is received from natural and divine sources. To receive without giving back, to consume without participating in the cycle of reciprocity, is theft — not from another individual but from the cosmic order itself. The yajña principle thus encompasses ecology, economics, and ethics simultaneously. One who participates in this sacred exchange — offering back a portion of what is received — lives in alignment with the universe's foundational design.

3:13.text: The righteous, who eat the remnants of sacrifice (yajña-śiṣṭa), are freed from all sins. But those sinful ones who cook only for themselves — they eat sin alone.
3:13.transliteration:
yajña-śiṣṭāśinaḥ santo mucyante sarva-kilbiṣaiḥ |
bhuñjate te tv aghaṃ pāpā ye pacanty ātma-kāraṇāt ||13||
3:13.devanagari:
यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः सन्तो मुच्यन्ते सर्वकिल्बिषैः ।
भुञ्जते ते त्वघं पापा ये पचन्त्यात्मकारणात् ॥ १३ ॥
3:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:13.explanation: 'Yajña-śiṣṭāśinaḥ' (those who eat the remnants of sacrifice) — in ancient practice this meant eating only after offerings to gods, ancestors, guests, animals, and all beings were made. In its broader Gita meaning, it describes anyone who first gives — to family, society, nature — and lives on what remains after fulfilling these obligations. 'Ātma-kāraṇāt' (for oneself alone) — the purely self-centered life, which gives nothing back. 'Agham' (sin, accumulation of negative karma) — such a person literally consumes accumulating moral debt with every meal. The Gita here embeds an entire philosophy of social ethics within the metaphor of food.

3:14.text: Beings arise from food; food arises from rain; rain arises from sacrifice (yajña); sacrifice arises from action (karma).
3:14.transliteration:
annād bhavanti bhūtāni parjanyād anna-sambhavaḥ |
yajñād bhavati parjanyo yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ ||14||
3:14.devanagari:
अन्नाद्भवन्ति भूतानि पर्जन्यादन्नसम्भवः ।
यज्ञाद्भवति पर्जन्यो यज्ञः कर्मसमुद्भवः ॥ १४ ॥
3:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:14.explanation: This verse articulates the Gita's cosmic chain of interdependence: food (anna) sustains all beings; food depends on rain (parjanya); rain is produced by yajña — the sacred acts of offering that maintain the cosmic order; and yajña depends on karma — conscious, purposeful action. This is simultaneously an ecological truth and a spiritual law. The cycle shows that human purposeful action is not isolated from nature — it participates in sustaining the very conditions that make life possible. Those who withdraw from this cycle (acting only for themselves) break a cosmic link. This verse grounds the Karma-yoga in nothing less than the physics of existence.

3:15.text: Know that action (karma) arises from the Vedas (Brahman); and the Vedas arise from the Imperishable (Akṣara, the Absolute). Therefore, the all-pervading Brahman is eternally established in yajña.
3:15.transliteration:
karma brahmodbhavaṃ viddhi brahmākṣara-samudbhavam |
tasmāt sarva-gataṃ brahma nityaṃ yajñe pratiṣṭhitam ||15||
3:15.devanagari:
कर्म ब्रह्मोद्भवं विद्धि ब्रह्माक्षरसमुद्भवम् ।
तस्मात्सर्वगतं ब्रह्म नित्यं यज्ञे प्रतिष्ठितम् ॥ १५ ॥
3:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:15.explanation: The chain of 3.14 now reaches its metaphysical origin. 'Brahma' here refers to the Vedas — the cosmic knowledge that prescribes dharmic action. The Vedas themselves arise from 'Akṣara' — the Imperishable, the Absolute Reality. Thus the chain is: Absolute → Vedas → prescribed action → yajña → rain → food → life. Every link in this chain is sacred. 'Sarva-gataṃ brahma nityaṃ yajñe pratiṣṭhitam' — the all-pervading Brahman is eternally present in sacrifice. Every genuine act of yajña (offering, service, selfless action) is therefore a point of contact with the Infinite. This is the theological foundation of Karma-yoga.

3:16.text: O Pārtha, one who does not follow this wheel thus set in motion in this world — living for the senses, sinful in life — lives in vain (mogham).
3:16.transliteration:
evaṃ pravartitaṃ cakraṃ nānuvartayatīha yaḥ |
aghāyur indriyārāmo moghaṃ pārtha sa jīvati ||16||
3:16.devanagari:
एवं प्रवर्तितं चक्रं नानुवर्तयतीह यः ।
अघायुरिन्द्रियारामो मोघं पार्थ स जीवति ॥ १६ ॥
3:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:16.explanation: 'Evaṃ pravartitaṃ cakram' (the wheel thus set in motion) refers to the cosmic yajña-cycle expounded in the preceding verses. One who opts out of this cycle — living only for personal sense-pleasure ('indriyārāma' — one who delights only in the senses), accumulating karmic debt ('aghāyu' — one whose life is sin-laden) — 'moghaṃ jīvati' (lives in vain, lives fruitlessly). 'Mogha' is a powerful word: such a life leaves no positive trace in the world, contributes nothing to the cosmic order, and achieves no spiritual growth. It is a life that passes without having truly been lived.

3:17.text: But one who delights only in the ātman, who is satisfied only by the ātman, and who is content only in the ātman — for that person, there remains no duty to perform.
3:17.transliteration:
yas tv ātmaratir eva syād ātma-tṛptaś ca māṇavaḥ |
ātmany eva ca santuṣṭas tasya kāryaṃ na vidyate ||17||
3:17.devanagari:
यस्त्वात्मरतिरेव स्यादात्मतृप्तश्च मानवः ।
आत्मन्येव च सन्तुष्टस्तस्य कार्यं न विद्यते ॥ १७ ॥
3:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:17.explanation: This verse introduces the important exception to the duty of yajña-action. The fully self-realized person — 'ātmarati' (one whose delight is the Self alone), 'ātma-tṛpta' (satisfied by the Self alone), 'ātma-santuṣṭa' (content in the Self alone) — has transcended the framework of obligatory duty. For such a person, the cosmic wheel of exchange has been internalized completely: they are already one with the source of all. However, this state is extraordinarily rare and is not an excuse for ordinary people to abandon duty. Verses 18–20 address the practical implications of this exception.

3:18.text: For that person, there is no purpose served by action done, nor any harm from action undone. Nor does that person depend on any being for any purpose whatsoever.
3:18.transliteration:
naiva tasya kṛtenārtho nākṛteneha kaścana |
na cāsya sarva-bhūteṣu kaścid artha-vyapāśrayaḥ ||18||
3:18.devanagari:
नैव तस्य कृतेनार्थो नाकृतेनेह कश्चन ।
न चास्य सर्वभूतेषु कश्चिदर्थव्यपाश्रयः ॥ १८ ॥
3:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:18.explanation: Verse 18 continues the portrait of the fully realized person from verse 17. 'Kṛtena' (by doing) and 'akṛtena' (by not doing) — for the self-realized, neither action nor inaction produces personal benefit or harm, since they have no personal stake in outcomes. 'Artha-vyapāśrayaḥ' (dependence on anyone for any purpose) — this person is entirely self-sufficient in the deepest sense: not materially, but ontologically. They need nothing from the world because they are already complete in the ātman. This does not mean they are isolated — verse 20 shows that even such a person acts, but without need. Their action is pure gift.

3:19.text: Therefore, always perform what must be done (kāryaṃ karma), free from attachment. For a person who acts without attachment attains the Supreme.
3:19.transliteration:
tasmād asaktaḥ satataṃ kāryaṃ karma samācara |
asakto hy ācaran karma param āpnoti pūruṣaḥ ||19||
3:19.devanagari:
तस्मादसक्तः सततं कार्यं कर्म समाचर ।
असक्तो ह्याचरन्कर्म परमाप्नोति पूरुषः ॥ १९ ॥
3:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:19.explanation: 'Tasmāt' (therefore) draws the practical conclusion from all that has been established. 'Satatam' (always, continuously) — Karma-yoga is not an occasional practice but a continuous orientation of life. 'Kāryaṃ karma' (action that should be done, obligatory duty) — not every action, but one's dharmic responsibilities. 'Asaktaḥ' (unattached) is the operative word: the same action performed with attachment binds; performed without attachment, it liberates. 'Param āpnoti' (attains the Supreme) — liberation is not the reward of inaction but the fruit of right action rightly performed. This verse is the axiomatic statement of Karma-yoga.

3:20.text: Indeed, it was through action alone that Janaka and others attained perfection (saṃsiddhi). You should also perform action with a view to the welfare of the world (loka-saṃgraha).
3:20.transliteration:
karmaṇaiva hi saṃsiddhim āsthitā janakādayaḥ |
loka-saṃgraham evāpi sampaśyan kartum arhasi ||20||
3:20.devanagari:
कर्मणैव हि संसिद्धिमास्थिता जनकादयः ।
लोकसंग्रहमेवापि सम्पश्यन्कर्तुमर्हसि ॥ २० ॥
3:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:20.explanation: King Janaka of Videha is the Gita's paradigmatic karma-yogī — a monarch who governed an entire kingdom while remaining fully established in ātma-jñāna, attaining liberation through action rather than withdrawal. 'Karmaṇaiva hi saṃsiddhim' — by action alone, not despite action. 'Loka-saṃgraha' is one of the Gita's most important sociological concepts: the holding-together, the welfare and coherence of the world. A realized person of influence has a special obligation — their example shapes the values and behavior of those around them. Arjuna's action or inaction on the battlefield will influence generations. This is the dimension of duty that goes beyond the personal.

3:21.text: Whatever a great person does, others follow. Whatever standard (pramāṇa) that person sets, the world follows that.
3:21.transliteration:
yad yad ācarati śreṣṭhas tat tad evetaro janaḥ |
sa yat pramāṇaṃ kurute lokas tad anuvartate ||21||
3:21.devanagari:
यद्यदाचरति श्रेष्ठस्तत्तदेवेतरो जनः ।
स यत्प्रमाणं कुरुते लोकस्तदनुवर्तते ॥ २१ ॥
3:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:21.explanation: 'Śreṣṭhas' (the best person, the leader, the exemplar) — one who commands social influence by virtue of wisdom, power, or example. 'Yad yad ācarati... tat tad evetaro janaḥ' (whatever the great person does, that very thing others do) — this is the universal law of social modeling. Leaders shape culture through behavior, not merely through instruction. 'Pramāṇam' (standard, proof, criterion) — the great person's conduct becomes the measure by which others calibrate their own. For Arjuna — a celebrated warrior whose courage or cowardice would be witnessed by both armies — the social stakes of his action are enormous. This verse turns personal duty into social responsibility.

3:22.text: O Pārtha, there is nothing whatsoever that I need to do in the three worlds; nothing unattained that needs to be attained — yet I remain engaged in action.
3:22.transliteration:
na me pārthāsti kartavyaṃ triṣu lokeṣu kiñcana |
nānavāptam avāptavyaṃ varta eva ca karmaṇi ||22||
3:22.devanagari:
न मे पार्थास्ति कर्तव्यं त्रिषु लोकेषु किञ्चन ।
नानवाप्तमवाप्तव्यं वर्त एव च कर्मणि ॥ २२ ॥
3:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:22.explanation: Krishna now offers himself as the supreme exemplar of Karma-yoga. 'Na me... kartavyam' (nothing I must do) — Krishna is complete, self-sufficient, and desires nothing from the three worlds. 'Nānavāptam avāptavyam' (nothing unattained that needs attaining) — nothing is lacking. Yet 'varta eva ca karmaṇi' (yet I remain in action) — he acts continuously, not from need but from the overflowing of his nature and his care for the world. This is the purest demonstration of what the Gita means by desireless action: not reluctant duty-fulfillment, but joyful, purposeless (in the sense of need-free) engagement. The unconditioned acting for the conditioned — the Infinite sustaining the finite.

3:23.text: For, O Pārtha, if I were ever to remain without diligent action, all people would follow my path in every way.
3:23.transliteration:
yadi hy ahaṃ na varteyaṃ jātu karmaṇy atandritaḥ |
mama vartmānuvartante manuṣyāḥ pārtha sarvaśaḥ ||23||
3:23.devanagari:
यदि ह्यहं न वर्तेयं जातु कर्मण्यतन्द्रितः ।
मम वर्त्मानुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः पार्थ सर्वशः ॥ २३ ॥
3:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:23.explanation: Krishna applies the principle of verse 21 to himself — the supreme exemplar. 'Atandritaḥ' (diligent, without sloth) — even the divine acts with care and intention. If Krishna were to withdraw from action, all beings would follow his example and universal inaction would result — chaos, dissolution. 'Mama vartmānuvartante manuṣyāḥ pārtha sarvaśaḥ' (all people follow my path in every direction) — the responsibility of the highest is proportional to their influence. For Krishna, this means sustaining the cosmos; for Arjuna, this means leading by the example of dharmic courage. The burden of greatness is the obligation to keep acting.

3:24.text: If I were not to perform action, these worlds would perish. I would be the author of confusion (saṃkara) and would destroy these beings.
3:24.transliteration:
utsīdeyur ime lokā na kuryāṃ karma ced aham |
saṃkarasya ca kartā syām upahanyām imāḥ prajāḥ ||24||
3:24.devanagari:
उत्सीदेयुरिमे लोका न कुर्यां कर्म चेदहम् ।
सङ्करस्य च कर्ता स्यामुपहन्यामिमाः प्रजाः ॥ २४ ॥
3:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:24.explanation: 'Utsīdeyur ime lokā' (these worlds would collapse) — Krishna's statement carries cosmic weight: the divine, by acting, sustains the fabric of existence. 'Saṃkarasya ca kartā syām' (I would be the maker of confusion) — this echoes Arjuna's own fear from Chapter 1 (1.41–43) where he warned of social chaos from the war. Here Krishna inverts the argument precisely: it is not righteous action (dharmic war) that creates chaos, but the abdication of duty. Inaction in the face of adharma is itself the most dangerous form of confusion. The very catastrophe Arjuna feared from fighting is what would result from not fighting.

3:25.text: O Bhārata, just as the ignorant act with attachment to action, so should the wise act without attachment, desiring the welfare of the world (loka-saṃgraha).
3:25.transliteration:
saktāḥ karmaṇy avidvāṃso yathā kurvanti bhārata |
kuryād vidvāṃs tathāsaktaś cikīrṣur loka-saṃgraham ||25||
3:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:25.explanation: This verse defines the karma-yogī's outer similarity and inner difference from the ordinary actor. 'Saktāḥ... avidvāṃsaḥ' (the ignorant, acting with attachment) — they perform the same actions but bound by the fruit of action, driven by craving. 'Vidvāṃs tathāsaktaḥ' (the wise, similarly but without attachment) — externally the behavior may be identical, but the interior is transformed. 'Cikīrṣur loka-saṃgraham' (desiring to hold the world together) — the wise act from a motive that transcends the personal: social cohesion, the welfare of all beings. The same action, two entirely different ontological positions.

3:26.text: Let no wise person disturb the minds of the ignorant who are attached to action. Acting in a spirit of yoga, the wise one should inspire them toward all their actions (joṣayet).
3:26.transliteration:
na buddhi-bhedaṃ janayed ajñānāṃ karma-saṅginām |
joṣayet sarva-karmāṇi vidvān yuktaḥ samācaran ||26||
3:26.devanagari:
न बुद्धिभेदं जनयेदज्ञानां कर्मसङ्गिनाम् ।
जोषयेत्सर्वकर्माणि विद्वान्युक्तः समाचरन् ॥ २६ ॥
3:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:26.explanation: 'Buddhi-bhedaṃ na janayet' (should not create confusion or division in the intellect) — a profound pedagogical principle. If a realized person suddenly tells the action-oriented masses that 'all is Brahman and action is ultimately unreal,' those people — not yet ready for that realization — may abandon their duties without gaining wisdom, falling into a spiritual no-man's land. 'Joṣayet sarva-karmāṇi' (let them find delight in all their actions) — instead, the wise person teaches through example, inspiring others to perform their duties with greater care, devotion, and enthusiasm. The highest teaching is demonstration, not disruption.

3:27.text: All actions are performed in every way by the guṇas of prakṛti (nature). The self deluded by ego thinks: 'I am the doer.'
3:27.transliteration:
prakṛteḥ kriyamāṇāni guṇaiḥ karmāṇi sarvaśaḥ |
ahaṃkāra-vimūḍhātmā kartāham iti manyate ||27||
3:27.devanagari:
प्रकृतेः क्रियमाणानि गुणैः कर्माणि सर्वशः ।
अहंकारविमूढात्मा कर्ताहमिति मन्यते ॥ २७ ॥
3:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:27.explanation: 'Prakṛteḥ kriyamāṇāni guṇaiḥ karmāṇi sarvaśaḥ' — this is the metaphysical bedrock beneath Karma-yoga. Body, mind, senses, intellect — all belong to prakṛti (nature), animated by its three guṇas. Actions arise from this complex natural machinery, not from the pure ātman. 'Ahaṃkāra-vimūḍhātmā' (the self bewildered by ego) — the ego is the instrument of this confusion: it superimposes 'I' onto the process of nature, claiming authorship of what is actually the guṇas acting upon the guṇas. 'Kartāham iti manyate' (thinks 'I am the doer') — this mistaken identification is the root of karmic bondage. When this illusion dissolves, action continues but bondage ends.

3:28.text: But one who knows the truth about the division of guṇas and karma, O mighty-armed, understanding that 'guṇas act upon guṇas,' is not attached.
3:28.transliteration:
tattva-vit tu mahābāho guṇa-karma-vibhāgayoḥ |
guṇā guṇeṣu vartanta iti matvā na sajjate ||28||
3:28.devanagari:
तत्त्ववित्तु महाबाहो गुणकर्मविभागयोः ।
गुणा गुणेषु वर्तन्त इति मत्वा न सज्जते ॥ २८ ॥
3:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:28.explanation: 'Tattva-vit' (the knower of truth) understands what the confused person of verse 27 does not: that all action is the interplay of nature's three guṇas upon one another — senses (guṇas) acting on sense-objects (guṇas), mind (guṇa) processing experience (guṇas). The ātman, the pure witnessing consciousness, is not the doer. 'Guṇā guṇeṣu vartante' (guṇas act upon guṇas) — this is the liberating insight. When a person knows this, they do not claim authorship of actions, do not cling to results, and therefore 'na sajjate' (is not attached, not stuck). Wisdom detaches action from ego and thereby detaches action from bondage.

3:29.text: Those deluded by the guṇas of prakṛti become attached to the functions of the guṇas. The person of complete knowledge (kṛtsna-vit) should not unsettle those of incomplete knowledge (akṛtsna-vidas) and slow understanding.
3:29.transliteration:
prakṛter guṇa-sammūḍhāḥ sajjante guṇa-karmasu |
tān akṛtsna-vido mandān kṛtsna-vin na vicālayet ||29||
3:29.devanagari:
प्रकृतेर्गुणसम्मूढाः सज्जन्ते गुणकर्मसु ।
तानकृत्स्नविदो मन्दान्कृत्स्नविन्न विचालयेत् ॥ २९ ॥
3:29.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:29.explanation: 'Akṛtsna-vidas' (those of incomplete knowledge) — the majority of people who are still identified with the ego-actor and have not yet seen through the guṇa-mechanism. Suddenly confronting them with 'you are not the doer; all is guṇas acting on guṇas' may intellectually destabilize them without giving them the inner foundation to stand on the new understanding. 'Kṛtsna-vin na vicālayet' (the fully knowing one should not unsettle them) — wisdom includes the wisdom of timing and calibration. This verse and 3.26 together constitute the Gita's teaching on pedagogical compassion: meet people where they are, lead by example, and elevate gradually.

3:30.text: Renouncing all actions in Me, with the mind fixed on the ātman, free from hope (nirāśī), free from the sense of 'mine' (nirmama), and free from mental fever (vigata-jvara) — fight.
3:30.transliteration:
mayi sarvāṇi karmāṇi saṃnyasyādhyātma-cetasā |
nirāśīr nirmamo bhūtvā yudhyasva vigata-jvaraḥ ||30||
3:30.devanagari:
मयि सर्वाणि कर्माणि संन्यस्याध्यात्मचेतसा ।
निराशीर्निर्ममो भूत्वा युध्यस्व विगतज्वरः ॥ ३० ॥
3:30.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:30.explanation: 'Mayi sarvāṇi karmāṇi saṃnyasya' (renouncing all actions in Me) — this is the pivot to bhakti within karma-yoga: surrender of every action as an offering to the divine. 'Adhyātma-cetasā' (with ātman-oriented consciousness) — acting from a place of self-knowledge, not ego. 'Nirāśīḥ' (without craving for results), 'nirmamaḥ' (without 'mine-ness'), 'vigata-jvaraḥ' (free from the fever of anxiety and grief) — three complete inner freedoms. 'Yudhyasva' (fight) — the simple, final instruction. All the philosophy of this chapter collapses into this one word: act, now, in this liberated manner. The path and the destination are one.

3:31.text: Those persons who constantly follow this teaching of Mine with faith and without caviling — they too are freed from the bondage of karma.
3:31.transliteration:
ye me matam idaṃ nityam anutiṣṭhanti mānavāḥ |
śraddhāvanto 'nasūyanto mucyante te 'pi karmabhiḥ ||31||
3:31.devanagari:
ये मे मतमिदं नित्यमनुतिष्ठन्ति मानवाः ।
श्रद्धावन्तोऽनसूयन्तो मुच्यन्ते तेऽपि कर्मभिः ॥ ३१ ॥
3:31.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:31.explanation: 'Nityam anutiṣṭhanti' (constantly practice, regularly follow) — not occasional compliance but sustained, habitual orientation. 'Śraddhāvantaḥ' (those endowed with faith) — śraddhā is not blind belief but the confident, receptive openness to truth that allows teaching to be absorbed and practiced. 'Anasūyantaḥ' (without fault-finding, without envy) — those who approach the teaching without the defensive reflex to criticize and dismiss. 'Mucyante te 'pi' (they too are freed) — 'api' (even/also) is significant: even those who follow this path without perfect understanding but with faith and consistency are liberated from karma's binding force.

3:32.text: But those who cavil at this teaching and do not follow My instruction — know them to be deluded in all knowledge, mindless, and lost.
3:32.transliteration:
ye tv etad abhyasūyanto nānutiṣṭhanti me matam |
sarva-jñāna-vimūḍhāṃs tān viddhi naṣṭān acetasaḥ ||32||
3:32.devanagari:
ये त्वेतदभ्यसूयन्तो नानुतिष्ठन्ति मे मतम् ।
सर्वज्ञानविमूढांस्तान्विद्धि नष्टानचेतसः ॥ ३२ ॥
3:32.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:32.explanation: 'Abhyasūyantaḥ' (those who find fault, who cavil, who resist with a critical, disparaging attitude) — the contrast to the faithful śraddhāvat of verse 31. 'Sarva-jñāna-vimūḍhān' (deluded in all knowledge) — such resistance is not a sophisticated intellectual position but a symptom of deep confusion that pervades all of their understanding, not just this teaching. 'Naṣṭān acetasaḥ' (lost, mindless, without true consciousness) — strong language indicating that those who reject this path with contempt cut themselves off from the very understanding that would liberate them. The word 'viddhi' (know, understand) addresses Arjuna as the recipient: this is for his discernment.

3:33.text: Even a person of knowledge acts in accordance with their own nature. All beings follow their nature — what will restraint (nigraha) accomplish?
3:33.transliteration:
sadṛśaṃ ceṣṭate svasyāḥ prakṛter jñānavān api |
prakṛtiṃ yānti bhūtāni nigrahaḥ kiṃ kariṣyati ||33||
3:33.devanagari:
सदृशं चेष्टते स्वस्याः प्रकृतेर्ज्ञानवानपि ।
प्रकृतिं यान्ति भूतानि निग्रहः किं करिष्यति ॥ ३३ ॥
3:33.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:33.explanation: This verse is sometimes misread as a counsel of fatalism. It is not. 'Jñānavān api' (even the person of wisdom) — even the realized person acts from their nature, but their nature has been purified; their svabhāva has been aligned with dharma and wisdom. 'Nigrahaḥ kiṃ kariṣyati' (what will mere restraint accomplish?) — external suppression without inner transformation is ineffective. A man who violently suppresses his anger without understanding it will be controlled by it. The Gita's path is transformation of nature through yoga, not the forcible suppression of natural impulses. Act from your highest nature — that is svadharma.

3:34.text: In the objects of each sense, attraction (rāga) and aversion (dveṣa) are established. One should not come under the sway of these two, for they are one's enemies on the path.
3:34.transliteration:
indriyasyendriyasyārthe rāga-dveṣau vyavasthitau |
tayor na vaśam āgacchet tau hy asya paripanthinau ||34||
3:34.devanagari:
इन्द्रियस्येन्द्रियस्यार्थे रागद्वेषौ व्यवस्थितौ ।
तयोर्न वशमागच्छेत्तौ ह्यस्य परिपन्थिनौ ॥ ३४ ॥
3:34.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:34.explanation: 'Rāga-dveṣau' (attraction and aversion) are the twin forces that drive the unexamined life: we pursue what we like and flee what we dislike, with no reference to wisdom or dharma. These are embedded in the very structure of sensory experience — every sight, sound, taste, and touch carries a valence of like or dislike. 'Vyavasthitau' (firmly established, situated) — they are not occasional intrusions but constant companions of sense-experience. 'Paripanthinau' (blocking the path, adversaries on the road) — they obstruct spiritual progress by enslaving action to impulse rather than wisdom. The teaching: know they exist, do not suppress them violently, but do not follow them blindly either.

3:35.text: One's own dharma, though imperfectly performed, is superior to the well-performed dharma of another. Death in one's own dharma is better; the dharma of another is fraught with danger.
3:35.transliteration:
śreyān sva-dharmo viguṇaḥ para-dharmāt sv-anuṣṭhitāt |
sva-dharme nidhanaṃ śreyaḥ para-dharmo bhayāvahaḥ ||35||
3:35.devanagari:
श्रेयान्स्वधर्मो विगुणः परधर्मात्स्वनुष्ठितात् ।
स्वधर्मे निधनं श्रेयः परधर्मो भयावहः ॥ ३५ ॥
3:35.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:35.explanation: This verse is so central to the Gita's message that it is repeated almost verbatim at 18.47. 'Sva-dharma' (one's own dharma) is not merely caste-duty in the narrow social sense — it is the total complex of one's nature, calling, capacity, stage of life, and social role. 'Para-dharma' (another's dharma) is what looks impressive from the outside but is not one's own genuine path. 'Viguṇaḥ' (deficient, imperfect) — even a stumbling walk on one's own path exceeds a graceful walk on someone else's. 'Sva-dharme nidhanaṃ śreyaḥ' — death while fulfilling one's own calling is liberation; abandoning that calling for apparent safety is the real danger. For Arjuna: fight.

3:36.text: Arjuna said: O Vārṣṇeya, then by what is a person compelled — even against their will, as if driven by force — to commit sin?
3:36.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
atha kena prayukto 'yaṃ pāpaṃ carati pūruṣaḥ |
anicchann api vārṣṇeya balād iva niyojitaḥ ||36||
3:36.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
अथ केन प्रयुक्तोऽयं पापं चरति पूरुषः ।
अनिच्छन्नपि वार्ष्णेय बलादिव नियोजितः ॥ ३६ ॥
3:36.speaker: Arjuna
3:36.explanation: This is one of the profoundest questions in the Gita — and in all of moral philosophy. Arjuna articulates the universal human predicament: the experience of being compelled to do what one knows to be wrong. 'Anicchann api' (even though not wanting to) — the person has moral awareness; 'balād iva niyojitaḥ' (as if forcibly recruited, compelled by force) — yet they act as if under compulsion. This is the phenomenology of temptation, addiction, rage, and impulse. The Gita's answer — given in the next verse and elaborated through the chapter's close — names the force: kāma (desire-driven craving), born of rajas, and its offspring, anger.

3:37.text: The Blessed Lord said: It is desire — it is anger — born of the quality of rajas. It is all-devouring, greatly sinful. Know this as the enemy here.
3:37.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
kāma eṣa krodha eṣa rajo-guṇa-samudbhavaḥ |
mahāśano mahā-pāpmā viddhy enam iha vairiṇam ||37||
3:37.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
काम एष क्रोध एष रजोगुणसमुद्भवः ।
महाशनो महापाप्मा विद्ध्येनमिह वैरिणम् ॥ ३७ ॥
3:37.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
