3:37.explanation: 'Kāma eṣa krodha eṣa' — Krishna names the enemy with precision: kāma (desire, craving, all forms of wanting what one does not have) is the root force; krodha (anger) is its immediate offspring when kāma is frustrated. Both arise from rajas — the guṇa of activity, passion, and agitation. 'Mahāśanaḥ' (all-devouring, with a huge appetite) — kāma is insatiable; satisfying it feeds it rather than reducing it, like pouring ghee on fire. 'Mahā-pāpmā' (greatly sinful, the great transgressor) — it is the cause behind virtually all moral failure. 'Vairiṇam' (the enemy) — singular, clear, named: desire is the inner enemy, the only one that truly matters.

3:38.text: Just as fire is covered by smoke, a mirror by dust, and an embryo by the womb's membrane (ulba) — so is this (wisdom) covered by that (desire).
3:38.transliteration:
dhūmenāvriyate vahnir yathādarśo malena ca |
yatholbenāvṛto garbhas tathā tenedam āvṛtam ||38||
3:38.devanagari:
धूमेनाव्रियते वह्निर्यथादर्शो मलेन च ।
यथोल्बेनावृतो गर्भस्तथा तेनेदमावृतम् ॥ ३८ ॥
3:38.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:38.explanation: A tripartite simile of increasing obscuration, traditionally understood as corresponding to the three guṇas. Fire covered by smoke (sāttvika — easily dispelled; fire and light are still visible); a mirror coated with dust (rājasika — moderate covering; the mirror exists but reflects nothing until cleaned); an embryo enclosed in the amnion (tāmasika — total concealment; no external sign of what lies within). These three represent the degrees to which desire can cover wisdom: light veiling (easily removed by practice), moderate veiling (requiring sustained effort), and total veiling (requiring profound transformation). All three have the same structure: the luminous reality (fire, mirror's reflective surface, the living child) is present but hidden. So too wisdom (jñāna) is never destroyed by desire — only obscured.

3:39.text: O Kaunteya, the wisdom of the wise is covered by this eternal enemy — in the form of desire, which is like an insatiable fire.
3:39.transliteration:
āvṛtaṃ jñānam etena jñānino nitya-vairiṇā |
kāma-rūpeṇa kaunteya duṣpūreṇānalena ca ||39||
3:39.devanagari:
आवृतं ज्ञानमेतेन ज्ञानिनो नित्यवैरिणा ।
कामरूपेण कौन्तेय दुष्पूरेणानलेन च ॥ ३९ ॥
3:39.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:39.explanation: 'Nitya-vairiṇā' (eternal enemy) — kāma is not a temporary adversary defeated once and done; it is a chronic condition that requires constant vigilance. 'Jñānino' (of the wise, of the knower) — even people of significant understanding are not immune; desire can veil wisdom in even advanced practitioners, making this an 'eternal enemy' rather than one vanquished at a particular stage. 'Kāma-rūpeṇa' (in the form of desire) — desire's nature is protean; it takes infinite forms and enters through every sense and thought. 'Duṣpūreṇānalena' (like an insatiable fire, impossible to fill) — fire fed by fuel does not diminish; it grows. So too desire: feeding it amplifies it.

3:40.text: The senses, mind, and intellect are said to be the dwelling places of this (desire). Through these, it deludes the embodied being (dehin) by veiling its wisdom.
3:40.transliteration:
indriyāṇi mano buddhir asyādhiṣṭhānam ucyate |
etair vimohayaty eṣa jñānam āvṛtya dehinam ||40||
3:40.devanagari:
इन्द्रियाणि मनो बुद्धिरस्याधिष्ठानमुच्यते ।
एतैर्विमोहयत्येष ज्ञानमावृत्य देहिनम् ॥ ४० ॥
3:40.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:40.explanation: 'Indriyāṇi mano buddhiḥ' — desire operates across three tiers of the human instrument. At the grossest level: senses (indriyāṇi) — desire hooks into sensory experience directly. At the intermediate level: mind (manas) — desire generates fantasies, memories, and longings that keep consciousness engaged with objects. At the subtlest and most dangerous level: intellect (buddhi) — desire corrupts the discriminative faculty itself, producing rationalizations that make wrong action appear right. 'Etair vimohayaty eṣa jñānam āvṛtya' (through these it deludes, having covered wisdom) — when desire reaches the intellect, the person's very capacity for discernment is compromised. This is precisely Arjuna's condition in Chapter 1.

3:41.text: Therefore, O best of the Bharatas, first restraining the senses, slay this sinful destroyer of knowledge (jñāna) and wisdom (vijñāna).
3:41.transliteration:
tasmāt tvam indriyāṇy ādau niyamya bharatarṣabha |
pāpmānaṃ prajahi hy enaṃ jñāna-vijñāna-nāśanam ||41||
3:41.devanagari:
तस्मात्त्वमिन्द्रियाण्यादौ नियम्य भरतर्षभ ।
पाप्मानं प्रजहि ह्येनं ज्ञानविज्ञाननाशनम् ॥ ४१ ॥
3:41.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:41.explanation: 'Ādau' (first, at the outset) — the sequence is deliberate: start with the grossest level (sense-restraint) and work inward. 'Bharat-arṣabha' (bull of the Bharatas, best of the Bharata lineage) — a title of power and dignity, reminding Arjuna that he has the capacity for this inner battle. 'Jñāna-vijñāna-nāśanam' — 'jñāna' is theoretical knowledge of the scriptures and the Self; 'vijñāna' is the direct experiential realization of that truth. Desire destroys both: it corrupts the conceptual map and destroys the possibility of direct experience. 'Prajahi' (slay, destroy completely) — the command for inner war, more important than any outer battle.

3:42.text: The senses are said to be superior (to the body); superior to the senses is the mind; superior to the mind is the intellect; and that which is superior even to the intellect — that is the Self (ātman).
3:42.transliteration:
indriyāṇi parāṇy āhur indriyebhyaḥ paraṃ manaḥ |
manasas tu parā buddhir yo buddheḥ paratas tu saḥ ||42||
3:42.devanagari:
इन्द्रियाणि पराण्याहुरिन्द्रियेभ्यः परं मनः ।
मनसस्तु परा बुद्धिर्यो बुद्धेः परतस्तु सः ॥ ४२ ॥
3:42.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:42.explanation: This verse presents the Sāṃkhya hierarchy of human faculties as a ladder of sublimation. Each level controls the one below it: the senses (indriyāṇi) direct the body; the mind (manas) coordinates the senses; the intellect (buddhi) discriminates and guides the mind; and the ātman is the witnessing Self that illuminates them all. 'Yaḥ buddheḥ parataḥ sa' (that which is beyond even the intellect — that) — the deliberate vagueness ('sa,' 'that') points to the ātman which cannot be fully named or objectified, since it is the subject of all experience. This hierarchy is the map for the final verse's instruction.

3:43.text: O mighty-armed one, thus knowing that which is beyond even the intellect (the ātman), steadying the self by the Self, slay the enemy in the form of desire — difficult to overcome.
3:43.transliteration:
evaṃ buddheḥ paraṃ buddhvā saṃstabhyātmānam ātmanā |
jahi śatruṃ mahābāho kāma-rūpaṃ durāsadam ||43||
3:43.devanagari:
एवं बुद्धेः परं बुद्ध्वा संस्तभ्यात्मानमात्मना ।
जहि शत्रुं महाबाहो कामरूपं दुरासदम् ॥ ४३ ॥
3:43.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
3:43.explanation: The chapter closes with a command that recapitulates everything. 'Buddheḥ paraṃ buddhvā' (having known what is beyond the intellect) — the foundational act of self-knowledge: recognizing the ātman as the witness-Self beyond all the instruments of knowing. 'Saṃstabhyātmānam ātmanā' (steadying the self by the Self) — using the higher Self (ātman) to stabilize and ground the lower self (the ego-complex of body-mind). This is the subtlest form of discipline: not the senses restraining themselves, not even the intellect asserting itself over the senses, but the recognition of the witnessing ātman that makes all inner transformation possible. 'Durāsadam' (difficult to overcome, hard to approach) — desire is formidable; but the Self that knows itself is more powerful still. 'Jahi' (slay) — the climactic war-cry of Chapter 3: the real enemy is within, the real weapon is self-knowledge, and the real battlefield is the mind.

4:1.text: The Blessed Lord said: I taught this imperishable yoga to Vivasvān (the Sun-god); Vivasvān told it to Manu; and Manu declared it to Ikṣvāku.
4:1.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
imaṃ vivasvate yogaṃ proktavān aham avyayam |
vivasvān manave prāha manur ikṣvākave 'bravīt ||1||
4:1.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
इमं विवस्वते योगं प्रोक्तवानहमव्ययम् ।
विवस्वान्मनवे प्राह मनुरिक्ष्वाकवेऽब्रवीत् ॥ १ ॥
4:1.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:1.explanation: 'Avyayam' (imperishable, eternal) — this yoga is not bound by time or circumstance; it is sanātana (eternal). Krishna's startling declaration — that he personally imparted this teaching to the Sun-god at the dawn of creation — is not mythology but a statement about the metaphysical source of all wisdom. The lineage Vivasvān → Manu → Ikṣvāku establishes a guru-paramparā (teacher-disciple succession) that grounds this yoga in the most ancient Vedic tradition. Ikṣvāku was the founding king of the Solar Dynasty from which Lord Rāma descended — making this teaching the foundation of dharmic kingship itself. Krishna signals to Arjuna that what he is receiving is not novel philosophy but the eternal science of action that has sustained civilization from the beginning.

4:2.text: In this manner, transmitted through an unbroken succession, the royal sages knew this yoga. But through the passage of a long period of time, O Parantapa, this yoga became lost in this world.
4:2.transliteration:
evaṃ paramparā-prāptam imaṃ rājarṣayo viduḥ |
sa kāleneha mahatā yogo naṣṭaḥ paraṃtapa ||2||
4:2.devanagari:
एवं परम्पराप्राप्तमिमं राजर्षयो विदुः ।
स कालेनेह महता योगो नष्टः परन्तप ॥ २ ॥
4:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:2.explanation: 'Rājarṣayaḥ' (royal sages) represents the ideal integration of wisdom and governance — rulers who were simultaneously masters of spiritual knowledge. Their understanding of this yoga enabled them to govern with dharma. 'Sa kālena mahatā naṣṭaḥ' — the yoga was lost not through any single act of negligence but through the gradual corrosion of time (mahā-kāla). This is the Gita's explanation for why scripture must be periodically renewed: transmission without realized understanding becomes mere repetition, and the living essence fades. The use of 'Paraṃtapa' (scorcher of enemies) subtly reminds Arjuna that this teaching belongs to warriors who can also be contemplatives — men of action who know the deeper truth.

4:3.text: That same ancient yoga has been declared by me to you today; for you are my devotee and my friend — and this is indeed the supreme secret.
4:3.transliteration:
sa evāyaṃ mayā te 'dya yogaḥ proktaḥ purātanaḥ |
bhakto 'si me sakhā ceti rahasyaṃ hy etad uttamam ||3||
4:3.devanagari:
स एवायं मया तेऽद्य योगः प्रोक्तः पुरातनः ।
भक्तोऽसि मे सखा चेति रहस्यं ह्येतदुत्तमम् ॥ ३ ॥
4:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:3.explanation: 'Purātanaḥ' (most ancient) — the yoga being imparted is not Krishna's invention but the eternal truth now freshly re-articulated. The declaration 'bhakto 'si me sakhā ca' (you are my devotee and my friend) is among the Gita's most intimate statements. It identifies the two qualifications for receiving the highest teaching: bhakti (devotion, surrender, openness of heart) and sakhā (friendship, equality of relationship, mutual trust). Without devotion, the teaching remains intellectual information; without friendship, it cannot be freely shared. 'Rahasyam uttamam' (supreme secret) — this is not secret in the sense of being withheld, but secret in the sense that only those with the right inner disposition can truly receive and live it.

4:4.text: Arjuna said: Your birth is recent, and the birth of Vivasvān was in ancient times — how then am I to understand that you declared this yoga to him in the beginning?
4:4.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
aparaṃ bhavato janma paraṃ janma vivasvataḥ |
katham etad vijānīyāṃ tvam ādau proktavān iti ||4||
4:4.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
अपरं भवतो जन्म परं जन्म विवस्वतः ।
कथमेतद्विजानीयां त्वमादौ प्रोक्तवानिति ॥ ४ ॥
4:4.speaker: Arjuna
4:4.explanation: Arjuna's question is a logically rigorous challenge, and it is the most important question in this chapter. He is applying ordinary human epistemology to what Krishna has just said: from a historical standpoint, the Sun-god Vivasvān precedes Krishna's birth by vast cosmic ages. How could a contemporary human being have taught an ancient god? 'Aparam bhavataḥ janma' (your birth is recent) and 'paraṃ janma vivasvataḥ' (the birth of Vivasvān is ancient) frame a genuine paradox. This question is the essential setup for the avatāra doctrine — Krishna's revelation in verses 5–9 of his transcendence over ordinary birth and death. Without this question, one of the Gita's greatest metaphysical passages would lack its context.

4:5.text: The Blessed Lord said: Many births of mine have passed, as well as yours, O Arjuna. I know them all, but you do not know them, O Parantapa.
4:5.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
bahūni me vyatītāni janmāni tava cārjuna |
tāny ahaṃ veda sarvāṇi na tvaṃ vettha paraṃtapa ||5||
4:5.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
बहूनि मे व्यतीतानि जन्मानि तव चार्जुन ।
तान्यहं वेद सर्वाणि न त्वं वेत्थ परन्तप ॥ ५ ॥
4:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:5.explanation: This verse simultaneously confirms two foundational truths: (1) Arjuna, like all embodied beings, has passed through innumerable past births — the doctrine of rebirth is here stated as matter-of-fact. (2) Krishna's relationship to his births is categorically different from Arjuna's. 'Tāny ahaṃ veda sarvāṇi' (I know all of them) — Krishna's consciousness is not bounded by any particular body or lifetime; he is the eternal witness-consciousness. 'Na tvaṃ vettha' (you do not know) — Arjuna's awareness is veiled by māyā and deha-abhimāna (identification with the body). This asymmetry between the all-knowing Lord and the forgetful embodied soul is the essential difference between Īśvara and jīva — and is the ground for the avatāra teaching that follows.

4:6.text: Although I am unborn and my Self is imperishable, although I am the Lord of all beings — yet by presiding over my own prakṛti, I manifest through my own māyā (yoga-śakti).
4:6.transliteration:
ajo 'pi sann avyayātmā bhūtānām īśvaro 'pi san |
prakṛtiṃ svām adhiṣṭhāya sambhavāmy ātma-māyayā ||6||
4:6.devanagari:
अजोऽपि सन्नव्ययात्मा भूतानामीश्वरोऽपि सन् ।
प्रकृतिं स्वामधिष्ठाय सम्भवाम्यात्ममायया ॥ ६ ॥
4:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:6.explanation: This is the philosophical cornerstone of the avatāra doctrine. Three attributes define the Lord's transcendence: (1) 'Ajaḥ' (unborn) — not subject to the ordinary cycle of birth, which is a consequence of karma; (2) 'avyayātmā' (imperishable Self) — the body assumed may be finite, but the consciousness inhabiting it is not diminished or altered; (3) 'bhūtānām Īśvaraḥ' (Lord of all beings) — the one in whom all beings inhere. 'Ātma-māyayā' (through his own māyā) is crucial: the Lord's māyā is Yoga-māyā — not the binding, obscuring māyā that veils the jīva, but the free, sovereign creative power by which the Infinite willingly takes finite form. The avatāra is not compelled by karma; it is a sovereign act of divine freedom and grace.

4:7.text: Whenever there is a decline of dharma, O Bhārata, and a rise of adharma — then I project myself forth.
4:7.transliteration:
yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata |
abhyutthānam adharmasya tadātmānaṃ sṛjāmy aham ||7||
4:7.devanagari:
यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत ।
अभ्युत्थानमधर्मस्य तदात्मानं सृजाम्यहम् ॥ ७ ॥
4:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:7.explanation: 'Yadā yadā' (whenever, as often as) — this is not a prophecy about a single historical event but a statement of cosmic law. 'Dharmasya glāniḥ' (decline of dharma) — glāni implies not mere violation but systemic erosion: when the very fabric of righteous order loses vitality, when dharma becomes weakened in the hearts of people. 'Abhyutthānam adharmasya' (rise of adharma) — adharma rising means the cosmic imbalance has tipped past a critical threshold. 'Tadātmānaṃ sṛjāmy aham' — the word 'sṛjāmi' (I create, I project) rather than 'jāyate' (I am born) is philosophically precise: the Lord does not undergo birth as a compelled event; he actively projects a form through sovereign creative will. This verse, paired with the next, constitutes the Gita's definitive statement on avatāra.

4:8.text: For the protection of the good, for the destruction of the wicked, and for the firm establishment of righteousness — I come into being age after age.
4:8.transliteration:
paritrāṇāya sādhūnāṃ vināśāya ca duṣkṛtām |
dharma-saṃsthāpanārthāya sambhavāmi yuge yuge ||8||
4:8.devanagari:
परित्राणाय साधूनां विनाशाय च दुष्कृताम् ।
धर्मसंस्थापनार्थाय सम्भवामि युगे युगे ॥ ८ ॥
4:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:8.explanation: This verse is the Gita's most famous declaration, and its three-fold purpose is both comprehensive and balanced. (1) 'Paritrāṇāya sādhūnām' (for the protection of the good) — 'sādhu' refers not merely to monks but to all those who live in accordance with dharma and truth; their protection is the first positive duty of the divine descent. (2) 'Vināśāya ca duṣkṛtām' (for the destruction of the wicked) — not punishment for its own sake, but the removal of forces that systematically destroy dharmic order. (3) 'Dharma-saṃsthāpanārthāya' (for the re-establishment of dharma) — this is the supreme constructive purpose: not merely to protect and destroy, but to re-found dharmic civilization. 'Yuge yuge' (age after age) — this is not a one-time event but the rhythm of cosmic history.

4:9.text: O Arjuna, one who knows my birth and action as thus divine — having left the body, he is not reborn; he comes to me.
4:9.transliteration:
janma karma ca me divyam evaṃ yo vetti tattvataḥ |
tyaktvā dehaṃ punar janma naiti mām eti so 'rjuna ||9||
4:9.devanagari:
जन्म कर्म च मे दिव्यमेवं यो वेत्ति तत्त्वतः ।
त्यक्त्वा देहं पुनर्जन्म नैति मामेति सोऽर्जुन ॥ ९ ॥
4:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:9.explanation: 'Divyam' (divine, transcendental) — not in a vague devotional sense but in precise philosophical terms: the Lord's birth is not driven by karma, not constrained by ignorance, not subject to ordinary causal chains. 'Tattvataḥ' (in truth, in essence) — not mere intellectual acknowledgment but realized understanding, as when the knower becomes identical with the known. The soteriological consequence is direct: 'punar janma naiti' (he is not reborn) — freedom from the cycle of saṃsāra. 'Mām eti' (he comes to me) — the positive formulation of liberation as divine union, not mere cessation. This verse is the Gita's earliest explicit statement that jñāna (knowledge of the Lord's nature) is itself a path to mokṣa.

4:10.text: Freed from desire, fear, and anger, absorbed in me, taking refuge in me — many have been purified by the tapas of knowledge and have attained my state of being.
4:10.transliteration:
vīta-rāga-bhaya-krodhā man-mayā mām upāśritāḥ |
bahavo jñāna-tapasā pūtā mad-bhāvam āgatāḥ ||10||
4:10.devanagari:
वीतरागभयक्रोधा मन्मया मामुपाश्रिताः ।
बहवो ज्ञानतपसा पूता मद्भावमागताः ॥ १० ॥
4:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:10.explanation: Three inner freedoms mark the matured seeker: freedom from rāga (desire, clinging), freedom from bhaya (fear, insecurity), and freedom from krodha (anger, reactivity). These are not random virtues but precisely the three distortions that prevent the mind from remaining clear and still enough to know the Self. 'Man-mayāḥ' (absorbed in me) — their consciousness has become saturated with the divine; they no longer experience themselves as separate from the Lord. 'Jñāna-tapasā pūtāḥ' — jñāna is itself a tapas (austerity); it purifies by burning away accumulated ignorance and conditioning. 'Mad-bhāvam āgatāḥ' (attained my state of being) — not merely union with the Lord but participation in the Lord's own nature: consciousness-bliss-existence (sac-cid-ānanda). This verse assures the seeker that the path is walked and the goal is real.

4:11.text: In whatever way people take refuge in me, in that very way do I respond to them, O Pārtha. Human beings follow my path in every direction.
4:11.transliteration:
ye yathā māṃ prapadyante tāṃs tathaiva bhajāmy aham |
mama vartmānuvartante manuṣyāḥ pārtha sarvaśaḥ ||11||
4:11.devanagari:
ये यथा मां प्रपद्यन्ते तांस्तथैव भजाम्यहम् ।
मम वर्त्मानुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः पार्थ सर्वशः ॥ ११ ॥
4:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:11.explanation: This verse is among the Gita's most profound statements on divine impartiality and universal inclusion. 'Ye yathā māṃ prapadyante' — the Lord responds to each seeker in the mode in which they approach: the jñānī seeking formless Brahman finds the formless; the bhakta seeking a personal Lord finds the personal. 'Tāṃs tathaiva bhajāmy aham' — God meets each person exactly where they are. This is not relativism (all paths equally lead anywhere) but the recognition that the one Reality appears in the form most appropriate to the seeker's nature and readiness. 'Mama vartmānuvartante manuṣyāḥ pārtha sarvaśaḥ' — all human paths — whether of knowledge, devotion, action, or renunciation — are ultimately paths within the divine reality, for there is nowhere else to go.

4:12.text: Those who desire the fruits of their actions worship the gods here in this world; for in the human realm, success born of action comes swiftly.
4:12.transliteration:
kāṃkṣantaḥ karmaṇāṃ siddhiṃ yajanta iha devatāḥ |
kṣipraṃ hi mānuṣe loke siddhir bhavati karmajā ||12||
4:12.devanagari:
काङ्क्षन्तः कर्मणां सिद्धिं यजन्त इह देवताः ।
क्षिप्रं हि मानुषे लोके सिद्धिर्भवति कर्मजा ॥ १२ ॥
4:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:12.explanation: This verse makes a pragmatic observation about human religiosity: those motivated by desire for specific results — wealth, progeny, health, victory — naturally turn to the devas (cosmic powers governing these domains). 'Kṣipraṃ hi' (indeed swiftly) — the human plane is uniquely positioned for rapid fruition because intention, action, and cosmic responsiveness align here in a particularly direct way. Krishna does not condemn deva-worship; he simply locates it within the hierarchy of motivations. The contrast with verse 11 is instructive: verse 11 describes the highest path where the Lord himself responds to all seekers; verse 12 acknowledges the legitimate but lower path of seeking specific results through specific deities. Both are real; they are not equally ultimate.

4:13.text: The fourfold order was created by me according to the division of qualities (guṇas) and action (karma). Though I am its creator, know me to be the imperishable non-doer.
4:13.transliteration:
cātur-varṇyaṃ mayā sṛṣṭaṃ guṇa-karma-vibhāgaśaḥ |
tasya kartāram api māṃ viddhy akartāram avyayam ||13||
4:13.devanagari:
चातुर्वर्ण्यं मया सृष्टं गुणकर्मविभागशः ।
तस्य कर्तारमपि मां विद्ध्यकर्तारमव्ययम् ॥ १३ ॥
4:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:13.explanation: 'Cātur-varṇyam' — the fourfold social order (brāhmaṇa, kṣatriya, vaiśya, śūdra). The critical qualifier is 'guṇa-karma-vibhāgaśaḥ' (according to the division of qualities and action) — the ordering principle is based on psychological disposition and natural aptitude (guṇa) and the kind of action one is drawn to perform (karma), not birth. This is a key hermeneutic note: the verse explicitly says the division is by guṇa and karma, not by jāti (birth-group). The second half pivots to the deeper teaching: even as the creator of this order, Krishna declares himself 'akartāram' (non-doer). This is the paradox that will govern the next several verses: action performed without ego-identification, without desire for results, leaves no karmic residue — the actor remains unbound even while acting.

4:14.text: Actions do not taint me; nor do I have any longing for the fruits of action. One who knows me thus is not bound by actions.
4:14.transliteration:
na māṃ karmāṇi limpanti na me karma-phale spṛhā |
iti māṃ yo 'bhijānāti karmabhir na sa badhyate ||14||
4:14.devanagari:
न मां कर्माणि लिम्पन्ति न मे कर्मफले स्पृहा ।
इति मां योऽभिजानाति कर्मभिर्न स बध्यते ॥ १४ ॥
4:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:14.explanation: This verse reveals the precise mechanism by which the Lord remains untainted by action — and how the knowing devotee replicates this freedom. 'Na māṃ karmāṇi limpanti' (actions do not cling to me) — karmic adhesion requires two conditions: ego-identification as the doer and desire for the result. Where neither is present, no karma accrues. 'Na me karma-phale spṛhā' (I have no longing for fruits) — 'spṛhā' is a strong word denoting deep craving, not mere preference. The liberating consequence: 'iti māṃ yo 'bhijānāti karmabhir na sa badhyate' — one who truly understands this about the Lord internalizes the same principle. This is not metaphorical: the jñāni who acts with identical non-attachment to results replicates the Lord's own freedom from karmic bondage. Knowledge of the Lord's nature becomes the direct means of the seeker's liberation.

4:15.text: Even the ancient seekers of liberation performed action with this understanding. Therefore, you too perform action just as those predecessors did from ancient times.
4:15.transliteration:
evaṃ jñātvā kṛtaṃ karma pūrvair api mumukṣubhiḥ |
kuru karmaiva tasmāt tvaṃ pūrvaiḥ pūrvataraṃ kṛtam ||15||
4:15.devanagari:
एवं ज्ञात्वा कृतं कर्म पूर्वैरपि मुमुक्षुभिः ।
कुरु कर्मैव तस्मात्त्वं पूर्वैः पूर्वतरं कृतम् ॥ १५ ॥
4:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:15.explanation: 'Mumukṣubhiḥ' (seekers of liberation) — not ordinary people driven by results, but those specifically aspiring to mokṣa. Yet even they acted. This is a crucial argument against the misunderstanding that liberation-seekers should abandon all action. 'Evaṃ jñātvā kṛtaṃ karma' — they acted with knowledge of what Krishna has just articulated: no ego as doer, no desire for results. 'Pūrvaiḥ pūrvataraṃ kṛtam' (done by predecessors from time immemorial) — Krishna grounds Arjuna in the weight of the tradition. This is not a new or experimental path; it is the ancient and tested way of those who achieved liberation while acting fully in the world. Arjuna is being asked to join that unbroken stream of dharmic action.

4:16.text: What is action and what is non-action? — Even the learned are confused about this. I shall declare to you that action, knowing which you will be freed from inauspiciousness.
4:16.transliteration:
kiṃ karma kim akarma iti kavayo 'py atra mohitāḥ |
tat te karma pravakṣyāmi yaj jñātvā mokṣyase 'śubhāt ||16||
4:16.devanagari:
किं कर्म किमकर्मेति कवयोऽप्यत्र मोहिताः ।
तत्ते कर्म प्रवक्ष्यामि यज्ज्ञात्वा मोक्ष्यसेऽशुभात् ॥ १६ ॥
4:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:16.explanation: 'Kavayo 'py atra mohitāḥ' (even the wise are confused here) — this acknowledgment of the depth of the problem is itself a teaching: the distinction between karma, akarma, and vikarma is not intuitive; it requires precise philosophical insight. The confusion of even learned people is not intellectual failure but testimony to the subtlety of the subject. 'Tat te karma pravakṣyāmi' (I shall declare that action to you) — this is a formal preface, signaling that what follows in verses 17–18 is the crux of the whole karmayoga teaching. 'Yaj jñātvā mokṣyase 'śubhāt' — the fruit promised is not a ritual reward but liberation from the very root of karmic bondage. The knowledge of true karma is itself mokṣa-sādhana (a means of liberation).

4:17.text: One must understand the nature of action, the nature of forbidden action (vikarma), and also the nature of non-action (akarma) — for the path of action is deep and difficult to understand.
4:17.transliteration:
karmaṇo hy api boddhavyaṃ boddhavyaṃ ca vikarmaṇaḥ |
akarmaṇaś ca boddhavyaṃ gahanā karmaṇo gatiḥ ||17||
4:17.devanagari:
कर्मणो ह्यपि बोद्धव्यं बोद्धव्यं च विकर्मणः ।
अकर्मणश्च बोद्धव्यं गहना कर्मणो गतिः ॥ १७ ॥
4:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:17.explanation: Three categories of action are defined: (1) 'Karma' — prescribed, right action: what ought to be done, in accordance with one's dharma and śāstra. (2) 'Vikarma' — prohibited action: what is forbidden, action that generates sin and bondage. (3) 'Akarma' — non-action: in the Gita's precise technical sense, this is not idleness or inaction but action performed without ego-identification and without desire for results, which therefore leaves no karmic impression. 'Gahanā karmaṇo gatiḥ' — the admission that karma's workings are deep and impenetrable is itself a teaching: this is not a topic to be understood by surface thinking alone. The following verse (4.18) will give the definitive and paradoxical formulation of akarma.

4:18.text: One who sees non-action in action and action in non-action — he is wise among humans; he is the one united in yoga, the doer of all actions.
4:18.transliteration:
karmaṇy akarma yaḥ paśyed akarmaṇi ca karma yaḥ |
sa buddhimān manuṣyeṣu sa yuktaḥ kṛtsna-karma-kṛt ||18||
4:18.devanagari:
कर्मण्यकर्म यः पश्येदकर्मणि च कर्म यः ।
स बुद्धिमान्मनुष्येषु स युक्तः कृत्स्नकर्मकृत् ॥ १८ ॥
4:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:18.explanation: This is one of the Gita's most celebrated and philosophically demanding verses. 'Karmaṇy akarma yaḥ paśyet' (who sees non-action in action) — the person who is externally fully engaged in the world's work, yet internally unidentified with the doing: there is no ego-agent behind the action and no desire for results. In this inner sense, they are an 'akartā' (non-doer) even while acting. 'Akarmaṇi ca karma yaḥ' (who sees action in non-action) — one who withdraws from external activity but remains mentally driven by desire and ego is still a 'kartā' (doer) in the meaningful sense: their inner action continues to generate karma. True akarma is not the stillness of the body but the stillness of the ego. 'Sa buddhimān... sa yuktaḥ... kṛtsna-karma-kṛt' — this person is genuinely wise, genuinely unified with the Supreme, and paradoxically the most complete doer of all — free action, free actor, free result.

4:19.text: One whose every undertaking is free from desire and deliberate intention — whose actions are burned by the fire of knowledge — him the wise call a paṇḍita (truly learned).
4:19.transliteration:
yasya sarve samārambhāḥ kāma-saṅkalpa-varjitāḥ |
jñānāgni-dagdha-karmāṇaṃ tam āhuḥ paṇḍitaṃ budhāḥ ||19||
4:19.devanagari:
यस्य सर्वे समारम्भाः कामसङ्कल्पवर्जिताः ।
ज्ञानाग्निदग्धकर्माणं तमाहुः पण्डितं बुधाः ॥ १९ ॥
4:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:19.explanation: 'Samārambhāḥ' (undertakings, endeavours) — every initiative of the jñānī. 'Kāma-saṅkalpa-varjitāḥ' (free from desire and deliberate intention) — two roots of ordinary action: first kāma (desire, wanting a particular result), then saṅkalpa (the deliberate intention formed to secure that result). When these two are absent, action no longer generates binding karma. 'Jñānāgni-dagdha-karmāṇam' — the image of knowledge as fire burning karma is central to Chapter 4 (it will return powerfully in verse 37). The fire of knowledge does not destroy the person's capacity for action; it destroys the ego-fuel and desire-fuel that would otherwise make those actions binding. 'Paṇḍitam' — the Gita's standard for true learning: not scholarship about texts but inner freedom from the mechanisms of compulsive wanting.

4:20.text: Having abandoned attachment to the fruits of action, always contented, depending on nothing — even while being fully engaged in action, he truly does nothing at all.
4:20.transliteration:
tyaktvā karma-phalāsaṅgaṃ nitya-tṛpto nirāśrayaḥ |
karmaṇy abhipravṛtto 'pi naiva kiñcit karoti saḥ ||20||
4:20.devanagari:
त्यक्त्वा कर्मफलासङ्गं नित्यतृप्तो निराश्रयः ।
कर्मण्यभिप्रवृत्तोऽपि नैव किञ्चित्करोति सः ॥ २० ॥
4:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:20.explanation: Three attributes characterize this liberated actor: (1) 'Tyaktvā karma-phalāsaṅgam' — total relinquishment of attachment to results; not merely tolerance of failure but genuine non-investment in outcomes, paralleling 2.47. (2) 'Nitya-tṛptaḥ' (ever content) — his contentment is not situational (dependent on circumstances going well) but structural: grounded in the Self which is already complete. (3) 'Nirāśrayaḥ' (without refuge/support) — not socially isolated, but not dependent on any external support for his inner stability. 'Naiva kiñcit karoti saḥ' — 'he truly does nothing': this is the experiential report of the ego-less actor. From the outside, he acts; from the inside, there is no 'one' performing the action. This is the living embodiment of verse 4.18.

4:21.text: Without aspiration, with mind and self restrained, having abandoned all possessiveness — performing action only for the body's sake, he incurs no sin.
4:21.transliteration:
nirāśīr yata-cittātmā tyakta-sarva-parigrahaḥ |
śārīraṃ kevalaṃ karma kurvan nāpnoti kilbiṣam ||21||
4:21.devanagari:
निराशीर्यतचित्तात्मा त्यक्तसर्वपरिग्रहः ।
शारीरं केवलं कर्म कुर्वन्नाप्नोति किल्बिषम् ॥ २१ ॥
4:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:21.explanation: 'Nirāśīḥ' (free from hope/aspiration) — not pessimism but the absence of self-interested craving; every action is undertaken without investing one's identity in the outcome. 'Yata-cittātmā' (with restrained mind and self) — both the cognitive faculty (citta) and the deeper self (ātmā here meaning the inner person as a whole) are disciplined and directed. 'Tyakta-sarva-parigrahaḥ' — 'parigraha' (accumulation, grasping) covers not just material possessions but the whole habit of psychological accumulation: storing experiences as identity, clinging to roles and self-images. 'Śārīraṃ kevalaṃ karma' (action only for the body) — the minimum necessary for sustaining the instrument of service. 'Nāpnoti kilbiṣam' (incurs no sin) — no karmic residue accumulates, because the engine of karmic binding — desire + ego-identification — is absent.

4:22.text: Content with whatever comes unsought, beyond the pairs of opposites, free from envy, and equal in success and failure — even having acted, he is not bound.
4:22.transliteration:
yadṛcchā-lābha-santuṣṭo dvandvātīto vimatsaraḥ |
samaḥ siddhāv asiddhau ca kṛtvāpi na nibadhyate ||22||
4:22.devanagari:
यदृच्छालाभसन्तुष्टो द्वन्द्वातीतो विमत्सरः ।
समः सिद्धावसिद्धौ च कृत्वापि न निबध्यते ॥ २२ ॥
4:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:22.explanation: Four qualities define the liberated actor: (1) 'Yadṛcchā-lābha-santuṣṭaḥ' (content with unsolicited gain) — accepting whatever arrives through circumstance without having calculated and pursued it; the mind's default is contentment, not craving. (2) 'Dvandvātītaḥ' (beyond the pairs of opposites) — the dvandvas (heat-cold, pleasure-pain, honor-dishonor) no longer produce reactivity; they are experienced but not grasped or resisted. (3) 'Vimatsaraḥ' (free from envy) — no comparison, no diminishment felt at another's success. (4) 'Samaḥ siddhāv asiddhau' (equal in success and failure) — the outcome of action does not alter the inner state. 'Kṛtvāpi na nibadhyate' — this is the practical definition of karma-yoga's liberation: continuous action in the world with no bondage accumulating.

4:23.text: For one whose attachment is gone, who is liberated, whose mind is established in knowledge — acting for the sake of sacrifice (yajña), his entire karma dissolves away.
4:23.transliteration:
gata-saṅgasya muktasya jñānāvasthita-cetasaḥ |
yajñāyācarataḥ karma samagraṃ pravilīyate ||23||
4:23.devanagari:
गतसङ्गस्य मुक्तस्य ज्ञानावस्थितचेतसः ।
यज्ञायाचरतः कर्म समग्रं प्रविलीयते ॥ २३ ॥
4:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:23.explanation: This verse synthesizes the preceding teachings into a single comprehensive statement of the path. Three inner conditions: (1) 'Gata-saṅgasya' (one whose attachment has gone) — not suppressed or controlled but organically dissolved through understanding. (2) 'Muktasya' (liberated) — jīvan-mukta: free even while embodied, not waiting for death to experience liberation. (3) 'Jñānāvasthita-cetasaḥ' (mind established in knowledge) — awareness grounded in the truth of the Self, not oscillating with circumstances. When combined with 'yajñāyācarataḥ' (acting as an act of sacrifice, as an offering) — all action as cosmic service rather than personal gain — 'samagraṃ pravilīyate' (the entire karma dissolves). The dissolution is not of the actions themselves but of their binding power.

4:24.text: The instrument of offering is Brahman; the oblation is Brahman; Brahman offers into the fire of Brahman — Brahman alone is to be reached by one who is absorbed in Brahman-action.
4:24.transliteration:
brahmārpaṇaṃ brahma havir brahmāgnau brahmaṇā hutam |
brahmaiva tena gantavyaṃ brahma-karma-samādhinā ||24||
4:24.devanagari:
ब्रह्मार्पणं ब्रह्म हविर्ब्रह्माग्नौ ब्रह्मणा हुतम् ।
ब्रह्मैव तेन गन्तव्यं ब्रह्मकर्मसमाधिना ॥ २४ ॥
4:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:24.explanation: This is one of the Gita's most profound and poetically elevated verses, with the word 'Brahman' appearing five times. The entire structure of the yajña (sacrifice) is declared to be Brahman: the ladle (brahmārpaṇam) is Brahman; the oblation (brahma haviḥ) is Brahman; the fire (brahmāgnau) is Brahman; the one who offers (brahmaṇā) is Brahman. When the practitioner's awareness reaches this level — perceiving the instrument, the action, the material, the agent, and the fire all as expressions of the one reality — they are in brahma-karma-samādhi (absorption in Brahman-action). 'Brahmaiva tena gantavyam' — such a practitioner reaches Brahman, because they are already in Brahman; the journey and the destination are identical. This verse describes the highest possible orientation toward all action.

4:25.text: Some yogīs offer worship to the deities as sacrifice; others offer the sacrifice itself into the fire of Brahman by means of sacrifice.
4:25.transliteration:
daivaṃ evāpare yajñaṃ yoginaḥ paryupāsate |
brahmāgnāv apare yajñaṃ yajñenaivopajuhvati ||25||
4:25.devanagari:
दैवमेवापरे यज्ञं योगिनः पर्युपासते ।
ब्रह्माग्नावपरे यज्ञं यज्ञेनैवोपजुह्वति ॥ २५ ॥
4:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:25.explanation: This verse opens a celebrated catalogue of yajñas (sacrifices) spanning verses 25-30, demonstrating that the principle of yajña encompasses all forms of disciplined spiritual practice. Two types are presented here: (1) 'Daivaṃ yajñam' — ritual worship of deities; these practitioners approach the infinite through particular divine forms. (2) 'Brahmāgnau yajñenaivopajuhvati' — offering sacrifice into the fire of Brahman by means of sacrifice itself. This second type represents the contemplative who has internalized the insight of 4.24 — perceiving the instrument of offering, the offering, and the fire all as Brahman — so that the very act of worship dissolves into the recognition of non-dual reality. Both are valid paths; the second is the more advanced.

4:26.text: Others offer the senses — hearing and the rest — into the fires of self-restraint; others offer sound and other sense-objects into the fires of the senses.
4:26.transliteration:
śrotrādīnīndriyāṇy anye saṃyamāgniṣu juhvati |
śabdādīn viṣayān anya indriyāgniṣu juhvati ||26||
4:26.devanagari:
श्रोत्रादीनीन्द्रियाण्यन्ये संयमाग्निषु जुह्वति ।
शब्दादीन्विषयानन्य इन्द्रियाग्निषु जुह्वति ॥ २६ ॥
4:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:26.explanation: Two more forms of inner sacrifice: (1) Those who offer the senses (śrotra — hearing, skin — touch, eyes — sight, tongue — taste, nose — smell) into the fire of self-restraint (saṃyama). This is the yoga of sense-withdrawal (pratyāhāra) — pulling the senses inward away from their objects, treating the restraint itself as the highest offering. (2) Those who offer sense-objects (sound, touch, form, taste, smell) into the fires of the senses — engaging the senses in experience but without clinging; the objects are 'consumed' without leaving residue in the mind. Both are legitimate yajñas: one restrains the senses, the other uses them without bondage. Together they represent the full spectrum of sensory yoga.

4:27.text: Others offer all the activities of the senses and all the activities of the vital breath (prāṇa) into the fire of the yoga of self-restraint, kindled by knowledge.
4:27.transliteration:
sarvāṇīndriya-karmāṇi prāṇa-karmāṇi cāpare |
ātma-saṃyama-yogāgnau juhvati jñāna-dīpite ||27||
4:27.devanagari:
सर्वाणीन्द्रियकर्माणि प्राणकर्माणि चापरे ।
आत्मसंयमयोगाग्नौ जुह्वति ज्ञानदीपिते ॥ २७ ॥
4:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:27.explanation: 'Ātma-saṃyama-yoga-agnau' (the fire of the yoga of self-mastery) represents the most interior of all sacrifices — the entire field of sensory and vital activity is surrendered into the flame of yogic absorption. 'Jñāna-dīpite' (kindled by knowledge) is crucial: this fire is not mere suppression or mortification, but a luminous interior restraint illumined by the direct recognition of the Self. When the yogi's understanding of the Self is stable, the activities of the senses and prāṇas (the five vital airs: prāṇa, apāna, samāna, vyāna, udāna) lose their autonomy and are naturally offered into the stillness of awareness. This describes the highest form of contemplative yoga — closest to samādhi.

4:28.text: Some ascetics, who are of sharpened vows, perform the sacrifice of material wealth (dravya-yajña), the sacrifice of austerity (tapo-yajña), and the sacrifice of yoga (yoga-yajña); others perform the sacrifice of scriptural study (svādhyāya) and the sacrifice of knowledge (jñāna-yajña).
4:28.transliteration:
dravya-yajñās tapo-yajñā yoga-yajñās tathāpare |
svādhyāya-jñāna-yajñāś ca yatayaḥ saṃśita-vratāḥ ||28||
4:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:28.explanation: Five more yajñas are enumerated, each representing a complete spiritual path: (1) Dravya-yajña — the sacrifice of material possessions; giving away wealth, food, clothing, serving others. (2) Tapo-yajña — austerity as sacrifice; the deliberate disciplines of body (kāya-tapas), speech (vāk-tapas), and mind (manas-tapas). (3) Yoga-yajña — the sacrifice of systematic yoga practice (aṣṭāṅga-yoga, dhyāna, etc.). (4) Svādhyāya — Vedic recitation, scripture study, mantra japa, self-study. (5) Jñāna-yajña — the sacrifice of knowledge; deep inquiry into the nature of reality and the Self. 'Yatayaḥ saṃśita-vratāḥ' — 'ascetics of sharpened vows' (saṃśita = sharpened like a blade) indicates the intensity required. Jñāna-yajña, established as supreme in 4.33, is the culmination of all.

4:29.text: Those devoted to prāṇāyāma offer the outgoing breath (prāṇa) into the incoming breath (apāna), and the incoming into the outgoing; and having restrained the movement of both prāṇa and apāna, they are fixed in breath retention.
4:29.transliteration:
apāne juhvati prāṇaṃ prāṇe 'pānaṃ tathāpare |
prāṇāpāna-gatī ruddhvā prāṇāyāma-parāyaṇāḥ ||29||
4:29.devanagari:
अपाने जुह्वति प्राणं प्राणेऽपानं तथापरे ।
प्राणापानगती रुद्ध्वा प्राणायामपरायणाः ॥ २९ ॥
4:29.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:29.explanation: This verse presents prāṇāyāma (regulation of vital breath) as a complete yajña in itself. The physiology: 'Prāṇa' is the upward-moving vital air (exhalation tendency, associated with the heart); 'apāna' is the downward-moving vital air (inhalation tendency, associated with the base). Three phases of prāṇāyāma as sacrifice: (1) Offering prāṇa into apāna — pūraka (inhalation), drawing the outer breath inward. (2) Offering apāna into prāṇa — recaka (exhalation), releasing the inner breath outward. (3) 'Prāṇāpāna-gatī ruddhvā' — kumbhaka (retention), suspending both movements at the midpoint. In kumbhaka, the mental oscillation produced by the breath naturally ceases, and the meditator enters a still, expansive awareness. Prāṇāyāma is thus framed not as mere physical breathing exercise but as the deepest inner fire-ritual.

4:30.text: Others, who have regulated their diet, offer prāṇas into prāṇas. All these, indeed, are knowers of sacrifice, and their impurities (sins) have been destroyed by sacrifice.
4:30.transliteration:
apare niyatāhārāḥ prāṇān prāṇeṣu juhvati |
sarve 'py ete yajña-vido yajña-kṣapita-kalmaṣāḥ ||30||
4:30.devanagari:
अपरे नियताहाराः प्राणान्प्राणेषु जुह्वति ।
सर्वेऽप्येते यज्ञविदो यज्ञक्षपितकल्मषाः ॥ ३० ॥
4:30.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:30.explanation: 'Niyatāhārāḥ' (those who have regulated their diet) — the discipline of āhāra (what one consumes — food, sense impressions, thoughts) is itself a yajña. 'Prāṇān prāṇeṣu juhvati' — offering prāṇas into prāṇas: the individual vital force is offered into the universal Prāṇa, the cosmic life-force. This represents the final dissolution of the personal into the universal at the level of prāṇic energy. The verse then closes the entire yajña-catalogue with a unifying statement: 'sarve 'py ete yajña-vidaḥ' — all of these practitioners, regardless of their specific path, are 'knowers of sacrifice.' Their diversity of method does not divide them; all converge in the understanding that dedicated offering purifies. 'Yajña-kṣapita-kalmaṣāḥ' — sacrifice has destroyed their impurities (karmic residues, mental conditioning, ego-identification).

4:31.text: Those who eat the nectar-like remnant of sacrifice go to the eternal Brahman. This world is not (joyful) for one who performs no sacrifice, O best of Kurus — how then the next world?
4:31.transliteration:
yajña-śiṣṭāmṛta-bhujo yānti brahma sanātanam |
nāyaṃ loko 'sty ayajñasya kuto 'nyaḥ kuru-sattama ||31||
4:31.devanagari:
यज्ञशिष्टामृतभुजो यान्ति ब्रह्म सनातनम् ।
नायं लोकोऽस्त्ययज्ञस्य कुतोऽन्यः कुरुसत्तम ॥ ३१ ॥
4:31.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:31.explanation: 'Yajña-śiṣṭāmṛta' — the nectar-like remnant after sacrifice. In Vedic ritual, one first offers to the gods, then to guests, then partakes of what remains — this prasāda is considered amṛta (deathless substance). At the philosophical level, what remains after the ego's offerings are consumed in jñāna-yajña is pure consciousness itself — the recognition of the immortal Self. 'Yānti brahma sanātanam' — they reach the eternal Brahman; not merely a better life but liberation itself. The negative formulation is powerful: 'Nāyaṃ loko 'sty ayajñasya' — for the one who lives in pure self-interest, performing no sacrifice of any kind (no seva, no practice, no offering), even this world does not go well. The logic: the universe runs on yajña (mutual giving); one who only takes without offering violates the cosmic law and suffers accordingly.

4:32.text: Thus many kinds of sacrifices are spread out in the mouth of Brahman (the Vedas). Know them all to be born from action; knowing this thus, you shall be liberated.
4:32.transliteration:
evaṃ bahu-vidhā yajñā vitatā brahmaṇo mukhe |
karma-jān viddhi tān sarvān evaṃ jñātvā vimokṣyase ||32||
4:32.devanagari:
एवं बहुविधा यज्ञा वितता ब्रह्मणो मुखे ।
कर्मजान्विद्धि तान्सर्वानेवं ज्ञात्वा विमोक्ष्यसे ॥ ३२ ॥
4:32.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:32.explanation: 'Brahmaṇo mukhe' (in the mouth of Brahman) — the Vedas are declared to be Brahman's own utterance (niḥśvāsa); the yajña-catalogue is thus rooted in eternal cosmic law, not human invention. 'Karma-jān viddhi tān sarvān' — know all these yajñas to be born of karma (action). This is not a limitation but a recognition: every spiritual practice is a form of conscious action, and as such participates in the field of transformation. 'Evaṃ jñātvā vimokṣyase' — the liberating insight is not merely knowing about the yajñas but understanding their common root: all offering leads to the dissolution of the ego's claim to ownership. When the practitioner truly comprehends that all action is sacrifice and all sacrifice is Brahman's, the knot of binding karma loosens. The next verse explains why knowledge-sacrifice surpasses all.

4:33.text: Superior to the sacrifice made of material wealth is the sacrifice of knowledge, O Parantapa. All action in its entirety, O Pārtha, reaches its culmination and fulfillment in knowledge.
4:33.transliteration:
śreyān dravya-mayād yajñāj jñāna-yajñaḥ parantapa |
sarvaṃ karmākhilaṃ pārtha jñāne parisamāpyate ||33||
4:33.devanagari:
श्रेयान्द्रव्यमयाद्यज्ञाज्ज्ञानयज्ञः परन्तप ।
सर्वं कर्माखिलं पार्थ ज्ञाने परिसमाप्यते ॥ ३३ ॥
4:33.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:33.explanation: This is one of the Gita's most decisive philosophical statements. 'Dravya-mayāt yajñāt' — material sacrifice: however elaborate the ritual, it operates within the finite (dravya = matter). Its effects are therefore also finite. 'Jñāna-yajñaḥ śreyān' — the sacrifice of knowledge is superior because knowledge is not a finite commodity; it destroys the very root of ignorance that generates bondage. 'Sarvaṃ karmākhilaṃ pārtha jñāne parisamāpyate' — all action, without exception, finds its ultimate completion (parisamāpyate — complete termination) in knowledge. This does not mean action is worthless, but that action performed with full understanding eventually dissolves the actor's identification with doership — which is liberation. All karma is a journey toward this knowing; jñāna-yajña is the destination itself.

4:34.text: Know that (knowledge) by prostration (praṇipāta), by sincere questioning (paripraśna), and by service (sevā); the wise who are seers of truth will teach you that knowledge.
4:34.transliteration:
tad viddhi praṇipātena paripraśnena sevayā |
upadekṣyanti te jñānaṃ jñāninas tattva-darśinaḥ ||34||
4:34.devanagari:
तद्विद्धि प्रणिपातेन परिप्रश्नेन सेवया ।
उपदेक्ष्यन्ति ते ज्ञानं ज्ञानिनस्तत्त्वदर्शिनः ॥ ३४ ॥
4:34.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:34.explanation: This verse encodes the entire Vedantic guru-śiṣya relationship in three words. (1) 'Praṇipāta' (complete prostration) — not a mere physical gesture but the surrender of the ego's claim to know-it-already. The single greatest obstacle to wisdom is the assumption that one already understands; praṇipāta dissolves this. (2) 'Paripraśna' (comprehensive questioning) — not argumentative cross-examination, not idle curiosity, but sustained, authentic inquiry. 'Pari' means thorough, from all sides; the student who questions from genuine perplexity (like Arjuna) creates the opening for transmission. (3) 'Sevā' (service) — serving the teacher and the teaching purifies the receiver's heart; the instrument of knowing (the mind) must be clean for knowledge to settle. 'Tattva-darśinaḥ' — seers of truth, not merely scholars; only one who has directly seen can transmit the living fire.

4:35.text: Knowing which, O Pāṇḍava, you shall not again fall into delusion like this — and by which you shall see all beings without exception in the Self, and then in Me.
4:35.transliteration:
yaj jñātvā na punar moham evaṃ yāsyasi pāṇḍava |
yena bhūtāny aśeṣāṇi drakṣyasy ātmany atho mayi ||35||
4:35.devanagari:
यज्ज्ञात्वा न पुनर्मोहमेवं यास्यसि पाण्डव ।
येन भूतान्यशेषाणि द्रक्ष्यस्यात्मन्यथो मयि ॥ ३५ ॥
4:35.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:35.explanation: The fruit of jñāna is stated in two ascending realizations. (1) 'Na punar moham evaṃ yāsyasi' — liberation from this specific delusion (the crisis of Chapter 1). 'Evam' — this kind: the confusion that arises when the ego mistakenly identifies with roles, relationships, and bodies. True knowledge does not merely dissolve one particular confusion; it removes the root of all confusion. (2) 'Bhūtāny aśeṣāṇi drakṣyasy ātmany' — you will see all beings without exception in the Self. This is the direct recognition of unity — the same consciousness in all forms. 'Atho mayi' — and then in Me: the final dissolution where even 'the Self as witness' is recognized as Kṛṣṇa's (the Supreme's) nature. The progression: individual self → universal Self → Absolute. This is the summit of Vedantic realization.

4:36.text: Even if you were the most sinful of all sinners, you shall indeed cross the entire ocean of sin by the boat of knowledge alone.
4:36.transliteration:
api ced asi pāpebhyaḥ sarvebhyaḥ pāpa-kṛttamaḥ |
sarvaṃ jñāna-plavenaiva vṛjinaṃ santariṣyasi ||36||
4:36.devanagari:
अपि चेदसि पापेभ्यः सर्वेभ्यः पापकृत्तमः ।
सर्वं ज्ञानप्लवेनैव वृजिनं सन्तरिष्यसि ॥ ३६ ॥
4:36.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:36.explanation: This verse proclaims the absolute supremacy and universality of jñāna with extraordinary force. 'Pāpa-kṛttamaḥ' — the superlative: the greatest sinner among all sinners. This rhetorical hyperbole does not encourage sin but declares that the capacity of knowledge is so vast that no quantum of karmic accumulation can withstand it. 'Jñāna-plavena' — the metaphor of the boat (plava): a boat does not avoid contact with water; it transforms the relationship with it — the waters that would drown become the medium of passage. 'Vṛjinam santariṣyasi' — the entire ocean of sin is crossed. Why? Because sin (pāpa) is rooted in avidyā (fundamental ignorance of one's nature). When the root is severed by knowledge — the realization that one is the limitless Self — the accumulation of past karma loses its power to define identity. This is the Gita's most inclusive and liberating promise.

4:37.text: Just as a blazing fire reduces its fuel (kindling) to ashes, O Arjuna, so the fire of knowledge reduces all karma to ashes in the same way.
4:37.transliteration:
yathaidhāṃsi samiddho 'gnir bhasmasāt kurute 'rjuna |
jñānāgniḥ sarva-karmāṇi bhasmasāt kurute tathā ||37||
4:37.devanagari:
यथैधांसि समिद्धोऽग्निर्भस्मसात्कुरुतेऽर्जुन ।
ज्ञानाग्निः सर्वकर्माणि भस्मसात्कुरुते तथा ॥ ३७ ॥
4:37.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:37.explanation: The Gita's most vivid metaphor for the relationship between jñāna and karma. The comparison is precise: fire leaves nothing of the wood — not even the ash persists once the fire is fully blazing. 'Jñānāgniḥ' — the fire of knowledge is not a slow erosion but a complete annihilation. 'Sarva-karmāṇi' — all karma: this includes saṃcita (accumulated past karma), prārabdha (karma currently bearing fruit), and āgāmī (karma being created now). Vedantic commentary clarifies: once the Self is known as the pure witness beyond all action, the sense that 'I am the doer' dissolves. With the 'doer' gone, no new karma accumulates; and past karma loses its power to produce new identity. The fire metaphor is especially apt: fire does not debate with the wood, does not partially burn it — knowledge, when complete, is equally total and unconditional.

4:38.text: Truly, nothing in this world is as purifying as knowledge. One who is perfected in yoga discovers it by himself within the Self in due course of time.
4:38.transliteration:
na hi jñānena sadṛśaṃ pavitram iha vidyate |
tat svayaṃ yoga-saṃsiddhaḥ kālenātmani vindati ||38||
4:38.devanagari:
न हि ज्ञानेन सदृशं पवित्रमिह विद्यते ।
तत्स्वयं योगसंसिद्धः कालेनात्मनि विन्दति ॥ ३८ ॥
4:38.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:38.explanation: 'Na hi jñānena sadṛśam pavitram' — the absolute declaration: nothing is as purifying as knowledge. All external purifying agents (sacred waters, pilgrimage, fasting, ritual) clean the external instrument; jñāna alone removes the root impurity — avidyā (ignorance of the Self's nature). 'Yoga-saṃsiddhaḥ' — one who has attained perfection in yoga (not just one chapter of practice, but the ripening of sustained, sincere practice over time). 'Kālena' — in due time; spiritual maturation follows its own timing; forcing or rushing it is impossible. 'Ātmani vindati' — discovers it WITHIN the Self. The key teaching: knowledge of the Self is not imported from outside; it is always already present as the ground of one's own being. The entire spiritual journey is the removal of what obscures what was never absent.

4:39.text: One who has faith, who is devoted to it, and who has mastered the senses, attains knowledge; having attained knowledge, he quickly reaches supreme peace.
4:39.transliteration:
śraddhāvāṁl labhate jñānaṃ tat-paraḥ saṃyatendriyaḥ |
jñānaṃ labdhvā parāṃ śāntim acireṇādhigacchati ||39||
4:39.devanagari:
श्रद्धावाँल्लभते ज्ञानं तत्परः संयतेन्द्रियः ।
ज्ञानं लब्ध्वा परां शान्तिमचिरेणाधिगच्छति ॥ ३९ ॥
4:39.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:39.explanation: Three essential prerequisites for the receipt of jñāna are stated precisely: (1) 'Śraddhāvān' — faith. Śraddhā in the Gita is not blind belief but the confident, open receptivity that trusts the validity of the path before all its fruits have been experienced. It is the antidote to cynicism, without being opposed to inquiry. (2) 'Tat-paraḥ' — devoted to That, making knowledge the supreme priority. A mind divided between many competing interests cannot sustain the sustained attention that jñāna requires. (3) 'Saṃyatendriyaḥ' — senses under governance; a mind whose attention is perpetually scattered by sensory stimulation cannot receive or retain subtle knowledge. 'Parāṃ śāntim' — the supreme peace is not a feeling-state but the settled recognition of one's own nature as unmoving awareness. 'Acireṇa' — without delay: once knowledge is real, liberation follows immediately because peace is the Self's own nature.

4:40.text: The ignorant, the faithless, and the doubting self perishes. For the doubting self there is neither this world, nor the next, nor any happiness.
4:40.transliteration:
ajñaś cāśraddadhānaś ca saṃśayātmā vinaśyati |
nāyaṃ loko 'sti na paro na sukhaṃ saṃśayātmanaḥ ||40||
4:40.devanagari:
अज्ञश्चाश्रद्दधानश्च संशयात्मा विनश्यति ।
नायं लोकोऽस्ति न परो न सुखं संशयात्मनः ॥ ४० ॥
4:40.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:40.explanation: The threefold obstruction is stated with the finality of a medical diagnosis: (1) 'Ajñaḥ' — the ignorant: one in whom neither the question of the Self has arisen, nor the recognition that their fundamental confusion about life has a solution. (2) 'Aśraddadhānaḥ' — the faithless: one who encounters the teaching but cannot open to it; cynicism prevents the receptivity required for transformation. (3) 'Saṃśayātmā' — the one whose very nature is doubt. This is the most serious condition: not productive inquiry-doubt that leads to resolution, but an existential paralysis of indecision. Such a person cannot commit to action, cannot complete a spiritual practice, cannot trust any guidance. 'Vinaśyati' — perishes (not annihilation of the ātman, which is eternal, but ruin of all opportunity). 'Na ayaṃ lokaḥ' — even ordinary life does not go well; the doubter cannot enjoy or act effectively anywhere. Doubt without resolution is the antithesis of the peace described in the previous verse.

4:41.text: Actions do not bind one who has renounced actions through yoga, whose doubts have been completely severed by knowledge, and who is established in the Self, O Dhanañjaya.
4:41.transliteration:
yoga-sannyasta-karmāṇaṃ jñāna-sañchinna-saṃśayam |
ātmavantaṃ na karmāṇi nibadhnanti dhanañjaya ||41||
4:41.devanagari:
योगसन्न्यस्तकर्माणं ज्ञानसञ्छिन्नसंशयम् ।
आत्मवन्तं न कर्माणि निबध्नन्ति धनञ्जय ॥ ४१ ॥
4:41.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:41.explanation: The portrait of the liberated actor — the answer to Chapter 3's question of who acts without bondage. Three characteristics, each indispensable: (1) 'Yoga-sannyasta-karmāṇam' — one who has renounced actions through yoga. Not external renunciation (abandoning duties) but the inner surrender of the claim to results; acting while releasing ownership of action and fruit. (2) 'Jñāna-sañchinna-saṃśayam' — doubts completely severed by knowledge. 'Sañchinna' — cut through completely, not merely suppressed. When the Self is known, the fundamental doubt ('who am I? Am I the body? The mind? The doer?') is resolved at its root. (3) 'Ātmavantam' — established in the Self; the center of gravity has permanently shifted from the ego-personality to the unmoving Self. 'Na karmāṇi nibadhnanti' — actions do not bind because there is no longer an ego claiming to be the agent of those actions. Bondage requires a binder and a bound; knowledge dissolves both.

4:42.text: Therefore, having cut with the sword of Self-knowledge this doubt born of ignorance, which dwells in your heart — stand firm in yoga and rise up, O Bhārata!
4:42.transliteration:
tasmād ajñāna-sambhūtaṃ hṛt-sthaṃ jñānāsinātmanaḥ |
chittvainaṃ saṃśayaṃ yogam ātiṣṭhottiṣṭha bhārata ||42||
4:42.devanagari:
तस्मादज्ञानसम्भूतं हृत्स्थं ज्ञानासिनात्मनः ।
छित्त्वैनं संशयं योगमातिष्ठोत्तिष्ठ भारत ॥ ४२ ॥
4:42.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
4:42.explanation: The magnificent closing verse of Chapter 4 — among the most electrifying calls to action in world spiritual literature. 'Ajñāna-sambhūtam' — born of ignorance: the doubt that paralyzes Arjuna (Chapter 1) is not an intellectual puzzle requiring more information; it is rooted in avidyā — the fundamental misconception of one's identity. 'Hṛt-stham' — dwelling in the heart: unlike intellectual doubt which yields to argument, this existential doubt lives in the heart and can only be resolved by direct experiential knowledge. 'Jñānāsinā' — with the sword of knowledge. The sword metaphor is deliberate: a sword cuts cleanly, completely, once. The half-severed is not severed; jñāna that is merely intellectual is no sword. 'Chittvā' — having cut: the imperative of complete action. 'Yogam ātiṣṭha' — be established in yoga. Then — 'Uttiṣṭha bhārata' — RISE! The ultimate imperative. Knowledge is not meant to produce passivity but the most effective, fearless, liberated action. This is the chapter's — and the Gita's — essence.

5:1.text: Arjuna said: O Krishna, You praise renunciation of actions and then also the yoga of action. Tell me with certainty which one of these two is better.
5:1.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
saṃnyāsaṃ karmaṇāṃ kṛṣṇa punar yogaṃ ca śaṃsasi |
yac chreya etayor ekaṃ tan me brūhi suniścitam ||1||
5:1.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
संन्यासं कर्मणां कृष्ण पुनर्योगं च शंससि ।
यच्छ्रेय एतयोरेकं तन्मे ब्रूहि सुनिश्चितम् ॥ १ ॥
5:1.speaker: Arjuna
5:1.explanation: Arjuna's question is entirely reasonable and practical. In the previous chapters, Krishna has spoken variously about jñāna-niṣṭhā (steadfastness in knowledge), jñāna-yajña (the sacrifice of knowledge), renunciation, and karma yoga — and the profusion of paths has genuinely confused him. He now asks for a clear, decisive verdict: 'suniścitam' (tell me with certainty) — not another philosophical elaboration, but a direct answer. This is the question that every sincere seeker faces: should I leave the world (sannyāsa) or engage it skillfully without attachment (karma yoga)? The tension between these two ideals runs through Indian philosophy, and Krishna's answer here constitutes one of the Gita's most important clarifications. Arjuna's readiness to ask this practical question also signals his growing philosophical maturity.

5:2.text: The Blessed Lord said: Both renunciation and the yoga of action lead to the highest good. But of the two, karma yoga is superior to the renunciation of action.
5:2.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
saṃnyāsaḥ karma-yogaś ca niḥśreyasa-karāv ubhau |
tayos tu karma-saṃnyāsāt karma-yogo viśiṣyate ||2||
5:2.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
संन्यासः कर्मयोगश्च निःश्रेयसकरावुभौ ।
तयोस्तु कर्मसंन्यासात्कर्मयोगो विशिष्यते ॥ २ ॥
5:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:2.explanation: Krishna's opening affirmation — 'niḥśreyasa-karāv ubhau' (both lead to the highest good, liberation) — is important: he does not dismiss sannyāsa. Both paths are valid routes to mokṣa. But the comparative verdict is clear: karma yoga is 'viśiṣyate' — distinctly superior in practical terms. Why? Because genuine sannyāsa requires complete inner renunciation — a total freedom from vāsanā (latent desires) in the mind. A person who merely abandons external action while the mind still seethes with craving has achieved nothing. Karma yoga — acting in the world, fulfilling one's duties, but without attachment to results — is accessible to ordinary householders and warriors alike. This answer liberates the vast majority of human beings from the false belief that spiritual progress requires abandoning worldly life.

5:3.text: He should be known as a perpetual renunciant who neither hates nor desires. For, free from all dualities, O mighty-armed, he is easily liberated from bondage.
5:3.transliteration:
jñeyaḥ sa nitya-saṃnyāsī yo na dveṣṭi na kāṅkṣati |
nirdvandvo hi mahābāho sukhaṃ bandhāt pramucyate ||3||
5:3.devanagari:
ज्ञेयः स नित्यसंन्यासी यो न द्वेष्टि न काङ्क्षति ।
निर्द्वन्द्वो हि महाबाहो सुखं बन्धात्प्रमुच्यते ॥ ३ ॥
5:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:3.explanation: Here Krishna provides his definitive redefinition of sannyāsa. The true renunciant is not one who has externally abandoned possessions, family, or responsibilities — it is one whose mind is free from 'dveṣa' (aversion, hatred) and 'kāṅkṣā' (craving, desire). This person is 'nirdvandva' — untouched by the pairs of opposites: pleasure and pain, honour and dishonour, gain and loss. When the mind neither pushes away nor grasps, it rests in natural equilibrium. 'Sukhaṃ pramucyate' — liberation then comes 'sukham' (easily, pleasantly) — not as a violent wrenching away but as the natural flowering of an unburdened mind. This verse makes clear that sannyāsa is fundamentally an inner state, not an outer circumstance. A renunciant can wear ochre robes in a monastery yet be seething with subtle desires; a householder at the busiest intersection of life can be a nityasannyāsī.

5:4.text: Children, not the wise, declare Sāṃkhya and Yoga to be different. One who is properly established in even one of them obtains the fruit of both.
5:4.transliteration:
sāṃkhya-yogau pṛthag bālāḥ pravadanti na paṇḍitāḥ |
ekam apy āsthitaḥ samyag ubhayor vindate phalam ||4||
5:4.devanagari:
सांख्ययोगौ पृथग्बालाः प्रवदन्ति न पण्डिताः ।
एकमप्यास्थितः सम्यगुभयोर्विन्दते फलम् ॥ ४ ॥
5:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:4.explanation: 'Bālāḥ' (children, the intellectually immature) see Sāṃkhya (the path of knowledge and discrimination) and Yoga (the path of action) as fundamentally opposed systems. 'Na paṇḍitāḥ' — the truly learned do not see it this way. The apparent opposition is superficial: Sāṃkhya works inwardly through discernment and withdrawal of identification with matter; Karma yoga works outwardly through detached action — but both converge on the same truth: the dissolution of ego-driven action and the realization of the Self. 'Samyag' (properly, rightly) is the crucial qualifier — not mere external adherence to one path, but genuine inner alignment with its principle. A karma yogi who acts in full non-attachment is living the same truth as the jñānī who has seen through the illusion of doership. The fruit is the same: liberation. This verse counsels seekers against the trap of path-obsession — endlessly debating which path is superior rather than walking any path fully.

5:5.text: The state attained by the Sāṃkhyas is also reached by the Yogis. He who sees Sāṃkhya and Yoga as one — he truly sees.
5:5.transliteration:
yat sāṃkhyaiḥ prāpyate sthānaṃ tad yogair api gamyate |
ekaṃ sāṃkhyaṃ ca yogaṃ ca yaḥ paśyati sa paśyati ||5||
5:5.devanagari:
यत्सांख्यैः प्राप्यते स्थानं तद्योगैरपि गम्यते ।
एकं सांख्यं च योगं च यः पश्यति स पश्यति ॥ ५ ॥
5:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:5.explanation: This verse crystallises the philosophical unity of the two great paths. 'Yat sāṃkhyaiḥ prāpyate sthānam' — the 'sthāna' (the final state, the supreme station) reached through Sāṃkhya's path of discriminative knowledge is identical to that reached through the Yoga of action. The destination is the same; only the approaches differ. The magnificent declaration 'yaḥ paśyati sa paśyati' — 'he who sees thus, he truly sees' — is characteristic of the Gita's style of praising the knower who has transcended apparent contradictions and perceived the deeper unity. The person who sees that jñāna-niṣṭhā and karma yoga are two faces of the same truth has arrived at genuine philosophical vision. This is not intellectual reconciliation — it is the direct perception of non-duality operating even within apparent diversity of methods.

5:6.text: But renunciation, O mighty-armed, is difficult to attain without yoga. The sage who is united through yoga reaches Brahman without delay.
5:6.transliteration:
saṃnyāsas tu mahābāho duḥkham āptum ayogataḥ |
yoga-yukto munir brahma na cireṇādhigacchati ||6||
5:6.devanagari:
संन्यासस्तु महाबाहो दुःखमाप्तुमयोगतः ।
योगयुक्तो मुनिर्ब्रह्म नचिरेणाधिगच्छति ॥ ६ ॥
5:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:6.explanation: 'Ayogataḥ' — without the inner yoga of non-attachment. Krishna is being precise: external renunciation without the inner preparation of karma yoga is 'duḥkham āptum' — painful and difficult to sustain. A mind still dense with vāsanās (latent impressions and desires) cannot simply abandon action and abide in Brahman — the unfulfilled desires will resurface with greater force in the silence of renunciation. The karma yogi, however — by continuously acting without self-interest — gradually purifies the mind, weakens the ego's grip, and arrives organically at the same inner state that a formal sannyāsī seeks through renunciation. 'Na cireṇa' — without much delay, swiftly — the yoga-yukta muni attains Brahman. Karma yoga is thus not merely an alternative to jñāna-sannyāsa but its natural preparation and doorway.

5:7.text: One who is united in yoga, pure in heart, master of the self, with senses controlled, and who sees his own Self in all beings — though acting, he is not bound.
5:7.transliteration:
yoga-yukto viśuddhātmā vijitātmā jitendriyaḥ |
sarva-bhūtātma-bhūtātmā kurvann api na lipyate ||7||
5:7.devanagari:
योगयुक्तो विशुद्धात्मा विजितात्मा जितेन्द्रियः ।
सर्वभूतात्मभूतात्मा कुर्वन्नपि न लिप्यते ॥ ७ ॥
5:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:7.explanation: This verse describes four defining qualities of the karma yogi who acts without incurring bondage. First, 'viśuddhātmā' — pure inner being, free from the pollutants of passion and ignorance. Second, 'vijitātmā' — one who has conquered the self, meaning the lower mind and the ego that falsely claims authorship of action. Third, 'jitendriyaḥ' — with senses disciplined and not driven by craving. Fourth, and most profoundly, 'sarva-bhūtātma-bhūtātmā' — one whose very ātman has become the ātman of all beings, who perceives the same Self everywhere. For such a person, the distinction between 'actor' and 'action' dissolves. He acts, but the 'I am doing' sense — the ahaṅkāra of doership — is absent. Without this ahaṅkāra, no karmic residue accumulates. 'Kurvann api na lipyate' — though constantly acting, he remains untouched, as a mirror reflects everything without retaining any image.

5:8.text: The one who is united, who knows the truth, thinks 'I do nothing at all' — even while seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, walking, sleeping, and breathing.
5:8.transliteration:
naiva kiñcit karomīti yukto manyeta tattva-vit |
paśyañ śṛṇvan spṛśañ jighrann aśnan gacchan svapan śvasan ||8||
5:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:8.explanation: This verse and the next form one of the most philosophically profound passages in the Gita. The 'tattva-vit' — the knower of reality — has realized the fundamental distinction between ātman (pure consciousness) and prakṛti (nature with its instruments: body, senses, mind). From the standpoint of this realized knowledge, all activities — seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, walking, sleeping, breathing — are functions of the sense-organs and the body, the instruments of prakṛti. The ātman is the witness-consciousness, the pure Seer that illuminates all these activities but performs none of them. 'Naiva kiñcit karomi' — 'I truly do nothing' — is not a psychological attitude artificially assumed; it is a metaphysical insight actually seen by the jñānī. The ego (ahaṅkāra), which falsely grafts 'I' onto the body's actions, has been seen through. What remains is pure, actionless awareness.

5:9.text: Even while speaking, releasing, grasping, opening or closing the eyes — holding firm the conviction that 'the senses are moving among their sense-objects; I am not doing.'
5:9.transliteration:
pralapan visṛjan gṛhṇann unmiṣan nimiṣann api |
indriyāṇīndriyārtheṣu vartanta iti dhārayan ||9||
5:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:9.explanation: Continuing seamlessly from verse 8, this verse completes the catalogue of activities: speaking (the function of vāk), releasing/excreting (bodily processes), grasping/handling, even the involuntary blinking of eyes — all these are functions of 'indriyāṇi' (the senses and bodily instruments) moving among 'indriyārtha' (their respective objects). The key word is 'dhārayan' — holding firm as a steady conviction, not merely as an occasional thought. This is the experiential state of the jñānī or mature karma yogi: fully engaged in all activities of life, yet permanently grounded in the witness-awareness that sees the body-mind complex as an instrument of nature functioning according to its own laws. The 'I' — the real ātman — remains untouched, doing nothing, being everything. This understanding does not paralyze action; on the contrary, it enables the most natural and effortless action because the ego's anxious interference has ceased.

5:10.text: One who acts by offering all actions to Brahman, abandoning attachment, is not tainted by sin, just as a lotus leaf is untouched by water.
5:10.transliteration:
brahmaṇy ādhāya karmāṇi saṅgaṃ tyaktvā karoti yaḥ |
lipyate na sa pāpena padma-patram ivāmbhasā ||10||
5:10.devanagari:
ब्रह्मण्याधाय कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा करोति यः ।
लिप्यते न स पापेन पद्मपत्रमिवाम्भसा ॥ १० ॥
5:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:10.explanation: 'Brahmaṇy ādhāya karmāṇi' — offering all actions to Brahman. This is the foundational attitude of karma yoga: every act becomes a yajña (sacrifice), an offering to the divine. There is no personal agenda, no ego-stake in the outcome. 'Saṅgaṃ tyaktvā' — releasing attachment, both to results and to the sense of 'I am the doer.' One who maintains this inner orientation is 'na lipyate pāpena' — not bound by the residue of action, what we might call karmic consequence. Then comes one of the Gita's most celebrated and beautiful similes: 'padma-patram ivāmbhasā' — like a lotus leaf in water. The lotus is born in water, grows in water, lives in water — yet its leaves are always dry, perfectly unwettable, repelling every drop. This is the ideal state of the karma yogi: fully immersed in the world — working, relating, responding — yet never contaminated by it, never leaving residue on the inner Self.

5:11.text: Yogis perform action by body, mind, intellect, and even by the senses alone, having abandoned attachment — for the purification of the self.
5:11.transliteration:
kāyena manasā buddhyā kevalair indriyair api |
yoginaḥ karma kurvanti saṅgaṃ tyaktvātma-śuddhaye ||11||
5:11.devanagari:
कायेन मनसा बुद्ध्या केवलैरिन्द्रियैरपि ।
योगिनः कर्म कुर्वन्ति सङ्गं त्यक्त्वात्मशुद्धये ॥ ११ ॥
5:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:11.explanation: Krishna enumerates the four instruments through which the yogi acts: kāya (the gross body), manas (the emotional mind), buddhi (the discriminating intellect), and kevalair indriyaiḥ (the senses alone — purely automatic bodily functions like breathing or reflexes, without the mind's involvement). 'Kevalaiḥ' is significant — meaning 'merely, purely' — indicating that even involuntary bodily functions are included in the yogi's field of action. The critical differentiator is the purpose: 'ātma-śuddhaye' — for the purification of the inner being. Every action, from the grandest to the most involuntary, when performed without attachment and dedicated to inner refinement rather than personal enjoyment, becomes a spiritual practice. Karma yoga thus transforms the entirety of daily life into a path of purification — nothing is outside of it.

5:12.text: The one who is united abandons the fruit of action and attains lasting peace. The one who is not united, driven by desire, attached to results, is bound.
5:12.transliteration:
yuktaḥ karma-phalaṃ tyaktvā śāntim āpnoti naiṣṭhikīm |
ayuktaḥ kāma-kāreṇa phale sakto nibadhyate ||12||
5:12.devanagari:
युक्तः कर्मफलं त्यक्त्वा शान्तिमाप्नोति नैष्ठिकीम् ।
अयुक्तः कामकारेण फले सक्तो निबध्यते ॥ १२ ॥
5:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:12.explanation: This verse delivers one of the Gita's most compact and powerful contrasts. The 'yukta' (the integrated, yoga-united person) who releases attachment to results attains 'naiṣṭhikī śāntim' — a peace that is 'naiṣṭhikī': final, foundational, unshakeable — not the temporary relief of desire-fulfilment but the abiding stillness of a mind no longer agitated by wanting. The 'ayukta' (the disintegrated, yoga-less person), driven by 'kāma-kāreṇa' (the compulsion of desire, the pressure of craving), and remaining 'phale saktaḥ' (clinging to results), is 'nibadhyate' — bound. This bondage is not a punishment from outside — it is the natural mechanics of attachment: one who invests identity in outcomes is enslaved to them, perpetually anxious before they arrive, perpetually bereft when they leave. This is the root of saṃsāra — the cycle of grasping and loss.

5:13.text: Having renounced all actions mentally, the self-controlled embodied one dwells happily in the nine-gated city, neither acting nor causing action.
5:13.transliteration:
sarva-karmāṇi manasā saṃnyasyāste sukhaṃ vaśī |
nava-dvāre pure dehī naiva kurvan na kārayan ||13||
5:13.devanagari:
सर्वकर्माणि मनसा संन्यस्यास्ते सुखं वशी ।
नवद्वारे पुरे देही नैव कुर्वन्न कारयन् ॥ १३ ॥
5:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:13.explanation: 'Nava-dvāre pure' — the nine-gated city is a richly poetic designation for the human body. The nine gates are the two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, the mouth, the anus, and the generative organ — the nine orifices through which the self interacts with the external world. 'Vaśī' — one who has mastered the self, who is not dragged by the senses through any of these gates into reactive compulsion. 'Manasā saṃnyasya' — mentally renouncing all actions: this is the interior gesture, not the exterior one. The jñānī lives in the body-city, uses all its faculties, but has no investment, no agency-claim, no ownership. 'Naiva kurvan na kārayan' — neither doing nor causing to be done — the ego-driven sense of authorship has completely dissolved. He inhabits the body as a guest inhabits a city: moving through it freely, using its facilities, but claiming nothing as his own.

5:14.text: The Lord (the Self) neither creates the agency of people, nor their actions, nor the connection of action with its fruit. It is nature (svabhāva) that moves and acts.
5:14.transliteration:
na kartṛtvaṃ na karmāṇi lokasya sṛjati prabhuḥ |
na karma-phala-saṃyogaṃ svabhāvas tu pravartate ||14||
5:14.devanagari:
न कर्तृत्वं न कर्माणि लोकस्य सृजति प्रभुः ।
न कर्मफलसंयोगं स्वभावस्तु प्रवर्तते ॥ १४ ॥
5:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:14.explanation: This verse addresses a profound philosophical question: if the ātman (the Self, prabhu) neither acts nor causes action, who is responsible for human agency, deeds, and their consequences? The answer: 'svabhāvas tu pravartate' — it is svabhāva (one's own nature — the accumulated guṇas, vāsanās, and saṃskāras of prakṛti) that sets everything in motion. The Lord — whether understood as the universal Self (Brahman) or the individual ātman in its true nature — creates neither kartṛtva (the sense of being a doer), nor the actions themselves, nor the karmic linkage between action and result. These all belong to the domain of prakṛti and its three guṇas. The ātman is pure witness-consciousness: its mere presence illuminates the activities of prakṛti, the way sunlight enables all activity on earth without itself moving or participating. This is the Advaita-Vedāntic understanding of divine non-doership.

5:15.text: The all-pervading Lord neither takes on anyone's sin nor their merit. Knowledge is veiled by ignorance — that is why creatures fall into delusion.
5:15.transliteration:
nādatte kasyacit pāpaṃ na caiva sukṛtaṃ vibhuḥ |
ajñānenāvṛtaṃ jñānaṃ tena muhyanti jantavaḥ ||15||
5:15.devanagari:
नादत्ते कस्यचित्पापं न चैव सुकृतं विभुः ।
अज्ञानेनावृतं ज्ञानं तेन मुह्यन्ति जन्तवः ॥ १५ ॥
5:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:15.explanation: 'Nādatte' — the all-pervading vibhu (the Self, Brahman) neither 'takes on' (receives, accumulates) anyone's sin nor anyone's virtue. This is a striking theological statement: God is not a moral accountant who collects karmic debits and credits. The cosmos runs on the impersonal law of karma (cause and effect), not on divine reward and punishment. Then why does suffering persist? Why do beings err? 'Ajñānenāvṛtaṃ jñānam' — because the natural knowing (jñāna, the self-luminous awareness of the ātman) is covered over by ajñāna (ignorance, avidyā). This concealment is the root cause of all confusion.
