The ātman is intrinsically radiant and knowing — but the veil of ignorance makes the jīva (individual self) misidentify with the body, claim doership, desire results, and thereby bind itself. 'Tena muhyanti jantavaḥ' — this is why all creatures are bewildered. The solution is not moral improvement alone but the removal of this fundamental ignorance through jñāna.

5:16.text: But for those whose ignorance of the Self has been destroyed by knowledge, that knowledge, like the sun, illuminates the supreme truth.
5:16.transliteration:
jñānena tu tad ajñānaṃ yeṣāṃ nāśitam ātmanaḥ |
teṣām āditya-vaj jñānaṃ prakāśayati tat param ||16||
5:16.devanagari:
ज्ञानेन तु तदज्ञानं येषां नाशितमात्मनः ।
तेषामादित्यवज्ज्ञानं प्रकाशयति तत्परम् ॥ १६ ॥
5:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:16.explanation: This is the luminous answer to the problem posed in verse 15. 'Jñānena tu tad ajñānaṃ nāśitam' — for those in whom that ignorance of the Self has been destroyed by knowledge — not suppressed, not bypassed, but truly destroyed (nāśitam). The simile is breathtaking: 'āditya-vaj jñānam' — that knowledge shines like the sun (āditya). The sun does not merely dispel darkness — it reveals the entire world in its true form, making visible what was always there but unseen. Similarly, ātma-jñāna does not 'create' Brahman — Brahman was always present, always the ground of all experience. What jñāna does is remove the veil of ajñāna and allow the self-luminous nature of the ātman to illumine 'tat param' — that supreme reality, which was always the case. The ātman is self-luminous; it only needed the clouds of ignorance to clear. No effort is needed to make the sun shine — only to remove the obscuration.

5:17.text: Those whose intellect is absorbed in That, whose very self is That, whose steadfastness is in That, whose supreme goal is That — their impurities cleansed by knowledge, they go to non-return.
5:17.transliteration:
tad-buddhayas tad-ātmānas tan-niṣṭhās tat-parāyaṇāḥ |
gacchanty apunar-āvṛttiṃ jñāna-nirdhūta-kalmaṣāḥ ||17||
5:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:17.explanation: This verse presents a fourfold portrait of the liberated person in beautifully escalating depth: (1) 'tad-buddhayas' — whose intellect rests in That (Brahman), meaning their thinking faculty is oriented toward and illuminated by Brahman; (2) 'tad-ātmānas' — whose very self IS That, the deepest level of identification dissolved in Brahman; (3) 'tan-niṣṭhāḥ' — firmly established and grounded in That, unshaken; (4) 'tat-parāyaṇāḥ' — whose sole refuge, goal, and ultimate movement is toward That. This is a spectrum from intellectual orientation to total being-absorption. 'Jñāna-nirdhūta-kalmaṣāḥ' — their impurities (moral stains, ignorance, subtle vāsanās) washed away by knowledge, as fire purifies gold of its dross. 'Apunar-āvṛttim gacchanti' — they go to non-return: they attain mokṣa, liberation from the cycle of rebirth. This is the supreme fruit of jñāna yoga.

5:18.text: The wise see with equal vision a learned and humble brāhmaṇa, a cow, an elephant, a dog, and even an outcast (dog-cooker).
5:18.transliteration:
vidyā-vinaya-sampanne brāhmaṇe gavi hastini |
śuni caiva śvapāke ca paṇḍitāḥ sama-darśinaḥ ||18||
5:18.devanagari:
विद्याविनयसम्पन्ने ब्राह्मणे गवि हस्तिनि ।
शुनि चैव श्वपाके च पण्डिताः समदर्शिनः ॥ १८ ॥
5:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:18.explanation: This is among the most radical and universally resonant verses of the Gita. Krishna defines the wisdom-vision (sama-darśana) of the jñānī through a deliberately extreme spectrum. At the highest rung of social and intellectual standing: 'vidyā-vinaya-sampanne brāhmaṇe' — a brāhmaṇa endowed with both learning and humility. Then animals: a cow (sacred and revered), an elephant (regal and powerful). Then at the lowest: a dog (considered impure) and a śvapāka — a 'dog-cooker,' one who ate dog flesh, the lowest of the low in the ancient social hierarchy. The sage sees the same Self — the same ātman, the same Brahman — shining through all these radically different forms. 'Sama-darśinaḥ' — seeing equally — is not naive blindness to the real differences in nature and capability between a scholar and a stray dog. It is the perception of the single luminous consciousness that animates them all. This is not social equality theory — it is metaphysical non-duality directly perceived.

5:19.text: Even here, by those whose minds are established in equality, the entire creation is conquered. Since Brahman is flawless and equal, they are therefore established in Brahman.
5:19.transliteration:
ihaiva tair jitaḥ sargo yeṣāṃ sāmye sthitaṃ manaḥ |
nirdoṣaṃ hi samaṃ brahma tasmād brahmaṇi te sthitāḥ ||19||
5:19.devanagari:
इहैव तैर्जितः सर्गो येषां साम्ये स्थितं मनः ।
निर्दोषं हि समं ब्रह्म तस्माद्ब्रह्मणि ते स्थिताः ॥ १९ ॥
5:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:19.explanation: 'Ihaiva' — even here, in this life itself, not after death. Those whose minds are anchored in 'sāmya' (equanimity, evenness, non-discrimination based on attachment or aversion) have 'jitaḥ sargaḥ' — conquered creation, which here means the cycle of conditioned existence (saṃsāra), the perpetual oscillation between hope and fear, gain and loss, birth and death. This conquest does not require the world to disappear — it means the world's power to determine one's inner state is broken. Then a beautiful equation: 'Brahman is nirdoṣam (without blemish, imperfection, partiality) and samam (equal, undifferentiated).' The person of equanimity mirrors exactly these qualities of Brahman. Therefore, by the principle of similarity, 'brahmaṇi te sthitāḥ' — they dwell in Brahman. Equanimity is not merely a psychological virtue; it is the functional signature of brahma-jñāna.

5:20.text: One who does not rejoice on obtaining the pleasant, nor becomes troubled on obtaining the unpleasant — such a one is of steady intellect, undeluded, a knower of Brahman, established in Brahman.
5:20.transliteration:
na prahṛṣyet priyaṃ prāpya nodvijet prāpya cāpriyam |
sthira-buddhir asammūḍho brahma-vid brahmaṇi sthitaḥ ||20||
5:20.devanagari:
न प्रहृष्येत्प्रियं प्राप्य नोद्विजेत्प्राप्य चाप्रियम् ।
स्थिरबुद्धिरसम्मूढो ब्रह्मविद्ब्रह्मणि स्थितः ॥ २० ॥
5:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:20.explanation: This verse describes the practical emotional signature of the brahma-vid (knower of Brahman) in daily life. The test is simple but penetrating: 'na prahṛṣyet priyaṃ prāpya' — does not become elated when something pleasant arrives. 'Nodvijet prāpya cāpriyam' — does not become troubled or shaken when something unpleasant arrives. This person is 'sthira-buddhi' (of stable, unshaking intellect): their inner ground is not destabilized by the weather of outer events. 'Asammūḍhaḥ' — undeluded: they have clearly seen that all pleasant and unpleasant experiences are impermanent modifications of prakṛti and cannot touch the permanent self. The deluded person, by contrast, is constantly thrown between elation and despair by the inevitable alternation of life's pleasures and pains. This verse echoes and deepens the description of the 'sthita-prajña' (person of steady wisdom) in Chapter 2. The knower of Brahman is recognizable not by what they know but by how they live.

5:21.text: One whose inner self is unattached to external sense-contacts finds the joy that is in the Self. That one, whose self is united in Brahma-yoga, tastes imperishable joy.
5:21.transliteration:
bāhya-sparśeṣv asaktātmā vindaty ātmani yat sukham |
sa brahma-yoga-yuktātmā sukham akṣayam aśnute ||21||
5:21.devanagari:
बाह्यस्पर्शेष्वसक्तात्मा विन्दत्यात्मनि यत्सुखम् ।
स ब्रह्मयोगयुक्तात्मा सुखमक्षयमश्नुते ॥ २१ ॥
5:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:21.explanation: 'Bāhya-sparśeṣu asaktātmā' — one whose inner being (ātmā used here for antaḥkaraṇa, the inner mind) is not hooked onto external sense-contacts. This does not mean avoidance of sensory experience — it means non-adhesion: the senses function, make contact, receive their inputs, but the inner self does not cling. 'Vindaty ātmani yat sukham' — such a person discovers the joy that exists within the Self itself. This is the crucial discovery: there is a joy inherent to ātman, a bliss that is not dependent on any object, situation, or relationship. This is ānanda — the 'ā' of sac-cid-ānanda (being-consciousness-bliss) that is the intrinsic nature of Brahman. Once tasted, this ātma-sukha makes sense-pleasures appear pallid by comparison. 'Sukham akṣayam' — imperishable joy: because it is not contingent on anything that can be taken away, it cannot be lost. The external world's pleasures are all 'kṣaya' (perishable); the joy of the ātman is 'akṣaya.'

5:22.text: The enjoyments born of sense-contact are sources of sorrow alone, O son of Kuntī. They have a beginning and an end — the wise do not delight in them.
5:22.transliteration:
ye hi saṃsparśajā bhogā duḥkha-yonaya eva te |
ādy-antavantaḥ kaunteya na teṣu ramate budhaḥ ||22||
5:22.devanagari:
ये हि संस्पर्शजा भोगा दुःखयोनय एव ते ।
आद्यन्तवन्तः कौन्तेय न तेषु रमते बुधः ॥ २२ ॥
5:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:22.explanation: 'Saṃsparśajā bhogāḥ' — the pleasures arising from sense-contact: every experience of delight that comes through the meeting of a sense-organ with its object — taste, sight, sound, touch, smell. Krishna's verdict is unambiguous: 'duḥkha-yonaya eva te' — these are sources of sorrow alone. This appears counter-intuitive — pleasures are sources of sorrow? The reason follows: 'ādy-antavantaḥ' — they have a beginning and an end. They are inherently impermanent. This temporal limitation is the source of their suffering-potential: the fear of losing them before they arrive, the anxiety of maintaining them, and the grief of their inevitable departure. Even in the midst of pleasure, the certainty of its ending casts a shadow. 'Na teṣu ramate budhaḥ' — the wise person (budha) does not 'ramate' — does not rest in them, does not find their ground in them. This is not ascetic hatred of pleasure; it is clear-sighted discernment (viveka) about where enduring joy can and cannot be found.

5:23.text: One who can withstand, even here in this life, before liberation from the body, the impulse born of desire and anger — that one is integrated (yukta), that one is a happy person.
5:23.transliteration:
śaknotīhaiva yaḥ soḍhuṃ prāk śarīra-vimokṣaṇāt |
kāma-krodhodbhavaṃ vegaṃ sa yuktaḥ sa sukhī naraḥ ||23||
5:23.devanagari:
शक्नोतीहैव यः सोढुं प्राक्शरीरविमोक्षणात् ।
कामक्रोधोद्भवं वेगं स युक्तः स सुखी नरः ॥ २३ ॥
5:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:23.explanation: 'Ihaiva' — even here, in this life, not some future liberation after death. 'Prāk śarīra-vimokṣaṇāt' — before the release from the body, i.e., during the course of one's present life. 'Kāma-krodhodbhavaṃ vegam' — the impulse (vega: the surging force, the momentum) that arises from kāma (desire, craving) and krodha (anger). These two are named in Chapter 3 (3.37) as the arch-enemies of human beings, born of rajas (the quality of passion and agitation). 'Soḍhum' — to withstand, to bear, to endure without being swept away. This is not suppression or repression — it is the capacity to feel the arising of desire or anger, to observe its force, and to not act from it blindly. 'Sa yuktaḥ' — that person is 'yukta,' truly integrated. 'Sa sukhī naraḥ' — that person is genuinely happy. True happiness is not the absence of difficulty but the inner stability that holds firm when desire and anger surge. This is the practical Gita applied in the crucible of daily life.

5:24.text: One who is happy within, who finds delight within, who is illumined within — that yogi, becoming Brahman, attains nirvāṇa in Brahman.
5:24.transliteration:
yo 'ntaḥ-sukho 'ntar-ārāmas tathāntar-jyotir eva yaḥ |
sa yogī brahma-nirvāṇaṃ brahma-bhūto 'dhigacchati ||24||
5:24.devanagari:
योऽन्तःसुखोऽन्तरारामस्तथान्तर्ज्योतिरेव यः ।
स योगी ब्रह्मनिर्वाणं ब्रह्मभूतोऽधिगच्छति ॥ २४ ॥
5:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:24.explanation: This verse describes the brahma-nirvāṇa state through three beautifully inward-pointing qualifiers. 'Antaḥ-sukhaḥ' — whose happiness is interior, whose joy does not depend on any external object or event. 'Antar-ārāmaḥ' — who finds their delight, play, and recreation within: the person who is never bored in solitude, who needs no external entertainment to be at ease. 'Antar-jyotiḥ' — whose light (intelligence, illumination, consciousness) is entirely self-generating from within, not dependent on external stimulation, information, or validation. These three describe someone who has become entirely self-sufficient in the deepest sense — not in the ego's sense of independence, but in the ātman's intrinsic completeness. 'Brahma-bhūtaḥ' — having become Brahman (the Brahman that they always were, now consciously realized). 'Brahma-nirvāṇam adhigacchati' — attains nirvāṇa in Brahman. The word 'nirvāṇa' (literally: extinction, blowing out) refers to the extinction of the individual ego-flame in the infinite ocean of Brahman.

5:25.text: Seers who have eliminated their impurities, whose doubts have been cut through, who are self-restrained, and who are devoted to the welfare of all beings — they attain nirvāṇa in Brahman.
5:25.transliteration:
labhante brahma-nirvāṇam ṛṣayaḥ kṣīṇa-kalmaṣāḥ |
chinna-dvaidhā yatātmānaḥ sarva-bhūta-hite ratāḥ ||25||
5:25.devanagari:
लभन्ते ब्रह्मनिर्वाणमृषयः क्षीणकल्मषाः ।
छिन्नद्वैधा यतात्मानः सर्वभूतहिते रताः ॥ २५ ॥
5:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:25.explanation: This verse identifies four qualities that characterize those who attain brahma-nirvāṇa. (1) 'Kṣīṇa-kalmaṣāḥ' — those whose impurities (moral stains, the accumulated dross of selfish action and passion) have been worn away. This refers to the purification of the antaḥkaraṇa (inner instrument) through sādhana. (2) 'Chinna-dvaidhāḥ' — those whose dvaidha (duality of mind, doubt, vacillation) has been severed — decisively, not merely paused. The sword of knowledge has cut through all wavering. (3) 'Yatātmānaḥ' — self-disciplined, those who have undertaken and maintained inner regulation. (4) Most beautifully: 'sarva-bhūta-hite ratāḥ' — those who take delight and are engaged in the welfare of all beings. This last quality is crucial: brahma-nirvāṇa is not an isolationist's achievement. The person who has realized Brahman, which IS all beings, naturally finds themself devoted to the flourishing of all. This is the natural compassion born of non-dual vision — the Mahāyāna bodhisattva ideal meets Advaita Vedānta here.

5:26.text: For the self-disciplined ascetics who are free from desire and anger, who have restrained the mind and have known the Self — brahma-nirvāṇa surrounds them on all sides.
5:26.transliteration:
kāma-krodha-viyuktānāṃ yatīnāṃ yata-cetasām |
abhito brahma-nirvāṇaṃ vartate viditātmanām ||26||
5:26.devanagari:
कामक्रोधवियुक्तानां यतीनां यतचेतसाम् ।
अभितो ब्रह्मनिर्वाणं वर्तते विदितात्मनाम् ॥ २६ ॥
5:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:26.explanation: 'Yatīnāṃ' — the yatis, the disciplined striving ones, those who maintain the discipline of inner restraint. 'Kāma-krodha-viyuktānāṃ' — those completely separated from desire and anger: not merely managing them occasionally but fundamentally disjoined from them as habitual patterns. 'Yata-cetasām' — those who have restrained and disciplined the mind-stuff (citta). 'Viditātmanām' — those who have known the Self: this is the ātma-jñāna, the direct knowledge of one's own nature as pure consciousness. For such persons, 'abhito brahma-nirvāṇam vartate' — brahma-nirvāṇa 'abides all around them' — on every side, near and immediate. This is a radical statement: liberation is not a destination to be reached after immense effort and distance — it is the ever-present ground of existence, recognized the moment the veils of desire, anger, and ignorance are removed. Mokṣa was never absent; it was always already here. Only our disqualifications made it appear far.

5:27.text: Having excluded external sense-contacts, fixing the gaze between the two eyebrows, having equalized the inward and outward breaths moving within the nostrils —
5:27.transliteration:
sparśān kṛtvā bahir bāhyāṃś cakṣuś caivāntare bhruvoḥ |
prāṇāpānau samau kṛtvā nāsābhyantara-cāriṇau ||27||
5:27.devanagari:
स्पर्शान्कृत्वा बहिर्बाह्यांश्चक्षुश्चैवान्तरे भ्रुवोः ।
प्राणापानौ समौ कृत्वा नासाभ्यन्तरचारिणौ ॥ २७ ॥
5:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:27.explanation: This verse and the next form one of the Gita's brief but important instructions in meditative practice — a condensed description of dhāraṇā (concentration) and prāṇāyāma (breath regulation) from the eight-limbed path (aṣṭāṅga yoga) tradition. 'Sparśān kṛtvā bahir bāhyān' — having placed external sense-objects outside: this is pratyāhāra (withdrawal of the senses), the fifth limb of Patañjali's yoga — turning the senses inward, away from external stimulation. 'Cakṣuś caivāntare bhruvoḥ' — fixing the gaze between the two eyebrows: this refers to bhrūmadhya, the point between the eyebrows associated with the ājñā cakra, a classical point of inner concentration known to all yogic systems. 'Prāṇāpānau samau kṛtvā' — equalizing prāṇa (exhalation) and apāna (inhalation): this is the foundational prāṇāyāma instruction of stabilizing and balancing the breath — the physical gateway to mental stillness. 'Nāsābhyantara-cāriṇau' — as they move within the nostrils: awareness is brought to the sensation of breath at the nasal passages, a classic anchor of mindful breath-observation. The verse ends in the middle of a sentence, continued in verse 28.

5:28.text: The sage who has controlled the senses, mind, and intellect, who is intent on liberation, who is free from desire, fear, and anger — such a one is always and forever free.
5:28.transliteration:
yatendriya-mano-buddhir munir mokṣa-parāyaṇaḥ |
vigata-icchā-bhaya-krodho yaḥ sadā mukta eva saḥ ||28||
5:28.devanagari:
यतेन्द्रियमनोबुद्धिर्मुनिर्मोक्षपरायणः ।
विगतेच्छाभयक्रोधो यः सदा मुक्त एव सः ॥ २८ ॥
5:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:28.explanation: This verse completes the meditative instruction of verse 27 and describes the fully realized state of the mokṣa-sādhaka (seeker of liberation). 'Yatendriya-mano-buddhi' — with controlled senses, mind (manas: the emotional-volitional faculty), and buddhi (intellect, discernment): these are the three graduated inner faculties, from outermost (senses) to deepest (buddhi). When all three are mastered, the practitioner is complete. 'Munir mokṣa-parāyaṇaḥ' — the muni (one who contemplates, who has taken the vow of inner silence) whose sole orientation is liberation. 'Vigata-icchā-bhaya-krodhaḥ' — free from the triad of icchā (desire), bhaya (fear), and krodha (anger): these three are the fundamental drivers of bondage. Desire reaches for what it wants; fear clings to what it has; anger strikes at what obstructs. When these three forces are dissolved, the root of saṃsāra is cut. 'Sā mukta eva saḥ' — 'that one is always-already liberated' — present tense, not future aspiration. This is jīvanmukti — liberation while living in the body, not waiting for death.

5:29.text: Knowing Me as the enjoyer of all sacrifices and austerities, the Great Lord of all worlds, and the selfless friend of all beings — one attains peace.
5:29.transliteration:
bhoktāraṃ yajña-tapasāṃ sarva-loka-maheśvaram |
suhṛdaṃ sarva-bhūtānāṃ jñātvā māṃ śāntim ṛcchati ||29||
5:29.devanagari:
भोक्तारं यज्ञतपसां सर्वलोकमहेश्वरम् ।
सुहृदं सर्वभूतानां ज्ञात्वा मां शान्तिमृच्छति ॥ २९ ॥
5:29.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
5:29.explanation: This is the magnificent closing verse of Chapter 5 — a direct divine self-revelation in three proclamations, and the key to peace. (1) 'Bhoktāraṃ yajña-tapasāṃ' — the enjoyer (ultimate recipient) of all yajñas (sacrifices, acts of worship) and tapas (austerities, disciplines). Every act of sacrifice and spiritual striving, if rightly understood, ultimately flows toward and is received by the Divine. One who does not know this may perform yajña but binds themselves to the yajña's limited results (see 9.24 for the fuller elaboration). (2) 'Sarva-loka-maheśvaram' — the Great Lord (Maheśvara) of all worlds, all dimensions of existence: this is a declaration of absolute cosmic sovereignty. The Brahman that the Gita's philosophy describes as the impersonal ground of being is here revealed to be simultaneously the personal Lord of all creation. (3) 'Suhṛdaṃ sarva-bhūtānāṃ' — the selfless, unconditional friend of all beings. 'Suhṛd' is a friend who seeks no return: the Divine's friendship toward every creature is absolute and without condition. 'Jñātvā māṃ śāntim ṛcchati' — knowing Me thus, one 'ṛcchati' (attains, arrives at) śānti — the peace that Chapter 5 has been building toward: not the temporary relief of a satisfied desire, but the unshakeable peace of one who rests in the ultimate truth.

6:1.text: The Blessed Lord said: One who performs the action that ought to be done without depending on the fruit of action — that person is both a sannyāsī and a yogī. Not the one who has abandoned fire, nor the one who has abandoned action.
6:1.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
anāśritaḥ karma-phalaṃ kāryaṃ karma karoti yaḥ |
sa saṃnyāsī ca yogī ca na niragnir na cākriyaḥ ||1||
6:1.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
अनाश्रितः कर्मफलं कार्यं कर्म करोति यः ।
स संन्यासी च योगी च न निरग्निर्न चाक्रियः ॥ १ ॥
6:1.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:1.explanation: Chapter 6 opens with a definitional revolution. 'Anāśritaḥ karma-phalam' — one who does not take refuge in, does not lean upon, the fruit of action. 'Kāryaṃ karma' — the action that must be done, one's duty. Such a person is simultaneously 'saṃnyāsī ca yogī ca' — both a renunciant AND a yogi. The negations are equally important: 'niragnir' (one who has merely abandoned the sacrificial fire — the external badge of the sannyāsī) and 'akriyaḥ' (one who has simply ceased all activity) — these external markers alone do not constitute true sannyāsa or yoga. The Gita here cuts through centuries of potential misunderstanding: spiritual identity is determined by inner orientation (non-attachment to results) and not by outer circumstance (robes, rituals abandoned, or inactivity). A soldier, a teacher, a farmer, a homemaker — any of them can be a sannyāsī and yogī simultaneously if they act from duty without grasping for fruit. This is the foundational pillar of the entire chapter.

6:2.text: O Pāṇḍava! Know that what they call sannyāsa is what is called yoga. For without renouncing the saṅkalpas (desires, intentions), no one ever becomes a yogi.
6:2.transliteration:
yaṃ saṃnyāsam iti prāhur yogaṃ taṃ viddhi pāṇḍava |
na hy asaṃnyasta-saṅkalpo yogī bhavati kaścana ||2||
6:2.devanagari:
यं संन्यासमिति प्राहुर्योगं तं विद्धि पाण्डव ।
न ह्यसंन्यस्तसङ्कल्पो योगी भवति कश्चन ॥ २ ॥
6:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:2.explanation: 'Yaṃ saṃnyāsam iti prāhuḥ' — what people call sannyāsa; 'taṃ yogaṃ viddhi' — know that to be the same as yoga. This equation is philosophically precise: genuine sannyāsa and genuine yoga have the same essential content. The key word is 'asaṃnyasta-saṅkalpah' — one who has not renounced 'saṅkalpa.' Saṅkalpa is the mind's tendency to form wishes, intentions, and plans colored by 'I want this, I must have that.' It is the mental act of willing driven by ego-desire. As long as saṅkalpa operates — as long as the mind is perpetually generating desiderative intentions — the mind cannot settle, cannot become still, and yoga (union, integration, meditation) remains impossible. 'Na hi... yogī bhavati kaścana' — no one whatsoever becomes a yogi. The renunciation of saṅkalpa is therefore the inner prerequisite for both formal sannyāsa and functional yoga. It is the cutting of the mind's perpetual outward movement toward desired objects.

6:3.text: For the sage who desires to ascend to yoga, action is said to be the means. For the same person who has already ascended to yoga, stillness (śama) is said to be the means.
6:3.transliteration:
ārurukṣor muner yogaṃ karma kāraṇam ucyate |
yogārūḍhasya tasyaiva śamaḥ kāraṇam ucyate ||3||
6:3.devanagari:
आरुरुक्षोर्मुनेर्योगं कर्म कारणमुच्यते ।
योगारूढस्य तस्यैव शमः कारणमुच्यते ॥ ३ ॥
6:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:3.explanation: This verse maps the two-phase trajectory of spiritual development with precision. 'Ārurukṣoḥ' — for the one who is 'desirous of ascending,' the one still climbing, the sādhaka who is on the path but has not yet reached the summit. For this seeker-sage (muni), 'karma kāraṇam ucyate' — action is declared the means. Specifically, karma yoga — the disciplined, detached performance of duty — purifies the mind, reduces the density of vāsanās, and makes the citta progressively more transparent and still. The second phase: 'yogārūḍhasya' — for the one who has 'ascended to yoga' — whose mind has been purified through karma yoga to the point where it naturally rests inward, where desire has attenuated. For this person, 'śamaḥ kāraṇam ucyate' — śama (inner stillness, mental withdrawal, the natural settling of the mind into its source) is the means. This is not a different path — it is the same path at its next level. The active engagement of karma yoga creates the conditions for the restful absorption of dhyāna. Action purifies; stillness illuminates.

6:4.text: When one is no longer attached to sense-objects and no longer attached to actions, then one who has renounced all saṅkalpas (desires, intentions) is said to have ascended to yoga.
6:4.transliteration:
yadā hi nendriyārtheṣu na karmasv anuṣajjate |
sarva-saṅkalpa-saṃnyāsī yogārūḍhas tadocyate ||4||
6:4.devanagari:
यदा हि नेन्द्रियार्थेषु न कर्मस्वनुषज्जते ।
सर्वसंकल्पसंन्यासी योगारूढस्तदोच्यते ॥ ४ ॥
6:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:4.explanation: This verse provides the observable signs of the yogārūḍha (one ascended to yoga), completing the thought from verse 3. Two freedoms characterize this state: 'nendriyārtheṣu anuṣajjate' — no adhesion to sense-objects (the five tanmātras: sound, touch, form, taste, smell); and 'na karmasu anuṣajjate' — no adhesion to actions either: no ambition, no ego-investment in the outcome of work, no 'I must do this' driven by personal desire. The crowning quality: 'sarva-saṅkalpa-saṃnyāsī' — one who has renounced ALL saṅkalpas. Saṅkalpa here means the mind's ceaseless generation of desire-tinted intentions — 'I want, I will get, I must achieve.' When this faculty is genuinely stilled — not suppressed — the mind naturally rests in itself. This is the prerequisite for deep meditation, not a state to be artificially manufactured. It is the flower that blooms naturally from the root of karma yoga practiced with sustained sincerity.

6:5.text: Let a person uplift oneself by the self; let one not degrade oneself. The self alone is the friend of the self; the self alone is the enemy of the self.
6:5.transliteration:
uddhared ātmanātmānaṃ nātmānam avasādayet |
ātmaiva hy ātmano bandhur ātmaiva ripur ātmanaḥ ||5||
6:5.devanagari:
उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं नात्मानमवसादयेत् ।
आत्मैव ह्यात्मनो बन्धुरात्मैव रिपुरात्मनः ॥ ५ ॥
6:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:5.explanation: This is among the most powerful and psychologically penetrating declarations in the entire Gita. 'Uddhared ātmanātmānam' — let one lift up the self by the self. The first 'ātmanā' refers to the higher faculty of purified mind and intellect (the instrument); the second 'ātmānam' refers to the jīvātman (the individual self, the one being elevated). 'Nātmānam avasādayet' — do not allow oneself to sink into avasāda: despondency, degradation, the downward pull of tamasic and rajasic habits. The second line is a philosophical equipoise: 'ātmaiva hy ātmano bandhuḥ' — the self alone is the friend of the self. 'Ātmaiva ripur ātmanaḥ' — the self alone is the enemy of the self. The same mind that, when mastered, becomes one's greatest ally and liberator — when left unmastered — becomes one's most relentless tormentor. No external force binds us as effectively as our own unexamined mind. No external savior can liberate us as completely as our own awakened self-discipline. This verse is the Gita's deepest statement of spiritual self-responsibility.

6:6.text: For the one who has conquered the self by the self, the self is a friend. But for one who has not conquered the self, the self acts as an enemy in the manner of a foe.
6:6.transliteration:
bandhur ātmātmanas tasya yenātmaivātmanā jitaḥ |
anātmanas tu śatrutve vartetātmaiva śatru-vat ||6||
6:6.devanagari:
बन्धुरात्मात्मनस्तस्य येनात्मैवात्मना जितः ।
अनात्मनस्तु शत्रुत्वे वर्तेतात्मैव शत्रुवत् ॥ ६ ॥
6:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:6.explanation: This verse completes the psychological teaching begun in verse 5. 'Yenātmaivātmanā jitaḥ' — for the one in whom the self has been conquered by the self: the mind-intellect complex has been disciplined and mastered. 'Bandhur ātmā' — the self (mind) is a friend. A conquered mind serves as the yogi's most faithful ally: it turns inward, maintains focus, sustains clarity, resists temptation, and brings the meditator ever closer to the ātman. 'Anātmanaḥ' — for the one who is not 'ātmavān' (self-possessed, self-mastered). 'Śatrutve varteta ātmaiva śatru-vat' — the very same self operates in the mode of enmity, like a foe. The unmastered mind constantly pulls toward sensory indulgence, generates craving and aversion, manufactures anxiety and regret, distorts perception with desire-colored bias, and prevents any depth of concentration. The same entity — neither good nor bad in itself — is transformed by the presence or absence of mastery. This is why the entire yoga tradition, regardless of its particular form, begins with the problem of the mind.

6:7.text: For the one who has conquered the self and is deeply at peace, the Paramātman is integrated (steadily present) — in cold and heat, pleasure and pain, as well as in honour and dishonour.
6:7.transliteration:
jitātmanaḥ praśāntasya paramātmā samāhitaḥ |
śītoṣṇa-sukha-duḥkheṣu tathā mānāpamānayoḥ ||7||
6:7.devanagari:
जितात्मनः प्रशान्तस्य परमात्मा समाहितः ।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु तथा मानापमानयोः ॥ ७ ॥
6:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:7.explanation: 'Jitātmanaḥ praśāntasya' — for the person who has conquered the lower self (mind-senses-ego) and dwells in 'praśānti,' a peace that is 'pra-śānta' (profoundly, completely peaceful — not merely the absence of disturbance but the positive presence of stillness). For such a one, 'paramātmā samāhitaḥ' — the Paramātman (the Supreme Self) is 'samāhita' — settled, integrated, constantly available. The Paramātman is present everywhere always — but the turbulent mind, like a muddy lake, cannot reflect the sky clearly. The still mind, like a clear lake, reflects the Self without distortion. Then the test-conditions: 'śītoṣṇa-sukha-duḥkheṣu' — in cold and heat (physical discomfort-comfort, the bodily pairs of opposites) and 'mānāpamānayoḥ' — in honour and dishonour (the social and psychological pairs of opposites). The jitātmā's inner communion with the Paramātman remains unbroken across all these fluctuations — because their peace is not contingent on any of them.

6:8.text: One whose inner self is satisfied by knowledge and direct realization, who is immovable, self-controlled in the senses, and who regards a clod of earth, a stone, and gold as equal — such a yogi is called 'yukta' (perfectly integrated).
6:8.transliteration:
jñāna-vijñāna-tṛptātmā kūṭastho vijitendriyaḥ |
yukta ity ucyate yogī sama-loṣṭāśma-kāñcanaḥ ||8||
6:8.devanagari:
ज्ञानविज्ञानतृप्तात्मा कूटस्थो विजितेन्द्रियः ।
युक्त इत्युच्यते योगी समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः ॥ ८ ॥
6:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:8.explanation: Four defining marks of the 'yukta' yogi. First: 'jñāna-vijñāna-tṛptātmā' — satisfied inwardly by both jñāna and vijñāna. 'Jñāna' is śāstric, theoretical knowledge — understanding through scripture and reasoning that 'the ātman is eternal, Brahman is all.' 'Vijñāna' is direct, experiential realization — the immediate knowing of these truths in one's own awareness, beyond intellectual understanding. 'Tṛptātmā' — satisfied, complete: this person desires no further acquisition of knowledge because their inner self is already full. Second: 'kūṭasthaḥ' — immovable, positioned at the summit (kūṭa = mountaintop, or an anvil — both immovable). Third: 'vijitendriyaḥ' — with senses fully mastered. Fourth and most vivid: 'sama-loṣṭāśma-kāñcanaḥ' — viewing equally a clod of earth, a stone, and gold. Three things of wildly different conventional value are perceived as equal — not because the yogi is intellectually denying their practical differences, but because at the level of Self-awareness, all are equally modifications of the same Brahman. This equanimity of vision is the spontaneous fruit of deep realization.

6:9.text: One who maintains equal-mindedness toward a well-wisher, a friend, an enemy, a neutral, an arbitrator, the hateful, and one's own kin — as well as toward the righteous and the sinful — such a one is distinguished (spiritually pre-eminent).
6:9.transliteration:
suhṛn-mitrāry-udāsīna-madhyastha-dveṣya-bandhuṣu |
sādhuṣv api ca pāpeṣu sama-buddhir viśiṣyate ||9||
6:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:9.explanation: This verse presents the most comprehensive catalogue in the Gita of the social relationships through which equanimity (sama-buddhi) must be tested and proven. Seven relational categories: 'suhṛd' (the unconditional well-wisher, the one who wishes good without any personal stake), 'mitra' (the friend, bound by mutual affinity), 'ari' (the enemy), 'udāsīna' (the indifferent neutral, uninvolved), 'madhyastha' (the mediator between parties), 'dveṣya' (the one toward whom one personally feels aversion — or who feels it toward you), and 'bandhu' (kinfolk, family). Then the final span: 'sādhuṣu api ca pāpeṣu' — among the virtuous AND among the sinful. 'Sama-buddhir viśiṣyate' — one of equal intelligence across all these is 'viśiṣyate': distinctly, specially excellent. Note that this sama-buddhi is wisdom-born, not emotional indifference. The jñānī sees the same ātman in friend and foe. This is not the suppression of appropriate discernment — it is the underlying metaphysical recognition that the same consciousness dwells in all.

6:10.text: Let the yogi continuously engage the self in yoga, stationed in solitude, alone, with mind and body restrained, free from hope and free from accumulation.
6:10.transliteration:
yogī yuñjīta satatam ātmānaṃ rahasi sthitaḥ |
ekākī yata-cittātmā nirāśīr aparigrahaḥ ||10||
6:10.devanagari:
योगी युञ्जीत सततमात्मानं रहसि स्थितः ।
एकाकी यतचित्तात्मा निराशीरपरिग्रहः ॥ १० ॥
6:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:10.explanation: Here begins the Gita's most detailed and practical manual of meditation practice. 'Satatam yuñjīta' — let the yogi continuously engage, maintain constant yogic practice (not occasional or fitful). 'Rahasi sthitaḥ' — stationed in solitude: a physically quiet place, removed from distraction, is the necessary environmental condition for the beginning meditator. 'Ekākī' — alone: not merely physically isolated but mentally singular, without the diffusion of social engagement. 'Yata-cittātmā' — with both the citta (the emotional-mental faculty) and the ātmā (one's own person/body) restrained and disciplined. 'Nirāśīḥ' — free from āśā (hope, expectation of results): the meditator does not practice with the goal of achieving some future state in view — that goal-orientation itself becomes an obstacle. 'Aparigrahaḥ' — free from accumulation: not hoarding objects, relationships, or even spiritual experiences. These five conditions together create the optimal inner and outer environment for deep meditation: solitude, singleness, self-restraint, hope-lessness, and non-accumulation.

6:11.text: Having established a firm seat for oneself in a clean place — neither too high nor too low — with cloth, deerskin, and kuśa grass layered upon it.
6:11.transliteration:
śucau deśe pratiṣṭhāpya sthiram āsanam ātmanaḥ |
nāty-ucchritaṃ nāti-nīcaṃ cailājina-kuśottaram ||11||
6:11.devanagari:
शुचौ देशे प्रतिष्ठाप्य स्थिरमासनमात्मनः ।
नात्युच्छ्रितं नातिनीचं चैलाजिनकुशोत्तरम् ॥ ११ ॥
6:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:11.explanation: The Gita now enters the realm of precise technical instruction for meditation preparation — a level of practical detail rare in philosophical texts. 'Śucau deśe' — in a clean (śuci) place: clean in three senses — physically clean (free of dirt and disturbance), energetically pure (a space conducive to meditation, perhaps regularly used for that purpose), and mentally peaceful (away from noise and activity). 'Sthiram āsanam' — a firm, stable seat: the physical stability of the seat mirrors and supports the mental stability sought in meditation. 'Nāty-ucchritaṃ nāti-nīcam' — neither too elevated (risk of falling, instability) nor too low (dampness from the earth, insects, cold). 'Cailājina-kuśottaram' — the three layers from bottom up: kuśa grass (considered purifying and insulating from earth-energies), deerskin (ajina — traditionally used for its insulating and grounding properties), and cloth on top. This three-layered seat is the complete traditional specification. Modern yoga practitioners' use of a mat continues this ancient tradition of a designated, clean, slightly raised meditation surface.

6:12.text: Having sat on that seat, with mind made one-pointed, with the functions of mind and senses restrained — let one practise yoga there for the purification of the self.
6:12.transliteration:
tatraikāgraṃ manaḥ kṛtvā yata-cittendriya-kriyaḥ |
upaviśyāsane yuñjyād yogam ātma-viśuddhaye ||12||
6:12.devanagari:
तत्रैकाग्रं मनः कृत्वा यतचित्तेन्द्रियक्रियः ।
उपविश्यासने युञ्ज्याद्योगमात्मविशुद्धये ॥ १२ ॥
6:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:12.explanation: 'Ekāgraṃ manaḥ kṛtvā' — having made the mind one-pointed. Ekāgratā (one-pointedness) is the essential quality of meditation — the gathering of the mind's diffuse attention into a single, unbroken focus. 'Yata-cittendriya-kriyaḥ' — with the activities of both citta (the mind-stuff) and the senses restrained: this is the combined action of pratyāhāra (withdrawal of the senses from their objects) and dhāraṇā (concentration — holding the mind steady on a single point). 'Upaviśyāsane' — having sat on the prepared seat. 'Yuñjyād yogam' — let one engage in yoga (the practice of union, of bringing the scattered self back to its centre). The stated purpose: 'ātma-viśuddhaye' — for the purification of the self. Meditation is not primarily a relaxation technique or a stress-management tool — it is a process of ātma-śuddhi: the progressive refinement and cleansing of the inner being, burning away the accumulated impressions, desires, and ignorance that obscure the self's natural luminosity.

6:13.text: Holding the body, head, and neck erect and still, steady, gazing at the tip of one's nose and not looking in any other direction —
6:13.transliteration:
samaṃ kāya-śiro-grīvaṃ dhārayann acalaṃ sthiraḥ |
saṃprekṣya nāsikāgraṃ svaṃ diśaś cānavalokayan ||13||
6:13.devanagari:
समं कायशिरोग्रीवं धारयन्नचलं स्थिरः ।
सम्प्रेक्ष्य नासिकाग्रं स्वं दिशश्चानवलोकयन् ॥ १३ ॥
6:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:13.explanation: This verse and the next deliver the Gita's precise instructions for physical posture during meditation — a level of specificity that matches later yoga texts. 'Samaṃ kāya-śiro-grīvam' — body, head, and neck held erect and aligned. This vertical alignment of the spinal column is not merely aesthetic — it is physiologically significant: the upright spine allows the free flow of prāṇa (vital energy) through the suṣumnā nāḍī (the central subtle channel), which is the physiological basis of deep meditative states. 'Acalaṃ sthiraḥ' — immovable and firm: physical stillness directly supports mental stillness; every bodily movement creates a ripple in the citta. 'Nāsikāgraṃ saṃprekṣya' — gazing at the tip of the nose: in this gaze, the eyes are half-closed, the visual field is restricted, and external distraction is minimized while drowsiness is prevented. Some commentators interpret this as nasikāgra-dṛṣṭi (literal nose-tip gaze) and others as metaphorical for inward focus. 'Diśaś cānavalokayan' — not looking in other directions: the gaze is anchored, not wandering. Physical eyes that wander carry the mind with them.

6:14.text: With a serene inner self, free from fear, established in the vow of brahmacarya, with the mind controlled — let one sit integrated, with consciousness in Me, devoted to Me as the supreme.
6:14.transliteration:
praśāntātmā vigata-bhīr brahmacāri-vrate sthitaḥ |
manaḥ saṃyamya mac-citto yukta āsīta mat-paraḥ ||14||
6:14.devanagari:
प्रशान्तात्मा विगतभीर्ब्रह्मचारिव्रते स्थितः ।
मनः संयम्य मच्चित्तो युक्त आसीत मत्परः ॥ १४ ॥
6:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:14.explanation: This verse describes the inner, qualitative dimension of the meditation posture, complementing the outer physical description of verses 11-13. 'Praśāntātmā' — with a serene inner being: the mind must be brought to a relative quietness before deep meditation is even possible; a churning mind cannot achieve concentration. 'Vigata-bhīḥ' — free from fear: fear is among the most potent disruptors of meditative depth — it contracts and agitates the mind-field. 'Brahmacāri-vrate sthitaḥ' — established in the vow of brahmacarya. The term brahmacarya literally means 'moving in Brahman' — its full meaning is comprehensive sense-restraint, not merely sexual continence. It is the disposition of one whose energy is gathered inward rather than dispersed through sensory indulgence. 'Manaḥ saṃyamya' — with the mind restrained, pulled back from its outward wandering. 'Mac-citto mat-paraḥ' — most significantly: 'with mind in Me, with Me as the supreme goal.' The meditation Krishna prescribes is not impersonal or abstract — it is centred in Him, in the personal divine. This is the meeting point of jñāna yoga, dhyāna yoga, and bhakti yoga.

6:15.text: Continuously engaging the self in yoga thus, the yogi of restrained mind attains the peace that culminates in nirvāṇa — established in Me.
6:15.transliteration:
yuñjann evaṃ sadātmānaṃ yogī niyata-mānasaḥ |
śāntiṃ nirvāṇa-paramāṃ mat-saṃsthām adhigacchati ||15||
6:15.devanagari:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी नियतमानसः ।
शान्तिं निर्वाणपरमां मत्संस्थामधिगच्छति ॥ १५ ॥
6:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:15.explanation: 'Yuñjann evaṃ sadātmānam' — continuously engaging the self in yoga in this manner: the adverb 'sadā' (always, continuously) signals that yoga is not an occasional exercise but a consistent and sustained orientation of the entire life. 'Niyata-mānasaḥ' — the yogī with a disciplined, controlled mind: 'niyata' implies that the mind is not merely restrained temporarily but has been brought under a stable, ongoing governance. 'Mat-saṃsthām' — established in Me: the final ground of this peace is not in the yogi's individual mind but in the Divine — in Krishna, in the Paramātman. 'Śāntiṃ nirvāṇa-paramām' — the peace whose apex, whose supreme expression, is nirvāṇa: the complete extinction of the individual ego's restless grasping and fearing in the vast stillness of the infinite. This is not death — it is the most alive state possible: total presence without the distortion of self-concern. The entire meditation practice described in verses 10-15 culminates here — in this supreme peace that is simultaneously personal devotion and impersonal liberation.

6:16.text: O Arjuna, yoga is not for the one who eats too much, nor for the one who eats nothing at all. Nor for the one who sleeps too much, nor for the one who is always awake.
6:16.transliteration:
nāty-aśnatas tu yogo 'sti na caikāntam anaśnataḥ |
na cāti-svapna-śīlasya jāgrato naiva cārjuna ||16||
6:16.devanagari:
नात्यश्नतस्तु योगोऽस्ति न चैकान्तमनश्नतः ।
न चातिस्वप्नशीलस्य जाग्रतो नैव चार्जुन ॥ १६ ॥
6:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:16.explanation: This and the next verse articulate the principle of yuktatā (measured, calibrated living) as the necessary foundation for meditative practice. Four extremes are disqualified: 'aty-aśnataḥ' — excessive eating: overeating makes the body heavy, generates tamasic lethargy, and directs prāṇa downward toward digestion rather than upward toward awareness. 'Anaśnataḥ' — eating nothing, extreme fasting: starvation depletes the body and agitates the mind — a starving meditator cannot maintain the calm steadiness needed for depth. 'Ati-svapna-śīlasya' — excessive sleeping: too much sleep increases tamas (inertia), dulls the mind, and makes concentration impossible. 'Jāgrataḥ' — the one who never sleeps: extreme sleep deprivation produces irritability, erratic thinking, and rajasic agitation. All four are anti-yogic extremes. The Gita's prescription here anticipates what it will articulate in verse 17: a middle path of measured balance. This is not asceticism and not indulgence — it is the precise calibration of the body's needs.

6:17.text: For one who is measured in eating and recreation, measured in effort in actions, measured in sleep and wakefulness — yoga becomes the destroyer of sorrow.
6:17.transliteration:
yuktāhāra-vihārasya yukta-ceṣṭasya karmasu |
yukta-svapnāvabodhasya yogo bhavati duḥkha-hā ||17||
6:17.devanagari:
युक्ताहारविहारस्य युक्तचेष्टस्य कर्मसु ।
युक्तस्वप्नावबोधस्य योगो भवति दुःखहा ॥ १७ ॥
6:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:17.explanation: This verse gives the positive prescription complementing the negatives of verse 16. 'Yukta' — measured, calibrated, appropriate: not a fixed formula but a living attentiveness to what the body and mind genuinely need. Three domains of yuktatā: (1) 'Yuktāhāra-vihārasya' — measured in āhāra (eating) and vihāra (recreation, relaxation, movement): food eaten in the right quantity at the right time of the right quality; rest and recreation taken neither excessively nor insufficiently. (2) 'Yukta-ceṣṭasya karmasu' — measured in effort in actions: work performed neither with draining overexertion nor with lazy half-heartedness. (3) 'Yukta-svapnāvabodhasya' — measured in sleep (svapna) and wakefulness (avabodha): the right amount of sleep — enough to restore without inducing torpor. For the one who lives this threefold balance: 'yogo bhavati duḥkha-hā' — yoga becomes the destroyer of sorrow. 'Duḥkha-hā' is a strong word: yoga does not merely manage sorrow — it destroys (hā) it at the root. The balanced life is not merely comfortable — it is the necessary soil in which the transformative flower of yoga can bloom.

6:18.text: When the disciplined mind rests in the Self alone, free from longing for all objects of desire — then that person is called 'yukta' (perfectly integrated, yoga-united).
6:18.transliteration:
yadā viniyataṃ cittam ātmany evāvatiṣṭhate |
niḥspṛhaḥ sarva-kāmebhyo yukta ity ucyate tadā ||18||
6:18.devanagari:
यदा विनियतं चित्तमात्मन्येवावतिष्ठते ।
निःस्पृहः सर्वकामेभ्यो युक्त इत्युच्यते तदा ॥ १८ ॥
6:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:18.explanation: 'Viniyataṃ cittam ātmany evāvatiṣṭhate' — the key word is 'eva' (alone, only): the disciplined mind rests in the Self alone — it has stopped going outward to objects and rests naturally in its own source. This is the turning point of dhyāna: the mind no longer needs to be effortfully restrained but has discovered a deeper rest within itself. 'Niḥspṛhaḥ sarva-kāmebhyaḥ' — completely without longing (spṛhā) for any and all desired objects: this is not suppression of desire but the natural dissolution of desire in the taste of something infinitely more satisfying — the ānanda of the ātman. When the inner ocean is found, the desire for drops from external sources naturally ceases. 'Yukta ity ucyate' — 'yukta' (from the root 'yuj' — to yoke, to join): this person has been yoked to the Supreme. This verse defines the practical criterion for 'yokedness': the mind at rest in the Self, free from the gravitational pull of desire. This is not a mystical special state — it is the natural condition of a purified mind.

6:19.text: As a lamp placed in a windless spot does not flicker — that is the simile recalled for the yogi of restrained mind who practises yoga of the Self.
6:19.transliteration:
yathā dīpo nivāta-stho neṅgate sopamā smṛtā |
yogino yata-cittasya yuñjato yogam ātmanaḥ ||19||
6:19.devanagari:
यथा दीपो निवातस्थो नेङ्गते सोपमा स्मृता ।
योगिनो यतचित्तस्य युञ्जतो योगमात्मनः ॥ १९ ॥
6:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:19.explanation: This is among the most celebrated similes in the entire Bhagavad Gita — exquisite in its simplicity and depth. 'Dīpo nivāta-stho neṅgate' — a lamp (dīpa) placed in a windless (nivāta) spot does not waver (neṅgate): its flame burns perfectly upright, luminous, steady, unwavering. 'Sopamā smṛtā' — 'that simile is remembered': the phrasing suggests this may have been a well-known traditional analogy that Krishna here employs. The yogi of restrained mind (yata-citta) who is engaged in ātma-yoga (the yoga of the Self) is like that flame. The mind is luminous (it is aware, it is active consciousness), yet it is completely still — undisturbed by the winds of sensory impulse, emotional agitation, discursive thought, or external distraction. Every wandering thought is like a gust that makes the flame flicker. The mastered mind of the dhyānī is the perfectly still flame: fully alive, fully alight, absolutely unswayed. This simile encapsulates the paradox of deep meditation — total aliveness and total stillness simultaneously.

6:20.text: The state where the mind, restrained by the practice of yoga, becomes quiescent — and where seeing the Self by the Self alone, one is satisfied within the Self.
6:20.transliteration:
yatroparamate cittaṃ niruddhaṃ yoga-sevayā |
yatra caivātmanātmānaṃ paśyann ātmani tuṣyati ||20||
6:20.devanagari:
यत्रोपरमते चित्तं निरुद्धं योगसेवया ।
यत्र चैवात्मनात्मानं पश्यन्नात्मनि तुष्यति ॥ २० ॥
6:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:20.explanation: This and the following three verses constitute the Gita's most intimate description of the samādhi state — the fruit of sustained dhyāna yoga. 'Yoga-sevayā niruddhaṃ cittam' — the mind restrained (niruddha — the same word used in Patañjali's Yoga Sūtra 1.2: 'yogaś citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ') through sustained yoga practice. 'Uparamate' — becomes 'upārama': this beautiful word means to 'become silent from above,' to withdraw from all external engagement and come to a complete inner standstill. 'Ātmanātmānaṃ paśyan' — seeing the Self by the Self: the most advanced form of self-knowledge where the instrument of knowing (the purified mind/intellect) and the object of knowing (the ātman) are experienced as one. The tripartite structure of subject-object-knowing collapses into a single, undivided experience. 'Ātmani tuṣyati' — contentment/satisfaction within the Self alone: the deepest possible satisfaction — not the satisfaction of a desire fulfilled but the natural fulfilment of the Self by the Self, which needs nothing outside itself.

6:21.text: The transcendent joy that is known there — graspable by the intellect yet beyond the senses — once established in that state, one does not move from the truth.
6:21.transliteration:
sukham ātyantikam yat tad buddhi-grāhyam atīndriyam |
vetti yatra na caivāyaṃ sthitaś calati tattvataḥ ||21||
6:21.devanagari:
सुखमात्यन्तिकं यत्तद्बुद्धिग्राह्यमतीन्द्रियम् ।
वेत्ति यत्र न चैवायं स्थितश्चलति तत्त्वतः ॥ २१ ॥
6:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:21.explanation: 'Sukham ātyantikam' — the joy that is 'ātyantika': absolute, without limit, without end, the terminal form of happiness — not the happiness of a particular pleasant experience but the happiness that is the very nature of being itself. Two precise qualifiers: (1) 'Buddhi-grāhyam' — graspable by the intellect (buddhi) in its purest, most refined state. This is not a raw sensory pleasure — the senses cannot reach it. It is apprehended through the highest instrument of inner knowledge, the śuddha-buddhi (purified intellect), when it has become transparent to the Self. (2) 'Atīndriyam' — beyond the senses: transcending the entire field of sensory perception. The five senses and their objects have no access here. 'Vetti' — one knows it: this knowing is not conceptual but direct, experiential, non-mediated. 'Sthitaś calati tattvataḥ na' — established in that state, one does not deviate from truth (tattva). This is absolute stability: once this ground is touched, nothing — no pleasure, no pain, no external event — can dislodge the yogi from it.

6:22.text: Obtaining which, one does not consider any other gain to be greater, and being established in which, one is not moved even by the deepest sorrow.
6:22.transliteration:
yaṃ labdhvā cāparaṃ lābhaṃ manyate nādhikaṃ tataḥ |
yasmin sthito na duḥkhena guruṇāpi vicālyate ||22||
6:22.devanagari:
यं लब्ध्वा चापरं लाभं मन्यते नाधिकं ततः ।
यस्मिन्स्थितो न दुःखेन गुरुणापि विचाल्यते ॥ २२ ॥
6:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:22.explanation: 'Yaṃ labdhvā cāparaṃ lābhaṃ manyate nādhikaṃ' — having gained which, one considers no other gain to be greater. This is the definition of the ultimate attainment: the state of complete inner fullness where the question 'what more?' simply does not arise. The world's entire catalogue of desirables — wealth, power, fame, relationships, knowledge, health — all appear as lesser gains in comparison. This is not the arrogance of dismissal but the simple recognition of having found the source from which all value flows. 'Yasmin sthito na duḥkhena guruṇāpi vicālyate' — established in which, one is not shaken even by the heaviest (guruṇā) sorrow. Note: 'guruṇā' — the greatest, most weighty kind of sorrow — not minor inconveniences but serious suffering. This state does not make one immune to pain in the sense of becoming unfeeling — it provides an inner ground so stable that even severe external suffering cannot uproot the yogi from their essential peace. This is the supreme test and the supreme gift of dhyāna yoga.

6:23.text: Let one know that which is called yoga to be the disjunction from the conjunction with sorrow. That yoga is to be practised with determination and with a mind that does not lose heart.
6:23.transliteration:
taṃ vidyād duḥkha-saṃyoga-viyogaṃ yoga-saṃjñitam |
sa niścayena yoktavyo yogo 'nirviṇṇa-cetasā ||23||
6:23.devanagari:
तं विद्याद्दुःखसंयोगवियोगं योगसंज्ञितम् ।
स निश्चयेन योक्तव्यो योगोऽनिर्विण्णचेतसा ॥ २३ ॥
6:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:23.explanation: This verse provides one of the most original and practically oriented definitions of yoga in the entire text. 'Duḥkha-saṃyoga-viyogaṃ yoga-saṃjñitam' — what is known by the name 'yoga' is the 'viyoga' (disjunction, disconnection) from the 'saṃyoga' (conjunction, connection) with duḥkha (sorrow, suffering). We are currently 'conjoined' with sorrow — our identity and experience are entangled with suffering. Yoga is precisely the severing of this entanglement. It is not primarily a technique for achieving bliss — it is the removal of the root connection to suffering. 'Sa niścayena yoktavyaḥ' — this yoga must be engaged 'with determination' (niścaya): without vacillation, without the lukewarm half-heartedness that picks up the practice for a few days and then drifts away. 'Anirviṇṇa-cetasā' — with a mind that is 'anirviṇṇa': not dejected, not disheartened, not tired of trying. Yoga practice is long. The mind will resist, progress will seem slow. The quality that sustains the practitioner through this is cheerful, unshakeable determination — not grim willpower but a deep, enthusiastic resolve.

6:24.text: Abandoning completely and without remainder all desires born of saṅkalpa, and restraining the entire company of senses by the mind alone on every side —
6:24.transliteration:
saṅkalpa-prabhavān kāmāṃs tyaktvā sarvān aśeṣataḥ |
manasaivendriya-grāmaṃ viniyamya samantataḥ ||24||
6:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:24.explanation: This verse and the next constitute the Gita's most concentrated description of the actual mechanics of meditation practice. 'Saṅkalpa-prabhavān kāmān tyaktvā sarvān aśeṣataḥ' — abandoning completely, without remainder, all desires 'born of saṅkalpa.' A crucial insight: desires (kāma) are not primary — they are secondary products of saṅkalpa (the mind's intentional desiring-faculty, its tendency to generate 'I want...' propositions). If the root is cut — if saṅkalpa itself is quieted — then desires have no source from which to arise. 'Aśeṣataḥ' — without residue, completely, not leaving even one desire untouched. This thoroughness is important: a single desire-seed left unaddressed will sprout in meditation and pull the mind outward. 'Manasaivendriya-grāmaṃ viniyamya samantataḥ' — and with the mind alone (the purified buddhi-guided mind as the instrument) restraining the entire collection (grāma) of senses from all sides: pratyāhāra — the systematic withdrawal of each sense from its corresponding object — achieved not through force but through the mind's discrimination and gentle, continuous return. This verse ends mid-sentence, continuing in verse 25.

6:25.text: Gradually, step by step, let one attain stillness through the intellect endowed with patience; having made the mind established in the Self, let one not think of anything at all.
6:25.transliteration:
śanaiḥ śanair uparamed buddhyā dhṛti-gṛhītayā |
ātma-saṃsthaṃ manaḥ kṛtvā na kiñcid api cintayet ||25||
6:25.devanagari:
शनैः शनैरुपरमेद्बुद्ध्या धृतिगृहीतया ।
आत्मसंस्थं मनः कृत्वा न किञ्चिदपि चिन्तयेत् ॥ २५ ॥
6:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:25.explanation: 'Śanaiḥ śanaiḥ' — slowly, gradually, step by step. This adverb pair is among the most practically wise instructions in the Gita's entire meditative section. The mind is not conquered through force or sudden violence — every attempt at aggressive mental suppression produces a counterforce of equal or greater resistance. The approach must be gentle, patient, and incremental. 'Buddhyā dhṛti-gṛhītayā' — by the intellect that is 'dhṛti-gṛhīta' (held by dhṛti: patience, fortitude, the quality of steadfast endurance). The purified, patient buddhi is the instrument of withdrawal — it gently disengages the mind from its objects, again and again, without frustration or self-judgment. 'Ātma-saṃsthaṃ manaḥ kṛtvā' — having settled the mind in the Self: this is the positive direction — not just 'away from objects' but 'toward the ātman.' The mind is given a place to rest. 'Na kiñcid api cintayet' — let one not think of ANYTHING at all. Not good thoughts, not spiritual thoughts, not visualizations — pure, objectless awareness is the goal. This is Patañjali's nirodha (cessation of mental modifications) articulated in the Gita's language.

6:26.text: Wherever the restless and unsteady mind wanders, from there, having restrained it, let one bring it back again and again into the Self alone.
6:26.transliteration:
yato yato niścarati manaś cañcalam asthiram |
tatas tato niyamyaitad ātmany eva vaśaṃ nayet ||26||
6:26.devanagari:
यतो यतो निश्चरति मनश्चञ्चलमस्थिरम् ।
ततस्ततो नियम्यैतदात्मन्येव वशं नयेत् ॥ २६ ॥
6:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:26.explanation: This is the single most practically applicable meditation instruction in the entire Bhagavad Gita — it speaks directly to the lived experience of every person who has ever sat down to meditate. 'Manaś cañcalam asthiram' — the mind is described with two adjectives: 'cañcala' (restless, flickering, jumping from object to object like a flame in wind) and 'asthira' (unsteady, unable to settle at any point). These are not pathological descriptions of a broken mind — they are the objective characteristics of the untrained human mind. 'Yato yato niścarati... tatas tato niyamya' — from wherever it wanders to, from there, having restrained it... The grammatical construction 'yato yato... tatas tatas' (wherever... from there...) captures the tracking quality — the yogi attentively notices where the mind has gone and gently retrieves it. 'Ātmany eva vaśaṃ nayet' — bring it under control INTO the Self alone. The genius of this verse is its normalization of wandering: Krishna does not say 'the mind should not wander.' He says 'when it wanders, bring it back.' The practice IS the returning. Each return to the Self is a repetition of the fundamental meditative act — and these repetitions, accumulated over years, deepen the groove of inner stability.

6:27.text: Supreme happiness comes to this yogi whose mind is deeply at peace, whose rajas is pacified, who has become Brahman, and who is free from all impurity.
6:27.transliteration:
praśānta-manasaṃ hy enaṃ yoginaṃ sukham uttamam |
upaiti śānta-rajasaṃ brahma-bhūtam akalmaṣam ||27||
6:27.devanagari:
प्रशान्तमनसं ह्येनं योगिनं सुखमुत्तमम् ।
उपैति शान्तरजसं ब्रह्मभूतमकल्मषम् ॥ २७ ॥
6:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:27.explanation: This verse describes the natural state and the natural reward of the yogi who has successfully practised the meditation described in verses 25-26. Four qualities define this yogi: (1) 'Praśānta-manasam' — whose mind is 'praśānta': not merely quiet but profoundly, deeply, completely peaceful — a peace that is qualitatively different from ordinary mental quietude. (2) 'Śānta-rajasam' — whose rajas has been pacified: rajas (the quality of restless activity, craving, agitation, ambition) is the primary obstacle to meditation. When rajas quiets, the mind stops its constant outward surge and settles naturally. (3) 'Brahma-bhūtam' — who has become Brahman: who has realized their identity as Brahman, not just intellectually acknowledged it but lived it as direct experience. (4) 'Akalmaṣam' — free from all impurity (kalmaṣa): the accumulated moral, psychological, and karmic residues have been dissolved. To such a yogi, 'sukham uttamam upaiti' — supreme happiness arrives. 'Upaiti' (comes to, approaches) — the happiness is not manufactured or achieved; it comes naturally, as light comes when darkness is removed. It is the Self's own ānanda, recognized.

6:28.text: Thus continuously engaging the self in yoga, the yogi who is free from impurity easily attains the transcendent joy of brahma-saṃsparśa — contact with Brahman.
6:28.transliteration:
yuñjann evaṃ sadātmānaṃ yogī vigata-kalmaṣaḥ |
sukhena brahma-saṃsparśam atyantaṃ sukham aśnute ||28||
6:28.devanagari:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी विगतकल्मषः ।
सुखेन ब्रह्मसंस्पर्शमत्यन्तं सुखमश्नुते ॥ २८ ॥
6:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:28.explanation: 'Brahma-saṃsparśam' — this is one of the most evocative compound words in the Gita: 'saṃsparśa' is complete, full contact — a touch that is whole, not partial. The experience of Brahman is here described not as a distant vision or an intellectual understanding, but as a direct, immediate, sensory-like 'touch' — something felt in the depths of being. This is the moment of samādhi: when the yogī's consciousness, purified through sustained practice, makes direct contact with Brahman — not the Brahman of scripture and philosophy but the living, luminous, blissful reality that was always present but veiled. 'Atyantaṃ sukham aśnute' — attains (aśnute: eats, experiences, savours) the absolute (atyanta) joy. 'Aśnute' suggests not passive reception but active tasting, a full and conscious enjoyment. 'Sukhena' — 'easily': the repeated use of this word in the Gita's meditation passages is deliberate — Krishna consistently frames the final stage of yoga not as a violent achievement but as the natural, effortless blossoming that occurs when the obstacles have been removed through patient practice.

6:29.text: The yoga-united self who sees everywhere equally perceives the Self in all beings and all beings in the Self.
6:29.transliteration:
sarva-bhūta-stham ātmānaṃ sarva-bhūtāni cātmani |
īkṣate yoga-yuktātmā sarvatra sama-darśanaḥ ||29||
6:29.devanagari:
सर्वभूतस्थमात्मानं सर्वभूतानि चात्मनि ।
ईक्षते योगयुक्तात्मा सर्वत्र समदर्शनः ॥ २९ ॥
6:29.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:29.explanation: This verse describes the vision of non-duality (advaita-dṛṣṭi) that arises as the natural fruit of deep yoga — the most direct vision of what the Upaniṣads describe as 'tat tvam asi' (that thou art). Two simultaneous perceptions constitute this vision: (1) 'Sarva-bhūta-stham ātmānam īkṣate' — he perceives the Self as abiding in all beings: the same single consciousness that illuminates his own inner awareness is recognized as the identical reality illuminating every creature's inner awareness. (2) 'Sarva-bhūtāni cātmani' — and perceives all beings within the Self: the Self is not a small individual entity that somehow fits inside all beings — it is the infinite ground in which all beings arise, move, and dissolve. These two perceptions together constitute the complete non-dual recognition. 'Yoga-yuktātmā sarvatra sama-darśanaḥ' — the yoga-united person who sees equally everywhere. This 'sama-darśana' (equal vision) is not a philosophical opinion about equality — it is the direct perceptual recognition that the same radiant consciousness is the substratum of every form. It is seeing with the eye of the Self itself.

6:30.text: One who sees Me everywhere and sees everything in Me — I am never lost to that one, and that one is never lost to Me.
6:30.transliteration:
yo māṃ paśyati sarvatra sarvaṃ ca mayi paśyati |
tasyāhaṃ na praṇaśyāmi sa ca me na praṇaśyati ||30||
6:30.devanagari:
यो मां पश्यति सर्वत्र सर्वं च मयि पश्यति ।
तस्याहं न प्रणश्यामि स च मे न प्रणश्यति ॥ ३० ॥
6:30.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:30.explanation: This is among the most tender and luminous verses in the entire Bhagavad Gita — a verse that transcends philosophy and touches the heart of devotion. 'Yo māṃ paśyati sarvatra sarvaṃ ca mayi paśyati' — who sees Me everywhere and sees everything in Me: this is the bhakti-version of the same non-dual vision described in verse 29, where the language was that of ātman. Here Krishna speaks in the first person — 'Me' — because the ātman of all beings and the personal God are not two different realities in the Gita's vision: they are the same single presence recognized with different emphases. 'Tasyāhaṃ na praṇaśyāmi' — I am never lost to that one: for the one who has learned to see the Divine everywhere, the Divine is never again obscured by the veil of ordinary perception. Every face, every form, every event becomes transparent to the Divine presence within. 'Sa ca me na praṇaśyati' — and that one is never lost to Me: Krishna's love for this devotee-seer is absolute and permanent. The mutual recognition of the lover and the Beloved — this is the mystical heart of the Gita. Once this seeing is established, neither party can lose the other.

6:31.text: One who, established in oneness, worships Me dwelling in all beings — that yogi, in whatever way they live, dwells in Me.
6:31.transliteration:
sarva-bhūta-sthitaṃ yo māṃ bhajaty ekatvam āsthitaḥ |
sarvathā vartamāno 'pi sa yogī mayi vartate ||31||
6:31.devanagari:
सर्वभूतस्थितं यो मां भजत्येकत्वमास्थितः ।
सर्वथा वर्तमानोऽपि स योगी मयि वर्तते ॥ ३१ ॥
6:31.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:31.explanation: 'Ekatvam āsthitaḥ' — established in ekatva (oneness, unity): grounded in the non-dual perception that all apparent multiplicity is one single Brahman-reality. 'Sarva-bhūta-sthitaṃ yo māṃ bhajaty' — who worships Me as the one dwelling in all beings. This is a revolutionary redefinition of bhajana (worship). Conventional bhajana is ritual: the act of singing, chanting, offering flowers, lighting lamps before an image. The bhajana described here is existential: to perceive the Divine in every creature and to serve and love each creature as a manifestation of the Divine. 'Sarvathā vartamāno 'pi' — in whatever way they live, whatever they do, whatever station of life they occupy, whatever activity they are engaged in. 'Sa yogī mayi vartate' — that yogi lives in Me. The extraordinary universality of this declaration: no particular external action or renunciation is required. The inner vision of unity is sufficient to make every moment of life a dwelling in the Divine. This is the practical climax of the Gita's non-dualism made accessible to every human being.

6:32.text: O Arjuna, one who sees everywhere with equality — by the measure of the Self — whether in pleasure or in pain — that yogi is considered supreme.
6:32.transliteration:
ātmaupamyena sarvatra samaṃ paśyati yo 'rjuna |
sukhaṃ vā yadi vā duḥkhaṃ sa yogī paramo mataḥ ||32||
6:32.devanagari:
आत्मौपम्येन सर्वत्र समं पश्यति योऽर्जुन ।
सुखं वा यदि वा दुःखं स योगी परमो मतः ॥ ३२ ॥
6:32.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:32.explanation: 'Ātmaupamyena' — by the measure of the Self, using the Self as the standard of comparison. This is the Gita's statement of the universal moral principle known in many traditions as the Golden Rule: just as I experience pleasure and pain, so does every other being. Because the same ātman inhabits all forms, all forms share the same fundamental capacity for joy and suffering. 'Sarvatra samaṃ paśyati sukhaṃ vā yadi vā duḥkham' — seeing equally everywhere, whether in pleasure or in pain: one who has realized the unity of the Self does not merely philosophize about the equality of all beings — one directly feels the other's pleasure as one's own pleasure and the other's pain as one's own pain. This is not manufactured empathy but spontaneous compassionate resonance arising from Self-knowledge. 'Sa yogī paramo mataḥ' — that yogi is considered the supreme one: this is the Gita's highest accolade. Not the yogi of deepest samādhi, not the one of greatest austerity or widest knowledge — but the one who has fully realized the unity of all experience. The inner and the outer, the mystical and the ethical, are here perfectly integrated.

6:33.text: Arjuna said: O Madhusūdana, this yoga of equanimity that You have described — I do not see a stable, lasting footing for it, due to the mind's restlessness.
6:33.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
yo 'yaṃ yogas tvayā proktaḥ sāmyena madhusūdana |
etasyāhaṃ na paśyāmi cañcalatvāt sthitiṃ sthirām ||33||
6:33.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
योऽयं योगस्त्वया प्रोक्तः साम्येन मधुसूदन ।
एतस्याहं न पश्यामि चञ्चलत्वात्स्थितिं स्थिराम् ॥ ३३ ॥
6:33.speaker: Arjuna
6:33.explanation: Arjuna's question here is the most universal and honest question in the entire Gita — the question that every sincere meditator has asked at some point. Krishna has described a beautiful, luminous yoga of equanimity, stillness, and self-mastery. Arjuna has listened with reverence and genuine interest. But now — speaking from his own direct experience of the mind — he raises the fundamental practical objection: 'cañcalatvāt sthitiṃ sthirām na paśyāmi' — due to the restlessness of the mind, I do not see a stable, enduring footing for this yoga. This is not pessimism or defeatism — it is the honest report of a person who has tried to hold the mind still and found it nearly impossible. The word 'cañcalatvāt' identifies the specific obstacle: cañcalatva, the quality of being restless, flickering, perpetually moving. Arjuna is not saying yoga is wrong — he is asking: is it actually achievable, given the nature of this mind? The address 'Madhusūdana' (slayer of the demon Madhu) carries an implicit appeal: you conquered that demon — can you help me conquer this one?

6:34.text: O Krishna, the mind is indeed restless, turbulent, powerful, and stubborn. I consider its restraint as difficult as restraining the wind.
6:34.transliteration:
cañcalaṃ hi manaḥ kṛṣṇa pramāthi balavad dṛḍham |
tasyāhaṃ nigrahaṃ manye vāyor iva suduṣkaram ||34||
6:34.devanagari:
चञ्चलं हि मनः कृष्ण प्रमाथि बलवद्दृढम् ।
तस्याहं निग्रहं मन्ये वायोरिव सुदुष्करम् ॥ ३४ ॥
6:34.speaker: Arjuna
6:34.explanation: Arjuna's description of the mind in this verse is one of the most accurate and universally relatable psychological portraits in all of world literature. Four precise attributes: (1) 'Cañcalam' — restless, flickering, never at rest for a single moment, moving incessantly from object to object. (2) 'Pramāthi' — the churner, the disturber: from 'pra-math' (to churn, to agitate violently). Just as the churning stick churns butter from curd, this mind churns and disrupts every settled state — peace, clarity, resolve, equanimity — leaving turbulence in its wake. (3) 'Balavat' — powerful, strong: it is not a weak, easily overridden impulse — it is a force of enormous momentum, built up through countless lifetimes of habit. (4) 'Dṛḍham' — firm, obstinate, stubborn: it clings to its established grooves and resists redirection with extraordinary tenacity. 'Vāyor iva suduṣkaram' — as difficult as restraining the wind. The simile is perfect: wind is invisible, ubiquitous, powerful, and cannot be grasped with the hand. Arjuna is not exaggerating — he is being precisely honest. And this honesty is the beginning of the genuine spiritual journey.

6:35.text: The Blessed Lord said: Undoubtedly, O mighty-armed, the mind is restless and difficult to restrain. But O son of Kuntī, it is brought under control by practice and by dispassion.
6:35.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
asaṃśayaṃ mahābāho mano durnigrahaṃ calam |
abhyāsena tu kaunteya vairāgyeṇa ca gṛhyate ||35||
6:35.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
असंशयं महाबाहो मनो दुर्निग्रहं चलम् ।
अभ्यासेन तु कौन्तेय वैराग्येण च गृह्यते ॥ ३५ ॥
6:35.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:35.explanation: Krishna's response is notable first for its complete honesty: 'asaṃśayam' — without any doubt, you are right. The mind IS restless and difficult to control. There is no minimization, no reassurance that it is easier than it appears, no spiritual bypassing of the genuine difficulty. This radical honesty is itself an act of profound respect for Arjuna's intelligence. Then: 'tu' — the pivotal 'but' that transforms the entire situation. 'Abhyāsena... vairāgyeṇa ca gṛhyate' — it is brought under control (gṛhyate: grasped, held, restrained) by two and only two means: (1) Abhyāsa — practice, the consistent, repeated, patient effort to return the mind to its object of meditation, again and again, without discouragement. This directly echoes Patañjali's Yoga Sūtra 1.12 ('abhyāsa-vairāgyābhyām tan-nirodhaḥ') — the control of the mind's fluctuations comes through practice and dispassion. (2) Vairāgya — dispassion, the progressive weakening of the mind's gravitational attraction toward sense-objects. These two work together: practice builds the positive groove of inner focus; vairāgya removes the competing pulls that draw the mind outward. They are the two blades of the scissors of mental mastery.

6:36.text: For one of uncontrolled self (mind), yoga is difficult to attain — such is my view. But for the self-controlled, striving person who uses the right means, it is possible to attain.
6:36.transliteration:
asaṃyatātmanā yogo duṣprāpa iti me matiḥ |
vaśyātmanā tu yatatā śakyo 'vāptum upāyataḥ ||36||
6:36.devanagari:
असंयतात्मना योगो दुष्प्राप इति मे मतिः ।
वश्यात्मना तु यतता शक्योऽवाप्तुमुपायतः ॥ ३६ ॥
6:36.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:36.explanation: 'Asaṃyatātmanā yogo duṣprāpaḥ' — for one whose mind is uncontrolled, yoga is difficult to attain: Krishna states this clearly and without sugar-coating. 'Iti me matiḥ' — such is my view, my considered opinion. This phrasing is notable: Krishna speaks with personal conviction rather than divine decree, inviting Arjuna to think along with him rather than simply accept a command. Then the counter-balancing affirmation: 'vaśyātmanā tu yatatā śakyo 'vāptum upāyataḥ' — but for the person whose mind is brought into control, who strives and applies the proper means (upāya — the specific tools of abhyāsa and vairāgya), yoga IS achievable (śakyaḥ). This verse is the Gita's most balanced statement about the difficulty and the possibility of yoga: neither falsely easy ('just try it!') nor falsely impossible ('it's for special souls only'). It is genuinely difficult AND genuinely possible — for anyone willing to do the inner work with the right understanding and the right tools. The path is real, the destination is real, and the means are knowable.

6:37.text: Arjuna said: O Krishna, the one who is endowed with faith yet lacking in effort, whose mind has strayed from yoga — not having attained perfection in yoga, what end does that one reach?
6:37.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
ayatiḥ śraddhayopeto yogāc calita-mānasaḥ |
aprāpya yoga-saṃsiddhiṃ kāṃ gatiṃ kṛṣṇa gacchati ||37||
6:37.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
अयतिः श्रद्धयोपेतो योगाच्चलितमानसः ।
अप्राप्य योगसंसिद्धिं कां गतिं कृष्ण गच्छति ॥ ३७ ॥
6:37.speaker: Arjuna
6:37.explanation: This is the most compassionate and universally anxious question in the entire Bhagavad Gita — a question that goes to the heart of every sincere spiritual seeker's deepest fear. Arjuna constructs a very specific and poignant character portrait: 'ayatiḥ' — one who lacks sustained effort, who began the path but could not maintain the discipline to the end. 'Śraddhayopetaḥ' — endowed with faith: crucially, this person had the right intention — they believed, they were sincere, they genuinely wanted liberation. 'Yogāc calita-mānasaḥ' — whose mind slipped from yoga: despite faith and some effort, the mind was pulled away — perhaps by life circumstances, perhaps by the sheer difficulty of maintaining the practice, perhaps by the old gravitational pull of desires. 'Aprāpya yoga-saṃsiddhim' — not having attained the full perfection of yoga. 'Kāṃ gatiṃ kṛṣṇa gacchati' — O Krishna, what destination does such a person reach? This question carries within it the anxiety of every person who has started a spiritual practice and then, looking at their own inconsistency, wondered: 'Is it too late for me? Was my partial effort wasted?' Krishna's answer will be the most reassuring passage in the Gita.

6:38.text: O mighty-armed, does that one not perish like a torn cloud — fallen from both, without support, bewildered on the path to Brahman?
6:38.transliteration:
kaccin nobhaya-vibhraṣṭaś chinnābhram iva naśyati |
apratiṣṭho mahābāho vimūḍho brahmaṇaḥ pathi ||38||
6:38.devanagari:
कच्चिन्नोभयविभ्रष्टश्छिन्नाभ्रमिव नश्यति ।
अप्रतिष्ठो महाबाहो विमूढो ब्रह्मणः पथि ॥ ३८ ॥
6:38.speaker: Arjuna
6:38.explanation: Arjuna's fear reaches its articulate peak in this verse, expressed through one of the most beautiful and melancholy similes in Sanskrit poetry. 'Ubhaya-vibhraṣṭaḥ' — fallen from both: neither the worldly householder's life (which was abandoned to pursue yoga) nor the yogī's liberation (which was not attained due to incomplete practice). The seeker who gave up the world for the spirit and then failed to reach the spirit — stranded between two worlds, belonging to neither. 'Apratiṣṭhaḥ' — without foundation, without ground beneath one's feet: neither world offers stability. 'Vimūḍho brahmaṇaḥ pathi' — bewildered, confused on the path to Brahman: lost on the very journey one had set out upon with such hope. 'Chinnābhram iva naśyati' — does that one perish like a torn cloud? This simile is exquisitely precise: a fragment of cloud separated from the whole — it cannot complete its journey to become rain, cannot return to the sky to rejoin the cloud-mass, cannot rest on the earth — it simply dissipates in the air, without accomplishing anything. Arjuna fears that the failed yogi is exactly like this: dissolving into nothing, with no world to call home.

6:39.text: O Krishna, You alone are fit to dispel this doubt of mine completely. No one other than You is fit to cut through this doubt.
6:39.transliteration:
etan me saṃśayaṃ kṛṣṇa chettum arhasy aśeṣataḥ |
tvad anyaḥ saṃśayasyāsya chettā na hy upapadyate ||39||
6:39.devanagari:
एतन्मे संशयं कृष्ण छेत्तुमर्हस्यशेषतः ।
त्वदन्यः संशयस्यास्य छेत्ता न ह्युपपद्यते ॥ ३९ ॥
6:39.speaker: Arjuna
6:39.explanation: 'Etan me saṃśayaṃ kṛṣṇa chettum arhasi aśeṣataḥ' — You, O Krishna, are fit to cut through (chettum: to sever, as with a sword — the same root as jñāna-asi, the sword of knowledge in 4.42) this doubt of mine without remainder (aśeṣataḥ: completely, leaving not even a fragment of doubt unresolved). Arjuna is asking not for a partial answer or a philosophical discussion but for a complete, root-level resolution. 'Tvad anyaḥ saṃśayasyāsya chettā na hi upapadyate' — no one other than You is suited (upapadyate: fit, appropriate, competent) to cut this doubt.
