This declaration of absolute trust in Krishna as the only adequate teacher for this question is both a statement of Krishna's unique spiritual authority and an expression of Arjuna's genuine and humble recognition of his own limitation. The seeker acknowledges: 'I cannot resolve this by myself; my own intellect cannot reach here.' This surrender of the problem to the Guru is itself a form of spiritual practice. Krishna's answer, beginning in the next verse, is the most loving and assuring in the entire Gita.

6:40.text: The Blessed Lord said: O Pārtha, neither in this world nor in the next does destruction come to that one. My dear one (tāta), one who does good never comes to an evil end.
6:40.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
pārtha naiveha nāmutra vināśas tasya vidyate |
na hi kalyāṇa-kṛt kaścid durgatiṃ tāta gacchati ||40||
6:40.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
पार्थ नैवेह नामुत्र विनाशस्तस्य विद्यते ।
न हि कल्याणकृत्कश्चिद्दुर्गतिं तात गच्छति ॥ ४० ॥
6:40.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:40.explanation: This verse contains what may be the single most comforting and unconditionally assuring statement in the entire Bhagavad Gita — the response of the Divine to the fear of the earnest, imperfect seeker. 'Pārtha naiveha nāmutra vināśas tasya vidyate' — O son of Pritha, neither in this world (iha) nor in the next (amutra) does destruction (vināśa) exist for that one. The yogī who strove sincerely but did not reach the goal is NOT lost. The sincere effort itself has eternal value and consequence. Then — and this address is exquisite: 'tāta' — this is the Sanskrit word a father uses for a beloved child, or a teacher for a dear student, conveying warmth, protection, and closeness. Krishna calls Arjuna 'tāta' — 'my dear one' — the tenderness in this single word is a universe of compassion. 'Na hi kalyāṇa-kṛt kaścid durgatiṃ tāta gacchati' — one who does good (kalyāṇa-kṛt) NEVER comes to an evil end (durgati). This is the Gita's fundamental moral law, stated without qualification: the direction of sincere aspiration toward the good is never wasted. The effort itself is transformative, regardless of whether the final goal was reached in this lifetime.

6:41.text: The fallen yogi attains the worlds of the meritorious, dwelling there for immeasurable years, and then takes birth in the home of the pure and prosperous.
6:41.transliteration:
prāpya puṇya-kṛtāṃ lokān uṣitvā śāśvatīḥ samāḥ |
śucīnāṃ śrīmatāṃ gehe yoga-bhraṣṭo 'bhijāyate ||41||
6:41.devanagari:
प्राप्य पुण्यकृतां लोकानुषित्वा शाश्वतीः समाः ।
शुचीनां श्रीमतां गेहे योगभ्रष्टोऽभिजायते ॥ ४१ ॥
6:41.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:41.explanation: Krishna now gives the detailed account of the fallen yogi's trajectory — and it is entirely reassuring. First: 'prāpya puṇya-kṛtāṃ lokān' — attaining the worlds (lokas) of those who have performed meritorious deeds: the accumulated merit of sincere yoga practice, even incomplete, earns the yogi access to elevated post-mortem states — the higher celestial realms where the fruits of good karma are experienced in states of expanded awareness and joy. 'Uṣitvā śāśvatīḥ samāḥ' — dwelling there for immeasurable, long stretches of time (śāśvatīḥ samāḥ: enduring spans of years). The enjoyment of these elevated states is the fruit of the saṃskāras (deep impressions) created by the yoga practice. Then: 'śucīnāṃ śrīmatāṃ gehe yoga-bhraṣṭo 'bhijāyate' — the fallen yogi takes birth in the household of the pure (śucī: morally and spiritually clean, dharmic) and the prosperous (śrīmān: endowed with wealth, culture, and good fortune). This is not accidental — it is the karmic continuation of the yogi's spiritual trajectory. A family of genuine spiritual culture, moral integrity, and material stability will provide the ideal conditions for the yogi to resume their interrupted practice in the next life.

6:42.text: Or else one takes birth in the very family of wise yogis — for such a birth as this is truly very rare in the world.
6:42.transliteration:
athavā yoginām eva kule bhavati dhīmatām |
etad dhi durlabhataraṃ loke janma yad īdṛśam ||42||
6:42.devanagari:
अथवा योगिनामेव कुले भवति धीमताम् ।
एतद्धि दुर्लभतरं लोके जन्म यदीदृशम् ॥ ४२ ॥
6:42.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:42.explanation: 'Athavā' — or alternatively, an even more fortunate possibility: 'yoginām eva kule bhavati dhīmatām' — the fallen yogi takes birth in the very family of wise (dhīmat) yogis. This is a step beyond verse 41's description of a morally upright and prosperous family. A family of actual yogis means: the child is born into an environment where the value of yoga is not merely theoretical but lived — where meditation, scripture, self-discipline, and spiritual aspiration are the natural texture of daily life. The child breathes yoga from birth. 'Etad dhi durlabhataraṃ loke janmaṃ yad īdṛśam' — such a birth is truly exceedingly rare (durlabhataram: comparative degree — rarer than rare) in this world. This verse affirms the extraordinary karmic continuity that yoga practice creates: the effort of one lifetime does not disappear at death but shapes the conditions of the next birth with remarkable precision. The fallen yogi's deep spiritual saṃskāras act as a karmic compass, steering the next incarnation toward the most conducive possible environment for continuation of the journey.

6:43.text: There, that one regains the intellectual connection (buddhi-saṃyoga) from the previous body, and strives even further toward perfection, O joy of the Kurus.
6:43.transliteration:
tatra taṃ buddhi-saṃyogaṃ labhate paurva-dehikam |
yatate ca tato bhūyaḥ saṃsiddhau kuru-nandana ||43||
6:43.devanagari:
तत्र तं बुद्धिसंयोगं लभते पौर्वदेहिकम् ।
यतते च ततो भूयः संसिद्धौ कुरुनन्दन ॥ ४३ ॥
6:43.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:43.explanation: 'Tatra taṃ buddhi-saṃyogaṃ labhate paurva-dehikam' — there, in that new birth, the soul retrieves the 'buddhi-saṃyoga' (the connection of the intellect with yoga wisdom) that was established in the previous body. This is one of the Gita's most extraordinary affirmations: the deep impressions (saṃskāras) of genuine spiritual practice are not lost at death. They are carried as subtle tendencies in the soul's subtle body and re-manifest in the new incarnation as an unusual native clarity, a natural spiritual bent, a spontaneous affinity for meditation and wisdom that other children in the same family may not have. This is why some children seem spiritually precocious — they are carrying the saṃskāras of their previous lives' practice. 'Tato bhūyaḥ saṃsiddhau yatate' — from that point (where they left off), one strives even further (bhūyaḥ: more, beyond) toward full perfection (saṃsiddhi). The spiritual journey is not reset to zero by death — it is resumed from the exact spiritual altitude reached in the previous life, like bookmarking a page in a book that spans many volumes.

6:44.text: By that very previous practice, he is carried along irresistibly — even without his will. Even one who merely inquires about yoga transcends the Verbal Brahman (the Vedic rituals).
6:44.transliteration:
pūrvābhyāsena tenaiva hriyate hy avaśo 'pi saḥ |
jijñāsur api yogasya śabda-brahmātivartate ||44||
6:44.devanagari:
पूर्वाभ्यासेन तेनैव ह्रियते ह्यवशोऽपि सः ।
जिज्ञासुरपि योगस्य शब्दब्रह्मातिवर्तते ॥ ४४ ॥
6:44.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:44.explanation: 'Pūrvābhyāsena tenaiva hriyate hy avaśo 'pi saḥ' — by that very previous practice (from the prior life), the soul is 'hriyate' (carried along, drawn, irresistibly pulled) — even 'avaśaḥ' (involuntarily, without conscious intention or effort). This is the extraordinary testimony to the power of spiritual saṃskāras: deep yoga practice creates grooves in the subtle body that are so powerful, so fundamental, that they become an automatic current drawing the soul toward yoga in the next life — whether the person consciously decides to practice or not. The soul's trajectory is set. 'Jijñāsur api yogasya śabda-brahmātivartate' — even someone who merely inquires into yoga (jijñāsuḥ: one with sincere inquiry, not yet a practitioner but genuinely seeking) transcends (atikramati) the 'śabda-brahma' — the domain of Vedic ritual prescription and the fruits it produces in higher worlds. Yoga's direct inquiry into the Self takes one beyond the entire domain of ritual action and its heavenly rewards, to the source itself.

6:45.text: But the yogi who strives with effort, whose sins have been thoroughly purified, and who has been perfected over many births — then attains the supreme goal.
6:45.transliteration:
prayatnād yatamānas tu yogī saṃśuddha-kilbiṣaḥ |
aneka-janma-saṃsiddhas tato yāti parāṃ gatim ||45||
6:45.devanagari:
प्रयत्नाद्यतमानस्तु योगी संशुद्धकिल्बिषः ।
अनेकजन्मसंसिद्धस्ततो याति परां गतिम् ॥ ४५ ॥
6:45.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:45.explanation: 'Prayatnād yatamānas tu' — but the yogi who strives with sustained, deliberate effort: 'prayatnāt' (with effort, intentionally) indicates that this is not passive evolution but active, willed engagement with the practice across multiple lifetimes. 'Saṃśuddha-kilbiṣaḥ' — whose kilbiṣas (sins, impurities, the accumulated karmic residues of many lifetimes of ignorance and selfish action) have been 'saṃśuddha' — thoroughly cleansed, not just reduced but purified to completion. This purification is the multi-lifetime work of sustained yoga practice. 'Aneka-janma-saṃsiddhaḥ' — perfected through many (aneka) births (janma): the word 'saṃsiddha' means 'thoroughly accomplished, fully ripened.' The yogī does not abandon the journey between lives — each life is a chapter in a single, unbroken story of spiritual ascent. 'Tato yāti parāṃ gatim' — then (after this multi-life ripening is complete) attains the supreme destination: mokṣa, Brahman, the ultimate liberation that is the end and the fullness of all spiritual seeking.

6:46.text: A yogi is superior to ascetics, superior even to those of knowledge, and superior to ritual performers. Therefore, O Arjuna, be a yogi.
6:46.transliteration:
tapasvibhyo 'dhiko yogī jñānibhyo 'pi mato 'dhikaḥ |
karmibhyaś cādhiko yogī tasmād yogī bhavārjuna ||46||
6:46.devanagari:
तपस्विभ्योऽधिको योगी ज्ञानिभ्योऽपि मतोऽधिकः ।
कर्मिभ्यश्चाधिको योगी तस्माद्योगी भवार्जुन ॥ ४६ ॥
6:46.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:46.explanation: This verse delivers the chapter's comparative verdict and its direct practical conclusion. Three comparisons establish yoga's supremacy: (1) 'Tapasvibhyo 'dhiko yogī' — the yogi excels over tapasvīs (ascetics who practise physical and mental austerities). Tapas develops willpower and purifies the body-mind, but it may lack the inner depth of sustained meditative integration. Yoga encompasses tapas and goes beyond it. (2) 'Jñānibhyo 'pi mato 'dhikaḥ' — the yogi is considered superior even to jñānīs (those who possess knowledge, primarily intellectual-philosophical understanding). Theoretical knowledge that has not been deepened and confirmed through direct meditative experience remains incomplete. The yogi's knowledge is not merely conceptual — it is vijnāna (realized, direct knowing). (3) 'Karmibhyaś cādhiko yogī' — and the yogi excels over karmīs (those engaged in ritual Vedic action). Ritual action produces specific limited results — temporary heavenly rewards, material benefits — not mokṣa. Yoga's fruits are unlimited and final. 'Tasmād yogī bhavārjuna' — Therefore, O Arjuna, BE a yogi. This is the most direct, concentrated imperative in Chapter 6: not 'consider becoming,' not 'understand yoga theoretically' — BE one. The entire magnificent teaching of this chapter culminates in this single, unambiguous instruction.

6:47.text: And among all yogis, the one who worships Me with inner self absorbed in Me, filled with faith — that one is considered by Me to be the most perfectly integrated (yuktatama).
6:47.transliteration:
yoginām api sarveṣāṃ mad-gatenāntar-ātmanā |
śraddhāvān bhajate yo māṃ sa me yuktatamo mataḥ ||47||
6:47.devanagari:
योगिनामपि सर्वेषां मद्गतेनान्तरात्मना ।
श्रद्धावान्भजते यो मां स मे युक्ततमो मतः ॥ ४७ ॥
6:47.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
6:47.explanation: This is the final and supreme verse of Chapter 6 — the chapter's climactic declaration and one of the most important verses in the entire Bhagavad Gita. 'Yoginām api sarveṣāṃ' — among ALL yogis: this includes every category of yogin — the tapasvin, the jñānī, the karma yogin, the rāja yogin, the dhyāna yogin — all of them. Among all these: 'mad-gatenāntar-ātmanā śraddhāvān bhajate yo māṃ' — the one whose inner self (antar-ātmā — the innermost being, the deepest level of subjectivity) has gone into Me (mad-gata: moved into, absorbed into, dissolved into Me); who has faith (śraddhāvān — infused with śraddhā: not merely belief but a living, loving trust that opens the heart); and who 'bhajate' (worships, loves, serves with devotion). 'Sa me yuktatamo mataḥ' — that one, in My considered view (matah: my opinion, my declaration), is the MOST completely united (yuktatama: the superlative of yukta — the most yogayukta of all). This final verse integrates the entire teaching of Chapter 6: meditation (dhyāna) without devotion (bhakti) lacks a living centre; devotion without meditative depth lacks the ground of silence. The union of both — the inner self absorbed in the Divine with faith and love — is the supreme yoga. This verse prepares the ground for everything that follows in the Gita's great bhakti teachings of Chapters 7–12.

7:1.text: The Blessed Lord said: O Pārtha, with mind attached to Me, practicing yoga taking refuge in Me — hear how you shall know Me completely and without doubt.
7:1.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
mayy āsakta-manāḥ pārtha yogaṃ yuñjan mad-āśrayaḥ |
asaṃśayaṃ samagraṃ māṃ yathā jñāsyasi tac chṛṇu ||1||
7:1.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
मय्यासक्तमनाः पार्थ योगं युञ्जन्मदाश्रयः ।
असंशयं समग्रं मां यथा ज्ञास्यसि तच्छृणु ॥ १ ॥
7:1.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:1.explanation: This opening verse of Chapter 7 makes a solemn promise: what follows will be 'samagram' (complete, total, without remainder) knowledge of the divine. Three preconditions are stipulated for the student: 'mayy āsakta-manāḥ' (mind devoted and attached to Me) — not mere intellectual curiosity but genuine love and orientation; 'mad-āśrayaḥ' (taking refuge in Me alone) — no divided allegiance; 'yogaṃ yuñjan' (practicing yoga) — sustained, disciplined effort. 'Asaṃśayam' (without doubt) — this knowledge, properly received, dissolves doubt entirely. 'Samagram' (wholly, completely) — Chapter 7 promises the full self-disclosure of the Absolute, not a fragment. The combination of devotion, surrender, and practice constitutes the readiness required to receive it.

7:2.text: I will declare to you this knowledge (jñāna) along with wisdom (vijñāna) completely, without remainder — knowing which, nothing else here remains to be known.
7:2.transliteration:
jñānaṃ te 'haṃ sa-vijñānam idaṃ vakṣyāmy aśeṣataḥ |
yaj jñātvā neha bhūyo 'nyaj jñātavyam avaśiṣyate ||2||
7:2.devanagari:
ज्ञानं तेऽहं सविज्ञानमिदं वक्ष्याम्यशेषतः ।
यज्ज्ञात्वा नेह भूयोऽन्यज्ज्ञातव्यमवशिष्यते ॥ २ ॥
7:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:2.explanation: 'Jñānam' (theoretical knowledge) and 'vijñānam' (experiential wisdom, direct realization) together constitute the complete knowing that the Gita offers. Jñāna is the conceptual map; vijñāna is the direct experience of the territory itself. Both are needed: jñāna without vijñāna remains intellectual; vijñāna without jñāna lacks discriminative clarity. 'Aśeṣataḥ' (without any remainder, completely) — nothing is held back. 'Neha bhūyo 'nyaj jñātavyam avaśiṣyate' (nothing else here remains to be known) — a declaration mirroring the Upaniṣadic proclamation: 'By knowing what, is everything else known?' (Muṇḍaka 1.1.3). To know the ground of being is to know all that rests upon it.

7:3.text: Among thousands of human beings, perhaps one strives for perfection (siddhi); and among those who strive and succeed, perhaps one knows Me in truth (tattvataḥ).
7:3.transliteration:
manuṣyāṇāṃ sahasreṣu kaścid yatati siddhaye |
yatatām api siddhānāṃ kaścin māṃ vetti tattvataḥ ||3||
7:3.devanagari:
मनुष्याणां सहस्रेषु कश्चिद्यतति सिद्धये ।
यततामपि सिद्धानां कश्चिन्मां वेत्ति तत्त्वतः ॥ ३ ॥
7:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:3.explanation: This verse establishes the rarity of genuine self-knowledge in three ascending stages: of all human beings, only a rare few even begin seeking liberation; of those seekers, fewer still attain siddhi (spiritual accomplishment); and even among the accomplished, perhaps one knows Krishna 'tattvataḥ' — in his true, essential nature, not conceptually or partially but directly and completely. This is not pessimism; it is the Gita calibrating the student's understanding of what is being offered. The very rarity of this knowledge elevates what follows. It also explains why, despite millennia of human civilization, such wisdom has always been precious and uncommon — it requires an exceptional convergence of preparation, sincerity, and grace.

7:4.text: Earth, water, fire, air, ether, mind, intellect, and ego — this is My nature divided eightfold, the lower nature (aparā prakṛti).
7:4.transliteration:
bhūmir āpo 'nalo vāyuḥ khaṃ mano buddhir eva ca |
ahaṃkāra itīyaṃ me bhinnā prakṛtir aṣṭadhā ||4||
7:4.devanagari:
भूमिरापोऽनलो वायुः खं मनो बुद्धिरेव च ।
अहङ्कार इतीयं मे भिन्ना प्रकृतिरष्टधा ॥ ४ ॥
7:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:4.explanation: 'Aparā prakṛti' (the lower nature) constitutes the totality of material existence — both outer and inner. The five gross elements (pañca mahābhūtas): earth (bhūmi), water (āpaḥ), fire (anala), air (vāyu), and ether (kha); and the three instruments of inner experience: mind (manas, the processing faculty), intellect (buddhi, the discriminating faculty), and ego (ahaṃkāra, the individualizing faculty). 'Bhinnā... aṣṭadhā' (divided eightfold) — though appearing multiple and diverse, these eight are one continuous nature emanating from a single source. This aligns with Sāṃkhya cosmology but situates it within theistic Vedānta: these are not independent realities but expressions of the divine.

7:5.text: O mighty-armed one, this (aparā) is the lower nature. Know My other nature — the higher (parā), which is the life-principle (jīva-bhūtā), by which this entire world is sustained.
7:5.transliteration:
apareyam itas tv anyāṃ prakṛtiṃ viddhi me parām |
jīva-bhūtāṃ mahābāho yayedaṃ dhāryate jagat ||5||
7:5.devanagari:
अपरेयमितस्त्वन्यां प्रकृतिं विद्धि मे पराम् ।
जीवभूतां महाबाहो ययेदं धार्यते जगत् ॥ ५ ॥
7:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:5.explanation: 'Parā prakṛti' (the higher nature) is the conscious, living principle — the jīva-element — that animates and sustains the inert material of aparā prakṛti. 'Jīva-bhūtām' (having become the jīva, i.e., the individualized life-force and consciousness in all beings) — this higher nature is what makes matter come alive, what turns a biological body into a living, experiencing being. 'Yayedaṃ dhāryate jagat' (by which this world is sustained) — without this conscious principle, the material world would have no principle of coherence or purpose. This Gita doctrine anticipates and answers the philosophical 'hard problem of consciousness': consciousness (parā) is not reducible to matter (aparā); it is a fundamentally distinct and higher principle.

7:6.text: Know that all beings have their womb (origin) in these two natures. I am the source (prabhava) and dissolution (pralaya) of the entire universe.
7:6.transliteration:
etad-yonīni bhūtāni sarvāṇīty upadhāraya |
ahaṃ kṛtsnasya jagataḥ prabhavaḥ pralayas tathā ||6||
7:6.devanagari:
एतद्योनीनि भूतानि सर्वाणीत्युपधारय ।
अहं कृत्स्नस्य जगतः प्रभवः प्रलयस्तथा ॥ ६ ॥
7:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:6.explanation: 'Etad-yonīni bhūtāni sarvāṇi' (all beings have their womb in these two natures) — every living being is a combination of aparā (body-mind complex) and parā (the conscious life-principle). These two prakṛtis together constitute the matrix of all existence. 'Ahaṃ kṛtsnasya jagataḥ prabhavaḥ pralayas tathā' — this is one of the Gita's grandest statements: Krishna declares himself the source and the dissolution of the entire universe. Not merely a deity within the universe, but the very ground from which it arises and into which it returns. This is the monotheistic heart of the Gita's theology: one absolute reality as both efficient and material cause of all existence.

7:7.text: O Dhananjaya, there is nothing whatsoever higher than Me. All this is strung on Me as pearls are strung on a thread.
7:7.transliteration:
mattaḥ parataraṃ nānyat kiñcid asti dhanañjaya |
mayi sarvam idaṃ protaṃ sūtre maṇi-gaṇā iva ||7||
7:7.devanagari:
मत्तः परतरं नान्यत्किञ्चिदस्ति धनञ्जय ।
मयि सर्वमिदं प्रोतं सूत्रे मणिगणा इव ॥ ७ ॥
7:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:7.explanation: 'Mattaḥ parataraṃ nānyat kiñcid asti' — this is one of the Gita's most absolute metaphysical declarations: nothing exists beyond, above, or outside of Krishna. He is the ultimate ground of all being — not one entity among others, but the very substratum of existence itself. The simile 'sūtre maṇi-gaṇā iva' (like gems strung on a thread) is one of Sanskrit literature's most luminous images: the gems (the multiplicity of beings and phenomena in the world) are all individually distinct, beautiful, and varied — yet all threaded on a single string (Brahman/Krishna). The string is invisible within the gems but sustains them all. Unity within diversity; the Absolute within the relative — this is the Gita's central vision.

7:8.text: O Kaunteya, I am the taste in water, the light in the moon and sun; I am the sacred syllable Om in all the Vedas, sound in ether, and the virility in human beings.
7:8.transliteration:
raso 'ham apsu kaunteya prabhāsmi śaśi-sūryayoḥ |
praṇavaḥ sarva-vedeṣu śabdaḥ khe pauruṣaṃ nṛṣu ||8||
7:8.devanagari:
रसोऽहमप्सु कौन्तेय प्रभास्मि शशिसूर्ययोः ।
प्रणवः सर्ववेदेषु शब्दः खे पौरुषं नृषु ॥ ८ ॥
7:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:8.explanation: Beginning here and extending through 7.11, Krishna maps his presence through the essential quality of each element and phenomenon — not claiming to be everything, but to be the essence, the defining quality, the 'isness' of each thing. 'Rasaḥ' (taste) in water — water's fundamental nature that distinguishes it as life-sustaining; 'prabhā' (light) in moon and sun — the luminosity that makes them what they are; 'praṇava' (Om) in all Vedas — the primordial sound-vibration from which all sacred knowledge flows; 'śabdaḥ' (sound) in ether — the element of ether whose defining quality is its capacity to carry sound; 'pauruṣam' (virility, strength, vital capacity) in human beings — the animating force of human excellence. The divine is the irreducible quality in each thing.

7:9.text: I am the sacred fragrance of the earth and the brilliance in fire; I am the life in all beings and the austerity (tapas) in ascetics.
7:9.transliteration:
puṇyo gandhaḥ pṛthivyāṃ ca tejaś cāsmi vibhāvasau |
jīvanaṃ sarva-bhūteṣu tapaś cāsmi tapasviṣu ||9||
7:9.devanagari:
पुण्यो गन्धः पृथिव्यां च तेजश्चास्मि विभावसौ ।
जीवनं सर्वभूतेषु तपश्चास्मि तपस्विषु ॥ ९ ॥
7:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:9.explanation: 'Puṇyo gandhaḥ pṛthivyāṃ' (the sacred fragrance of the earth) — the primal, nourishing scent of earth (particularly the petrichor after rain) that connects all living beings to their planetary home. 'Tejaś cāsmi vibhāvasau' (the brilliance in fire) — fire's defining quality of heat and light, its transformative capacity. 'Jīvanaṃ sarva-bhūteṣu' (life in all beings) — not merely biological life but the animating principle itself, the parā prakṛti incarnate in every living being. 'Tapaś cāsmi tapasviṣu' (tapas in ascetics) — the heat of spiritual discipline, the concentrated effort of transformation that austerity generates. In each case, the divine is the functional core — that without which the thing would not be itself.

7:10.text: Know Me as the eternal seed of all beings, O Pārtha. I am the intelligence of the intelligent and the brilliance of the brilliant.
7:10.transliteration:
bījaṃ māṃ sarva-bhūtānāṃ viddhi pārtha sanātanam |
buddhir buddhimatām asmi tejas tejasvinām aham ||10||
7:10.devanagari:
बीजं मां सर्वभूतानां विद्धि पार्थ सनातनम् ।
बुद्धिर्बुद्धिमतामस्मि तेजस्तेजस्विनामहम् ॥ १० ॥
7:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:10.explanation: 'Bījaṃ... sanātanam' (the eternal seed of all beings) — the seed metaphor is profound: the entire potential of the tree exists invisibly within the seed. Brahman/Krishna is the eternal, inexhaustible potential from which all beings arise, each unfolding their specific nature as a tree unfolds from its seed. The seed is not the tree, yet the tree is entirely implicit in the seed. 'Buddhir buddhimatām' (the intelligence of the intelligent) — wherever genuine discriminative wisdom functions, that is Krishna's presence. 'Tejas tejasvinām' (the brilliance of the brilliant) — the luminous quality, the natural authority and radiance, of those who possess genuine vitality and spiritual power. The best in every being is a window to the divine.

7:11.text: O best of the Bharatas, I am the strength of the strong that is devoid of desire and passion; and in beings I am the desire that is not opposed to dharma.
7:11.transliteration:
balaṃ balavatāṃ cāhaṃ kāma-rāga-vivarjitam |
dharamāviruddho bhūteṣu kāmo 'smi bharatarṣabha ||11||
7:11.devanagari:
बलं बलवतां चाहं कामरागविवर्जितम् ।
धर्माविरुद्धो भूतेषु कामोऽस्मि भरतर्षभ ॥ ११ ॥
7:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:11.explanation: 'Balaṃ... kāma-rāga-vivarjitam' (strength devoid of desire and passion) — pure power uncorrupted by selfish motive; strength in service of what is right rather than what is wanted. This is the strength of the truly mighty. 'Dharmāviruddho bhūteṣu kāmo 'smi' (I am the desire in beings that is not contrary to dharma) — this is a gracious acknowledgment: not all desire is the enemy named in Chapter 3. Desire aligned with dharma — the wish for knowledge, for love, for meaningful work, for rightful family life — is itself a manifestation of the divine. The Gita does not demand the annihilation of all desire, only the sublimation of desire contrary to dharma. Life lived rightly is itself sacred.

7:12.text: Whatever states exist — sāttvic, rājasic, and tāmasic — know that all of them proceed from Me alone. Yet I am not in them; they are in Me.
7:12.transliteration:
ye caiva sāttvikā bhāvā rājasās tāmasāś ca ye |
matta eveti tān viddhi na tv ahaṃ teṣu te mayi ||12||
7:12.devanagari:
ये चैव सात्त्विका भावा राजसास्तामसाश्च ये ।
मत्त एवेति तान्विद्धि न त्वहं तेषु ते मयि ॥ १२ ॥
7:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:12.explanation: This verse articulates one of the Gita's most philosophically precise formulations of the divine-world relationship. All three categories of phenomenal existence (the guṇa-states) proceed from Krishna — he is their source. Yet 'na tv ahaṃ teṣu' (I am not in them) — he is not defined, limited, or conditioned by any of them. 'Te mayi' (they are in Me) — the world is in the divine, not the divine reduced to the world. This is the classic Vedāntic position: the Absolute is the ground of the relative, yet transcends it. Like space containing all objects but not being limited by any object; like consciousness containing all experiences but not being identified with any experience. Immanent yet transcendent — both simultaneously.

7:13.text: Deluded by these three guṇa-constituted states, this entire world fails to recognize Me — who am beyond them, imperishable (avyaya).
7:13.transliteration:
tribhir guṇamayair bhāvair ebhiḥ sarvam idaṃ jagat |
mohitaṃ nābhijānāti māmebhyaḥ param avyayam ||13||
7:13.devanagari:
त्रिभिर्गुणमयैर्भावैरेभिः सर्वमिदं जगत् ।
मोहितं नाभिजानाति मामेभ्यः परमव्ययम् ॥ १३ ॥
7:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:13.explanation: 'Tribhir guṇamayair bhāvaiḥ' (by these three guṇa-made states) — the entire phenomenal universe, including the human mind, is constituted by the interplay of sattva, rajas, and tamas. This is not just the physical world but the whole domain of thought, feeling, perception, and experience. 'Mohitam' (deluded, bewildered) — the guṇas create a totalizing enchantment; the mind caught in guṇa-experience mistakes the guṇas for ultimate reality. 'Māmebhyaḥ param avyayam' (Me, who is beyond these, imperishable) — the eternal, unchanging ground of Consciousness that is Krishna's true nature remains invisible to the guṇa-bound mind. This is the function of māyā: not an illusion that does not exist, but a real power that prevents us from seeing what is most real.

7:14.text: Indeed, this divine māyā of Mine, constituted of the guṇas, is difficult to cross. But those who take refuge in Me alone — they cross beyond this māyā.
7:14.transliteration:
daivī hy eṣā guṇamayī mama māyā duratyayā |
mām eva ye prapadyante māyām etāṃ taranti te ||14||
7:14.devanagari:
दैवी ह्येषा गुणमयी मम माया दुरत्यया ।
मामेव ये प्रपद्यन्ते मायामेतां तरन्ति ते ॥ १४ ॥
7:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:14.explanation: 'Daivī' (divine) — māyā is not a human error or an intellectual mistake; it is a cosmic power (śakti) that belongs to the divine itself. 'Duratyayā' (exceedingly difficult to cross) — māyā cannot be overcome by willpower, intellect, or asceticism alone; it is the Lord's own power and only the Lord can withdraw it. 'Mām eva ye prapadyante' (those who take refuge in Me alone) — 'eva' (alone, exclusively) and 'prapadyante' (fully surrender, take complete refuge) indicate that the resolution requires genuine, undivided orientation toward the divine. The logic is perfect: māyā is Krishna's power; only Krishna can lift it. No outside agent can deliver what only the source can give. This verse is the theological heart of the Gita's path of surrender.

7:15.text: The evildoers, the foolish, the lowest among men, those whose wisdom has been stripped by māyā, and those who have taken refuge in the demonic nature — they do not take refuge in Me.
7:15.transliteration:
na māṃ duṣkṛtino mūḍhāḥ prapadyante narādhamāḥ |
māyayāpahṛta-jñānā āsuraṃ bhāvam āśritāḥ ||15||
7:15.devanagari:
न मां दुष्कृतिनो मूढाः प्रपद्यन्ते नराधमाः ।
माययापहृतज्ञाना आसुरं भावमाश्रिताः ॥ १५ ॥
7:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:15.explanation: After verse 14's promise (those who take refuge are freed), this verse identifies those who do not seek that refuge and why. Four types are named: 'duṣkṛtinaḥ' (evildoers, those whose will is oriented toward harm); 'mūḍhāḥ' (the foolish, those blinded by ignorance); 'narādhamāḥ' (the lowest among humans — those who have squandered their human capacity for wisdom); and 'māyayāpahṛta-jñānāḥ' (those whose knowledge has been stolen by māyā — they may once have had understanding, but habitual wrong-living has dissolved it). 'Āsuraṃ bhāvam āśritāḥ' (those who have taken refuge in the demonic disposition) — pride, denial of the divine, and the conviction of absolute self-sufficiency block the very surrender that liberation requires.

7:16.text: O Bharatarṣabha Arjuna, four kinds of virtuous people (sukṛtinaḥ) worship Me: the distressed (ārta), the seeker of knowledge (jijñāsu), the seeker of wealth (arthārthī), and the wise (jñānī).
7:16.transliteration:
catur-vidhā bhajante māṃ janāḥ sukṛtino 'rjuna |
ārto jijñāsur arthārthī jñānī ca bharatarṣabha ||16||
7:16.devanagari:
चतुर्विधा भजन्ते मां जनाः सुकृतिनोऽर्जुन ।
आर्तो जिज्ञासुरर्थार्थी ज्ञानी च भरतर्षभ ॥ १६ ॥
7:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:16.explanation: 'Sukṛtinaḥ' (the meritorious, those of good action) — all four are positively characterized; none are condemned. Their motivations differ in maturity and orientation, but all face toward the divine. The 'ārta' (the distressed, the afflicted) worships out of crisis — this is perhaps the most common entry-point into genuine prayer, when suffering breaks through the ego's self-sufficiency. The 'jijñāsu' (the curious seeker) is motivated by genuine desire to understand reality. The 'arthārthī' (the seeker of worldly welfare) prays for material benefit — still motivated by desire, but directed toward the divine. The 'jñānī' (the wise one) worships out of pure knowledge. All four are embraced — the Gita's spiritual vision is inclusive rather than exclusionary.

7:17.text: Of these, the jñānī who is ever-united (nitya-yukta) and of single-pointed devotion (eka-bhakti) is distinguished. For I am exceedingly dear to the jñānī, and that one is exceedingly dear to Me.
7:17.transliteration:
teṣāṃ jñānī nitya-yukta eka-bhaktir viśiṣyate |
priyo hi jñānino 'tyartham ahaṃ sa ca mama priyaḥ ||17||
7:17.devanagari:
तेषां ज्ञानी नित्ययुक्त एकभक्तिर्विशिष्यते ।
प्रियो हि ज्ञानिनोऽत्यर्थमहं स च मम प्रियः ॥ १७ ॥
7:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:17.explanation: 'Nitya-yukta' (ever-united, always connected) — the jñānī's devotion is not situational (crisis, desire, curiosity) but continuous; the divine is always present, always primary. 'Eka-bhakti' (single-pointed devotion) — undivided, without the division of attention across multiple goals and objects. These two qualities make the jñānī's worship qualitatively different from the others — though all four are accepted. What follows is one of the Gita's most intimate moments: 'Priyo hi jñānino 'tyartham aham' (I am exceedingly dear to the jñānī). And in return: 'sa ca mama priyaḥ' (and that one is exceedingly dear to Me). The divine reciprocates love. The absolute cherishes those who truly know it — a statement of extraordinary spiritual intimacy.

7:18.text: All of these are indeed noble; but the jñānī, I hold, is My very Self. For that one, with a unified mind (yuktātmā), abides in Me alone as the supreme goal (anuttamāṃ gatim).
7:18.transliteration:
udārāḥ sarva evaite jñānī tv ātmaiva me matam |
āsthitaḥ sa hi yuktātmā mām evānuttamāṃ gatim ||18||
7:18.devanagari:
उदाराः सर्व एवैते ज्ञानी त्वात्मैव मे मतम् ।
आस्थितः स हि युक्तात्मा मामेवानुत्तमां गतिम् ॥ १८ ॥
7:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:18.explanation: 'Udārāḥ sarva evaite' (all of these are indeed noble) — Krishna affirms each of the four devotees from verse 16 without condescension. But then comes the supreme statement: 'jñānī tv ātmaiva me matam' (the jñānī, in My view, is My very Self). This is perhaps the most astonishing claim in the Gita: the fully realized devotee is not separate from the divine but is declared to be the divine's own Self. 'Yuktātmā' (one whose self is unified, integrated) — the jñānī has dissolved the apparent boundary between individual and Absolute. 'Mām evānuttamāṃ gatim' (Me alone as the supreme, unsurpassed goal) — the jñānī has no other refuge, no other aim, no other reality. Complete identity, complete surrender, complete love.

7:19.text: At the end of many births, the one possessed of knowledge (jñānavān) takes refuge in Me, realizing 'Vāsudeva is all.' Such a great soul (mahātmā) is exceedingly rare.
7:19.transliteration:
bahūnāṃ janmanām ante jñānavān māṃ prapadyate |
vāsudevaḥ sarvam iti sa mahātmā su-durlabhaḥ ||19||
7:19.devanagari:
बहूनां जन्मनामन्ते ज्ञानवान्मां प्रपद्यते ।
वासुदेवः सर्वमिति स महात्मा सुदुर्लभः ॥ १९ ॥
7:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:19.explanation: 'Bahūnāṃ janmanām ante' (at the end of many births) — full realization is the culmination of an entire spiritual evolution, often spanning multiple lifetimes of progressive purification and understanding. 'Vāsudevaḥ sarvam' (Vāsudeva is everything) — these four words constitute the Gita's ultimate philosophical declaration. Not merely 'God exists' or 'God is great,' but the total, unreserved recognition that the divine ground is the entirety of existence — nothing stands outside it. This is not a belief to be held but a living realization to be experienced. 'Su-durlabhaḥ' (exceedingly rare) — echoing verse 7.3, this rarity makes the teaching more precious. Such a mahātmā (great soul) who has crossed the entire ocean of becoming deserves reverence.

7:20.text: Those whose wisdom has been carried away by various desires take refuge in other gods, following this or that rule (of worship), governed by their own nature.
7:20.transliteration:
kāmais tais tair hṛta-jñānāḥ prapadyante 'nya-devatāḥ |
taṃ taṃ niyamam āsthāya prakṛtyā niyatāḥ svayā ||20||
7:20.devanagari:
कामैस्तैस्तैर्हृतज्ञानाः प्रपद्यन्तेऽन्यदेवताः ।
तं तं नियममास्थाय प्रकृत्या नियताः स्वया ॥ २० ॥
7:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:20.explanation: 'Kāmais tais tair hṛta-jñānāḥ' (whose wisdom has been carried away by various desires) — specific desires (for health, wealth, progeny, power, victory) narrow the scope of the devotional gaze. Unable to see the Absolute behind all appearances, such seekers project their specific needs onto specific divine powers and worship accordingly. 'Tām tam niyamam āsthāya' (following this or that prescribed rule) — they observe the specific rituals and disciplines of particular deities. 'Prakṛtyā niyatāḥ svayā' (governed by their own nature) — their saṃskāras and guṇa-constitution determine which deity they approach. This is not condemnation — these worshippers are sincere (7.21-22 shows their faith is valid and their wishes are fulfilled) — but their path leads to a limited destination.

7:21.text: Whatever form a devotee wishes to worship with faith — I make that same faith of that devotee unwavering.
7:21.transliteration:
yo yo yāṃ yāṃ tanuṃ bhaktaḥ śraddhayārcitum icchati |
tasya tasyācalāṃ śraddhāṃ tām eva vidadhāmy aham ||21||
7:21.devanagari:
यो यो यां यां तनुं भक्तः श्रद्धयार्चितुमिच्छति ।
तस्य तस्याचलां श्रद्धां तामेव विदधाम्यहम् ॥ २१ ॥
7:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:21.explanation: 'Yo yo yāṃ yāṃ tanuṃ bhaktaḥ śraddhayārcitum icchati' (whatever form any devotee wishes to worship with faith) — the divine does not restrict itself to one approach. 'Tasyācalāṃ śraddhāṃ tām eva vidadhāmy aham' (I make that very devotee's faith in that form steady and unwavering) — Krishna is the hidden sustainer of all genuine faith. Every sincere act of devotion, whatever its form or direction, is ultimately sustained by the universal divine ground. This verse reveals the extraordinary inclusiveness of the Gita's theology: the Absolute works through every authentic religious impulse. The diversity of worship paths does not separate devotees from the divine — it is the divine itself that animates and sustains each path from within.

7:22.text: Endowed with that faith, that devotee worships that deity and obtains the desired objects from it — which are in fact granted by Me alone.
7:22.transliteration:
sa tayā śraddhayā yuktas tasyārādhanam īhate |
labhate ca tataḥ kāmān mayaiva vihitān hi tān ||22||
7:22.devanagari:
स तया श्रद्धया युक्तस्तस्याराधनमीहते ।
लभते च ततः कामान्मयैव विहितान्हि तान् ॥ २२ ॥
7:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:22.explanation: 'Mayaiva vihitān hi tān' (which are in fact ordained by Me alone) — this is the Gita's subtle and important clarification: the various gods are not independent dispensers of boons but channels of the one divine will. The devotee's faith is real, the worship is genuine, the results materialize — but the ultimate source of all fulfillment is the one Absolute. No deity acts independently of Krishna's universal will. This maintains both the validity of diverse worship and the supremacy of the ultimate divine. The bhakta is not wrong to worship other forms — the divine accepts all sincere worship — but understanding the ultimate source leads to a fuller and more liberating relationship.

7:23.text: But that fruit of those small-minded ones is finite (antavat). Those who worship the gods go to the gods; My devotees come to Me.
7:23.transliteration:
antavat tu phalaṃ teṣāṃ tad bhavaty alpa-medhasām |
devān deva-yajo yānti mad-bhaktā yānti mām api ||23||
7:23.devanagari:
अन्तवत्तु फलं तेषां तद्भवत्यल्पमेधसाम् ।
देवान्देवयजो यान्ति मद्भक्ता यान्ति मामपि ॥ २३ ॥
7:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:23.explanation: 'Antavat tu phalam' (but the fruit is finite, has an end) — this is the crucial distinction. The gods deliver their promised rewards — health, wealth, victory, progeny, pleasurable afterlife states — but all of these are temporary. They exist within the realm of time and causation, and what begins must end. 'Alpa-medhasām' (of the small-minded) — not a moral condemnation but a cognitive observation: their vision doesn't reach to the eternal. 'Devān deva-yajo yānti' (those who worship gods go to gods) — they arrive at their destination but it is a relative destination. 'Mad-bhaktā yānti mām api' (My devotees come to Me) — the destination is Krishna himself, the Absolute, from which there is no further journey because it is the ground of all being. This is the difference between arriving at a station and arriving home.

7:24.text: The unintelligent (abuddhayaḥ) think that I, the Unmanifest, have come into manifestation. They do not know My supreme, imperishable, unsurpassed nature.
7:24.transliteration:
avyaktaṃ vyaktim āpannaṃ manyante mām abuddhayaḥ |
paraṃ bhāvam ajānanto mamāvyayam anuttamam ||24||
7:24.devanagari:
अव्यक्तं व्यक्तिमापन्नं मन्यन्ते मामबुद्धयः ।
परं भावमजानन्तो ममाव्ययमनुत्तमम् ॥ २४ ॥
7:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:24.explanation: 'Avyaktaṃ vyaktim āpannaṃ manyante mām abuddhayaḥ' — those without discriminative understanding see Krishna's human form and conclude that he is simply an exceptionally gifted human being who has 'become' divine, or that the Absolute has been reduced to a finite physical form. This misses the entire point. 'Paraṃ bhāvam ajānanto mamāvyayam anuttamam' (not knowing My supreme, imperishable, unsurpassed nature) — Krishna's physical form is a window to his infinite nature, not a limitation of it. The divine did not become finite; the infinite chose to manifest a finite expression while remaining wholly itself. This is the Gita's understanding of avatāra: not reduction of the Absolute but its gracious, purposeful self-disclosure.

7:25.text: Veiled by My yoga-māyā, I am not manifest to all. This deluded world does not know Me — the unborn (aja), the imperishable (avyaya).
7:25.transliteration:
nāhaṃ prakāśaḥ sarvasya yoga-māyā-samāvṛtaḥ |
mūḍho 'yaṃ nābhijānāti loko mām ajam avyayam ||25||
7:25.devanagari:
नाहं प्रकाशः सर्वस्य योगमायासमावृतः ।
मूढोऽयं नाभिजानाति लोको मामजमव्ययम् ॥ २५ ॥
7:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:25.explanation: 'Yoga-māyā-samāvṛtaḥ' (veiled by My yoga-māyā) — yoga-māyā is Krishna's own power of concealment, distinct from the ordinarily binding māyā of verse 7.14. It is the veil that keeps the divine from being casually perceived by those not prepared to receive the revelation. 'Na ahaṃ prakāśaḥ sarvasya' (I am not visible to everyone) — the divine is omnipresent but not omnipercipient; it takes a prepared consciousness to receive the divine disclosure. 'Aja' (unborn) and 'avyaya' (imperishable) — these two epithets affirm that Krishna's true nature is beyond the cycle of birth and death, beyond time. The world's delusion (mūḍha) consists precisely in failing to recognize the eternal in what appears temporal.

7:26.text: O Arjuna, I know all beings — past, present, and future — but no one knows Me.
7:26.transliteration:
vedāhaṃ samatītāni vartamānāni cārjuna |
bhaviṣyāṇi ca bhūtāni māṃ tu veda na kaścana ||26||
7:26.devanagari:
वेदाहं समतीतानि वर्तमानानि चार्जुन ।
भविष्याणि च भूतानि मां तु वेद न कश्चन ॥ २६ ॥
7:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:26.explanation: 'Vedāhaṃ samatītāni vartamānāni... bhaviṣyāṇi ca bhūtāni' (I know all beings — past, present, future) — omniscience across all three dimensions of time. The divine holds all of history and all of possibility in a single, timeless awareness. But 'māṃ tu veda na kaścana' (but no one knows Me) — the divine knows everything; beings know the divine as little as fish know the ocean they live in. This asymmetry is the precise problem that the Gita exists to address: the teaching exists to give beings the knowledge of the divine that they lack, the knowledge that the divine already has of them. Grace is the bridge across this gap — which is why 7.14's instruction to 'take refuge in Me' is the practical solution.

7:27.text: O Bhārata, O Parantapa, all beings fall into complete delusion at birth through the delusion of the pairs of opposites (dvandva-moha) arising from desire and aversion.
7:27.transliteration:
icchā-dveṣa-samutthena dvandva-mohena bhārata |
sarva-bhūtāni sammohaṃ sarge yānti parantapa ||27||
7:27.devanagari:
इच्छाद्वेषसमुत्थेन द्वन्द्वमोहेन भारत ।
सर्वभूतानि सम्मोहं सर्गे यान्ति परन्तप ॥ २७ ॥
7:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:27.explanation: 'Icchā-dveṣa-samutthena' (arising from desire and aversion) — the root cause of dvandva-moha (the delusion produced by the pairs of opposites). Desire pulls toward the pleasant; aversion pushes away from the unpleasant. This dual movement of attraction and rejection is the operating system of the ego-conditioned mind. 'Dvandva-mohena' (by the delusion of pairs) — pleasure-pain, gain-loss, honor-dishonor, friend-enemy: all existence appears to the conditioned mind through this binary filter. 'Sarge yānti' (they fall into this at birth) — this is the primordial condition, the existential starting point of embodied consciousness. Not a moral failure but a structural given — which the Gita's teaching exists to transcend.

7:28.text: But those persons of virtuous deeds whose sin has come to an end — freed from the delusion of pairs of opposites, they worship Me with unwavering resolve (dṛḍha-vratāḥ).
7:28.transliteration:
yeṣāṃ tv anta-gataṃ pāpaṃ janānāṃ puṇya-karmaṇām |
te dvandva-moha-nirmuktā bhajante māṃ dṛḍha-vratāḥ ||28||
7:28.devanagari:
येषां त्वन्तगतं पापं जनानां पुण्यकर्मणाम् ।
ते द्वन्द्वमोहनिर्मुक्ता भजन्ते मां दृढव्रताः ॥ २८ ॥
7:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:28.explanation: The resolution to dvandva-moha (verse 27) lies in the progressive purification of the moral-karmic field. 'Anta-gataṃ pāpam' (sin that has reached its end, been exhausted) — accumulated negative saṃskāras and karmic impurities gradually dissolve through 'puṇya-karmaṇām' (virtuous action). As the inner field is purified, the grip of dvandva-moha loosens. 'Dvandva-moha-nirmuktāḥ' (freed from the delusion of pairs) — no longer yanked between desire and aversion, between like and dislike as absolute governors of action. 'Dṛḍha-vratāḥ' (with firm resolve, unwavering vow) — the purified person's devotion is stable, not fluctuating with circumstances. The sequence: virtuous action → purification → freedom from duality → firm devotion — is the Gita's practical roadmap.

7:29.text: Those who strive for liberation from old age and death, taking refuge in Me — they know that Brahman in full, the adhyātman, and all about karma.
7:29.transliteration:
jarā-maraṇa-mokṣāya mām āśritya yatanti ye |
te brahma tad viduḥ kṛtsnam adhyātmaṃ karma cākhilam ||29||
7:29.devanagari:
जरामरणमोक्षाय मामाश्रित्य यतन्ति ये ।
ते ब्रह्म तद्विदुः कृत्स्नमध्यात्मं कर्म चाखिलम् ॥ २९ ॥
7:29.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:29.explanation: 'Jarā-maraṇa-mokṣāya' (for liberation from old age and death) — this is the fundamental human aspiration framed with complete honesty: the desire to be free from the inexorable deterioration and dissolution of the body. 'Mām āśritya yatanti' (striving with Me as refuge) — the combination of personal effort and divine shelter. Such seekers come to know three comprehensive domains: 'Brahma' (the Absolute, the ultimate reality); 'adhyātmam' (the self-discipline, the science of the Self in relation to body, mind, and cosmos); and 'karma' — the full understanding of action, its nature, its binding power, and its transcendence. These three form the complete intellectual and experiential map of liberation. This verse also prepares the ground for Arjuna's opening questions in Chapter 8.

7:30.text: Those who know Me together with adhibhūta (the material domain), adhidaiva (the divine domain), and adhiyajña (the domain of sacrifice) — those of integrated consciousness (yukta-cetasaḥ) know Me even at the time of death (prayāṇa-kāle).
7:30.transliteration:
sādhibhūtādhidaivaṃ māṃ sādhiyajñaṃ ca ye viduḥ |
prayāṇa-kāle 'pi ca māṃ te vidur yukta-cetasaḥ ||30||
7:30.devanagari:
साधिभूताधिदैवं मां साधियज्ञं च ये विदुः ।
प्रयाणकालेऽपि च मां ते विदुर्युक्तचेतसः ॥ ३० ॥
7:30.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
7:30.explanation: This closing verse of Chapter 7 simultaneously concludes the chapter and opens the door to Chapter 8. Three cosmological domains are introduced without yet being defined — they will be Arjuna's first questions at 8.1: 'adhibhūta' (that which pertains to all perishable beings, the realm of material existence); 'adhidaiva' (that which pertains to the divine cosmic powers, the realm of subtle governing intelligences); and 'adhiyajña' (the inner lord of all sacrifice, the Puruṣa within the heart). 'Prayāṇa-kāle 'pi' (even at the moment of departure, i.e., at death) — this is the ultimate test and the ultimate fruit: one whose realization is complete carries that knowledge through the threshold of death itself. 'Yukta-cetasaḥ' (those whose consciousness is united, integrated) — the quality that makes this possible. Chapter 8 will explore what happens at death and why this knowledge is the highest preparation.

8:1.text: Arjuna said: O Puruṣottama (best among men), what is that Brahman? What is adhyātman? What is karma? What is said to be adhibhūta? And what is adhidaiva called?
8:1.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
kiṃ tad brahma kim adhyātmaṃ kiṃ karma puruṣottama |
adhibhūtaṃ ca kiṃ proktam adhidaivaṃ kim ucyate ||1||
8:1.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
किं तद्ब्रह्म किमध्यात्मं किं कर्म पुरुषोत्तम ।
अधिभूतं च किं प्रोक्तमधिदैवं किमुच्यते ॥ १ ॥
8:1.speaker: Arjuna
8:1.explanation: At the close of Chapter 7 (verses 29–30), Krishna introduced six technical terms — Brahman, adhyātman, karma, adhibhūta, adhidaiva, and adhiyajña — without defining them, and added that those who know him thus, even at the moment of death, truly know him. Arjuna now asks with both intellectual rigor and practical urgency. 'Puruṣottama' (the highest among persons) — this address places Krishna above all relative categories and frames the coming answers as coming from the supreme source. The five questions here and two in the next verse form a seven-part inquiry that Chapter 8 answers systematically. Arjuna's precision as a questioner matches Krishna's precision as a teacher.

8:2.text: O Madhusūdana, who is adhiyajña here, and how does it reside in this body? And how are You to be known at the time of departure (prayāṇa-kāle) by those of steadfast self (niyatātmabhiḥ)?
8:2.transliteration:
adhiyajñaḥ kathaṃ ko 'tra dehe 'smin madhusūdana |
prayāṇa-kāle ca kathaṃ jñeyo 'si niyatātmabhiḥ ||2||
8:2.devanagari:
अधियज्ञः कथं कोऽत्र देहेऽस्मिन्मधुसूदन ।
प्रयाणकाले च कथं ज्ञेयोऽसि नियतात्मभिः ॥ २ ॥
8:2.speaker: Arjuna
8:2.explanation: The seventh and most urgent question: 'prayāṇa-kāle ca kathaṃ jñeyo 'si' (how are You to be known at the moment of death?). This question is the living heart of Chapter 8 — everything else in the chapter either prepares for or elaborates upon this. 'Niyatātmabhiḥ' (those of disciplined, steadied self) — only one who has trained the mind throughout life can deploy that training at death's threshold. 'Madhusūdana' (slayer of the demon Madhu) — the epithet resonates: Krishna slays the demon of delusion; at death, only that power can part the veil of māyā. 'Ko 'tra dehe' (who here in this body) — the inner adhiyajña is the divine witness residing in the cave of the heart, the indwelling Puruṣa. All seven questions together constitute the most concentrated doctrinal question-set in the Gita.

8:3.text: The Blessed Lord said: The Supreme Imperishable (akṣara) is Brahman; one's own essential nature (svabhāva) is called adhyātman; and the creative offering (visarga) that brings forth the existence of beings is called karma.
8:3.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
akṣaraṃ brahma paramaṃ svabhāvo 'dhyātmam ucyate |
bhūta-bhāvodbhava-karo visargaḥ karma-saṃjñitaḥ ||3||
8:3.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
अक्षरं ब्रह्म परमं स्वभावोऽध्यात्ममुच्यते ।
भूतभावोद्भवकरो विसर्गः कर्मसंज्ञितः ॥ ३ ॥
8:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:3.explanation: Krishna's answers are compressed but precise. 'Akṣaraṃ brahma paramam' — Brahman is the Supreme Imperishable, the Indestructible. 'Akṣara' (that which does not perish, the non-decaying) is Brahman's defining character: unlike everything in the manifest world, which arises and dissolves, Brahman is the changeless ground of all change. 'Svabhāvaḥ adhyātmam ucyate' — adhyātman is svabhāva, one's own essential nature, the individual ātman considered in its own right as the self-referential principle of each being. 'Bhūta-bhāvodbhava-karo visargaḥ karma-saṃjñitaḥ' — karma is the creative offering (visarga) that produces the existence (bhāva) and arising (udbhava) of beings; it is the yajña-principle of generative action that sustains the cosmic order.

8:4.text: Adhibhūta is the perishable (kṣara) nature of beings; the cosmic Puruṣa (Hiraṇyagarbha) is adhidaivata; and O best among the embodied, I myself am the adhiyajña here in this body.
8:4.transliteration:
adhibhūtaṃ kṣaro bhāvaḥ puruṣaś cādhidaivatam |
adhiyajño 'ham evātra dehe deha-bhṛtāṃ vara ||4||
8:4.devanagari:
अधिभूतं क्षरो भावः पुरुषश्चाधिदैवतम् ।
अधियज्ञोऽहमेवात्र देहे देहभृतां वर ॥ ४ ॥
8:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:4.explanation: 'Kṣaro bhāvaḥ adhibhūtam' — adhibhūta is the perishable (kṣara) aspect of existence: everything that belongs to the realm of the five elements and is subject to change, decay, and dissolution. 'Puruṣaś cādhidaivatam' — adhidaiva is Puruṣa — the cosmic Hiraṇyagarbha (the Golden Womb, the universal cosmic intelligence) that presides over all cosmic forces and deities. 'Adhiyajño 'ham evātra dehe' — the most intimate revelation: the Lord of all sacrifice (adhiyajña) is Krishna himself, residing within this very body. Every sincere act of worship, every offering, every sacrifice, is received and witnessed by the divine indwelling presence in the heart of the worshipper. The sacred is not external — it is interior.

8:5.text: And whoever, at the time of death, departs remembering Me alone, leaving the body — that person attains My state (mad-bhāvaṃ). Of this there is no doubt.
8:5.transliteration:
anta-kāle ca mām eva smaran muktvā kalevaram |
yaḥ prayāti sa mad-bhāvaṃ yāti nāsty atra saṃśayaḥ ||5||
8:5.devanagari:
अन्तकाले च मामेव स्मरन्मुक्त्वा कलेवरम् ।
यः प्रयाति स मद्भावं याति नास्त्यत्र संशयः ॥ ५ ॥
8:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:5.explanation: 'Anta-kāle ca mām eva smaran' (at the time of death, remembering Me alone) — the final moment of consciousness is decisive because it is the mind's last impression, the deepest groove cut into awareness as it releases its hold on the body. 'Mad-bhāvaṃ yāti' (attains My state, becomes My being) — the consciousness shaped by that final remembrance of Krishna reaches Krishna's own reality. 'Nāsty atra saṃśayaḥ' (there is no doubt here at all) — Krishna's language is absolute. The certainty of this promise is itself part of the teaching: the student who doubts cannot commit fully, and full commitment is required at the moment of death. This verse is the Gita's direct answer to Arjuna's central question from 8.2.

8:6.text: O Kaunteya, whatever state of being one remembers when one finally leaves the body — that state alone is reached, always having been absorbed in that very state of being.
8:6.transliteration:
yaṃ yaṃ vāpi smaran bhāvaṃ tyajaty ante kalevaram |
taṃ tam evaiti kaunteya sadā tad-bhāva-bhāvitaḥ ||6||
8:6.devanagari:
यं यं वापि स्मरन्भावं त्यजत्यन्ते कलेवरम् ।
तं तमेवैति कौन्तेय सदा तद्भावभावितः ॥ ६ ॥
8:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:6.explanation: 'Yaṃ yaṃ vāpi smaran bhāvaṃ tyajaty ante kalevaram / taṃ tam evaiti' — this is the Gita's universal law of becoming: the final state of consciousness at death — shaped by lifelong orientation — determines the next destination of the soul. 'Sadā tad-bhāva-bhāvitaḥ' (always having been shaped by that very state) — 'sadā' (always) is the key: the deathbed thought is not a random occurrence but the inevitable crystallization of a lifetime's habit of mind. One does not suddenly think of Krishna at the moment of death unless Krishna has been the continuous orientation of one's life. This verse is simultaneously a cosmic law and a practical imperative: what you will be at death is what you are practicing being right now.

8:7.text: Therefore, at all times remember Me and fight. With mind and intellect surrendered to Me (mayy arpita-mano-buddhiḥ), you shall come to Me alone — without doubt.
8:7.transliteration:
tasmāt sarveṣu kāleṣu mām anusmara yudhya ca |
mayy arpita-mano-buddhir mām evaiṣyasy asaṃśayam ||7||
8:7.devanagari:
तस्मात्सर्वेषु कालेषु मामनुस्मर युध्य च ।
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्मामेवैष्यस्यसंशयम् ॥ ७ ॥
8:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:7.explanation: 'Tasmāt sarveṣu kāleṣu mām anusmara yudhya ca' — this is one of the Gita's most commanding practical instructions, a perfect synthesis of its entire teaching. 'Anusmara' (remember, keep in mind constantly) — not momentary recollection but continuous, practiced awareness of the divine ground of one's being. 'Yudhya ca' (and also fight) — simultaneously engage your worldly duty with full commitment. Neither the world nor God is to be abandoned; both are to be held together. 'Mayy arpita-mano-buddhiḥ' (with mind and intellect offered to Me) — total inner offering while total outer engagement. 'Asaṃśayam' (without doubt) — the certainty of the promise is part of the teaching. This verse is the Gita in one breath.

8:8.text: O Pārtha, one who meditates continuously with a mind made steadfast through the yoga of practice (abhyāsa-yoga), not wandering to anything else — that person reaches the Supreme Divine Puruṣa.
8:8.transliteration:
abhyāsa-yoga-yuktena cetasā nānya-gāminā |
paramaṃ puruṣaṃ divyaṃ yāti pārthānucintayan ||8||
8:8.devanagari:
अभ्यासयोगयुक्तेन चेतसा नान्यगामिना ।
परमं पुरुषं दिव्यं याति पार्थानुचिन्तयन् ॥ ८ ॥
8:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:8.explanation: 'Abhyāsa-yoga-yuktena cetasā' (with a mind disciplined through the yoga of repetitive practice) — 'abhyāsa' (practice, the repeated return of the mind to its chosen object) is the technical term for the systematic training of attention. This is not willpower but conditioning: the mind is trained, through thousands of repetitions, to return naturally to the divine. 'Nānya-gāminā' (not wandering to anything else) — one-pointed, without the habitual scattering that is the untrained mind's default state. 'Divyaṃ paramaṃ puruṣam' (the Supreme Divine Puruṣa) — the ultimate Person who transcends all material categories. This verse establishes that the death-moment meditation of 8.5 is only possible as the fruit of a lifetime of abhyāsa.

8:9.text: Whoever meditates on the Seer (kavi), the Ancient (purāṇa), the Ruler (anuśāsitāram), subtler than the atom (aṇor aṇīyāṃsam), the Sustainer of all (sarvasya dhātāram), of inconceivable form (acintya-rūpam), luminous as the sun (āditya-varṇam), beyond all darkness —
8:9.transliteration:
kaviṃ purāṇam anuśāsitāram
aṇor aṇīyāṃsam anusmared yaḥ |
sarvasya dhātāram acintya-rūpam
āditya-varṇaṃ tamasaḥ parastāt ||9||
8:9.devanagari:
कविं पुराणमनुशासितारम्
अणोरणीयांसमनुस्मरेद्यः ।
सर्वस्य धातारमचिन्त्यरूपम्
आदित्यवर्णं तमसः परस्तात् ॥ ९ ॥
8:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:9.explanation: This verse and the next form one complete sentence. Here Krishna enumerates the attributes of the supreme object of meditation with rare poetic and philosophical density. 'Kavi' (seer, omniscient) — who knows all, past and future; 'purāṇa' (the Ancient, the primordial) — before whom nothing preceded; 'anuśāsitāra' (the regulator, the controller of all) — the cosmic lawgiver; 'aṇor aṇīyāṃsam' (subtler than the subtlest) — smaller than an atom, pervading everything precisely because it is not limited by size; 'sarvasya dhātāram' (the bearer, the sustainer of all) — the ground that holds every being in existence; 'acintya-rūpam' (of inconceivable form) — beyond the capacity of thought; 'āditya-varṇam' (of the color/luminosity of the sun) — self-luminous; 'tamasaḥ parastāt' (beyond darkness) — beyond ignorance itself.

8:10.text: At the time of departure, with steady mind, endowed with devotion and the power of yoga, fixing the prāṇa (vital force) rightly between the eyebrows — that person reaches that Supreme Divine Puruṣa.
8:10.transliteration:
prayāṇa-kāle manasācalena
bhaktyā yukto yoga-balena caiva |
bhruvor madhye prāṇam āveśya samyak
sa taṃ paraṃ puruṣam upaiti divyam ||10||
8:10.devanagari:
प्रयाणकाले मनसाचलेन
भक्त्या युक्तो योगबलेन चैव ।
भ्रुवोर्मध्ये प्राणमावेश्य सम्यक्
स तं परं पुरुषमुपैति दिव्यम् ॥ १० ॥
8:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:10.explanation: This verse completes the meditation portrait begun in 8.9. Three instruments are named: 'manasācalena' (with unwavering mind) — the stillness that is the fruit of years of practice; 'bhaktyā yuktaḥ' (endowed with devotion) — the heart's orientation toward the divine; 'yoga-balena' (with the power of yoga) — the concentrated energy of long spiritual discipline. The technical instruction 'bhruvor madhye prāṇam āveśya samyak' (fixing the prāṇa correctly between the eyebrows) describes the yogic technique of directing the life-force upward through the suṣumnā at death — the practice of conscious, directed departure known in the tradition as utkrānti. The three together — devotion, steadiness, and yogic skill — constitute the complete preparation for the moment of death.

8:11.text: That which the knowers of the Vedas call the Imperishable (akṣaram), which the passion-free ascetics enter, desiring which they practice celibacy (brahmacarya) — that goal I will declare to you briefly.
8:11.transliteration:
yad akṣaraṃ veda-vido vadanti
viśanti yad yatayo vīta-rāgāḥ |
yad icchanto brahmacaryaṃ caranti
tat te padaṃ saṃgraheṇa pravakṣye ||11||
8:11.devanagari:
यदक्षरं वेदविदो वदन्ति
विशन्ति यद्यतयो वीतरागाः ।
यदिच्छन्तो ब्रह्मचर्यं चरन्ति
तत्ते पदं सङ्ग्रहेण प्रवक्ष्ये ॥ ११ ॥
8:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:11.explanation: 'Akṣaram' (the Imperishable) — the same word that defined Brahman in 8.3; here it identifies the ultimate destination of all sincere spiritual practice. Three witnesses to its value are invoked: the Veda-knowers (veda-vidaḥ) who name it; the passion-free ascetics (yatayo vīta-rāgāḥ) who dissolve into it; and those who observe brahmacarya (the discipline of energy-conservation and divine-orientation) specifically in pursuit of it. This convergence of testimony from different paths — scriptural, ascetic, and disciplinary — establishes the akṣara-pada as the universally recognized supreme goal. 'Saṃgraheṇa pravakṣye' (I will explain briefly) — the next two verses provide the concise technical instruction.

8:12.text: Having restrained all the gates of the body (sense-organs), having confined the mind in the heart, having placed the prāṇa in the head, established in yogic concentration (yoga-dhāraṇā) —
8:12.transliteration:
sarva-dvārāṇi saṃyamya mano hṛdi nirudhya ca |
mūrdhny ādhāyātmanaḥ prāṇam āsthito yoga-dhāraṇām ||12||
8:12.devanagari:
सर्वद्वाराणि संयम्य मनो हृदि निरुध्य च ।
मूर्ध्न्याधायात्मनः प्राणमास्थितो योगधारणाम् ॥ १२ ॥
8:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:12.explanation: This verse and the next form one complete practice-instruction. The three-stage process described here is the yogic technique of conscious departure: 'sarva-dvārāṇi saṃyamya' (restraining all the gates) — withdrawing the senses completely inward, closing all the apertures of the body through which consciousness leaks into the external world (this is the deepest pratyāhāra, sense-withdrawal); 'mano hṛdi nirudhya' (confining the mind in the heart) — gathering scattered mental energy into the heart center, the seat of the ātman; 'mūrdhny ādhāyātmanaḥ prāṇam' (placing the prāṇa at the crown of the head) — directing the vital force upward through the suṣumnā to the brahmarandhra, the gateway of liberation at the crown. 'Yoga-dhāraṇā' (the fixing of yoga) — sustained, concentrated fixation that makes this possible.

8:13.text: Uttering Om — the single-syllable Brahman — and remembering Me, whoever departs leaving the body — that person attains the supreme destination (paramāṃ gatim).
8:13.transliteration:
om ity ekākṣaraṃ brahma vyāharan mām anusmaran |
yaḥ prayāti tyajan dehaṃ sa yāti paramāṃ gatim ||13||
8:13.devanagari:
ओमित्येकाक्षरं ब्रह्म व्याहरन्मामनुस्मरन् ।
यः प्रयाति त्यजन्देहं स याति परमां गतिम् ॥ १३ ॥
8:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:13.explanation: 'Om ity ekākṣaraṃ brahma' — Om, the single-syllable that is Brahman itself. In the Upaniṣadic tradition, Om is the primordial sound-vibration, the acoustic form of the Absolute, the seed-syllable from which all manifest creation arises and into which it dissolves. 'Vyāharan' (uttering, sounding) — whether audibly or as an inner resonance of consciousness. 'Mām anusmaran' (remembering Me) — the simultaneity of Om-utterance and Krishna-remembrance creates a complete orientation: the sound points to the Absolute; the memory of Krishna gives it personal, relational warmth. Together they constitute the full practice. 'Paramāṃ gatim' (the supreme destination) — the final journey that does not circle back into rebirth.

8:14.text: O Pārtha, for one who continuously and constantly remembers Me with an undivided mind (ananya-cetāḥ) — for that ever-united yogī, I am easily attained (sulabhaḥ).
8:14.transliteration:
ananya-cetāḥ satataṃ yo māṃ smarati nityaśaḥ |
tasyāhaṃ sulabhaḥ pārtha nitya-yuktasya yoginaḥ ||14||
8:14.devanagari:
अनन्यचेताः सततं यो मां स्मरति नित्यशः ।
तस्याहं सुलभः पार्थ नित्ययुक्तस्य योगिनः ॥ १४ ॥
8:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:14.explanation: 'Ananya-cetāḥ' (with an undivided, one-pointed mind) — 'ananya' means literally 'not-other'; the mind that holds only Krishna, without the scattering of attention across other objects and desires. 'Satatam... nityaśaḥ' (continuously and constantly) — the two adverbs together emphasize unbroken continuity: not periodic remembrance but the steady, unceasing background-awareness of the divine. 'Nitya-yuktasya yoginaḥ' (of the ever-united yogī) — one for whom the state of yoga (union) is not an occasional attainment but a permanent condition. 'Sulabhaḥ' (easily attained) — this is the Gita's most gracious declaration: the divine is not distant or difficult; the difficulty lies entirely in the mind's inattention. For the attentive mind, the divine is immediately present.

8:15.text: Having attained Me, those great souls (mahātmānaḥ) who have reached the highest perfection do not take birth again in this transient abode of suffering (duḥkhālayam aśāśvatam).
8:15.transliteration:
mām upetya punar janma duḥkhālayam aśāśvatam |
nāpnuvanti mahātmānaḥ saṃsiddhiṃ paramāṃ gatāḥ ||15||
8:15.devanagari:
मामुपेत्य पुनर्जन्म दुःखालयमशाश्वतम् ।
नाप्नुवन्ति महात्मानः संसिद्धिं परमां गताः ॥ १५ ॥
8:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:15.explanation: 'Duḥkhālayam aśāśvatam' — these four syllables constitute the Gita's most direct and unsentimental assessment of saṃsāra (the cycle of birth and death): it is 'the house of suffering' (duḥkhālaya) and 'impermanent' (aśāśvata). Not a place of evil but a structural limitation — the realm of conditioned existence is inherently characterized by impermanence and the suffering that impermanence generates. 'Mahātmānaḥ... saṃsiddhiṃ paramāṃ gatāḥ' (great souls who have reached the highest perfection) — those who have fulfilled the journey. 'Mām upetya punar janma... nāpnuvanti' (having reached Me, they do not take birth again) — liberation as the final termination of the cycle of compelled return. This is mokṣa — not in the abstract but as the concrete cessation of an otherwise endless process.

8:16.text: O Arjuna, all the worlds — up to and including the realm of Brahmā (Brahmaloka) — are subject to return (punar āvartinaḥ). But O Kaunteya, having reached Me, rebirth does not occur.
8:16.transliteration:
ā-brahma-bhuvanāl lokāḥ punar āvartino 'rjuna |
mām upetya tu kaunteya punar janma na vidyate ||16||
8:16.devanagari:
आब्रह्मभुवनाल्लोकाः पुनरावर्तिनोऽर्जुन ।
मामुपेत्य तु कौन्तेय पुनर्जन्म न विद्यते ॥ १६ ॥
8:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:16.explanation: 'Ā-brahma-bhuvanāl lokāḥ punar āvartinaḥ' — this is the Gita's most sweeping cosmological statement on the limits of non-liberation. All realms of existence, from the earth to the highest heaven (Brahmaloka, the realm of the Creator), are subject to the law of return: beings who arrive there through accumulated merit eventually exhaust that merit and return to embodied existence. Even the most exalted celestial attainment remains within saṃsāra. 'Mām upetya tu... punar janma na vidyate' (but having reached Me, rebirth does not occur) — only the supreme destination, Krishna himself (Brahman), is beyond the cycle of return. This is the definitional difference between svarga (heaven) and mokṣa (liberation).

8:17.text: Those who know that the day of Brahmā extends to a thousand yugas, and that the night of Brahmā also extends to a thousand yugas — they know the reality of day and night.
8:17.transliteration:
sahasra-yuga-paryantam ahar yad brahmaṇo viduḥ |
rātriṃ yuga-sahasrāntāṃ te 'ho-rātra-vido janāḥ ||17||
8:17.devanagari:
सहस्रयुगपर्यन्तमहर्यद्ब्रह्मणो विदुः ।
रात्रिं युगसहस्रान्तां तेऽहोरात्रविदो जनाः ॥ १७ ॥
8:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:17.explanation: This verse opens the Gita's remarkable cosmological teaching on cosmic time-cycles. 'Sahasra-yuga-paryantam ahar brahmaṇaḥ' — one day of Brahmā (the creator) spans a thousand mahāyugas (each mahāyuga = approximately 4.32 million human years), totaling approximately 4.32 billion years. The night of Brahmā is equal in length. The total lifespan of Brahmā is 311 trillion years. This scale dwarfs all human conceptions of history and time. The teaching serves 8.16: even Brahmaloka, governed by these vast but finite time-cycles, is not the final home. 'Te aho-rātra-vidaḥ janāḥ' (they who know the day and night) — only those who understand cosmic time can properly assess the value of liberation versus even the highest temporal attainments.

8:18.text: At the coming of Brahmā's day, all manifest beings arise from the Unmanifest (avyakta); at the coming of night, they dissolve into that same Unmanifest.
8:18.transliteration:
avyaktād vyaktayaḥ sarvāḥ prabhavanty ahar-āgame |
rātry-āgame pralīyante tatraivāvyakta-saṃjñake ||18||
8:18.devanagari:
अव्यक्ताद्व्यक्तयः सर्वाः प्रभवन्त्यहरागमे ।
रात्र्यागमे प्रलीयन्ते तत्रैवाव्यक्तसंज्ञके ॥ १८ ॥
8:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:18.explanation: 'Avyaktād vyaktayaḥ sarvāḥ prabhavanty ahar-āgame' — at the dawn of each cosmic day (kalpa), the entire universe of manifest beings unfolds from the avyakta (the unmanifested, undifferentiated ground of being). This is the cosmic sṛṣṭi (creation). 'Rātry-āgame pralīyante tatraivāvyakta-saṃjñake' — at the onset of the cosmic night (pralaya), every manifest form dissolves back into the same unmanifest ground. This is the rhythm of the universe: a vast breathing in and out, a cosmic pulsation of manifestation and dissolution occurring on the scale of billions of years. The teaching's purpose is practical: no being in the manifested cosmos, however exalted, exists outside this cycle — except the supreme Brahman itself.

8:19.text: O Pārtha, this same multitude of beings (bhūta-grāmaḥ), arising again and again, dissolves helplessly (avaśaḥ) at the coming of night and rises again at the coming of day.
8:19.transliteration:
bhūta-grāmaḥ sa evāyaṃ bhūtvā bhūtvā pralīyate |
rātry-āgame 'vaśaḥ pārtha prabhavaty ahar-āgame ||19||
8:19.devanagari:
भूतग्रामः स एवायं भूत्वा भूत्वा प्रलीयते ।
रात्र्यागमेऽवशः पार्थ प्रभवत्यहरागमे ॥ १९ ॥
8:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:19.explanation: 'Bhūtvā bhūtvā pralīyate' (arising again and again, it dissolves) — three words that capture the tragedy of conditioned existence: the cycle of compelled emergence and compelled dissolution, repeated endlessly without the freedom of genuine choice. 'Sa evāyaṃ' (this very same) — it is always the same fundamental multitude of beings, reappearing in different forms but driven by the same karmic propulsions. 'Avaśaḥ' (helpless, without agency, compelled) — this is the pivotal word. The beings caught in the cosmic cycle of creation and dissolution do not choose to emerge or dissolve; they are impelled by their unexhausted karma. Liberation (mokṣa) is precisely the transition from 'avaśa' (compelled) to 'svatantra' (free) — and that freedom is reached only through Krishna.

8:20.text: But beyond that Unmanifest, there is yet another eternal Unmanifest — which does not perish even when all beings perish.
8:20.transliteration:
paras tasmāt tu bhāvo 'nyo 'vyakto 'vyaktāt sanātanaḥ |
yaḥ sa sarveṣu bhūteṣu naśyatsu na vinaśyati ||20||
8:20.devanagari:
परस्तस्मात्तु भावोऽन्योऽव्यक्तोऽव्यक्तात्सनातनः ।
यः स सर्वेषु भूतेषु नश्यत्सु न विनश्यति ॥ २० ॥
8:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:20.explanation: 'Paras tasmāt tu bhāvo 'nyo 'vyakto 'vyaktāt sanātanaḥ' — a crucial ontological distinction. The 'avyakta' of verses 8.18–19 (the unmanifest ground into which creation dissolves at pralaya) is itself part of the cosmic cycle — it is prakṛti, the primordial material nature that alternates between manifestation and dissolution. Beyond even this avyakta (beyond prakṛti) lies another Unmanifest — Brahman itself, the Absolute that neither manifests nor dissolves but simply is, eternally. 'Sarveṣu bhūteṣu naśyatsu na vinaśyati' (does not perish even when all beings perish) — even during mahāpralaya, the total dissolution, this principle remains. It is the 2.20 ātman writ cosmically: 'it is not born, nor does it die at any time.'

8:21.text: That which is called the Unmanifest, the Imperishable (akṣara) — that they call the supreme destination (paramāṃ gatim). That which, having attained, none return — that is My supreme abode (tad dhāma paramaṃ mama).
8:21.transliteration:
avyakto 'kṣara ity uktas tam āhuḥ paramāṃ gatim |
yaṃ prāpya na nivartante tad dhāma paramaṃ mama ||21||
8:21.devanagari:
अव्यक्तोऽक्षर इत्युक्तस्तमाहुः परमां गतिम् ।
यं प्राप्य न निवर्तन्ते तद्धाम परमं मम ॥ २१ ॥
8:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:21.explanation: 'Avyakto 'kṣara ity uktas' — the supreme principle introduced in 8.20 is given its full names: avyakta (the Unmanifest — beyond all perceptual and conceptual grasp), and akṣara (the Imperishable — the title with which Brahman was defined in 8.3). 'Paramāṃ gatim' (the supreme destination) — all spiritual paths, properly followed, converge here. 'Yaṃ prāpya na nivartante' (reaching which, none return) — the absolute, irrevocable finality of this attainment: there is no further journey, no further compulsion, no further becoming. 'Tad dhāma paramaṃ mama' (that is My supreme abode) — Krishna's most intimate self-disclosure: the Absolute is his home, and the devotee is invited there. This is perhaps the Gita's most beautiful verse.

8:22.text: O Pārtha, that Supreme Puruṣa is attained through undivided devotion (ananyā bhakti) — in whom all beings abide, and by whom all this is pervaded.
8:22.transliteration:
puruṣaḥ sa paraḥ pārtha bhaktyā labhyas tv ananyayā |
yasyāntaḥ-sthāni bhūtāni yena sarvam idaṃ tatam ||22||
8:22.devanagari:
पुरुषः स परः पार्थ भक्त्या लभ्यस्त्वनन्यया ।
यस्यान्तःस्थानि भूतानि येन सर्वमिदं ततम् ॥ २२ ॥
8:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:22.explanation: 'Bhaktyā labhyas tv ananyayā' — after the vast cosmological teaching of the previous verses, the method of attainment is strikingly intimate and simple: ananyā bhakti — undivided, single-pointed devotion. Not complex ritual, not ascetic mastery, not cosmological knowledge alone, but love — love that holds nothing else as ultimate. 'Ananyā' (without-other, not-divided) — the same quality as 'ananya-cetāḥ' in 8.14: total orientation of the being toward the divine. 'Yasyāntaḥ-sthāni bhūtāni' (in whom all beings dwell) and 'yena sarvam idaṃ tatam' (by whom all this is pervaded) — the supreme object of devotion is simultaneously the innermost ground of all beings and the outermost pervader of all existence. The path to this all-containing, all-pervading reality is: love.

8:23.text: O best of the Bharatas, I will describe the times (paths) in which yogīs departing attain non-return (anāvṛttim — liberation) and return (āvṛttim — rebirth).
8:23.transliteration:
yatra kāle tv anāvṛttim āvṛttiṃ caiva yoginaḥ |
prayātā yānti taṃ kālaṃ vakṣyāmi bharatarṣabha ||23||
8:23.devanagari:
यत्र काले त्वनावृत्तिमावृत्तिं चैव योगिनः ।
प्रयाता यान्ति तं कालं वक्ष्यामि भरतर्षभ ॥ २३ ॥
8:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:23.explanation: This verse introduces the classic Vedic teaching of the two paths of departure, elaborated in the Chāndogya Upaniṣad as the 'devayāna' (path of the gods) and the 'pitṛyāna' (path of the ancestors), and in later tradition as the uttarāyaṇa (northern solstice path) and dakṣiṇāyana (southern solstice path). 'Anāvṛttim' (non-return, liberation) — the path that does not lead back to rebirth; 'āvṛttim' (return, rebirth) — the path that leads back into the cycle. While the immediate level of teaching concerns specific cosmic times and conditions of departure, the deeper teaching is about the quality of consciousness at the moment of death — which itself is the product of the quality of life lived.

8:24.text: Fire, light, daytime, the bright fortnight (śukla-pakṣa), and the six months of the northern solstice (uttarāyaṇa) — departing in these, the knowers of Brahman (brahma-vidaḥ) go to Brahman.
8:24.transliteration:
agnir jyotir ahaḥ śuklaḥ ṣaṇ-māsā uttarāyaṇam |
tatra prayātā gacchanti brahma brahma-vido janāḥ ||24||
8:24.devanagari:
अग्निर्ज्योतिरहः शुक्लः षण्मासा उत्तरायणम् ।
तत्र प्रयाता गच्छन्ति ब्रह्म ब्रह्मविदो जनाः ॥ २४ ॥
8:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:24.explanation: 'Agnir jyotir ahaḥ śuklaḥ ṣaṇ-māsā uttarāyaṇam' — this is the 'bright path' (śukla-gati or devayāna) described in symbolic terms drawn from the Vedic and Upaniṣadic tradition. The six luminous symbols — fire, light, day, the bright lunar fortnight, and the northern solstice period — all denote states of heightened clarity, awareness, and illumination. 'Brahma brahma-vido janāḥ' (knowers of Brahman go to Brahman) — those whose inner life has attained brahma-vidyā, whose consciousness is genuinely illuminated, traverse this path at death and reach the supreme. The cosmological symbolism is real at its own level but points to the deeper reality: it is the quality of one's realization, not the calendar date of death, that determines the path.

8:25.text: Smoke, night, the dark fortnight (kṛṣṇa-pakṣa), and the six months of the southern solstice (dakṣiṇāyana) — in these, the yogī reaches the lunar light (cāndramasaṃ jyotir) and returns.
8:25.transliteration:
dhūmo rātris tathā kṛṣṇaḥ ṣaṇ-māsā dakṣiṇāyanam |
tatra cāndramasaṃ jyotir yogī prāpya nivartate ||25||
8:25.devanagari:
धूमो रात्रिस्तथा कृष्णः षण्मासा दक्षिणायनम् ।
तत्र चान्द्रमसं ज्योतिर्योगी प्राप्य निवर्तते ॥ २५ ॥
8:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:25.explanation: 'Dhūmo rātris tathā kṛṣṇaḥ ṣaṇ-māsā dakṣiṇāyanam' — the four dark symbols (smoke, night, the dark lunar fortnight, and the southern solstice period) constitute the 'dark path' (kṛṣṇa-gati or pitṛyāna), the path of return. 'Cāndramasaṃ jyotiḥ' (the lunar light) — the yogī who travels this path reaches the realm of the moon (Chandraloka), where the fruits of virtuous deeds and partial spiritual practice are enjoyed. 'Prāpya nivartate' (having reached there, returns) — but this enjoyment is finite; once the accumulated merit is exhausted, the soul returns to embodied existence. This is the 7.23 teaching applied cosmologically: finite paths lead to finite destinations. Even a partial yogī travels this path — complete realization alone leads to non-return.

8:26.text: These two paths — the bright (śukla) and the dark (kṛṣṇa) — are considered eternal (śāśvate) for the world. By one, non-return (liberation) is attained; by the other, one returns again.
8:26.transliteration:
śukla-kṛṣṇe gatī hy ete jagataḥ śāśvate mate |
ekayā yāty anāvṛttim anyayāvartate punaḥ ||26||
8:26.devanagari:
शुक्लकृष्णे गती ह्येते जगतः शाश्वते मते ।
एकया यात्यनावृत्तिमन्ययावर्तते पुनः ॥ २६ ॥
8:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
