8:26.explanation: 'Śukla-kṛṣṇe gatī... jagataḥ śāśvate mate' (these two paths, bright and dark, are considered the eternal paths of the world) — the teaching draws on the most ancient layer of Vedic cosmological tradition (cf. Chāndogya Upaniṣad 5.10, Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 6.2) and confirms it as perennial, not historically contingent. 'Ekayā yāty anāvṛttim anyayāvartate punaḥ' — the bifurcation is absolute: one path leads out of saṃsāra; the other leads back in. The practical import for the living: the path chosen at death is determined by the orientation chosen in life. Begin now. This verse, together with 8.7 ('mām anusmara yudhya ca'), forms the practical heart of Chapter 8.

8:27.text: O Pārtha, knowing these two paths, no yogī is deluded. Therefore, O Arjuna, be yoga-yukta (united with yoga) at all times.
8:27.transliteration:
naite sṛtī pārtha jānan yogī muhyati kaścana |
tasmāt sarveṣu kāleṣu yoga-yukto bhavārjuna ||27||
8:27.devanagari:
नैते सृती पार्थ जानन्योगी मुह्यति कश्चन ।
तस्मात्सर्वेषु कालेषु योगयुक्तो भवार्जुन ॥ २७ ॥
8:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:27.explanation: 'Naite sṛtī pārtha jānan yogī muhyati kaścana' — knowledge of the two paths functions as the antidote to delusion. The yogī who understands this cosmological framework is freed from the enchantment of the perishable world precisely because the stakes are fully visible: one orientation leads home; the other leads back into the cycle of compelled return. 'Tasmāt sarveṣu kāleṣu yoga-yukto bhavārjuna' (therefore, at all times, be united with yoga, O Arjuna) — this is the chapter's practical imperative, directly echoing 8.7's 'mām anusmara yudhya ca.' Yoga here means the continuous condition of inner union with the divine — not a practice done for an hour but a state of being maintained throughout life. This is the preparation for death that is simultaneously the fullness of life.

8:28.text: Whatever meritorious fruits have been declared for the study of the Vedas, for sacrifices, for austerities, and for charitable gifts — the yogī who knows all this transcends all of these and attains the supreme, primordial abode (paraṃ sthānam ādyam).
8:28.transliteration:
vedeṣu yajñeṣu tapaḥsu caiva
dāneṣu yat puṇya-phalaṃ pradiṣṭam |
atyeti tat sarvam idaṃ viditvā
yogī paraṃ sthānam upaiti cādyam ||28||
8:28.devanagari:
वेदेषु यज्ञेषु तपःसु चैव
दानेषु यत्पुण्यफलं प्रदिष्टम् ।
अत्येति तत्सर्वमिदं विदित्वा
योगी परं स्थानमुपैति चाद्यम् ॥ २८ ॥
8:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
8:28.explanation: 'Vedeṣu yajñeṣu tapaḥsu caiva dāneṣu yat puṇya-phalaṃ pradiṣṭam' — the four pillars of Vedic dharmic life (scripture-study, sacrifice, austerity, and charity) produce immense merit and their rewards in this world and the next are great. Yet 'atyeti tat sarvam' (transcends all of that) — the yogī who has received and integrated the teaching of this chapter goes beyond even the highest fruits of conventional religious life. 'Idaṃ viditvā' (having known this) — the transformative power of genuine understanding. 'Paraṃ sthānam ādyam' (the supreme and primordial abode) — the ultimate destination, prior to all creation and surviving all dissolution, the 'tad dhāma paramaṃ mama' of 8.21. Chapter 8 ends where it must: with the promise of the home from which none return, attained not through accumulated merit but through wisdom and love.

9:1.text: The Blessed Lord said: To you who are free from envy, I shall now fully declare this most secret knowledge combined with direct realization, knowing which you shall be liberated from all that is inauspicious.
9:1.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
idaṃ tu te guhyatamaṃ pravakṣyāmy anasūyave |
jñānaṃ vijñānasahitaṃ yaj jñātvā mokṣyase 'śubhāt ||1||
9:1.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
इदं तु ते गुह्यतमं प्रवक्ष्याम्यनसूयवे ।
ज्ञानं विज्ञानसहितं यज्ज्ञात्वा मोक्ष्यसेऽशुभात् ॥ १ ॥
9:1.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:1.explanation: 'Guhyatamam' — the most secret, surpassing even what was called 'guhyam' in Chapter 7. The qualifier 'anasūyave' (to one without envy or fault-finding) is crucial: this supreme knowledge is not withheld due to secrecy but because it can only be received by a mind free of ego-distortion, jealousy, and skepticism. 'Jñānaṃ vijñānasahitam' — theoretical knowledge (jñāna) united with direct, experiential realization (vijñāna): both scripture and inner recognition. 'Aśubhāt' — from all inauspiciousness, meaning the entire cycle of birth, death, and bondage. With this opening, Krishna simultaneously states the qualification of the student and the promise of the teaching: come without ego, and receive liberation.

9:2.text: This is the sovereign knowledge, the sovereign secret, the supreme purifier; directly perceptible, in accordance with dharma, very easy to practice, and imperishable.
9:2.transliteration:
rājavidyā rājaguhyaṃ pavitram idam uttamam |
pratyakṣāvagamaṃ dharmyaṃ susukhaṃ kartum avyayam ||2||
9:2.devanagari:
राजविद्या राजगुह्यं पवित्रमिदमुत्तमम् ।
प्रत्यक्षावगमं धर्म्यं सुसुखं कर्तुमव्ययम् ॥ २ ॥
9:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:2.explanation: Six majestic qualities define this teaching: (1) 'Rājavidyā' — king among all sciences, the master-knowledge that illuminates all else; (2) 'Rājaguhyam' — the king of all secrets, not withheld but simply beyond the reach of the unready; (3) 'Pavitram' — supremely purifying, cleansing the mind of all ignorance-stains; (4) 'Pratyakṣāvagamam' — directly perceivable through one's own experience, not merely inferred or accepted on authority; (5) 'Susukhaṃ kartum' — delightfully easy to practice, for devotion requires no special caste, scholarship, or austerity; (6) 'Avyayam' — imperishable, what is once realized cannot be lost. This verse also gives the chapter its title and sets the tone: the teaching that follows is at once the highest and the most accessible.

9:3.text: O scorcher of foes, those persons who have no faith in this dharma, not attaining Me, return to the path of death and rebirth.
9:3.transliteration:
aśraddadhānāḥ puruṣā dharmasyāsya parantapa |
aprāpya māṃ nivartante mṛtyusaṃsāravartmani ||3||
9:3.devanagari:
अश्रद्दधानाः पुरुषा धर्मस्यास्य परन्तप ।
अप्राप्य मां निवर्तन्ते मृत्युसंसारवर्त्मनि ॥ ३ ॥
9:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:3.explanation: 'Aśraddadhānāḥ' — those without śraddhā. Śraddhā here is not mere intellectual belief but the receptive orientation of the heart that allows truth to penetrate and transform. Without this inner openness, even the highest teaching remains merely decorative knowledge. 'Mṛtyu-saṃsāra-vartmani' — the path of death and worldly cycling. Having extolled this teaching in verses 1–2, Krishna now shows the consequence of rejecting it: not punishment, but the natural continuation of ignorance. Those who do not receive cannot receive — this is not condemnation but cosmic logic. The word 'nivartante' (they return) implies they had a chance but chose to turn back.

9:4.text: By Me, in My unmanifest form, this entire universe is pervaded. All beings exist in Me, yet I am not contained within them.
9:4.transliteration:
mayā tatam idaṃ sarvaṃ jagad avyaktamūrtinā |
matsthāni sarvabhūtāni na cāhaṃ teṣv avasthitaḥ ||4||
9:4.devanagari:
मया ततमिदं सर्वं जगदव्यक्तमूर्तिना ।
मत्स्थानि सर्वभूतानि न चाहं तेष्ववस्थितः ॥ ४ ॥
9:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:4.explanation: This verse states one of the most profound paradoxes in all philosophy: 'All beings are in Me, yet I am not in them.' 'Avyakta-mūrtinā' — by My unmanifest aspect, my formless, all-pervading nature (not the form of Krishna standing before Arjuna, but the infinite Consciousness underlying it). The paradox is not a contradiction but a pointer beyond ordinary spatial logic: just as all waves are in the ocean but the ocean is not in any individual wave, all existence is contained within the Infinite, yet the Infinite is not limited by or contained within any finite thing. This is the simultaneous teaching of divine immanence (God within all) and divine transcendence (God beyond all).

9:5.text: Nor do beings rest in Me — behold My divine mystery (yoga)! My Self, which brings forth and sustains all beings, does not dwell within them.
9:5.transliteration:
na ca matsthāni bhūtāni paśya me yogam aiśvaram |
bhūtabhṛn na ca bhūtastho mamātmā bhūtabhāvanaḥ ||5||
9:5.devanagari:
न च मत्स्थानि भूतानि पश्य मे योगमैश्वरम् ।
भूतभृन्न च भूतस्थो ममात्मा भूतभावनः ॥ ५ ॥
9:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:5.explanation: Having said in verse 4 that all beings rest in Me, Krishna now adds the complement: 'nor do they rest in Me' — and calls this apparent contradiction his divine yoga (aiśvara yoga), his sovereign mystery. This is not logical inconsistency but a pointer to the nature of infinite Consciousness, which transcends the categories of 'in' and 'out.' 'Bhūtabhṛt' — the sustainer of all; 'bhūtabhāvanaḥ' — the originator of all; 'na bhūtasthaḥ' — yet not located within any being as a part within a whole. The Infinite contains the finite without being reduced to it; it sustains without being modified. This is the essence of the non-dual teaching: Brahman alone is, appearing as all, yet remaining unaltered.

9:6.text: Just as the mighty wind, moving everywhere, eternally abides in space (ākāśa), so understand that all beings abide in Me.
9:6.transliteration:
yathākāśasthito nityaṃ vāyuḥ sarvatrago mahān |
tathā sarvāṇi bhūtāni matsthānīty upadhāraya ||6||
9:6.devanagari:
यथाकाशस्थितो नित्यं वायुः सर्वत्रगो महान् ।
तथा सर्वाणि भूतानि मत्स्थानीत्युपधारय ॥ ६ ॥
9:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:6.explanation: To resolve the apparent paradox of verses 4–5, Krishna offers a luminous analogy: wind and space. The great wind (vāyu) — powerful, all-moving, vast — is always within space (ākāśa). Space holds the wind, sustains it, is its ground. Yet the wind does not affect space; space is not altered, compressed, or moved by the wind's force. 'Sarvatragaḥ mahān' — however mighty and pervasive the wind, it remains smaller than space. Similarly, all creation, however vast and dynamic, exists within the Infinite Consciousness — moves within it, is sustained by it — without diminishing or modifying it in the slightest. This analogy is not only philosophical explanation; in Vedantic meditation, space (ākāśa) is traditionally used as a symbol and gateway to realize Brahman.

9:7.text: O son of Kunti, all beings merge into My nature at the end of a kalpa (cosmic cycle), and at the beginning of the next kalpa, I send them forth again.
9:7.transliteration:
sarvabhūtāni kaunteya prakṛtiṃ yānti māmikām |
kalpakṣaye punas tāni kalpādau visṛjāmy aham ||7||
9:7.devanagari:
सर्वभूतानि कौन्तेय प्रकृतिं यान्ति मामिकाम् ।
कल्पक्षये पुनस्तानि कल्पादौ विसृजाम्यहम् ॥ ७ ॥
9:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:7.explanation: A kalpa is one day of Brahmā, approximately 4.32 billion years — vast cosmic time cycles that dwarf all human conceptions of history. At the end of each kalpa, there is mahā-pralaya (great dissolution): all beings return to their unmanifest state within Krishna's own nature (māmikāṃ prakṛtim), not annihilated but dormant like seeds. At the dawn of the next cycle, creation begins again. This is the cosmic rhythm — the universe breathing in and out. 'Visṛjāmi aham' — 'I project them forth' — establishes that Krishna/Brahman is the sole ultimate cause of all creation, not a secondary creator. Beings do not truly cease to exist; they rest in their source and are re-expressed.

9:8.text: Presiding over My own nature, I project forth again and again this entire multitude of beings, which are helpless, being subject to the force of nature (prakṛti).
9:8.transliteration:
prakṛtiṃ svām avaṣṭabhya visṛjāmi punaḥ punaḥ |
bhūtagrāmam imaṃ kṛtsnam avaśaṃ prakṛter vaśāt ||8||
9:8.devanagari:
प्रकृतिं स्वामवष्टभ्य विसृजामि पुनः पुनः ।
भूतग्राममिमं कृत्स्नमवशं प्रकृतेर्वशात् ॥ ८ ॥
9:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:8.explanation: 'Avaṣṭabhya' — holding, presiding, acting as the sovereign ground. Krishna does not personally manufacture each being but presides over His prakṛti — His creative power — as its conscious substratum, enabling the creative force to flow. 'Bhūtagrāmam kṛtsnam' — the entire aggregate of beings, the full scope of creation. 'Avaśam prakṛter vaśāt' — helpless under the power of prakṛti: beings do not choose the moment of their birth or their fundamental conditioning — they arise from and are governed by cosmic nature. Yet the key word is 'punaḥ punaḥ' — again and again — this is an eternal process, not a one-time event. Krishna is the conscious witness and ground of the process, not bound by it, as the next verse will clarify.

9:9.text: And these actions do not bind Me, O Dhanañjaya, for I remain seated as one indifferent (udāsīnavat), unattached to those actions.
9:9.transliteration:
na ca māṃ tāni karmāṇi nibadhnanti dhanañjaya |
udāsīnavad āsīnam asaktaṃ teṣu karmasu ||9||
9:9.devanagari:
न च मां तानि कर्माणि निबध्नन्ति धनञ्जय ।
उदासीनवदासीनमसक्तं तेषु कर्मसु ॥ ९ ॥
9:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:9.explanation: The cosmic acts of creation and dissolution — described in verses 7–8 as recurring endlessly — generate no karmic bondage for Krishna. 'Udāsīnavat' is a precise and important word: it does not mean 'indifferent' in the sense of uncaring, but rather 'seated above' the action, like a witness who is present but uninvested in the outcomes. 'Asaktam' — utterly unattached. This is the living demonstration of karma yoga's deepest principle: action performed without ego-identification and desire for fruit leaves no karmic residue. Krishna is the cosmic exemplar of this. Arjuna is being told, implicitly: fight as I create — fully, without personal attachment to result, and no bondage will follow.

9:10.text: Under My superintendence, O Kaunteya, Nature (prakṛti) brings forth the moving and the unmoving. By this reason the world revolves (is constantly in flux).
9:10.transliteration:
mayādhyakṣeṇa prakṛtiḥ sūyate sacarācaram |
hetunānena kaunteya jagad viparivartate ||10||
9:10.devanagari:
मयाध्यक्षेण प्रकृतिः सूयते सचराचरम् ।
हेतुनानेन कौन्तेय जगद्विपरिवर्तते ॥ १० ॥
9:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:10.explanation: 'Mayādhyakṣeṇa' — under My oversight, with Me as the supreme witness-superintendent. Prakṛti has its own laws, forces, and processes; yet it is not autonomous. Just as sunlight enables all activity on earth without the sun 'doing' anything directly — its mere presence is the enabling cause — so Krishna's Consciousness is the ultimate enabling ground of all that prakṛti does. 'Sacarācaram' — all moving (mobile life: animals, humans, wind, water) and non-moving (immobile: mountains, rocks, earth) creation. 'Viparivartate' — is constantly in motion, revolving through its cycles. This verse establishes Krishna as the nimittakāraṇa (efficient cause) of the universe, while prakṛti is the upādāna-kāraṇa (material cause).

9:11.text: Fools disregard Me when I dwell in a human form, not knowing My higher nature as the great Lord of all beings.
9:11.transliteration:
avajānanti māṃ mūḍhā mānuṣīṃ tanum āśritam |
paraṃ bhāvam ajānanto mama bhūtamaheśvaram ||11||
9:11.devanagari:
अवजानन्ति मां मूढा मानुषीं तनुमाश्रितम् ।
परं भावमजानन्तो मम भूतमहेश्वरम् ॥ ११ ॥
9:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:11.explanation: 'Avajānanti' — they look down upon, disregard, dismiss. 'Mūḍhāḥ' — those whose intellect is clouded by ignorance and the veil of the apparent. When Krishna stood before Arjuna as a charioteer-king, many saw a man. The profound error: they saw the form and missed the formless. 'Mānuṣīṃ tanum āśritam' — taking a human form as His vehicle. Taking a body is not a limitation on Krishna's infinite nature but a compassionate self-revelation. 'Paraṃ bhāvam ajānantaḥ' — not knowing His supreme nature as the mahā-īśvara, the great sovereign of all existence. This verse addresses the universal human tendency to limit the Infinite to what can be physically perceived — to see the finger pointing at the moon but miss the moon entirely.

9:12.text: Those of bewildered mind take refuge in the deluding nature of demons and fiends — their hopes are vain, their actions vain, their knowledge vain.
9:12.transliteration:
moghāśā moghakarmāṇo moghajñānā vicetasaḥ |
rākṣasīm āsurīṃ caiva prakṛtiṃ mohinīṃ śritāḥ ||12||
9:12.devanagari:
मोघाशा मोघकर्माणो मोघज्ञाना विचेतसः ।
राक्षसीमासुरीं चैव प्रकृतिं मोहिनीं श्रिताः ॥ १२ ॥
9:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:12.explanation: The consequence of the error described in verse 11 is now elaborated: three kinds of futility that plague those who deny the divine. 'Moghāśāḥ' — their hopes are vain: without the Divine as the ultimate ground, every aspiration eventually crumbles, for all finite achievements perish. 'Moghakarmāṇaḥ' — their actions are vain: karma performed without awareness of the Infinite perpetuates bondage rather than liberation. 'Moghajñānāḥ' — their knowledge is vain: learning divorced from wisdom about the source of Consciousness is ultimately hollow. 'Rākṣasīm āsurīṃ prakṛtim' — the deluding nature characteristic of rākṣasas and asuras: defined by ego, violence, denial of God, and excessive self-centeredness. This is not a permanent damnation but a description of a quality of being that perpetuates suffering.

9:13.text: But O Pārtha, great souls (mahātmās), sheltered in the divine nature, worship Me with undivided mind, knowing Me as the imperishable origin of all beings.
9:13.transliteration:
mahātmānas tu māṃ pārtha daivīṃ prakṛtim āśritāḥ |
bhajanti ananyamanaso jñātvā bhūtādim avyayam ||13||
9:13.devanagari:
महात्मानस्तु मां पार्थ दैवीं प्रकृतिमाश्रिताः ।
भजन्त्यनन्यमनसो ज्ञात्वा भूतादिमव्ययम् ॥ १३ ॥
9:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:13.explanation: 'Mahātmānas' — great souls, those whose inner nature has expanded beyond ego-contraction into a vast, inclusive consciousness. 'Daivīṃ prakṛtim āśritāḥ' — sheltered in the divine nature: sāttvika, truth-oriented, God-facing. In contrast to the asuric nature of verse 12 (which turns away from the Divine), the mahātmā's nature naturally moves toward the Source. 'Jñātvā bhūtādim avyayam' — knowing Me as the imperishable, original cause of all beings: their devotion is not blind but rooted in understanding. 'Ananyamanasaḥ' — with undivided mind, without the mind splitting its attention between the Divine and countless worldly desires. This first explicit mention of ananya (undivided) bhakti establishes the gold standard of devotion in the Gita.

9:14.text: Always singing My glories, striving with firm resolve, bowing before Me with devotion — these ever-steadfast ones worship Me.
9:14.transliteration:
satataṃ kīrtayanto māṃ yatantaś ca dṛḍhavratāḥ |
namasyantaś ca māṃ bhaktyā nityayuktā upāsate ||14||
9:14.devanagari:
सततं कीर्तयन्तो मां यतन्तश्च दृढव्रताः ।
नमस्यन्तश्च मां भक्त्या नित्ययुक्ता उपासते ॥ १४ ॥
9:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:14.explanation: This verse describes the practical, lived expression of the mahātmā's devotion in four active forms: (1) 'Satataṃ kīrtayantaḥ' — perpetually singing My glories, not only in formal worship but as the natural overflow of a heart saturated with the Divine; (2) 'Yatantaḥ dṛḍhavratāḥ' — striving with firm, unshaken vows — spiritual effort is not passive, it requires committed, sustained practice; (3) 'Namasyantaḥ' — bowing, prostrating — the physical gesture of surrendering the ego; (4) 'Nityayuktāḥ' — ever-yoked, perpetually connected — the continuity of God-remembrance through all activities. 'Bhaktyā upāsate' — they worship with bhakti: not mechanical ritual but the spontaneous movement of love toward its source. This verse maps onto the navadha-bhakti (ninefold devotion) described in the Bhāgavata Purāṇa.

9:15.text: And others, worshipping through the sacrifice of knowledge (jñāna-yajña), worship Me as the One, as the Distinct, and in many forms facing all directions.
9:15.transliteration:
jñānayajñena cāpy anye yajanto mām upāsate |
ekatvena pṛthaktvena bahudhā viśvatomukham ||15||
9:15.devanagari:
ज्ञानयज्ञेन चाप्यन्ये यजन्तो मामुपासते ।
एकत्वेन पृथक्त्वेन बहुधा विश्वतोमुखम् ॥ १५ ॥
9:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:15.explanation: Having described the path of devotional bhakti (verses 13–14), Krishna now acknowledges other valid paths of worship. 'Jñāna-yajñena' — through the sacrifice of knowledge: Vedantic inquiry, scriptural study, direct contemplation. Three modalities: (1) 'Ekatvena' — as the One, the non-dual Absolute: the path of Advaita Vedānta — 'I and Brahman are one'; (2) 'Pṛthaktvena' — as the Distinct, worshipping God as a separate, personal deity: the path of dualistic devotion; (3) 'Bahudhā viśvatomukham' — in many forms, seeing the Divine in all faces of the universe: cosmic vision. The Gita's stunning inclusivity: Krishna accepts all sincere paths as approaches to Himself. The methods differ; the destination is one. This demolishes all spiritual exclusivism.

9:16.text: I am the ritual (kratu), I am the sacrifice (yajña), I am the oblation to ancestors (svadhā), I am the medicinal herb (auṣadha), I am the sacred mantra, I am the clarified butter (ājya), I am the sacred fire, and I am the act of offering.
9:16.transliteration:
ahaṃ kratur aham yajñaḥ svadhāham aham auṣadham |
mantro 'ham aham evājyam aham agnir ahaṃ hutam ||16||
9:16.devanagari:
अहं क्रतुरहं यज्ञः स्वधाहमहमौषधम् ।
मन्त्रोऽहमहमेवाज्यमहमग्निरहं हुतम् ॥ १६ ॥
9:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:16.explanation: Eight-fold 'I am' declarations encompassing the entire Vedic sacrificial universe. Kratu: the Śrauta (solemn Vedic rite); yajña: the Smārta (household rite); svadhā: the ancestral oblation; auṣadham: the medicinal plant (food offering); mantra: the sacred word; ājya: the clarified butter fed to fire; agni: the sacred fire itself; hutam: the act of offering. The profound implication: in any Vedic yajña, every component — the performer, the instrument, the offering, the recipient fire, the action, the result — all are the Divine. This dissolves the distinction between worshipper and worshipped. The Infinite is not merely honored in the ritual; the Infinite IS the ritual, in all its parts. This is a complete ontological re-visioning of sacrificial practice.

9:17.text: I am the father of this universe, the mother, the sustainer, the grandsire; I am the knowable (vedyam), the purifier, the syllable Oṃ; and I am the Ṛg, the Sāma, and the Yajur Veda.
9:17.transliteration:
pitāham asya jagato mātā dhātā pitāmahaḥ |
vedyaṃ pavitram oṃkāra ṛk sāma yajur eva ca ||17||
9:17.devanagari:
पिताहमस्य जगतो माता धाता पितामहः ।
वेद्यं पवित्रमोङ्कार ऋक्साम यजुरेव च ॥ १७ ॥
9:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:17.explanation: The 'I am' declarations continue, now moving from ritual to cosmic parenthood and sacred knowledge: Father — the projecting, ruling principle; Mother — the nourishing, protective, sustaining aspect (manifest as Prakṛti); Dhātā (sustainer) — the one who holds cosmic order; Pitāmahaḥ (grandfather, grandsire) — the ancestral root of all lineages and time. Then the knowledge tradition: 'Vedyam' — that which is to be known, the supreme object of all inquiry, Brahman Itself; 'Pavitram' — the ultimate purifier; 'Oṃkāra' — the primordial sound, the name of Brahman, the Praṇava that contains all speech; 'Ṛk, Sāma, Yajus' — the three principal Vedas. All revealed knowledge, all sacred sound, all family bonds — all rooted in the One.

9:18.text: I am the goal, the sustainer, the Lord, the witness, the abode, the refuge, the selfless friend; I am origin and dissolution, the foundation, the resting place, and the imperishable seed.
9:18.transliteration:
gatir bhartā prabhuḥ sākṣī nivāsaḥ śaraṇaṃ suhṛt |
prabhavaḥ pralayaḥ sthānaṃ nidhānaṃ bījam avyayam ||18||
9:18.devanagari:
गतिर्भर्ता प्रभुः साक्षी निवासः शरणं सुहृत् ।
प्रभवः प्रलयः स्थानं निधानं बीजमव्ययम् ॥ १८ ॥
9:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:18.explanation: Twelve sovereign attributes in a single verse: (1) Gatiḥ — the supreme Goal toward which all paths lead; (2) Bhartā — the sustainer, the one who nourishes and upholds all life; (3) Prabhuḥ — the sovereign Lord; (4) Sākṣī — the eternal Witness, present within all experience, unmoved, untouched; (5) Nivāsaḥ — the inner abode, the ātman dwelling in every heart; (6) Śaraṇam — the refuge, the ultimate shelter when all else fails; (7) Suhṛt — the disinterested friend, who loves without agenda or condition; (8) Prabhavaḥ — the source of creation; (9) Pralayaḥ — the point of dissolution; (10) Sthānam — the unchanging foundation during manifest existence; (11) Nidhānam — the resting repository; (12) Bījam avyayam — the imperishable seed, the undying creative potential within all. This verse is one of the most complete self-descriptions of Brahman in the entire Gita.

9:19.text: I give heat; I withhold and I send forth the rain; I am immortality and also death; I am both being (sat) and non-being (asat), O Arjuna.
9:19.transliteration:
tapāmy aham ahaṃ varṣaṃ nigṛhṇāmy utsṛjāmi ca |
amṛtaṃ caiva mṛtyuś ca sad asac cāham arjuna ||19||
9:19.devanagari:
तपाम्यहमहं वर्षं निगृह्णाम्युत्सृजामि च ।
अमृतं चैव मृत्युश्च सदसच्चाहमर्जुन ॥ १९ ॥
9:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:19.explanation: The final verse in the 'I am' sequence unites all apparent opposites within the One. As the sun, Krishna radiates heat; as the controller of atmospheric forces, He withholds and sends rain — the cosmic environmental process is His self-expression. 'Amṛtaṃ ca mṛtyuś ca' — He is immortality (the deathless ātman, mokṣa) and also death (the inevitable dissolution of forms): both the continuation of life and its cessation are divine. 'Sad asac ca' — both being (the manifest, existent world) and non-being (the unmanifest, latent state) are He. This is the scriptural fulfillment of 'Sarvam khalv idaṃ Brahma' — all of this is Brahman. The universe of contradictions resolves into a single, seamless Reality.

9:20.text: The knowers of the three Vedas, who drink the soma and are purified of sin, worship Me through sacrifices, praying for passage to heaven. They reach the holy realm of Indra and enjoy the divine pleasures of the gods in heaven.
9:20.transliteration:
traividyā māṃ somapāḥ pūtapāpā
yajñair iṣṭvā svargatiṃ prārthayante |
te puṇyam āsādya surendralokam
aśnanti divyān divi devabhogān ||20||
9:20.devanagari:
त्रैविद्या मां सोमपाः पूतपापा
यज्ञैरिष्ट्वा स्वर्गतिं प्रार्थयन्ते ।
ते पुण्यमासाद्य सुरेन्द्रलोक-
मश्नन्ति दिव्यान्दिवि देवभोगान् ॥ २० ॥
9:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:20.explanation: This verse and the next form a teaching pair contrasting conditional devotion with unconditional devotion. 'Traividyāḥ' — masters of the three Vedas (Ṛg, Sāma, Yajus). 'Somapāḥ' — drinkers of soma, participants in the solemn Vedic Somayāga (the ritual centered on the sacred soma plant). 'Pūtapāpāḥ' — purified of sin through ritual observance. These are sincere, learned, disciplined practitioners. They worship correctly — 'yajñair iṣṭvā' — through prescribed sacrifices. Yet their orientation is toward 'svargatim' — passage to heaven, the realm of sensory enjoyment on a divine scale. They reach Indra's realm and enjoy celestial pleasures. This is honored as a valid achievement — but it is not the final destination, as verse 21 will show.

9:21.text: Having enjoyed that vast heavenly world, when their merit is exhausted, they re-enter the mortal world. Thus, following the dharma of the three Vedas, those who desire enjoyment attain only the cycle of coming and going.
9:21.transliteration:
te taṃ bhuktvā svargalokaṃ viśālaṃ
kṣīṇe puṇye martyalokaṃ viśanti |
evaṃ trayīdharmam anuprapannā
gatāgataṃ kāmakāmā labhante ||21||
9:21.devanagari:
ते तं भुक्त्वा स्वर्गलोकं विशालं
क्षीणे पुण्ये मर्त्यलोकं विशन्ति ।
एवं त्रयीधर्ममनुप्रपन्ना
गतागतं कामकामा लभन्ते ॥ २१ ॥
9:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:21.explanation: The critical limitation of heaven-seeking devotion: it is finite. 'Kṣīṇe puṇye' — when the accumulated merit is depleted, like a bank account running dry. Heaven is not eternal; it is the enjoyment of a reward proportional to accumulated virtue. Once consumed, the soul returns to earth — 'martyalokaṃ viśanti.' 'Gatāgatam' — the going and coming, the endless cycle. 'Kāmakāmāḥ' — those who desire desires, whose inner orientation is toward obtaining outcomes rather than union with the Source. This is the Gita's most honest statement about Vedic ritualism: it is valuable, it is purifying, it leads to elevated experience — but so long as desire drives the practice, liberation is not the result. Only desireless devotion — yearning for God Himself, not His gifts — breaks the cycle permanently.

9:22.text: For those persons who worship Me with undivided devotion, meditating on My transcendental form — to those ever-steadfast ones I personally carry what they lack and preserve what they have (yoga-kṣemam).
9:22.transliteration:
ananyāś cintayanto māṃ ye janāḥ paryupāsate |
teṣāṃ nityābhiyuktānāṃ yogakṣemaṃ vahāmy aham ||22||
9:22.devanagari:
अनन्याश्चिन्तयन्तो मां ये जनाः पर्युपासते ।
तेषां नित्याभियुक्तानां योगक्षेमं वहाम्यहम् ॥ २२ ॥
9:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:22.explanation: One of the most beloved and consoling verses in all spiritual literature. 'Ananyāḥ' — those of undivided, one-pointed devotion, whose entire being is oriented toward the Divine alone. 'Nityābhiyuktānām' — those perpetually yoked to the Divine, in continuous God-remembrance. 'Yogakṣemaṃ vahāmy aham' — 'I personally bear their yoga and kṣema.' Yoga = securing what they have not yet obtained; kṣema = protecting what they already have. This is Krishna's personal guarantee: the devotee who is totally surrendered need not strategize for their security or advancement — the Divine Itself becomes their providential guardian. 'Vahāmi' — I carry: not delegate, not enable — personally carry. This verse has been treasured for millennia as the scriptural foundation of trust in divine grace.

9:23.text: O Kaunteya, even those devotees who, endowed with faith, worship other gods — they too worship Me alone, but in an improper (unregulated) manner.
9:23.transliteration:
ye 'py anyadevatābhaktā yajante śraddhayānvitāḥ |
te 'pi mām eva kaunteya yajanty avidhipūrvakam ||23||
9:23.devanagari:
येऽप्यन्यदेवताभक्ता यजन्ते श्रद्धयान्विताः ।
तेऽपि मामेव कौन्तेय यजन्त्यविधिपूर्वकम् ॥ २३ ॥
9:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:23.explanation: 'Anya-devatābhaktāḥ' — devotees of other divinities: Indra, Varuṇa, Durgā, Lakṣmī, and all manner of divine forms. 'Yajante śraddhayānvitāḥ' — they worship with sincere faith, which is honorable. 'Te 'pi mām eva' — they too worship Me alone — because all divine forms are manifestations or expressions of the One Brahman, the single Consciousness underlying all deities. 'Avidhipūrvakam' — improperly, without complete understanding: not knowing that the ultimate referent of all devotion is the One. This is not condemnation but compassionate acknowledgment: the destination is reached, but the path is indirect and the knowledge incomplete. Like addressing a letter with a slight error — the letter may still arrive. The Gita's universal vision: all sincere devotion reaches the Divine, directly or indirectly.

9:24.text: For I am the only enjoyer and the only Lord of all sacrifices. Those who do not know Me in truth fall down (from their attainment).
9:24.transliteration:
ahaṃ hi sarvayajñānāṃ bhoktā ca prabhur eva ca |
na tu mām abhijānanti tattvenātas cyavanti te ||24||
9:24.devanagari:
अहं हि सर्वयज्ञानां भोक्ता च प्रभुरेव च ।
न तु मामभिजानन्ति तत्त्वेनातश्च्यवन्ति ते ॥ २४ ॥
9:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:24.explanation: 'Sarvayajñānāṃ bhoktā' — the sole ultimate receiver of all acts of sacrifice. The devatas (individual deities) may be proximate recipients, channels through whom the offering passes, but the ultimate ground and enjoyer is the single Brahman-Consciousness. 'Prabhuḥ' — the sole sovereign Lord governing the entire sacrificial order. 'Na tu mām abhijānanti tattvena' — they do not know Me in truth: they honor the form but miss the formless ground behind it; they see the river but not the ocean it flows from. 'Atas cyavanti' — therefore they fall: they gain temporary heavenly states but, not being anchored in the Eternal, they eventually return. True knowledge — knowing the One behind all forms — is the difference between temporary elevation and permanent liberation.

9:25.text: Worshippers of the gods go to the gods; those devoted to ancestors reach the ancestors; those who worship spirits go to the spirits; and those who worship Me come to Me.
9:25.transliteration:
yānti devavratā devān pitṝn yānti pitṛvratāḥ |
bhūtāni yānti bhūtejyā yānti madyājino 'pi mām ||25||
9:25.devanagari:
यान्ति देवव्रता देवान्पितॄन्यान्ति पितृव्रताः ।
भूतानि यान्ति भूतेज्या यान्ति मद्याजिनोऽपि माम् ॥ २५ ॥
9:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:25.explanation: A precise cosmological law of spiritual correspondence: the nature of your devotion determines the nature of your destination. 'Devavratāḥ' — vowed to the gods — reach the divine realms; 'pitṛvratāḥ' — devoted to ancestral worship — reach the ancestral worlds; 'bhūtejyāḥ' — worshippers of elemental spirits — reach those planes. 'Madyājinaḥ' — those who sacrifice for Me — reach Me. None of these paths is false or improper in itself; the difference is in the final destination: all worlds other than the Divine are finite — the gods, ancestors, and spirits themselves are bound by time. Only the Infinite is imperishable. The subtle encouragement: come to Me, and you receive what no other destination can offer. And the way to come to Me is revealed in the next verse — simpler than any elaborate ritual.

9:26.text: Whoever offers Me with devotion a leaf, a flower, a fruit, or water — that offering of love from the pure-hearted one I accept.
9:26.transliteration:
patraṃ puṣpaṃ phalaṃ toyaṃ yo me bhaktyā prayacchati |
tad ahaṃ bhaktyupahṛtam aśnāmi prayatātmanaḥ ||26||
9:26.devanagari:
पत्रं पुष्पं फलं तोयं यो मे भक्त्या प्रयच्छति ।
तदहं भक्त्युपहृतमश्नामि प्रयतात्मनः ॥ २६ ॥
9:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:26.explanation: One of the most beloved, tender, and democratizing verses in the Bhagavad Gita. Four of nature's simplest gifts: a leaf, a flower, a fruit, water — available to the poorest person on earth. No gold, no elaborate ritual, no priestly intermediary, no expensive sacrifice. 'Bhaktyā prayacchati' — given with devotion: it is the quality of the inner offering that matters, not its market value. 'Prayatātmanaḥ' — from the pure-hearted: sincerity, cleanness of intention, openness of heart. 'Aśnāmi' — I eat, I accept, I receive: Krishna uses this intimate, personal language — He is moved by the offering of love. This verse is the Gita's declaration that spirituality is not the privilege of the wealthy or the learned. It belongs equally to every sincere human heart, regardless of social position, wealth, or ritual knowledge.

9:27.text: Whatever you do, whatever you eat, whatever you offer in sacrifice, whatever you give in charity, whatever austerity you practice — O Kaunteya, do that as an offering to Me.
9:27.transliteration:
yat karoṣi yad aśnāsi yaj juhoṣi dadāsi yat |
yat tapasyasi kaunteya tat kuruṣva madarpaṇam ||27||
9:27.devanagari:
यत्करोषि यदश्नासि यज्जुहोषि ददासि यत् ।
यत्तपस्यसि कौन्तेय तत्कुरुष्व मदर्पणम् ॥ २७ ॥
9:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:27.explanation: The definitive statement of karma-as-offering: a formula for transforming every moment of life into worship. Five categories encompassing all of human activity: (1) 'Karoṣi' — all actions, all daily work and duty; (2) 'Aśnāsi' — eating, the most ordinary biological necessity; (3) 'Juhoṣi' — ritual offerings, formal spiritual acts; (4) 'Dadāsi' — charitable giving, service to others; (5) 'Tapasyasi' — austerity, disciplined spiritual practice. 'Tat kuruṣva madarpaṇam' — let all of that be an offering to Me. When life itself becomes the offering, the distinction between sacred and secular dissolves. The kitchen becomes a temple, the meal a prasāda, the workplace a field of worship. This is the practical integration that karma yoga aims for — not escape from life, but consecration of life.

9:28.text: Thus you shall be liberated from the bondage of actions with their good and evil results. With the spirit disciplined in the yoga of renunciation (sannyāsa-yoga), you shall become free and come to Me.
9:28.transliteration:
śubhāśubhaphalair evaṃ mokṣyase karmabandhanaiḥ |
sannyāsayogayuktātmā vimukto mām upaiṣyasi ||28||
9:28.devanagari:
शुभाशुभफलैरेवं मोक्ष्यसे कर्मबन्धनैः ।
सन्न्यासयोगयुक्तात्मा विमुक्तो मामुपैष्यसि ॥ २८ ॥
9:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:28.explanation: 'Śubhāśubhaphalaḥ' — both good (puṇya) and evil (pāpa) fruits create bondage: golden chains and iron chains are both chains. The offering-surrender described in verse 27 dissolves both. 'Sannyāsa-yoga-yuktātmā' — one whose spirit is united with both sannyāsa (inner renunciation of fruit) and yoga (active dedicated action): not the external renunciation of abandoning home, but the inner renunciation of abandoning personal claim over outcomes. This is the karma-sannyāsa synthesis — full engagement in life, full inner detachment from outcomes. 'Vimukto mām upaiṣyasi' — fully liberated, you shall come to Me. The path: offer everything → internal bondage dissolves → freedom → union with the Divine. The sequence is logically complete.

9:29.text: I am equal toward all beings; none is hateful to Me and none is dear. Yet those who worship Me with devotion are in Me, and I am also in them.
9:29.transliteration:
samo 'haṃ sarvabhūteṣu na me dveṣyo 'sti na priyaḥ |
ye bhajanti tu māṃ bhaktyā mayi te teṣu cāpy aham ||29||
9:29.devanagari:
समोऽहं सर्वभूतेषु न मे द्वेष्योऽस्ति न प्रियः ।
ये भजन्ति तु मां भक्त्या मयि ते तेषु चाप्यहम् ॥ २९ ॥
9:29.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:29.explanation: Two profound truths in a single verse, apparently in tension but actually complementary. First: 'Samo 'ham sarvabhūteṣu' — I am equidistant from and equal toward all beings, like the sun that shines impartially on all. 'Na me dveṣyaḥ' — no one is hateful to Me, however deeply they may have erred. 'Na priyaḥ' — none is specially dear in a preferential sense. God plays no favorites. Second: 'Ye bhajanti māṃ bhaktyā mayi te teṣu cāpy aham' — yet those who worship with devotion enter into a special mutual indwelling with Me. This is not contradiction: the sun shines equally on all, but only the soul who turns a mirror toward it receives its full focused reflection. Devotion is that turning. God does not change; the devotee's capacity to receive changes.

9:30.text: Even if a person of very evil conduct worships Me with undivided devotion, he must be regarded as righteous (sādhu), for he has resolved rightly.
9:30.transliteration:
api cet sudurācāro bhajate mām ananyabhāk |
sādhur eva sa mantavyaḥ samyag vyavasito hi saḥ ||30||
9:30.devanagari:
अपि चेत्सुदुराचारो भजते मामनन्यभाक् ।
साधुरेव स मन्तव्यः सम्यग्व्यवसितो हि सः ॥ ३० ॥
9:30.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:30.explanation: One of the most revolutionary and compassionate statements in all of scripture. 'Sudurācāraḥ' — one of the worst conduct, the most thoroughgoing sinner. 'Ananyabhāk' — with undivided, single-pointed devotion. The verdict: 'Sādhur eva sa mantavyaḥ' — he is to be considered a sādhu (righteous person) nonetheless. The reason: 'Samyag vyavasitaḥ' — he has resolved rightly, meaning his inner orientation has decisively turned from wrongness toward the Divine. The pivotal moment is the turning, not the completion of the journey. Like a traveler who reverses direction: in that instant of reversal, they are already on the correct path, even if far from the destination. This verse makes the radical declaration that divine grace is greater than the accumulation of any past wrongdoing. Orientation is destiny.

9:31.text: He swiftly becomes a righteous soul and attains everlasting peace. O Kaunteya, declare with certainty: My devotee is never lost.
9:31.transliteration:
kṣipraṃ bhavati dharmātmā śaśvacchāntiṃ nigacchati |
kaunteya pratijānīhi na me bhaktaḥ praṇaśyati ||31||
9:31.devanagari:
क्षिप्रं भवति धर्मात्मा शश्वच्छान्तिं निगच्छति ।
कौन्तेय प्रतिजानीहि न मे भक्तः प्रणश्यति ॥ ३१ ॥
9:31.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:31.explanation: 'Kṣipraṃ bhavati dharmātmā' — swiftly he becomes a soul of dharma: the transformation is rapid because devotion activates a power beyond ordinary moral effort. 'Śaśvacchāntiṃ nigacchati' — he reaches everlasting peace, not temporary relief but the permanent peace of liberation. Now the most solemn moment: 'Kaunteya pratijānīhi' — O Arjuna, YOU declare it — Krishna turns to Arjuna as witness and spokesman, making Arjuna a vehicle of this divine proclamation. 'Na me bhaktaḥ praṇaśyati' — My devotee is never destroyed, never lost, never ultimately fails. This is the supreme divine guarantee of the Gita: not 'perhaps,' not 'if,' but an absolute, unconditional assurance. The devotee may stumble, may err, may even be a great sinner — but the one who holds the Divine will not ultimately perish.

9:32.text: O Pārtha, even those who may be born of sinful wombs — women, Vaiśyas, and Śūdras — even they, taking refuge in Me, attain the supreme destination.
9:32.transliteration:
māṃ hi pārtha vyapāśritya ye 'pi syuḥ pāpayonayaḥ |
striyo vaiśyās tathā śūdrās te 'pi yānti parāṃ gatim ||32||
9:32.devanagari:
मां हि पार्थ व्यपाश्रित्य येऽपि स्युः पापयोनयः ।
स्त्रियो वैश्यास्तथा शूद्रास्तेऽपि यान्ति परां गतिम् ॥ ३२ ॥
9:32.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:32.explanation: This verse must be read in its historical context: the ancient social framework of the Gita's composition, in which access to Vedic study and formal liberation paths was restricted by caste and gender. Within that framework, Krishna's declaration is radical and inclusive. 'Pāpayonayaḥ' — those born in circumstances considered lowly by the prevailing system. 'Striyaḥ' — women, who were denied Vedic study. 'Vaiśyāḥ' — merchants, the third order. 'Śūdrāḥ' — the service class, the fourth. 'Te 'pi yānti parāṃ gatim' — even they attain the supreme destination by taking refuge in Me. The liberating principle: access to the Divine is not mediated by birth, caste, gender, or social rank. Bhakti — the turning of the heart toward the Divine — is the great equalizer. This is the Gita's revolutionary democratization of mokṣa.

9:33.text: How much more, then, the pure Brāhmaṇas and the devoted royal sages! Having obtained this transient and joyless world, worship Me.
9:33.transliteration:
kiṃ punar brāhmaṇāḥ puṇyā bhaktā rājarṣayas tathā |
anityam asukhaṃ lokam imaṃ prāpya bhajasva mām ||33||
9:33.devanagari:
किं पुनर्ब्राह्मणाः पुण्या भक्ता राजर्षयस्तथा ।
अनित्यमसुखं लोकमिमं प्राप्य भजस्व माम् ॥ ३३ ॥
9:33.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:33.explanation: 'Kiṃ punaḥ' — 'how much more then' — a rhetorical escalation completing the argument: if even those in the most disadvantaged circumstances by the ancient social system can reach the supreme goal, then the pure Brāhmaṇa (one established in knowledge and virtue) and the rājarṣi (the king who lives as a sage) — they can reach it with even greater certainty. The argument moves from the hardest case to the easiest — from the margins to the center — making liberation universally achievable. Then the pivot: 'Anityam asukhaṃ lokam imaṃ prāpya bhajasva mām' — 'having obtained this transient and joyless world — worship Me.' The human birth is both rare and precarious: rare in its potential for liberation, precarious in its brevity. The urgency is not fear but opportunity. Now is the moment; the instrument (human birth) is in your hands.

9:34.text: Fix your mind on Me, be devoted to Me, worship Me, bow down to Me. Having thus disciplined yourself, with Me as your supreme goal, you shall come to Me alone.
9:34.transliteration:
manmanā bhava madbhakto madyājī māṃ namaskuru |
mām evaiṣyasi yuktvaivam ātmānaṃ matparāyaṇaḥ ||34||
9:34.devanagari:
मन्मना भव मद्भक्तो मद्याजी मां नमस्कुरु ।
मामेवैष्यसि युक्त्वैवमात्मानं मत्परायणः ॥ ३४ ॥
9:34.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
9:34.explanation: The closing verse of the Ninth Chapter is the crystallized essence of the entire Gita's devotional path — four disciplines in a single verse, each progressive and complementary: (1) 'Manmanā bhava' — Let your mind rest in Me: the mind, which is the root instrument of bondage and liberation, directed wholly toward the Divine; (2) 'Madbhaktaḥ' — Be My devotee: not a formal title but an inner orientation of love; (3) 'Madyājī' — Worship Me: external and internal acts of sacred offering; (4) 'Māṃ namaskuru' — Bow to Me: the ego surrenders its claim of separateness. 'Matparāyaṇaḥ' — holding Me as the supreme, ultimate goal — not one among many aims, but the final aim. 'Mām evaiṣyasi' — you shall come to Me alone. This verse is so pivotal that Krishna virtually repeats it in 18.65 at the close of the entire Gita, calling it there 'the highest secret.' It is the Gita's recurring refrain: the path is surrender of the mind, heart, action, and ego — and the destination is the Divine itself.

10:1.text: The Blessed Lord said: O mighty-armed, hear once more My supreme word, which I shall speak to you with love, desiring your welfare.
10:1.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
bhūya eva mahābāho śṛṇu me paramaṃ vacaḥ |
yat te 'haṃ prīyamāṇāya vakṣyāmi hitakāmyayā ||1||
10:1.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
भूय एव महाबाहो शृणु मे परमं वचः ।
यत्तेऽहं प्रीयमाणाय वक्ष्यामि हितकाम्यया ॥ १ ॥
10:1.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:1.explanation: 'Bhūyaḥ' — again, once more. The teaching does not end; it deepens. Chapter 9 ended with the most intimate of invitations; Chapter 10 opens with the same tender energy. 'Paramaṃ vacaḥ' — the supreme word, a declaration that what follows surpasses what has been said before. 'Prīyamāṇāya' — to you who are dear to Me: the motivation for this highest teaching is love, not duty. 'Hitakāmyayā' — out of desire for your welfare: not self-promotion, not philosophical exhibition, but the compassionate impulse of a teacher who sees a student he loves and wants to give him everything needed for liberation. The opening of Chapter 10 is thus itself a teaching: the highest knowledge flows only in the channel of love.

10:2.text: Neither the hosts of gods nor the great sages know My origin, for I am in every way the ultimate source (ādi) of the gods and great sages.
10:2.transliteration:
na me viduḥ suragaṇāḥ prabhavaṃ na maharṣayaḥ |
aham ādir hi devānāṃ maharṣīṇāṃ ca sarvaśaḥ ||2||
10:2.devanagari:
न मे विदुः सुरगणाः प्रभवं न महर्षयः ।
अहमादिर्हि देवानां महर्षीणां च सर्वशः ॥ २ ॥
10:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:2.explanation: 'Suragaṇāḥ' — the entire pantheon of gods, including Brahmā, Indra, and Varuṇa. 'Maharṣayaḥ' — the great sages who are the fountainheads of Vedic knowledge. Neither knows Krishna's prabhavam — His origin, arising, source. The reason is stated directly: 'Aham ādiḥ' — I am the very origin of them all. No effect can fully know its cause from within itself. The gods and the sages arise from this Consciousness; they move within it, are sustained by it — but they cannot step outside it to perceive its ground. This is not a limitation of intelligence but a structural impossibility: the known cannot completely encompass the knower. Only direct revelation — as Krishna is giving right now — can bridge this gap.

10:3.text: He who knows Me as unborn (aja), without beginning (anādi), and the great Lord of all worlds — among mortals, he is undeluded, and is freed from all sins.
10:3.transliteration:
yo mām ajam anādiṃ ca vetti lokamahīśvaram |
asammūḍhaḥ sa martyeṣu sarvapāpaiḥ pramucyate ||3||
10:3.devanagari:
यो मामजमनादिं च वेत्ति लोकमहेश्वरम् ।
असम्मूढः स मर्त्येषु सर्वपापैः प्रमुच्यते ॥ ३ ॥
10:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:3.explanation: Three defining qualities of Krishna's supreme nature: (1) 'Ajam' — unborn: the body appears and disappears, but the Consciousness underlying it is never born. Krishna's incarnation is a voluntary appearance, not a birth compelled by karma. (2) 'Anādim' — without beginning: prior to time, the uncaused ground of all causation. (3) 'Lokamahīśvaram' — the great sovereign Lord of all worlds: not one divinity among many but the ultimate sovereign of all planes of existence. 'Asaṃmūḍhaḥ' — undeluded: this knowledge itself is liberation-in-progress. 'Sarva-pāpaiḥ pramucyate' — freed from all sins: sin is the fruit of ignorance; ignorance at the root is the misidentification of the Infinite with the finite. When the root-ignorance is dissolved by this supreme knowledge, the sins that grew from it fall away like leaves from a cut tree.

10:4.text: Intelligence, knowledge, freedom from delusion, patience, truth, self-restraint of the senses, calmness of mind, pleasure, pain, birth, death, fear, and fearlessness —
10:4.transliteration:
buddhir jñānam asammohaḥ kṣamā satyaṃ damaḥ śamaḥ |
sukhaṃ duḥkhaṃ bhavo 'bhāvo bhayaṃ cābhayam eva ca ||4||
10:4.devanagari:
बुद्धिर्ज्ञानमसम्मोहः क्षमा सत्यं दमः शमः ।
सुखं दुःखं भवोऽभावो भयं चाभयमेव च ॥ ४ ॥
10:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:4.explanation: This verse and the next form a single declaration (the predicate comes in verse 5). Here thirteen qualities of being are listed: intelligence (buddhi), knowledge (jñāna), freedom from delusion (assammoha), patience (kṣamā), truthfulness (satya), sense-restraint (dama), mental calmness (śama), pleasure (sukha), pain (duḥkha), birth/arising (bhava), dissolution/death (abhāva), fear (bhaya), and fearlessness (abhaya). The crucial philosophical point: both poles of every pair — pleasure and pain, birth and death, fear and fearlessness — are equally from the Divine. The Infinite is not the source of only the noble, the pleasant, or the elevated; it is the source and substratum of the entire range of existence. This dismantles any theology that divides the universe into 'God's territory' and 'evil's territory.'

10:5.text: Non-violence, equanimity, contentment, austerity, charity, fame, and infamy — these diverse states of beings all arise from Me alone.
10:5.transliteration:
ahiṃsā samatā tuṣṭis tapo dānaṃ yaśo 'yaśaḥ |
bhavanti bhāvā bhūtānāṃ matta eva pṛthagvidhāḥ ||5||
10:5.devanagari:
अहिंसा समता तुष्टिस्तपो दानं यशोऽयशः ।
भवन्ति भावा भूतानां मत्त एव पृथग्विधाः ॥ ५ ॥
10:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:5.explanation: 'Bhavanti bhāvā bhūtānāṃ matta eva pṛthagvidhāḥ' — all the multifarious states of beings arise from Me alone. Seven more qualities complete the list: non-violence (ahiṃsā — considered the highest dharma in Indian ethics), equanimity (samatā), contentment (tuṣṭi), austerity (tapas), charity (dāna), fame (yaśas), and infamy (ayaśas). Once again the polarities are included: fame AND infamy both arise from the Divine. The metaphysical implication: God is not merely the patron of virtue; God is the substratum of ALL states of being without exception. This is the Vedantic principle of absolute non-duality — there is nowhere the Infinite is not. The sage who sees this clearly is not disturbed by fame or infamy, for both are seen as modifications of the single, ever-present Source.

10:6.text: The seven great sages of old, and the four (Sanaka and his brothers) before them, and the Manus — born from My mind, imbued with My power — from them come all the creatures of the world.
10:6.transliteration:
maharṣayaḥ sapta pūrve catvāro manavas tathā |
madbhāvā mānasā jātā yeṣāṃ loka imāḥ prajāḥ ||6||
10:6.devanagari:
महर्षयः सप्त पूर्वे चत्वारो मनवस्तथा ।
मद्भावा मानसा जाता येषां लोक इमाः प्रजाः ॥ ६ ॥
10:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:6.explanation: 'Maharṣayaḥ sapta' — the Seven Great Sages (Saptarṣis): Marīci, Atri, Aṅgiras, Pulaha, Kratu, Pulastya, Vasiṣṭha — the Vedic seers whose lineages encompass all Vedic knowledge. 'Catvāraḥ pūrve' — the four before even them: Sanaka, Sanandana, Sanātana, Sanatkumāra — Brahmā's first mind-born sons, eternal celibates who carry the seed of liberation-wisdom. 'Manavaḥ' — the fourteen Manus who preside over cosmic time-cycles (manvantaras), the progenitors of humanity. 'Madbhāvā mānasā jātāḥ' — born from My being, from My mind-will alone: not born of physical union but of divine creative intent. These foundational beings carry Krishna's power, and through them all of creation is seeded. The entire lineage of cosmic knowledge traces back to a single Source.

10:7.text: He who knows, in truth, this My divine glory (vibhūti) and power (yoga) — he becomes united with Me through unwavering yoga. Of this there is no doubt.
10:7.transliteration:
etāṃ vibhūtiṃ yogaṃ ca mama yo vetti tattvataḥ |
so 'vikampena yogena yujyate nātra saṃśayaḥ ||7||
10:7.devanagari:
एतां विभूतिं योगं च मम यो वेत्ति तत्त्वतः ।
सोऽविकम्पेन योगेन युज्यते नात्र संशयः ॥ ७ ॥
10:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:7.explanation: 'Vibhūtim' — divine glory, pervasion, the manner in which the Infinite pours into and expresses through all finite forms. 'Yogam ca' — the divine power, the sovereign capacity by which all this is accomplished. 'Tattvataḥ' — in truth, in its essential reality — not merely intellectual acquaintance but genuine, penetrating recognition. 'Avikampena yogena yujyate' — he becomes established in unwavering yoga: a state of inner connection with the Divine that does not tremble before the storms of circumstance. 'Nātra saṃśayaḥ' — there is no doubt about this. This verse is the thesis of the entire chapter: understanding the divine vibhūtis is not mere theological knowledge — it is itself the path to unshakeable inner stability.

10:8.text: I am the source of all; everything proceeds from Me. Knowing this, the wise, endowed with true feeling, worship Me.
10:8.transliteration:
ahaṃ sarvasya prabhavo mattaḥ sarvaṃ pravartate |
iti matvā bhajante māṃ budhā bhāvasamanvitāḥ ||8||
10:8.devanagari:
अहं सर्वस्य प्रभवो मत्तः सर्वं प्रवर्तते ।
इति मत्वा भजन्ते मां बुधा भावसमन्विताः ॥ ८ ॥
10:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:8.explanation: 'Ahaṃ sarvasya prabhavaḥ' — one of the Gita's supreme cosmological declarations: I am the origin, the arising-ground, the prabhava (that from which all proceeds) of everything without exception. 'Mattaḥ sarvaṃ pravartate' — from Me alone everything proceeds and operates. 'Iti matvā' — knowing this: the word 'matvā' implies genuine understanding, not mere repetition of a doctrine. 'Budhāḥ bhāvasamanvitāḥ' — the wise who are endowed with bhāva — profound inner feeling, devotional resonance: this is the union of jñāna and bhakti. They do not hold this truth abstractly in the intellect; they feel its weight and beauty in the heart. 'Bhajante māṃ' — they worship Me: when understanding reaches its depth, it naturally flowers into devotion. Knowledge and love are not opposites; love is knowledge fully ripened.

10:9.text: With their minds fixed on Me, with their life-breath offered to Me, enlightening each other, and always speaking of Me, they are ever contented and delighted.
10:9.transliteration:
maccittā madgataprāṇā bodhayantaḥ parasparam |
kathayantaś ca māṃ nityaṃ tuṣyanti ca ramanti ca ||9||
10:9.devanagari:
मच्चित्ता मद्गतप्राणा बोधयन्तः परस्परम् ।
कथयन्तश्च मां नित्यं तुष्यन्ति च रमन्ति च ॥ ९ ॥
10:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:9.explanation: A luminous portrait of the community of devotees. Four interlocking qualities: (1) 'Maccittāḥ' — minds fixed in Me: the primary faculty of knowing wholly oriented toward the Divine; (2) 'Madgataprāṇāḥ' — life-breath given to Me: the prāṇa, the vital energy, consecrated. Not a poetic flourish but a description of one in whom every breath is devotional; (3) 'Bodhayantaḥ parasparam' — enlightening one another: the mutual elevation of satsaṅga (sacred community), where friends become teachers and students to each other; (4) 'Kathayantaś ca māṃ nityam' — ever speaking of Me: discourses, songs, stories, inquiry — the continuous flow of divine conversation. The fruit: 'Tuṣyanti' — they are satisfied, inwardly full; 'Ramanti' — they delight, they rejoice. A joy not contingent on external conditions but arising from the Source within.

10:10.text: To those who are ever devoted, who worship Me with love — I give that yoga of intelligence (buddhi-yoga) by which they come to Me.
10:10.transliteration:
teṣāṃ satatayuktānāṃ bhajatāṃ prītipūrvakam |
dadāmi buddhiyogaṃ taṃ yena mām upayānti te ||10||
10:10.devanagari:
तेषां सततयुक्तानां भजतां प्रीतिपूर्वकम् ।
ददामि बुद्धियोगं तं येन मामुपयान्ति ते ॥ १० ॥
10:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:10.explanation: 'Satatayuktānām' — ever-yoked, uninterruptedly connected. 'Prītipūrvakam' — with love: the distinction between a bhakta and a mere ritualist is exactly this word — the quality of love suffusing the practice. 'Dadāmi buddhiyogam' — I give them the yoga of intelligence: not ordinary intellectual cleverness but a divinely-granted discriminative wisdom (prajñā) — the inner clarity that sees through the veil of māyā, the subtle understanding that guides directly toward liberation. 'Yena mām upayānti' — by which they come to Me: this buddhi-yoga is the divine gift that completes the devotee's journey. This verse reveals the doctrine of divine grace completing what sincere effort begins: the devotee strives with love; God grants the final insight. These together produce liberation.

10:11.text: Out of compassion for them alone, dwelling within their very being, I destroy the darkness born of ignorance with the brilliant lamp of knowledge.
10:11.transliteration:
teṣām evānukampārtham aham ajñānajaṃ tamaḥ |
nāśayāmy ātmabhāvastho jñānadīpena bhāsvatā ||11||
10:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:11.explanation: One of the most tenderly compassionate verses in the Gita. 'Anukampārtham' — out of compassion alone: no agenda, no transaction, only the overflow of divine love. 'Ātmabhāvasthaḥ' — dwelling within their own being: God does not come from outside; He is already within as the ātman — the innermost presence. The guru is external, but the illumination happens internally, from the ground of one's own deepest nature. 'Ajñānajaṃ tamaḥ' — the darkness born of ignorance: ignorance is not a substance but an absence — like darkness, which is merely the absence of light. 'Jñānadīpena bhāsvatā' — with the brilliant, shining lamp of knowledge. One lamp can light a million other lamps without diminishing. This is the divine act: God-within illuminating the darkness of the God-forgetting mind from the inside out. The highest grace.

10:12.text: Arjuna said: You are the supreme Brahman, the supreme abode (dhāma), the supreme purifier. You are the eternal, divine Puruṣa, the primordial God (ādideva), unborn and all-pervading (vibhu).
10:12.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
paraṃ brahma paraṃ dhāma pavitraṃ paramaṃ bhavān |
puruṣaṃ śāśvataṃ divyam ādidevaṃ ajaṃ vibhum ||12||
10:12.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
परं ब्रह्म परं धाम पवित्रं परमं भवान् ।
पुरुषं शाश्वतं दिव्यमादिदेवमजं विभुम् ॥ १२ ॥
10:12.speaker: Arjuna
10:12.explanation: Arjuna now answers Krishna's teaching not with further questions but with recognition — a hymn of realized understanding. Eight great designations: (1) 'Paraṃ Brahma' — the supreme Brahman, the ultimate Reality of the Upaniṣads; (2) 'Paraṃ dhāma' — the supreme abode, the final resting place of all; (3) 'Paramaṃ pavitram' — the greatest purifier; (4) 'Śāśvatam' — the Eternal; (5) 'Divyam' — the transcendent, of a nature beyond ordinary being; (6) 'Ādidevaṃ' — the original God, from whom all divinities descend; (7) 'Ajam' — unborn; (8) 'Vibhum' — all-pervading, unlimited. This is not flattery — it is recognition. Arjuna, having received the teachings of Chapters 7–10, now articulates what he has come to understand. His words echo the Upaniṣadic formulations, showing that the Gita's teaching is continuous with the highest Vedic wisdom.

10:13.text: All the sages declare this of You — the divine sage Nārada, Asita, Devala, and Vyāsa — and You Yourself tell me so.
10:13.transliteration:
āhus tvām ṛṣayaḥ sarve devarṣir nāradas tathā |
asito devalo vyāsaḥ svayaṃ caiva bravīṣi me ||13||
10:13.devanagari:
आहुस्त्वामृषयः सर्वे देवर्षिर्नारदस्तथा ।
असितो देवलो व्यासः स्वयं चैव ब्रवीषि मे ॥ १३ ॥
10:13.speaker: Arjuna
10:13.explanation: Arjuna grounds his recognition in three converging authorities. (1) 'Sarve ṛṣayaḥ' — all the sages, the collective Vedic testimony. (2) Named specifically: Nārada (the devarṣi, sage of the gods, great devotee and cosmic musician), Asita (a Vedic sage), Devala (a dharmaśāstra authority), and Vyāsa (the greatest — compiler of the Vedas, author of the Mahābhārata and Purāṇas, the very sage who preserves this text). (3) 'Svayaṃ ca bravīṣi' — and You Yourself are saying so to me. Three modes of pramāṇa (valid knowledge): scriptural authority (āgama), trustworthy persons (āpta-vacana), and direct testimony of the source (svayam). When all three converge, doubt dissolves. Arjuna is not receiving this on faith alone — the evidence is overwhelming and multi-directional.

10:14.text: All this that You tell me, O Keśava, I hold to be true. O Lord (Bhagavan), neither the gods nor the demons know Your true manifestation.
10:14.transliteration:
sarvam etad ṛtaṃ manye yan māṃ vadasi keśava |
na hi te bhagavan vyaktiṃ vidur devā na dānavāḥ ||14||
10:14.devanagari:
सर्वमेतदृतं मन्ये यन्मां वदसि केशव ।
न हि ते भगवन्व्यक्तिं विदुर्देवा न दानवाः ॥ १४ ॥
10:14.speaker: Arjuna
10:14.explanation: 'Sarvam etad ṛtam manye' — all this I hold to be true (ṛtam — the Vedic word for ultimate truth, the cosmic law). Arjuna's acceptance is not intellectual surrender but recognition resonating from within. 'Vyaktim' — Your true manifestation, Your real nature. 'Na devā na dānavāḥ' — neither gods nor demons know it: Arjuna echoes precisely what Krishna said in verse 2, showing that the teaching has not merely been heard but absorbed and owned. The address shifts: in the Gita's opening, Arjuna called Krishna 'Mādhava,' 'Janārdana,' 'friend'; now he says 'Bhagavan' — the one endowed with all six divine qualities (aiśvarya, dharma, yaśas, śrī, jñāna, vairāgya). Recognition has ripened into reverence.

10:15.text: You alone know Yourself by Yourself, O Puruṣottama, O source of beings (Bhūtabhāvana), O Lord of beings (Bhūteśa), O God of gods (Devadeva), O Lord of the universe (Jagatpate).
10:15.transliteration:
svayam evātmanātmānaṃ vettha tvaṃ puruṣottama |
bhūtabhāvana bhūteśa devadeva jagatpate ||15||
10:15.devanagari:
स्वयमेवात्मनात्मानं वेत्थ त्वं पुरुषोत्तम ।
भूतभावन भूतेश देवदेव जगत्पते ॥ १५ ॥
10:15.speaker: Arjuna
10:15.explanation: 'Svayam evātmanātmānam vettha tvam' — a profound epistemological statement: You alone know Yourself by Yourself. This points to the self-luminous (svaprakāśa) nature of Brahman — It does not need an external witness to know Itself, for It IS knowledge. The knowing subject and the known object are identical in Brahman. No finite being can completely encompass this knowledge from outside — only the Infinite can know Itself as Infinite. Five magnificent addresses complete the verse: Puruṣottama (highest among persons), Bhūtabhāvana (the nourisher of all beings), Bhūteśa (Lord of all beings), Devadeva (God of the gods), Jagatpate (Lord of the universe). Each successive title expands the scope of Krishna's sovereignty. Arjuna is not guessing — he is naming what has been revealed to him.

10:16.text: You alone are worthy of fully declaring Your own divine glories (vibhūtis) — by which manifestations You pervade and stand in these worlds.
10:16.transliteration:
vaktum arhasy aśeṣeṇa divyā hy ātmavibhūtayaḥ |
yābhir vibhūtibhir lokān imāṃs tvaṃ vyāpya tiṣṭhasi ||16||
10:16.devanagari:
वक्तुमर्हस्यशेषेण दिव्या ह्यात्मविभूतयः ।
याभिर्विभूतिभिर्लोकानिमांस्त्वं व्याप्य तिष्ठसि ॥ १६ ॥
10:16.speaker: Arjuna
10:16.explanation: 'Vaktum arhasi' — You alone are able and fit to tell this. No one else can fully describe the divine self-manifestation. 'Aśeṣeṇa' — completely, without remainder: Arjuna asks for the full account. 'Divyā ātmavibhūtayaḥ' — Your own divine glories: the word 'ātma-' personalizes it — these are self-expressions of Your very being, not external attributes. 'Lokān imāṃs tvam vyāpya tiṣṭhasi' — by which You stand pervading these worlds. Arjuna's question is not academic curiosity — knowing the divine's expressions in the world enables the meditator to see God in all things. The vibhūtis serve as meditation objects, lamps through which the divine fire can be recognized everywhere.

10:17.text: O Yogin, O Lord, how may I know You, always meditating? And in which particular forms (bhāvas) are You to be contemplated by me?
10:17.transliteration:
kathaṃ vidyām ahaṃ yogiṃs tvāṃ sadā paricintayan |
keṣu keṣu ca bhāveṣu cintyo 'si bhagavan mayā ||17||
10:17.devanagari:
कथं विद्यामहं योगिंस्त्वां सदा परिचिन्तयन् ।
केषु केषु च भावेषु चिन्त्योऽसि भगवन्मया ॥ १७ ॥
10:17.speaker: Arjuna
10:17.explanation: Two interlocked practical questions from a genuine seeker: (1) 'Kathaṃ vidyām' — how am I to know You? What is the method? (2) 'Keṣu keṣu ca bhāveṣu cintyo 'si' — in which specific forms/manifestations should You be contemplated? This is a deeply practical meditation question. Meditation on the formless Absolute is declared difficult in 12.5; the vibhūtis provide accessible, form-based entry points through which the formless can be approached. Every category of excellence in the world becomes a window into the Divine. 'Yogin' as address — Krishna is the master of yoga, and Arjuna approaches Him precisely as a student seeking a yogic method. The answer beginning in verse 19 is effectively a meditation manual.

10:18.text: Tell me again in full, O Janārdana, of Your yoga-power and divine glories — for in listening to this nectar (amṛtam), I am never satisfied.
10:18.transliteration:
vistareṇātmano yogaṃ vibhūtiṃ ca janārdana |
bhūyaḥ kathaya tṛptir hi śṛṇvato nāsti me 'mṛtam ||18||
10:18.devanagari:
विस्तरेणात्मनो योगं विभूतिं च जनार्दन ।
भूयः कथय तृप्तिर्हि शृण्वतो नास्ति मेऽमृतम् ॥ १८ ॥
10:18.speaker: Arjuna
10:18.explanation: 'Tṛptir hi śṛṇvato nāsti me amṛtam' — 'I am never satisfied listening to Your nectar-words': one of the most beautiful expressions of the devotee's relationship with divine wisdom. The more one drinks of this amṛta, the greater the thirst — because this thirst is not of the ego's craving but of the soul's genuine recognition of its home. Each teaching is a drop; the ocean is infinite. 'Janārdana' — the rescuer and sustainer of beings. 'Bhūyaḥ' — again, once more, even more. 'Vistareṇa' — in full, with complete detail: not summarized but poured out completely. This verse is Arjuna's formal request for the vibhūti-list that follows in verses 19–40 — the great catalogue of divine manifestations that forms the heart of this chapter.

10:19.text: The Blessed Lord said: Very well! I shall tell you of My divine glories, O best of the Kurus — but only the principal ones, for there is no end to My extent.
10:19.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
hanta te kathayiṣyāmi divyā hy ātmavibhūtayaḥ |
prādhānyataḥ kuruśreṣṭha nāsty anto vistarasya me ||19||
10:19.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
हन्त ते कथयिष्यामि दिव्या ह्यात्मविभूतयः ।
प्राधान्यतः कुरुश्रेष्ठ नास्त्यन्तो विस्तरस्य मे ॥ १९ ॥
10:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:19.explanation: 'Hanta' — an exclamation of affectionate consent: 'Very well! Alright! I gladly will.' Krishna is moved by Arjuna's inexhaustible appetite for the divine. 'Prādhānyataḥ' — principally, by way of the most prominent examples: a crucial qualification. The list that follows (verses 20–40) is explicitly a representative selection, not an exhaustive enumeration. 'Nāsty anto vistarasya me' — there is no end to My extent: the Infinite cannot be fully catalogued. This disclaimer itself is the deepest teaching of the chapter — whatever examples follow are pointers, not boundaries. The universe is infinite in its divine expressions; the list is a garland of illustrations, not a fence around the Infinite.

10:20.text: O Guḍākeśa (conqueror of sleep), I am the Self (ātman) dwelling in the hearts of all beings. I am the beginning, the middle, and the end of all beings.
10:20.transliteration:
aham ātmā guḍākeśa sarvabhūtāśayasthitaḥ |
aham ādiś ca madhyaṃ ca bhūtānām anta eva ca ||20||
10:20.devanagari:
अहमात्मा गुडाकेश सर्वभूताशयस्थितः ।
अहमादिश्च मध्यं च भूतानामन्त एव च ॥ २० ॥
10:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:20.explanation: The first and foundational vibhūti — the root from which all others grow. 'Aham ātmā' — I am the Ātman: not a distant God in a remote heaven, not a power acting from outside, but the innermost Consciousness present at the core of every single being. 'Sarvabhūtāśayasthitaḥ' — dwelling in the āśaya (the heart-space, the innermost chamber) of all creatures. 'Ādiś ca madhyaṃ ca anta eva ca' — the beginning (the cause of arising), the middle (the sustaining principle during existence), and the end (the ground into which everything resolves). God is not only at the start and finish of creation — He is every moment of it. 'Guḍākeśa' — conqueror of sleep (guḍāka = sleep; this address honors Arjuna as one who has overcome the sleep of delusion) — fittingly addressed as the highest secret is revealed.

10:21.text: Among the Ādityas I am Viṣṇu; among lights, the radiant sun; among the Maruts I am Marīci; among the stars I am the moon.
10:21.transliteration:
ādityānām ahaṃ viṣṇur jyotiṣāṃ ravir aṃśumān |
marīcir marutām asmi nakṣatrāṇām ahaṃ śaśī ||21||
10:21.devanagari:
आदित्यानामहं विष्णुर्ज्योतिषां रविरंशुमान् ।
मरीचिर्मरुतामस्मि नक्षत्राणामहं शशी ॥ २१ ॥
10:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:21.explanation: The vibhūti-list begins with the celestial and divine orders: (1) Among the twelve Ādityas (solar deities), I am Viṣṇu — the preeminent of this cosmic group, the all-pervading one; (2) 'Jyotiṣāṃ ravir aṃśumān' — among all luminous things I am the radiant sun: the greatest light in experience, giver of all life on earth; (3) Among the forty-nine Maruts (wind gods), I am Marīci — the most brilliant, associated with the principle of light within the air; (4) Among the twenty-seven nakṣatras (lunar mansions and stars), I am the moon — the most brilliant, most influential on life, tides, and mind. The principle of selection: in every category, the supreme expression, the most luminous or powerful instance, is the divine presence.

10:22.text: Among the Vedas I am the Sāmaveda; among the gods I am Vāsava (Indra); among the senses I am the mind; and among beings I am consciousness (cetanā).
10:22.transliteration:
vedānāṃ sāmavedo 'smi devānām asmi vāsavaḥ |
indriyāṇāṃ manaś cāsmi bhūtānām asmi cetanā ||22||
10:22.devanagari:
वेदानां सामवेदोऽस्मि देवानामस्मि वासवः ।
इन्द्रियाणां मनश्चास्मि भूतानामस्मि चेतना ॥ २२ ॥
10:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:22.explanation: Four more categories: (1) 'Vedānāṃ sāmavedo 'smi' — among the four Vedas, the Sāmaveda: the Sāmaveda is set to music, its verses chanted with melodic precision. Music is the most immediate pathway to the transcendent — it bypasses argument and addresses the soul directly. (2) 'Devānām vāsavaḥ' — among the gods, Indra (Vāsava): the king of the thirty-three Vedic devas, lord of rain, thunder, and cosmic vitality. (3) 'Indriyāṇāṃ manaḥ' — among the senses, the mind: the mind is not merely one sense but the master of the senses — it is through the mind that all sensory experience is processed and either liberated or bound. (4) 'Bhūtānām cetanā' — among beings, consciousness: not a physical attribute but the essential light by which anything is known at all. Consciousness is the most fundamental of all divine expressions in the living world.

10:23.text: Among the Rudras I am Śaṅkara (Śiva); among the Yakṣas and Rākṣasas I am Kubera (Vitteśa, lord of wealth); among the Vasus I am Pāvaka (fire); and among mountains I am Meru.
10:23.transliteration:
rudrāṇāṃ śaṅkaraś cāsmi vitteśo yakṣarakṣasām |
vasūnāṃ pāvakaś cāsmi meruḥ śikhariṇām aham ||23||
10:23.devanagari:
रुद्राणां शङ्करश्चास्मि वित्तेशो यक्षरक्षसाम् ।
वसूनां पावकश्चास्मि मेरुः शिखरिणामहम् ॥ २३ ॥
10:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:23.explanation: Four more supreme expressions: (1) 'Rudrāṇāṃ Śaṅkaraḥ' — among the eleven Rudras, Śiva (Śaṅkara — the auspicious one): the sovereign of dissolution and transformation, destroyer of ego, lord of yoga, the benevolent great god. (2) 'Vitteśo yakṣarakṣasām' — among the Yakṣas and Rākṣasas, Kubera (lord of wealth): the keeper of cosmic treasure, guardian of the north direction. (3) 'Vasūnāṃ pāvakaḥ' — among the eight Vasus, Agni (pāvaka = fire, the purifier): fire that carries sacrificial offerings to the gods, the cosmic mediator. (4) 'Meruḥ śikhariṇām' — among mountains, Meru: the mythic cosmic mountain at the center of the universe, the pivot around which the cosmos revolves, made of gold, home of the gods.

10:24.text: O Pārtha, know Me as Bṛhaspati, the chief among priests; among generals I am Skanda; among bodies of water I am the ocean.
10:24.transliteration:
purodhasāṃ ca mukhyaṃ māṃ viddhi pārtha bṛhaspatim |
senānīnām ahaṃ skandaḥ sarasām asmi sāgaraḥ ||24||
10:24.devanagari:
पुरोधसां च मुख्यं मां विद्धि पार्थ बृहस्पतिम् ।
सेनानीनामहं स्कन्दः सरसामस्मि सागरः ॥ २४ ॥
10:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:24.explanation: Three more sovereign expressions: (1) 'Purodhasāṃ mukhyam bṛhaspatim' — among household priests and chief priests, Bṛhaspati: the preceptor of the gods, lord of sacred speech and wisdom, the ultimate guardian of sacrificial knowledge. His authority governs the entire Vedic ritual order. (2) 'Senānīnāṃ skandaḥ' — among military commanders, Skanda (Kārttikeya): the six-faced general of the celestial armies, son of Śiva, who destroyed the demon Tāraka and restored cosmic order — representing perfected warrior energy. (3) 'Sarasām sāgaraḥ' — among bodies of water, the ocean: all rivers, lakes, and streams receive their water ultimately from and return to the ocean. The ocean's unfathomable depth, its inexhaustible vastness, its stillness beneath all surface storms — all speak of Brahman.

10:25.text: Among the great sages I am Bhṛgu; among words I am the single syllable Oṃ; among sacrifices I am the sacrifice of silent repetition (japa-yajña); and among immovable things I am the Himālaya.
10:25.transliteration:
maharṣīṇāṃ bhṛgur ahaṃ girām asmy ekam akṣaram |
yajñānāṃ japayajño 'smi sthāvarāṇāṃ himālayaḥ ||25||
10:25.devanagari:
महर्षीणां भृगुरहं गिरामस्म्येकमक्षरम् ।
यज्ञानां जपयज्ञोऽस्मि स्थावराणां हिमालयः ॥ २५ ॥
10:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:25.explanation: Four more vibhūtis of great richness: (1) 'Maharṣīṇāṃ Bhṛguḥ' — among the great sages, Bhṛgu: one of the most ancient, powerful, and fiery Vedic seers — author of the Bhṛgu-saṃhitā and a figure of immense spiritual authority; (2) 'Girām ekam akṣaram' — among all speech and words, the single syllable Oṃ: the Praṇava, primordial vibration, the seed-sound from which all language and all existence arises. The most compressed, most pregnant expression of the Infinite; (3) 'Yajñānāṃ japa-yajñaḥ' — among sacrifices, the sacrifice of japa (silent or whispered repetition of the divine name or mantra): superior to all external rituals requiring materials, fire, and priests, japa-yajña requires only awareness and devotion — it is the inner sacrifice accessible always; (4) 'Sthāvarāṇāṃ Himālayaḥ' — among immovable things, the Himālaya: the abode of gods and sages, the source of sacred rivers, the physical embodiment of permanence and spiritual aspiration.

10:26.text: Among all trees I am the aśvattha (peepal/sacred fig); among the divine sages I am Nārada; among the Gandharvas I am Citraratha; among the Siddhas I am the sage Kapila.
10:26.transliteration:
aśvatthaḥ sarvavṛkṣāṇāṃ devarṣīṇāṃ ca nāradaḥ |
gandharvaṇāṃ citrarathaḥ siddhānāṃ kapilo muniḥ ||26||
10:26.devanagari:
अश्वत्थः सर्ववृक्षाणां देवर्षीणां च नारदः ।
गन्धर्वाणां चित्ररथः सिद्धानां कपिलो मुनिः ॥ २६ ॥
10:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:26.explanation: Four more: (1) 'Aśvatthaḥ sarvavṛkṣāṇāṃ' — among all trees, the aśvattha (sacred peepal, Ficus religiosa): the most sacred tree in Indian tradition, associated with Viṣṇu and Brahmā, the tree under which the Buddha attained enlightenment, and the very cosmic-tree symbol of Chapter 15; (2) 'Devarṣīṇāṃ Nāradaḥ' — among divine sages, Nārada: the eternal devotee, cosmic messenger, author of the Nārada Bhakti Sūtras, who travels the three worlds proclaiming the glory of the Divine; (3) 'Gandharvaṇāṃ Citrarathaḥ' — among the Gandharvas (celestial musicians), Citraratha: the king and most accomplished of the divine musicians; (4) 'Siddhānāṃ Kapilo muniḥ' — among the Siddhas (perfected beings), the sage Kapila: founder of the Sāṃkhya philosophical system, revered as a divine incarnation in the Bhāgavata.

10:27.text: Among horses know Me as Uccaiḥśravas, born of the nectar-churning; among the lordly elephants, Airāvata; and among men, the king.
10:27.transliteration:
uccaiḥśravasam aśvānāṃ viddhi mām amṛtodbhavam |
airāvataṃ gajendrāṇāṃ narāṇāṃ ca narādhipam ||27||
10:27.devanagari:
उच्चैःश्रवसमश्वानां विद्धि माममृतोद्भवम् ।
ऐरावतं गजेन्द्राणां नराणां च नराधिपम् ॥ २७ ॥
10:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:27.explanation: Three more: (1) 'Uccaiḥśravasam aśvānāṃ amṛtodbhavam' — among horses, Uccaiḥśravas: the magnificent seven-headed white horse that arose from the churning of the cosmic ocean (samudra-manthana), Indra's divine mount, 'born of nectar' — supreme symbol of speed, power, and divine origin; (2) 'Airāvataṃ gajendrāṇāṃ' — among lordly elephants, Airāvata: the snow-white celestial elephant, also born of the ocean-churning, Indra's vehicle, emblem of cosmic sovereignty; (3) 'Narāṇāṃ ca narādhipam' — among men, the king: the one who embodies, protects, and upholds the dharmic order of society — the highest among men is the one who carries the greatest responsibility for others' welfare.

10:28.text: Among weapons I am the thunderbolt (vajra); among cows, the wish-fulfilling cow (Kāmadhuk); among progenitors I am Kandarpa (Kāmadeva, god of love); among serpents I am Vāsuki.
10:28.transliteration:
āyudhānām ahaṃ vajraṃ dhenūnām asmi kāmadhuk |
prajanaś cāsmi kandarpaḥ sarpāṇām asmi vāsukiḥ ||28||
10:28.devanagari:
आयुधानामहं वज्रं धेनूनामस्मि कामधुक् ।
प्रजनश्चास्मि कन्दर्पः सर्पाणामस्मि वासुकिः ॥ २८ ॥
10:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:28.explanation: Four more spanning weapons, abundance, life-force, and the serpentine: (1) 'Āyudhānāṃ vajram' — among all weapons, the vajra: Indra's indestructible thunderbolt, forged from the bones of the sage Dadhīci who voluntarily sacrificed himself — the supreme weapon of cosmic order; (2) 'Dhenūnāṃ kāmadhuk' — among cows, Kāmadhenu (the wish-fulfilling cow): the divine cow that arose from the ocean-churning, granting every wish, embodiment of infinite abundance; (3) 'Prajanaḥ Kandarpaḥ' — among progenitors, Kāmadeva: the god of love and creative desire. Significantly, Krishna claims the principle of dharmic desire, life-creative love, as His vibhūti — desire that creates and sustains life is divine; (4) 'Sarpāṇāṃ Vāsukiḥ' — among serpents, Vāsuki: the king of the nāgas, the serpent used as the churning rope in the samudra-manthana.

10:29.text: Among the Nāgas I am Ananta (Śeṣa); among aquatic beings I am Varuṇa; among the ancestors I am Aryamā; among those who maintain order I am Yama.
10:29.transliteration:
anantaś cāsmi nāgānāṃ varuṇo yādasām aham |
pitṝṇām aryamā cāsmi yamaḥ saṃyamatām aham ||29||
10:29.devanagari:
अनन्तश्चास्मि नागानां वरुणो यादसामहम् ।
पितॄणामर्यमा चास्मि यमः संयमतामहम् ॥ २९ ॥
10:29.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:29.explanation: Four more traversing cosmic depth, water, ancestral realms, and justice: (1) 'Nāgānāṃ Anantaḥ' — among the divine serpents (nāgas), Ananta (Śeṣa): the great infinite cosmic serpent on whom Viṣṇu reclines in the waters of dissolution, who bears the weight of all worlds — 'Ananta' meaning the Endless; (2) 'Yādasām Varuṇaḥ' — among aquatic beings, Varuṇa: one of the oldest Vedic gods, lord of the cosmic ocean, guardian of ṛta (the cosmic moral order), associated with divine justice; (3) 'Pitṝṇām aryamā' — among the ancestors (pitṛs), Aryamā: the chief of the pitṛs who presides over the ancestral realm; (4) 'Yamaḥ saṃyamatāṃ' — among those who maintain restraint and order, Yama: the god of death and dharmic justice — death itself is a divine administration ensuring cosmic accountability.
