10:30.text: Among the Daityas I am Prahlāda; among reckoners I am Time (Kāla); among beasts I am the lion (king of animals); and among birds I am Vainateyā (Garuḍa).
10:30.transliteration:
prahlādaś cāsmi daityānāṃ kālaḥ kalayatām aham |
mṛgāṇāṃ ca mṛgendro 'haṃ vainateyaś ca pakṣiṇām ||30||
10:30.devanagari:
प्रह्लादश्चास्मि दैत्यानां कालः कलयतामहम् ।
मृगाणां च मृगेन्द्रोऽहं वैनतेयश्च पक्षिणाम् ॥ ३० ॥
10:30.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:30.explanation: Four vibhūtis, each with layered significance. Prahlāda among the Daityas (anti-gods): born into a lineage of divine opposition, yet the most supremely devoted soul in all of scripture — a devotee who maintained unbroken love for Viṣṇu against the most violent resistance, including his own father Hiraṇyakaśipu. He is the Divine's manifestation among the most unlikely: the grace of God that cannot be suppressed even by heredity and environment. 'Kālaḥ kalayatām' — Time, among all that reckons and measures: the supreme accountant that counts down every existence, the great force before which all else is humbled. Among beasts, the lion (mṛgendra): sovereign strength, fearlessness, the apex predator who is himself afraid of nothing. Among birds, Garuḍa (Vainateyā, son of Vinatā): the celestial eagle, vehicle of Viṣṇu, destroyer of serpents — swift, luminous, divine.

10:31.text: Among purifiers I am the wind (Pavana); among weapon-bearers I am Rāma; among fish I am the Makara (crocodile/sea-monster); and among rivers I am the Jāhnavī (Gaṅgā).
10:31.transliteration:
pavanaḥ pavatām asmi rāmaḥ śastrabhṛtām aham |
jhaṣāṇāṃ makaraś cāsmi srotasām asmi jāhnavī ||31||
10:31.devanagari:
पवनः पवतामस्मि रामः शस्त्रभृतामहम् ।
झषाणां मकरश्चास्मि स्रोतसामस्मि जाह्नवी ॥ ३१ ॥
10:31.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:31.explanation: 'Pavanaḥ pavatām' — the wind, foremost among all that purifies. 'Pavana' (from the root 'to purify') is the universal breath — it sustains all life, moves through everything, kindles fire, keeps water from stagnation. As prāṇa (life-force), it is the inner wind that purifies the subtle body. 'Rāmaḥ śastrabhṛtām' — Rāma of Ayodhyā, foremost among all who bear weapons. Rāma is not merely the greatest warrior but the embodiment of dharma-in-action: the king who sacrifices personal happiness for righteous order, the archer whose bow is an instrument of cosmic justice. 'Makaraḥ' — the makara, a mythic sea-creature (sometimes identified as a crocodilian or a hybrid divine beast) — the greatest of aquatic beings, emblem of Kāma-deva's banner and the vehicle of Varuṇa; 'Jāhnavī' — the Gaṅgā (daughter of sage Jahnu), the holiest of all rivers, descended from heaven, liberator of the dead — the river that personifies purification and mokṣa.

10:32.text: O Arjuna, I am the beginning, the middle, and also the end of all creations. Among all knowledge-systems I am the science of the Self (adhyātma-vidyā); and among those who reason and debate, I am the truth-seeking argument (vāda).
10:32.transliteration:
sargāṇām ādir antaś ca madhyaṃ caivāham arjuna |
adhyātmavidyā vidyānāṃ vādaḥ pravadatām aham ||32||
10:32.devanagari:
सर्गाणामादिरन्तश्च मध्यं चैवाहमर्जुन ।
अध्यात्मविद्या विद्यानां वादः प्रवदतामहम् ॥ ३२ ॥
10:32.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:32.explanation: 'Sargāṇām ādir antaś ca madhyaṃ ca' — in every act of creation, I am the beginning (the impulse that originates), the middle (the sustaining process), and the end (the resolution into which it subsides). This is not a serial statement but a simultaneous one: Brahman pervades the entire arc of every created thing from origin through existence to dissolution. 'Adhyātmavidyā vidyānām' — among all branches of knowledge (sciences, arts, philosophies), the science of the Self is supreme: all other knowledge examines the known object; adhyātma-vidyā turns the light of inquiry upon the knower itself — the only knowledge that, when fully realized, liberates. 'Vādaḥ pravadatām' — among speakers and debaters, I am vāda: the Nyāya school of Indian philosophy distinguished three types of argument — vāda (truth-seeking dialogue), jalpa (argumentative winning at all costs), and vitaṇḍā (mere refutation without a position). Only vāda, which seeks truth, is divine.

10:33.text: Among letters I am the letter 'A'; among grammatical compounds I am the dvandva (copulative compound). I am imperishable Time itself, and I am the Creator facing in all directions (viśvatomukha).
10:33.transliteration:
akṣarāṇām akāro 'smi dvandvaḥ sāmāsikasya ca |
aham evākṣayaḥ kālo dhātāhaṃ viśvatomukhaḥ ||33||
10:33.devanagari:
अक्षराणामकारोऽस्मि द्वन्द्वः सामासिकस्य च ।
अहमेवाक्षयः कालो धाताहं विश्वतोमुखः ॥ ३३ ॥
10:33.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:33.explanation: 'Akāraḥ akṣarāṇām' — the letter 'A' (akāra), the first and foundational phoneme of the Sanskrit alphabet. In the Upaniṣads, 'a' is identified with Brahman and is the first element of Oṃ (A-U-M). Every word of Sanskrit — and thus every mantra, every Vedic verse — begins in this primordial sound; it is the mouth from which all language emerges. 'Dvandvaḥ sāmāsikasya' — of all Sanskrit compound formations (samāsas), I am the dvandva (copulative compound): in a dvandva, both members are equal and coordinate — neither subordinates the other (e.g., Rāma-Lakṣmaṇa, Śiva-Śakti). It represents the unity of paired principles. 'Akṣayaḥ kālaḥ' — imperishable Time: not time as the destroyer (mentioned in verse 30), but as the infinite, undecaying substratum. 'Dhātā viśvatomukhaḥ' — the cosmic Creator (Brahmā in one reading, Brahman in the Vedantic reading) who faces all directions simultaneously — the omnidirectional source of all creation.

10:34.text: I am death that seizes all, and I am the origin of those yet to be born. Among the feminine virtues, I am Fame (Kīrti), Prosperity (Śrī), Speech (Vāk), Memory (Smṛti), Intelligence (Medhā), Steadfastness (Dhṛti), and Forbearance (Kṣamā).
10:34.transliteration:
mṛtyuḥ sarvaharaś cāham udbhavaś ca bhaviṣyatām |
kīrtiḥ śrīr vāk ca nārīṇāṃ smṛtir medhā dhṛtiḥ kṣamā ||34||
10:34.devanagari:
मृत्युः सर्वहरश्चाहमुद्भवश्च भविष्यताम् ।
कीर्तिः श्रीर्वाक्च नारीणां स्मृतिर्मेधा धृतिः क्षमा ॥ ३४ ॥
10:34.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:34.explanation: 'Mṛtyuḥ sarvaharaḥ' — death that takes all: the great leveler, the force before which no created thing, however mighty, retains its form. And yet Krishna pairs it immediately with its opposite: 'udbhavaś ca bhaviṣyatām' — the source of origination for all yet to come. Death and birth are two faces of the same divine process; what appears as termination from one angle is inception from another. The Divine is simultaneously the end and the beginning of every arc of existence. Then seven feminine principles (listed in the feminine grammatical gender, as divine śaktis): Kīrti — renown, the luminous reputation that outlasts the body; Śrī — sacred prosperity, Lakṣmī, the grace of flourishing; Vāk — the power of speech, Sarasvatī, the Goddess of articulate knowledge; Smṛti — the faculty of memory, without which learning is impossible; Medhā — retention and depth of intellect; Dhṛti — steadfastness, the courage to hold one's course; Kṣamā — forgiveness, the great spiritual strength of not being diminished by the wrongs of others.

10:35.text: Among the Sāma hymns I am the Bṛhatsāma; among poetic meters I am the Gāyatrī; among months I am Mārgaśīrṣa (November–December); and among seasons I am the flower-bearing spring (Kusumākara).
10:35.transliteration:
bṛhatsāma tathā sāmnāṃ gāyatrī chandasām aham |
māsānāṃ mārgaśīrṣo 'ham ṛtūnāṃ kusumākaraḥ ||35||
10:35.devanagari:
बृहत्साम तथा साम्नां गायत्री छन्दसामहम् ।
मासानां मार्गशीर्षोऽहमृतूनां कुसुमाकरः ॥ ३५ ॥
10:35.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:35.explanation: 'Bṛhatsāma' — the Bṛhat (great/vast) Sāma, the most exalted of all the Sāmaveda chants; sung at the morning sacrifice, its resonance was considered the highest vibrational offering in Vedic liturgy. 'Gāyatrī chandasām' — among all Vedic meters (chandas), the Gāyatrī (24 syllables, 3 lines of 8) is supreme: it is called 'Veda-Mātā' (Mother of the Vedas); the Gāyatrī Mantra (Ṛg Veda 3.62.10) is the mantra of enlightenment, invoking the solar intelligence (Savitṛ) to illuminate the mind. 'Mārgaśīrṣaḥ māsānām' — the month of Mārgaśīrṣa (roughly November–December in the Indian calendar): traditionally the month of harvest and sacred celebration, the time when the sun enters Sagittarius and the air is clear; later tradition connects this to Kṛṣṇa's own divine playfulness (the Bhāgavata Purāṇa's Gopi Gīta begins in this month). 'Kusumākaraḥ' — Vasanta (spring), the season of blossoming — nature's own celebration of life renewed.

10:36.text: Among the deceitful I am gambling (dyūta); I am the splendor of the splendorous; I am victory; I am resolute effort (vyavasāya); and I am the goodness (sattva) of the good.
10:36.transliteration:
dyūtaṃ chalayatām asmi tejas tejasvinām aham |
jayo 'smi vyavasāyo 'smi sattvaṃ sattvavatām aham ||36||
10:36.devanagari:
द्यूतं छलयतामस्मि तेजस्तेजस्विनामहम् ।
जयोऽस्मि व्यवसायोऽस्मि सत्त्वं सत्त्ववतामहम् ॥ ३६ ॥
10:36.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:36.explanation: 'Dyūtaṃ chalayatām' — among the deceitful, I am gambling. This is one of the most philosophically charged vibhūtis. Like verse 9.19 (where Krishna claims to be both sat and asat), this declares that even the energy within deception — the supreme instance of which is the cosmic game of māyā itself — is divine. The universe's own 'game' (Brahman's cosmic play, līlā) involves a kind of ultimate 'deception' in the sense that māyā conceals the Real beneath appearances. This is not a moral endorsement of gambling but a metaphysical declaration: every manifestation of energy, even when misdirected, draws from the same divine source. 'Tejasvinām tejaḥ' — the splendor within all that shines: the luminous aura of greatness that distinguishes the extraordinary from the ordinary. 'Jayaḥ' — victory, the divine energy of triumph. 'Vyavasāyaḥ' — resolute determination (echoing the 'vyavasāyātmikā buddhi' of 2.41). 'Sattvaṃ sattvavatām' — the purity and luminous goodness in those who are genuinely good.

10:37.text: Among the Vṛṣṇis I am Vāsudeva (Krishna Himself); among the Pāṇḍavas I am Dhanañjaya (Arjuna); among sages I am Vyāsa; and among poets I am Uśanā (Śukrācārya).
10:37.transliteration:
vṛṣṇīnāṃ vāsudevo 'smi pāṇḍavānāṃ dhanañjayaḥ |
munīnām apy ahaṃ vyāsaḥ kavīnām uśanā kaviḥ ||37||
10:37.devanagari:
वृष्णीनां वासुदेवोऽस्मि पाण्डवानां धनञ्जयः ।
मुनीनामप्यहं व्यासः कवीनामुशना कविः ॥ ३७ ॥
10:37.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:37.explanation: The most personal and self-referential vibhūti verse. 'Vāsudevaḥ' — among the Vṛṣṇi clan (the Yādava family), I am Vāsudeva: Krishna explicitly names himself as his own supreme vibhūti — a moment of divine self-transparency, the Infinite acknowledging its own highest expression within the context of this human lineage. 'Dhanañjayaḥ' — among the Pāṇḍavas, I am Arjuna — the very person hearing these words! Krishna declares the student the supreme vibhūti of the Pāṇḍava line: a moment of extraordinary personal affirmation that dissolves the distance between teacher and student. 'Vyāsaḥ munīnām' — among sages, Vyāsa: the author who divided the primordial Vedic wisdom into four, composed the Mahābhārata (within which this Gita lives), and the Purāṇas — the greatest transmitter of spiritual knowledge in human history. 'Uśanā kavīnām' — among poets, Uśanā (Śukrācārya): the preceptor of the asuras, an unmatched master of both poetry and esoteric sciences, whose knowledge extends to the science of reviving the dead (Mṛtasañjīvanī vidyā).

10:38.text: Among those who punish and subdue, I am the Rod of Chastisement (Daṇḍa); among those who seek to win, I am statecraft (Nīti); among secrets I am Silence (Mauna); and I am the knowledge of those who know.
10:38.transliteration:
daṇḍo damayatām asmi nītir asmi jigīṣatām |
maunaṃ caivāsmi guhyānāṃ jñānaṃ jñānavatām aham ||38||
10:38.devanagari:
दण्डो दमयतामस्मि नीतिरस्मि जिगीषताम् ।
मौनं चैवास्मि गुह्यानां ज्ञानं ज्ञानवतामहम् ॥ ३८ ॥
10:38.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:38.explanation: 'Daṇḍo damayatām' — Daṇḍa, the scepter of authority, the power of chastisement and disciplined enforcement: among all methods of governance and control, the sanctioned, just use of disciplinary power (daṇḍa-nīti) is supreme in the Arthaśāstric tradition — the sovereign capacity to maintain order. 'Nītiḥ jigīṣatām' — Nīti (statecraft, strategy, wise policy) among those who seek victory: not brute force but intelligent, ethical strategy wins in the long arc of history. 'Maunaṃ guhyānām' — Silence among all that is secret: the most profound truth is beyond words. The Upaniṣads declare that Brahman is where speech returns without having grasped it ('yato vāco nivartante'); the most sacred teaching is ultimately the silence that holds all language. Mauna is the vibhūti of the inexpressible. 'Jñānaṃ jñānavatām' — among the wise, I am Knowledge: not information or scholarship, but the self-illuminating awareness that is itself the nature of Ātman-Brahman — knowledge that is identical with the knower and the known.

10:39.text: And whatever is the seed-cause of all beings, O Arjuna, that too am I. There is no being — moving or unmoving — that can exist without Me.
10:39.transliteration:
yac cāpi sarvabhūtānāṃ bījaṃ tad aham arjuna |
na tad asti vinā yat syān mayā bhūtaṃ carācaram ||39||
10:39.devanagari:
यच्चापि सर्वभूतानां बीजं तदहमर्जुन ।
न तदस्ति विना यत्स्यान्मया भूतं चराचरम् ॥ ३९ ॥
10:39.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:39.explanation: This verse draws the philosophical curtain over the entire vibhūti catalogue, revealing the principle behind every individual manifestation named in verses 20–38. 'Bījaṃ sarvabhūtānām' — the seed of all beings: a seed contains within it the entire future tree — roots, trunk, branches, leaves, fruit, and future seeds — all in unmanifest potential. Brahman is the imperishable seed of all existence: before creation, during creation, and after dissolution, the seed-reality persists. 'Na tad asti vinā yat syān mayā' — there exists nothing whatsoever, in the entire moving and unmoving universe, that could exist even for a moment apart from Me. This is not a claim of domination but a statement of ontological truth: existence itself is a mode of Brahman. The vibhūtis enumerated across this chapter are not exceptional cases — they are simply the places where the Brahman-light shines through most brightly, making it easier for the eye of the student to see what is, in fact, everywhere.

10:40.text: O Parantapa, there is no end to My divine manifestations. What I have declared is only an indicative enumeration (uddeśataḥ) — a sample — of My infinite glory.
10:40.transliteration:
nānto 'sti mama divyānāṃ vibhūtīnāṃ parantapa |
eṣa tūddeśataḥ prokto vibhūter vistaro mayā ||40||
10:40.devanagari:
नान्तोऽस्ति मम दिव्यानां विभूतीनां परन्तप ।
एष तूद्देशतः प्रोक्तो विभूतेर्विस्तरो मया ॥ ४० ॥
10:40.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:40.explanation: 'Nānto 'sti mama divyānāṃ vibhūtīnām' — there is no end to My divine manifestations: Krishna, having spent twenty-two verses cataloguing his vibhūtis from verse 20 onward, now acknowledges that all of it was the barest indication. 'Uddeśataḥ' — by way of example, as a pointer, a sample: like showing a few grains from a granary that has no walls. The word is precise — not an exhaustive list but an indicative gesture toward the Infinite. This is one of the most intellectually honest statements in all theology: the Divine itself declares that its own infinite nature exceeds the capacity of language and enumeration to capture it. The student who has heard the vibhūtis should understand: every beautiful, powerful, excellent, glorious thing in the universe participates in this same outpouring. The list was to train the eye, not to set a boundary. What is truly needed now is a principle — and the next verse provides it.

10:41.text: Whatever being is endowed with glory (vibhūtimat), or is prosperous (śrīmat), or is powerfully vigorous (ūrjitam) — know that every such being arises from but a spark of My splendor.
10:41.transliteration:
yad yad vibhūtimat sattvaṃ śrīmad ūrjitam eva vā |
tat tad evāvagaccha tvaṃ mama tejomśasambhavam ||41||
10:41.devanagari:
यद्यद्विभूतिमत्सत्त्वं श्रीमदूर्जितमेव वा ।
तत्तदेवावगच्छ त्वं मम तेजोंऽशसम्भवम् ॥ ४१ ॥
10:41.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:41.explanation: This is the master key of the Tenth Chapter — a universal principle that renders any further enumeration of vibhūtis unnecessary. Three qualities that identify a divine manifestation: (1) 'Vibhūtimat' — endowed with magnificence, excellence, or supernatural potency; (2) 'Śrīmat' — radiant with prosperity, beauty, or sacred luster; (3) 'Ūrjitam' — charged with energy, power, and vigor. 'Tat tad evāvagaccha' — know each and every such thing as arising from My splendor. 'Tejomśasambhavam' — born from a fraction of My radiance. This verse transforms the student's relationship with the entire perceptible world: from now on, every encounter with greatness — whether in a person, a natural phenomenon, a work of art, a blazing fire, a noble deed — is an occasion for recognizing the divine source. This is the practical yoga of the Tenth Chapter: use every perception of excellence as a doorway to the Infinite. The world becomes a continuous darśana.

10:42.text: Or rather, O Arjuna, what is the use of this much detailed knowledge for you? I pervade and sustain this entire universe with but a single fraction (ekāṃśena) of Myself — and yet remain.
10:42.transliteration:
athavā bahunaitena kiṃ jñātena tavārjuna |
viṣṭabhyāham idaṃ kṛtsnam ekāṃśena sthito jagat ||42||
10:42.devanagari:
अथवा बहुनैतेन किं ज्ञातेन तवार्जुन ।
विष्टभ्याहमिदं कृत्स्नमेकांशेन स्थितो जगत् ॥ ४२ ॥
10:42.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
10:42.explanation: The closing verse of the Tenth Chapter is one of the most breathtaking statements in all of world scripture. 'Athavā' — 'or rather' — a sudden pivot: having spent the entire chapter cataloguing divine manifestations, Krishna now steps back from all of it. 'Bahunaitena kiṃ jñātena' — 'what is the use of all this elaborate knowledge?' — the Divine itself questioning the sufficiency of descriptive enumeration. The answer to all the vibhūtis is not more vibhūtis: it is the single, staggering fact that follows. 'Ekāṃśena sthito jagat' — with ONE portion of Myself I pervade and sustain this entire universe. The word 'ekāṃśena' (by one fraction) is the philosophical earthquake of this verse: this entire universe — every galaxy, every moment of time, every being, every vibhūti described in this chapter — all of it is just ONE fraction of Brahman. The rest — infinite, unnamed, beyond all conception — remains transcendent, unexpressed, ineffable. This verse is the threshold that opens directly into Chapter 11 (the Viśvarūpa-darśana), where Arjuna will ask to see even that one fraction — and will be overwhelmed.

11:1.text: Arjuna said: In order to show grace to me, You have declared the supreme secret known as adhyātma (the knowledge of the Self). Through those words of Yours, this delusion of mine has been dispelled.
11:1.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
mad anugrahāya paramaṃ guhyam adhyātmasañjñitam |
yat tvayoktaṃ vacas tena moho 'yaṃ vigato mama ||1||
11:1.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
मदनुग्रहाय परमं गुह्यमध्यात्मसंज्ञितम् ।
यत्त्वयोक्तं वचस्तेन मोहोऽयं विगतो मम ॥ १ ॥
11:1.speaker: Arjuna
11:1.explanation: Arjuna opens by acknowledging the transformative effect of what he has just received in Chapters 9 and 10. 'Mad-anugrahāya' — for my sake, out of pure grace: this knowledge was not earned but freely given in compassion. 'Paramaṃ guhyam adhyātmasañjñitam' — the supremely secret teaching on the nature of the Self and the Divine — the vibhūti knowledge of Chapter 10 and the 'I am all of this' revelations. 'Moho 'yaṃ vigataḥ' — this delusion has departed: not the initial delusion of Chapter 1 (grief over kinsmen) but a deeper metaphysical confusion about the relationship between God and the universe. Hearing has done what hearing can do. But now a further need arises: direct perception. Chapter 11 is the movement from śravaṇa (hearing) to darśana (direct vision).

11:2.text: O Lotus-eyed One, I have heard in detail from You about the origin and dissolution of all beings, and also Your inexhaustible greatness.
11:2.transliteration:
bhavāpyayau hi bhūtānāṃ śrutau vistaraśo mayā |
tvattaḥ kamalapatrākṣa māhātmyam api cāvyayam ||2||
11:2.devanagari:
भवाप्ययौ हि भूतानां श्रुतौ विस्तरशो मया ।
त्वत्तः कमलपत्राक्ष माहात्म्यमपि चाव्ययम् ॥ २ ॥
11:2.speaker: Arjuna
11:2.explanation: 'Bhavāpyayau' — origin (bhava) and dissolution (apyaya): the two great cosmic processes described in Chapter 9 (kalpa-kṣaye and kalpādau). 'Vistaraśaḥ' — in great detail, showing that Arjuna has been a fully attentive student throughout. 'Kamalapatrākṣa' — 'lotus-petal-eyed': the name is deliberately tender and personal, evoking the gentle, beautiful form of Krishna — significant because Arjuna is about to ask for something terrifying. He invokes the sweetness of the familiar face even as he seeks to see beyond it. 'Avyayam māhātmyam' — the inexhaustible greatness: the vibhūtis of Chapter 10 and especially the culminating 'ekāṃśena sthito jagat' — the universe sustained by one fraction of the Infinite.

11:3.text: O Supreme Lord, what You have spoken about Yourself is exactly so — I accept it as truth. Yet, O Puruṣottama, I wish to see Your lordly form (aiśvara-rūpa) directly.
11:3.transliteration:
evam etad yathāttha tvam ātmānaṃ parameśvara |
draṣṭum icchāmi te rūpam aiśvaraṃ puruṣottama ||3||
11:3.devanagari:
एवमेतद्यथात्थ त्वमात्मानं परमेश्वर ।
द्रष्टुमिच्छामि ते रूपमैश्वरं पुरुषोत्तम ॥ ३ ॥
11:3.speaker: Arjuna
11:3.explanation: 'Evam etad yathāttha' — I accept what You have said as exactly true: Arjuna does not doubt; he first affirms faith before making his request. This is the mark of a ripe student — not demanding proof of what has been heard but yearning to move from the heard to the seen. 'Draṣṭum icchāmi' — I wish to see: the movement from śravaṇa (hearing, Ch.9–10) to darśana (direct vision, Ch.11). 'Aiśvaraṃ rūpam' — the lordly, sovereign form: the actual reality behind the vibhūtis described in Chapter 10. The student has received the map; now he wants to see the territory. 'Parameśvara' and 'Puruṣottama' — two supreme addresses together: the Highest Lord and the Highest Being — expressing that Arjuna understands to Whom he is speaking, and still dares to ask.

11:4.text: O Lord, if You think it possible for me to see it — then O Yogeśvara (Lord of Yoga), reveal to me Your imperishable Self.
11:4.transliteration:
manyase yadi tac chakyaṃ mayā draṣṭum iti prabho |
yogeśvara tato me tvaṃ darśayātmānam avyayam ||4||
11:4.devanagari:
मन्यसे यदि तच्छक्यं मया द्रष्टुमिति प्रभो ।
योगेश्वर ततो मे त्वं दर्शयात्मानमव्ययम् ॥ ४ ॥
11:4.speaker: Arjuna
11:4.explanation: 'Manyase yadi tac chakyam mayā draṣṭum' — If You consider it possible for me to see: Arjuna does not demand; he surrenders the decision entirely to Krishna. He has no certainty that he is worthy of this vision and says so. This humility is itself the qualification. 'Yogeśvara' — Lord of all Yoga: the one who has all power and who alone can decide whether to grant what cannot be given by any human effort. 'Avyayam ātmānam' — Your imperishable Self: not a temporary or created form but the eternal ground-reality behind all the vibhūtis — the 'ekāṃśena sthito jagat' made visible. The conditionality ('if You think') and the reverent address together constitute perfect petitionary prayer: I desire this, but I submit to Your wisdom.

11:5.text: The Blessed Lord said: O Pārtha, behold My forms — hundreds and thousands of them, of many kinds, divine, of many colors and shapes.
11:5.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
paśya me pārtha rūpāṇi śataśo 'tha sahasraśaḥ |
nānāvidhāni divyāni nānāvarṇākṛtīni ca ||5||
11:5.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
पश्य मे पार्थ रूपाणि शतशोऽथ सहस्रशः ।
नानाविधानि दिव्यानि नानावर्णाकृतीनि च ॥ ५ ॥
11:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:5.explanation: Krishna's response is immediate and unqualified: 'Paśya' — See! The humble, qualified request of verse 4 is met with instant, unconditional grace. There is no condition imposed, no worthiness tested further — the devotee asked with the right spirit, and the Divine responds without delay. 'Śataśo 'tha sahasraśaḥ' — hundreds, nay thousands: not a precise count but a declaration of limitlessness that no finite number can bound. 'Nānāvidhāni divyāni' — many-natured and divine: these are not physical forms visible to the ordinary eye but spiritual realities of a different ontological order. 'Nānāvarṇākṛtīni' — of countless colors and shapes: the infinite diversity of all created things is about to be revealed as the one body of the Divine.

11:6.text: Behold the Ādityas, the Vasus, the Rudras, the two Aśvins, and the Maruts — behold the many wonders, O Bhārata, never seen before.
11:6.transliteration:
paśyādityān vasūn rudrān aśvinau marutas tathā |
bahūny adṛṣṭapūrvāṇi paśyāścaryāṇi bhārata ||6||
11:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:6.explanation: The Vedic divine hierarchy is here revealed as constituent parts of a single Body. 'Ādityāḥ' — twelve solar deities, the twelve aspects of the sun across the year. 'Vasavaḥ' — eight Vasus, elemental powers (fire, wind, water, earth, sky, moon, stars, dawn). 'Rudrāḥ' — eleven Rudras, forces of dissolution and transformation. 'Aśvinau' — the twin divine physicians, givers of health and healing. 'Marutaḥ' — the forty-nine Maruts, storm-wind deities of tremendous energy. All the separate divine beings that Vedic tradition worshipped as distinct — Indra, Varuṇa, Agni, Mitra — are here collapsed into one vision. 'Adṛṣṭapūrvāṇi āścaryāṇi' — wonders never seen before by any human eye: what follows in the next verses is unprecedented in human spiritual experience.

11:7.text: O Guḍākeśa (conqueror of sleep), behold now, here in My body, the entire universe — moving and unmoving — gathered in one place, and whatever else you wish to see.
11:7.transliteration:
ihaikasthaṃ jagat kṛtsnaṃ paśyādya sacarācaram |
mama dehe guḍākeśa yac cānyad draṣṭum icchasi ||7||
11:7.devanagari:
इहैकस्थं जगत्कृत्स्नं पश्याद्य सचराचरम् ।
मम देहे गुडाकेश यच्चान्यद्द्रष्टुमिच्छसि ॥ ७ ॥
11:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:7.explanation: 'Ihaikastham jagat kṛtsnam' — the entire universe, in one place, right here: this is Advaita made visible. The non-dual teaching that all is One is not merely a philosophical proposition but a directly perceivable fact — and Arjuna is about to perceive it. 'Sacarācaram' — both the moving (rivers, animals, wind, thought) and the unmoving (mountains, rocks, the earth itself). 'Mama dehe' — in My body: not in the four-cubit human body of the charioteer but in the viśva-deha, the cosmic body that is the universe Itself. 'Guḍākeśa' — master of sleep (guḍākā = sleep, īśa = lord): Arjuna conquered drowsiness and inertia through discipline — and now he must conquer the subtler sleep of ordinary perception to see what is about to be shown. 'Yac cānyat' — and whatever else you wish: the Infinite has no limitation on what it can reveal.

11:8.text: But you are not able to see Me with these ordinary eyes of yours. I grant you a divine eye. Behold My lordly Yoga (supreme power).
11:8.transliteration:
na tu māṃ śakyase draṣṭum anenaiva svacakṣuṣā |
divyaṃ dadāmi te cakṣuḥ paśya me yogam aiśvaram ||8||
11:8.devanagari:
न तु मां शक्यसे द्रष्टुमनेनैव स्वचक्षुषा ।
दिव्यं ददामि ते चक्षुः पश्य मे योगमैश्वरम् ॥ ८ ॥
11:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:8.explanation: A crucial epistemological declaration: the Cosmic Form cannot be perceived through physical senses. 'Svacakṣuṣā' — with these ordinary eyes: the biological eyes evolved to perceive the relative, material world; they have no capacity to take in the Infinite. 'Divyaṃ dadāmi te cakṣuḥ' — I give you a divine eye: this divine sight is not earned by Arjuna's austerities or practice — it is freely given by grace. Just as Vyāsa granted Sañjaya divine sight to witness the battlefield, Krishna now grants Arjuna a higher perceptual faculty to witness the Cosmic Form. 'Yogam aiśvaram' — My lordly power of Yoga: the sovereign creative and revelatory capacity by which the Infinite makes Itself temporarily visible within the range of a single finite consciousness. The vision depends entirely on the Giver, not the receiver.

11:9.text: Sañjaya said: O King, having spoken thus, the great Lord of all Yoga — Hari — revealed to Pārtha His supreme and lordly form.
11:9.transliteration:
sañjaya uvāca |
evam uktvā tato rājan mahāyogeśvaro hariḥ |
darśayām āsa pārthāya paramaṃ rūpam aiśvaram ||9||
11:9.devanagari:
सञ्जय उवाच ।
एवमुक्त्वा ततो राजन्महायोगेश्वरो हरिः ।
दर्शयामास पार्थाय परमं रूपमैश्वरम् ॥ ९ ॥
11:9.speaker: Sañjaya
11:9.explanation: The narrative shifts: Sañjaya, the divine-sighted narrator, now takes over from the dialogue to describe the Vision as it unfolds. This shift is itself dramatically significant — the cosmic event is so overwhelming that it requires a separate witness-narrator to convey it to Dhṛtarāṣṭra and through him to us. 'Mahāyogeśvaraḥ' — the great Lord of all Yoga: superlative of superlatives, emphasizing that what follows exceeds the range of any ordinary yogic power. 'Hariḥ' — Hari: the name that means 'the Remover' (of sins, sorrows, and ignorance) — even in the midst of the most terrifying cosmic revelation, this name reminds us that the Vision is ultimately one of liberation, not destruction. 'Darśayām āsa' — showed, revealed: the gracious act of divine self-disclosure.

11:10.text: With many mouths and eyes, with many wondrous aspects to behold, with many divine ornaments, with many divine weapons upraised —
11:10.speaker: Sañjaya
11:10.explanation: Sañjaya's description of the Cosmic Form begins — spanning verses 10–12 in a crescendo of imagery. The repetition of 'aneka-' (many / countless) is deliberate and rhythmic: not just two or three but literally uncountable. 'Aneka-vaktra-nayanam' — countless mouths and eyes: every living being's face and gaze is part of this one form; the billions of eyes that have ever looked at the sky are all eyes of the one Cosmic Body. 'Anekādbhutadarśanam' — displaying countless wonders: each aspect of the form reveals something unprecedented and staggering. 'Aneka-divyābharaṇam' — adorned with countless divine ornaments: each ornament is a cosmic power, a force of creation, each galaxy a jewel in this body. 'Divyānekodyatāyudham' — countless divine weapons raised: the Cosmic Form is not merely beautiful; it wields the power of dissolution. Creation and destruction coexist.

11:11.text: Wearing divine garlands and garments, anointed with divine fragrances — all-wondrous, infinite, facing in all directions — that Divine Being —
11:11.transliteration:
divyamālyāmbaradharaṃ divyagandhānulepanam |
sarvāścaryamayaṃ devam anantaṃ viśvatomukham ||11||
11:11.speaker: Sañjaya
11:11.explanation: The description continues in flowing participial construction, building a total image of the Cosmic Form. 'Divyamālyāmbaradharam' — wearing divine garlands and garments: in Indian iconography, garlands and garments are not mere decoration but expression of divine majesty; the cosmic garland encircles all of space. 'Divyagandhānulepanam' — anointed with divine fragrance: all the sweet scents that arise from flowers, rain, earth, and the sacred — their source is this one Being. 'Sarvāścaryamayam' — made entirely of wonder: every instance of awe and astonishment in the universe is a fragment of this form. 'Anantam' — infinite, without boundary or end: this is the supreme qualifier — the Cosmic Form has no edge, no limit, no conclusion. 'Viśvatomukham' — facing all directions simultaneously: omnipresent perception, awareness pointing in every direction at once.

11:12.text: If a thousand suns were to blaze forth simultaneously in the sky — that radiance might approach the radiance of that Great Being.
11:12.transliteration:
divi sūryasahasrasya bhaved yugapad utthitā |
yadi bhāḥ sadṛśī sā syād bhāsas tasya mahātmanaḥ ||12||
11:12.devanagari:
दिवि सूर्यसहस्रस्य भवेद्युगपदुत्थिता ।
यदि भाः सदृशी सा स्याद्भासस्तस्य महात्मनः ॥ १२ ॥
11:12.speaker: Sañjaya
11:12.explanation: The most celebrated simile of the entire Gita, and one of the supreme descriptions of divine light in world literature. 'Sūryasahasrasya bhaved yugapad utthitā' — a thousand suns rising at once: Sañjaya reaches for the most extreme natural analogy available — not one sun, but a thousand, and not at different times but simultaneously — and even this staggering image is merely 'sadṛśī' (similar to, approaching) the radiance of the Cosmic Form. The 'yadi' (if) is itself profound: the comparison is conditional and ultimately insufficient. Even a thousand simultaneous suns fall short. This verse was famously cited by J. Robert Oppenheimer on witnessing the first nuclear test at Trinity, New Mexico in 1945 — though the verse itself is not about destruction but about the overwhelming luminosity of divine Consciousness made manifest.

11:13.text: Then, in the body of the God of gods, Pāṇḍava (Arjuna) saw the entire universe — unified in one place, yet divided in many ways.
11:13.transliteration:
tatraikasthaṃ jagat kṛtsnaṃ pravibhaktam anekadhā |
apaśyad devadevasya śarīre pāṇḍavas tadā ||13||
11:13.devanagari:
तत्रैकस्थं जगत्कृत्स्नं प्रविभक्तमनेकधा ।
अपश्यद्देवदेवस्य शरीरे पाण्डवस्तदा ॥ १३ ॥
11:13.speaker: Sañjaya
11:13.explanation: 'Tatraikastham jagat kṛtsnam' — the entire universe gathered in one place: verse 11.7's promise ('behold the entire universe here in My body') is now fulfilled in Arjuna's direct perception. 'Pravibhaktam anekadhā' — yet divided in many ways: the unity does not obliterate diversity. The one Cosmic Body contains infinite distinct forms — mountains, oceans, galaxies, beings, moments — all simultaneously present, all clearly distinct, yet all contained within a single divine presence. This is the visual fulfillment of the Ṛgvedic insight: 'Ekaṃ sat viprāḥ bahudhā vadanti' — Truth is one; the wise speak of it in many ways. 'Devadevasya śarīre' — in the body of the God of all gods: beyond all hierarchy of divine beings, this is the supreme substratum of all divinity. Arjuna is the first human to see this directly.

11:14.text: Then, overwhelmed with wonder, with hair standing on end, Dhanañjaya bowed his head before the Divine, and with palms joined, spoke.
11:14.transliteration:
tataḥ sa vismayāviṣṭo hṛṣṭaromā dhanañjayaḥ |
praṇamya śirasā devaṃ kṛtāñjalir abhāṣata ||14||
11:14.devanagari:
ततः स विस्मयाविष्टो हृष्टरोमा धनञ्जयः ।
प्रणम्य शिरसा देवं कृताञ्जलिरभाषत ॥ १४ ॥
11:14.speaker: Sañjaya
11:14.explanation: Arjuna's first response to the Cosmic Form is entirely physical and pre-verbal — the body responds before the mind can organize itself into speech. 'Vismayāviṣṭaḥ' — seized by astonishment: not just feeling wonder but possessed by it, taken over by it completely. 'Hṛṣṭaromā' — hair standing on end, the Sanskrit technical term for horripilation (pulakāñcita), which Indian aesthetics recognizes as a physiological sign of bhakti and awe simultaneously. 'Praṇamya śirasā' — bowing the head, the complete physical gesture of surrender. 'Kṛtāñjaliḥ' — with palms joined in añjali, the universal gesture of reverence. Only after the body has prostrated itself and the hands have offered themselves in prayer does the voice emerge. This sequence — body first, then voice — is itself the teaching: before theological understanding comes total surrender.

11:15.text: Arjuna said: O Lord, in Your body I see all the gods and also the hosts of all kinds of beings — Brahmā seated on the lotus, Śiva (Lord of all), all the sages, and the divine serpents.
11:15.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
paśyāmi devāṃs tava deva dehe
sarvāṃs tathā bhūtaviśeṣasaṃghān |
brahmāṇam īśaṃ kamalāsanastham
ṛṣīṃś ca sarvān uragāṃś ca divyān ||15||
11:15.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
पश्यामि देवांस्तव देव देहे
सर्वांस्तथा भूतविशेषसंघान् ।
ब्रह्माणमीशं कमलासनस्थ-
मृषींश्च सर्वानुरगांश्च दिव्यान् ॥ १५ ॥
11:15.speaker: Arjuna
11:15.explanation: Arjuna's hymn of direct vision begins — and note the shift in meter. From here through verse 31, the Gita uses Triṣṭubh meter (11-syllable lines), its longest and most solemn verse form, which gives the passage a weightiness and grandeur that the shorter Anuṣṭubh of the surrounding verses cannot convey. 'Paśyāmi' — present tense, 'I see': this is happening now, in real time, not recollection. 'Tava deva dehe' — in Your divine body: the charioteer's form has expanded beyond all physical boundaries into the cosmos itself. 'Sarvāṃs tathā bhūtaviśeṣasaṃghān' — all beings of every special kind: not just gods but every category of sentient and non-sentient existence. 'Brahmāṇam kamalāsanastham' — Brahmā seated on the lotus: the creator himself is a detail in this larger divine body.

11:16.text: I behold You everywhere — infinite in form, with countless arms, bellies, mouths, and eyes. O Lord of the Universe, O Universal Form, I see no end, no middle, and no beginning of You.
11:16.transliteration:
anekabāhūdaravaktranertaṃ
paśyāmi tvāṃ sarvato 'nantarūpam |
nāntaṃ na madhyaṃ na punas tavādiṃ
paśyāmi viśveśvara viśvarūpa ||16||
11:16.devanagari:
अनेकबाहूदरवक्त्रनेत्रं
पश्यामि त्वां सर्वतोऽनन्तरूपम् ।
नान्तं न मध्यं न पुनस्तवादिं
पश्यामि विश्वेश्वर विश्वरूप ॥ १६ ॥
11:16.speaker: Arjuna
11:16.explanation: 'Anekabāhūdaravaktranertam' — countless arms, torsos, mouths, and eyes: every limb that any being possesses is a limb of this one Cosmic Body. 'Sarvato 'nantarūpam' — infinite in form in every direction: look east, west, up, down, inward, outward — everywhere the same Infinite. 'Nāntaṃ na madhyaṃ na punas tavādim' — no end, no middle, no beginning: this is the most precise statement of spatial infinity possible — a form that cannot be bounded by the three spatial references that structure ordinary perception. You cannot stand 'outside' this form to see its edges, because there are no edges. You cannot find its center because every point is equally central. You cannot trace its beginning because it has always been. 'Viśveśvara viśvarūpa' — Lord of the universe, Universal Form: the addresses here do not invoke a deity but name what Arjuna is directly seeing.

11:17.text: I behold You with crown, mace, and discus — a mass of radiance blazing everywhere, difficult to look at, shining like blazing fire and the sun, immeasurable.
11:17.transliteration:
kirīṭinaṃ gadinaṃ cakriṇaṃ ca
tejorāśiṃ sarvato dīptimantam |
paśyāmi tvāṃ durnirīkṣyaṃ samantād
dīptānalārkadyutim aprameyam ||17||
11:17.devanagari:
किरीटिनं गदिनं चक्रिणं च
तेजोराशिं सर्वतो दीप्तिमन्तम् ।
पश्यामि त्वां दुर्निरीक्ष्यं समन्ता-
द्दीप्तानलार्कद्युतिमप्रमेयम् ॥ १७ ॥
11:17.speaker: Arjuna
11:17.explanation: 'Kirīṭinaṃ gadinaṃ cakriṇaṃ ca' — bearing crown, mace, and discus: the three signature divine attributes of Viṣṇu-Kṛṣṇa, here magnified to cosmic scale. The crown (kirīṭa) represents sovereignty; the mace (gadā) represents cosmic order and power; the discus (cakra, the Sudarśana) represents the wheel of time and cosmic law. 'Tejorāśim sarvato dīptimantam' — a gathered mass of effulgence, blazing from all sides: here the abstract philosophical description of verse 11.12 (a thousand suns) becomes Arjuna's lived visual experience. 'Durnirīkṣyam samantāt' — extremely difficult to gaze at from any direction: there is no angle from which this form can be comfortably observed; it overwhelms every perceptual faculty. 'Aprameyam' — beyond all measure: no instrument of knowledge — perception, inference, scripture — can fully encompass this.

11:18.text: You are the imperishable Supreme, the highest to be known, the ultimate repository of this universe. You are the undecaying guardian of eternal dharma. I regard You as the primeval, everlasting Puruṣa.
11:18.transliteration:
tvam akṣaraṃ paramaṃ veditavyaṃ
tvam asya viśvasya paraṃ nidhānam |
tvam avyayaḥ śāśvatadharmagoptā
sanātanas tvaṃ puruṣo mato me ||18||
11:18.devanagari:
त्वमक्षरं परमं वेदितव्यं
त्वमस्य विश्वस्य परं निधानम् ।
त्वमव्ययः शाश्वतधर्मगोप्ता
सनातनस्त्वं पुरुषो मतो मे ॥ १८ ॥
11:18.speaker: Arjuna
11:18.explanation: In the midst of overwhelming visual experience, Arjuna articulates a moment of theological clarity — the Cosmic Vision produces not only awe but insight. 'Akṣaraṃ paramaṃ veditavyam' — the supreme Imperishable that all inquiry seeks: the Kaṭha Upaniṣad's 'akṣara' (the Indestructible) is no longer an abstract term but a visual reality for Arjuna. 'Asya viśvasya paraṃ nidhānam' — the supreme repository of this entire universe: everything rests in You, as waves in the ocean, as images in a mirror. 'Avyayaḥ śāśvatadharmagoptā' — the undecaying guardian of eternal dharma: not merely a law-keeper but the very ground of cosmic moral order, the principle that prevents the universe from dissolving into chaos. 'Sanātanaḥ puruṣaḥ' — the Primeval, Eternal Person: the Puruṣa of the Puruṣa-Sūkta, the cosmic Being whose body is the universe. 'Mato me' — this is my understanding: even in ecstatic vision, reason is not suspended.

11:19.text: Without beginning, middle, or end, of infinite valor, with endless arms, with the moon and sun as Your eyes, I see You — with blazing fire-mouths, warming this entire universe with Your own radiance.
11:19.transliteration:
anādimadhyāntam anantavīryam
anantabāhuṃ śaśisūryanetram |
paśyāmi tvāṃ dīptahutāśavaktraṃ
svatejasā viśvam idaṃ tapantam ||19||
11:19.devanagari:
अनादिमध्यान्तमनन्तवीर्य-
मनन्तबाहुं शशिसूर्यनेत्रम् ।
पश्यामि त्वां दीप्तहुताशवक्त्रं
स्वतेजसा विश्वमिदं तपन्तम् ॥ १९ ॥
11:19.speaker: Arjuna
11:19.explanation: 'Anādimadhyāntam anantavīryam' — without beginning, middle, or end: not sequential infinity but the absolute absence of temporal and spatial boundaries; and with this, infinite valor — the power that sustains creation is undiminished and inexhaustible. 'Anantabāhuṃ śaśisūryanetram' — with endless arms and the moon and sun as eyes: one of the most beautiful and profound images in the Gita. The moon and sun — cool silver light and hot golden light, night-illuminator and day-creator — are both eyes of the same Being. What appear to be opposites (cool/warm, gentle/fierce, night/day) are perspectives of the same single divine vision. 'Dīptahutāśavaktram' — with mouths like blazing fire: the first hint of the terrifying aspect that will dominate the coming verses. 'Svatejasā viśvam tapantam' — warming the entire universe with Your own intrinsic radiance, without borrowing light from anywhere.

11:20.text: O Great Being, the entire space between heaven and earth and all the directions are pervaded by You alone. The three worlds tremble with fear upon beholding this wondrous and terrible form of Yours.
11:20.transliteration:
dyāvāpṛthivyor idam antaraṃ hi
vyāptaṃ tvayaikena diśaś ca sarvāḥ |
dṛṣṭvādbhutaṃ rūpam ugraṃ tavedaṃ
lokatrayaṃ pravyathitaṃ mahātman ||20||
11:20.devanagari:
द्यावापृथिव्योरिदमन्तरं हि
व्याप्तं त्वयैकेन दिशश्च सर्वाः ।
दृष्ट्वाद्भुतं रूपमुग्रं तवेदं
लोकत्रयं प्रव्यथितं महात्मन् ॥ २० ॥
11:20.speaker: Arjuna
11:20.explanation: 'Dyāvāpṛthivyor idam antaraṃ' — the entire space between heaven and earth: in Indian cosmology this refers to the 'antarikṣa,' the middle world of atmospheric space that connects the terrestrial and celestial. 'Vyāptaṃ tvayaikena diśaś ca sarvāḥ' — pervaded by You alone, in all directions: the philosophical teaching of 9.4 ('by Me all this is pervaded') is now Arjuna's direct perceptual reality. 'Tvayaikena' — by You alone, by one: the oneness of the pervading principle is emphasized. 'Dṛṣṭvādbhutaṃ rūpam ugraṃ' — beholding this wondrous yet terrifying form: 'adbhuta' (wondrous) and 'ugra' (fierce, terrifying) — both simultaneously. The divine in its fullness is not merely beautiful; it contains the power of dissolution. 'Lokatrayaṃ pravyathitam' — the three worlds (heaven, earth, netherworld) are convulsed with terror: not only Arjuna but all conscious beings throughout all realms tremble at this vision.

11:21.text: These hosts of gods are entering into You; some, struck with fear, praise You with joined palms; the great sages and perfected ones (siddhas) cry 'May it be auspicious!' and praise You with magnificent hymns.
11:21.transliteration:
amī hi tvāṃ surasaṃghā viśanti
kecid bhītāḥ prāñjalayo gṛṇanti |
svastīty uktvā maharṣisiddhasaṃghāḥ
stuvanti tvāṃ stutibhiḥ puṣkalābhiḥ ||21||
11:21.devanagari:
अमी हि त्वां सुरसंघा विशन्ति
केचिद्भीताः प्राञ्जलयो गृणन्ति ।
स्वस्तीत्युक्त्वा महर्षिसिद्धसंघाः
स्तुवन्ति त्वां स्तुतिभिः पुष्कलाभिः ॥ २१ ॥
11:21.speaker: Arjuna
11:21.explanation: Arjuna now sees that the impact of the Cosmic Vision extends to all beings throughout the cosmos — he is not alone in his terror and awe. Three responses witnessed simultaneously: (1) 'Viśanti' — the divine hosts are entering into You: the gods themselves are being absorbed into the Cosmic Form, suggesting the process of cosmic dissolution that the form embodies. (2) 'Bhītāḥ prāñjalayo gṛṇanti' — some, frightened, praise with joined palms: even the immortal gods are afraid before this vision, their normal composure shattered. (3) 'Svastīty uktvā... stuvanti puṣkalābhiḥ' — the great sages and Siddhas cry 'svasti' (may all be well, may there be auspiciousness) and sing magnificent hymns: those of highest realization respond to the Cosmic Form with praise rather than terror, recognizing it as the ground of all auspiciousness even in its terrible aspect.

11:22.text: The Rudras, Ādityas, Vasus, Sādhyas, Viśvedevas, the two Aśvins, the Maruts, the Uṣmapas (ancestral beings), Gandharvas, Yakṣas, Asuras, and the hosts of Siddhas — all behold You in astonishment.
11:22.transliteration:
rudrādityā vasavo ye ca sādhyā
viśve 'śvinau marutas coṣmapāś ca |
gandharvayakṣāsurasiddhasaṃghā
vīkṣante tvāṃ vismitāś caiva sarve ||22||
11:22.devanagari:
रुद्रादित्या वसवो ये च साध्या
विश्वेऽश्विनौ मरुतश्चोष्मपाश्च ।
गन्धर्वयक्षासुरसिद्धसंघा
वीक्षन्ते त्वां विस्मिताश्चैव सर्वे ॥ २२ ॥
11:22.speaker: Arjuna
11:22.explanation: A complete inventory of the Vedic and Purāṇic divine hierarchy in a single verse, all united in a single response: astonishment. Rudras (forces of dissolution), Ādityas (solar powers), Vasus (elemental powers), Sādhyas (accomplished divine beings), Viśvedevas (universal gods), Aśvins (divine physicians), Maruts (storm-gods), Uṣmapāḥ (ancestral beings sustained by fire-offerings), Gandharvas (celestial musicians), Yakṣas (nature-guardians), Asuras (anti-gods — enemies of the Devas!) and Siddhas (perfected spiritual masters). Even the Asuras — normally enemies of the gods and of the cosmic order — stand here in shared astonishment. The Cosmic Vision transcends all divisions: gods and anti-gods, friends and enemies, all look up with the same expression — 'vismitāḥ' — wonderstruck, speechless.

11:23.text: O Mighty-armed One, beholding Your vast form with many mouths and eyes, many arms, thighs, and feet, many bellies, and many terrible tusks — the worlds are terrified, and so am I.
11:23.transliteration:
rūpaṃ mahat te bahuvaktranertaṃ
mahābāho bahubāhūrupādam |
bahūdaraṃ bahudaṃṣṭrākarālaṃ
dṛṣṭvā lokāḥ pravyathitās tathā 'ham ||23||
11:23.devanagari:
रूपं महत्ते बहुवक्त्रनेत्रं
महाबाहो बहुबाहूरुपादम् ।
बहूदरं बहुदंष्ट्राकरालं
दृष्ट्वा लोकाः प्रव्यथितास्तथाऽहम् ॥ २३ ॥
11:23.speaker: Arjuna
11:23.explanation: 'Bahudaṃṣṭrākarālam' — terrible with many tusks and fangs: this is the first clear description of the terrifying aspect of the Cosmic Form. Fangs and tusks in Sanskrit iconography represent the capacity to devour, to consume utterly — and the Cosmic Form that devours all beings is about to be described in the next few verses. 'Mahābāho' — mighty-armed: Arjuna addresses Krishna by a name that praises his strength, even as he stands in terror before the amplified infinity of those arms. 'Lokāḥ pravyathitāḥ tathā 'ham' — the worlds are terrified and so am I: Arjuna aligns himself with the universal response of the created order. Mahā-warrior Arjuna, conqueror of celestial battles, who has never admitted fear before any adversary, here confesses his terror — this is the most honest moment in the Gita thus far. The Cosmic Form does not spare even the bravest heart.

11:24.text: Beholding You touching the sky, blazing, many-colored, with gaping mouths, with huge flaming eyes — my inner self is terrified, O Viṣṇu, and I find neither courage nor peace.
11:24.transliteration:
nabhaḥspṛśaṃ dīptam anekavaṇaṃ
vyāttānanaṃ dīptaviśālanetram |
dṛṣṭvā hi tvāṃ pravyathitāntarātmā
dhṛtiṃ na vindāmi śamaṃ ca viṣṇo ||24||
11:24.devanagari:
नभःस्पृशं दीप्तमनेकवर्णं
व्यात्ताननं दीप्तविशालनेत्रम् ।
दृष्ट्वा हि त्वां प्रव्यथितान्तरात्मा
धृतिं न विन्दामि शमं च विष्णो ॥ २४ ॥
11:24.speaker: Arjuna
11:24.explanation: 'Nabhaḥspṛśam' — touching the sky: the Cosmic Form is not contained within any spatial dimension; it reaches to and beyond the sky. 'Dīptam anekavaṇam' — blazing and many-colored: every wavelength of light, every hue that exists or can be imagined. 'Vyāttānanam' — with gaping mouths: mouths open in readiness to consume — this is the form of Kāla (Time-as-Death) beginning to reveal itself. 'Dīptaviśālanetram' — with huge blazing eyes: eyes that do not offer comfort but consume with their gaze. 'Pravyathitāntarātmā' — my innermost self is shaken: the terror has penetrated beyond the surface of fear into the deepest interior of Arjuna's being. 'Dhṛtiṃ na vindāmi śamaṃ ca Viṣṇo' — I cannot find courage or calm, O Viṣṇu: the all-pervading One (Viṣṇu = 'he who pervades') is addressed precisely as Arjuna experiences total pervasion by terror.

11:25.text: Beholding Your mouths — terrible with fangs, like the fires of cosmic dissolution — I lose all sense of direction and find no comfort. Be gracious, O Lord of Gods, O Abode of the Universe.
11:25.transliteration:
daṃṣṭrākarālāni ca te mukhāni
dṛṣṭvaiva kālānalasannibhāni |
diśo na jāne na labhe ca śarma
prasīda deveśa jagannivāsa ||25||
11:25.devanagari:
दंष्ट्राकरालानि च ते मुखानि
दृष्ट्वैव कालानलसन्निभानि ।
दिशो न जाने न लभे च शर्म
प्रसीद देवेश जगन्निवास ॥ २५ ॥
11:25.speaker: Arjuna
11:25.explanation: 'Kālānalasannibhāni' — resembling the fires of cosmic dissolution (kālānala): not ordinary fire but the pralaya-fire, the fire that consumes all of creation at the end of a cosmic cycle. This is the most explicit identification yet of the Cosmic Form with Time-as-Destroyer. 'Diśo na jāne' — I no longer know the directions: when the Infinite fills every direction simultaneously, the ordinary spatial orientation of the mind collapses. There is no 'north' or 'south' from inside the Infinite. 'Na labhe ca śarma' — and I find no shelter, no comfort, no safety: every instinct for protection and refuge fails before this form. Then, crucially, Arjuna turns from description to prayer: 'Prasīda deveśa jagannivāsa' — Be gracious, be pleased, O Lord of all gods, O Dwelling-place of the universe: this is the prayer that the terrified soul utters when all other resources have been exhausted.

11:26.text: All these sons of Dhritarashtra, together with the hosts of kings — Bhishma, Drona, and that son of a charioteer (Karna), and also the chief warriors of our own side — all are entering into You.
11:26.transliteration:
amī ca tvāṃ dhṛtarāṣṭrasya putrāḥ sarve sahaivāvanipālasaṅghaiḥ |
bhīṣmo droṇaḥ sūtaputras tathāsau sahāsmadīyair api yodhamukhyaiḥ ||26||
11:26.devanagari:
अमी च त्वां धृतराष्ट्रस्य पुत्राः सर्वे सहैवावनिपालसङ्घैः ।
भीष्मो द्रोणः सूतपुत्रस्तथासौ सहास्मदीयैरपि योधमुख्यैः ॥ २६ ॥
11:26.speaker: Arjuna
11:26.explanation: Arjuna's vision reaches its most personally devastating moment: he begins naming names. Until now he has seen masses entering the cosmic maw; now individual identities emerge from the terror. The sons of Dhritarashtra — his enemies. Bhīṣma — the grandsire, the near-invincible patriarch. Droṇa — his own teacher, the supreme master of arms. Karna — 'sūtaputra,' son of a charioteer, the most tragic hero of the Mahābhārata. Crucially: 'sahāsmadīyaiḥ api' — and also those of our own side. The cosmic form plays no favorites: both armies, friend and foe alike, are entering those blazing mouths. Kāla (Time) is impartial. Arjuna stands between both currents and watches them all converge on the same annihilating absolute.

11:27.text: Rushing swiftly, they enter Your terrible mouths armed with fearful tusks. Some are seen dangling between Your teeth with their heads crushed to powder.
11:27.transliteration:
vaktrāṇi te tvaramāṇā viśanti daṃṣṭrākarālāni bhayānakāni |
kecid vilagṇā daśanāntareṣu sandṛśyante cūrṇitair uttamāṅgaiḥ ||27||
11:27.devanagari:
वक्त्राणि ते त्वरमाणा विशन्ति दंष्ट्राकरालानि भयानकानि ।
केचिद्विलग्ना दशनान्तरेषु सन्दृश्यन्ते चूर्णितैरुत्तमाङ्गैः ॥ २७ ॥
11:27.speaker: Arjuna
11:27.explanation: 'Daṃṣṭrākarālāni' — terrible with tusks, gaping mouths armed like the jaws of Time itself. 'Tvaramāṇāḥ viśanti' — rushing swiftly: they do not enter reluctantly or slowly; the current of Kāla sucks them in with terrifying velocity. This is not individual death at the appointed moment — this is the mass, simultaneous annihilation of entire warrior-orders. 'Uttamāṅgaiḥ' — the highest limb, the head — the most honored part of the human body, the seat of consciousness — crushed between divine teeth. This verse represents the vision's most viscerally horrifying moment: not abstract destruction but concrete, identifiable, personal obliteration. Arjuna watches men he knows — by name, by face, by relation — being ground between the teeth of the Absolute. The Viśvarūpa is not merely awe-inspiring; it is the truest face of existence's transience.

11:28.text: As the many rushing torrents of rivers flow irresistibly toward the ocean, so these heroes of the human world enter into Your blazing mouths.
11:28.transliteration:
yathā nadīnāṃ bahavo 'mbuvegāḥ samudram evābhimukhā dravanti |
tathā tavāmī naralokavīrā viśanti vaktrāṇy abhivijvalanti ||28||
11:28.devanagari:
यथा नदीनां बहवोऽम्बुवेगाः समुद्रमेवाभिमुखा द्रवन्ति ।
तथा तवामी नरलोकवीरा विशन्ति वक्त्राण्यभिविज्वलन्ति ॥ २८ ॥
11:28.speaker: Arjuna
11:28.explanation: Arjuna reaches for analogy to contain the uncontainable, and finds the river-and-ocean image — one of the most perfect metaphors in all of the Gita. 'Ambuvegāḥ' — the rushing torrents of rivers: rivers do not choose to enter the ocean. Gravity, the terrain's slope, the laws of hydraulics — these carry the water inevitably to its source-destination. 'Naralokavīrāḥ' — the heroes of the human world: however mighty, however armored, however celebrated — they are rivers flowing toward an ocean that will absorb them all without remainder. 'Abhivijvalanti' — the mouths blaze with fire, making the image more terrible: these are not calm waters but blazing abysses. The analogy does something profound: it de-personalizes the mass death Arjuna is witnessing, relocating it within natural law. What looks like catastrophe is, from the cosmic vantage, simply gravity — the irresistible pull of all finite things toward their infinite source.

11:29.text: As moths rush with great speed into a blazing fire for their own destruction, so too do these creatures rush into Your mouths with great speed for their destruction.
11:29.transliteration:
yathā pradīptaṃ jvalanaṃ pataṅgā viśanti nāśāya samṛddhavegāḥ |
tathāiva nāśāya viśanti lokās tavāpi vaktrāṇi samṛddhavegāḥ ||29||
11:29.devanagari:
यथा प्रदीप्तं ज्वलनं पतङ्गा विशन्ति नाशाय समृद्धवेगाः ।
तथैव नाशाय विशन्ति लोकास्तवापि वक्त्राणि समृद्धवेगाः ॥ २९ ॥
11:29.speaker: Arjuna
11:29.explanation: The second analogy — moths and flame — is the most psychologically penetrating of all the Viśvarūpa images. The river flows to the ocean by natural law; the moth rushes to flame by fatally misdirected desire. 'Pataṅgāḥ' — moths: they are drawn to light, for which they were made, but tragedy lies in mistaking a destructive fire for the liberating light. 'Nāśāya samṛddhavegāḥ' — with great speed toward destruction: the velocity is full, unhesitating, even joyful. The word 'nāśāya' (for destruction) is repeated in both lines of the verse — a deliberate poetic hammer-blow. The warriors rushing to Kurukshetra are the human moths: propelled by ambition, ego, and the intoxication of strength toward the flame that will consume them. The Viśvarūpa-darśana reveals what their confident march toward war actually is from the vantage of the eternal — a blaze attracting moths who do not know they are about to be extinguished.

11:30.text: O Viṣṇu! Licking all the worlds from every side with Your blazing mouths, You devour them. Your fierce radiances fill the entire universe, scorching it with their brilliance.
11:30.transliteration:
lelihyase grasamānaḥ samantāl lokān samagrān vadanair jvaladbhiḥ |
tejobhir āpūrya jagat samagraṃ bhāsas tavograḥ pratapanti viṣṇo ||30||
11:30.devanagari:
लेलिह्यसे ग्रसमानः समन्ताल्लोकान्समग्रान्वदनैर्ज्वलद्भिः ।
तेजोभिरापूर्य जगत्समग्रं भासस्तवोग्राः प्रतपन्ति विष्णो ॥ ३० ॥
11:30.speaker: Arjuna
11:30.explanation: 'Lelihyase' — an onomatopoeic, viscerally kinetic word: the repeated licking action of a flame's tongue, the insatiable movement of fire consuming its fuel. 'Grasamānaḥ' — devouring: the root 'gras' (to swallow, to devour) is the Sanskrit root of the word for the eclipse (grāsa) — the cosmic swallowing. 'Samantāt lokān samagrān' — all the worlds from every direction: not just earth but every plane of existence, all fourteen worlds of the Vedic cosmos, simultaneously consumed. 'Tejobhiḥ āpūrya jagat' — filling the universe with Your radiances: this is not only the darkness of destruction but also the blinding light of the Absolute, which overwhelms all lesser lights. 'Viṣṇo' — O Viṣṇu (the all-pervading one, from 'viś,' to pervade): Arjuna uses this epithet precisely because what he is seeing is not a localized destruction but a universal pervasion — the Infinite claiming every inch of the finite.

11:31.text: Tell me — who are You in this fierce form? My salutations to You, O best among gods; be gracious. I wish to know You, the Primordial — for I do not comprehend Your activity (pravṛtti).
11:31.transliteration:
ākhyāhi me ko bhavān ugrarūpo namo 'stu te devavara prasīda |
vijñātum icchāmi bhavantam ādyaṃ na hi prajānāmi tava pravṛttim ||31||
11:31.devanagari:
आख्याहि मे को भवानुग्ररूपो नमोऽस्तु ते देववर प्रसीद ।
विज्ञातुमिच्छामि भवन्तमाद्यं न हि प्रजानामि तव प्रवृत्तिम् ॥ ३१ ॥
11:31.speaker: Arjuna
11:31.explanation: At the apex of terror and awe, Arjuna asks the question that stands at the center of all mystical experience: 'Ko bhavān?' — who are You? This is not ignorance about Krishna's identity — Arjuna stands before his friend and teacher. The question is ontological: what is this force that I am beholding? What aspect of reality manifests as universal destruction? 'Ugrarūpa' — O terrible-formed one: the epithet acknowledges that what he is seeing is not the gentle flute-player but the primordial force of dissolution. 'Devavara prasīda' — O best of gods, be gracious: the plea of a devotee overwhelmed by a vision beyond his capacity to bear. 'Ādyam' — the Primordial: Arjuna reaches for the deepest possible category. 'Pravṛttim' — Your activity, Your intention: I see what You are doing but cannot comprehend why. The answer Krishna gives in verse 32 is the most shattering declaration in all of the Bhagavad Gītā.

11:32.text: The Blessed Lord said: I am mighty, world-destroying Time (Kāla), now fully manifested here to annihilate these worlds. Even without you, all the warriors arrayed in the opposing armies shall cease to exist.
11:32.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
kālo 'smi lokakṣayakṛt pravṛddho
lokān samāhartum iha pravṛttaḥ |
ṛte 'pi tvāṃ na bhaviṣyanti sarve
ye 'vasthitāḥ pratyanīkeṣu yodhāḥ ||32||
11:32.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
कालोऽस्मि लोकक्षयकृत्प्रवृद्धो
लोकान्समाहर्तुमिह प्रवृत्तः ।
ऋतेऽपि त्वां न भविष्यन्ति सर्वे
येऽवस्थिताः प्रत्यनीकेषु योधाः ॥ ३२ ॥
11:32.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:32.explanation: 'Kālo 'smi' — I am Time. Three words that stand among the most shattering self-declarations in all of world literature. In the Universal Form, Arjuna had just witnessed all the warriors rushing headlong into Krishna's flaming mouths (11.26–28). Now Krishna names what Arjuna saw: he is Kāla — not merely time as we ordinarily understand it, but Mahākāla, the great consuming Time that is the engine of cosmic dissolution. 'Pravṛddhaḥ' — fully matured, fully swollen, like a flood that has already crested: there is nothing left to prevent it. 'Lokān samāhartum pravṛttaḥ' — set in motion to gather all these worlds back into Myself: the decision has already been made at the cosmic level; the battle of Kurukshetra is already over in the dimension where Time operates. 'Ṛte 'pi tvām' — even without you: Arjuna is not the author of the deaths that are about to occur; he is the instrument. The warriors are already slain by the will of Time. This is the verse J. Robert Oppenheimer quoted at the first nuclear detonation in 1945 — the words that bridged the ancient cosmic and the modern catastrophic.

11:33.text: Therefore, arise! Win glory! Conquer your enemies and enjoy a prosperous kingdom. These warriors have already been slain by Me long before — be only the instrument, O Savyasācin (Arjuna, the ambidextrous archer).
11:33.transliteration:
tasmāt tvam uttiṣṭha yaśo labhasva
jītvā śatrūn bhuṅkṣva rājyaṃ samṛddham |
mayaivāite nihatāḥ pūrvam eva
nimittamātraṃ bhava savyasācin ||33||
11:33.devanagari:
तस्मात्त्वमुत्तिष्ठ यशो लभस्व
जित्वा शत्रून् भुङ्क्ष्व राज्यं समृद्धम् ।
मयैवैते निहताः पूर्वमेव
निमित्तमात्रं भव सव्यसाचिन् ॥ ३३ ॥
11:33.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:33.explanation: 'Tasmāt' — therefore: the cosmic declaration of verse 32 becomes the grounds for the most direct command in the Gita. 'Uttiṣṭha' — arise!: the same word used in 2.3 at the very opening of Krishna's teaching, now reiterated from the depths of the Universal Form — but now Arjuna knows who is commanding him. 'Mayaiva ete nihatāḥ pūrvam eva' — these warriors have already been slain by Me, even before this moment: in the dimension of Kāla (cosmic Time), what is determined has already occurred; the physical battle is only the sequential unfolding of what exists timelessly in the absolute. This is the metaphysical resolution of Arjuna's original grief: he feared he would be the killer. Krishna reveals: the slaying is Mine; you are not the killer, only the occasion. 'Nimittamātram bhava' — be only the instrument: not an agent acting for personal outcome, but a transparent vehicle of the Divine will. 'Savyasācin' — the archer who draws the bowstring with his left hand as fluently as with his right — Arjuna's special epithet, meaning complete mastery, the perfected instrument.

11:34.text: Drona, Bhīṣma, Jayadratha, Karṇa, and the other great warrior-heroes — already slain by Me — do you slay them. Be not distressed. Fight! You shall conquer your enemies in battle.
11:34.transliteration:
droṇaṃ ca bhīṣmaṃ ca jayadrathaṃ ca
karṇaṃ tathānyān api yodhavīrān |
mayā hatāṃs tvaṃ jahi mā vyathiṣṭhā
yudhyasva jetāsi raṇe sapatnān ||34||
11:34.devanagari:
द्रोणं च भीष्मं च जयद्रथं च
कर्णं तथान्यानपि योधवीरान् ।
मया हतांस्त्वं जहि मा व्यथिष्ठा
युध्यस्व जेतासि रणे सपत्नान् ॥ ३४ ॥
11:34.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:34.explanation: Four names that carry the full weight of Arjuna's original grief from Chapter 1 — now re-encountered at the summit of the Universal Form vision. Droṇa — his revered teacher, the master archer whose death he dreaded most; Bhīṣma — the grandsire, who held the boon of choosing his own death-moment, seemingly unkillable; Jayadratha — the Sindhu king, the chief conspirator in Abhimanyu's slaying, whom Arjuna had sworn to kill before sunset; Karṇa — Arjuna's greatest rival, whose true identity as his eldest brother he does not yet know. 'Mayā hatān' — already killed by Me: the divine act precedes the human act; the physical killing is only the temporal echo of a metaphysical event already accomplished in the fullness of Kāla. 'Mā vyathiṣṭhāḥ' — do not grieve: the specific word 'vyathā' (distress, anguish) is the same root used to describe Arjuna's condition in 1.28 — the very affliction that opened the Gita is here directly healed by the vision of cosmic Truth. 'Jetāsi' — you shall conquer: not a hope but a declaration from Time itself.

11:35.text: Sañjaya said: Having heard this utterance of Keśava, the crowned one (Kirīṭī, Arjuna) — trembling, hands folded in reverence — bowed to Kṛṣṇa, and once more prostrating himself in great fear, spoke with a voice choked with emotion —
11:35.transliteration:
sañjaya uvāca |
etac chrutvā vacanaṃ keśavasya
kṛtāñjalir vepamānaḥ kirīṭī |
namaskṛtvā bhūya evāha kṛṣṇaṃ
sagadgadaṃ bhītabhītaḥ praṇamya ||35||
11:35.devanagari:
सञ्जय उवाच ।
एतच्छ्रुत्वा वचनं केशवस्य
कृताञ्जलिर्वेपमानः किरीटी ।
नमस्कृत्वा भूय एवाह कृष्णं
सगद्गदं भीतभीतः प्रणम्य ॥ ३५ ॥
11:35.speaker: Sañjaya
11:35.explanation: Sañjaya's narrative art reaches its most refined in this transitional verse — a single verse that gives us five simultaneous physical and emotional states of Arjuna. 'Kṛtāñjaliḥ' — hands joined in the gesture of reverence, the body's spontaneous expression of the soul recognizing the Sacred. 'Vepamānaḥ' — trembling: not merely nervous but shaken at the cellular level by the vision of the Absolute. 'Kirīṭī' — the crowned one: Arjuna's divine epithet given by Indra, a reminder that this trembling figure is the greatest warrior of his age — yet even he is undone by the Viśvarūpa. 'Namaskṛtvā' and 'praṇamya' — two separate acts of bowing, as though one prostration were insufficient for what he has witnessed. 'Bhīta-bhītaḥ' — the doubled word for fear, indicating extreme, overwhelmed terror. 'Sagadgadaṃ' — in a voice broken with emotion, choked by bhāva (the surge of inner feeling that breaks through the instrument of speech). Sañjaya gives us not a report but a painting — he renders the moment in its full sensory and emotional texture before Arjuna speaks.

11:36.text: Arjuna said: O Hṛṣīkeśa (master of the senses), it is right and fitting that the world is delighted and becomes deeply enamored at the singing of Your glory. The rākṣasas, terror-stricken, flee in all directions, while all the assemblies of siddhas (perfected beings) bow to You.
11:36.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
sthāne hṛṣīkeśa tava prakīrtyā
jagat prahṛṣyaty anurajyate ca |
rakṣāṃsi bhītāni diśo dravanti
sarve namasyanti ca siddhasaṃghāḥ ||36||
11:36.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
स्थाने हृषीकेश तव प्रकीर्त्या
जगत्प्रहृष्यत्यनुरज्यते च ।
रक्षांसि भीतानि दिशो द्रवन्ति
सर्वे नमस्यन्ति च सिद्धसंघाः ॥ ३६ ॥
11:36.speaker: Arjuna
11:36.explanation: Verses 36 through 44 constitute Arjuna's great hymn to the Universal Form — the most sublime poetry of the Gita and one of the highest moments in Sanskrit devotional literature. 'Sthāne' — it is fitting, it is right: Arjuna's recognition that the world's joy in God is not mere sentiment but the natural response of creation to its own source, the way a magnet drawn to its pole 'rejoices.' 'Hṛṣīkeśa' — master of all senses: the one whose awareness pervades and governs every faculty of perception in every being. 'Tava prakīrtyā' — by the singing of Your glory: even the recollection of the Divine name stirs the world into joy. 'Jagat prahṛṣyati' — the world is delighted: creation recognizes its own ground and responds with ananda (bliss). 'Rakṣāṃsi bhītāni diśo dravanti' — the demons flee in all directions: darkness, when the absolute light appears, has nowhere to remain. 'Siddhasaṃghāḥ namasyanti' — the perfected ones bow: those who have attained the highest states of realization are precisely those most aware of the Infinite's greatness, and they bow most deeply.

11:37.text: O great soul (Mahātman), why should they not bow to You — who are greater even than Brahmā, the original creator? O Infinite (Ananta)! O Lord of the gods (Deveśa)! O Abode of the universe (Jagannivāsa)! You are that imperishable (Akṣara) which is beyond both sat (being) and asat (non-being) — that supreme Reality.
11:37.transliteration:
kasmāc ca te na nameran mahātman
garīyase brahmaṇo 'py ādikartre |
ananta deveśa jagannivāsa
tvam akṣaraṃ sadasat tat paraṃ yat ||37||
11:37.devanagari:
कस्माच्च ते न नमेरन्महात्मन्
गरीयसे ब्रह्मणोऽप्यादिकर्त्रे ।
अनन्त देवेश जगन्निवास
त्वमक्षरं सदसत्तत्परं यत् ॥ ३७ ॥
11:37.speaker: Arjuna
11:37.explanation: 'Garīyase brahmaṇo 'py ādikartre' — greater even than Brahmā, who is Himself the creator of all that is created: in the Vedantic framework, Brahmā is the first-born, the cosmic projector — yet Krishna is the ground from which even Brahmā emerges, thus transcending the highest created intelligence. Four names unfold in ascending depth: 'Ananta' — the Infinite, beyond all spatial and temporal limitation; 'Deveśa' — Lord of all divine beings, the sovereignty above all sovereigns; 'Jagannivāsa' — the dwelling-place of the universe, the Infinite Space within which all worlds are as temporary configurations; 'Akṣaram' — the Imperishable, the unchanging constant beneath all that changes, identical with the Akṣara Brahman of Chapter 8. 'Sadasat tat paraṃ yat' — that which is beyond both sat (the manifest, the existent) and asat (the unmanifest, the potential): this is Brahman's absolute transcendence, beyond even the categories of being and non-being — the Silence that precedes and succeeds all sound.

11:38.text: You are the first God (Ādideva), the ancient Person (Purāṇa-Puruṣa); You are the supreme resting place (paraṃ nidhānam) of this universe. You are the Knower, the Knowable, and the Supreme Abode. O Infinite-formed (Ananta-rūpa), by You is this universe pervaded.
11:38.transliteration:
tvam ādidevah puruṣah purāṇas
tvam asya viśvasya paraṃ nidhānam |
vettāsi vedyaṃ ca paraṃ ca dhāma
tvayā tataṃ viśvam anantarūpa ||38||
11:38.devanagari:
त्वमादिदेवः पुरुषः पुराण-
स्त्वमस्य विश्वस्य परं निधानम् ।
वेत्तासि वेद्यं च परं च धाम
त्वया ततं विश्वमनन्तरूप ॥ ३८ ॥
11:38.speaker: Arjuna
11:38.explanation: 'Ādidevah' — the first God, the God before the gods: anteceding even the highest cosmic intelligences. 'Purāṇaḥ puruṣaḥ' — the ancient Person: direct evocation of the Upaniṣadic 'purāṇaḥ puruṣaḥ' (the eternal, primordial Person) found in Kaṭha and Śvetāśvatara, the same Being toward whom Vedantic inquiry ultimately points. 'Paraṃ nidhānam asya viśvasya' — the supreme resting place of the whole universe: in 9.18 Krishna himself named 'nidhānam' as one of his attributes; Arjuna, having directly witnessed the Universal Form, now confirms this from experience rather than just from hearing. 'Vettāsi vedyaṃ ca' — the most epistemologically radical claim: You are simultaneously the Knower (subject), the Known (object), and presumably the knowing itself — the entire epistemic triad collapses into One. 'Paraṃ ca dhāma' — the Supreme Abode, mokṣa's home, the final destination. 'Anantarūpa' — of infinite forms: not describing a characteristic but naming a direct perception — Arjuna has witnessed this infinity and speaks from it.

11:39.text: You are Vāyu (wind), Yama (death), Agni (fire), Varuṇa (water), Śaśāṅka (moon), Prajāpati (Brahmā), and even the father of Brahmā (prapitāmaha). Salutation to You! Salutation a thousand times! And again — salutation upon salutation to You!
11:39.transliteration:
vāyur yamo 'gnir varuṇaḥ śaśāṅkaḥ
prajāpatis tvaṃ prapitāmahaś ca |
namo namas te 'stu sahasrakṛtvaḥ
punaś ca bhūyo 'pi namo namas te ||39||
11:39.devanagari:
वायुर्यमोऽग्निर्वरुणः शशाङ्कः
प्रजापतिस्त्वं प्रपितामहश्च ।
नमो नमस्तेऽस्तु सहस्रकृत्वः
पुनश्च भूयोऽपि नमो नमस्ते ॥ ३९ ॥
11:39.speaker: Arjuna
11:39.explanation: Arjuna names the great directional and cosmic deities and sees each as a face of the One Universal Form. Vāyu — the wind-god, the cosmic life-breath; Yama — the lord of death and dharmic justice; Agni — the sacred fire, the mouth of the gods; Varuṇa — lord of cosmic order, water, and moral truth; Śaśāṅka — the moon, the governor of mind and tides; Prajāpati — Brahmā, lord of progeny and cosmic generation; Prapitāmaha — the father even of Brahmā: the Grandfather's Grandfather, the source that precedes even the first projector of creation. Every major deity of the Vedic-Purāṇic tradition is subsumed in the one Viśvarūpa. Then: 'Namo namaḥ sahasrakṛtvaḥ' — salutation a thousand times — and yet again, and again. The repetition is not rhetoric but honest recognition: language cannot contain what Arjuna is witnessing. A thousand prostrations are still inadequate before the Infinite. The very act of saying 'and yet again' acknowledges the inexhaustibility of the Divine and the finitude of human expression.

11:40.text: Salutation from the front and from behind! O All (Sarva), salutation from every side! O You of infinite valor and boundless might — You pervade everything, and therefore You are everything.
11:40.transliteration:
namaḥ purastād atha pṛṣṭhatas te
namo 'stu te sarvata eva sarva |
anantavīryāmitavikramas tvaṃ
sarvaṃ samāpnoṣi tato 'si sarvaḥ ||40||
11:40.devanagari:
नमः पुरस्तादथ पृष्ठतस्ते
नमोऽस्तु ते सर्वत एव सर्व ।
अनन्तवीर्यामितविक्रमस्त्वं
सर्वं समाप्नोषि ततोऽसि सर्वः ॥ ४० ॥
11:40.speaker: Arjuna
11:40.explanation: 'Namaḥ purastād atha pṛṣṭhataḥ' — salutation from the front and from behind: Arjuna turns in every direction to bow, recognizing that there is no direction that is 'outside' the Universal Form — all directions are within it. 'Sarvataḥ eva sarva' — from every side, O Sarva (the All): 'Sarva' is one of the most complete divine names — not a quality predicated of God but an identification of God with totality itself. 'Ananta-vīrya' — of infinite power: not limited power that can be exhausted, but the power that is the energetic ground of all energy in the universe. 'Amita-vikrama' — of unmeasured valor: beyond any scale of comparison. 'Sarvaṃ samāpnoṣi tato 'si sarvaḥ' — because You pervade everything, You are everything: the logical completion of the Vedantic insight. Pervasion is not the relationship of a part to a whole — the Infinite doesn't 'pervade' in the way water fills a jar; rather, the jar is only a modality of the water. Arjuna has directly lived 'sarvam khalv idaṃ brahma' — all of this is Brahman.

11:41.text: Whatever I have said rashly — treating You as a friend, without knowing this greatness of Yours — 'O Kṛṣṇa! O Yādava! O my friend!' — whether from carelessness or from love —
11:41.transliteration:
sakheti matvā prasabhaṃ yad uktaṃ
he kṛṣṇa he yādava he sakheti |
ajānatā mahimānaṃ tavedam
mayā pramādāt praṇayena vāpi ||41||
11:41.devanagari:
सखेति मत्वा प्रसभं यदुक्तं
हे कृष्ण हे यादव हे सखेति ।
अजानता महिमानं तवेदं
मया प्रमादात्प्रणयेन वापि ॥ ४१ ॥
11:41.speaker: Arjuna
11:41.explanation: Verses 41–42 together form Arjuna's apology — the most tender and humanly relatable passage in the Bhagavad Gita. Until this moment, Arjuna related to Krishna as a sakhā — a friend, a cousin, a trusted charioteer. He had called him 'Kṛṣṇa' casually, 'Yādava' familiarly, 'sakhā' with the easy intimacy of equals sharing a chariot and a life. The Universal Form has shattered that frame of reference irrevocably. 'Ajānatā mahimānam tava' — not knowing this greatness of Yours: the confession of ignorance is also a recognition — now he knows. 'Pramādāt' — from heedlessness, from carelessness: he was not malicious, only unaware. 'Praṇayena vāpi' — or from love: and here is the Gita's exquisite mercy — the impudence of love is different in kind from the impudence of arrogance. God does not punish the familiar speech of the lover; He receives it as a gift. This verse is incomplete in syntax — it flows into verse 42 for its resolution.

11:42.text: During our sports, our resting, our sitting, and our meals — alone or even in the presence of others — O Acyuta (the unfaltering)! — for whatever disrespect was shown through jesting or insult, I ask forgiveness of You, the Immeasurable (Aprameyam).
11:42.transliteration:
yac cāvahāsārtham asatkṛto 'si
vihāraśayyāsanabhojaheṣu |
eko 'thavāpy acyuta tat samakṣaṃ
tat kṣāmaye tvām aham aprameyam ||42||
11:42.devanagari:
यच्चावहासार्थमसत्कृतोऽसि
विहारशय्यासनभोजनेषु ।
एकोऽथवाप्यच्युत तत्समक्षं
तत्क्षामये त्वामहमप्रमेयम् ॥ ४२ ॥
11:42.speaker: Arjuna
11:42.explanation: The sentence that began in verse 41 completes here in one of the most intimately human moments in all sacred literature. Arjuna lists the four ordinary situations of shared life in which he treated Krishna as a peer rather than the Absolute: 'Vihāra' — during their walks and wanderings together; 'śayyā' — while resting, in the easy familiarity of shared repose; 'āsana' — sitting together in council; 'bhojana' — at meals, the most domestic and egalitarian of human contexts. In all these ordinary, intimate moments, Arjuna laughed with Krishna, joked at his expense, perhaps dismissed his words, perhaps spoke too freely. 'Eko 'thavāpy acyuta tat samakṣam' — whether this was in private between the two of them, or in front of others. 'Acyuta' — the Unfaltering, the one who never slips from his nature: even as Arjuna was casually irreverent, Krishna was constant. 'Aprameyam' — the Immeasurable: the very friend who sat beside him on the chariot is identical with what no measurement can capture. The gap between those two recognitions — friend on a chariot / the Immeasurable — is the entire Gita.

11:43.text: You are the Father of the world — of all that moves and is unmoving. You are the most venerable and the greatest Guru. In the three worlds there is none equal to You — then how could there be one greater, O You of incomparable glory (Apratimaprabhāva)?
11:43.transliteration:
pitāsi lokasya carācarasya
tvam asya pūjyaś ca gurur garīyān |
na tvatsamo 'sty abhyadhikaḥ kuto 'nyo
lokatraye 'py apratimaprabhāva ||43||
11:43.devanagari:
पितासि लोकस्य चराचरस्य
त्वमस्य पूज्यश्च गुरुर्गरीयान् ।
न त्वत्समोऽस्त्यभ्यधिकः कुतोऽन्यो
लोकत्रयेऽप्यप्रतिमप्रभाव ॥ ४३ ॥
11:43.speaker: Arjuna
11:43.explanation: After the apology of verses 41–42, Arjuna's hymn resumes — but now with a new kind of depth, the knowledge gained from having directly witnessed the Universal Form. 'Pitāsi lokasya carācarasya' — You are the Father of the entire world, of all that moves and stands still: in 9.17 Krishna himself declared 'pitāham asya jagataḥ'; now Arjuna confirms this not from scripture but from direct perception. 'Pūjyaś ca gurur garīyān' — the most venerable, the Guru above all gurus: not a teacher among teachers but the very source from which all true teaching flows. 'Na tvatsamo 'sty abhyadhikaḥ kuto 'nyaḥ' — there is nothing equal to You in the three worlds — how then could there be anything exceeding You? This is not hyperbole but ontological precision: the Infinite cannot have an equal because a second Infinite would be a contradiction. 'Apratimaprabhāva' — of incomparable power and glory: 'a-pratima' — without likeness, without analogy, without any valid comparison.

11:44.text: Therefore, laying my body down in prostration, I entreat You — the adorable Lord — for Your grace. O God, just as a father bears with his son, a friend with his friend, and a lover with a beloved — so You must bear with me.
11:44.transliteration:
tasmāt praṇamya praṇidhāya kāyaṃ
prasādaye tvām aham īśam īḍyam |
piteva putrasya sakheva sakhyuḥ
priyaḥ priyāyārhasi deva soḍhum ||44||
11:44.devanagari:
तस्मात्प्रणम्य प्रणिधाय कायं
प्रसादये त्वामहमीशमीड्यम् ।
पितेव पुत्रस्य सखेव सख्युः
प्रियः प्रियायार्हसि देव सोढुम् ॥ ४४ ॥
11:44.speaker: Arjuna
11:44.explanation: This verse is the emotional summit of Arjuna's hymn — at once a deep prostration and the most tender supplication in all the Gita. 'Praṇidhāya kāyam' — literally laying the body down: sāṣṭāṅga daṇḍavat (the full-body prostration where all eight limbs touch the earth).
