'Prasādaye' — I seek your prasāda (grace, favor, pleased countenance): the same root as the word for divine grace flowing downward, 'pleased.' Three analogies of forgiving love, each representing a distinct rasa (sacred emotional register) of devotion: (1) 'Piteva putrasya' — as a father bears with a son (vātsalya-rasa: parental love that is unconditional and endures childish behaviour); (2) 'Sakheva sakhyuḥ' — as a friend bears with a friend (sakhya-rasa: the love of equals, where all pretense is dropped); (3) 'Priyaḥ priyāyāḥ' — as a lover bears with the beloved (mādhurya-rasa: the most intimate, the love where familiarity itself is the offering). Arjuna has lived all three relationships with Krishna and now invokes all three as grounds for forgiveness.

11:45.text: Having seen what has never been seen before, I am exhilarated — and yet my mind is overwhelmed with fear. Show me that form of Yours as the Deva (the gentle divine form), O Lord of Gods (Deveśa), O Abode of the Universe (Jagannivāsa)! Be gracious.
11:45.transliteration:
adṛṣṭapūrvaṃ hṛṣito 'smi dṛṣṭvā
bhayena ca pravyathitaṃ mano me |
tad eva me darśaya devarūpaṃ
prasīda deveśa jagannivāsa ||45||
11:45.devanagari:
अदृष्टपूर्वं हृषितोऽस्मि दृष्ट्वा
भयेन च प्रव्यथितं मनो मे ।
तदेव मे दर्शय देवरूपं
प्रसीद देवेश जगन्निवास ॥ ४५ ॥
11:45.speaker: Arjuna
11:45.explanation: 'Adṛṣṭapūrvam' — never seen before: not by Arjuna, not by any human, not perhaps by any being in the history of creation except in this singular grace-event. 'Hṛṣito 'smi' — I am thrilled, exhilarated: the witness of the Absolute experiences bliss. 'Bhayena ca pravyathitam mano me' — and yet my mind is overwhelmed with fear: simultaneously. This co-presence of rapture and terror is the precise phenomenology of encounter with the numinous — what Rudolf Otto called 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans' — that which simultaneously terrifies and magnetically draws. The Taittirīya Upaniṣad calls this 'bhīṣā' (from which all things tremble) combined with the bliss of Brahman. 'Tad eva me darśaya devarūpam' — show me that form of yours as the Deva: crucially, Arjuna is not asking for the Universal Form to return — he has seen it, been broken by it, and now asks for the gentle, divine form of Krishna he knows. The devotee who has seen the Infinite asks for the Personal. This is the turning point of Chapter 11.

11:46.text: O Thousand-armed (Sahasrabāho), O Cosmic Form (Viśvamūrte) — I wish to see You just as You were before: with the crown (kirīṭa), holding the mace (gadā), with the discus (cakra) in hand. Please appear in that four-armed form.
11:46.transliteration:
kirīṭinaṃ gadinaṃ cakrahasta-
micchāmi tvāṃ draṣṭum ahaṃ tathaiva |
tenaiva rūpeṇa caturbhujena
sahasrabāho bhava viśvamūrte ||46||
11:46.devanagari:
किरीटिनं गदिनं चक्रहस्त-
मिच्छामि त्वां द्रष्टुमहं तथैव ।
तेनैव रूपेण चतुर्भुजेन
सहस्रबाहो भव विश्वमूर्ते ॥ ४६ ॥
11:46.speaker: Arjuna
11:46.explanation: This verse discloses one of the Gita's deepest insights about devotion. Arjuna has witnessed the Universal Form — the thousand-armed, all-consuming, boundless cosmic reality. And his request is: please come back as the four-armed Viṣṇu-Kṛṣṇa. The three specific identifying marks of the gentle form: 'Kirīṭinam' — wearing the divine crown; 'gadinam' — bearing the mace (symbol of sovereign power and justice); 'cakrahastam' — holding the Sudarśana discus (symbol of the cosmic mind's discrimination). 'Caturbhujena rūpeṇa' — the four-armed form: the majestic yet approachable form of Viṣṇu that sages have meditated on for millennia. The profundity: Arjuna has seen the Viśvarūpa and chooses the personal. He is not retreating from knowledge; he is moving from knowledge to love. The Absolute has been perceived — now the heart asks for the presence that can be loved, spoken to, embraced. This is the Gita's answer to the question: 'If Brahman is ultimately formless, why devotion to form?' Because the devotee's love needs a face.

11:47.text: The Blessed Lord said: O Arjuna, by My grace and through My own divine power (ātmayogāt), I have shown you this supreme, luminous, universal, infinite, and primordial Form — which has never been seen before by anyone other than you.
11:47.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
mayā prasannena tavārjunedam
rūpaṃ paraṃ darśitam ātmayogāt |
tejomayaṃ viśvam anantam ādyaṃ
yan me tvadanyena na dṛṣṭapūrvam ||47||
11:47.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
मया प्रसन्नेन तवार्जुनेदं
रूपं परं दर्शितमात्मयोगात् ।
तेजोमयं विश्वमनन्तमाद्यं
यन्मे त्वदन्येन न दृष्टपूर्वम् ॥ ४७ ॥
11:47.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:47.explanation: 'Prasannena' — by My grace, in a state of delight: this opening word answers Arjuna's plea of 'prasīda' (be gracious) in verse 44. The Universal Form was not a display of cosmic power but an act of love — the Divine revealing Itself out of affection for the devotee. 'Ātmayogāt' — through My own intrinsic divine power: the Viśvarūpa was not produced by any external method, ritual, or technique; it manifested purely from Krishna's own sovereign will and inner creative force. 'Tejomayam' — composed entirely of radiance; 'viśvam' — all-encompassing; 'anantam' — infinite; 'ādyam' — the primordial, the original Form that precedes all other forms. 'Tvadanyena na dṛṣṭapūrvam' — never seen before by anyone other than you: the absolute rarity of the grace being bestowed. This is the Gita's statement of the uniqueness of Arjuna's position — not out of favoritism but because the perfect convergence of qualification, occasion, and divine will made this moment singular in all of history.

11:48.text: O best of the Kurus (Kurupravīra), this form of Mine cannot be seen in the human world by anyone other than you — not through study of the Vedas, not through sacrifice, not through charity, not through ritual acts, and not through fierce austerities.
11:48.transliteration:
na vedayajñādhyayanair na dānair
na ca kriyābhir na tapobhir ugraiḥ |
evaṃrūpaḥ śakya ahaṃ nṛloke
draṣṭuṃ tvadanyena kurupravīra ||48||
11:48.devanagari:
न वेदयज्ञाध्ययनैर्न दानै-
र्न च क्रियाभिर्न तपोभिरुग्रैः ।
एवंरूपः शक्य अहं नृलोके
द्रष्टुं त्वदन्येन कुरुप्रवीर ॥ ४८ ॥
11:48.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:48.explanation: Five traditional pathways of spiritual accomplishment — and all five are declared insufficient for this particular vision. 'Vedayajñādhyayanaiḥ' — study of the Vedas, participation in sacrificial rites: the path of śruti (revealed scripture) and karma-kāṇḍa (ritual section). 'Dānaiḥ' — charitable giving, the path of dāna as accumulated merit. 'Kriyābhiḥ' — ritual acts, the path of prescribed religious actions. 'Tapobhiḥ ugraiḥ' — fierce austerities: the path of tapas, including fasting, self-mortification, and yogic discipline of extreme intensity. All of these are honored paths — yet none of them can produce the Viśvarūpa-darśana. Why? Because this vision is not a technique's product but grace's gift. The Viśvarūpa cannot be 'achieved' by any amount of human effort — it can only be received. This is the decisive distinction between karma-yoga, jñāna-yoga, and the grace-oriented bhakti that Krishna will now foreground. 'Tvadanyena' — by no one other than you: underscoring again the singular nature of this moment.

11:49.text: Do not be distressed, and do not be bewildered, having seen this terrible form of Mine. Free from fear, with a gladdened heart — see once again this (gentle) form of Mine.
11:49.transliteration:
mā te vyathā mā ca vimūḍhabhāvo
dṛṣṭvā rūpaṃ ghoram īdṛṅ mamedam |
vyapetabhīḥ prītamanāḥ punas tvaṃ
tad eva me rūpam idaṃ prapaśya ||49||
11:49.devanagari:
मा ते व्यथा मा च विमूढभावो
दृष्ट्वा रूपं घोरमीदृङ्ममेदम् ।
व्यपेतभीः प्रीतमनाः पुनस्त्वं
तदेव मे रूपमिदं प्रपश्य ॥ ४९ ॥
11:49.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:49.explanation: Krishna responds to Arjuna's plea of verses 45–46 with the most consoling words in the chapter. 'Mā te vyathā' — let there be no distress for you: the same root 'vyathā' (anguish) that began Arjuna's crisis in Chapter 1 (1.28) and was addressed in verse 34 — here it is addressed from the very depth of the cosmic revelation, in a voice of unmistakable tenderness. 'Mā vimūḍhabhāvaḥ' — let there be no bewilderment: the Viśvarūpa had not been designed to overwhelm but to liberate; the bewilderment is the appropriate response to finitude encountering the Infinite, and Krishna now dissolves it. 'Vyapetabhīḥ' — free from all fear. 'Prītamanāḥ' — with a heart filled with love, not terror: the relationship between the devotee and the Divine is ultimately that of love, not of trembling before a cosmic power. 'Punas tvaṃ tad eva me rūpam idaṃ prapaśya' — see once again this, My form: the gentle, four-armed, crowned, smiling Kṛṣṇa. The journey is complete: from person → cosmic vision → return to person, now known to be infinite.

11:50.text: Sañjaya said: Having thus spoken to Arjuna, Vāsudeva revealed His own (gentle) form once again. The great soul (Mahātmā), reassuming His gentle human form, reassured the frightened Arjuna.
11:50.transliteration:
sañjaya uvāca |
ity arjunaṃ vāsudevas tathoktā
svakaṃ rūpaṃ darśayām āsa bhūyaḥ |
āśvāsayām āsa ca bhītam enaṃ
bhūtvā punaḥ saumyavapurmahātmā ||50||
11:50.devanagari:
सञ्जय उवाच ।
इत्यर्जुनं वासुदेवस्तथोक्त्वा
स्वकं रूपं दर्शयामास भूयः ।
आश्वासयामास च भीतमेनं
भूत्वा पुनः सौम्यवपुर्महात्मा ॥ ५० ॥
11:50.speaker: Sañjaya
11:50.explanation: Sañjaya now resumes his role as the narrator — and this transitional verse witnesses one of the most extraordinary moments in all scripture: the Infinite folding itself back into the finite, the Cosmic contracting into the Personal. 'Vāsudevaḥ' — the son of Vasudeva: this human-lineage name is deliberately chosen. After the vast sweep of the Universal Form, the return to this intimate name signals the restoration of the human relationship. 'Svakam rūpam darśayām āsa' — showed His own form: 'svakam' (own, characteristic, familiar) — the form that is most naturally His own in this context, the beloved four-armed or two-armed form that Arjuna knows and loves. 'Saumyavapuḥ' — of gentle, gracious appearance: the divine form that invites love rather than terror, the face that can be looked at. 'Āśvāsayāmāsa bhītam' — He reassured the frightened one: Arjuna was still shaking; Krishna's response is not philosophy but care — the immediate act of comfort.

11:51.text: Arjuna said: O Janārdana, seeing this gentle human form of Yours, I have now regained my composure and am returned to my own natural state (prakṛtim gataḥ).
11:51.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
dṛṣṭvedam mānuṣaṃ rūpaṃ tava saumyaṃ janārdana |
idānīm asmi saṃvṛttaḥ sacetāḥ prakṛtiṃ gataḥ ||51||
11:51.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
दृष्ट्वेदं मानुषं रूपं तव सौम्यं जनार्दन ।
इदानीमस्मि संवृत्तः सचेताः प्रकृतिं गतः ॥ ५१ ॥
11:51.speaker: Arjuna
11:51.explanation: 'Mānuṣam rūpam' — this human form: remarkably, Arjuna calls Krishna's gentle two-or-four-armed form 'human' (mānuṣam) — highlighting that after the immensity of the Viśvarūpa, even the divine four-armed Viṣṇu-form is felt as intimate, accessible, proportionate to the human scale of love. 'Saumyam' — gentle, gracious: the form that invites proximity rather than awe. 'Sacetāḥ' — restored to full consciousness: the Viśvarūpa had overwhelmed Arjuna's ordinary cognitive faculties; the return of the gentle form restores them. 'Prakṛtiṃ gataḥ' — returned to my own nature: this is the most precise phrase. Arjuna's prakṛti (inherent nature) in this relationship is that of devotee-friend-student; the Universal Form had briefly displaced him from that ground. Now, with the familiar form before him, he is home again in the relationship. 'Janārdana' — the one who delights people and the world: perfectly chosen here, for Krishna has just delighted and restored Arjuna by resuming the form the heart knows.

11:52.text: The Blessed Lord said: This form of Mine that you have seen is extremely difficult to behold. Even the gods eternally long for the vision of this form.
11:52.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
suduḥdarśam idaṃ rūpaṃ dṛṣṭavān asi yan mama |
devā apy asya rūpasya nityaṃ darśanakāṅkṣiṇaḥ ||52||
11:52.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
सुदुर्दर्शमिदं रूपं दृष्टवानसि यन्मम ।
देवा अप्यस्य रूपस्य नित्यं दर्शनकाङ्क्षिणः ॥ ५२ ॥
11:52.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:52.explanation: A significant exegetical question arises here: which 'form' is 'extremely difficult to behold'? Commentators — Śaṅkarācārya, Rāmānuja, Madhvācārya — largely interpret this as referring to the four-armed or two-armed divine form of Viṣṇu-Kṛṣṇa (not the Viśvarūpa), consistent with the verse that follows (11.53) and verse 54's resolution. The point is that this intimate divine form — the gentle, gracious personal form of the Absolute — is itself rarer and more precious than even the cosmic display. 'Suduḥdarśam' — supremely difficult to see: the superlative degree of rarity. 'Devā apy' — even the gods: the great cosmic intelligences — Indra, Brahmā, the divine hosts who inhabit the highest planes of creation — even they yearn perpetually for this vision. 'Nityam darśanakāṅkṣiṇaḥ' — eternally longing for the sight of it. The profound irony: Arjuna, sitting on his chariot, was in the constant company of what heaven itself eternally strains to perceive. The ordinary was the extraordinary all along.

11:53.text: Not through the Vedas, not through austerity, not through charity, and not through sacrifice can I be seen in this manner as you have seen Me.
11:53.transliteration:
nāhaṃ vedair na tapasā na dānena na cejyayā |
śakya evaṃvidho draṣṭuṃ dṛṣṭavān asi māṃ yathā ||53||
11:53.devanagari:
नाहं वेदैर्न तपसा न दानेन न चेज्यया ।
शक्य एवंविधो द्रष्टुं दृष्टवानसि मां यथा ॥ ५३ ॥
11:53.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:53.explanation: An echo and intensification of verse 48 — but now the context is clearer: it is the intimate, personal divine form (not only the Viśvarūpa) that cannot be accessed through the four great traditional pathways. 'Vedaiḥ' — through Vedic knowledge alone: the path of scripture and jñāna. 'Tapasā' — through austerity: the path of physical and mental discipline, however intense. 'Dānena' — through charitable giving: the path of meritorious action. 'Ijyayā' — through ritual sacrifice: the path of karma-kāṇḍa. These four pillars of traditional religious life are honored but declared insufficient for this particular mode of vision — 'evaṃvidhaḥ draṣṭum' — to see in this manner, as you have seen. The 'this manner' is crucial: what is being distinguished is not ordinary religious experience but the direct, living, relational experience of the Personal Absolute — face to face, heart to heart. This requires something none of the four paths can supply alone. The answer comes in verse 54: ananya-bhakti.

11:54.text: But O Parantapa (Arjuna), by undivided devotion (ananyayā bhaktyā) alone can I be known in truth, seen thus, and entered into.
11:54.transliteration:
bhaktyā tv ananyayā śakya aham evaṃvidho 'rjuna |
jñātuṃ draṣṭuṃ ca tattvena praveṣṭuṃ ca parantapa ||54||
11:54.devanagari:
भक्त्या त्वनन्यया शक्य अहमेवंविधोऽर्जुन ।
ज्ञातुं द्रष्टुं च तत्त्वेन प्रवेष्टुं च परन्तप ॥ ५४ ॥
11:54.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:54.explanation: The 'tu' (but) at the opening is the pivot of the entire chapter and perhaps of the entire Gita: after listing all that is insufficient, the single sufficient means is declared. 'Bhaktyā ananyayā' — by devotion that is ananya (without any other, undivided, one-pointed): not bhakti supplemented by Vedic study, not bhakti combined with tapas as its foundation, but bhakti itself, complete and absolute in its orientation. Three progressive stages of the devotee's journey that only this bhakti can complete: (1) 'Jñātum tattvena' — to know Me in truth, not as an intellectual proposition but as the living reality of one's own deepest identity; (2) 'Draṣṭum' — to see Me, as Arjuna has just seen, directly and experientially; (3) 'Praveṣṭum' — to enter into Me — not merely to perceive the Infinite as an object but to merge with it, to realize the non-difference of the devotee and the Beloved. This is mokṣa. The arc: knowledge → vision → union. And the vehicle throughout: ananya-bhakti. This verse is Chapter 11's answer to Chapter 9's promise of 'yogakṣemaṃ vahāmy aham' — the means by which that promise is activated.

11:55.text: O Pāṇḍava, one who performs actions for My sake, who regards Me as the Supreme, who is devoted to Me, who is free from attachment, and who is free from enmity toward all beings — such a one attains Me.
11:55.transliteration:
matkarmakṛn matparamo madbhaktaḥ saṅgavarjitaḥ |
nirvairaḥ sarvabhūteṣu yaḥ sa mām eti pāṇḍava ||55||
11:55.devanagari:
मत्कर्मकृन्मत्परमो मद्भक्तः सङ्गवर्जितः ।
निर्वैरः सर्वभूतेषु यः स मामेति पाण्डव ॥ ५५ ॥
11:55.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
11:55.explanation: The final verse of the Eleventh Chapter — the chapter of the grandest cosmic vision in scripture — ends not in cosmic abstraction but in the most practical, livable description of the devotee. Five qualities that together constitute the complete portrait of the one who reaches the Divine: (1) 'Matkarmakṛt' — one who performs all actions for My sake: karma yoga at its fulfillment, where every act is an offering; (2) 'Matparamaḥ' — one who holds Me as the Supreme: the orientation of the will and the hierarchy of values, with the Divine at the apex; (3) 'Madbhaktaḥ' — My devotee: the direct, loving, personal relationship; (4) 'Saṅgavarjitaḥ' — free from attachment: to outcomes, to objects, to identities — the inner freedom that allows full outer engagement without bondage; (5) 'Nirvairaḥ sarvabhūteṣu' — free from enmity toward all beings: the expansive, all-inclusive love that sees the same Divine Self in every creature and therefore holds no creature as enemy. 'Sa mām eti' — that one comes to Me: not as a destination reached by effort but as the natural homecoming of a being who has become transparent to its own source. The Viśvarūpa-darśana-yoga ends where it must: in the heart of the devotee, at work in the world.

12:1.text: Arjuna said: Those devotees who are ever steadfast and worship You thus — and those who worship the Imperishable, the Unmanifest (akṣaram avyaktam) — which of these know yoga better?
12:1.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
evaṃ satata-yuktā ye bhaktās tvāṃ paryupāsate |
ye cāpy akṣaram avyaktaṃ teṣāṃ ke yoga-vittamāḥ ||1||
12:1.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
एवं सततयुक्ता ये भक्तास्त्वां पर्युपासते ।
ये चाप्यक्षरमव्यक्तं तेषां के योगवित्तमाः ॥ १ ॥
12:1.speaker: Arjuna
12:1.explanation: This question arises at the precise right moment — after the overwhelming vision of the Cosmic Form in Chapter 11. Arjuna has seen the infinite, terrifying, all-consuming universal Krishna, and now he asks the most practical question that vision generates: should I worship this personal form I know, or the impersonal Absolute that underlies it? 'Satata-yuktā' (ever steadfast, continuously united) — the quality of unwavering orientation that both paths demand. 'Akṣaram avyaktam' (the Imperishable Unmanifest) — the formless, attributeless Brahman of the Upaniṣadic tradition. 'Yoga-vittamāḥ' (those who know yoga best, the supreme masters of yoga) — the comparative is precise; Arjuna does not ask which path is correct, but which leads to the deepest knowing. This question is the hinge on which the entire Bhakti Yoga chapter turns.

12:2.text: The Blessed Lord said: Those who, fixing their mind on Me, worship Me, ever steadfast and endowed with supreme faith (śraddhayā parayā) — they, in My view, are the most united (yuktatamāḥ).
12:2.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
mayy āveśya mano ye māṃ nitya-yuktā upāsate |
śraddhayā parayopetāḥ te me yuktatamā matāḥ ||2||
12:2.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
मय्यावेश्य मनो ये मां नित्ययुक्ता उपासते ।
श्रद्धया परयोपेताः ते मे युक्ततमा मताः ॥ २ ॥
12:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:2.explanation: 'Mayy āveśya manaḥ' (having immersed the mind in Me) — 'āveśya' is a strong word: not merely directing attention but flooding, saturating the mind with the divine. This is not periodic remembrance but a quality of continuous absorption. 'Nitya-yuktāḥ' (ever steadfast, permanently united) — the devotion described is not circumstantial but a stable condition of being. 'Śraddhayā parayopetāḥ' (endowed with supreme faith) — 'paraya' (supreme, highest) qualifies the faith: not tentative belief but complete, unconditional trust. 'Yuktatamāḥ' (the most united, the best yogīs) — the superlative is unambiguous: Krishna declares the path of personal devotion to himself to be the highest form of yoga. The answer is immediate, personal, and decisive — a characteristic of genuine love: it does not equivocate.

12:3.text: But those who worship the Imperishable (akṣara), the Undefinable (anirdeśyam), the Unmanifest, the Omnipresent, the Inconceivable, the Immutable (kūṭa-stha), the Immovable, the Constant —
12:3.transliteration:
ye tv akṣaram anirdeśyam avyaktaṃ paryupāsate |
sarvatra-gam acintyaṃ ca kūṭa-stham acalaṃ dhruvam ||3||
12:3.devanagari:
ये त्वक्षरमनिर्देश्यमव्यक्तं पर्युपासते ।
सर्वत्रगमचिन्त्यं च कूटस्थमचलं ध्रुवम् ॥ ३ ॥
12:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:3.explanation: This verse and the next form one complete sentence. Here, rather than dismissing the path of the formless, Krishna describes it in eight increasingly precise attributes — each capturing a different dimension of the Absolute's transcendence. 'Anirdeśyam' (undefinable, that which cannot be pointed out) — it has no 'thisness,' no particular location, no describable feature. 'Avyaktam' (unmanifest) — beyond all sensory and perceptual access. 'Sarvatra-gam' (moving everywhere, omnipresent) — not localized anywhere because present everywhere. 'Acintyam' (inconceivable) — beyond the grasp of even the subtlest mental operations. 'Kūṭa-stham' (standing at the summit, immovably fixed) — the anvil on which all experience is struck but which itself is never struck. 'Acalam' (immovable) — unchanging in itself. 'Dhruvam' (the constant, the pole star of existence) — the eternal fixed point. This is the full portrait of nirguṇa Brahman.

12:4.text: Having thoroughly restrained all the senses, even-minded everywhere (sama-buddhayaḥ), devoted to the welfare of all beings (sarva-bhūta-hite ratāḥ) — they also reach Me alone.
12:4.transliteration:
sanniyamyendriya-grāmaṃ sarvatra sama-buddhayaḥ |
te prāpnuvanti mām eva sarva-bhūta-hite ratāḥ ||4||
12:4.devanagari:
सन्नियम्येन्द्रियग्रामं सर्वत्र समबुद्धयः ।
ते प्राप्नुवन्ति मामेव सर्वभूतहिते रताः ॥ ४ ॥
12:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:4.explanation: 'Te prāpnuvanti mām eva' (they also reach Me alone) — the 'also' (eva in the sense of inclusivity) is the key: those who worship the formless Absolute equally reach Krishna. The destination is one; the paths are two. Three conditions are required: 'sanniyamyendriya-grāmam' (having thoroughly restrained the entire group of senses) — a demanding prerequisite that makes the formless path harder; 'sarvatra sama-buddhayaḥ' (even-minded everywhere, equanimous in all circumstances) — seeing the same Brahman in all situations and beings; 'sarva-bhūta-hite ratāḥ' (devoted to the welfare of all beings) — universal compassion as the outward expression of the inward realization of unity. This last quality is remarkable: it makes the welfare of all beings a condition for the highest realization, grounding the most abstract path in the most concrete ethical commitment.

12:5.text: For those whose minds are attached to the Unmanifest, the difficulty (kleśa) is far greater; for the way of the Unmanifest is attained with difficulty by embodied beings.
12:5.transliteration:
kleśo 'dhikataras teṣām avyaktāsakta-cetasām |
avyaktā hi gatir duḥkhaṃ deha-vadbhir avāpyate ||5||
12:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:5.explanation: 'Kleśo 'dhikataras' (the suffering, the difficulty is greater) — Krishna's compassionate pragmatism: he does not condemn the path of the formless, but he does not conceal its difficulty either. 'Avyaktāsakta-cetasām' (for those whose minds are attached to the Unmanifest) — the effort required to hold an object-less object of meditation is vastly greater than holding a personal form. 'Deha-vadbhir avāpyate' (it is attained by the embodied with difficulty) — this is the crux: as long as one inhabits a body, sensory experience is the mind's natural medium. The formless has no handle, no image, no story, no relationship. The body-mind complex gravitates toward the concrete; forcing it toward the formless requires extraordinary sustained effort. This verse is not an argument against the nirguṇa path — it is an honest assessment of where most human beings actually stand.

12:6.text: But those who, having surrendered all actions to Me, holding Me as the Supreme, worship Me, meditating on Me with undivided yoga (ananyena yogena) —
12:6.transliteration:
ye tu sarvāṇi karmāṇi mayi saṃnyasya mat-parāḥ |
ananyenaiva yogena māṃ dhyāyanta upāsate ||6||
12:6.devanagari:
ये तु सर्वाणि कर्माणि मयि संन्यस्य मत्पराः ।
अनन्येनैव योगेन मां ध्यायन्त उपासते ॥ ६ ॥
12:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:6.explanation: This verse and the next form one complete sentence — a portrait of the devotee whom Krishna will specially protect. Three defining qualities: 'sarvāṇi karmāṇi mayi saṃnyasya' (having surrendered all actions in Me) — echoing 3.30's instruction, this is the complete offering of every act to the divine, leaving no action as one's own possession; 'mat-parāḥ' (holding Me as the Supreme, taking refuge in Me alone) — the devotee has no other ultimate priority, no competing supreme authority; 'ananyenaiva yogena' (with undivided yoga alone) — 'ananya' (not-other) is the hallmark quality of the highest devotee throughout the Gita: a mind that holds only Krishna. 'Dhyāyantaḥ upāsate' (meditating and worshipping) — the outer and inner forms of devotion working together.

12:7.text: O Pārtha, for those whose consciousness is absorbed in Me (mayy āveśita-cetasām), I become their swift deliverer (samuddhartā) from the ocean of death-bound existence (mṛtyu-saṃsāra-sāgarāt).
12:7.transliteration:
teṣām ahaṃ samuddhartā mṛtyu-saṃsāra-sāgarāt |
bhavāmi na cirāt pārtha mayy āveśita-cetasām ||7||
12:7.devanagari:
तेषामहं समुद्धर्ता मृत्युसंसारसागरात् ।
भवामि नचिरात्पार्थ मय्यावेशितचेतसाम् ॥ ७ ॥
12:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:7.explanation: 'Mṛtyu-saṃsāra-sāgarāt' — this three-word compound is one of the Gita's most haunting images: the 'ocean of the world of death,' the vast, dark sea of compelled birth and death that has no horizon the unaided swimmer can reach. 'Teṣām ahaṃ samuddhartā bhavāmi' (I become their deliverer) — Krishna takes personal responsibility. Not 'I will show the way' but 'I will lift them out.' 'Na cirāt' (not after a long time, swiftly) — the rescue is not delayed; the promise is urgent. 'Mayy āveśita-cetasām' (those whose consciousness is immersed in Me) — the single condition: the mind absorbed in Krishna. This verse contains the entire theology of grace in two lines: the devotee offers the mind; the Lord delivers the soul.

12:8.text: Fix your mind on Me alone; place your intellect in Me. Hereafter you shall dwell in Me alone — of this there is no doubt.
12:8.transliteration:
mayy eva mana ādhatsva mayi buddhiṃ niveśaya |
nivasisyasi mayy eva ata ūrdhvaṃ na saṃśayaḥ ||8||
12:8.devanagari:
मय्येव मन आधत्स्व मयि बुद्धिं निवेशय ।
निवसिष्यसि मय्येव अत ऊर्ध्वं न संशयः ॥ ८ ॥
12:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:8.explanation: 'Mayy eva mana ādhatsva' (fix the mind on Me alone) — the simplest possible instruction: place the mind in Krishna. Not a complex technique but a single direction. 'Mayi buddhiṃ niveśaya' (place the intellect in Me) — two faculties are addressed: manas (the emotive, associative mind) and buddhi (the discriminative, deciding intellect). Both are to be turned toward the divine. When both the emotional and the rational faculties orient themselves to Krishna, the whole person is unified in devotion. 'Nivasisyasi mayy eva' (you shall dwell in Me alone) — the result is assured: not 'you might' but 'you shall.' 'Ata ūrdhvam na saṃśayaḥ' (from this point forward, there is no doubt) — certainty is offered, not probability. This verse is the Gita's simplest and most complete instruction.

12:9.text: O Dhanañjaya, if you are unable to hold the mind steady in Me, then seek to attain Me through the yoga of practice (abhyāsa-yoga).
12:9.transliteration:
atha cittaṃ samādhātuṃ na śaknoṣi mayi sthiram |
abhyāsa-yogena tato mām icchāptuṃ dhanañjaya ||9||
12:9.devanagari:
अथ चित्तं समाधातुं न शक्नोषि मयि स्थिरम् ।
अभ्यासयोगेन ततो मामिच्छाप्तुं धनञ्जय ॥ ९ ॥
12:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:9.explanation: Krishna now offers a graduated ladder for the earnest but struggling seeker. 'Atha' (but if) — the conditional opens the compassionate alternative. The highest teaching of 12.8 (fix the mind directly in Me) is given first; now the fallback for those who cannot immediately achieve that direct absorption. 'Abhyāsa-yoga' (the yoga of repeated practice) — the systematic, disciplined, patient return of the wandering mind to its chosen divine object. Not a burst of spiritual effort but sustained, unglamorous practice: sit, remember, return, repeat. 'Mām icchāptum' (desiring to attain Me) — even the desire to reach Krishna is a valid and sufficient beginning. The phrase is remarkably gentle: even the aspiration, even the turning of one's wanting toward the divine, counts as a step. Krishna does not demand immediate perfection.

12:10.text: If you are even incapable of practice (abhyāsa), then be devoted to performing actions for My sake. Even performing actions for My sake, you shall attain perfection (siddhi).
12:10.transliteration:
abhyāse 'py asamartho 'si mat-karma-paramo bhava |
mad-artham api karmāṇi kurvan siddhim avāpsyasi ||10||
12:10.devanagari:
अभ्यासेऽप्यसमर्थोऽसि मत्कर्मपरमो भव ।
मदर्थमपि कर्माणि कुर्वन्सिद्धिमवाप्स्यसि ॥ १० ॥
12:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:10.explanation: The third rung of the compassionate ladder. 'Abhyāse 'py asamarthaḥ' (even incapable of practice) — the third concession, each one more accommodating than the last. For those who cannot sustain systematic meditation practice: 'mat-karma-paramah bhava' (be devoted to actions for My sake, consider My service the highest) — this is karma-yoga infused with bhakti. Every act of service — to the temple, to the community, to suffering beings, to one's family — performed with the awareness of serving Krishna, becomes a form of worship. 'Mad-artham api karmāṇi kurvan' (even performing actions for My sake) — the 'api' (even) is tender: even this, even just the orientation of one's actions toward the divine, is sufficient. 'Siddhim avāpsyasi' (you shall attain perfection) — the promise holds at every level.

12:11.text: If you are unable to do even this, then, taking refuge in My yoga, with a controlled self (yatātmavān), renounce the fruits of all actions.
12:11.transliteration:
athaitad apy aśakto 'si kartuṃ mad-yogam āśritaḥ |
sarva-karma-phala-tyāgaṃ tataḥ kuru yatātmavān ||11||
12:11.devanagari:
अथैतदप्यशक्तोऽसि कर्तुं मद्योगमाश्रितः ।
सर्वकर्मफलत्यागं ततः कुरु यतात्मवान् ॥ ११ ॥
12:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:11.explanation: The fourth and final rung — the most universally accessible. 'Athaitad apy aśaktaḥ' (if incapable even of this) — the concessions accumulate with extraordinary generosity: Krishna does not demand that every seeker start at the top. 'Mad-yogam āśritaḥ' (taking refuge in My yoga) — even this minimal practice is grounded in the divine; it is done with Krishna as the supporting context. 'Yatātmavān' (with a disciplined, controlled self) — whatever the level, inner self-governance is required. 'Sarva-karma-phala-tyāgam... kuru' (renounce the fruits of all actions) — this is karma-yoga's foundational practice: not the abandonment of action but the relinquishment of ownership over outcomes. This is the simplest possible practice, available to everyone in every circumstance. The ladder is complete: absorption → practice → service → fruit-renunciation.

12:12.text: Knowledge is better than practice (abhyāsa); meditation is superior to knowledge; renunciation of the fruits of action (karma-phala-tyāga) is superior to meditation; and from renunciation, peace follows immediately (anantaram).
12:12.transliteration:
śreyo hi jñānam abhyāsāj jñānād dhyānaṃ viśiṣyate |
dhyānāt karma-phala-tyāgas tyāgāc chāntir anantaram ||12||
12:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:12.explanation: 'Śreyo hi jñānam abhyāsāt' — mechanical practice without understanding is less than practice informed by real knowledge. 'Jñānād dhyānaṃ viśiṣyate' — even knowledge, if merely conceptual, is less than meditation, where knowledge is embodied and lived from the inside. 'Dhyānāt karma-phala-tyāgaḥ' — yet even meditation, as a specific practice, can be superseded by the constant orientation of fruit-renunciation, which operates in every action throughout the day. 'Tyāgāc chāntir anantaram' (from renunciation, peace immediately after) — this is the verse's startling payoff: the word 'anantaram' means without any interval, without delay, immediately. When the knot of fruit-craving is released, peace is not a future reward; it is the present state that was always there beneath the tension. Renunciation does not produce peace; it reveals it.

12:13.text: One who harbors no hatred toward any being (adveṣṭā sarva-bhūtānām), who is friendly (maitraḥ) and compassionate (karuṇaḥ), free from the sense of 'mine' (nirmamaḥ), free from ego (nirahaṃkāraḥ), equanimous in pain and pleasure (sama-duḥkha-sukhaḥ), and forgiving (kṣamī) —
12:13.transliteration:
adveṣṭā sarva-bhūtānāṃ maitraḥ karuṇa eva ca |
nirmamo nirahaṃkāraḥ sama-duḥkha-sukhaḥ kṣamī ||13||
12:13.devanagari:
अद्वेष्टा सर्वभूतानां मैत्रः करुण एव च ।
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः समदुःखसुखः क्षमी ॥ १३ ॥
12:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:13.explanation: With this verse, the Gita enters its most beloved passage — the extended portrait of the devotee who is dearest to Krishna (12.13–20). This verse and the next form one sentence, ending in 'sa me priyaḥ.' Seven qualities are enumerated in this verse: 'adveṣṭā sarva-bhūtānām' (harboring no hatred toward any being) — this is foundational: the devotee of the all-pervading divine cannot despise any expression of that divine. 'Maitraḥ' (friendly, one whose disposition is friendship) — active goodwill toward all. 'Karuṇaḥ' (compassionate) — moved by the suffering of others. 'Nirmamaḥ' (without sense of 'mine') — no possessive claim over anything. 'Nirahaṃkāraḥ' (without ego) — the false 'I' dissolved. 'Sama-duḥkha-sukhaḥ' (equanimous in pain and pleasure) — not driven by the hedonic calculus. 'Kṣamī' (forgiving, patient) — absorbing offense without retaliation.

12:14.text: Who is always content, a yogī, self-controlled (yatātmā), of firm conviction (dṛḍha-niścayaḥ), with mind and intellect offered to Me — that devotee of Mine is dear to Me (sa me priyaḥ).
12:14.transliteration:
santuṣṭaḥ satataṃ yogī yatātmā dṛḍha-niścayaḥ |
mayy arpita-mano-buddhir yo mad-bhaktaḥ sa me priyaḥ ||14||
12:14.devanagari:
सन्तुष्टः सततं योगी यतात्मा दृढनिश्चयः ।
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः ॥ १४ ॥
12:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:14.explanation: 'Sa me priyaḥ' — these three words are the emotional center of the entire chapter. The divine speaks as a person who has preferences, who has dearest ones, who is moved by qualities. Five more attributes complete the portrait from verse 13: 'santuṣṭaḥ satatam' (always content) — contentment that is not circumstance-dependent but a permanent inner condition; 'yogī' (one united with the divine); 'yatātmā' (self-governed, with the self under inner discipline); 'dṛḍha-niścayaḥ' (of firm resolution) — the unshakeable commitment of one who has chosen the path and will not be turned from it; 'mayy arpita-mano-buddhiḥ' (with mind and intellect surrendered to Me) — echoing 12.8, here it appears as a description of the devotee rather than an instruction to Arjuna. When all these qualities are present, this is the person the Lord calls dear.

12:15.text: One from whom the world is not distressed, and who is not distressed by the world; who is free from joy, envy, fear, and agitation (harṣāmarṣa-bhayodvegaiḥ) — that one is also dear to Me.
12:15.transliteration:
yasmān nodvijate loko lokān nodvijate ca yaḥ |
harṣāmarṣa-bhayodvegair mukto yaḥ sa ca me priyaḥ ||15||
12:15.devanagari:
यस्मान्नोद्विजते लोको लोकान्नोद्विजते च यः ।
हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैर्मुक्तो यः स च मे प्रियः ॥ १५ ॥
12:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:15.explanation: 'Yasmān nodvijate lokaḥ' (from whom the world is not distressed) — the first test faces outward: does this person's presence create anxiety, disturbance, or threat in others? The sage whose inner stillness has become so complete that they emanate only safety. 'Lokān nodvijate ca yaḥ' (who is also not distressed by the world) — the second test faces inward: is this person moved to distress by what others do? 'Harṣāmarṣa-bhayodvegaiḥ muktaḥ' (freed from elation, envy, fear, and agitation) — four reactive emotional states that keep most people perpetually unsettled: harṣa (excessive elation at pleasant events), amarṣa (jealousy, indignation), bhaya (fear), udvega (anxious agitation). Freedom from all four — not suppression but genuine absence. 'Sa ca me priyaḥ' — and this one too is dear to Me.

12:16.text: One who is without expectation (anapekṣaḥ), pure (śuciḥ), capable (dakṣaḥ), impartial (udāsīnaḥ), free from anguish (gata-vyathaḥ), who has abandoned all ego-driven undertakings (sarvārambha-parityāgī) — that devotee of Mine is dear to Me.
12:16.transliteration:
anapekṣaḥ śucir dakṣa udāsīno gata-vyathaḥ |
sarvārambha-parityāgī yo mad-bhaktaḥ sa me priyaḥ ||16||
12:16.devanagari:
अनपेक्षः शुचिर्दक्ष उदासीनो गतव्यथः ।
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः ॥ १६ ॥
12:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:16.explanation: Six more qualities define the beloved devotee: 'anapekṣaḥ' (without expectation, without dependency on outcomes or others) — the person who is genuinely self-sufficient in the ātman, needing nothing from anyone; 'śuciḥ' (pure) — in both outer conduct and inner intention; 'dakṣaḥ' (capable, skillful, adept) — an important quality: the devotee is not withdrawn and inept but fully competent in the world; 'udāsīnaḥ' (impartial, not taking sides unnecessarily) — not the absence of caring but the absence of partisan bias; 'gata-vyathaḥ' (from whom anguish has departed) — suffering has left its natural home in this person; 'sarvārambha-parityāgī' (one who has renounced all ego-driven enterprises) — 'ārambha' here refers specifically to initiatives born from ego-ambition, not to action itself. This person acts but does not initiate self-aggrandizing projects.

12:17.text: One who neither rejoices (na hṛṣyati) nor hates, neither grieves nor desires; who has renounced both the auspicious and the inauspicious — that devotee, full of bhakti (bhaktimān), is dear to Me.
12:17.transliteration:
yo na hṛṣyati na dveṣṭi na śocati na kāṅkṣati |
śubhāśubha-parityāgī bhaktimān yaḥ sa me priyaḥ ||17||
12:17.devanagari:
यो न हृष्यति न द्वेष्टि न शोचति न काङ्क्षति ।
शुभाशुभपरित्यागी भक्तिमान्यः स मे प्रियः ॥ १७ ॥
12:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:17.explanation: 'Yo na hṛṣyati na dveṣṭi na śocati na kāṅkṣati' — four negations that describe the freedom from the four fundamental reactive states: excessive elation (hṛṣyati), hatred (dveṣṭi), grief (śocati), and craving (kāṅkṣati). These four represent the poles of the emotional pendulum that govern the ordinary mind: it swings between pleasure (elation, craving) and pain (hatred, grief). The devotee described here has stepped off the pendulum. 'Śubhāśubha-parityāgī' (renouncer of both the auspicious and the inauspicious) — this is subtler than simply avoiding evil: even attachment to what is good, virtuous, or meritorious is released. Not that the devotee does evil — but they are not bound even to goodness as a personal possession. 'Bhaktimān' — the beautiful word that closes the description: all these qualities flow from bhakti, not from willpower.

12:18.text: One who is equal to enemy and friend, equal in honor and dishonor; equal in cold, heat, pleasure and pain; free from attachment (saṅga-vivarjitaḥ) —
12:18.transliteration:
samaḥ śatrau ca mitre ca tathā mānāpamānayoḥ |
śītoṣṇa-sukha-duḥkheṣu samaḥ saṅga-vivarjitaḥ ||18||
12:18.devanagari:
समः शत्रौ च मित्रे च तथा मानापमानयोः ।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु समः सङ्गविवर्जितः ॥ १८ ॥
12:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:18.explanation: This verse and the next form one complete sentence. Four dimensions of equanimity (samatā): 'samaḥ śatrau ca mitre ca' (equal toward enemy and friend) — not the absence of relationship but the absence of differential valuation of beings based on their relation to one's ego. The enemy receives the same fundamental goodwill as the friend because both are expressions of the same Brahman. 'Tathā mānāpamānayoḥ' (equal in honor and dishonor) — the social valuation that others place on us is not internalized as the basis of self-worth. 'Śītoṣṇa-sukha-duḥkheṣu samaḥ' (equal in cold and heat, in pleasure and pain) — equanimity extends from the existential to the sensory; neither physical discomfort nor comfort disturbs the inner state. 'Saṅga-vivarjitaḥ' (free from attachment) — the absence of the sticky clinging that turns every pleasant experience into a craving and every unpleasant one into an aversion.

12:19.text: Equal in praise and blame (tulya-nindā-stutiḥ), silent (maunī), content with whatever comes (santuṣṭo yena kenacit), homeless (aniketaḥ), of steady mind (sthira-matiḥ), full of devotion — that person is dear to Me.
12:19.transliteration:
tulya-nindā-stutir maunī santuṣṭo yena kenacit |
aniketaḥ sthira-matir bhaktimān me priyo naraḥ ||19||
12:19.devanagari:
तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिर्मौनी सन्तुष्टो येन केनचित् ।
अनिकेतः स्थिरमतिर्भक्तिमान्मे प्रियो नरः ॥ १९ ॥
12:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:19.explanation: 'Tulya-nindā-stutiḥ' (equal in blame and praise) — perhaps the most practically demanding quality on the entire list: to remain genuinely unaffected by both criticism and flattery is the mark of a self that draws its identity from the ātman rather than from social validation. 'Maunī' (the silent one) — silence here encompasses both outer restraint of speech and inner quietness; words are offered only when necessary and purposeful. 'Santuṣṭo yena kenacit' (content with whatever comes) — the antithesis of consumer desire; this person receives each situation as exactly sufficient. 'Aniketaḥ' (without a fixed home, homeless) — not necessarily literally without a house but without attachment to any particular place as 'mine'; the whole world is equally home. 'Sthira-matiḥ' (of steady, unwavering wisdom) — the climactic quality: all the others rest on this foundation of unshakeable inner clarity. 'Me priyo naraḥ' — this human being is dear to Me.

12:20.text: But those who with faith (śraddadhānāḥ), holding Me as the Supreme (mat-paramāḥ), worship this dharmic nectar (dharmyāmṛtam) as expounded — those devotees are exceedingly (atīva) dear to Me.
12:20.transliteration:
ye tu dharmyāmṛtam idaṃ yathoktaṃ paryupāsate |
śraddadhānā mat-paramā bhaktās te 'tīva me priyāḥ ||20||
12:20.devanagari:
ये तु धर्म्यामृतमिदं यथोक्तं पर्युपासते ।
श्रद्दधाना मत्परमा भक्तास्तेऽतीव मे प्रियाः ॥ २० ॥
12:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
12:20.explanation: 'Dharmyāmṛtam idaṃ' — these words name what the entire chapter has described: 'the nectar of dharma,' the immortalizing ambrosia of righteous devotion. Not a set of rules but a living substance — the qualities enumerated from verse 13 onward constitute an elixir that, when lived, transmutes ordinary human existence into something immortal. 'Yathoktam paryupāsate' (who worship as it has been taught, fully and precisely) — not selective adoption of convenient virtues but the whole portrait, lived completely. 'Śraddadhānā' (those who have faith) — śraddhā, the willing and receptive openness to the divine, is the vessel that holds the nectar. 'Mat-paramā' (holding Me as the Supreme) — the final, non-negotiable condition: Krishna as the ultimate reality, the highest value, the first and last priority. 'Atīva me priyāḥ' (exceedingly dear to Me) — the chapter closes not with a doctrinal statement but with a declaration of love. The Lord's last word to the devoted seeker is: you are dear to me. Exceedingly dear.

13:1.text: Arjuna said: O Keśava, I wish to know Prakṛti and Puruṣa, the Field and the Knower of the Field, and Knowledge and the Object of Knowledge. The Blessed Lord said: O son of Kuntī, this body is called the 'Field' (kṣetra). Those who know this say that the one who knows it is the 'Knower of the Field' (kṣetrajña).
13:1.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
prakṛtiṃ puruṣaṃ caiva kṣetraṃ kṣetrajñam eva ca |
etad veditum icchāmi jñānaṃ jñeyaṃ ca keśava ||
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
idaṃ śarīraṃ kaunteya kṣetram ity abhidhīyate |
etad yo vetti taṃ prāhuḥ kṣetrajña iti tadvidaḥ ||
13:1.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
प्रकृतिं पुरुषं चैव क्षेत्रं क्षेत्रज्ञमेव च ।
एतद्वेदितुमिच्छामि ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं च केशव ॥
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
इदं शरीरं कौन्तेय क्षेत्रमित्यभिधीयते ।
एतद्यो वेत्ति तं प्राहुः क्षेत्रज्ञ इति तद्विदः ॥
13:1.speaker: Arjuna / Śrī Bhagavān
13:1.explanation: This combined opening verse — Arjuna's philosophically precise question and Krishna's immediate, compressed response — together form the seed-statement of the entire chapter. Arjuna's question is one of the most mature he poses anywhere in the Gita: he has moved from the emotional collapse of Chapter 1 to asking, with philosophical precision, about six foundational concepts of Sāṃkhya-Vedānta in a single breath. The six paired concepts: 'Prakṛti' (the primordial material nature, the substratum of all matter and psychological phenomena); 'Puruṣa' (the conscious witnessing principle, the Self); 'kṣetra' (the field — the body-mind complex as the arena of experience); 'kṣetrajña' (the knower of the field — pure consciousness that illuminates the field without being part of it); 'jñānam' (knowledge — here meaning both the faculty and the ethical-psychological qualities that constitute true knowledge); and 'jñeyam' (the knowable — the ultimate object of all knowing, Brahman). Together these six concepts span the complete map of reality: the entire phenomenal world (kṣetra/prakṛti) and its transcendent witness (kṣetrajña/Puruṣa), the means of knowing (jñāna) and the supreme object (jñeya, Brahman). Krishna's response is characteristically economical: 'idaṃ śarīraṃ kṣetram ity abhidhīyate' — this body is called the kṣetra. The Sanskrit 'kṣetra' literally means an agricultural field: just as a farmer sows seeds and reaps the harvest, the jīva sows actions (karma) in this body-field and experiences their results. But as subsequent verses reveal, 'kṣetra' encompasses the entire psycho-physical complex — all 24 evolutes of Prakṛti. 'Etad yo vetti taṃ prāhuḥ kṣetrajña iti tadvidaḥ' — the one who knows this field, who stands apart from it as its witness and illuminator, is called the kṣetrajña (field-knower) by those who know truth. This foundational distinction — between the known object (field) and the knowing subject (field-knower) — is the cornerstone of the entire Vedāntic project. Identification with the field is saṃsāra; recognition of oneself as the kṣetrajña is liberation, simultaneous with that very recognition.

13:2.text: O Bhārata, know Me also as the Kṣetrajña in all fields. The knowledge of the field and the knower of the field — that, in My view, is (true) knowledge.
13:2.transliteration:
kṣetrajñaṃ cāpi māṃ viddhi sarva-kṣetreṣu bhārata |
kṣetra-kṣetrajñayor jñānaṃ yat taj jñānaṃ mataṃ mama ||2||
13:2.devanagari:
क्षेत्रज्ञं चापि मां विद्धि सर्वक्षेत्रेषु भारत ।
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञयोर्ज्ञानं यत्तज्ज्ञानं मतं मम ॥ २ ॥
13:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:2.explanation: 'Kṣetrajñaṃ cāpi māṃ viddhi sarva-kṣetreṣu' — know Me as the Kṣetrajña in ALL fields. This is one of the Gita's most audacious and philosophically consequential declarations, building directly on the preceding verse's introduction of the kṣetrajña concept. Where verse 1 established the individual-level distinction (this body is the field; the one who knows it is the field-knower), verse 2 immediately universalises it: the single kṣetrajña present in every body everywhere is Krishna himself — the Paramātman, the universal witnessing consciousness. 'Cāpi' (also, even) is the pivotal word: it signals the non-dual identification — the individual ātman in each body is, at the deepest level, non-different from the cosmic Paramātman. In every organism, in every sentient being across all worlds, the single awareness that makes experience possible is this one Brahman. 'Kṣetra-kṣetrajñayor jñānaṃ yat taj jñānaṃ mataṃ mama' — the knowledge of the distinction between field and field-knower is, in Krishna's considered view (mataṃ mama: this is My considered judgment), what constitutes genuine jñāna (knowledge). All other learning — information, worldly skill, even scriptural erudition — remains secondary. The one knowledge that liberates is viveka: the lived, felt, direct discrimination between the known (matter, body, mind — the entire kṣetra) and the knower (ātman, pure consciousness — the kṣetrajña). This verse is the chapter's thesis statement in a single phrase: know this distinction, and liberation follows necessarily.

13:3.text: Hear from Me in brief — what the kṣetra is and of what nature; what its modifications are, from what it arose; who that one (kṣetrajña) is, and what his power (prabhāva).
13:3.transliteration:
tat kṣetraṃ yac ca yādṛk ca yad-vikāri yataś ca yat |
sa ca yo yat-prabhāvaś ca tat samāsena me śṛṇu ||3||
13:3.devanagari:
तत्क्षेत्रं यच्च यादृक्च यद्विकारि यतश्च यत् ।
स च यो यत्प्रभावश्च तत्समासेन मे श्रृणु ॥ ३ ॥
13:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:3.explanation: This verse is a programmatic declaration — a structured preview of the entire chapter's subject matter, compressed into five distinct questions. 'Yat kṣetram' (what the kṣetra is — its definition and enumeration); 'yādṛk' (of what qualitative nature — its character as a field of modification and experience); 'yad-vikāri' (what its modifications are — how it perpetually transforms through birth, growth, disease, and death); 'yataś ca yat' (from what it arose — its causal derivation in primordial Prakṛti); 'sa ca yaḥ' (who the kṣetrajña is — the precise nature of the witnessing consciousness that knows the field); 'yat-prabhāvaḥ' (what his power is — how the kṣetrajña, by its mere presence, illuminates and animates the entire field). 'Samāsena' — in brief, in summary: given the vastness of this subject (spanning the entire cosmological scheme of Sāṃkhya and the metaphysics of Vedānta), Krishna will present the essential outline, not the encyclopaedic treatment. This pedagogical move — mapping the territory before entering it — reflects the classical Vedāntic teaching method (anubandha-catuṣṭaya: preliminary orientation before the main exposition). The student's mind is prepared and oriented before the technical philosophical content arrives.

13:4.text: This has been sung by the seers in many ways, separately in various hymns (chandas), and also in the reasoned and conclusive passages of the Brahma-sūtra.
13:4.transliteration:
ṛṣibhir bahudhā gītaṃ chandobhir vividhaiḥ pṛthak |
brahma-sūtra-padaiś caiva hetumadbhir viniścitaiḥ ||4||
13:4.devanagari:
ऋषिभिर्बहुधा गीतं छन्दोभिर्विविधैः पृथक् ।
ब्रह्मसूत्रपदैश्चैव हेतुमद्भिर्विनिश्चितैः ॥ ४ ॥
13:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:4.explanation: This verse performs a crucial rhetorical and philosophical move: it roots the impending teaching not in Krishna's personal revelation alone but in the entire weight of the Vedic-Upaniṣadic-Vedāntic tradition. 'Ṛṣibhir bahudhā gītam' — this knowledge has been sung by the seers (the Vedic ṛṣis) in many ways: in the four Vedas, the Upaniṣads, the Brāhmaṇas, the Āraṇyakas, and in the various Smṛtis. The word 'gītam' (sung, chanted) carries emotional and aesthetic weight — this knowledge is not merely intellectually transmitted but has been lived and celebrated as sacred song across millennia. 'Chandobhir vividhaiḥ pṛthak' — in diverse metrical forms, separately: the same truth appears through many poetic and prosaic vehicles — each illuminating a different facet of the field/field-knower distinction. 'Brahma-sūtra-padaiś caiva hetumadbhir viniścitaiḥ' — and also in the 'reasoned and definitive passages' (hetu-madbhiḥ = supported by logical reasoning; viniścitaiḥ = conclusively established, free from residual doubt) of the Brahma-sūtra composed by Bādarāyaṇa-Vyāsa. This mention of the Brahma-sūtra is historically significant: together with the Upaniṣads and the Bhagavad Gita itself, the Brahma-sūtra forms the 'prasthāna-trayī' (the triple canonical foundation) of all Vedāntic philosophical schools. Krishna is thus saying: what I am about to teach is not novel — it is the most ancient, most carefully reasoned, and most universally transmitted spiritual knowledge known to human civilization.

13:5.text: The five great elements, ego (ahaṃkāra), intellect (buddhi), and the unmanifest (avyakta); the ten senses and one (mind); and the five sense-objects —
13:5.transliteration:
mahā-bhūtāny ahaṃkāro buddhir avyaktam eva ca |
indriyāṇi daśaikaṃ ca pañca cendriya-gocarāḥ ||5||
13:5.devanagari:
महाभूतान्यहङ्कारो बुद्धिरव्यक्तमेव च ।
इन्द्रियाणि दशैकं च पञ्च चेन्द्रियगोचराः ॥ ५ ॥
13:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:5.explanation: This verse and the next together enumerate the twenty-four constituents of the kṣetra (field) — drawn directly from the Sāṃkhya cosmological analysis, which is the metaphysical backbone of this chapter. The enumeration moves from the grossest (mahābhūtas) to the most subtle (avyakta), tracing the field's entire range. This verse enumerates: (1) 'Mahābhūtāni' — the five great elements: earth (pṛthvī), water (āpaḥ), fire (agni), air (vāyu), ether (ākāśa) — the five gross building-blocks of all physical matter, from which the entire observable universe is composed. (2) 'Ahaṃkāraḥ' — the ego-principle: the faculty of self-individuation that generates the sense 'I am this particular entity, distinct from all others.' In Sāṃkhya, ahaṃkāra arises from buddhi and is the source of all subsequent differentiation into the mental and sensory faculties. (3) 'Buddhiḥ' — the intellect: the first and highest product of Prakṛti's unfolding, responsible for discrimination (viveka), decision, and the clearest form of cognition available within the field — the faculty that, when purified, becomes the instrument of liberation. (4) 'Avyaktam' — the unmanifest: primordial Prakṛti in its undifferentiated state — the causal ground from which all manifestation emerges; prior to all form, name, and distinction; the Sāṃkhya 'pradhāna.' (5) 'Indriyāṇi daśa' — the ten senses: five jñānendriyās (sight, hearing, smell, taste, touch) and five karmendriyās (speech, hands, feet, the generative organ, the eliminative organ). (6) 'Ekaṃ ca' — and one: the manas (mind), the coordinating, emotive faculty that links the five senses into unified experience. (7) 'Pañca indriya-gocarāḥ' — the five sense-objects: sound, touch, form, taste, smell. The verse ends mid-sentence, completed in the next verse.

13:6.text: Desire, aversion, pleasure, pain, the aggregate (body-complex), consciousness, and steadiness — this, with its modifications, is the kṣetra (field) described briefly.
13:6.transliteration:
icchā dveṣaḥ sukhaṃ duḥkhaṃ saṃghātaś cetanā dhṛtiḥ |
etat kṣetraṃ samāsena sa-vikāram udāhṛtam ||6||
13:6.devanagari:
इच्छा द्वेषः सुखं दुःखं सङ्घातश्चेतना धृतिः ।
एतत्क्षेत्रं समासेन सविकारमुदाहृतम् ॥ ६ ॥
13:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:6.explanation: This verse completes the enumeration of the kṣetra by listing seven further constituents — specifically the psychological and phenomenological dimensions of the field's inner life. These seven: (1) 'Icchā' — desire, the mind's reaching toward what it wants: the engine of all purposive activity; (2) 'Dveṣaḥ' — aversion, the mind's pushing away of what it dislikes — together, icchā and dveṣa are the twin energies that drive all saṃsāric motion; (3) 'Sukham' — pleasure, the subjective experience of joy as felt within the field; (4) 'Duḥkham' — pain, the subjective experience of sorrow; (5) 'Saṃghātaḥ' — the aggregate, the composite body: the assembled whole of the five elements organized into a living organism, the gross physical body as a unified system; (6) 'Cetanā' — consciousness as it appears to function within the field — the reflected awareness in the mind, not to be confused with the true self-luminous consciousness of the kṣetrajña, which is pure and unchanging. Cetanā here is the apparently sentient quality of the mind when the ātman's light shines through it, like a mirror reflecting the sun; (7) 'Dhṛtiḥ' — steadiness, the holding-power of the mind, the capacity to sustain intention, resolve, and attention over time. 'Sa-vikāram udāhṛtam' — described together with its modifications: vikāra is the defining characteristic of the entire kṣetra — it is always in flux, always becoming something other than it is. The kṣetrajña, by absolute contrast, admits of no modification.

13:7.text: Absence of pride (amānitva), absence of pretension (adambhitva), non-violence (ahiṃsā), patience (kṣānti), uprightness (ārjava), service to the teacher (ācāryopāsana), cleanliness (śauca), steadiness (sthairya), self-restraint (ātma-vinigraha) —
13:7.transliteration:
amānitvam adambhitvam ahiṃsā kṣāntir ārjavam |
ācāryopāsanaṃ śaucaṃ sthairyam ātma-vinigrahaḥ ||7||
13:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:7.explanation: With this verse begins one of the Gita's most practically ethical passages: the twenty qualities of jñāna (knowledge), spanning verses 7-11. Critically, Krishna is not listing the qualities of a realized person as the fruit of knowledge — he is defining jñāna ITSELF as consisting of these ethical-psychological qualities. Knowledge, in the Gita's understanding, is not merely intellectual information or cognitive clarity but a holistic quality of being that encompasses ethics, psychology, relationship, and inner orientation. The nine qualities in this verse: (1) 'Amānitvam' — freedom from the craving for honour and recognition: the ego's hunger for validation is one of the subtlest and most persistent obstacles to wisdom; (2) 'Adambhitvam' — freedom from pretension, hypocrisy, and the performance of virtue for social display: being exactly what one appears to be; (3) 'Ahiṃsā' — non-violence in thought, word, and deed: the foundational ethical principle from which all other ethics flows; (4) 'Kṣānti' — patience and forbearance: the capacity to endure provocation, insult, and hardship without retaliation or inner collapse, staying steady in the face of adversity; (5) 'Ārjavam' — uprightness, the integration of inner intention and outer expression, the absence of duplicity; (6) 'Ācāryopāsanam' — devoted, humble service to the teacher: the indispensable vehicle of authentic knowledge transmission across generations; (7) 'Śaucam' — purity of both body and mind, the outer and inner cleanliness that creates a fit vessel for wisdom; (8) 'Sthairyam' — steadiness, the mental stability that does not waver with the vicissitudes of circumstance; (9) 'Ātma-vinigrahaḥ' — self-restraint: mastery over the lower mind and senses, the ability to act from deliberate intention rather than reactive impulse.

13:8.text: Dispassion toward sense-objects (vairāgya); freedom from ego (anahaṃkāra); and the continuous perception of the evil inherent in birth, death, old age, disease, sorrow, and fault —
13:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:8.explanation: The catalogue of jñāna's qualities deepens further. (1) 'Indriyārtheṣu vairāgyam' — dispassion toward sense-objects: the progressive weakening of the mind's gravitational pull toward the objects of the five senses. This vairāgya is born of genuine understanding (viveka-pūrvaka-vairāgya) — the recognition that sense-pleasure is inherently transient, involves dependence on perishable objects, and inevitably ends in frustration or loss. It is not forcible suppression but the natural cooling of interest that comes from seeing clearly — like a child who loved a toy losing interest when they understand it is hollow. (2) 'Anahaṃkāraḥ' — freedom from ego: the dissolution of the false identification that says 'I am the doer, I am the body, I am my social persona.' Ahaṃkāra (the ego-faculty) is the root of all bondage because it superimposes a false identity onto the kṣetrajña, obscuring the witnessing consciousness with the fiction of a personal, separate self. (3) 'Janma-mṛtyu-jarā-vyādhi-duḥkha-doṣānudarśanam' — the continuous contemplation (anudarśana: seeing-alongside, keeping in constant view) of the inherent evil (doṣa) in: birth (janma), death (mṛtyu), old age (jarā), disease (vyādhi), sorrow (duḥkha), and the fundamental faultedness (doṣa) of conditioned existence. This is not pessimism — it is the clear-eyed seeing of reality that naturally generates vairāgya and motivates the pursuit of what is truly permanent. The practice of 'anudarśana' — sustained, recurrent contemplation of impermanence — is the experiential foundation of dispassion.

13:9.text: Non-attachment (asakti) and non-clinging (anabhiṣvaṅga) to son, wife, home, and so on; and constant evenness of mind in the arising of the desirable and the undesirable —
13:9.transliteration:
asaktiḥ anabhiṣvaṅgaḥ putra-dāra-gṛhādiṣu |
nityaṃ ca sama-cittatvam iṣṭāniṣṭopapattiṣu ||9||
13:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:9.explanation: Two further qualities of jñāna. (1) 'Asaktiḥ' and 'anabhiṣvaṅgaḥ' — these two Sanskrit words capture a carefully graduated psychology of non-attachment. 'Asakti' is the absence of gluing-attachment, the non-sticking of the mind to desired objects or relationships. 'Anabhiṣvaṅga' goes deeper: 'abhiṣvaṅga' means deep emotional over-identification, the total enmeshment of one's sense of self in another person or place — 'If I lose my son, I lose myself entirely.' The 'an-' prefix negates this existential over-identification: the jñānī can love deeply, warmly, and fully without this disintegrating dependency. Applied specifically to 'putra-dāra-gṛha' (son, wife, home) — the three classic anchor-points of conventional worldly identity in Indian social life — this teaching is not a call to emotional coldness or family abandonment but to a love that is free from possessiveness, fear, and existential dependence. (2) 'Nityaṃ sama-cittatvam iṣṭāniṣṭopapattiṣu' — constant (nityam) evenness of mind (sama-cittatva) in the arising (upapattiṣu: occurrence, arrival) of both the desired (iṣṭa) and the undesirable (aniṣṭa). This is the practical daily expression of equanimity: circumstances fluctuate constantly — gains and losses, successes and failures, pleasant and unpleasant events arrive and depart. The jñānī's inner balance is unshaken by these fluctuations because the inner recognizes that neither gain adds to the Self nor does loss diminish it.

13:10.text: Unwavering devotion to Me through exclusive yoga (ananya-yoga); resorting to solitary places; and distaste for the company of crowds —
13:10.transliteration:
mayi cānanya-yogena bhaktir avyabhicāriṇī |
vivikta-deśa-sevitvam aratir jana-saṃsadi ||10||
13:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:10.explanation: Three more qualities of jñāna, and here bhakti makes its significant entry into what has thus far been primarily an analytical-ethical catalogue. (1) 'Ananya-yogena bhaktir avyabhicāriṇī mayi' — unwavering devotion (bhakti) to Me through exclusive (ananya: no-other, undivided) yoga. 'Avyabhicāriṇī' literally means 'non-straying' (from 'vyabhicāra': to stray, to be faithless to a commitment) — a bhakti that does not waver, does not shift its orientation, does not mix or dilute its devotion with other competing objects of attachment. In the context of jñāna, this means that the highest form of knowledge is inseparable from devotion — the intellectual recognition of Brahman and the heart's love for the personal Divine are not competing paths but one integrated truth. True jñāna IS bhakti at its depth, and genuine bhakti IS jñāna in its heart. (2) 'Vivikta-deśa-sevitvam' — habitual resorting to solitary places: physical solitude is the environment most conducive to inner inquiry, sustained contemplation, and the deepening of meditative awareness. The word 'sevitvam' (the quality of serving, frequenting, attending to) suggests an active seeking of solitude, not mere passive aloneness. (3) 'Aratir jana-saṃsadi' — distaste for the company of worldly crowds: not misanthropy or antisocial aversion but the natural preference of the inquiring mind for meaningful over trivial engagement. Gatherings driven by gossip, competitive display, and empty entertainment are recognized as obstacles to the inward-turning attention required for wisdom.

13:11.text: Constancy in self-knowledge (adhyātma-jñāna-nityatva) and the perception of the purpose of true knowledge (tattva-jñānārtha-darśana) — this is declared to be knowledge (jñāna). What is contrary to this is ignorance (ajñāna).
13:11.transliteration:
adhyātma-jñāna-nityatvaṃ tattva-jñānārtha-darśanam |
etaj jñānam iti proktam ajñānaṃ yad ato 'nyathā ||11||
13:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:11.explanation: This verse completes the twenty-quality catalogue of jñāna and delivers a decisive definitional judgment. The final two qualities: (1) 'Adhyātma-jñāna-nityatvaṃ' — constancy and permanence in adhyātma-jñāna (self-knowledge, knowledge of the inner Self): not occasional inquiry or episodic interest but an unbroken, lifelong orientation toward the investigation of one's own true nature. 'Adhyātma' literally means 'pertaining to the Self' (adhi = over, concerning; ātma = Self) — the inward-turning gaze that never finally abandons the fundamental question 'What am I?' This is the attitude of the sincere Vedāntic student: the questions are never set aside as answered or as irrelevant. (2) 'Tattva-jñānārtha-darśanam' — the perception (darśana: direct seeing, not mere intellectual awareness) of the PURPOSE (artha) of true knowledge (tattva-jñāna): the recognition that the goal of all this philosophical inquiry is not intellectual distinction, social respect, or competitive debate-victory but mokṣa — liberation, the direct realization of the kṣetrajña as one's own nature. 'Etaj jñānam iti proktam' — this twenty-fold quality-complex IS what is called jñāna. 'Ajñānaṃ yad ato 'nyathā' — what is contrary to all of this — pride, pretension, violence, clinging, ego-inflation, sensory indulgence, instability, self-aggrandizement — THAT is ajñāna. Ignorance is not a cognitive lack alone; it is a moral and existential condition that prevents the discrimination of kṣetra from kṣetrajña.

13:12.text: I will declare that which is to be known (jñeyam) — by knowing which, one attains immortality (amṛta). It is the beginningless Supreme Brahman — it is said to be neither existent (sat) nor non-existent (asat).
13:12.transliteration:
jñeyaṃ yat tat pravakṣyāmi yaj jñātvāmṛtam aśnute |
anādi mat-paraṃ brahma na sat tan nāsad ucyate ||12||
13:12.devanagari:
ज्ञेयं यत्तत्प्रवक्ष्यामि यज्ज्ञात्वामृतमश्नुते ।
अनादिमत्परं ब्रह्म न सत्तन्नासदुच्यते ॥ १२ ॥
13:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:12.explanation: 'Jñeyaṃ yat tat pravakṣyāmi yaj jñātvāmṛtam aśnute' — I will now declare the jñeya (the object of knowledge, that which is ultimately worth knowing), by knowing which one attains amṛta (immortality, freedom from death, mokṣa). The three terms of the epistemic triad are now complete: jñātā (the knower = kṣetrajña), jñāna (the qualities and means of knowing, just enumerated), and jñeya (the object to be known = Brahman). What follows in verses 12-17 is the Gita's most exalted description of Brahman — a passage that echoes the Upaniṣadic 'neti neti' (not this, not this) method of approaching the Absolute by negating all limiting categories. 'Anādi' — beginningless: Brahman has no temporal origin. Unlike all objects of the manifest world which have a moment of coming-into-being, Brahman is prior to time and the very source of time itself. 'Mat-param' — 'My supreme' or 'supreme beyond Me': a deliberate theological subtlety suggesting both that Brahman as nirguna transcends even Krishna's personal form, and that it is the supreme ground in which the personal Absolute (Krishna) is grounded. 'Na sat tan nāsad ucyate' — it is called neither 'sat' (existent — which would confine it to the categories of phenomenal existence) nor 'asat' (non-existent — which would deny it any being whatsoever). Brahman transcends both categories: it is the ground of all existence that cannot be placed within existence's own framework.

13:13.text: With hands and feet everywhere, with eyes, heads, and mouths everywhere, with ears everywhere — it abides in the world, encompassing all.
13:13.transliteration:
sarvataḥ pāṇi-pādaṃ tat sarvato 'kṣi-śiro-mukham |
sarvataḥ śrutimat loke sarvam āvṛtya tiṣṭhati ||13||
13:13.devanagari:
सर्वतः पाणिपादं तत्सर्वतोऽक्षिशिरोमुखम् ।
सर्वतः श्रुतिमल्लोके सर्वमावृत्य तिष्ठति ॥ १३ ॥
13:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:13.explanation: This verse begins the positive description of Brahman through grand paradoxical affirmations — the complement to the negative 'neither sat nor asat' of verse 12. 'Sarvataḥ pāṇi-pādam' — with hands and feet everywhere. This is not mythological anthropomorphism but a precise philosophical statement: Brahman has no particular, located hands and feet because every hand, every foot in the entire cosmos is an expression of Brahman. The biological hand of every living being is, at its ground of being, Brahman's hand — because the power that animates it is Brahman's own presence. This echoes the Puruṣasūkta of the Ṛgveda (10.90): the universal Puruṣa manifests as the entire cosmos. 'Sarvato 'kṣi-śiro-mukham' — with eyes, heads, and mouths everywhere: every act of seeing in the universe is Brahman's seeing; every face is Brahman's face; every voice is Brahman's voice. 'Sarvataḥ śrutimat' — with ears everywhere: every sound that is heard anywhere is heard by Brahman — Brahman is the ultimate, universal witness of all experience because it is the consciousness within each witness. 'Sarvam āvṛtya tiṣṭhati' — it stands pervading (āvṛtya: having wrapped, covered, encompassed) all: Brahman is neither identical with the world (which would be crude pantheism) nor entirely separate from it (which would make it finite) — it encompasses the entire world while remaining itself unconditioned. This is the Gita's panentheistic vision: all things within Brahman, Brahman beyond all things.

13:14.text: Illuminating the functions of all senses, yet without any senses; unattached yet the supporter of all; devoid of qualities (nirguṇa), yet the experiencer of qualities —
13:14.transliteration:
sarvendriya-guṇābhāsaṃ sarvendriya-vivarjitam |
asaktaṃ sarva-bhṛc caiva nirguṇaṃ guṇa-bhoktṛ ca ||14||
13:14.devanagari:
सर्वेन्द्रियगुणाभासं सर्वेन्द्रियविवर्जितम् ।
असक्तं सर्वभृच्चैव निर्गुणं गुणभोक्तृ च ॥ १४ ॥
13:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:14.explanation: This verse is the philosophical climax of the Gita's description of Brahman — a sequence of four brilliant paradoxes, each pointing to an aspect of Brahman that transcends conventional logical categories and points toward a reality that contains both sides of every opposition. (1) 'Sarvendriya-guṇābhāsam' — illuminating the qualities/functions of all senses: Brahman is the self-luminous consciousness whose light makes all sensory experience possible. Every act of seeing, hearing, touching, tasting, and smelling is powered by the single consciousness of Brahman — just as sunlight makes all colour visible without itself having colour. The ābhāsa (reflection, illumination, manifesting quality) of all sense-functions is the Brahman-consciousness itself. (2) 'Sarvendriya-vivarjitam' — yet itself without any sense-organs: Brahman does not see with eyes, hear with ears — it is the witness-consciousness prior to and independent of all sensory apparatus. (3) 'Asaktam' — unattached: though it pervades and sustains all things, nothing clings to it and it clings to nothing — like the sky that holds the clouds without being held by them. (4) 'Sarva-bhṛc caiva' — yet the sustainer of all: the entire universe is held in existence by Brahman's being. (5) 'Nirguṇam' — without the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas): Brahman is not constituted by any of the three qualities that constitute all of Prakṛti. (6) 'Guṇa-bhoktṛ ca' — yet the experiencer of all qualities: the entirety of guṇa-activity is, from the perspective of pure consciousness, Brahman's witness-experience. These six attributes are not contradictions but invitations to transcend binary thinking.

13:15.text: That Knowable (Brahman) is outside and inside all beings, the unmoving and the moving alike. Because of its subtlety it is incomprehensible. It is far away, and yet it is near.
13:15.transliteration:
bahir antaś ca bhūtānām acaraṃ caram eva ca |
sūkṣmatvāt tad avijñeyaṃ dūrasthaṃ cāntike ca tat ||15||
13:15.devanagari:
बहिरन्तश्च भूतानामचरं चरमेव च ।
सूक्ष्मत्वात्तदविज्ञेयं दूरस्थं चान्तिके च तत् ॥ १५ ॥
13:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:15.explanation: 'Bahir antaś ca bhūtānām' — Brahman is simultaneously outside (as the pervasive ground of all spatial existence, as ether, air, the encompassing void that holds all forms) and inside (as the ātman, the innermost witness-consciousness dwelling in the cave of the heart of every being, the 'antarātman'). 'Acaraṃ caram eva ca' — it pervades both the acara (the non-moving: mountains, stones, trees, the entire mineral and vegetable kingdom) and the cara (the moving: all animals, humans, wind, rivers). This comprehensive bipolar statement leaves nothing outside Brahman's pervasion — it is both the stillness and the motion, both the substrate and the process. 'Sūkṣmatvāt tad avijñeyam' — because of its extreme subtlety (sūkṣmatva), it is avijñeya: impossible to grasp through the ordinary instruments of sense-perception and analytical thought. The senses are gross (sthūla) and can only apprehend objects of comparable grossness; the infinitely subtle Brahman slips through every sensory and conceptual net. It can only be known through that which is its own nature — through consciousness itself, in direct self-recognition. 'Dūrasthaṃ cāntike ca' — simultaneously both far (to the seeker lost in ignorance, Brahman seems infinitely remote, an abstract ideal) and near (to the realized sage, it is the immediate, ever-present ground of one's own awareness, closer than the closest, nearer than breathing).

13:16.text: That Knowable appears as divided among beings yet remains undivided. It is the sustainer of all beings, and it is the devourer and the creator.
13:16.transliteration:
avibhaktaṃ ca bhūteṣu vibhaktam iva ca sthitam |
bhūta-bhartṛ ca taj jñeyaṃ grasiṣṇu prabhaviṣṇu ca ||16||
13:16.devanagari:
अविभक्तं च भूतेषु विभक्तमिव च स्थितम् ।
भूतभर्तृ च तज्ज्ञेयं ग्रसिष्णु प्रभविष्णु च ॥ १६ ॥
13:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:16.explanation: 'Avibhaktaṃ ca bhūteṣu vibhaktam iva ca sthitam' — this is among the most philosophically penetrating descriptions of Brahman's relationship to multiplicity in the entire Gita. 'Avibhaktam' — undivided, non-partitioned, whole: Brahman is not a substance that can be cut, apportioned, or distributed; it is the single indivisible reality. Yet 'vibhaktam iva ca sthitam' — it appears as though divided, as though distributed among the infinite multiplicity of individual beings and forms. The pivotal word is 'iva' (like, as though, apparently): not actually divided but appearing so to the ignorant eye. This is the mystery of māyā — how the One appears as many without ceasing to be One. The classical Upaniṣadic analogy: a single space appears as many 'spaces' when enclosed in different pots — the ghaṭākāśas (pot-spaces) appear separate, but mahākāśa (the great space) is one and indivisible; break the pots and the space merges seamlessly, having never actually been divided. 'Bhūta-bhartṛ' — the sustainer of all beings (from 'bhṛ': to bear, maintain, nourish). 'Grasiṣṇu' — the devourer, the absorber, the power of cosmic dissolution (pralaya): all creation eventually returns to its unmanifest ground. 'Prabhaviṣṇu' — the source of all arising, the creative power (sṛṣṭi). Creation, sustenance, and dissolution — the Trimūrti's three functions — are all movements of this single Brahman.

13:17.text: That is the Light of lights, said to be beyond darkness. It is knowledge, the Knowable, and the goal of knowledge — established in the heart of all.
13:17.transliteration:
jyotiṣām api taj jyotiḥ tamasaḥ param ucyate |
jñānaṃ jñeyaṃ jñāna-gamyaṃ hṛdi sarvasya viṣṭhitam ||17||
13:17.devanagari:
ज्योतिषामपि तज्ज्योतिस्तमसः परमुच्यते ।
ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं ज्ञानगम्यं हृदि सर्वस्य विष्ठितम् ॥ १७ ॥
13:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:17.explanation: 'Jyotiṣām api taj jyotiḥ' — the Light even of lights: an ascending superlative that reaches its philosophical limit. The sun illuminates all physical things; fire illuminates darkened spaces; the mind illuminates ideas — but what illuminates consciousness itself? What is the light by which the mind knows, the intellect discerns, the senses perceive? That inner light of pure awareness — the self-luminous consciousness that is prior to and independent of all other lights — is Brahman. This exactly echoes the Muṇḍakopaniṣad, Kaṭhopaniṣad, and the Śvetāśvatara: 'na tatra sūryo bhāti na candra-tārakaṃ... tam eva bhāntam anubhāti sarvaṃ tasya bhāsā sarvam idaṃ vibhāti' — the sun does not shine there, nor moon, nor stars... only when That shines does everything shine after it; by its light all this is illuminated. 'Tamasaḥ param' — transcending darkness (tamas as both physical darkness and, more profoundly, avidyā — the fundamental ignorance that veils the Self). Brahman is svayam-prakāśa — self-luminous, needing no other light to reveal it. Then the epistemological trinity: 'jñānam' (Brahman IS the very faculty of knowing — consciousness is not a property of Brahman, consciousness IS Brahman), 'jñeyam' (the Knowable — Brahman is the only thing ultimately worth knowing), and 'jñāna-gamyam' (attainable through knowledge — jñāna yoga is the direct path). 'Hṛdi sarvasya viṣṭhitam' — established, seated in the heart of all beings: the supreme light is not in some remote transcendent realm but in the cave of the heart (hṛd-guhā) of every single being without exception.

13:18.text: Thus the Field, Knowledge, and the Knowable have been described in brief. My devotee, knowing this through direct realization (vijñāna), attains My very being.
13:18.transliteration:
iti kṣetraṃ tathā jñānaṃ jñeyaṃ coktaṃ samāsataḥ |
mad-bhakta etad vijñāya mad-bhāvāyopapadyate ||18||
13:18.devanagari:
इति क्षेत्रं तथा ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं चोक्तं समासतः ।
मद्भक्त एतद्विज्ञाय मद्भावायोपपद्यते ॥ १८ ॥
13:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:18.explanation: This verse serves as the formal summary-closure of the chapter's first grand movement. Three subjects have been systematically expounded: (1) 'Kṣetram' — the Field (the body and its 24 constituent elements enumerated in verses 5-6: five great elements, ego, intellect, the unmanifest, ten senses, five sense-objects, plus desire, aversion, pleasure, pain, the aggregate, consciousness, and steadiness); (2) 'Jñānam' — Knowledge (the twenty qualities of wisdom from amānitva to tattvajñāna described in verses 7-11); (3) 'Jñeyam' — the Knowable (Brahman in its transcendent, immanent, paradoxical, and luminous nature, described in verses 12-17). 'Mad-bhakta etad vijñāya' — My devotee, having known this through vijñāna. The shift to 'mad-bhakta' (My devotee) is striking and deliberate: this rigorous philosophical knowledge of kṣetra, jñāna, and jñeya is not merely academic — it is the actual content of genuine devotion.
