The true devotee is not one who avoids inquiry but one who pursues it to its depths and finds therein the Divine. 'Vijñāya' — the word 'vijñāna' (direct experiential knowledge, the 'vi-' prefix intensifying 'jñāna' into firsthand realization) signals that what transforms is not conceptual understanding alone but living, felt, embodied recognition. 'Mad-bhāvāyopapadyate' — becomes fit for, arrives at My very being, My nature: the ultimate non-difference between the devotee-knower and the Beloved-Known is revealed.

13:19.text: Know both Prakṛti and Puruṣa to be beginningless. And know that modifications (vikāras) and the guṇas are born from Prakṛti.
13:19.transliteration:
prakṛtiṃ puruṣaṃ caiva viddhy anādī ubhāv api |
vikārāṃś ca guṇāṃś caiva viddhi prakṛti-sambhavān ||19||
13:19.devanagari:
प्रकृतिं पुरुषं चैव विद्ध्यनादी उभावपि ।
विकारांश्च गुणांश्चैव विद्धि प्रकृतिसम्भवान् ॥ १९ ॥
13:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:19.explanation: With this verse begins the chapter's second grand movement — the analysis of Prakṛti (Nature) and Puruṣa (Consciousness/Spirit): the twin foundational categories of Sāṃkhya philosophy, which the Gita absorbs and transforms within its own theistic-devotional framework. 'Prakṛtiṃ puruṣaṃ caiva viddhy anādī ubhāv api' — know both Prakṛti and Puruṣa to be 'anādī' (without beginning, dual form). This is a philosophical declaration of the highest order: neither Prakṛti nor Puruṣa was created at some point in time; neither emerges from the other; neither has a temporal origin. They are co-eternal, co-original. Prakṛti (literally: 'what was before,' the primordial matrix) is the undifferentiated ground of all matter, energy, and psychological processes — the field of the three guṇas, ever active, ever transforming. Puruṣa (literally: 'the one who dwells in the city of the body,' from puri-śaya) is pure consciousness, the witnessing Self — unbounded, unmodified, eternal, beyond all transformation. 'Vikārāṃś ca guṇāṃś caiva viddhi prakṛti-sambhavān' — all vikāras (modifications, the entire manifest universe of body, mind, senses, intellect, ego) and all guṇas (the three qualities — sattva, rajas, tamas — that are Prakṛti's fundamental constituents and operating modes) arise exclusively from Prakṛti. Puruṣa produces nothing — it is the witnessing light in whose presence Prakṛti's entire drama of modification unfolds.

13:20.text: Prakṛti is said to be the cause in the agency of effects and causes. Puruṣa is said to be the cause in the experience of pleasure and pain.
13:20.transliteration:
kārya-kāraṇa-kartṛtve hetuḥ prakṛtir ucyate |
puruṣaḥ sukha-duḥkhānāṃ bhoktṛtve hetur ucyate ||20||
13:20.devanagari:
कार्यकारणकर्तृत्वे हेतुः प्रकृतिरुच्यते ।
पुरुषः सुखदुःखानां भोक्तृत्वे हेतुरुच्यते ॥ २० ॥
13:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:20.explanation: This verse delivers one of the most precise and consequential philosophical distinctions in the Gita — the exact delineation of Prakṛti's and Puruṣa's respective domains of causation. 'Kārya-kāraṇa-kartṛtve hetuḥ prakṛtir ucyate' — Prakṛti is declared the cause (hetu) in the domain of kartṛtva (doership, agency) with respect to both kārya (effects — the gross body, the world of objects, all that is produced) and kāraṇa (causes — the instruments of action: senses, mind, intellect, ego). All action, whether physical movement, sensory perception, emotional reaction, or intellectual deliberation, is entirely the activity of Prakṛti's three guṇas operating through their evolved instruments. The ātman (Puruṣa) is not the doer of any of it — it only appears to be so through the superimposition (adhyāsa) of Prakṛti's activities onto the passive Puruṣa. 'Puruṣaḥ sukha-duḥkhānāṃ bhoktṛtve hetur ucyate' — Puruṣa is declared the cause in the domain of bhoktṛtva (experiencing, enjoying/suffering) of pleasure and pain. This is a subtle and crucial philosophical point: Prakṛti's instruments generate the modifications of pleasure and pain, but these modifications are 'illuminated' and 'experienced' only because Puruṣa (consciousness) is present. When Puruṣa mistakenly identifies with Prakṛti — taking ownership of its activities and states — this misidentification (bhrānti, avidyā) generates bondage. Restore the clarity of their distinction and bondage dissolves at its root.

13:21.text: Puruṣa, established in Prakṛti, experiences the guṇas born of Prakṛti. Attachment to the guṇas is the cause of its births in good and evil wombs.
13:21.transliteration:
puruṣaḥ prakṛti-stho hi bhuṅkte prakṛti-jān guṇān |
kāraṇaṃ guṇa-saṅgo 'sya sad-asad-yoni-janmasu ||21||
13:21.devanagari:
पुरुषः प्रकृतिस्थो हि भुङ्क्ते प्रकृतिजान्गुणान् ।
कारणं गुणसङ्गोऽस्य सदसद्योनिजन्मसु ॥ २१ ॥
13:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:21.explanation: 'Puruṣaḥ prakṛti-sthaḥ' — Puruṣa established in (identified with) Prakṛti: the crucial word 'sthaḥ' (situated, dwelling in) here indicates not the true nature of Puruṣa (which is always free, always the witness) but its apparent situation through the mistaken identification with Prakṛti's instruments — the ego (ahaṃkāra) that says 'I am the body, I am the doer, I am the one who feels.' This false identification (adhyāsa) is the root of all bondage: not an external imposition forced from outside but a case of mistaken identity arising from within through avidyā. 'Bhuṅkte prakṛti-jān guṇān' — it experiences (bhuṅkte: eats, tastes, enjoys-suffers) the guṇas born of Prakṛti: the fluctuating qualities of sattva (clarity, joy, knowledge), rajas (passion, agitation, desire), and tamas (dullness, delusion, inertia) that constitute the entire inner and outer experience of conditioned life. In truth, the guṇas experience the guṇas — but the misidentified Puruṣa experiences them as 'my' pleasures and 'my' pains. 'Kāraṇaṃ guṇa-saṅgo 'sya sad-asad-yoni-janmasu' — the cause of this Puruṣa's births in both good (sat) and evil (asat) wombs is guṇa-saṅga: clinging to the guṇas — the gravitational pull toward sāttvic pleasures, rājasic desires, tāmasic habits. The guṇa-composition of one's attachment at the moment of death determines the quality of the next birth. This is the precise mechanics of karma and rebirth articulated through the Sāṃkhya analytical lens.

13:22.text: The Supreme Puruṣa in this body is also called the Witness (upadraṣṭā), the Permitter (anumantā), the Sustainer (bhartā), the Experiencer (bhoktā), the Great Lord (maheśvaraḥ), and the Supreme Self (paramātmā).
13:22.transliteration:
upadraṣṭānumantā ca bhartā bhoktā maheśvaraḥ |
paramātmeti cāpy ukto dehe 'smin puruṣaḥ paraḥ ||22||
13:22.devanagari:
उपद्रष्टानुमन्ता च भर्ता भोक्ता महेश्वरः ।
परमात्मेति चाप्युक्तो देहेऽस्मिन्पुरुषः परः ॥ २२ ॥
13:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:22.explanation: This is one of the most philosophically dense and theologically rich verses in the entire Bhagavad Gita — a verse that has generated centuries of commentary across all schools of Vedānta and represents the Gita's highest Brahma-vākya (utterance about Brahman). Six designations for the one Supreme Puruṣa dwelling in this body: (1) 'Upadraṣṭā' — the near-witness, the one who witnesses from close proximity (upa = near, draṣṭā = seer). This is the ātman as pure, non-interfering awareness that observes all the body-mind's activities without becoming entangled in them. The Muṇḍakopaniṣad's two-bird image (dvā suparṇā) precisely illuminates this: one bird (the jīva) eats the fruits of the tree (experiences pleasure and pain), while the other bird (the ātman-Paramātman) merely observes without eating, without being affected. (2) 'Anumantā' — the permitter, the one who gives tacit consent without compelling or coercing any action: the philosophical basis for the concept of free will within a theistic framework. (3) 'Bhartā' — the sustainer, the one who bears and maintains all existence in being. (4) 'Bhoktā' — the experiencer, in the transcendent sense of the witness-consciousness in whose light all experience appears and is illuminated. (5) 'Maheśvaraḥ' — the Great Lord, the supreme controller and governor of all creation. (6) 'Paramātmā' — the Supreme Self, the highest Self that is the ground and reality of all individual selves. All six names refer to the same single reality — this one Supreme Puruṣa present in this very body, described from six different philosophical perspectives.

13:23.text: One who knows Puruṣa and Prakṛti together with the guṇas in this way — in whatever way they live — is not born again.
13:23.transliteration:
ya evaṃ vetti puruṣaṃ prakṛtiṃ ca guṇaiḥ saha |
sarvathā vartamāno 'pi na sa bhūyo 'bhijāyate ||23||
13:23.devanagari:
य एवं वेत्ति पुरुषं प्रकृतिं च गुणैः सह ।
सर्वथा वर्तमानोऽपि न स भूयोऽभिजायते ॥ २३ ॥
13:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:23.explanation: 'Ya evaṃ vetti puruṣaṃ prakṛtiṃ ca guṇaiḥ saha' — one who knows (vetti: knows with direct experiential certainty, not merely conceptually) Puruṣa (the Self as pure witness-consciousness, the upadraṣṭā-paramātman just described in verse 22) and Prakṛti together with its guṇas (the entire field of nature with its three qualities and all their modifications, as described in verses 19-21). This is not merely knowing about Puruṣa and Prakṛti as philosophical categories — it is the realized, lived discrimination (viveka) between them: the constant inner recognition 'I am the witness, not the doer; I am the Puruṣa, not the Prakṛti or any of its guṇas; I am the kṣetrajña, not the kṣetra.' 'Sarvathā vartamāno 'pi' — in whatever way they live: this phrase is critically liberating. It means regardless of external life circumstances — whether one is a householder or a monk, a king or a commoner, a merchant or a soldier. The liberating knowledge is entirely internal and does not require any particular external lifestyle, profession, or social renunciation. 'Na sa bhūyo 'bhijāyate' — that person is not born again. This is the Gita's most direct statement of the fruit of kṣetra-kṣetrajña vijñāna: the cessation of rebirth, the attainment of mokṣa, through the lived discrimination between the Self (Puruṣa) and Nature (Prakṛti with its guṇas).

13:24.text: Some behold the Self in the Self by the Self through meditation; others through the yoga of Sāṃkhya; and still others through karma yoga.
13:24.transliteration:
dhyānenātmani paśyanti kecid ātmānam ātmanā |
anye sāṃkhyena yogena karma-yogena cāpare ||24||
13:24.devanagari:
ध्यानेनात्मनि पश्यन्ति केचिदात्मानमात्मना ।
अन्ये सांख्येन योगेन कर्मयोगेन चापरे ॥ २४ ॥
13:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:24.explanation: 'Dhyānenātmani paśyanti kecid ātmānam ātmanā' — some see the Self (ātmānam — the object of vision) within the Self (ātmani — in the inner space of awareness, in the cave of the heart) by the Self (ātmanā — using the purified mind-intellect as the instrument of seeing). The triple use of 'ātman' is philosophically precise and carries the full weight of non-dual realization: the instrument of seeing, the space of seeing, and the object of seeing are all the same ātman — this is the non-dual structure of the deepest meditation where subject-object duality collapses into a single, undivided witnessing awareness. This is the path of dhyāna yoga (meditation, as expounded systematically in Chapter 6). 'Anye sāṃkhyena yogena' — others through the yoga of Sāṃkhya: the discriminative path of philosophical analysis, the rigorous distinguishing of Puruṣa from Prakṛti, the real from the unreal, the Self from the not-Self — this is jñāna yoga, whose theoretical foundation was laid in Chapter 2 and whose application is this very chapter 13. 'Karma-yogena cāpare' — and yet others through karma yoga: the path of desireless, surrendered action, purifying the mind through selfless service until the mind becomes transparent to the Self that has always been present. The Gita's philosophical pluralism is fully expressed here: multiple paths suited to different temperaments, capacities, and life-situations — one single destination.

13:25.text: But others, not knowing thus, worship having heard from others. Even they cross beyond death — those who are devoted to what they have heard.
13:25.transliteration:
anye tv evam ajānantaḥ śrutvānyebhya upāsate |
te 'pi cātitaranty eva mṛtyuṃ śruti-parāyaṇāḥ ||25||
13:25.devanagari:
अन्ये त्वेवमजानन्तः श्रुत्वान्येभ्य उपासते ।
तेऽपि चातितरन्त्येव मृत्युं श्रुतिपरायणाः ॥ २५ ॥
13:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:25.explanation: Verse 24 named three primary active paths to Self-realization: meditation (dhyāna), discriminative knowledge (Sāṃkhya/jñāna yoga), and karma yoga. Now verse 25 introduces the fourth and most universally accessible path: the path of śravaṇa — devoted, faithful, receptive listening. 'Anye tv evam ajānantaḥ' — but others who do not know thus: those who lack the concentrated depth for sustained meditation, the intellectual equipment for rigorous Sāṃkhya inquiry, or the inner fortitude for karma yoga as a formal discipline. 'Śrutvānyebhya upāsate' — they worship having heard from others: they receive the teaching through a guru's living transmission, through scriptural recitation and study, through the company of the wise (satsaṅg), through the stories and lives of realized beings. 'Te 'pi cātitaranty eva' — even THEY most certainly cross beyond: the double emphasis — 'api' (even, extending the liberating truth inclusively) and 'eva' (certainly, unmistakably, without doubt) — is deliberate and emphatic. The simplest person, the one with no formal yoga practice, no philosophical training, no meditative accomplishment, who simply listens with genuine faith and worships accordingly — even that person crosses the ocean of conditioned existence and death. 'Śruti-parāyaṇāḥ' — those for whom śruti (what is heard, revealed wisdom) is their ultimate refuge and orientation. This verse is the Gita's broadest, most compassionate embrace of all humanity: no sincere seeker, however limited in capacity, is excluded from liberation.

13:26.text: O best of the Bharatas, know that whatever being is born — moving or unmoving — arises from the union of the Field and the Knower of the Field.
13:26.transliteration:
yāvat sañjāyate kiñcit sattvaṃ sthāvara-jaṅgamam |
kṣetra-kṣetrajña-saṃyogāt tad viddhi bharatarṣabha ||26||
13:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:26.explanation: 'Yāvat sañjāyate kiñcit sattvaṃ sthāvara-jaṅgamam' — whatever being comes into existence, of any kind whatsoever: 'sthāvara' (stationary, unmoving — mountains, trees, minerals, the entire inert world) and 'jaṅgama' (moving — animals, humans, birds, rivers, wind). These two categories together encompass the entire manifest cosmos without remainder: there is no form of existence that falls outside this bipolar classification. 'Yāvat' (whatever, to whatever extent) and 'kiñcit' (anything at all) together establish the absolute comprehensiveness of the statement — no exceptions, no partial coverage. 'Kṣetra-kṣetrajña-saṃyogāt tad viddhi' — know that ALL of it arises from the saṃyoga (union, conjunction, coming-together) of kṣetra (the Field: matter, body, nature, prakṛti in all its forms) and kṣetrajña (the Knower of the Field: consciousness, the puruṣa, the witnessing Self). This is the Gita's most comprehensive single statement of cosmogony — the metaphysics of manifestation: nothing in the universe exists that is not the product of this primordial union of consciousness and matter. A stone is kṣetra minimally animated by the bare presence of consciousness; a human being is kṣetra fully animated with all its subtle faculties functioning. The word 'saṃyoga' (union) is philosophically careful: it does not say that kṣetra and kṣetrajña are identical or merged — they are united in manifestation while remaining metaphysically distinct. The practical import of this verse is the recognition which is liberation: to identify the eternal kṣetrajña within oneself and cease mistaking the transient kṣetra for the true Self.

13:27.text: One who sees the Supreme Lord abiding equally in all beings, the imperishable within the perishable — that one truly sees.
13:27.transliteration:
samaṃ sarveṣu bhūteṣu tiṣṭhantaṃ parameśvaram |
vinaśyatsv avinaśyantaṃ yaḥ paśyati sa paśyati ||27||
13:27.devanagari:
समं सर्वेषु भूतेषु तिष्ठन्तं परमेश्वरम् ।
विनश्यत्स्वविनश्यन्तं यः पश्यति स पश्यति ॥ २७ ॥
13:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:27.explanation: 'Samaṃ sarveṣu bhūteṣu tiṣṭhantaṃ parameśvaram' — the Parameśvara (Supreme Lord, the universal kṣetrajña identified with the Paramātman in verse 22) abides 'samam' (equally, uniformly, without gradation or partiality) in ALL beings. Not more divinity in the saint, less in the sinner; not more in the human, less in the animal; not more in the living, absent in the apparently inert. The same single luminous consciousness is the inner ground of every form of existence — from the greatest to the most minute, from the most evolved to the most primitive. 'Vinaśyatsv avinaśyantam' — the imperishable (avinaśyat) abiding within the perishable (vinaśyat): this is the paradox that is simultaneously the most obvious truth and the hardest to perceive. The body is born and dies — the consciousness that illuminates the body is unborn and deathless. Every moment of life is this paradox fully present; every death is the departure of the imperishable from the perishable container. 'Yaḥ paśyati sa paśyati' — 'he who sees, truly sees': the Gita uses this solemn doubled formulation with great deliberateness to distinguish real seeing (ātma-darśana, Brahma-darśana) from mere sensory perception. Ordinary perception sees only the surface — diversity, change, individual identities, rise and fall. The sage sees the one unbroken consciousness shining through all forms equally, as the one sun shines through a thousand windows. That vision is not mystical imagination — it is the direct recognition of what is actually the case, always and everywhere. That vision IS liberation.

13:28.text: Seeing the Lord dwelling equally everywhere, one does not destroy the Self by the self — and thereby attains the supreme goal.
13:28.transliteration:
samaṃ paśyan hi sarvatra samavasthitam īśvaram |
na hinasty ātmanātmānaṃ tato yāti parāṃ gatim ||28||
13:28.devanagari:
समं पश्यन्हि सर्वत्र समवस्थितमीश्वरम् ।
न हिनस्त्यात्मनात्मानं ततो याति परां गतिम् ॥ २८ ॥
13:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:28.explanation: 'Samaṃ paśyan hi sarvatra samavasthitam īśvaram' — seeing the Lord as uniformly present (samavasthitam: equally situated, consistently established) everywhere and in all beings: this 'sama' (equal) vision is the living, practical expression of all the philosophical knowledge described throughout this chapter. It is not a doctrine held intellectually but a direct perception — Brahma-darśana — that transforms the seer. 'Na hinasty ātmanātmānam' — does not injure the Self by the self. This phrase is philosophically rich and contains the Gita's most penetrating statement of the ethics of non-violence. 'Ātmanā' (the instrumental: by the self — the individual ego-self as the instrument of harm) and 'ātmānam' (the accusative: the Self — the universal Paramātman that dwells in all beings as the object of potential harm). When a person, ignorant of the Self's omnipresence, harms, exploits, demeans, or violates another being — they are unknowingly striking at the very Self that dwells identically within both the harmer and the harmed. This is the deepest meaning of harm: it is the self (ahaṃkāra) attacking the Self (Paramātman) in profound ignorance of their non-difference. Conversely, the one who has seen the equal presence of the Lord everywhere cannot harm any being — not primarily for ethical-moral reasons but because they directly perceive that 'I and that being are grounded in the same consciousness.' 'Tato yāti parāṃ gatim' — therefore, they attain the supreme destination (parā gati): mokṣa. Equal vision (sama-darśana) is simultaneously the highest ethics and the most direct path to liberation — in this verse, the two are revealed to be one and the same truth.

13:29.text: One who sees that all actions are performed in every way by Prakṛti alone, and that the Self is the non-doer — that one truly sees.
13:29.transliteration:
prakṛtyaiva ca karmāṇi kriyamāṇāni sarvaśaḥ |
yaḥ paśyati tathātmānam akartāraṃ sa paśyati ||29||
13:29.devanagari:
प्रकृत्यैव च कर्माणि क्रियमाणानि सर्वशः ।
यः पश्यति तथात्मानमकर्तारं स पश्यति ॥ २९ ॥
13:29.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:29.explanation: 'Prakṛtyaiva ca karmāṇi kriyamāṇāni sarvaśaḥ' — all actions are performed in every way (sarvaśaḥ: completely, in all modes, without exception) by Prakṛti alone. The word 'eva' (alone, precisely, only) is emphatic: it is Prakṛti and nothing other than Prakṛti that performs every action. Every activity in the universe — physical, mental, biological, cosmic — is the operation of the three guṇas of Prakṛti: sattva (purity, illumination, clarity), rajas (activity, passion, agitation), and tamas (inertia, heaviness, delusion). Seeing, hearing, thinking, digesting, breathing, creating, destroying — all these are the guṇas engaging with their own modifications (guṇā guṇeṣu vartante, 3.28). 'Tathātmānam akartāram' — and (seeing) the Self as the non-doer (akartā: the one who does not act, who has no agency): the ātman is pure, unchanging, witness-consciousness. It illuminates all these activities of Prakṛti without participating in them, as a lamp illuminates a room without itself performing any of the room's activities, as the sun illuminates all movement without itself moving. 'Yaḥ paśyati sa paśyati' — the one who sees this truly sees. This is not passive resignation to fatalism — it is the most precise metaphysical vision of what is actually occurring in every moment. The ego's claim 'I am the doer' (ahaṃkāra-kartṛtva) is the root of bondage and all its consequences: pride, guilt, fear, attachment, and grief. Seeing through that claim — recognizing Prakṛti as the doer and the Self as the witness — is the root of freedom. This realization is the practical fruit of the entire kṣetra-kṣetrajña analysis.

13:30.text: When one perceives the separate existence of all beings as resting in the One, and the expansion of all from that same One — then one becomes Brahman.
13:30.transliteration:
yadā bhūta-pṛthag-bhāvam ekastham anupaśyati |
tata eva ca vistāraṃ brahma sampadyate tadā ||30||
13:30.devanagari:
यदा भूतपृथग्भावमेकस्थमनुपश्यति ।
तत एव च विस्तारं ब्रह्म सम्पद्यते तदा ॥ ३० ॥
13:30.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:30.explanation: 'Bhūta-pṛthag-bhāvam ekastham anupaśyati' — when one perceives (anupaśyati: sees continuously, sees with sustained accompanying vision, the 'anu' prefix indicating constancy and thoroughness) the separate existence (pṛthag-bhāva: the sense of distinction, individuality, multiplicity) of all beings as actually 'ekastham': resting in, grounded in, the One. The world's astonishing multiplicity — infinite forms, species, minerals, consciousnesses — all of it seen to be resting in and arising from a single substratum, as the infinite multiplicity of waves rests in and arises from the one ocean. 'Tata eva ca vistāraṃ' — and sees the expansion (vistāra: spreading out, unfolding, proliferation) of all from that very One (tata eva: from that alone). This is the double vision of non-dual realization — not a single movement but a simultaneous binocular perception: first, the many-to-one movement (all distinctions are seen to rest in Brahman, dissolving apparent separateness); and simultaneously, the one-to-many movement (Brahman is seen to be the very substance of all these distinctions — it expands INTO the forms, the forms being nothing other than Brahman-appearing). This is what Advaita Vedānta calls 'vivartavāda' — the understanding of how the One appears as many. 'Brahma sampadyate tadā' — then one 'becomes Brahman.' 'Sampadyate' (from sam + pad: to arrive at completely, to become fully, to attain perfectly) — this is the language of recognition: not that one learns about Brahman, not that one approaches Brahman from outside — one IS recognized as what one has always been. The jñeya and the jñātā are one.

13:31.text: O son of Kuntī, this Supreme Self — being beginningless and beyond the guṇas — is imperishable. Though dwelling in the body, it neither acts nor is tainted.
13:31.transliteration:
anāditvān nirguṇatvāt paramātmāyam avyayaḥ |
śarīrastho 'pi kaunteya na karoti na lipyate ||31||
13:31.devanagari:
अनादित्वान्निर्गुणत्वात्परमात्मायमव्ययः ।
शरीरस्थोऽपि कौन्तेय न करोति न लिप्यते ॥ ३१ ॥
13:31.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:31.explanation: Three metaphysical attributes are here stated as logical foundations (hetu: reason) for two liberating consequences — the ātman's non-doership (akartṛtva) and its absolute freedom from contamination (aliptata). (1) 'Anāditvāt' — because it is beginningless (anādi): the Paramātman has no temporal origin, no moment of creation, no causal history. What was never born cannot die; what has no beginning has no end; what exists prior to time cannot be governed by time's laws of change, decay, and causation. The ātman stands outside the entire causal matrix that governs all objects within Prakṛti. (2) 'Nirguṇatvāt' — because it is beyond the guṇas (nirguṇa): the three guṇas — sattva, rajas, tamas — are the constituents of all matter and all mental activity. Every action, every modification, every experience in the universe is the operation of these three qualities upon each other. The Paramātman transcends all three. Since all action (karma) is the operation of the guṇas, and the ātman is by nature beyond the guṇas, the ātman is, by metaphysical necessity, beyond action (akartā) and beyond the karma that action generates. (3) 'Avyayaḥ' — imperishable, inexhaustible, undiminished: no change, no decay, no increase, no decrease occurs in the ātman ever. 'Śarīrastho 'pi... na karoti na lipyate' — though residing in the body (śarīrasthaḥ: body-situated), it neither acts nor is tainted. The 'api' (even though) holds the crucial tension: yes, the Self is present within the body — but the body's characteristics (action, aging, disease, death, karmic accumulation, moral staining) do not adhere to the Self. Its presence enables the body's consciousness while itself remaining eternally untouched, as space is not dampened by the rain that falls within it.

13:32.text: Just as the all-pervading ether (ākāśa) is not tainted because of its subtlety — so too does the Self, present everywhere in bodies, remain untainted.
13:32.transliteration:
yathā sarva-gataṃ saukṣmyād ākāśaṃ nopalipyate |
sarvatrāvasthito dehe tathātmā nopalipyate ||32||
13:32.devanagari:
यथा सर्वगतं सौक्ष्म्यादाकाशं नोपलिप्यते ।
सर्वत्रावस्थितो देहे तथात्मा नोपलिप्यते ॥ ३२ ॥
13:32.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:32.explanation: This is one of the most precise, beautiful, and practically illuminating similes in the entire Bhagavad Gita — explaining through an analogy from universal daily experience the metaphysical truth of the ātman's absolute, untouched purity. 'Yathā sarva-gataṃ saukṣmyād ākāśaṃ nopalipyate' — just as the all-pervading ākāśa (space, ether — the subtlest of the five great elements) is not tainted (nopalipyate: is not smeared, is not contaminated, does not receive the imprint of) because of its subtlety (saukṣmyāt). Ākāśa pervades literally everything: it fills the most sacred temple and the most degraded space equally. Smoke from a fire fills a room — yet when the fire is extinguished and the smoke clears, the space is as pure as before, showing that it never actually absorbed the smoke. The most intense pollution passes through space without contaminating it in any lasting way. Why? Because of its saukṣmya — its utter subtlety, its nature as non-material, non-particulate pure extension that has no surface, no substance, no texture on which pollution can adhere or accumulate. 'Sarvatrāvasthito dehe tathātmā nopalipyate' — so the ātman (tathā: similarly), though present everywhere (sarvatrāvasthitaḥ: situated everywhere) in all bodies, is equally untainted. The ātman is subtler even than space — it is pure consciousness itself, the subtlest of the subtle (aṇor aṇīyān), with no material surface, no guṇa-texture, no quality that could receive or bind impressions. Birth, disease, desire, action, suffering, moral staining — all happen in the kṣetra — the ātman is the witnessing ākāśa within, eternally, irrevocably, effortlessly pure.

13:33.text: O Bhārata, just as the one sun illuminates this entire world — so does the Kṣetrī (the Self, Knower of the Field) illuminate the entire Field.
13:33.transliteration:
yathā prakāśayaty ekaḥ kṛtsnaṃ lokam imaṃ raviḥ |
kṣetraṃ kṣetrī tathā kṛtsnaṃ prakāśayati bhārata ||33||
13:33.devanagari:
यथा प्रकाशयत्येकः कृत्स्नं लोकमिमं रविः ।
क्षेत्रं क्षेत्री तथा कृत्स्नं प्रकाशयति भारत ॥ ३३ ॥
13:33.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:33.explanation: This is the chapter's final and most luminous simile — the sun as the image of the ātman — and it deepens the previous verse's ākāśa simile by adding the dimension of active illumination. The ākāśa (space) taught that the Self is uncontaminated despite universal presence; the sun (ravi) now teaches that the Self is the active illuminator — the conscious light that makes all knowing possible. 'Yathā prakāśayaty ekaḥ kṛtsnaṃ lokam imaṃ raviḥ' — just as the ONE (ekaḥ: singular, indivisible) sun illuminates this entire world (kṛtsnam: completely, without leaving any corner in darkness). The sun is a single source without division or multiplication. Yet its light reaches every mountain, every ocean, every face, every grain of sand without discrimination or partial coverage — the entire world is equally, simultaneously, completely illuminated by that single source. 'Kṣetraṃ kṣetrī tathā kṛtsnaṃ prakāśayati' — so does the kṣetrī (the kṣetrajña, the Self) illuminate the entire kṣetra (the Field: body, senses, mind, intellect) completely (kṛtsnam). The Self is the single consciousness that makes seeing possible for the eyes, hearing possible for the ears, thinking possible for the mind, discrimination possible for the intellect, and selfhood possible for the ego. Without the illumination of the ātman, the entire body-mind complex is an inert mechanism — no different from an extraordinarily sophisticated machine without power. The ātman is to the body what the sun is to the world: the single, prior, self-luminous source without which no other knowing, perceiving, or experiencing is possible. And as one sun illuminates countless windows through their apertures — appearing as many reflections while remaining one light — the one Brahman-consciousness illuminates countless bodies through the aperture of individual awareness, appearing as many selves while remaining one undivided Self.

13:34.text: Those who know, through the eye of knowledge, the distinction between the Field and the Knower of the Field — and liberation from the Prakṛti of beings — they attain the Supreme.
13:34.transliteration:
kṣetra-kṣetrajñayor evam antaram jñāna-cakṣuṣā |
bhūta-prakṛti-mokṣaṃ ca ye vidur yānti te param ||34||
13:34.devanagari:
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञयोरेवमन्तरं ज्ञानचक्षुषा ।
भूतप्रकृतिमोक्षं च ये विदुर्यान्ति ते परम् ॥ ३४ ॥
13:34.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
13:34.explanation: This is the chapter's magisterial conclusion — the entire edifice of Chapter 13's teaching condensed into one luminous closing verse that simultaneously names the instrument, the knowledge, and the fruit, gathering every thread of the preceding 33 verses into a single final declaration. 'Evam antaram' — the distinction (antara) as described throughout this chapter: the Field (the body with its 24 evolutes of Prakṛti — mahābhūtas, ahaṅkāra, buddhi, avyakta, the ten senses, manas, the five sense-objects, plus desire, aversion, pleasure, pain, the aggregate, reflected consciousness, and steadiness — verses 5-6) versus the Knower of the Field (the pure, beginningless, nirguṇa, imperishable, self-luminous consciousness that is upadraṣṭā, anumantā, bhartā, bhoktā, maheśvara, and paramātman — verses 1, 2, 22). 'Jñāna-cakṣuṣā' — through the eye of knowledge: 'jñāna-cakṣus' is the inner eye of viveka (discrimination), the faculty of discernment that sees through the surface multiplicity to the underlying unity, through the apparent identity of body and Self to their actual distinction. This is not developed by birth or ordinary intellect — it is cultivated through the twenty qualities of jñāna (verses 7-11), kindled by the grace of the teacher, and consummated in direct experience (vijñāna). Physical eyes see only forms; the jñāna-cakṣus sees the truth behind and within forms. 'Bhūta-prakṛti-mokṣaṃ ca ye viduḥ' — and who know liberation (mokṣa) from the Prakṛti of beings: not merely the theoretical mapping of the distinction but the realized understanding of how beings are bound by Prakṛti (through the false identification of ātman with the body-mind, as described in verses 19-21) and how that bondage is dissolved (through the viveka of kṣetra versus kṣetrajña, the sama-darśana of verse 27, the recognition of akartṛtva in verse 29, the perception of Brahman's indivisibility in verse 30). 'Yānti te param' — they attain the Supreme. The entire chapter's journey has been leading to this single word: 'param' — the Supreme, Brahman, liberation, the infinite, the boundless, the self-luminous. The knowing of the distinction IS the liberation. There is no gap between the recognition and the freedom — they are one and the same event.

14:1.text: Śrī Bhagavān said: I shall declare once more the supreme knowledge — the highest of all knowledges — knowing which all the sages have gone from this world to the highest perfection.
14:1.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
paraṃ bhūyaḥ pravakṣyāmi jñānānāṃ jñānam uttamam |
yaj jñātvā munayaḥ sarve parāṃ siddhim ito gatāḥ ||1||
14:1.devanagari:
श्री भगवानुवाच ।
परं भूयः प्रवक्ष्यामि ज्ञानानां ज्ञानमुत्तमम् ।
यज्ज्ञात्वा मुनयः सर्वे परां सिद्धिमितो गताः ॥ १ ॥
14:1.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:1.explanation: 'Bhūyaḥ' (once more) indicates that Krishna is beginning a fresh, deeper teaching beyond what was given before. 'Jñānānāṃ jñānam uttamam' — this is the knowledge of all knowledges, supreme because it identifies the very root of bondage: the three Guṇas of Prakṛti. 'Munayaḥ sarve' — all the sages, across all traditions, have been liberated through this exact understanding. 'Parāṃ siddhim' — not merely heavenly reward, but the highest perfection: direct realisation of Brahman and final liberation (mokṣa). This opening verse establishes the extraordinary authority and liberating power of what is to follow.

14:2.text: Taking refuge in this knowledge and attaining identity with my own nature, they are neither born at creation nor disturbed at dissolution.
14:2.transliteration:
idaṃ jñānam upāśritya mama sādharmyam āgatāḥ |
sarge 'pi nopajāyante pralaye na vyathanti ca ||2||
14:2.devanagari:
इदं ज्ञानमुपाश्रित्य मम साधर्म्यमागताः ।
सर्गेऽपि नोपजायन्ते प्रलये न व्यथन्ति च ॥ २ ॥
14:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:2.explanation: 'Mama sādharmyam āgatāḥ' — they attain the same essential nature as mine: beyond birth, death, and all transformation. This is liberation in its most absolute form. 'Sarge 'pi nopajāyante' — even at the cosmic re-creation (sarga), they do not take birth again because they have transcended the Guṇas that are the cause of all rebirth. 'Pralaye na vyathanti' — at the final dissolution (pralaya) of the cosmos, they are completely undisturbed, because they already rest in that which is beyond all cosmic cycles. This verse reveals the ultimate fruit of the knowledge to be taught: identity with the deathless, birth-free reality of Brahman.

14:3.text: My womb is the great Brahma (Mahā Prakṛti); in that I place the embryo. From that, O Bhārata, is the birth of all beings.
14:3.transliteration:
mama yonir mahad brahma tasmin garbhaṃ dadhāmy aham |
sambhavaḥ sarvabhūtānāṃ tato bhavati bhārata ||3||
14:3.devanagari:
मम योनिर्महद्ब्रह्म तस्मिन्गर्भं दधाम्यहम् ।
सम्भवः सर्वभूतानां ततो भवति भारत ॥ ३ ॥
14:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:3.explanation: 'Mahad brahma' here refers not to the supreme Brahman but to Mahā Prakṛti — the primal, unmanifest, threefold material nature, the ultimate material cause of all creation. 'Tasmin garbhaṃ dadhāmy aham' — Krishna places the seed of consciousness (the aggregate of all individual souls, the Puruṣa principle) within Prakṛti. This describes the primordial interaction of Puruṣa (conscious witnessing principle) and Prakṛti (material nature) that initiates all creation. 'Sarvabhūtānāṃ sambhavaḥ' — gods, humans, animals, plants — all arise from this one primordial union. This verse encapsulates the entire Sāṃkhya cosmogony in two lines.

14:4.text: O Kaunteya, whatever forms are born in all wombs, the Great Brahma (Prakṛti) is their mother-womb, and I am the seed-giving father.
14:4.transliteration:
sarvayoniṣu kaunteya mūrtayaḥ sambhavanti yāḥ |
tāsāṃ brahma mahad yonir ahaṃ bījapradaḥ pitā ||4||
14:4.devanagari:
सर्वयोनिषु कौन्तेय मूर्तयः सम्भवन्ति याः ।
तासां ब्रह्म महद्योनिरहं बीजप्रदः पिता ॥ ४ ॥
14:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:4.explanation: 'Sarvayoniṣu' — in all wombs: divine, human, animal, plant — every category of embodied existence without exception. 'Mahad yoniḥ' — Prakṛti, the Great Nature, is the universal mother — the material container from which all forms emerge. 'Ahaṃ bījapradaḥ pitā' — I am the seed-giving father: the conscious principle (Puruṣa/Ātman/Brahman) that animates all these Prakṛtic forms. This verse establishes a universal spiritual kinship — all beings, across all species and realms, share the same ultimate parentage: Consciousness (Father) and Matter (Mother). The diversity of forms is Prakṛtic; the unity of the indwelling Self is divine.

14:5.text: O Mighty-armed, Sattva, Rajas, and Tamas — these Guṇas, born of Prakṛti, bind the imperishable dweller-in-the-body (the Ātman) to the body.
14:5.transliteration:
sattvaṃ rajas tama iti guṇāḥ prakṛtisambhavāḥ |
nibadhnanti mahābāho dehe dehinam avyayam ||5||
14:5.devanagari:
सत्त्वं रजस्तम इति गुणाः प्रकृतिसम्भवाः ।
निबध्नन्ति महाबाहो देहे देहिनमव्ययम् ॥ ५ ॥
14:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:5.explanation: This is the foundational verse of the chapter's entire teaching. The three Guṇas are defined: 'Sattva' — the quality of luminosity, clarity, knowledge, and peace; 'Rajas' — the quality of passion, desire, activity, and agitation; 'Tamas' — the quality of inertia, delusion, darkness, and sloth. 'Prakṛtisambhavāḥ' — these Guṇas belong entirely to Prakṛti (matter), not to the Ātman. Yet they bind 'dehinam avyayam' — the imperishable Ātman — to the body. This is the paradox at the heart of bondage: the Self is intrinsically free, yet the Guṇas create the illusion of identification with body and mind. Liberation (mokṣa) is precisely the transcendence of this triple bondage.

14:6.text: Among these, Sattva, being pure, is illuminating and disease-free, O sinless one; yet it binds through attachment to happiness and attachment to knowledge.
14:6.transliteration:
tatra sattvaṃ nirmalatvāt prakāśakam anāmayam |
sukhasaṅgena badhnāti jñānasaṅgena cānagha ||6||
14:6.devanagari:
तत्र सत्त्वं निर्मलत्वात्प्रकाशकमनामयम् ।
सुखसङ्गेन बध्नाति ज्ञानसङ्गेन चानघ ॥ ६ ॥
14:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:6.explanation: 'Nirmalatvāt' — Sattva is the most refined of the Guṇas; hence it illuminates (prakāśakam) the intellect and keeps one free from disease and suffering (anāmayam). Yet the crucial warning follows: even Sattva binds. 'Sukhasaṅgena badhnāti' — when one identifies with the happiness Sattva brings and clings to it ('I am happy', 'my life is pure and peaceful'), that attachment creates bondage. 'Jñānasaṅgena' — similarly, pride in one's knowledge or spirituality ('I am enlightened', 'I am wise') is itself a subtle Sāttvic bondage. 'Anagha' (O sinless one) is significant — Arjuna is sinless precisely because he is on the verge of this supreme understanding: that even virtue must be transcended.

14:7.text: Know that Rajas is passion-natured, born of thirst and attachment; O Kaunteya, it binds the embodied soul through attachment to action.
14:7.transliteration:
rajo rāgātmakaṃ viddhi tṛṣṇāsaṅgasamudbhavam |
tan nibadhnāti kaunteya karmasaṅgena dehinam ||7||
14:7.devanagari:
रजो रागात्मकं विद्धि तृष्णासङ्गसमुद्भवम् ।
तन्निबध्नाति कौन्तेय कर्मसङ्गेन देहिनम् ॥ ७ ॥
14:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:7.explanation: 'Rāgātmakam' — the very essence of Rajas is rāga, passion — the fundamental pull of consciousness toward objects. 'Tṛṣṇāsaṅgasamudbhavam' — it arises from tṛṣṇā (craving for the unattained) and saṅga (clinging to the attained). 'Karmasaṅgena dehinam nibadhnāti' — it binds the soul through constant compulsion toward action driven by desire for results. The Rājasic person is perpetually active — driven by ambition, competition, restlessness, and craving for recognition. This is a subtle bondage because it masquerades as enthusiasm and dynamism. But all Rājasic action ultimately leads to duḥkha (suffering) as its fruit.

14:8.text: But know that Tamas is born of ignorance, the deluder of all embodied beings; O Bhārata, it binds through heedlessness, sloth, and sleep.
14:8.transliteration:
tamas tv ajñānajaṃ viddhi mohanaṃ sarvadehinām |
pramādālasyanidrābhis tan nibadhnāti bhārata ||8||
14:8.devanagari:
तमस्त्वज्ञानजं विद्धि मोहनं सर्वदेहिनाम् ।
प्रमादालस्यनिद्राभिस्तन्निबध्नाति भारत ॥ ८ ॥
14:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:8.explanation: 'Ajñānajam' — Tamas is born of ignorance (avidyā) and perpetuates ignorance — a vicious cycle of darkness feeding darkness. 'Mohanam sarvadehinām' — it deludes all embodied beings without exception, creating fundamental confusion about what is real, what is valuable, and what one's actual nature is. The three instruments of Tāmasic bondage: 'Pramāda' — heedlessness, negligence, misdirected attention; 'Ālasya' — sloth, torpor, the collapse of energy and will; 'Nidrā' — sleep and unconsciousness, not just physical sleep but spiritual stupor. Tamas is the most dangerous Guṇa because it does not merely bind — it blinds, preventing the very recognition of bondage.

14:9.text: O Bhārata, Sattva attaches to happiness; Rajas to action; while Tamas, covering knowledge, attaches to heedlessness.
14:9.transliteration:
sattvaṃ sukhe sañjayati rajaḥ karmaṇi bhārata |
jñānam āvṛtya tu tamaḥ pramāde sañjayaty uta ||9||
14:9.devanagari:
सत्त्वं सुखे सञ्जयति रजः कर्मणि भारत ।
ज्ञानमावृत्य तु तमः प्रमादे सञ्जयत्युत ॥ ९ ॥
14:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:9.explanation: The functional domain of each Guṇa is clearly mapped: Sattva pulls consciousness toward 'sukha' — peace, clarity, meditation, spiritual joy. Rajas pulls toward 'karma' — relentless action, ambition, competition, the compulsion to produce and achieve. Tamas is the most insidious: 'jñānam āvṛtya' — it first covers, obscures, and smothers knowledge itself, so the person cannot even recognize their bondage. Then 'pramāde sañjayati' — having removed knowledge, it sinks consciousness into heedlessness and stupor. This verse provides the functional cartography of the three Guṇas: Sāttvic realm is happiness, Rājasic realm is action, and Tāmasic realm is delusion and sloth.

14:10.text: O Bhārata, Sattva arises by suppressing Rajas and Tamas; Rajas by suppressing Sattva and Tamas; and Tamas by suppressing Sattva and Rajas.
14:10.transliteration:
rajas tamaś cābhibhūya sattvaṃ bhavati bhārata |
rajaḥ sattvaṃ tamaś caiva tamaḥ sattvaṃ rajas tathā ||10||
14:10.devanagari:
रजस्तमश्चाभिभूय सत्त्वं भवति भारत ।
रजः सत्त्वं तमश्चैव तमः सत्त्वं रजस्तथा ॥ १० ॥
14:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:10.explanation: The three Guṇas are not in absolute opposition but in dynamic competition: like three men wrestling, each can pin the other two down, but the suppressed ones are never destroyed, only temporarily subdued. In every moment of human experience, this competition plays out. Sāttvic practice (meditation, good company, pure food, selfless service) suppresses Rajas and Tamas, allowing Sattva to rise. This verse has a crucial practical implication for the spiritual aspirant: the path begins with cultivating Sattva (to replace Rajas and Tamas), but the ultimate goal is to transcend all three — including Sattva — since even Sattva, as we have seen, binds.

14:11.text: When in all the gates of this body, knowledge and light arise — then one should know that Sattva is predominant.
14:11.transliteration:
sarvatvāreṣu dehe 'smin prakāśa upajāyate |
jñānaṃ yadā tadā vidyād vivṛddhaṃ sattvam ity uta ||11||
14:11.devanagari:
सर्वद्वारेषु देहेऽस्मिन्प्रकाश उपजायते ।
ज्ञानं यदा तदा विद्याद्विवृद्धं सत्त्वमित्युत ॥ ११ ॥
14:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:11.explanation: 'Sarvadvāreṣu' — all the gates of the body: the five sense organs (eyes, ears, nose, tongue, skin), the mind, intellect, and ego — each of these is a 'doorway' through which consciousness perceives the world. When Sattva increases, light (prakāśa) and knowledge (jñāna) flow through all these gates simultaneously. The practical signs: the eyes become bright, the mind becomes joyful and clear, perception becomes acute, thoughts become noble, and behavior becomes restrained and dharmic. This verse provides the experiential diagnostic: when you notice clarity, alertness, and wisdom pervading your entire being, know that Sattva is in ascendance.

14:12.text: O Best of the Bharatas, when Rajas increases, greed, activity, the undertaking of actions, restlessness, and craving arise.
14:12.transliteration:
lobhaḥ pravṛttir ārambhaḥ karmaṇām aśamaḥ spṛhā |
rajasy etāni jāyante vivṛddhe bharatarṣabha ||12||
14:12.devanagari:
लोभः प्रवृत्तिरारम्भः कर्मणामशमः स्पृहा ।
रजस्येतानि जायन्ते विवृद्धे भरतर्षभ ॥ १२ ॥
14:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:12.explanation: Five clear diagnostic signs of increasing Rajas: (1) 'Lobhaḥ' — greed, the persistent sense that one does not have enough, the inability to be content. (2) 'Pravṛttiḥ' — compulsive outward engagement, the inability to remain still. (3) 'Ārambhaḥ karmaṇām' — the constant initiation of new projects, the restless need to 'do' and 'achieve.' (4) 'Aśamaḥ' — mental restlessness, the inability of the mind to settle into peace. (5) 'Spṛhā' — intense craving, the burning desire for what is not yet attained. A Rājasic person is perpetually driven, perpetually dissatisfied, perpetually initiating — often mistaken for productivity and success, but rooted in fundamental inner restlessness.

14:13.text: O Joy of the Kurus, when Tamas increases, darkness, inactivity, heedlessness, and delusion arise.
14:13.transliteration:
aprakāśo 'pravṛttiś ca pramādo moha eva ca |
tamasy etāni jāyante vivṛddhe kurunandana ||13||
14:13.devanagari:
अप्रकाशोऽप्रवृत्तिश्च प्रमादो मोह एव च ।
तमस्येतानि जायन्ते विवृद्धे कुरुनन्दन ॥ १३ ॥
14:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:13.explanation: Four diagnostic signs of increasing Tamas: (1) 'Aprakāśaḥ' — darkness, the absence of the inner light of discrimination and understanding; the mind becomes clouded and dull. (2) 'Apravṛttiḥ' — inactivity, the inability or unwillingness to engage in right action or spiritual practice; torpor. (3) 'Pramādaḥ' — heedlessness, negligence toward one's duties and the things that truly matter. (4) 'Mohaḥ' — fundamental delusion, the inability to distinguish between right and wrong, real and unreal, dharma and adharma. Significantly, the contrast with Rajas is perfect: Rajas produces hyper-activity; Tamas produces total inactivity. Both are forms of bondage — one in the extreme of doing, the other in the extreme of not-doing.

14:14.text: When the embodied one departs when Sattva is predominant, they attain the pure worlds of those who know the highest.
14:14.transliteration:
yadā sattve pravṛddhe tu pralayaṃ yāti dehabhṛt |
tadottamavidāṃ lokān amalān pratipadyate ||14||
14:14.devanagari:
यदा सत्त्वे प्रवृद्धे तु प्रलयं याति देहभृत् ।
तदोत्तमविदां लोकानमलान्प्रतिपद्यते ॥ १४ ॥
14:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:14.explanation: This begins a three-verse sequence (14–16) describing the post-mortem destinations determined by whichever Guṇa predominates at the time of death. 'Sattve pravṛddhe' — when Sattva is dominant at the moment of death: the mind is calm, discrimination is clear, consciousness is luminous. 'Uttamavidāṃ lokān amalān' — such a person attains the pure, untainted realms of the highest knowers — the Sāttvic heavens, the abodes of sages and enlightened beings. Note: this is still rebirth, not mokṣa, because Sattva too is a Guṇa. But it is an excellent rebirth — into conditions supremely conducive to further spiritual progress and ultimate liberation.

14:15.text: Dying in Rajas, one is born among those attached to action; dying in Tamas, one is born in the wombs of the deluded.
14:15.transliteration:
rajasi pralayaṃ gatvā karmasaṅgiṣu jāyate |
tathā pralīnas tamasi mūḍhayoniṣu jāyate ||15||
14:15.devanagari:
रजसि प्रलयं गत्वा कर्मसङ्गिषु जायते ।
तथा प्रलीनस्तमसि मूढयोनिषु जायते ॥ १५ ॥
14:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:15.explanation: Rājasic death: 'Karmasaṅgiṣu jāyate' — the Rājasic person is reborn among humans who are action-addicted — driven by desire, ambition, and ceaseless activity. This is a neutral rebirth: another human life, with all its Rājasic patterns intact. Tāmasic death: 'Mūḍhayoniṣu jāyate' — the Tāmasic person is reborn in 'foolish wombs' — animal, insect, or plant births — where the faculty of rational discrimination is severely limited. This is a downward trajectory. The profound implication: the state of consciousness at the moment of death — which Guṇa predominates — is the seed of the next birth. Hence the great importance in all Indian traditions of maintaining Sāttvic awareness at the time of death.

14:16.text: They say the fruit of righteous (Sāttvic) action is pure and flawless; the fruit of Rajas is suffering; and the fruit of Tamas is ignorance.
14:16.transliteration:
karmaṇaḥ sukṛtasyāhuḥ sāttvikaṃ nirmalaṃ phalam |
rajasas tu phalaṃ duḥkham ajñānaṃ tamasaḥ phalam ||16||
14:16.devanagari:
कर्मणः सुकृतस्याहुः सात्त्विकं निर्मलं फलम् ।
रजसस्तु फलं दुःखमज्ञानं तमसः फलम् ॥ १६ ॥
14:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:16.explanation: A clear enumeration of the fruits produced by actions performed under each Guṇa: Sāttvic action produces 'nirmalaṃ phalam' — a pure, untainted fruit: clarity, joy, spiritual knowledge, and inner peace. Rājasic action produces 'duḥkham' — suffering: even when worldly success is achieved through Rājasic effort, the inner state remains one of restlessness, comparison, fear of loss, and fundamental dissatisfaction. Tāmasic action produces 'ajñānam' — ignorance: it deepens the darkness, multiplying delusion and confusion. This is the moral ecology of the Guṇas: the seed determines the fruit. The aspirant's practical takeaway: if you want a pure life, act from Sattva; if you want to transcend the results altogether, transcend the Guṇas themselves.

14:17.text: From Sattva, knowledge arises; from Rajas, greed arises; from Tamas, heedlessness, delusion, and ignorance arise.
14:17.transliteration:
sattvāt sañjāyate jñānaṃ rajaso lobha eva ca |
pramādamohau tamaso bhavato 'jñānam eva ca ||17||
14:17.devanagari:
सत्त्वात्सञ्जायते ज्ञानं रजसो लोभ एव च ।
प्रमादमोहौ तमसो भवतोऽज्ञानमेव च ॥ १७ ॥
14:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:17.explanation: The psychological offspring of each Guṇa: Sattva → jñāna (discriminative wisdom, self-knowledge, clarity of mind). Rajas → lobha (greed, the hunger to accumulate, the inability to feel 'enough'). Tamas → pramāda + moha + ajñāna — Tamas generates a triple set of inner darkness because it is the densest Guṇa. This verse complements the previous verse: verse 16 described the external fruits of action; verse 17 describes the internal psychological products of each Guṇa. Combining them: Sattva breeds knowledge → leading to pure, luminous outcomes; Rajas breeds greed → leading to suffering despite apparent success; Tamas breeds delusion → leading to deeper ignorance in a self-perpetuating spiral.

14:18.text: Those established in Sattva rise upward; those in Rajas remain in the middle; those in Tamas, established in the lowest Guṇa's functions, go downward.
14:18.transliteration:
ūrdhvaṃ gacchanti sattvasthaḥ madhye tiṣṭhanti rājasāḥ |
jaghanyaguṇavṛttisthā adho gacchanti tāmasāḥ ||18||
14:18.devanagari:
ऊर्ध्वं गच्छन्ति सत्त्वस्था मध्ये तिष्ठन्ति राजसाः ।
जघन्यगुणवृत्तिस्था अधो गच्छन्ति तामसाः ॥ १८ ॥
14:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:18.explanation: This verse provides the cosmic vertical map of the three Guṇas' trajectory. 'Ūrdhvaṃ gacchanti sattvasthaḥ' — the Sāttvic ascend upward to higher realms (dev-loka, Brahma-loka), where conditions for further spiritual development are even more refined. 'Madhye tiṣṭhanti rājasāḥ' — the Rājasic remain in the middle — the human realm — continuing their cycle of desire-driven action and rebirth. 'Adho gacchanti tāmasāḥ' — the Tāmasic descend to lower births — animal, insect, and subhuman existences — where consciousness becomes increasingly contracted. This is not divine punishment but the natural gravitational law of the Guṇas: Sattva is light and rises; Tamas is dense and sinks; Rajas is agitated and holds its own level.

14:19.text: When the seer perceives no other agent than the Guṇas, and knows what is beyond the Guṇas — he attains my nature.
14:19.transliteration:
nānyaṃ guṇebhyaḥ kartāraṃ yadā draṣṭānupaśyati |
guṇebhyaś ca paraṃ vetti mad-bhāvaṃ so 'dhigacchati ||19||
14:19.devanagari:
नान्यं गुणेभ्यः कर्तारं यदा द्रष्टानुपश्यति ।
गुणेभ्यश्च परं वेत्ति मद्भावं सोऽधिगच्छति ॥ १९ ॥
14:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:19.explanation: This is the pivotal verse of the chapter's liberating insight. 'Nānyaṃ guṇebhyaḥ kartāram anupaśyati' — when the witness-consciousness clearly perceives that all action in the phenomenal world is performed by the Guṇas alone — that the Ātman (the true 'I') is not the doer — this is the ultimate application of the principle stated in 3.28 ('guṇā guṇeṣu vartante'). 'Guṇebhyaś ca paraṃ vetti' — and simultaneously knows that which is beyond all Guṇas — the pure witness-consciousness, Brahman — this is the twin knowledge of transcendence. 'Mad-bhāvaṃ so 'dhigacchati' — such a person attains my nature — the state of Brahman. This is not a future attainment but the recognition of what one always already is.

14:20.text: Having transcended these three Guṇas born of the body, the embodied soul, freed from birth, death, old age, and suffering, attains immortality.
14:20.transliteration:
guṇān etān atītya trīn dehī dehasamudbhavān |
janmamṛtyujarāduḥkhair vimukto 'mṛtam aśnute ||20||
14:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:20.explanation: 'Guṇān etān atītya trīn' — having transcended all three Guṇas — including even the subtly binding Sattva. 'Dehasamudbhavān' — these Guṇas belong to the body-mind complex, to Prakṛti, not to the Ātman — hence transcendence is not only possible but is the Ātman's very nature. The fourfold liberation: freed from 'janma' (birth), 'mṛtyu' (death), 'jarā' (old age — the decay of form), and 'duḥkha' (suffering of all kinds). 'Amṛtam aśnute' — such a being experiences amṛta, immortality — not merely long life but the deathless awareness of Brahman. This verse encapsulates the entire promise of the Gita: transcend the Guṇas, transcend mortality, rest in the eternal.

14:21.text: Arjuna said: O Lord, by what marks is one who has transcended these three Guṇas known? What is his conduct? And how does he transcend these three Guṇas?
14:21.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
kair liṅgais trīn guṇān etān atīto bhavati prabho |
kimācāraḥ kathaṃ caitāṃs trīn guṇān ativartate ||21||
14:21.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
कैर्लिङ्गैस्त्रीन्गुणानेतानतीतो भवति प्रभो ।
किमाचारः कथं चैतांस्त्रीन्गुणानतिवर्तते ॥ २१ ॥
14:21.speaker: Arjuna
14:21.explanation: Arjuna's question is threefold — the mark of an excellent student who seeks both theory and practice: (1) 'Kair liṅgaiḥ' — What are the external signs by which a Guṇātīta (one who has transcended the Guṇas) is recognizable? (2) 'Kimācāraḥ' — What is his behavior, conduct, and manner of living in the world? (3) 'Kathaṃ... ativartate' — What is the method, the process, by which one transcends the three Guṇas? This beautifully parallels Arjuna's earlier question in 2.54 ('sthitaprajñasya kā bhāṣā') about the marks of a man of steady wisdom. Together, these questions reveal the essential unity of the Sthitaprajña and the Guṇātīta — both describe the same state of liberated consciousness.

14:22.text: Śrī Bhagavān said: O Pāṇḍava, one who does not resist when illumination (Sattva), activity (Rajas), and delusion (Tamas) arise — and does not crave them when they cease — is said to have transcended the Guṇas.
14:22.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
prakāśaṃ ca pravṛttiṃ ca moham eva ca pāṇḍava |
na dveṣṭi sampravṛttāni na nivṛttāni kāṅkṣati ||22||
14:22.devanagari:
श्री भगवानुवाच ।
प्रकाशं च प्रवृत्तिं च मोहमेव च पाण्डव ।
न द्वेष्टि सम्प्रवृत्तानि न निवृत्तानि काङ्क्षति ॥ २२ ॥
14:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:22.explanation: The first and most profound mark of the Guṇātīta. Crucially: this is not a person in whom the Guṇas no longer arise — they still arise. Illumination (Sattva), activity (Rajas), and delusion (Tamas) continue to occur in the body-mind. But the Guṇātīta has two qualities: (1) When Guṇas arise — no aversion: even when Tamas or Rajas appear, there is no inner resistance, no self-judgment, no 'I shouldn't be feeling this.' (2) When Guṇas cease — no craving: when Sattva or peace passes away, there is no desperate grasping to maintain it. This is the perfection of the witness-state (sākṣī-bhāva) — pure observation without attraction or repulsion, without clinging or pushing away. This is the highest equanimity.

14:23.text: Seated as one unconcerned (neutral), undisturbed by the Guṇas, knowing that 'the Guṇas alone are functioning' — remaining firm and unshaken — he transcends the Guṇas.
14:23.transliteration:
udāsīnavad āsīno guṇair yo na vicālyate |
guṇā vartanta ity evaṃ yo 'vatiṣṭhati neṅgate ||23||
14:23.devanagari:
उदासीनवदासीनो गुणैर्यो न विचाल्यते ।
गुणा वर्तन्त इत्येव योऽवतिष्ठति नेङ्गते ॥ २३ ॥
14:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:23.explanation: 'Udāsīnavad āsīnaḥ' — seated 'like one who is indifferent' — this is deeply significant: not truly indifferent or withdrawn from life, but maintaining the inner posture of the uninvolved witness. 'Guṇair yo na vicālyate' — not shaken by the fluctuations of the Guṇas: neither elated by Sāttvic joy nor disturbed by Rājasic agitation nor sunk by Tāmasic dullness. 'Guṇā vartanta ity evam' — resting in the clear understanding: 'these are all movements of the Guṇas — nature acting upon nature — and my essential Self has no part in it.' 'Neṅgate' — and he does not waver from this recognition. This is the living embodiment of 3.28's liberating insight that 'guṇās guṇeṣu vartante.'

14:24.text: The same in pleasure and pain, self-abiding, seeing a clod of earth, stone, and gold as equal, the same toward the agreeable and disagreeable, steady in mind, equal in blame and self-praise —
14:24.transliteration:
samaduḥkhasukhaḥ svasthaḥ samaloṣṭāśmakāñcanaḥ |
tulyapriyāpriyo dhīras tulyanindātmasaṃstutiḥ ||24||
14:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:24.explanation: A radiant sequence of practical marks of the Guṇātīta: (1) 'Samaduḥkhasukhaḥ' — genuinely equanimitous in pleasure and pain — not suppressing one or performing the other, but genuinely transcended. (2) 'Svasthaḥ' — established in the Self ('sva' = Self, 'stha' = established) — the deepest meaning of 'well-being'. (3) 'Samaloṣṭāśmakāñcanaḥ' — a clod, a stone, and gold all appear as equal — a person beyond the artificial valuations society places on things. (4) 'Tulyapriyāpriyaḥ' — equal toward the pleasant and unpleasant. (5) 'Dhīraḥ' — steady, firm in wisdom. (6) 'Tulyanindātmasaṃstutiḥ' — unmoved by blame and unmoved by praise — the most demanding of all tests, for the ego clings most tenaciously to reputation.

14:25.text: Equal in honor and dishonor, equal toward friend and foe, renouncing all initiative born of ego — such a one is said to have transcended the Guṇas.
14:25.transliteration:
mānapamānayos tulyas tulyo mitrāripakṣayoḥ |
sarvārambhaparityāgī guṇātītaḥ sa ucyate ||25||
14:25.devanagari:
मानापमानयोस्तुल्यस्तुल्यो मित्रारिपक्षयोः ।
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी गुणातीतः स उच्यते ॥ २५ ॥
14:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:25.explanation: The description of the Guṇātīta concludes: (1) 'Mānapamānayos tulyas' — equal in honor and dishonor: the Guṇātīta's inner state is not shaped by others' treatment — neither puffed up by respect nor diminished by contempt. (2) 'Tulyo mitrāripakṣayoḥ' — genuinely equal toward friend and foe: this does not mean coldness, but freedom from the inner distortion of preference and aversion. (3) 'Sarvārambhaparityāgī' — renouncing all 'ārambha' — all ego-initiated projects and compulsive new undertakings. This person acts when dharma calls but without the ego's agenda of self-interest driving the initiation. 'Guṇātītaḥ sa ucyate' — precisely this person is called one who has transcended the Guṇas — the conclusion of the description that began in verse 22.

14:26.text: And one who serves Me with unswerving devotion (avyabhicāriṇī bhakti-yoga) — having transcended all these Guṇas — becomes fit for Brahman.
14:26.transliteration:
māṃ ca yo 'vyabhicāreṇa bhakti-yogena sevate |
sa guṇān samatītyaitān brahmabhūyāya kalpate ||26||
14:26.devanagari:
मां च योऽव्यभिचारेण भक्तियोगेन सेवते ।
स गुणान्समतीत्यैतान्ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते ॥ २६ ॥
14:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:26.explanation: The answer to Arjuna's third question: 'How does one transcend the Guṇas?' — through 'avyabhicāriṇī bhakti-yoga.' 'Avyabhicāriṇī' — unswerving, undivided, unwavering devotion that never turns aside to anything else. This is not mere emotional devotion but a whole-life orientation of consciousness toward the Divine — knowledge-grounded, continuous, and complete. 'Sevate' — serves — not merely worships in ritual, but offers the entire life as service. 'Brahmabhūyāya kalpate' — becomes fit for (i.e., naturally attains) Brahman-nature. This verse establishes one of the Gita's most important unifications: devotion (bhakti) is not separate from or inferior to jñāna — it is itself the most direct path to transcending the Guṇas and realizing Brahman.

14:27.text: For I am the very foundation of Brahman — of the immortal, the imperishable, of eternal dharma, and of absolute bliss.
14:27.transliteration:
brahmaṇo hi pratiṣṭhāham amṛtasyāvyayasya ca |
śāśvatasya ca dharmasya sukhasyaikāntikasya ca ||27||
14:27.devanagari:
ब्रह्मणो हि प्रतिष्ठाहममृतस्याव्ययस्य च ।
शाश्वतस्य च धर्मस्य सुखस्यैकान्तिकस्य च ॥ २७ ॥
14:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
14:27.explanation: The chapter's supreme and final declaration. 'Brahmaṇo hi pratiṣṭhāham' — I (Krishna, the supreme Puruṣottama) am the very foundation, the ground, the substrate of Brahman itself. This is one of the Gita's most audacious metaphysical statements: Brahman — the ultimate reality of the Upaniṣads — is itself grounded in, supported by, the personal Absolute (Bhagavān). Four aspects of what Krishna is the foundation of: 'Amṛtasya' — of immortality; 'Avyayasya' — of the imperishable; 'Śāśvatasya dharmasya' — of eternal dharma, the cosmic moral-spiritual order; 'Sukhasyaikāntikasya' — of absolute, undivided, unconditional bliss (not Guṇa-born happiness, but the natural bliss of Brahman). This closing verse unifies the chapter's entire teaching: the Guṇas bind, Brahman liberates, and the Supreme Person (Bhagavān) is the very ground of that liberation.

15:1.text: Śrī Bhagavān said: They speak of an eternal Aśvattha tree with roots above and branches below, whose leaves are the Vedic hymns. One who knows this tree is a knower of the Vedas.
15:1.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
ūrdhvamūlam adhaḥśākham aśvatthaṃ prāhur avyayam |
chandāṃsi yasya parṇāni yas taṃ veda sa vedavit ||1||
15:1.devanagari:
श्री भगवानुवाच ।
ऊर्ध्वमूलमधःशाखमश्वत्थं प्राहुरव्ययम् ।
छन्दांसि यस्य पर्णानि यस्तं वेद स वेदवित् ॥ १ ॥
15:1.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:1.explanation: 'Ūrdhvamūlam' — roots above: the supreme Brahman is the root of this cosmic tree, positioned 'above' in the transcendent, causal domain, the unmanifest source of all. 'Adhaḥśākham' — branches below: all the worlds from Brahmaloka down through the human realm to the subtlest hells are its downward-spreading branches. 'Aśvattham' — the Aśvattha (sacred fig, Ficus religiosa) is etymologically 'that which does not remain the same until tomorrow' (na śvas tiṣṭhati), pointing to the ceaseless flux of phenomenal existence. 'Avyayam' — yet it is called imperishable because the beginningless cycle of saṃsāra, the chain of birth and death, has no ultimate end in itself; its root cause (avidyā, Brahman as substratum) is never destroyed. 'Chandāṃsi parṇāni' — the Vedic hymns are its leaves: as leaves protect and sustain a tree, the Vedic rites and injunctions sustain and perpetuate the cosmic-social order (dharma) that keeps the tree of saṃsāra flourishing. 'Yas taṃ veda sa vedavit' — one who truly knows this tree — its inverted nature, its illusory appearance, its dependence on Brahman as its root — is the real knower of the Vedas, not merely one who has memorised the hymns. The metaphor is drawn directly from Kaṭha Upaniṣad (2.3.1): 'ūrdhvamūlo 'vākśākha eṣo aśvatthaḥ sanātanaḥ.'

15:2.text: Its branches spread both downward and upward, nourished by the three Guṇas, with sense objects as its shoots. And below, in the world of men, its roots extend downward, bound to action.
15:2.transliteration:
adhaś cordhvaṃ prasṛtās tasya śākhā
guṇapravṛddhā viṣayapravālāḥ |
adhaś ca mūlāny anusantatāni
karmānubandhīni manuṣyaloke ||2||
15:2.devanagari:
अधश्चोर्ध्वं प्रसृतास्तस्य शाखा
गुणप्रवृद्धा विषयप्रवालाः ।
अधश्च मूलान्यनुसन्ततानि
कर्मानुबन्धीनि मनुष्यलोके ॥ २ ॥
15:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:2.explanation: 'Adhaś cordhvaṃ prasṛtāḥ śākhāḥ' — the branches of this cosmic tree extend both upward (into the higher realms — Brahmaloka, the heavens, the abodes of the gods) and downward (into the lower realms — animal kingdoms, hellish states), encompassing all fourteen worlds. 'Guṇapravṛddhāḥ' — they are nourished by the three Guṇas: Sattva proliferates the higher branches (luminous, elevated states of being), Rajas the middle (active, striving human lives), and Tamas the lower (inert, deluded existences). 'Viṣayapravālāḥ' — sense objects (sound, touch, form, taste, smell) are the tender shoots of these branches — attractive, enticing, and precisely what draws the jīva outward and downward into greater entanglement. 'Manuṣyaloke karmānubandhīni mūlāni' — in the world of humans alone, secondary roots take hold bound to action (karma). This is significant: only human beings can generate new karma (meritorious or demeritorious), and these karmas become new roots driving the tree deeper into existence — new births, new bodies, new entanglements. The verse reveals saṃsāra's self-perpetuating structure: Guṇas nourish, senses tempt, karma roots — a system of mutual reinforcement that keeps the tree eternally flourishing.

15:3.text: Its true form is not perceived as such here; neither its end, nor its beginning, nor its real foundation is known. Cutting down this Aśvattha tree with its deep-entrenched roots by the firm sword of non-attachment...
15:3.transliteration:
na rūpam asyeha tathopalabhyate
nānto na cādir na ca sampratiṣṭhā |
aśvattham enaṃ suvīrūḍhamūlam
asaṅgaśastreṇa dṛḍhena chittvā ||3||
15:3.devanagari:
न रूपमस्येह तथोपलभ्यते
नान्तो न चादिर्न च सम्प्रतिष्ठा ।
अश्वत्थमेनं सुविरूढमूलम्
असङ्गशस्त्रेण दृढेन छित्त्वा ॥ ३ ॥
15:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:3.explanation: 'Na rūpam asyeha tathopalabhyate' — the true form of this cosmic tree of saṃsāra cannot be perceived here, in the condition of ordinary phenomenal experience clouded by Māyā. What we perceive of the world is a distorted, fragmentary image, not its metaphysical reality. Three-fold negation follows: 'Nāntaḥ' — no end can be perceived; the cosmos appears boundless, with no terminus. 'Na ādiḥ' — no beginning can be traced; saṃsāra is beginningless (anādi), the chain of cause and effect stretching backward without a first link. 'Na sampratiṣṭhā' — no real foundation or independent ground exists; the entire structure rests on Māyā (cosmic illusion), which is itself not an independent reality but superimposition upon Brahman. These three negations — no end, no beginning, no real foundation — constitute the Vedāntic characterisation of Māyā as anādi and anirvacanīya (indescribable). 'Suvīrūḍhamūlam' — yet despite its unreality, the roots are extremely deep-set, reinforced by beginningless vāsanās (latent impressions) and accumulated karma. 'Asaṅgaśastreṇa' — the only instrument that can sever this is asaṅga: radical non-attachment, the complete withdrawal of clinging identification from all objects, relationships, and roles. This verse continues into verse 4 as a single syntactic unit.

15:4.text: ...thereafter that supreme state must be sought, having reached which, none return again. Taking refuge in that very primal Person alone — from whom this ancient flow of creation has streamed forth.
15:4.transliteration:
tataḥ padaṃ tat parimārgitavyaṃ
yasmin gatā na nivartanti bhūyaḥ |
tam eva cādyaṃ puruṣaṃ prapadye
yataḥ pravṛttiḥ prasṛtā purāṇī ||4||
15:4.devanagari:
ततः पदं तत्परिमार्गितव्यं
यस्मिन्गता न निवर्तन्ति भूयः ।
तमेव चाद्यं पुरुषं प्रपद्ये
यतः प्रवृत्तिः प्रसृता पुराणी ॥ ४ ॥
15:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:4.explanation: This verse completes the sentence begun in verse 3. After severing the deeply-rooted tree of saṃsāra with the sword of non-attachment, the seeker must actively pursue the Supreme State. 'Padaṃ tat parimārgitavyam' — that state must be diligently sought (parimārgitavya), searched for with complete intentionality; liberation is not an accident but a deliberate quest. 'Yasmin gatā na nivartanti bhūyaḥ' — from which none ever return: this is the Gita's classic definition of mokṣa — irreversible liberation, the dissolution of the individual's false identity into the ocean of Brahman, from which no re-embodiment is possible. 'Tam eva cādyaṃ puruṣaṃ prapadye' — the means to reach this state is prapadye: taking complete refuge in the Primal Person (Ādi Puruṣa), the first and original Being, through total self-surrender (prapattiḥ). This is one of the Gita's pivotal statements on bhakti as the path of liberation. 'Yataḥ pravṛttiḥ prasṛtā purāṇī' — that Primal Person from whom the eternal ancient flow of manifestation has emanated — the very source of the cosmic tree whose branches we must eventually transcend. The instruction is complete: cut the bonds, then surrender to the source.

15:5.text: Free from pride and delusion, with the evil of attachment conquered, ever dwelling in the Self, with desires completely extinguished, liberated from the pairs of opposites known as pleasure and pain — the undeluded wise reach that imperishable state.
15:5.transliteration:
nirmānamohā jitasaṅgadoṣā
adhyātmanityā vinivṛttakāmāḥ |
dvandvair vimuktāḥ sukhaduḥkhasaṃjñair
gacchanty amūḍhāḥ padam avyayaṃ tat ||5||
15:5.devanagari:
निर्मानमोहा जितसङ्गदोषा
अध्यात्मनित्या विनिवृत्तकामाः ।
द्वन्द्वैर्विमुक्ताः सुखदुःखसंज्ञैर्
गच्छन्त्यमूढाः पदमव्ययं तत् ॥ ५ ॥
15:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:5.explanation: This verse enumerates five inner qualifications of those who reach the imperishable state described in verses 3–4. (1) 'Nirmānamohāḥ' — free from māna (the ego's craving for honour, status, recognition) and moha (delusion, false identification with body-mind-world). These are the two fundamental manifestations of avidyā at the psychological level. (2) 'Jitasaṅgadoṣāḥ' — having conquered the fault of saṅga (attachment): recognising attachment not as a neutral preference but as a defect (doṣa), an active impediment, and having genuinely overcome it. (3) 'Adhyātmanityāḥ' — ever established in adhyātma: the continuous inner practice of Self-inquiry, Self-knowledge, Self-abidance. The mind is habitually turned inward toward the Ātman rather than outward toward objects. (4) 'Vinivṛttakāmāḥ' — with desires completely withdrawn (not merely suppressed): this is the state where the pull of all desire — gross and subtle — has naturally subsided through wisdom. (5) 'Sukhaduḥkhasaṃjñair dvandvair vimuktāḥ' — liberated from the pairs of opposites called pleasure and pain: established in equanimity beyond all dualistic fluctuation. 'Amūḍhāḥ' — the undeluded: those whose vision is clear and unobstructed by Māyā. These are those who arrive at 'padam avyayam' — the imperishable state of Brahman.

15:6.text: That which the sun does not illumine, nor the moon, nor fire — that is my supreme abode, reaching which, none return.
15:6.transliteration:
na tad bhāsayate sūryo na śaśāṅko na pāvakaḥ |
yad gatvā na nivartante tad dhāma paramaṃ mama ||6||
15:6.devanagari:
न तद्भासयते सूर्यो न शशाङ्को न पावकः ।
यद्गत्वा न निवर्तन्ते तद्धाम परमं मम ॥ ६ ॥
15:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:6.explanation: 'Na tad bhāsayate sūryaḥ' — even the sun, the most brilliant of all phenomenal luminaries, cannot illumine that supreme state. Nor the moon; nor fire — the three great lights of the phenomenal world are all negated. Why? Because all three are external, objective lights — they illumine other things, but they are themselves perceived by a higher awareness. The Supreme Abode (paramaṃ dhāma) is self-luminous (svayaṃprakāśa): it is Pure Consciousness itself — the very light by which all other lights are known. It does not require an external illuminator because it IS the ground of all illumination. This verse directly echoes one of the most celebrated passages of the Upaniṣads — Kaṭha Upaniṣad (2.2.15), Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad (2.2.10), and Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad (6.14): 'na tatra sūryo bhāti na candratārakam' — there the sun does not shine, nor the moon and stars. 'Yad gatvā na nivartante' — reaching which none return: the irreversibility of liberation. Unlike the heavens (svarga), which are fruits of karma and from which one descends when the merit is exhausted, this Supreme Abode is beyond karma and time altogether. 'Tad dhāma paramaṃ mama' — this is my supreme abode: not a geographic location but the state of Pure Consciousness, identical with Brahman, which is Krishna's essential nature.

15:7.text: An eternal portion of myself, having become the individual soul (jīva) in the world of the living, draws to itself the five senses and the mind as the sixth — all abiding in Prakṛti.
15:7.transliteration:
mamaivāṃśo jīvaloke jīvabhūtaḥ sanātanaḥ |
manaḥṣaṣṭhānīndriyāṇi prakṛtisthāni karṣati ||7||
15:7.devanagari:
ममैवांशो जीवलोके जीवभूतः सनातनः ।
मनःषष्ठानीन्द्रियाणि प्रकृतिस्थानि कर्षति ॥ ७ ॥
15:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:7.explanation: 'Mamaivāṃśaḥ' — an eternal portion of me alone. This is one of the most profound and carefully worded statements in the Gita regarding the relationship between the individual soul (jīvātman) and the Supreme. 'Aṃśa' here does not mean a material fragment (as Brahman is indivisible and partless) but a reflection, an apparent individuation arising through the limiting adjunct (upādhi) of body-mind — analogous to the reflection of the infinite sky appearing as a finite sky within a pot, or the one sun appearing as many reflections in many pools. 'Sanātanaḥ' — this jīvātman is eternal, beginningless, and deathless — never truly born or destroyed, only apparently so. 'Manaḥṣaṣṭhāni indriyāṇi' — the six instruments: the five organs of perception (ear, skin, eye, tongue, nose) plus the mind as the sixth — the internal instrument that coordinates and interprets the five. 'Prakṛtisthāni karṣati' — the jīva draws these toward itself, making them its instruments; they are rooted in Prakṛti but activated by the pull of the jīva's identification. This verse establishes the paradox at the heart of the jīva's situation: divine in essence (eternal portion of the Supreme), yet apparently limited by Prakṛtic instruments.

15:8.text: When this lord (the jīva) obtains a body and when it departs from it, it takes these (senses and mind) along with it — just as the wind carries fragrances from their source.
15:8.transliteration:
śarīraṃ yad avāpnoti yac cāpy utkrāmatīśvaraḥ |
gṛhītvaitāni saṃyāti vāyur gandhān ivāśayāt ||8||
15:8.devanagari:
शरीरं यदवाप्नोति यच्चाप्युत्क्रामतीश्वरः ।
गृहीत्वैतानि संयाति वायुर्गन्धानिवाशयात् ॥ ८ ॥
15:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:8.explanation: 'Īśvaraḥ' — here the word 'Īśvara' is applied to the jīva, the individual soul, in the sense that it is the lord and ruler of its own body (not the Supreme Lord in this context). 'Yad avāpnoti śarīram' — when it acquires a body at birth; 'yac cāpy utkrāmati' — and when it departs from it at death. In both transitions — birth and death — the jīva does not travel alone. 'Gṛhītvaitāni saṃyāti' — it takes these (mind and five senses in their subtle form) along with it. This describes the sūkṣma śarīra (subtle body) — the vehicle of the soul between embodiments, carrying all its vāsanās (latent impressions), saṃskāras (deep conditioning), unfulfilled desires, and karmic residues from one life to the next. 'Vāyur gandhān ivāśayāt' — the simile is exquisite in its precision: wind, though invisible, carries the equally invisible particles of fragrance from flowers. The wind does not 'carry' in a gross way; the fragrance permeates it. Similarly, the subtle senses permeate the subtle body and travel invisibly with the soul. This verse is the scriptural basis for the doctrine of metempsychosis (transmigration) in Vedānta.

15:9.text: Presiding over the ear, eye, the organ of touch, taste, and smell, as well as the mind — this jīva experiences the sense objects.
15:9.transliteration:
śrotraṃ cakṣuḥ sparśanaṃ ca rasanaṃ ghrāṇam eva ca |
adhiṣṭhāya manaś cāyaṃ viṣayān upasevate ||9||
15:9.devanagari:
श्रोत्रं चक्षुः स्पर्शनं च रसनं घ्राणमेव च ।
अधिष्ठाय मनश्चायं विषयानुपसेवते ॥ ९ ॥
15:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:9.explanation: 'Śrotram' — the ear: the faculty of hearing, apprehending sound. 'Cakṣuḥ' — the eye: apprehending form-and-colour. 'Sparśanam' — the skin: apprehending touch, texture, temperature. 'Rasanam' — the tongue: apprehending taste. 'Ghrāṇam' — the nose: apprehending smell. 'Manaś ca' — and the mind: the inner instrument (antaḥkaraṇa) that coordinates, interprets, and synthesises all sensory data. 'Adhiṣṭhāya' — presiding over, inhabiting: the jīvātman does not merely passively receive sensory input; it actively dwells within and operates through these six instruments as their conscious ground. 'Viṣayān upasevate' — it experiences, enjoys, serves the sense objects. This verse reveals an important metaphysical point: the sense organs themselves are inert instruments — they do not truly 'experience' anything. The real experiencer (bhoktā) is the jīvātman, which inhabits them and thereby illumines their objects with consciousness. Together, verses 7, 8, and 9 give a complete picture of the jīva's situation: its divine origin (v.7), its mode of migration (v.8), and its mode of experience in embodiment (v.9).

15:10.text: The deluded do not perceive this (jīva) as it departs, dwells, or experiences, united with the Guṇas. Those with the eye of knowledge perceive it.
15:10.transliteration:
utkrāmantaṃ sthitaṃ vāpi bhuñjānaṃ vā guṇānvitam |
vimūḍhā nānupaśyanti paśyanti jñānacakṣuṣaḥ ||10||
15:10.devanagari:
उत्क्रामन्तं स्थितं वापि भुञ्जानं वा गुणान्वितम् ।
विमूढा नानुपश्यन्ति पश्यन्ति ज्ञानचक्षुषः ॥ १० ॥
15:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:10.explanation: Three temporal states of the jīvātman are named: 'Utkrāmantam' — departing, i.e., at the moment of death when the subtle body leaves the physical frame; 'sthitam' — dwelling, i.e., during the entire span of life as it inhabits the body; 'bhuñjānam' — experiencing, i.e., in the act of sensory and mental experience moment to moment.
