'Guṇānvitam' — coloured by, enveloped in the three Guṇas — even as the soul migrates, it is wrapped in its Guṇic conditioning, its particular Prakṛtic colouring. 'Vimūḍhāḥ nānupaśyanti' — the thoroughly deluded (vimūḍha) do not see this jīva in any of these states. They see only the physical body: a corpse at death, a personality during life, a sensory organism during experience. The inner witness — the jīvātman — remains invisible to their gross perception. 'Paśyanti jñānacakṣuṣaḥ' — those who possess the eye of knowledge (jñāna-cakṣus): the inner organ of discriminative wisdom, developed through sustained Ātma-vicāra (Self-inquiry), meditation, and scriptural study. The Ātman is not perceived by the physical eyes or even by ordinary intellect — only by the purified, refined instrument of jñāna does the soul become self-evident.

15:11.text: Striving yogīs behold this (jīva) established in the Self. But the unrefined — those of uncultivated inner being — though striving, do not perceive it.
15:11.transliteration:
yatanto yoginaś cainaṃ paśyanty ātmany avasthitam |
yatanto 'py akṛtātmāno nainaṃ paśyanty acetasaḥ ||11||
15:11.devanagari:
यतन्तो योगिनश्चैनं पश्यन्त्यात्मन्यवस्थितम् ।
यतन्तोऽप्यकृतात्मानो नैनं पश्यन्त्यचेतसः ॥ ११ ॥
15:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:11.explanation: 'Yatanto yoginaḥ' — those who are diligent yogīs, actively engaged in practice (meditation, self-inquiry, inner discipline): they perceive this jīvātman 'ātmany avasthitam' — established within their own Self, in the cave of the heart. The Ātman need not be sought elsewhere; it is the very ground of one's own being, and the yogī who turns attention inward discovers it directly. 'Yatanto 'py akṛtātmānaḥ' — but even those who make effort, if they are 'akṛtātmā' — one whose inner being is not yet refined, purified, prepared — they do not perceive it. 'Kṛtātmā' (literally 'one who has made the Self') refers to one whose antaḥkaraṇa (inner instrument: mind, intellect, ego, memory) has been refined through sustained practice of śama (mental calm), dama (sense-control), titikṣā (forbearance), śraddhā (faith), and mumukṣutva (intense desire for liberation). 'Acetasaḥ' — those of impure or dull consciousness, lacking discriminative awareness. This verse is a crucial teaching on the psychology of spiritual realisation: effort (yatna) is necessary but not sufficient. Inner purification (kṛtātmatā) is the indispensable prerequisite — without a refined instrument, the subtlest of realities remains invisible.

15:12.text: Know that the light in the sun which illumines the entire world, and that which is in the moon, and that which is in fire — know that brilliance to be mine.
15:12.transliteration:
yad ādityagataṃ tejo jagad bhāsayate 'khilam |
yac candramasi yac cāgnau tat tejo viddhi māmakam ||12||
15:12.devanagari:
यदादित्यगतं तेजो जगद्भासयतेऽखिलम् ।
यच्चन्द्रमसि यच्चाग्नौ तत्तेजो विद्धि मामकम् ॥ १२ ॥
15:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:12.explanation: Beginning with verse 12 and continuing through verse 15, Krishna reveals his presence as the innermost power within all cosmic phenomena. 'Yad ādityagataṃ tejaḥ' — the effulgence that resides in the sun: not merely light in a physical sense but the fundamental luminous energy that makes all perception possible. 'Jagad bhāsayate 'khilam' — that illumines the entire world (all fourteen realms of existence). 'Yac candramasi' — and the tejas in the moon: the soft, nourishing, reflective light that sustains lunar cycles, tides, plant growth, and the emotional life of all beings. 'Yac cāgnau' — and the tejas in fire: the transformative energy of combustion, digestion, purification — the sacred power of Agni. 'Tat tejo viddhi māmakam' — know all of this brilliance as mine. This is the Vedāntic 'antaryāmi' doctrine: the Lord is the inner controller and ultimate source of every natural force in the cosmos. The three luminaries — sun, moon, fire — represent the three great light-principles of the universe (solar, lunar, terrestrial), and Krishna declares all three as manifestations of his own divine radiance. This teaching transforms the practitioner's relationship with the natural world: every beam of sunlight becomes a glimpse of the Divine.

15:13.text: Entering the earth, I sustain all beings by my energy; and becoming the Soma (moon), full of rasa (vital essence), I nourish all plants.
15:13.transliteration:
gām āviśya ca bhūtāni dhārayāmy aham ojasā |
puṣṇāmi cauṣadhīḥ sarvāḥ somo bhūtvā rasātmakaḥ ||13||
15:13.devanagari:
गामाविश्य च भूतानि धारयाम्यहमोजसा ।
पुष्णामि चौषधीः सर्वाः सोमो भूत्वा रसात्मकः ॥ १३ ॥
15:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:13.explanation: 'Gām āviśya' — entering the earth (gā, go = bhūmi, the earth principle): Krishna pervades and in-dwells the earth element itself. 'Bhūtāni dhārayāmy aham ojasā' — I sustain all beings by my ojas (radiant vital force, divine energy). The earth's capacity to bear the enormous weight and diversity of all life — without collapsing, without failing — is because the Lord himself indwells it and empowers its sustaining capacity. This is the Vedāntic vision: matter is not inert but divinely animated. 'Puṣṇāmi cauṣadhīḥ sarvāḥ' — I nourish all herbs, plants, medicines, grains — the entire plant kingdom. 'Somo bhūtvā rasātmakaḥ' — becoming Soma (the moon), which is the essence of rasa (liquid vital essence, nourishment). In the Vedic cosmological understanding, the moon's nectar (amṛta) descends as dew and rain, absorbed by the earth and carried through the sap (rasa) of all plants, granting them life and nutritive power. The food chain — sunlight, rain, earth, plants, animals, humans — is not a mechanical process but a divine cycle of nourishment sustained by the Lord's own indwelling presence at every link.

15:14.text: Having become Vaiśvānara (the universal digestive fire), dwelling in the bodies of living beings, and united with the prāṇa and apāna vital breaths, I digest the four types of food.
15:14.transliteration:
ahaṃ vaiśvānaro bhūtvā prāṇināṃ deham āśritaḥ |
prāṇāpānasamāyuktaḥ pacāmy annaṃ caturvidham ||14||
15:14.devanagari:
अहं वैश्वानरो भूत्वा प्राणिनां देहमाश्रितः ।
प्राणापानसमायुक्तः पचाम्यन्नं चतुर्विधम् ॥ १४ ॥
15:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:14.explanation: 'Vaiśvānara' — the fire that dwells in all (viśva) beings (nara): specifically the jāṭharāgni, the gastric fire, the metabolic fire of digestion located in the abdomen. This is one of the most concrete and intimate of Krishna's cosmic declarations — he is not only the fire in the external sun and in the sacrificial altar but the fire within every living body. 'Prāṇināṃ deham āśritaḥ' — inhabiting the bodies of all breathing creatures, without exception — humans, animals, birds, all. 'Prāṇāpānasamāyuktaḥ' — in union with prāṇa (the upward-moving vital breath) and apāna (the downward-moving vital breath): these two prāṇic currents fan the digestive fire and regulate the entire metabolic process. 'Pacāmy annaṃ caturvidham' — I digest the four types of food: (1) bhakṣya — food that is chewed; (2) bhojya — food that is swallowed in liquid form; (3) lehya — food that is licked; (4) coṣya — food that is sucked. The implication is transformative: the body's capacity to digest and assimilate nourishment is not a mechanical chemical process but a direct expression of divine power. The Lord himself is the digestive fire in every body. Every meal partaken is, in this vision, an act of cosmic participation.

15:15.text: And I am seated in the hearts of all. From me alone come memory, knowledge, and their removal. I alone am to be known by all the Vedas; I am the author of Vedānta and the knower of the Vedas.
15:15.transliteration:
sarvasya cāhaṃ hṛdi sanniviṣṭo
mattaḥ smṛtir jñānam apohanaṃ ca |
vedaiś ca sarvair aham eva vedyo
vedāntakṛd vedavid eva cāham ||15||
15:15.devanagari:
सर्वस्य चाहं हृदि सन्निविष्टो
मत्तः स्मृतिर्ज्ञानमपोहनं च ।
वेदैश्च सर्वैरहमेव वेद्यो
वेदान्तकृद्वेदविदेव चाहम् ॥ १५ ॥
15:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:15.explanation: This verse is the culmination of verses 12–15, and simultaneously one of the most comprehensive metaphysical statements in all of Sanskrit scripture. 'Sarvasya cāhaṃ hṛdi sanniviṣṭaḥ' — I am seated (sanniviṣṭa: deeply, intimately installed) in the heart of every being. This is the doctrine of the antaryāmin (the inner controller), elaborated in the Antaryāmi-Brāhmaṇa of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad (3.7): the Lord dwells in every being, directing from within, unknown to the being itself. 'Mattaḥ smṛtiḥ' — from me alone comes memory: the capacity to recall past experience, which makes continuity of identity possible, is a divine gift. 'Jñānam' — from me comes knowledge: the cognitive faculty, the power of recognition and understanding. 'Apohanaṃ ca' — and from me comes the withdrawal of both: forgetting and loss of understanding are equally within the Lord's sovereign dispensation. This means the human mind is never truly autonomous — it operates entirely within the field of divine intelligence. 'Vedaiś ca sarvair aham eva vedyaḥ' — by all the Vedas, I alone am to be known: the entire Vedic corpus — Ṛg, Sāma, Yajur, Atharva, with all their branches, Upaniṣads, Brāhmaṇas — has one ultimate goal: the knowledge of Brahman-Puruṣottama. 'Vedāntakṛt' — I am the author of Vedānta (the Upaniṣads, the end and essence of the Vedas). 'Vedavid eva cāham' — and I am the real knower of the Vedas: the scriptures speak of me, and I am the one who truly comprehends what they speak of.

15:16.text: Two Puruṣas exist in the world — the perishable (Kṣara) and the imperishable (Akṣara). The Kṣara is all beings; the Kūṭastha (the immovable) is called the Akṣara.
15:16.transliteration:
dvāv imau puruṣau loke kṣaraś cākṣara eva ca |
kṣaraḥ sarvāṇi bhūtāni kūṭastho 'kṣara ucyate ||16||
15:16.devanagari:
द्वाविमौ पुरुषौ लोके क्षरश्चाक्षर एव च ।
क्षरः सर्वाणि भूतानि कूटस्थोऽक्षर उच्यते ॥ १६ ॥
15:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:16.explanation: Here begins the chapter's central philosophical revelation — a three-tier doctrine of ultimate reality. 'Dvāv imau puruṣau' — two Puruṣas (principles of being) are declared to exist in this world. First, 'Kṣaraḥ' — the perishable, the mutable, the ever-changing: 'sarvāṇi bhūtāni' — the totality of all embodied beings across all realms and species — the entire manifest world of individual forms, each subject to birth, change, decay, and death. This is the domain of saṃsāra. Second, 'Akṣaraḥ' — the imperishable, the immutable, the indestructible. 'Kūṭasthaḥ' — kūṭa means a hammer-head (anvil), something fixed, immovable despite constant impact; 'kūṭastha' thus means supremely stable, absolutely unmoved by the changes of the phenomenal world. This refers to the unmanifest Prakṛti (Māyā), the cosmic causal ground that persists through all cosmic cycles unchanged, or according to some commentators, the aggregate of all individual jīvas in their pure, unmanifest state. The verse deliberately introduces only two principles at this stage, setting up the third — which surpasses both — in the verses that follow.

15:17.text: But the Supreme Person is indeed another — spoken of as Paramātman, who, as the imperishable Īśvara, pervades and sustains all three worlds.
15:17.transliteration:
uttamaḥ puruṣas tv anyaḥ paramātmety udāhṛtaḥ |
yo lokatrayam āviśya bibharty avyaya īśvaraḥ ||17||
15:17.devanagari:
उत्तमः पुरुषस्त्वन्यः परमात्मेत्युदाहृतः ।
यो लोकत्रयमाविश्य बिभर्त्यव्यय ईश्वरः ॥ १७ ॥
15:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:17.explanation: 'Uttamaḥ puruṣas tv anyaḥ' — but the highest Puruṣa is entirely other — different in kind, not merely in degree, from both the Kṣara and the Akṣara. This is the chapter's supreme revelation: a third, transcendent principle that surpasses both the mutable world of beings and the immutable unmanifest ground. 'Paramātmā iti udāhṛtaḥ' — this highest Puruṣa is called Paramātman (the Supreme Self, the Supreme Soul) in the revealed scriptures — Upaniṣads, Vedānta. 'Avyaya Īśvaraḥ' — the imperishable (avyaya: without decrease or deterioration) Sovereign Lord (Īśvara): not just the unmanifest ground but the actively sovereign, omniscient, omnipotent Ruler of all. 'Lokatrayam āviśya' — pervading, entering into all three worlds (the earthly, the intermediate, and the celestial — or more broadly, the gross, subtle, and causal realms of existence). 'Bibharti' — sustaining, upholding, nourishing all three worlds: this Supreme Person is not a distant, uninvolved Absolute but the immanent sustainer of all existence. He transcends the Kṣara (nothing in the manifest world conditions him) and surpasses the Akṣara (he is not passive or merely potential, but actively sovereign). This is Puruṣottama.

15:18.text: Since I transcend the perishable and am higher even than the imperishable, I am celebrated in the world and in the Veda as Puruṣottama — the Supreme Person.
15:18.transliteration:
yasmāt kṣaram atīto 'ham akṣarād api cottamaḥ |
ato 'smi loke vede ca prathitaḥ puruṣottamaḥ ||18||
15:18.devanagari:
यस्मात्क्षरमतीतोऽहमक्षरादपि चोत्तमः ।
अतोऽस्मि लोके वेदे च प्रथितः पुरुषोत्तमः ॥ १८ ॥
15:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:18.explanation: 'Yasmāt kṣaram atīto 'ham' — because I entirely transcend the Kṣara (the perishable world of all beings): the entire domain of change, birth, decay, and death has no hold upon me whatsoever. 'Akṣarād api cottamaḥ' — and I am higher even than the Akṣara (the unmanifest, the Kūṭastha): I surpass even the immutable causal ground because while the Akṣara is merely inert, unconscious potentiality — the unmanifest womb of creation — Puruṣottama is living, knowing, willing, sustaining, the sovereign Consciousness that pervades and upholds all. 'Ataḥ asmi loke vede ca prathitaḥ puruṣottamaḥ' — therefore I am celebrated in both loka (popular tradition, the common wisdom of the world) and veda (the highest revealed scriptures) as Puruṣottama — the Uttama (highest) among all Puruṣas. The title 'Puruṣottama' encapsulates the entire three-tier cosmology of this chapter: beyond the perishable (Kṣara), transcending the imperishable (Akṣara), the living Sovereign Consciousness who sustains all three worlds from within. This verse is the pivot upon which the entire Gita's metaphysics turns — the supreme self-revelation of the Absolute as personal, transcendent, and immanent simultaneously.

15:19.text: O Bhārata, one who, without delusion, thus knows me as Puruṣottama — the Supreme Person — knowing all, worships me with his entire being.
15:19.transliteration:
yo mām evam asammūḍho jānāti puruṣottamam |
sa sarvavid bhajati māṃ sarvabhāvena bhārata ||19||
15:19.devanagari:
यो मामेवमसम्मूढो जानाति पुरुषोत्तमम् ।
स सर्वविद्भजति मां सर्वभावेन भारत ॥ १९ ॥
15:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:19.explanation: 'Yo mām evam asammūḍhaḥ jānāti puruṣottamam' — one who knows me as Puruṣottama in precisely this way — through the three-tiered understanding just expounded (Kṣara, Akṣara, Puruṣottama) — without delusion or confusion (asammūḍha): not with a partial, borrowed, or merely intellectual understanding, but with clear, unobstructed, direct wisdom. This knowing is not mere information retention; it is the settled, realised knowledge of Brahman as the living, sustaining, transcendent-immanent Puruṣottama. 'Sa sarvavit' — that person becomes omniscient (sarvavit): not necessarily in the sense of knowing all factual data, but in the deeper sense of knowing the Source of all — the ground from which all has emerged. To know the root is to know the tree. The Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad (1.3) makes the same claim: 'kasmin nu bhagavo vijñāte sarvam idaṃ vijñātaṃ bhavati' — knowing which, all this becomes known. 'Bhajati māṃ sarvabhāvena' — he worships me with his entire being (sarvabhāvena: with all of his modes of existence — mind, intellect, heart, vital breath, action). This verse reveals the magnificent integration at the heart of the Gita's teaching: the highest knowledge (jñāna) of Puruṣottama naturally and spontaneously overflows into the highest devotion (bhakti) — they are not separate paths but two aspects of a single awakening.

15:20.text: Thus this most secret scripture has been declared by me, O sinless one. Knowing this, O Bhārata, one becomes truly wise and all duties are fulfilled.
15:20.transliteration:
iti guhyatamaṃ śāstram idam uktaṃ mayānagha |
etad buddhvā buddhimān syāt kṛtakṛtyaś ca bhārata ||20||
15:20.devanagari:
इति गुह्यतमं शास्त्रमिदमुक्तं मयानघ ।
एतद्बुद्ध्वा बुद्धिमान्स्यात्कृतकृत्यश्च भारत ॥ २० ॥
15:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
15:20.explanation: 'Iti guhyatamaṃ śāstram idam uktaṃ mayā' — thus has this most secret teaching been declared by me. 'Guhyatamam' — the superlative of guhya (secret): the most secret of all teachings, more secret than even the knowledge of the field and the knower of the field (Chapter 13) or the knowledge of the three Guṇas (Chapter 14). Why most secret? Because this knowledge of Puruṣottama is the subtlest, the hardest to truly grasp and assimilate — it requires a thoroughly purified inner instrument, a mind divested of gross identification with the perishable (Kṣara) and not content merely with the recognition of the imperishable (Akṣara), but reaching through and beyond to the living, personal, sustaining Supreme. 'Anagha' — O sinless one: by addressing Arjuna as 'without sin' (an-agha), Krishna signals that the qualified recipient of this supreme teaching must be one of pure heart, free from the obscuration of accumulated impurities. This is the teaching's inherent selectivity — not a sectarian exclusion but a natural requirement of inner preparation. 'Etad buddhvā buddhimān syāt' — knowing this, one becomes truly intelligent (buddhimān): not merely clever, but one whose buddhi (discriminative intellect) is established in the knowledge of Brahman as Puruṣottama, the ground of all. 'Kṛtakṛtyaś ca' — and becomes kṛtakṛtya: one who has accomplished all that was to be accomplished, for whom nothing further remains to be done. This is the Vedāntic descriptor of the liberated state — when the supreme knowledge is realised, the entire purpose of human existence is fulfilled. No further duty remains; no further seeking is needed. The seeker has become the sought.

16:1.text: Śrī Bhagavān said: Fearlessness, purity of being, steadfastness in the yoga of knowledge, charity, self-restraint, sacrifice, scriptural study, austerity, and straightforwardness —
16:1.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
abhayaṃ sattvasaṃśuddhir jñānayogavyavasthitiḥ |
dānaṃ damaś ca yajñaś ca svādhyāyas tapa ārjavam ||1||
16:1.devanagari:
श्री भगवानुवाच ।
अभयं सत्त्वसंशुद्धिर्ज्ञानयोगव्यवस्थितिः ।
दानं दमश्च यज्ञश्च स्वाध्यायस्तप आर्जवम् ॥ १ ॥
16:1.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:1.explanation: This verse opens the enumeration of daivī sampad (divine endowment), which spans verses 1–3 and lists twenty-six qualities in total. 'Abhayam' — fearlessness: the premier divine quality. One who is grounded in the Ātman fears nothing — not death, not dishonour, not loss. This fearlessness is not bravado but the natural fruit of Ātma-jñāna, the knowledge that one's essential Self is indestructible and beyond all harm. 'Sattva-saṃśuddhiḥ' — purity of being (sattva = inner nature, mind-substance; saṃśuddhi = thorough purification): the inner instrument of mind, intellect, and ego completely cleansed of the distortions of rajas and tamas, becoming transparent and clear like a polished mirror. 'Jñāna-yoga-vyavasthitiḥ' — steadfast abidance in the yoga of knowledge: not merely theoretical familiarity with the Vedāntic teachings, but the disciplined, sustained practice of keeping the mind oriented toward the Self, even in the midst of daily activity. 'Dānam' — charity: giving to the right recipient, at the right place and time, without expectation of return — the outward expression of the inner conviction that nothing truly belongs to the ego-self. 'Damaḥ' — self-restraint of the external senses: the deliberate, trained capacity to withdraw the senses from their objects when wisdom requires it. 'Yajñaḥ' — sacrifice in all its forms: ritual, service, the offering of every act to the Divine. 'Svādhyāyaḥ' — self-study through scripture: the regular, deep study of the Vedas and Upaniṣads that nourishes and deepens the inner life of wisdom. 'Tapaḥ' — austerity: the disciplined willingness to undergo discomfort for spiritual growth — body, speech, and mind (elaborated in Ch. 17). 'Ārjavam' — uprightness, straightforwardness: the alignment of thought, word, and deed — no duplicity, no hidden agendas, the inner and outer made identical.

16:2.text: Non-violence, truthfulness, freedom from anger, renunciation, tranquility, absence of calumny, compassion for all beings, freedom from covetousness, gentleness, modesty, and absence of fickleness —
16:2.transliteration:
ahiṃsā satyam akrodhas tyāgaḥ śāntir apaiśunam |
dayā bhūteṣv aloluptvaṃ mārdavaṃ hrīr acāpalam ||2||
16:2.devanagari:
अहिंसा सत्यमक्रोधस्त्यागः शान्तिरपैशुनम् ।
दया भूतेष्वलोलुप्त्वं मार्दवं ह्रीरचापलम् ॥ २ ॥
16:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:2.explanation: The second group of eleven qualities of daivī sampad. 'Ahiṃsā' — non-violence in thought, speech, and action toward all beings: the foundational ethical principle of the Gita's moral framework, rooted in the recognition of the one Ātman dwelling in all. 'Satyam' — truthfulness: speaking what is true, beneficial, and kind; not merely factual accuracy but alignment with the deeper Truth of Brahman. 'Akrodhaḥ' — freedom from anger: the capacity to remain unshaken even when provoked. Anger is the smoke of ignorance — where true knowledge is established, anger loses its fuel. 'Tyāgaḥ' — renunciation of the fruits of action and of ego-identification with results — performing all duty without claiming ownership of outcomes. 'Śāntiḥ' — tranquility: not the absence of external stimulus but a deep, unshakeable inner stillness that remains undisturbed regardless of circumstances. 'Apaiśunam' — absence of malicious gossip or tale-bearing: the discipline of not diminishing others behind their backs — the highest expression of verbal purity. 'Dayā bhūteṣu' — compassion toward all living beings: not selective sympathy but universal compassion arising from the direct perception of the same Self in all — the natural emotional expression of advaitic awareness. 'Aloluptvam' — freedom from covetousness: the complete absence of craving — the senses not rushing after sense objects but remaining calm even in their presence. 'Mārdavam' — gentleness, softness, inner flexibility: the antithesis of rigidity and aggression; the quality that makes one approachable and kind. 'Hrīḥ' — modesty, shame in the sense of a natural inner resistance to doing what is wrong — moral sensitivity that functions as an internal ethical compass. 'Acāpalam' — steadiness: the absence of unnecessary, restless movement of body, speech, and eyes — composed, deliberate, unhurried behaviour.

16:3.text: Vigour, forgiveness, fortitude, purity, freedom from malice, and absence of excessive pride — these are the (marks of one) born to the divine endowment, O Bhārata.
16:3.transliteration:
tejaḥ kṣamā dhṛtiḥ śaucam adroho nātimānitā |
bhavanti sampadaṃ daivīm abhijātasya bhārata ||3||
16:3.devanagari:
तेजः क्षमा धृतिः शौचमद्रोहो नातिमानिता ।
भवन्ति सम्पदं दैवीमभिजातस्य भारत ॥ ३ ॥
16:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:3.explanation: The final six qualities of daivī sampad, completing the list of twenty-six across three verses. 'Tejaḥ' — vigour, luminous energy: the inner radiance, authority, and power that arises naturally from wisdom and sustained spiritual practice. This is not physical strength but ātmic force — the capacity to inspire, illumine, and uplift. 'Kṣamā' — forgiveness: the willingness to not retaliate even when one has the power to do so; the capacity to absorb insult, injury, and error without contracting into resentment. Far from weakness, kṣamā is the mark of supreme inner strength — it requires mastery of ego. 'Dhṛtiḥ' — fortitude, firmness: the inner steadiness that maintains one's course of right action without wavering under pressure, difficulty, or discouragement. This is the quality that sustains sādhana through all obstacles. 'Śaucam' — purity: both external (cleanliness of body, food, environment) and internal (purity of intention, thought, and feeling — freedom from envy, greed, and malice in the subtle mind). 'Adrohaḥ' — freedom from malice: the complete absence of hostile intent (droha) toward any being — in thought, speech, or action. One who sees the same Self in all cannot harbour genuine hostility. 'Nātimānitā' — absence of excessive self-importance (na + ati + mānitā): not demanding excessive honour or recognition, not positioning oneself above others. This is the natural humility that flows from genuine Self-knowledge — one who sees Brahman equally in all cannot claim a privileged position. 'Bhavanti sampadaṃ daivīm abhijātasya' — all twenty-six of these constitute the daivī sampad of one born to the divine nature: this complete enumeration is the Gita's definitive charter of the spiritually prepared human being.

16:4.text: O Pārtha, hypocrisy, arrogance, excessive pride, anger, harshness, and ignorance — these are (the marks of one) born to the demoniac endowment.
16:4.transliteration:
dambho darpo 'bhimānaś ca krodhaḥ pāruṣyam eva ca |
ajñānaṃ cābhijātasya pārtha sampadam āsurīm ||4||
16:4.devanagari:
दम्भो दर्पोऽभिमानश्च क्रोधः पारुष्यमेव च ।
अज्ञानं चाभिजातस्य पार्थ सम्पदमासुरीम् ॥ ४ ॥
16:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:4.explanation: In striking contrast to the twenty-six qualities of daivī sampad spread across three verses, āsurī sampad is compressed into six qualities — because these six are the root from which all demoniac behaviour grows. 'Dambhaḥ' — ostentation, hypocrisy: the performance of virtue without its substance, the projection of an image of religiosity or goodness not supported by actual inner quality. This is the direct antithesis of sattva-saṃśuddhi (purity of being). 'Darpaḥ' — arrogance: pride in one's power, beauty, wealth, or ability taken to the point of contempt for others. 'Abhimānaḥ' — excessive ego-identification: the persistent sense of one's own supreme importance and others' inferiority — the antithesis of adroha and nātimānitā. 'Krodhaḥ' — anger: the fire kindled when desires are obstructed or ego is threatened — which destroys discrimination (viveka), relationships, and one's own spiritual progress. 'Pāruṣyam' — harshness: cruelty and roughness in speech and action — the direct antithesis of mārdava (gentleness) and dayā (compassion). 'Ajñānam' — ignorance: this is the deepest and most fundamental of all six, the root from which the others branch: ignorance of the Self, of Brahman, of karma, of dharma. All five preceding qualities are its fruits. The brevity of this list is itself significant: evil is simple in its root (ignorance), while goodness is multifaceted and diverse in its expressions — twenty-six forms of the good, six forms of the demoniac root.

16:5.text: The divine endowment is considered conducive to liberation, the demoniac to bondage. Do not grieve, O Pāṇḍava — you are born to the divine endowment.
16:5.transliteration:
daivī sampad vimokṣāya nibandhāyāsurī matā |
mā śucaḥ sampadaṃ daivīm abhijāto 'si pāṇḍava ||5||
16:5.devanagari:
दैवी सम्पद्विमोक्षाय निबन्धायासुरी मता ।
मा शुचः सम्पदं दैवीमभिजातोऽसि पाण्डव ॥ ५ ॥
16:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:5.explanation: 'Daivī sampad vimokṣāya' — the divine endowment (the twenty-six qualities of verses 1–3) leads to liberation (vimokṣa): these qualities systematically dismantle the structures of ego, attachment, and ignorance that constitute bondage. They thin the walls of the prison of saṃsāra until liberation becomes natural. 'Nibandhāyāsurī matā' — the demoniac endowment is understood to lead to bondage (nibandha): these qualities — hypocrisy, arrogance, anger, ignorance — deepen the ego's investment in the world, multiply karmic entanglements, and pull the jīva further into the cycles of rebirth. 'Mā śucaḥ' — do not grieve: this compassionate interjection is significant. Throughout the Gita, Krishna returns repeatedly to consoling Arjuna's grief — here reassuring him not about external battle-outcomes but about his own inner nature. One suspects Arjuna may be troubled by self-doubt about whether he truly possesses the divine endowment or whether the darkness he senses in himself is āsurī. 'Sampadaṃ daivīm abhijāto 'si pāṇḍava' — O Pāṇḍava, you are born to the divine endowment. This is not flattery — this is the teacher's direct confirmation of the student's essential nature, one of the most powerful pedagogical acts: the guru reflecting back to the disciple their true face. This is the Gita's characteristic method: not merely issuing commands but confirming the learner's divine identity as the basis for right action.

16:6.text: Two kinds of beings are created in this world — the divine and the demoniac. The divine has been described at length. Hear from me about the demoniac, O Pārtha.
16:6.transliteration:
dvau bhūtasargau loke 'smin daiva āsura eva ca |
daivo vistaraśaḥ prokta āsuraṃ pārtha me śṛṇu ||6||
16:6.devanagari:
द्वौ भूतसर्गौ लोकेऽस्मिन्दैव आसुर एव च ।
दैवो विस्तरशः प्रोक्त आसुरं पार्थ मे श्रृणु ॥ ६ ॥
16:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:6.explanation: 'Dvau bhūtasargau loke 'smin' — in this world, two fundamental orders of created beings exist. This binary is not a classification by birth, ethnicity, religion, or social standing — it is a classification of inner orientation, the direction in which consciousness habitually moves. 'Daiva' — those oriented toward knowledge, dharma, truth, and Brahman; whose inner gravity pulls them upward toward liberation. 'Āsura' — those oriented away from the Divine, toward ego, desire, and exclusively materialistic ends; whose inner gravity pulls them downward into ever-greater entanglement. These two archetypes co-exist in all cultures, times, and societies — and significantly, as elements in tension within every individual human being. The choice between daivī and āsurī is made continuously, in every thought, word, and act. 'Daivo vistaraśaḥ proktaḥ' — the divine endowment has been described in full detail (verses 1–3). 'Āsuraṃ pārtha me śṛṇu' — now hear about the demoniac from me. What follows (verses 7–20) is not a supernatural mythology about demons but a precise, forensic psychological portrait of a particular human character-type — one that is, as the tradition insists, entirely possible to inhabit, and entirely possible to leave.

16:7.text: The demoniac do not know what to do (pravṛtti) and what to refrain from (nivṛtti). Neither purity, nor right conduct, nor truth is found in them.
16:7.transliteration:
pravṛttiṃ ca nivṛttiṃ ca janā na vidur āsurāḥ |
na śaucaṃ nāpi cācāro na satyaṃ teṣu vidyate ||7||
16:7.devanagari:
प्रवृत्तिं च निवृत्तिं च जना न विदुरासुराः ।
न शौचं नापि चाचारो न सत्यं तेषु विद्यते ॥ ७ ॥
16:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:7.explanation: 'Pravṛttiṃ ca nivṛttiṃ ca na viduḥ' — this is the most foundational intellectual-moral deficit of the demoniac: they do not know pravṛtti (what ought to be done — the range of actions enjoined by dharma: sacrifice, service, charity, truthfulness) and nivṛtti (what ought not to be done — actions to be abstained from: violence, theft, falsehood, exploitation). This is not merely an absence of religious information; it is the complete collapse of the internal compass that distinguishes dharma from adharma. This compass is what the twenty-six qualities of daivī sampad collectively build — its absence is the fertile ground for all demoniac behaviour. 'Na śaucam' — no purity (śauca): neither external cleanliness nor the internal purity of intention. 'Na ācaraḥ' — no right conduct (ācāra): no adherence to the social, ethical, and religious codes that sustain civilised life and spiritual progress. 'Na satyam' — no truth: neither honesty in dealings nor orientation toward the metaphysical truth of existence. The triple absence — no purity, no right conduct, no truth — flows from the one root absence: ajñāna (ignorance), named in verse 4. This verse diagnoses the problem at three levels simultaneously: practical ethics (pravṛtti-nivṛtti), social conduct (ācāra), and spiritual orientation (satya).

16:8.text: They say: the world has no truth, no foundation, no Lord. It arose from mutual union alone — what else? — with desire as its only cause.
16:8.transliteration:
asatyam apratiṣṭhaṃ te jagad āhur anīśvaram |
aparasparasambhūtaṃ kim anyat kāmahaitukam ||8||
16:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:8.explanation: This verse is the ideological heart of the āsurī orientation — its complete metaphysical worldview expressed in three devastating clauses, which serve as the philosophical root of all the moral pathologies described in what follows. 'Asatyam' — the world is without truth: no moral truth, no spiritual truth, no permanent reality — only shifting, transient matter. Good and evil, sin and merit, dharma and adharma are human constructs with no objective basis. 'Apratiṣṭham' — without foundation: no cosmic order (ṛta), no law of karma, no moral-spiritual structure underlying existence. 'Anīśvaram' — without Lord: no omniscient, just, sovereign Intelligence presiding over creation — the cosmos arose by itself, purposelessly. 'Aparasparasambhūtam' — born from mutual union alone: the universe is the product of matter interacting with matter, without any conscious ground — pure materialist emergentism. 'Kim anyat kāmahaitukam' — what else but desire (kāma) is the cause? The ultimate causal principle is not Brahman, not Consciousness, not dharma — but sheer biological desire, the sexual drive. This is the ancient Indian philosophical school of Cārvāka / Lokāyata materialism articulated with remarkable precision. Its practical implication is lethal: if there is no God, no karma, no moral order — then power alone determines what one may do. The strong may exploit the weak. There is no ultimate accountability. This worldview directly generates the behaviours described in verses 9–20.

16:9.text: Holding fast to this view, these self-ruined ones of stunted intellect arise in this world as enemies of the world, performing terrible deeds for its destruction.
16:9.transliteration:
etāṃ dṛṣṭim avaṣṭabhya naṣṭātmāno 'lpabuddhayaḥ |
prabhavanty ugrakarmāṇaḥ kṣayāya jagato 'hitāḥ ||9||
16:9.devanagari:
एतां दृष्टिमवष्टभ्य नष्टात्मानोऽल्पबुद्धयः ।
प्रभवन्त्युग्रकर्माणः क्षयाय जगतोऽहिताः ॥ ९ ॥
16:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:9.explanation: 'Etāṃ dṛṣṭim avaṣṭabhya' — grasping, clinging to this nihilistic worldview (no truth, no foundation, no Lord, only desire) — not merely holding it as a theoretical position but embodying it, making it the entire frame through which reality is interpreted and action is taken. 'Naṣṭātmānaḥ' — the self-ruined: those whose ātman (inner discriminative wisdom, capacity for Self-knowledge) has been effectively extinguished by the accumulated weight of ego, desire, and wrong metaphysical assumptions. This is the deepest form of self-destruction — not physical but spiritual; the loss of access to one's own deepest nature. 'Alpabuddhayaḥ' — of stunted, narrow intellect: capable of cunning and cleverness but not of wisdom; seeing only the immediate and proximate, blind to the larger reality and long-range consequences of action. 'Ugrakarmāṇaḥ' — performers of terrible deeds: their actions are marked by violence, exploitation, cruelty, and destruction — because without the restraint of belief in moral order or divine accountability, raw self-interest has no limit. 'Kṣayāya jagataḥ' — for the destruction of the world: these individuals, pursuing narrow self-interest, systematically destroy what sustains collective life — nature, community, dharma, truth. 'Ahitāḥ' — enemies of the world. This verse makes an urgent and eternally relevant philosophical point: ideas have consequences. The metaphysics one holds — whether the world has moral order or is fundamentally purposeless — directly determines how one acts in it.

16:10.text: Taking refuge in insatiable desire, full of hypocrisy, pride and arrogance, through delusion grasping at false notions, they move through life with impure resolves.
16:10.transliteration:
kāmam āśritya duṣpūraṃ dambhamānamadānvitāḥ |
mohād gṛhītvāsadgrāhān pravartante 'śucivrataḥ ||10||
16:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:10.explanation: 'Kāmam āśritya duṣpūram' — leaning upon insatiable (duṣpūra: impossible to fill, to sate) desire as their fundamental orientation. This is the defining characteristic of desire: it is structurally incapable of satisfaction — like fire fed with ghee, it blazes higher with each fulfilment. These individuals have made this insatiable hunger the very axis of their lives. 'Dambhamānamadānvitāḥ' — filled with the triple poison of dambha (hypocrisy: projecting virtue without possessing it), māna (excessive self-regard, placing oneself above others), and mada (the intoxication of ego — when pride reaches the point of a kind of madness that blinds one to reality). All three are fuelled by ego, not by genuine achievement or wisdom. 'Mohād gṛhītvā asadgrāhān' — through moha (delusion, the fundamental misperception that mistakes the false for the true) grasping asad grāhāḥ — false concepts, erroneous worldviews: 'I am this body', 'sense-pleasure is the supreme good', 'the powerful are above accountability', 'the universe is without moral order'. These false convictions (asad = not-sat, not-true) structure their entire interpretation of experience. 'Aśucivrataḥ' — those whose resolves and intentions (vrata) are themselves impure (aśuci): the contamination is at the level of fundamental purpose, the direction in which one's entire life-energy moves. The verb 'pravartante' — they proceed, they are set in motion: this is not occasional lapse but the ongoing, self-reinforcing cycle of demoniac existence.

16:11.text: Beset by immeasurable anxieties lasting until death, holding the gratification of desire as the highest aim, convinced that this alone is all there is —
16:11.transliteration:
cintām aparimeyāṃ ca pralayāntām upāśritāḥ |
kāmopabhogaparamā etāvad iti niścitāḥ ||11||
16:11.devanagari:
चिन्तामपरिमेयां च प्रलयान्तामुपाश्रिताः ।
कामोपभोगपरमा एतावदिति निश्चिताः ॥ ११ ॥
16:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:11.explanation: 'Cintām aparimeyāṃ pralayāntām upāśritāḥ' — burdened by innumerable, immeasurable anxieties that last until the moment of death (pralaya here meaning the body's dissolution at death). This is psychologically acute: those who place sense-pleasure at the centre of life do not thereby find peace — they multiply their anxieties exponentially. The anxiety of acquiring pleasure, of holding onto it, of its inevitable loss, of acquiring more, of competitors, of aging and death that will take everything — these proliferate without end. The pursuit of pleasure is itself the engine of perpetual anxiety. 'Kāmopabhogaparamāḥ' — those for whom the enjoyment of desire (kāma-upabhoga: sensory and material gratification) is the supreme (parama) goal — who have no knowledge of or interest in mokṣa, jñāna, dharma, or Brahman. Their horizon of meaning extends no further than the body and its pleasures. 'Etāvad iti niścitāḥ' — fully convinced (niścita: with firm, unshakeable certainty) that this alone is all there is — that the universe of sensory experience is the totality of reality and that nothing lies beyond it. This certainty is their most profound tragedy: they do not know what they do not know. Certainty within ignorance is the deepest and most self-sealing form of bondage.

16:12.text: Bound by hundreds of snares of hope, given over to desire and anger, they seek to amass wealth by unjust means for the gratification of their desires.
16:12.transliteration:
āśāpāśaśatair baddhāḥ kāmakrodhaparāyaṇāḥ |
īhante kāmabhogārtham anyāyenārthasañcayān ||12||
16:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:12.explanation: 'Āśāpāśaśatair baddhāḥ' — bound by hundreds of snares (pāśa: ropes, nooses) of hope (āśā: desire, expectation, wishful anticipation): each unfulfilled desire creates a noose, and these multiply exponentially — hundreds of entangling threads of 'if only this would happen, if only that were mine, if only this person would do that'. This is a devastating image: hope, normally considered positive, is here shown as itself becoming bondage when rooted in ego-desire rather than in equanimity. 'Kāmakrodhaparāyaṇāḥ' — wholly devoted to (parāyaṇa: with one's entire being oriented toward) desire and anger: the two are inseparably linked. Desire (kāma) for objects and outcomes; anger (krodha) when those desires are thwarted or threatened. They are the twin engines of demoniac psychology — described as early as Chapter 3, verse 37, as the supreme enemies of the wise. 'Īhante kāmabhogārtham anyāyenārthasañcayān' — they strive (īhante) to accumulate wealth (artha-sañcayān) by unjust means (anyāyena: by fraud, exploitation, theft, violence, deceit) for the purpose of satisfying their desires (kāmabhogārtham). The word 'anyāyena' (unjustly) is the moral hinge: these are not merely people who seek wealth, but those who have abandoned the constraint of dharma in doing so, because their nihilistic worldview (verse 8) has removed all moral accountability.

16:13.text: 'This has today been gained by me; this desire I shall also attain. This wealth is mine, and this too shall be mine again in the future.'
16:13.transliteration:
idam adya mayā labdham imaṃ prāpsye manoratham |
idam astīdam api me bhaviṣyati punar dhanam ||13||
16:13.devanagari:
इदमद्य मया लब्धमिमं प्राप्स्ये मनोरथम् ।
इदमस्तीदमपि मे भविष्यति पुनर्धनम् ॥ १३ ॥
16:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:13.explanation: Verses 13–15 constitute the demoniac's interior monologue — Krishna, in a remarkable dramatic turn, gives direct voice to the inner speech of the āsurī character, granting the reader direct access to the cognitive structure of this psychology. This is one of the Gita's most artistically vivid passages. 'Idam adya mayā labdham' — 'This has been obtained by me today.' Not gratitude for a gift received, not wonder at the abundance of existence, but the ego's proprietary accounting of its acquisitions. 'Imaṃ prāpsye manoratham' — 'And this desire (manoratham: what the mind runs toward) I shall also attain.' The next item on the list already being targeted. 'Idam asti' — 'This is mine.' The assertion of possession, the drawing of the boundary of 'mine'. 'Idam api me bhaviṣyati punar dhanam' — 'And this further wealth too shall be mine again in the future.' The restless mental reaching forward into ever-more acquisition. This is the psychology of the perpetually dissatisfied ego — never present, never grateful, always reaching. The mind that operates this way can never find rest, because it is structurally constituted to always want what it does not yet have. The present moment — where Brahman actually dwells — is precisely where this consciousness is never available to be.

16:14.text: 'That enemy has been slain by me, and I shall slay others too. I am the lord, I am the enjoyer, I am perfect, powerful and happy.'
16:14.transliteration:
asau mayā hataḥ śatrur haniṣye cāparān api |
īśvaro 'ham ahaṃ bhogī siddho 'haṃ balavān sukhī ||14||
16:14.devanagari:
असौ मया हतः शत्रुर्हनिष्ये चापरानपि ।
ईश्वरोऽहमहं भोगी सिद्धोऽहं बलवान्सुखी ॥ १४ ॥
16:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:14.explanation: The interior monologue continues — now entering the domains of violence, dominion, and grandiose self-proclamation. 'Asau mayā hataḥ śatruḥ' — 'That enemy has been destroyed by me.' The ego's pride in violence and domination, the savouring of enemies vanquished. 'Haniṣye cāparān api' — 'And I shall slay the others too.' — the appetite for violence extending forward into the future, the enemy-list continuously expanding. Then five ego-proclamations in rapid succession: 'Īśvaro 'ham' — 'I am the lord (Īśvara).' The most audacious claim: appropriating the title that belongs to the Supreme Sovereign of the cosmos, making oneself the measure and master of all. 'Ahaṃ bhogī' — 'I am the enjoyer.' — defining oneself entirely through one's capacity to consume experience and pleasure. 'Siddho 'ham' — 'I am perfect, accomplished.' — here 'siddha' means worldly success and completion, not spiritual realisation; the ego claiming totality where only partial acquisition exists. 'Balavān' — 'I am powerful.' 'Sukhī' — 'I am happy.' — a claim made even while, as verse 11 describes, this same person is beset by immeasurable anxieties unto death. The five 'aham' declarations represent ego's supremest inflation — the ahaṃkāra (I-making faculty) has colonised the entire space of consciousness, crowding out the actual 'aham' — the pure 'I am' of Brahman — with its own deluded projection.

16:15.text: 'I am wealthy, of noble birth. Who else is equal to me? I shall sacrifice, I shall give, I shall rejoice.' — thus, deluded by ignorance.
16:15.transliteration:
āḍhyo 'bhijanavān asmi ko 'nyo 'sti sadṛśo mayā |
yakṣye dāsyāmi modiṣya ity ajñānavimohitāḥ ||15||
16:15.devanagari:
आढ्योऽभिजनवानस्मि कोऽन्योऽस्ति सदृशो मया ।
यक्ष्ये दास्यामि मोदिष्य इत्यज्ञानविमोहिताः ॥ १५ ॥
16:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:15.explanation: The interior monologue reaches its climax and conclusion. 'Āḍhyo 'bhijanavān asmi' — 'I am wealthy and of noble birth' — both forms of worldly distinction asserted simultaneously: artha (wealth) and kula (lineage). The ego's two greatest props: what I have and where I came from. 'Ko 'nyo 'sti sadṛśo mayā' — 'Who else is equal to me?' — the pinnacle of nātimānitā's inverse: not freedom from excessive pride but its absolute extreme, the proclamation of one's own incomparable superiority. 'Yakṣye' — 'I shall perform sacrifice.' 'Dāsyāmi' — 'I shall give (charity).' 'Modiṣye' — 'I shall rejoice.' These three statements are a perfect irony: they echo the vocabulary of the divine endowment (yajña, dāna), yet they are spoken entirely from the platform of egotism — as public displays of power and status, not as acts of devotion, surrender, or genuine generosity. This is dambha (hypocrisy and ostentation) in its clearest form: using the forms of dharma to serve the purpose of ahaṃkāra. 'Ity ajñānavimohitāḥ' — thus deluded by ignorance. The quotation closes. Krishna steps back from within the demoniac consciousness and diagnoses its entire structure: every grandiose claim, every anxiety, every acquisition drive, every self-proclamation — all of it is ajñāna-vimohita: produced by and pervaded by the delusion of ignorance.

16:16.text: Bewildered by many thoughts, enveloped in the net of delusion, deeply addicted to sensual pleasures — they fall into a foul hell.
16:16.transliteration:
anekacittavibhrāntā mohajālasamāvṛtāḥ |
prasaktāḥ kāmabhogeṣu patanti narake 'śucau ||16||
16:16.devanagari:
अनेकचित्तविभ्रान्ता मोहजालसमावृताः ।
प्रसक्ताः कामभोगेषु पतन्ति नरकेऽशुचौ ॥ १६ ॥
16:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:16.explanation: 'Anekacittavibhrāntāḥ' — 'aneka-citta': of many (scattered) minds, bewildered by multitude. The demoniac consciousness is characterised by radical centrifugal diffusion — the mind flying in countless directions simultaneously, unable to settle, unable to focus, because it is pulled by hundreds of desires each demanding attention. This is the cognitive expression of the āsurī predicament: the utter absence of the 'one-pointedness' (ekāgratā) that is the hallmark of daivī consciousness. 'Mohajālasamāvṛtāḥ' — enveloped in the net (jāla) of delusion (moha): the net is a perfect metaphor — fish do not see the net as their prison; they experience it simply as the water they swim in. The demoniac person does not experience their moha as delusion; it is simply the reality they inhabit. They cannot see their own blindness. 'Prasaktāḥ kāmabhogeṣu' — deeply attached (prasaktā: stuck, unable to leave) to the pleasures of desire: an attachment that has become addictive in its structure — compulsive, self-reinforcing, impossible to release through mere willpower. 'Patanti narake 'śucau' — they fall into a foul (aśuci: impure) hell. 'Naraka' here operates at two levels: the inner hell of a life dominated by anxiety, dissatisfaction, moral corruption, and the absence of peace — which is itself a form of hell experienced within embodied life; and the post-mortem consequence of deeply āsurī karma: birth in lower states of existence.

16:17.text: Self-conceited, stubborn, filled with the pride of wealth and honour, they perform sacrifice in name only, with ostentation, disregarding scriptural injunctions.
16:17.transliteration:
ātmasambhāvitāḥ stabdhā dhanamānamadānvitāḥ |
yajante nāmayajñais te dambhenāvidhipūrvakam ||17||
16:17.devanagari:
आत्मसम्भाविताः स्तब्धा धनमानमदान्विताः ।
यजन्ते नामयज्ञैस्ते दम्भेनाविधिपूर्वकम् ॥ १७ ॥
16:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:17.explanation: 'Ātmasambhāvitāḥ' — self-conceited: those who regard themselves as the supreme, the great, the worthy of admiration — their entire value-system is self-referential, self-generated, self-validating. They do not look upward toward the Divine or outward toward truth; they look inward and see only their own reflection, which they have decided is magnificent. 'Stabdhāḥ' — stubborn, rigid, inflexible: they cannot be taught because they believe they have nothing to learn. The guru-śiṣya relationship, essential for Vedāntic growth, is impossible with one who considers himself already arrived. 'Dhanamānamadānvitāḥ' — intoxicated by the pride (mada: the particular madness of intoxication) of wealth (dhana) and honour (māna). 'Yajante nāmayajñaiḥ' — they perform 'nāma-yajña' (sacrifice in name only): the external form of religious observance — the visible ceremony, the public ritual — without its inner substance of śraddhā (faith), proper intent, actual surrender to the Divine, or adherence to scriptural method. 'Dambhena' — with dambha (ostentation and hypocrisy): performed entirely for public impression and social prestige, not for the Divine. 'Avidhipūrvakam' — without following the proper scriptural procedure. This verse reveals a particularly insidious form of the demoniac character: one who uses the forms of dharma (ritual, sacrifice) in the service of ego. Dambha-dharma — hypocritical religiosity — is perhaps the most dangerous form, precisely because it mimics genuine virtue.

16:18.text: Taking refuge in egoism, power, arrogance, desire and anger, these malicious persons hate me who dwells in their own and in others' bodies.
16:18.transliteration:
ahaṃkāraṃ balaṃ darpaṃ kāmaṃ krodhaṃ ca saṃśritāḥ |
mām ātmaparadeheṣu pradviṣanto 'bhyasūyakāḥ ||18||
16:18.devanagari:
अहङ्कारं बलं दर्पं कामं क्रोधं च संश्रिताः ।
मामात्मपरदेहेषु प्रद्विषन्तोऽभ्यसूयकाः ॥ १८ ॥
16:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:18.explanation: 'Ahaṃkāraṃ balaṃ darpaṃ kāmaṃ krodhaṃ ca saṃśritāḥ' — taking shelter in five interlocking forces: ahaṃkāra (the ego-making faculty; the fundamental misidentification of the Self with the personal self), balam (raw power — physical, political, financial), darpa (the arrogance that power generates), kāma (desire as the engine of all striving), and krodha (anger as the weapon of all frustration). Together these five constitute the complete armature of the āsurī psychology. 'Mām ātmaparadeheṣu pradviṣantaḥ' — they hate me (Krishna, the Supreme) who dwells in both their own bodies (ātmadeha) and the bodies of others (paradeha). This is the chapter's most theologically profound declaration: the Divine indwells every being — the antaryāmin doctrine of verse 15.15 restated here in the context of hatred. Every act of violence, every hatred directed at another being, every exploitation of another — is directed at the Divine who indwells that very being. 'Abhyasūyakāḥ' — those filled with abhyasūyā (malicious fault-finding, envious censure): those who cannot see others without searching for their deficiencies — the inverse of the daivī quality of dayā (compassion). This is the supreme spiritual tragedy: the one who hates is not merely hurting the one hated but is, in the deepest metaphysical sense, directing that hatred at God — who is, according to verse 15.15, seated in the very heart they have chosen to wound.

16:19.text: These hateful, cruel, vilest among men, these wicked ones — I continuously hurl them into demoniac wombs in the ocean of saṃsāra.
16:19.transliteration:
tān ahaṃ dviṣataḥ krūrān saṃsāreṣu narādhamān |
kṣipāmy ajasram aśubhān āsurīṣv eva yoniṣu ||19||
16:19.devanagari:
तानहं द्विषतः क्रूरान्संसारेषु नराधमान् ।
क्षिपाम्यजस्रमशुभानासुरीष्वेव योनिषु ॥ १९ ॥
16:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:19.explanation: 'Tān dviṣataḥ krūrān' — those who hate (the Divine dwelling in all) and those who are cruel (performing violent, destructive acts without conscience). 'Saṃsāreṣu narādhamān' — the vilest of humans (narādhama: lowest among men): this is a powerful characterisation — not a condemnation based on birth or social position but based on moral conduct. One who possesses the human birth — which the tradition calls the supreme opportunity for liberation, the vehicle by which mokṣa is possible — and who uses it entirely for destructive, self-aggrandising, divine-denying ends, has wasted what is considered the most precious of all possible births. 'Aśubhān' — those of inauspicious nature: whose every impulse, thought, and action generates suffering. 'Kṣipāmy ajasram āsurīṣv eva yoniṣu' — I continuously (ajasram: without interruption) cast (kṣipāmi: hurl, throw) them into āsurī wombs (demoniac births). The mechanism here is not arbitrary divine punishment but the precise operation of karma: the consciousness one has cultivated determines the nature of one's next embodiment. An āsurī consciousness gravitates naturally toward an āsurī birth — a realm of existence that deepens its fundamental ignorance and distances it further from liberation. The Lord's 'hurling' is the cosmic law of karma, expressed in personal language.

16:20.text: Fallen into demoniac wombs, birth after birth, these deluded ones, O Kaunteya, not attaining Me, sink to the lowest condition from there.
16:20.transliteration:
āsurīṃ yonim āpannā mūḍhā janmani janmani |
mām aprāpyaiva kaunteya tato yānty adhamāṃ gatim ||20||
16:20.devanagari:
आसुरीं योनिमापन्ना मूढा जन्मनि जन्मनि ।
मामप्राप्यैव कौन्तेय ततो यान्त्यधमां गतिम् ॥ २० ॥
16:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:20.explanation: 'Āsurīṃ yonim āpannāḥ' — they fall into demoniac wombs: and crucially, 'janmani janmani' — not once, but birth after birth. This describes the self-reinforcing cycle of āsurī existence: each demoniac birth deepens the conditioning that necessitates another. The consciousness one has cultivated becomes the seed of one's next embodiment, and āsurī consciousness, left unchecked, perpetuates and intensifies itself across lifetimes. 'Mūḍhāḥ' — deluded, spiritually blind: not a harsh condemnation but an accurate diagnosis. Those who have lost sight of the Ātman, who operate entirely from the ego-driven, matter-identified standpoint, are in a state of fundamental moha (delusion) — they do not see reality as it is. 'Mām aprāpyaiva' — without ever attaining Me: this is the poignant core of the verse. The human birth was the supreme opportunity — the only vehicle by which mokṣa (liberation) is attainable. To have possessed that opportunity and to have squandered it through āsurī living is described here as the highest spiritual tragedy. 'Tato yānty adhamāṃ gatim' — from there (even from the demoniac birth) they descend further to a still lower condition. This describes a downward spiral: each āsurī birth entrenches the tendencies that produce an even more degraded next birth. The Lord's tone here is not vengeful but gravely compassionate — warning Arjuna, and through him all practitioners, of what awaits those who choose the path of egoism, violence, and divine-denial. This verse completes the description of the fate of the āsurī type begun in verse 19.

16:21.text: Threefold is the gate of this hell, destructive of the self — lust, anger, and greed. Therefore one should abandon these three.
16:21.transliteration:
tri-vidhaṃ narakasyedaṃ dvāraṃ nāśanam ātmanaḥ |
kāmaḥ krodhas tathā lobhas tasmād etat trayaṃ tyajet ||21||
16:21.devanagari:
त्रिविधं नरकस्येदं द्वारं नाशनमात्मनः ।
कामः क्रोधस्तथा लोभस्तस्मादेतत्त्रयं त्यजेत् ॥ २१ ॥
16:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:21.explanation: 'Tri-vidhaṃ narakasya dvāram' — the threefold gate of hell: the word 'naraka' here carries both its literal meaning (the hellish states of future births described in the preceding verses) and its immediate, psychological meaning — the inner hell created in this very lifetime by these three forces. Lust, anger, and greed generate their own torment: the restless craving of kāma, the consuming fire of krodha, the perpetual dissatisfaction of lobha — these are internal hell-states, not only posthumous ones. 'Nāśanam ātmanaḥ' — destructive of the self (ātman): this is the most precise and devastating characterisation. These three do not merely damage worldly life — they destroy the soul's very capacity for liberation. Kāma obscures viveka (discrimination); krodha destroys samyak-jñāna (right understanding), as stated in 2.63 ('krodhād bhavati sammohaḥ...'); lobha corrupts dharmic action. Together they block the gate to mokṣa. 'Kāmaḥ' — lust, desire in its compulsive, unregulated form: not desire per se (which can be sublimated) but kāma as uncontrolled craving, which intensifies upon satisfaction and converts to krodha upon frustration. 'Krodhaḥ' — anger: the explosive reaction of thwarted kāma; also the generalised tendency to react with hostility whenever the ego feels threatened. Krodha blinds the intellect and bypasses all reasoning capacity. 'Lobhaḥ' — greed: the chronic inability to feel satisfied with what one has, the compulsive grasping for more — which generates deception, exploitation, and adharma as its instruments. 'Tasmād etat trayaṃ tyajet' — therefore, one should abandon this triad: the remedy is clear and non-negotiable. Tyāga here does not mean suppression (which is futile and counter-productive) but the discrimination-based dismantling of these tendencies at their root — through viveka, vairāgya, and consistent sādhanā.

16:22.text: Released from these three gates of darkness, O Kaunteya, a person practises what is good for the self — and thence attains the supreme goal.
16:22.transliteration:
etair vimuktaḥ kaunteya tamo-dvārais tribhir naraḥ |
ācaraty ātmanaḥ śreyas tato yāti parāṃ gatim ||22||
16:22.devanagari:
एतैर्विमुक्तः कौन्तेय तमोद्वारैस्त्रिभिर्नरः ।
आचरत्यात्मनः श्रेयस्ततो याति परां गतिम् ॥ २२ ॥
16:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:22.explanation: 'Etair vimuktaḥ' — released from these three: the word 'vimuktaḥ' (thoroughly freed) implies not mere behavioural restraint but inner liberation from the grip of these tendencies. When kāma, krodha, and lobha no longer dominate the inner landscape, the entire quality of one's consciousness changes. 'Tamo-dvārais tribhiḥ' — from the three gates of tamas: an illuminating designation. The Lord calls these three not merely 'sinful' or 'harmful' but specifically 'tamo-dvāras' — gateways of tamas, the guna of inertia, darkness, and delusion. This reveals the psychological mechanism: these three forces are not just ethical violations — they actively suppress the inner light of sattva and deepen the dominance of tamas, which in turn perpetuates spiritual blindness. 'Naraḥ' — a person (lit. 'man'): the human birth is specifically named because it alone possesses the reflective, discriminating capacity needed to accomplish this liberation. 'Ācaraty ātmanaḥ śreyaḥ' — acts for the genuine welfare of the self: 'śreyaḥ' is a technical Vedāntic term contrasted with 'preyaḥ' (the pleasant). Preyaḥ = immediate gratification; śreyaḥ = the ultimately beneficial, the genuinely good for the Ātman's growth and liberation. When the three obscurations are removed, the natural intelligence of the ātman asserts itself and one spontaneously gravitates toward what is genuinely good. 'Tato yāti parāṃ gatim' — thence attains the supreme goal: 'parā gatiḥ' = the highest destination — liberation, Self-realisation, mokṣa. This verse is the luminous counterpart to verse 21: where that verse prescribed the abandonment of the three gates, this verse reveals the magnificent result of that abandonment. The Gītā's essential optimism is expressed here — the path to the supreme is open to every human being who is willing to discipline these three fundamental inner forces.

16:23.text: He who, casting aside the injunctions of scripture, acts driven by the impulse of desire — he attains neither perfection, nor happiness, nor the supreme goal.
16:23.transliteration:
yaḥ śāstra-vidhim utsṛjya vartate kāma-kārataḥ |
na sa siddhim avāpnoti na sukhaṃ na parāṃ gatim ||23||
16:23.devanagari:
यः शास्त्रविधिमुत्सृज्य वर्तते कामकारतः ।
न स सिद्धिमवाप्नोति न सुखं न परां गतिम् ॥ २३ ॥
16:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:23.explanation: 'Yaḥ śāstra-vidhim utsṛjya' — he who, having discarded the ordinances of scripture: 'śāstra-vidhi' = the injunctions and principles of the śāstras — the accumulated wisdom of millennia of spiritual inquiry, practice, and realisation, codified to guide human beings toward their highest potential. To 'utsṛjya' (literally, 'throw away, abandon') these guidelines is to allow the ego's momentary preferences to override the collective intelligence of the tradition. 'Kāma-kārataḥ' — driven by the impulse of desire: 'kāma-kāra' is a compound indicating compulsive action driven purely by personal desire, with no reference to dharma, śāstra, or the welfare of others. It is a state of radical subjectivism — 'I will do what I want, when I want' — which, the Gītā observes, invariably produces not freedom but deeper bondage. 'Na sa siddhim avāpnoti' — he attains no perfection: 'siddhi' refers here to both worldly fulfilment and spiritual accomplishment. Without the discipline of śāstra — without the structured, tested path — desires multiply and diversify without producing the deep satisfaction that comes from their transcendence. The person chases desire after desire and finds each inadequate. 'Na sukham' — nor happiness: this is the profound irony the Gītā highlights. The person who abandons restraint in the pursuit of pleasure achieves neither liberation nor even sustained happiness. The very instrument he uses to chase sukha — unrestrained kāma — is constitutionally incapable of producing lasting sukha, because its nature is to intensify craving rather than satisfy it. 'Na parāṃ gatim' — nor the supreme goal: liberation, mokṣa, the final purpose of human existence — this remains utterly inaccessible to one who has made personal desire, rather than cosmic dharma and the wisdom of the śāstra, the compass of his life. The verse makes a three-tiered statement: worldly perfection is lost, ordinary happiness is forfeit, and the supreme goal is foreclosed — all three consequences of the one error of abandoning śāstra in favour of kāma.

16:24.text: Therefore, let scripture be your authority in determining what ought to be done and what ought not to be done. Knowing what is enjoined by scriptural ordinance, you should perform action here in this world.
16:24.transliteration:
tasmāc chāstraṃ pramāṇaṃ te kāryākārya-vyavasthitau |
jñātvā śāstra-vidhānoktaṃ karma kartum ihārhasi ||24||
16:24.devanagari:
तस्माच्छास्त्रं प्रमाणं ते कार्याकार्यव्यवस्थितौ ।
ज्ञात्वा शास्त्रविधानोक्तं कर्म कर्तुमिहार्हसि ॥ २४ ॥
16:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
16:24.explanation: 'Tasmāc chāstraṃ pramāṇaṃ te' — therefore, let scripture be your pramāṇa (authoritative means of knowledge): this is the verse's centrepiece. 'Pramāṇa' is a technical term in Indian epistemology — it denotes the valid instrument by which genuine knowledge is established. Pramāṇas traditionally include pratyakṣa (direct perception), anumāna (inference), and śabda (verbal testimony, i.e., scripture). Here the Lord designates śāstra as the supreme pramāṇa for ethical and spiritual decision-making. 'Kāryākārya-vyavasthitau' — in the determination of what is to be done and what is not to be done: every human being faces this question constantly. The criteria by which one decides cannot be mere personal desire (kāma) — the previous verse showed where that leads. The śāstra, as the distilled wisdom of realised sages across millennia, provides a tested, systematic, and wisdom-based framework for these determinations. 'Jñātvā śāstra-vidhānoktam' — having known (understood) what is enjoined by scriptural ordinance: the critical word is 'jñātvā' — having understood, not merely having read or obeyed. The Gītā never advocates blind, mechanical obedience to scripture. The instruction is to understand the principles behind the śāstra's injunctions — to know the why, not merely the what — and then to act from that informed understanding. 'Karma kartum ihārhasi' — you should perform action here (in this world): 'iha' (here) grounds the teaching in practical, present-moment applicability. This is the concluding verse of Chapter 16, and it functions as the magnificent capstone of the chapter's entire argument. After the detailed analysis of daivī and āsurī natures (verses 1–18), the description of the āsurī fate (19–20), the identification of the three gates of hell (21–22), and the consequences of desire-driven scriptural rejection (23) — the chapter's final word is constructive and empowering: the śāstra is your guide; understand it deeply; act accordingly. This verse also serves as the perfect bridge to Chapter 17, where Arjuna will immediately ask the pivotal question: 'What of those who act with śraddhā (faith) but without following the śāstra — what is their condition?'

17:1.text: Arjuna said: O Kṛṣṇa, what is the condition of those who, setting aside the injunctions of scripture, perform worship with faith — is it sattva, rajas, or tamas?
17:1.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
ye śāstra-vidhim utsṛjya yajante śraddhayānvitāḥ |
teṣāṃ niṣṭhā tu kā kṛṣṇa sattvam āho rajas tamaḥ ||1||
17:1.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
ये शास्त्रविधिमुत्सृज्य यजन्ते श्रद्धयान्विताः ।
तेषां निष्ठा तु का कृष्ण सत्त्वमाहो रजस्तमः ॥ १ ॥
17:1.speaker: Arjuna
17:1.explanation: This question arises directly from the conclusion of Chapter 16 (16.23–24), which declared that one who abandons śāstra and acts from personal desire achieves nothing — no perfection, no happiness, no liberation. Arjuna now raises a nuanced counter-case: what about those who lack scriptural knowledge but possess śraddhā (genuine faith)? The question is both intellectually precise and spiritually generous. 'Śraddhayānvitāḥ' — endowed with faith: these people are not atheists, not malicious — they simply act from their inner conviction rather than from prescribed procedure. Arjuna's underlying question is whether inner quality (faith, sincerity) can compensate for external deficiency (scriptural ignorance). By offering all three guṇas as possible answers, Arjuna leaves the question genuinely open, showing the maturity of a real seeker who does not pre-load the answer. 'Niṣṭhā tu kā' — what exactly is their condition: 'niṣṭhā' denotes one's abiding state or orientation — the guṇa that fundamentally characterises their inner life and worship. This question unlocks one of the Gītā's most comprehensive psychological taxonomies: the remainder of Chapter 17 will map the three guṇas systematically across faith, food, sacrifice, austerity, and charity — demonstrating that every form of human behaviour is coloured by one's guṇa-constitution.

17:2.text: Śrī Bhagavān said: The faith of the embodied beings, born of their inherent nature, is threefold — sāttvika, rājasa, and tāmasa. Hear now of these.
17:2.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
tri-vidhā bhavati śraddhā dehināṃ sā svabhāva-jā |
sāttvikī rājasī caiva tāmasī ceti tāṃ śṛṇu ||2||
17:2.devanagari:
श्री भगवानुवाच ।
त्रिविधा भवति श्रद्धा देहिनां सा स्वभावजा ।
सात्त्विकी राजसी चैव तामसी चेति तां शृणु ॥ २ ॥
17:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:2.explanation: 'Tri-vidhā bhavati śraddhā' — faith is of three kinds: the Gītā's deployment of the guṇa-framework to categorise śraddhā itself is one of its most original psychological contributions. Śraddhā is not merely religious belief in the narrow sense — it is the fundamental orientation of the entire being: what one trusts, what one values, what one is willing to commit oneself to. In this deep sense, it is the most basic determinant of character. 'Svabhāva-jā' — born of one's own nature (svabhāva): this is the crucial qualifier. The Gītā is not declaring that one type of śraddhā is correct and the others are failures. Rather, it observes that the nature of one's faith is determined by one's guṇa-constitution — which is itself the accumulated result of past saṃskāras and karma. Faith is not a wilful choice made in a vacuum; it grows organically from the soil of one's inner nature. 'Dehināṃ' — of the embodied: all beings with bodies. This is a universal taxonomy that applies to every human being without exception. 'Sāttvikī rājasī caiva tāmasī ca' — the three types: each will be elaborated through the chapter. The principle established here — that one's guṇa-constitution determines one's śraddhā, which in turn determines all one's actions and worship — is the conceptual key to the entire chapter.

17:3.text: O Bhārata, the faith of every person is in accordance with their inner nature (sattva). This person is made of faith — whatever one's faith is, that indeed one is.
17:3.transliteration:
sattv'ānurūpā sarvasya śraddhā bhavati bhārata |
śraddhāmayo 'yaṃ puruṣo yo yac-chraddhāḥ sa eva saḥ ||3||
17:3.devanagari:
सत्त्वानुरूपा सर्वस्य श्रद्धा भवति भारत ।
श्रद्धामयोऽयं पुरुषो यो यच्छ्रद्धः स एव सः ॥ ३ ॥
17:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:3.explanation: 'Sattvānurūpā sarvasya śraddhā' — the faith of every person corresponds to their inner nature: here 'sattva' means not just the sattva-guṇa but the total inner constitution — the accumulated impress of all past saṃskāras expressed as one's present character. Faith is not an arbitrary mental act; it is the natural expression of who one fundamentally is at the deepest level. 'Śraddhāmayo 'yaṃ puruṣaḥ' — this person is constituted of faith: this is one of the Gītā's most celebrated and penetrating psychological insights. A human being, in their deepest essence, is not defined by their body, social role, profession, or even their stated beliefs — but by their śraddhā, their fundamental trust and conviction, the thing they are willing to stake their life on. This is a definition of the person at the most essential level. 'Yo yac-chraddhāḥ sa eva saḥ' — as one's faith is, so one is: the identity statement. If your faith is in sensory pleasure, you are a hedonist at the deepest level, regardless of what you profess. If your faith is in power, in wealth, in the opinion of others — these things constitute your actual self. If your faith is in the Self (Ātman), in liberation, in the divine — your entire being is oriented toward that. This verse is simultaneously a psychological description and a spiritual invitation: it invites the sincere practitioner to examine 'What is my śraddhā? What do I truly trust? Where is my deepest conviction placed?' — because the answer to that question is, quite literally, what one is.

17:4.text: The sāttvika worship the gods; the rājasa worship the yakṣas and rākṣasas; and the others — the tāmasa — worship departed spirits and hosts of lower beings.
17:4.transliteration:
yajante sāttvikā devān yakṣa-rakṣāṃsi rājasāḥ |
pretān bhūta-gaṇāṃś cānye yajante tāmasā janāḥ ||4||
17:4.devanagari:
यजन्ते सात्त्विका देवान्यक्षरक्षांसि राजसाः ।
प्रेतान्भूतगणांश्चान्ये यजन्ते तामसा जनाः ॥ ४ ॥
17:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:4.explanation: 'Yajante sāttvikā devān' — the sāttvika worship the gods (devas): the devas represent light, purity, order, and transcendent virtue. Those with sāttvika śraddhā are naturally drawn to these qualities — their worship orients toward what is luminous, life-affirming, and conducive to liberation. 'Yakṣa-rakṣāṃsi rājasāḥ' — the rājasa worship yakṣas and rākṣasas: yakṣas are the demigods associated with wealth and power; rākṣasas with destruction and unbridled enjoyment. Those with rājasa śraddhā, driven by ambition and the desire for worldly dominance, seek the patronage of these forces — their worship is transactional, aimed at acquiring power, wealth, and supremacy. 'Pretān bhūta-gaṇāṃś ca tāmasā janāḥ' — the tāmasa worship the deceased and lower spirits: this is the expression of tāmasic śraddhā — an orientation toward dark, obscuring, and subterranean forces. The principle illustrated across these three verses (2–4) is that the object of one's worship reveals the nature of one's inner faith. The Gītā is not condemning lower forms of worship with hostility but mapping them within the objective guṇa-framework. The implicit invitation is toward the sāttvika — and ultimately beyond even the sāttvika toward the nirguṇa (beyond-guṇa) worship of the formless Brahman, which transcends all three categories.

17:5.text: Those persons who perform severe austerities not enjoined by scripture — filled with hypocrisy and egoism, impelled by the force of desire and attachment —
17:5.transliteration:
aśāstra-vihitaṃ ghoraṃ tapyante ye tapo janāḥ |
dambhāhaṃkāra-saṃyuktāḥ kāma-rāga-balānvitāḥ ||5||
17:5.devanagari:
अशास्त्रविहितं घोरं तप्यन्ते ये तपो जनाः ।
दम्भाहंकारसंयुक्ताः कामरागबलान्विताः ॥ ५ ॥
17:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:5.explanation: This verse (continued into verse 6) identifies a specific and spiritually dangerous category: those who engage in extreme, self-designed austerities driven not by genuine spiritual aspiration but by pride, desire, and the need to display spiritual prowess. 'Aśāstra-vihitam ghoram' — severe austerity not prescribed by scripture: these are self-invented, tradition-less practices that violate the guidelines of the śāstra. They appear impressive in their externality but lack the wisdom-foundation that makes tapas genuinely transformative. 'Ghoram' (terrible, fierce) suggests intensity of external appearance without corresponding inner purity. 'Dambhāhaṃkāra-saṃyuktāḥ' — joined with hypocrisy (dambha) and egoism (ahaṃkāra): dambha is the performance of religious virtue for public recognition — the external show of piety with a self-aggrandising internal motive. Ahaṃkāra here is the spiritual ego: 'I am a great ascetic, I am superior, I have powers others lack.' 'Kāma-rāga-balānvitāḥ' — impelled by the force of desire and attachment: the tapas is not motivated by genuine mumukṣutā (desire for liberation) but by specific worldly desires — the acquisition of occult powers, social dominance, revenge, or sensory goals that the tapas is presumed to unlock. These verses warn against what we might call 'ego-driven spirituality' — the use of spiritual forms for ego-gratification rather than ego-transcendence.

17:6.text: Torturing the aggregate of elements within the body, and also Me who dwells within the body — know these senseless ones to be of demoniac resolve.
17:6.transliteration:
karśayantaḥ śarīra-sthaṃ bhūta-grāmam acetasaḥ |
māṃ caivāntaḥ śarīra-sthaṃ tān viddhy āsura-niścayān ||6||
17:6.devanagari:
कर्शयन्तः शरीरस्थं भूतग्राममचेतसः ।
मां चैवान्तःशरीरस्थं तान्विद्ध्यासुरनिश्चयान् ॥ ६ ॥
17:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:6.explanation: 'Karśayantaḥ śarīra-sthaṃ bhūta-grāmam' — tormenting the aggregate of elements residing in the body: the 'bhūta-grāma' (aggregate of elements) refers to the physical body as constituted by the five elements (earth, water, fire, air, and space). Extreme, self-designed austerities that inflict genuine physical harm on this body are not spiritual — they are a misunderstanding of what tapas means. 'Māṃ caivāntaḥ śarīra-sthaṃ' — and also Me, who dwells within the body: this is the verse's most profound and unexpected statement. When one tortures the body, one is not merely harming a lump of matter — one is harming the dwelling place of the Lord Himself. The Ātman, which is one with Brahman, resides within the body as its innermost witness (antaryāmin). To assault the body with gratuitous violence in the name of tapas is to assault the divine presence within it. 'Acetasaḥ' — the senseless ones, those without proper understanding: this is not personal condemnation but a statement of ignorance — they do not know what they are doing. 'Tān viddhy āsura-niścayān' — know them to be of demoniac resolve: despite the external appearance of piety and severe discipline, those who torture themselves with unscriptural practices, driven by ego and desire, are characterised by the Gītā as āsurī in their deepest conviction. This verse also implicitly teaches respect for the body as a sacred instrument — not to be coddled, but not to be abused.

17:7.text: Even the food that is dear to all is of three kinds; and so too are sacrifice, austerity, and charity threefold. Hear now of this distinction.
17:7.transliteration:
āhāras tv api sarvasya tri-vidho bhavati priyaḥ |
yajñas tapas tathā dānaṃ teṣāṃ bhedam imaṃ śṛṇu ||7||
17:7.devanagari:
आहारस्त्वपि सर्वस्य त्रिविधो भवति प्रियः ।
यज्ञस्तपस्तथा दानं तेषां भेदमिमं शृणु ॥ ७ ॥
17:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:7.explanation: 'Āhāras tv api sarvasya tri-vidho bhavati priyaḥ' — even food, dear to all, is of three kinds: the inclusion of āhāra (food) in this spiritual taxonomy is one of the Gītā's most holistic and practically relevant teachings. Food is not merely a material affair — it is, according to the Vedāntic understanding, intimately connected with the quality of consciousness. The Chāndogya Upaniṣad states: 'āhāra-śuddhau sattva-śuddhi' — the purity of food leads to purity of the inner being (sattva). What one eats is not religiously neutral but reflects and reinforces one's guṇa-constitution. 'Priyaḥ' — dear, preferred: the word 'priya' is careful — it says not what one should eat, but what one naturally prefers, which reveals one's inner guṇa-state. 'Yajñas tapas tathā dānam' — sacrifice, austerity, and charity: these three constitute the pillars of dharmic life in the Gītā's framework. Each of these major domains will now be systematically analysed through the guṇa-lens, providing the practitioner with a precise diagnostic tool for assessing the quality of their own practice. This verse functions as an introductory heading for the three sections that follow.

17:8.text: Foods that promote longevity, vitality, strength, health, happiness, and delight — which are flavourful, smooth, substantial, and pleasing to the heart — are dear to the sāttvika.
17:8.transliteration:
āyuḥ-sattva-balārogya-sukha-prīti-vivardhanāḥ |
rasyāḥ snigdhāḥ sthirā hṛdyā āhārāḥ sāttvika-priyāḥ ||8||
17:8.devanagari:
आयुःसत्त्वबलारोग्यसुखप्रीतिविवर्धनाः ।
रस्याः स्निग्धाः स्थिरा हृद्या आहाराः सात्त्विकप्रियाः ॥ ८ ॥
17:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:8.explanation: This verse provides the Gītā's most detailed characterisation of sāttvika food, analysed through both its effects and its qualities. The six effects of sāttvika food: (1) 'Āyuḥ' — promotes longevity, life-span; (2) 'Sattva' — promotes the sattva of the inner being (clarity of mind, intelligence, discrimination); (3) 'Bala' — promotes strength; (4) 'Ārogya' — promotes health and freedom from disease; (5) 'Sukha' — promotes happiness; (6) 'Prīti' — promotes delight, inner joy and love. The four qualities of sāttvika food: 'Rasyāḥ' — flavourful, possessing good taste: sāttvika food is not bland or austere in taste — it is genuinely satisfying. 'Snigdhāḥ' — smooth, oleaginous, moist: incorporating healthy fats such as ghee, sesame, milk — not dry and rough. 'Sthirāḥ' — substantial, stable, sustaining: nourishing and well-digested, providing sustained energy. 'Hṛdyāḥ' — pleasing to the heart: enjoyed with genuine appreciation, harmonious with one's being.
