Sāttvika foods traditionally include fresh fruits, vegetables, grains, legumes, dairy, honey, and natural sweeteners. The principle is not merely vegetarianism but the holistic quality of food that promotes all six enumerated benefits — particularly the crucial second one: enhancement of the sattva of the inner instrument of consciousness.

17:9.text: Foods that are bitter, sour, salty, excessively hot, pungent, dry, and burning — these are liked by the rājasika and produce pain, grief, and disease.
17:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:9.explanation: The seven qualities characterising rājasika food (kaṭu = bitter, amla = sour, lavaṇa = salty, ati-uṣṇa = excessively hot, tīkṣṇa = pungent/spicy, rūkṣa = dry/rough, vidāhī = burning/caustic to the digestive tract) collectively describe foods that strongly stimulate and agitate both body and mind. This stimulation is the food-analogue of the rajas-guṇa itself — which is characterised by activity, agitation, and restlessness. 'Rājasasyeṣṭāḥ' — dear to the rājasika: the rājasika person is naturally attracted to intensely flavoured, stimulating foods because such foods resonate with and reinforce their inner guṇa-state. The attraction itself is a symptom. 'Duḥkha-śoka-āmaya-pradāḥ' — producing pain, grief, and disease: the consequences of rājasika food are threefold. Physical disease (āmaya): irritation of the digestive system, inflammation, cardiovascular strain. Mental pain (duḥkha): the agitated mind produced by such food experiences greater suffering and dissatisfaction. Grief (śoka): heightened emotional reactivity and vulnerability to sorrow. Modern nutritional research corroborates the Gītā's observation that excessively spiced, processed, high-sodium, and inflammatory foods negatively impact both physical health and mental wellbeing.

17:10.text: Food that is stale, tasteless, putrid, leftover and decayed — and that which is unfit for sacrifice (impure) — is the food dear to the tāmasa.
17:10.transliteration:
yāta-yāmaṃ gata-rasaṃ pūti paryuṣitaṃ ca yat |
ucchiṣṭam api cāmedhyaṃ bhojanaṃ tāmasa-priyam ||10||
17:10.devanagari:
यातयामं गतरसं पूति पर्युषितं च यत् ।
उच्छिष्टमपि चामेध्यं भोजनं तामसप्रियम् ॥ १० ॥
17:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:10.explanation: 'Yāta-yāmaṃ' — food whose 'time has gone', that has lost its vital energy through excessive ageing (prepared more than three praharas ago — approximately nine hours); 'Gata-rasam' — devoid of taste and nourishment, its essence departed; 'Pūti' — putrid, foul-smelling, in the process of decay; 'Paryuṣitam' — stale, left overnight or longer; 'Ucchiṣṭam' — leftovers, food already partially consumed by another; 'Āmedhyam' — impure in the ritual sense, unfit to be offered in a sacred fire — the most comprehensive characterisation of ritual and energetic impurity. All these qualities share a common principle: they describe food that has lost its prāṇa (vital force), its freshness, its capacity to nourish and enliven. Tāmasic food thus mirrors the tāmas-guṇa itself — inertia, decay, heaviness, obscuration. Such food naturally promotes these qualities in the one who eats it. The tāmasa person's natural preference for this type of food is both a symptom of their guṇa-state and a reinforcer of it. The three-verse analysis of food (verses 8–10) forms a coherent dietary philosophy rooted in the understanding that food is not merely physical fuel but a determinant of consciousness — a teaching that finds corroboration in modern research on the gut-brain connection.

17:11.text: That sacrifice which is performed according to scriptural ordinance, by those who desire no fruit, with the conviction that 'it is to be performed' and with mind well-focused — that is sāttvika.
17:11.transliteration:
aphala-prepsunā yajño vidhi-dṛṣṭo ya ijyate |
yaṣṭavyam eveti manaḥ samādhāya sa sāttvikaḥ ||11||
17:11.devanagari:
अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिर्यज्ञो विधिदृष्टो य इज्यते ।
यष्टव्यमेवेति मनः समाधाय स सात्त्विकः ॥ ११ ॥
17:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:11.explanation: 'Aphala-prepsunā' — by one who desires no fruit: this is the cardinal mark of sāttvika sacrifice. The Gītā's teaching on niṣkāma karma (action without desire for results), elaborated comprehensively in Chapter 3, is here applied specifically to yajña. The sacrifice is performed not because it will yield wealth, fame, heaven, or any other benefit, but because it is dharma, because it is right, because the tradition enjoins it — and that is sufficient. 'Vidhi-dṛṣṭaḥ' — in accordance with scriptural prescription: sāttvika yajña observes the proper forms — the correct mantras, materials, procedures, time, and recipient — all as specified by the śāstra. Adherence to procedure without desire for outcome is the complete formula. 'Yaṣṭavyam eveti' — 'it must be performed, simply because it is to be performed': the word 'eva' (only, simply) is critical — the motivational structure is 'this is my duty, therefore I do it', with no supporting calculation of benefit. 'Manaḥ samādhāya' — with the mind concentrated, settled, fully present: the sacrifice is performed with undivided attention and complete inner offering. The three marks of sāttvika yajña are thus: (1) freedom from result-seeking, (2) scriptural conformity, (3) concentrated, wholehearted performance.

17:12.text: O best of Bhāratas, know that sacrifice which is performed with an eye to its fruit, and also for the sake of ostentation — that sacrifice to be rājasika.
17:12.transliteration:
abhisandhāya tu phalaṃ dambhārtham api caiva yat |
ijyate bharata-śreṣṭha taṃ yajñaṃ viddhi rājasam ||12||
17:12.devanagari:
अभिसन्धाय तु फलं दम्भार्थमपि चैव यत् ।
इज्यते भरतश्रेष्ठ तं यज्ञं विद्धि राजसम् ॥ १२ ॥
17:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:12.explanation: 'Abhisandhāya tu phalam' — with the intention (of attaining) fruit: the rājasika yajña is transactional. It is performed as an investment — a calculated spiritual offering intended to yield specific material or celestial returns: wealth, health, progeny, prestige, heavenly rewards. The relationship between the worshipper and the divine is conceived as commercial: I give this; I receive that. 'Dambhārtham api ca eva' — and also for the sake of display: dambha = ostentatious piety, the performance of religious acts for the purpose of social admiration. The practitioner wants to be seen, recognised, praised, and respected as a devout person. The yajña is as much a social performance as a spiritual act, with reputation as a key intended outcome. The critical word 'api' (also) indicates that these two motivations — fruit-seeking and vanity — can coexist, reinforcing each other. 'Taṃ yajñaṃ viddhi rājasam' — know that sacrifice to be rājasika: the instruction to 'know' (viddhi) is an invitation to honest self-examination. The sincere spiritual practitioner regularly scrutinises their own practice: 'Is my pūjā, my sādhanā, my service, my generosity — sāttvika or rājasika?' The ability to make this distinction is itself a mark of spiritual maturity.

17:13.text: That sacrifice which is contrary to scriptural ordinance, which distributes no food, which lacks mantras, which makes no gifts to priests, and which is devoid of faith — that is declared tāmasika.
17:13.transliteration:
vidhi-hīnam asṛṣṭānnaṃ mantra-hīnam adakṣiṇam |
śraddhā-virahitaṃ yajñaṃ tāmasaṃ paricakṣate ||13||
17:13.devanagari:
विधिहीनमसृष्टान्नं मन्त्रहीनमदक्षिणम् ।
श्रद्धाविरहितं यज्ञं तामसं परिचक्षते ॥ १३ ॥
17:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:13.explanation: The tāmasika yajña is defined by five absences — a portrait of spiritual practice stripped of its essential elements: (1) 'Vidhi-hīnam' — without prescribed procedure: performed in defiance of or indifference to scriptural ordinance, following personal whim rather than tradition's distilled wisdom. (2) 'Asṛṣṭānnam' — without distribution of food: the yajña's social dimension is absent. The traditional yajña included the feeding of brāhmaṇas, guests, and the poor — its benefits were to flow outward to the community. Tāmasika yajña is hoarded, performed without generosity. (3) 'Mantra-hīnam' — without mantras: the verbal, vibrational dimension of yajña is absent. Mantras are not mere words but concentrated vehicles of spiritual intention — their absence leaves the yajña without its animating consciousness-force. (4) 'Adakṣiṇam' — without proper remuneration to officiating priests: symbolising the performer's unwillingness to honour those who facilitate the rite, and their general reluctance to give. (5) 'Śraddhā-virahitam' — devoid of faith: the most comprehensive and damning absence. Without śraddhā, every other element becomes hollow. Faith is the soul of the yajña — its absence reduces the entire performance to an empty, mechanical gesture with no capacity to transform the performer or elevate the offering. 'Paricakṣate' — it is called, it is known as: this is the established traditional verdict, not merely the speaker's personal judgement.

17:14.text: Worship of the gods, the twice-born, the teachers, and the wise; purity; uprightness; continence; and non-violence — these are said to be the austerity of the body.
17:14.transliteration:
deva-dvija-guru-prājña-pūjanaṃ śaucam ārjavam |
brahmacaryam ahiṃsā ca śārīraṃ tapa ucyate ||14||
17:14.devanagari:
देवद्विजगुरुप्राज्ञपूजनं शौचमार्जवम् ।
ब्रह्मचर्यमहिंसा च शारीरं तप उच्यते ॥ १४ ॥
17:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:14.explanation: With this verse begins the Gītā's remarkable threefold analysis of tapas across its three dimensions — physical (śārīra), verbal (vāṅmaya), and mental (mānasa) — each then assessed through the three guṇas (verses 17–19). The physical austerities (śārīra tapas) enumerated here involve the body and its outward conduct: 'Deva-dvija-guru-prājña-pūjanam' — the fourfold reverence of: (1) the gods (deva) through worship and ritual; (2) the twice-born (dvija, traditionally brāhmaṇas) as bearers of sacred learning and culture; (3) the guru as the personal guide to liberation — the highest human relationship in the Vedic framework; (4) the wise (prājña) — those of realised understanding. This pūjana is 'physical' tapas because it manifests as prostration, physical service, ceremonial acts, and respectful bodily conduct. 'Śaucam' — purity: both external (bodily cleanliness, hygiene, ritual purity) and internal (purity of intent). 'Ārjavam' — uprightness: non-duplicity expressed physically — body language and conduct that are transparent, without hidden agendas. 'Brahmacaryam' — continence: the disciplined regulation of the sensual-sexual energy, preserving and redirecting it for spiritual purposes. 'Ahiṃsā' — non-violence: not causing physical pain or harm to any being. Together these constitute the use of the body as an instrument of dharma rather than of ego-satisfaction.

17:15.text: Speech that causes no agitation, that is truthful, pleasant, and beneficial — and the practice of scriptural study (svādhyāya) — these are said to be the austerity of speech.
17:15.transliteration:
anudvega-karaṃ vākyaṃ satyaṃ priya-hitaṃ ca yat |
svādhyāyābhyasanaṃ caiva vāṅmayaṃ tapa ucyate ||15||
17:15.devanagari:
अनुद्वेगकरं वाक्यं सत्यं प्रियहितं च यत् ।
स्वाध्यायाभ्यसनं चैव वाङ्मयं तप उच्यते ॥ १५ ॥
17:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:15.explanation: The austerity of speech (vāṅmaya tapas) — the disciplined refinement of one's verbal conduct — is described through four qualities: (1) 'Anudvega-karam' — that which causes no agitation or distress to the listener: the verbal equivalent of ahiṃsā. Speech that disturbs, unsettles, or causes emotional pain, even if factually accurate, has not reached the standard of vāṅmaya tapas. This requires sensitivity and compassion in communication. (2) 'Satyam' — truthful: speech grounded in reality, not in falsehood, flattery, or self-serving distortion. Satyam is the irreducible foundation without which speech has no integrity. (3) 'Priyam' — pleasant, agreeable: delivered with kindness and gentleness, not with harshness. (4) 'Hitam' — beneficial, conducive to the listener's wellbeing: oriented toward the genuine welfare of the recipient, not merely their momentary comfort. The conjunction of these four creates the standard of ideal speech — a standard that is simultaneously ethically demanding and practically profound: truth must be pleasant AND beneficial AND non-agitating. When truth is unpleasant, wisdom is required to find the right mode, moment, and manner of expression. 'Svādhyāyābhyasanam' — the regular practice of scriptural study: the habitual reading and reflection on sacred texts (Veda, Upaniṣad, Gītā), which sanctifies and elevates the quality of all speech by keeping the mind steeped in the highest truths.

17:16.text: Serenity of mind, gentleness, silence, self-restraint, and purity of inner disposition — this is declared the austerity of the mind.
17:16.transliteration:
manaḥ-prasādaḥ saumyatvaṃ maunam ātma-vinigrahaḥ |
bhāva-saṃśuddhir ity etat tapo mānasam ucyate ||16||
17:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:16.explanation:
'Manaḥ-prasādaḥ' — serenity of mind: 'prasāda' literally means clarity, luminosity, and undisturbed calm — the mind's natural healthy state when it is free from agitation, anxiety, and turbulence. Just as still water perfectly reflects the sun, the serene mind perfectly reflects the Ātman within. This is not a forced suppression of the mind but its natural settling into its own clear ground.

'Saumyatvam' — gentleness, mildness: the quality of inner softness and non-harshness. The word 'saumya' derives from 'soma' — associated with the moon's cooling grace and the nectar of immortality. A saumya mind is free from the harsh, aggressive, and combative textures of rajas.

'Maunam' — silence: in this context, not merely the cessation of external speech (which is a vāṅmaya tapas) but the deeper silence of the mind — the reduction of unnecessary mental chatter, the quieting of the vritti-stream. This is the silence that meditation cultivates, and is a prerequisite for the contemplative states described in Chapter 6.

'Ātma-vinigrahaḥ' — self-restraint, self-governance: the disciplined withdrawal of the mind from its outward dispersion toward sense-objects, and its redirection inward. 'Vinigrahah' is stronger than mere 'nigraha' — it implies thorough, complete restraint. This is the practice Kṛṣṇa prescribes in 6.35 — the mind is restrained through practice (abhyāsa) and non-attachment (vairāgya).

'Bhāva-saṃśuddhiḥ' — purification of inner disposition: the most profound of the five. 'Bhāva' refers not to surface emotions but to the fundamental orientation and intention underlying all thought, word, and action. Bhāva-śuddhi means that every activity arises from a motive that is free from egoism, desire, and animosity — that all of one's inner life is oriented by genuine, selfless, loving intention. This is the deepest form of tapas, for it purifies the very source from which all experience flows. Together these five constitute the complete programme of mental discipline that the Gītā consistently holds as the highest tier of spiritual practice.

17:17.text: That threefold austerity — (physical, verbal, and mental) — which is performed by persons of resolute (yoga), with supreme faith and without expectation of fruit — that is called sāttvika.
17:17.transliteration:
śraddhayā parayā taptaṃ tapas tat tri-vidhaṃ naraiḥ |
aphala-kāṅkṣibhir yuktaiḥ sāttvikaṃ paricakṣate ||17||
17:17.devanagari:
श्रद्धया परया तप्तं तपस्तत्त्रिविधं नरैः ।
अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिर्युक्तैः सात्त्विकं परिचक्षते ॥ १७ ॥
17:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:17.explanation:
'Śraddhayā parayā' — with supreme, highest faith: 'parā' qualifies this śraddhā as of the highest order — not ordinary religious conviction but the unshakeable, deeply rooted, thoroughly genuine inner trust that is the hallmark of the spiritually mature practitioner. This connects back to the chapter's central teaching (verse 3: 'śraddhāmayo 'yaṃ puruṣaḥ') — the quality of one's śraddhā determines the quality of everything that flows from it, including one's tapas.

'Tat tri-vidham' — that threefold (austerity): a crucial detail. The sāttvika classification applies when all three dimensions of tapas — physical (verse 14), verbal (verse 15), and mental (verse 16) — are simultaneously performed with the two distinguishing qualities listed here. It is not sufficient for one dimension to be sāttvika; the integration across all three planes is required for the total tapas to qualify.

'Aphala-kāṅkṣibhiḥ' — by those who desire no fruit: the mark of niṣkāma practice appears identically in the descriptions of sāttvika yajña (verse 11) and sāttvika dāna (verse 20). This is the universal signature of sāttvika action in the Gītā — action performed because it is right and in alignment with dharma, not because of the personal benefits it is expected to yield. The practitioner's orientation is toward the perfection of the act itself, not toward any consequence.

'Yuktaiḥ' — by those who are steadfast, disciplined, engaged in practice: 'yukta' in this context means not merely involved but systematically absorbed in the practice with sustained discipline — the regular practitioner for whom tapas is not an occasional exercise but a defining way of life.

The verse thus identifies the two irreducible criteria of sāttvika tapas: (1) parama śraddhā as the inner condition, and (2) aphala-kāṅkṣā as the motivational purity — applied simultaneously across all three dimensions of tapas. This integration of inner condition, motivational purity, and multi-dimensional practice constitutes the Gītā's complete model of sāttvika spiritual discipline.

17:18.text: That austerity which is performed for the sake of honour, respect, and worship, and with ostentation — that is here declared rājasika, unstable, and impermanent.
17:18.transliteration:
satkāra-māna-pūjārthaṃ tapo dambhena caiva yat |
kriyate tad iha proktaṃ rājasaṃ calam adhruvam ||18||
17:18.devanagari:
सत्कारमानपूजार्थं तपो दम्भेन चैव यत् ।
क्रियते तदिह प्रोक्तं राजसं चलमध्रुवम् ॥ १८ ॥
17:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:18.explanation:
'Satkāra-māna-pūjārtham' — for the sake of honour, respect, and worship: the three social currencies of ego-recognition drive rājasika tapas. (1) 'Satkāra' = warm public reception, acclaim, the being-welcomed as a distinguished person; (2) 'māna' = respect, the honour of being regarded as a great ascetic or holy person — the desire for a particular identity in the eyes of others; (3) 'pūjā' = reverence, the desire to be worshipped, to occupy the highest position in the social-spiritual hierarchy. All three are forms of ego-gratification sought through the instrumentalisation of spiritual practice. The profound irony is that tapas is precisely the practice whose purpose is the dissolution of the ego — rājasika tapas uses this ego-dissolving practice to build and sustain the ego instead.

'Dambhena ca eva' — and with hypocrisy: 'dambha' here signifies the deliberate performance of spiritual virtue for an audience — the theatrical display of renunciation, austerity, and sanctity while the inner life remains driven by desire and ambition. It is the systematic misalignment of inner reality and outer presentation.

'Calam' — unstable, wavering: the fruit of rājasika tapas is characterised by impermanence in a specific sense — it fluctuates with the fluctuating opinions of others. When praised, the rājasika tapasvin feels temporarily fulfilled; when not praised (or ignored or criticised), the entire motivation structure collapses. This impermanence is not accidental but intrinsic to a practice built on the shifting ground of social validation.

'Adhruvam' — non-enduring: 'dhruva' (the North Star) symbolises that which is eternally fixed and reliable. 'Adhruva' means that which is not enduring — neither in this world (the social recognition fades) nor in the deeper spiritual sense (no lasting purification of the antaḥkaraṇa is achieved). This word is the Gītā's definitive verdict: rājasika tapas, despite its external impressiveness, cannot deliver the stable, lasting spiritual transformation that is the true goal of tapas.

17:19.text: That austerity which is performed with foolish obstinacy, with self-torture, or for the purpose of destroying another — that is declared tāmasika.
17:19.transliteration:
mūḍha-grāheṇātmano yat pīḍayā kriyate tapaḥ |
parasyotsādanārthaṃ vā tat tāmasam udāhṛtam ||19||
17:19.devanagari:
मूढग्राहेणात्मनो यत्पीडया क्रियते तपः ।
परस्योत्सादनार्थं वा तत्तामसमुदाहृतम् ॥ १९ ॥
17:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:19.explanation:
'Mūḍha-grāheṇa' — with foolish obstinacy, with ignorant tenacity: 'mūḍha' = confused, unenlightened; 'grāha' = the act of grasping, clinging stubbornly. The compound indicates a person who, in a state of fundamental spiritual ignorance, clings to a self-devised programme of austerity with blind persistence — refusing guidance, refusing scriptural wisdom, convinced by sheer stubbornness that their self-constructed path is valid. This is tamas operating at its most characteristic — as the obstruction to learning, the refusal of light, the inertia that masquerades as discipline.

'Ātmanaḥ pīḍayā' — through self-torture: the deliberate infliction of physical and psychological pain upon oneself in the name of tapas. This continues the critique begun in verses 5–6, where those who perform aśāstra-vihita (scripturally unauthorised) austierties are characterised as āsurī. Physical self-mortification that damages the body — the temple of the indwelling Self — is not tapas in the Gītā's understanding; it is the violence of tamas turned inward. Genuine tapas refines; tāmasika 'tapas' only destroys.

'Parasyotsādanārtham vā' — or for the purpose of destroying another: this is the most alarming expression of tāmasika tapas — the use of severe spiritual practice as a weapon against another person. 'Utsādana' (destruction, ruination, uprooting) as the explicit intention. This encompasses the use of austerity to generate occult powers for harmful purposes — curses, black magic, the deliberate spiritual assault of an enemy. The very forms of spiritual practice are here perverted into instruments of malice and destruction — which is the precise inversion of tapas's true purpose (inner purification, refinement, liberation).

'Vā' — or: either self-torture or destruction of another is independently sufficient to classify tapas as tāmasika; the presence of both is even more damning.

The three-verse analysis of tapas (17–19) reveals that the external form of tapas is not self-validating: identical physical austerities can be sāttvika (when performed with genuine faith and desirelessness), rājasika (when performed for honour and recognition), or tāmasika (when performed with ignorant stubbornness, self-destructiveness, or malice). The decisive factor is always the inner condition — the guṇa, śraddhā, and bhāva from which the action proceeds.

17:20.text: That gift which is given with the conviction 'it is to be given', to one who cannot repay, in the right place, at the right time, and to a worthy recipient — that gift is remembered as sāttvika.
17:20.transliteration:
dātavyam iti yad dānaṃ dīyate 'nupakāriṇe |
deśe kāle ca pātre ca tad dānaṃ sāttvikaṃ smṛtam ||20||
17:20.devanagari:
दातव्यमिति यद्दानं दीयतेऽनुपकारिणे ।
देशे काले च पात्रे च तद्दानं सात्त्विकं स्मृतम् ॥ २० ॥
17:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:20.explanation:
'Dātavyam iti' — with the conviction 'it must be given': the defining mark of sāttvika dāna is the identical niṣkāma orientation that defines sāttvika yajña (verse 11: 'yaṣṭavyam eva') and sāttvika tapas (verse 17: 'aphala-kāṅkṣibhiḥ'). The gift is given not as investment, not for recognition, not in expectation of any return — but solely because giving is right, because dharma demands generosity, because the opportunity to give is itself the reward. This is the Gītā's consistent signature of the sāttvika mode across all domains.

'Anupakāriṇe' — to one who is unable to repay: this is the verse's most psychologically incisive element. When one gives to someone who can give back, there is always the possibility of a concealed transactional motive — the gift is at least partially an investment in a relationship of reciprocity. True sāttvika dāna eliminates this possibility entirely by specifically directing itself to those from whom no return is possible: the destitute, the helpless, the sick, the isolated. When the recipient cannot repay in any form, the purity of the giver's motive is confirmed.

'Deśe' — in the right place: (1) a sacred location (tīrtha, place of pilgrimage) where giving multiplies in merit; (2) a location where the need genuinely exists — giving to those who actually lack, not to those who merely request; (3) a place sanctioned by tradition as appropriate for dāna.

'Kāle' — at the right time: (1) at auspicious junctures — festivals, solar/lunar events, moments of community need; (2) when the recipient actually needs it — timeliness transforms adequacy into excellence; (3) the appropriate season and circumstance.

'Pātre' — to a worthy recipient: 'pātra' (literally 'vessel') signifies one who can properly receive and fruitfully use the gift — a person of learning, a sincere spiritual practitioner, a genuinely poor person, someone in real distress. The concept of pātratā (worthiness of the recipient) is integral to the Vedic understanding of dāna — it is not indiscriminate giving but discerning generosity that serves genuine welfare. The convergence of all five elements — the right motive, right recipient, right place, right time, and right person — constitutes the complete standard of sāttvika dāna.

17:21.text: But that gift which is given for the sake of a return favour, or again with a view to some fruit, and which is given grudgingly — that gift is remembered as rājasika.
17:21.transliteration:
yat tu pratyupakārārthaṃ phalam uddiśya vā punaḥ |
dīyate ca parikliṣṭaṃ tad dānaṃ rājasaṃ smṛtam ||21||
17:21.devanagari:
यत्तु प्रत्युपकारार्थं फलमुद्दिश्य वा पुनः ।
दीयते च परिक्लिष्टं तद्दानं राजसं स्मृतम् ॥ २१ ॥
17:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:21.explanation:
'Pratyupakārārtham' — for the sake of reciprocal benefit: the rājasika gift is transactional at its core. 'Pratyupakāra' (return-favour) reveals the hidden ledger of mutual obligation that underlies this giving — 'I give now so that you are indebted to me; when I need something, you will give in return.' This is social-capital investment, not genuine dāna. The giver retains a psychological claim on the recipient that precisely cancels the freedom and grace that characterise true giving.

'Phalam uddiśya' — with a view to some fruit: distinct from pratyupakāra (which is interpersonal reciprocity), this indicates the seeking of transcendent or cosmic returns — the accumulation of puṇya (merit), attainment of heaven, propitiation of deities, or other spiritual-material rewards. The gift is a calculated contribution to the giver's personal spiritual profit account. While this orientation is not without its place in the Vedic system, the Gītā places it firmly in the rājasika category — for the same reason that rājasika yajña is so classified: the motivational impurity of fruit-seeking.

'Parikliṣṭam' — with strain, grudgingly: 'parikliṣṭa' indicates that the giving is accompanied by inner agitation, reluctance, and a sense of painful loss. Even as the gift leaves the hand, the mind is contracted with the thought of what is being sacrificed. The body gives; the heart withholds. This internal protest against the giving — even when the giving itself occurs — is the defining symptom of rājasika dāna.

'Dīyate' — it is given: crucially, the rājasika gift IS given — it does not fail to materialise as tāmasika dāna often does. There is thus a real act of giving; the deficiency is motivational and dispositional, not behavioural. This is why rājasika dāna occupies the middle position — it is better than no giving, but it fails to achieve the full spiritual transformation that sāttvika dāna accomplishes because it leaves the ego's self-centredness fundamentally intact.

17:22.text: That gift which is given in an inappropriate place and time, to unworthy recipients, without respect, and with contempt — that is declared tāmasika.
17:22.transliteration:
adeśa-kāle yad dānam apātrebhyaś ca dīyate |
asat-kṛtam avajñātaṃ tat tāmasam udāhṛtam ||22||
17:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:22.explanation:
'Adeśa-kāle' — in an inappropriate place and time: the precise inversion of the sāttvika requirement of 'deśe kāle ca' (verse 20). Tāmasika dāna fails the test of contextual appropriateness — it is given at the wrong place (where such giving is not fitting, or where no genuine need exists) and at the wrong time (at an inappropriate juncture, or when it cannot serve any genuine purpose). The ignorance (tamas) underlying this giving prevents the discrimination of what is truly called for in a given situation.

'Apātrebhyaś ca' — and to unworthy recipients: the direct inversion of the 'pātre' requirement. 'Apātra' (unworthy vessel) designates one who will not or cannot use the gift for genuine good — who will misuse it, squander it, or deploy it harmfully. Giving to the unworthy is not merely ineffective — it actively enables harm. The tāmasika giver lacks the discernment (viveka) to distinguish worthy from unworthy recipients, or simply does not care.

'Asat-kṛtam' — without proper honour, disrespectfully: 'asat-kṛta' = not done well, without the proper ceremonial acknowledgment of the recipient's dignity. The Vedic tradition insists that the act of giving must honour the recipient — the recipient of dāna stands momentarily in the position of the divine, as a representative of the need that dharma calls one to meet. To give without this recognition — carelessly, perfunctorily — reduces the gift to a mere transfer of objects stripped of its transformative spiritual dimension.

'Avajñātam' — with contempt, with disdain: 'avajñā' = disrespect, looking down upon. The giving is accompanied by visible contempt for the recipient — the gift is thrown rather than offered, the giver's demeanor communicates the recipient's inferiority. This is the worst form of tāmasika dāna: the gift simultaneously given and used as a vehicle for humiliation. The receiver leaves not with dignity intact but with it further diminished. This contradicts the deepest purpose of dāna, which is to recognise the divine in the other and to meet their need in a spirit of equality and compassion. The contrast with sāttvika dāna (verse 20) could not be more complete: where sāttvika dāna honours, respects, and serves, tāmasika dāna misdirects, disrespects, and harms.

17:23.text: 'Oṃ Tat Sat' — this is declared the threefold designation of Brahman. By this were the brāhmaṇas, the Vedas, and the sacrifices ordained of old.
17:23.transliteration:
oṃ tat sad iti nirdeśo brahmaṇas tri-vidhaḥ smṛtaḥ |
brāhmaṇās tena vedāś ca yajñāś ca vihitāḥ purā ||23||
17:23.devanagari:
ॐ तत्सदिति निर्देशो ब्रह्मणस्त्रिविधः स्मृतः ।
ब्राह्मणास्तेन वेदाश्च यज्ञाश्च विहिताः पुरा ॥ २३ ॥
17:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:23.explanation:
With this verse begins the chapter's culminating section (verses 23–28) on the sacred formula 'Oṃ Tat Sat' — a teaching that elevates the entire preceding analysis from the psychological to the metaphysical, providing a divine sanction for all righteous action. The three syllables are Brahman's three-dimensional self-designation:

'Oṃ' — the praṇava: the primordial sacred syllable that is Brahman expressed as sound. The Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad famously opens: 'All this is Oṃ (om ity etad akṣaram idam sarvam).' Oṃ is the vibrational matrix from which all creation emerges and into which it dissolves. Every Vedic mantra, every yajña, every act of worship begins with Oṃ — signalling that the act is a participation in the divine creative-sustaining order (Ṛta).

'Tat' — That: the pronoun pointing to the Absolute beyond all names and forms. 'Tat' is the word used in the great Upaniṣadic mahāvākya 'Tat tvam asi' (Chāndogya Up. 6.8.7) — 'That thou art.' 'Tat' designates Brahman as the unnameable, unconditioned, quality-less (nirguṇa) Absolute that transcends all conceptual frameworks, including the three guṇas that have structured this entire chapter. By this word, all action is dedicated beyond the personal.

'Sat' — Being, Truth, Goodness: designating Brahman as the supreme reality — that which truly IS, as opposed to the apparent-but-contingent reality of the phenomenal world. 'Sat' also carries the meaning of 'good' and 'true' — Brahman is the ground of all genuine goodness and all genuine truth. The use of 'sat' will be elaborated in verses 26–27.

'Brāhmaṇās tena vedāś ca yajñāś ca vihitāḥ purā' — by this, the brāhmaṇas, Vedas, and sacrifices were ordained of old: this statement roots the entire Vedic civilisation — its knowledge-bearers (brāhmaṇas), its knowledge-texts (Vedas), and its knowledge-enactments (yajñas) — in the primordial utterance of Brahman. 'Oṃ Tat Sat' is thus not merely a formula but the metaphysical foundation of dharma itself.

17:24.text: Therefore, the acts of sacrifice, charity, and austerity prescribed by ordinance among the followers of Brahman always proceed after uttering 'Oṃ'.
17:24.transliteration:
tasmād om ity udāhṛtya yajña-dāna-tapaḥ-kriyāḥ |
pravartante vidhānoktāḥ satataṃ brahma-vādinām ||24||
17:24.devanagari:
तस्मादोमित्युदाहृत्य यज्ञदानतपःक्रियाः ।
प्रवर्तन्ते विधानोक्ताः सततं ब्रह्मवादिनाम् ॥ २४ ॥
17:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:24.explanation:
'Tasmāt' — therefore: the logical consequence flowing from verse 23. Since 'Oṃ Tat Sat' constitutes the primordial triple-designation of Brahman and since all Vedic order (brāhmaṇas, Vedas, yajñas) was established through this designation — it follows that every righteous action must begin by invoking this foundation.

'Om ity udāhṛtya' — after uttering 'Oṃ': 'udāhṛtya' = having proclaimed, having declared. Every sacred act begins with the invocation of Oṃ — this is the traditional Vedic practice without exception. Whether reciting a Vedic mantra, beginning a yajña, giving dāna, or commencing tapas — the initial utterance of Oṃ consecrates the act by situating it within the cosmic creative order that Oṃ represents. The act is thus no longer merely a human performance but a participation in the divine creative-sustaining movement (Ṛta).

'Yajña-dāna-tapaḥ-kriyāḥ' — the acts of sacrifice, charity, and austerity: the three pillars of dharmic life announced as the chapter's subject in verse 7. The entire preceding analysis of these three — through all their guṇa-variations — now culminates in this instruction: regardless of the guṇa-category within which a given practice falls, every practice is made sacred and oriented toward liberation by the invocation of Oṃ.

'Vidhānoktāḥ' — prescribed by ordinance, enjoined by scriptural rule: this practice is not arbitrary or optional — it is śāstra-mandated (shāstra-sanjñaptam). The invocation of Oṃ before all sacred acts is an ancient and non-negotiable element of Vedic discipline.

'Satatam' — always, continuously, without exception: there is no class of sacred action for which this beginning is inappropriate. The continuity ('satatam') indicates that this is not a special practice for special occasions but the constant ground of all righteous action.

'Brahma-vādinām' — of those who speak of Brahman, of the knowers and followers of Brahman: the practitioners of the Vedic tradition — those who live by and transmit the sacred knowledge. For them, every act of yajña, dāna, and tapas is simultaneously an act of Brahma-smaraṇa (remembrance of Brahman) through the invariable invocation of Oṃ.

17:25.text: Uttering 'Tat', without aspiration for fruit, the acts of sacrifice and austerity, and the various acts of charity, are performed by those who aspire to liberation.
17:25.transliteration:
tad ity anabhisandhāya phalaṃ yajña-tapaḥ-kriyāḥ |
dāna-kriyāś ca vividhāḥ kriyante mokṣa-kāṅkṣibhiḥ ||25||
17:25.devanagari:
तदित्यनभिसन्धाय फलं यज्ञतपःक्रियाः ।
दानक्रियाश्च विविधाः क्रियन्ते मोक्षकाङ्क्षिभिः ॥ २५ ॥
17:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:25.explanation:
'Tad iti anabhisandhāya phalam' — uttering 'That' without fixation on fruit: if 'Oṃ' inaugurates and consecrates the act (verse 24), 'Tat' is the act of its complete dedication and release. When the practitioner consciously invokes 'Tat' — 'That, the unnameable Brahman' — they are making an explicit declaration: 'This act is not mine. It belongs to That which is beyond name and form. I release all claim to its fruits.' This transforms the act from a personal acquisition strategy into an act of self-transcending offering.

'Anabhisandhāya phalam' — without intentional fixing of the mind on fruit: 'anabhisandhāya' is stronger than mere 'aphala-kāṅkṣā' (desirelessness) — it indicates the active non-orientation of the mind toward outcome. The mind, instead of calculating potential gains, is turned entirely toward the act itself and its dedication to the Supreme. This is the practical mechanism by which the Gītā's cardinal teaching of niṣkāma karma (action without desire for fruits, first introduced in 2.47: 'mā phaleṣu kadācana') is enacted in the domain of yajña, tapas, and dāna.

'Mokṣa-kāṅkṣibhiḥ' — by those who aspire to liberation: the juxtaposition of 'mokṣa-kāṅkṣā' (aspiration for liberation) and 'anabhisandhāya phalam' (no aspiration for fruits) is intentional and illuminating. The desire for mokṣa is not a 'fruit-desire' in the ordinary sense — it is the aspiration to transcend the very condition of fruit-seeking and ego-driven action. The mumukṣu (seeker of liberation) knows that clinging to any fruit — even the fruit of yajña, dāna, or tapas — perpetuates the very saṃsāric bondage they seek to escape. Hence the 'Tat' formula: release the act to That which is beyond both action and fruit.

The sequential logic of the three sacred syllables is now clear: Oṃ = the consecration of the beginning; Tat = the liberation of the outcome through dedication; Sat (verses 26–27) = the affirmation of the inherent goodness and reality of the act itself.

17:26.text: O Pārtha, 'Sat' is used in the sense of existence-reality and in the sense of goodness; and likewise the word 'Sat' is applied to an auspicious action.
17:26.transliteration:
sad-bhāve sādhu-bhāve ca sad ity etat prayujyate |
praśaste karmaṇi tathā sac-chabdaḥ pārtha yujyate ||26||
17:26.devanagari:
सद्भावे साधुभावे च सदित्येतत्प्रयुज्यते ।
प्रशस्ते कर्मणि तथा सच्छब्दः पार्थ युज्यते ॥ २६ ॥
17:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:26.explanation:
'Sad-bhāve' — in the sense of reality, being, existence: 'Sat' in its primary metaphysical meaning designates that which IS — permanent, self-existent being as opposed to the transient, dependent appearance of the phenomenal world. Brahman is the supreme 'Sat' — the Taittirīya Upaniṣad's 'satyam jñānam anantam brahma' identifies Sat (being) with Brahman. This is 'Sat' in its ontological register — the affirmation of Brahman as the only ultimate reality.

'Sādhu-bhāve' — in the sense of goodness, virtue, excellence: 'Sat' in its ethical-evaluative meaning designates that which is genuinely good, virtuous, excellent, dharmic. A 'sat-puruṣa' is a genuinely good person; a 'sat-karma' is a genuinely good action. This usage reflects the Gītā's consistent identification of ultimate metaphysical reality (Brahman) with ultimate ethical value (the Good) — reality and goodness are not accidentally related but constitute a single truth viewed from two angles. What is ultimately real is ultimately good; what is genuinely good participates in the ultimately real.

'Praśaste karmaṇi tathā sac-chabdaḥ pārtha yujyate' — and in an auspicious, praiseworthy action the word 'Sat' is also used: the third register of 'Sat' — the practical-ritual-evaluative. An action that is praiseworthy (praśasta), scripturally sanctioned, properly executed, and conducive to genuine welfare is called 'sat'. The invocation of 'Sat' in such an action is the affirmation: 'This act is real, this act is good, this act participates in Brahman.' It is both a description (this is genuine dharma) and a consecration (I affirm this act in the name of the Supreme Reality).

This verse reveals the extraordinary semantic richness of the syllable 'Sat': it simultaneously holds together the metaphysical (Brahman as ultimate being), the ethical (genuine goodness and virtue), and the practical (the quality of a properly executed sacred act). The next verse (27) applies this threefold 'Sat' specifically to yajña, tapas, and dāna, completing the triad's application to this chapter's three main subjects.

17:27.text: Steadfast commitment in sacrifice, austerity, and charity is also called 'Sat'; and action performed for the sake of these (or for Brahman's sake) is likewise called only 'Sat'.
17:27.transliteration:
yajñe tapasi dāne ca sthitiḥ sad iti cocyate |
karma caiva tad-arthīyaṃ sad ity evābhidhīyate ||27||
17:27.devanagari:
यज्ञे तपसि दाने च स्थितिः सदिति चोच्यते ।
कर्म चैव तदर्थीयं सदित्येवाभिधीयते ॥ २७ ॥
17:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:27.explanation:
'Yajñe tapasi dāne ca sthitiḥ sad iti ca ucyate' — steadfastness, constancy in sacrifice, austerity, and charity is also called 'Sat': 'Sthiti' here means the unwavering, sustained, long-term commitment to these practices — not the occasional, enthusiasm-driven gesture but the deep and consistent discipline that marks genuine sādhanā. A single act of sacrifice, performed once and abandoned, does not transform; the sustained, daily, lifelong orientation of one's entire being toward yajña, tapas, and dāna — this is 'Sthiti', and it is 'Sat'. This usage expands 'Sat' from a description of the quality of an individual act to a characterisation of the entire orientation of a life — to be sthita (steadfast) in these three is to be 'Sat', to be genuinely good, to be rooted in ultimate reality.

'Karma ca eva tad-arthīyaṃ sad ity evābhidhīyate' — action performed for the sake of these (i.e., of yajña, tapas, and dāna, or for Brahman's sake) is called only 'Sat': 'Tad-arthīyam' = aimed at those (yajña, tapas, dāna and their ultimate telos — liberation, Brahman). All the preparatory, supportive, and auxiliary actions that enable the performance of yajña, tapas, and dāna are also 'Sat' — they participate in the quality of 'Sat' by virtue of being oriented toward it. More broadly: any action whatsoever that is performed with the orientation toward Brahman — with 'Tat' as its direction and 'Sat' as its affirmation — is by that very orientation itself designated 'Sat'.

The 'eva' (only, certainly) in 'sad ity evābhidhīyate' carries emphasis: there is no ambiguity — such action IS 'Sat'. This verse completes the practical application of 'Oṃ Tat Sat': the syllable 'Oṃ' consecrates the beginning; 'Tat' dedicates the fruit; 'Sat' affirms the intrinsic value and grounded reality of the act, the commitment, and all that serves them. Together they constitute the complete spiritual technology for transforming every act of dharma into a direct participation in Brahman.

17:28.text: O Pārtha, whatever is offered in sacrifice, given in charity, practiced as austerity, or done in any way without faith is called Asat. It yields no result either here or hereafter.
17:28.transliteration:
aśraddhayā hutaṃ dattaṃ tapastaptaṃ kṛtaṃ ca yat |
asad ity ucyate pārtha na ca tat pretya no iha ||28||
17:28.devanagari:
अश्रद्धया हुतं दत्तं तपस्तप्तं कृतं च यत् ।
असदित्युच्यते पार्थ न च तत्प्रेत्य नो इह ॥ २८ ॥
17:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
17:28.explanation: This concluding verse of Chapter 17 delivers the final and absolute criterion by which all human action is evaluated in the Bhagavad Gita. Throughout this chapter, Krishna has explained that faith (śraddhā) shapes food, worship, austerity, and charity. Here, He reveals the decisive principle: no matter how sacred, scriptural, or socially respected an action may appear externally, it loses all spiritual validity when performed without śraddhā — inner faith, sincerity, reverence, and existential commitment to truth. Sacrifice, charity, and austerity are traditionally regarded as purifying acts, but when they are motivated by habit, fear, social pressure, pride, or mere mechanical obedience, they fail to touch the inner being. The term 'Asat' does not merely indicate something immoral or incorrect; it signifies that which is unreal, groundless, and devoid of ontological substance. Such action does not participate in Sat — ultimate reality — and therefore cannot transform the soul. An act done without faith produces neither inner purification nor lasting peace in this life, because the heart remains unchanged. Nor does it generate any enduring spiritual consequence after death, because it is disconnected from truth itself. Krishna’s declaration is uncompromising: faith alone gives ontological reality to action. Without śraddhā, even actions that are outwardly correct, scripturally sanctioned, and socially praised collapse inwardly into emptiness and meaninglessness.

18:1.text: Arjuna said: O mighty-armed one, O Hṛṣīkeśa, O slayer of Keśī, I wish to know the truth of sannyāsa and tyāga separately.
18:1.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
sannyāsasya mahā-bāho tattvam icchāmi veditum |
tyāgasya ca hṛṣīkeśa pṛthak keśi-niṣūdana ||1||
18:1.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
संन्यासस्य महाबाहो तत्त्वमिच्छामि वेदितुम् ।
त्यागस्य च हृषीकेश पृथक्केशिनिषूदन ॥ १ ॥
18:1.speaker: Arjuna
18:1.explanation: This question — asked at the very opening of the Gītā's final chapter — elegantly recapitulates the foundational philosophical tension of the entire text: the apparent conflict between renunciation (sannyāsa, the complete abandonment of action) and renunciation-in-action (tyāga, the giving up of the fruits of action). Since Chapter 3 Arjuna has been wrestling with this: is it better to act or to abandon action? Chapter 18 will provide the definitive resolution. The three epithets Arjuna uses are not decorative — each invokes a specific dimension of Kṛṣṇa's teaching: 'mahā-bāho' (mighty-armed) honours Kṛṣṇa's cosmic power; 'hṛṣīkeśa' (master of the senses) invokes his authority over the very faculties involved in action and renunciation; 'keśi-niṣūdana' (slayer of Keśī) invokes his role as destroyer of demonic forces — here symbolising the inner enemies of clarity. Asking for the truths 'pṛthak' (separately, distinctly) shows Arjuna's philosophical maturity: he does not want a synthesis that blurs the distinction but a clear analytical account of each.

18:2.text: Śrī Bhagavān said: The wise understand sannyāsa to be the giving up of desire-motivated actions; the discerning declare tyāga to be the renunciation of the fruits of all actions.
18:2.transliteration:
śrī bhagavān uvāca |
kāmyānāṃ karmaṇāṃ nyāsaṃ sannyāsaṃ kavayo viduḥ |
sarva-karma-phala-tyāgaṃ prāhus tyāgaṃ vicakṣaṇāḥ ||2||
18:2.devanagari:
श्री भगवानुवाच ।
काम्यानां कर्मणां न्यासं संन्यासं कवयो विदुः ।
सर्वकर्मफलत्यागं प्राहुस्त्यागं विचक्षणाः ॥ २ ॥
18:2.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:2.explanation: 'Kāmyānāṃ karmaṇāṃ nyāsam' — the setting aside of desire-prompted actions: 'kāmya karma' refers specifically to actions performed with a particular fruit in mind — Vedic rituals for heaven, special vows for a son, ceremonies for worldly prosperity, etc. One tradition calls the abandonment of these 'sannyāsa'. 'Sarva-karma-phala-tyāgaṃ' — giving up the fruits of all actions: another, broader tradition calls this 'tyāga'. Under this definition, one continues all actions — including nityakarma (daily duties), naimittika-karma (occasional duties), and even activities in the world — but surrenders all claim to their fruits. These are two different traditions ('kavayaḥ' — the poet-seers; 'vicakṣaṇāḥ' — the discerning) with two different formulations. By presenting both definitions objectively first, Kṛṣṇa signals that a synthesis is coming — the following verses will establish precisely what kind of renunciation is correct, and which forms of abandonment constitute error.

18:3.text: Some wise thinkers declare that all action is flawed and should be abandoned; while others say that acts of sacrifice, charity, and austerity should never be abandoned.
18:3.transliteration:
tyājyaṃ doṣavad ity eke karma prāhur manīṣiṇaḥ |
yajña-dāna-tapaḥ-karma na tyājyam iti cāpare ||3||
18:3.devanagari:
त्याज्यं दोषवदित्येके कर्म प्राहुर्मनीषिणः ।
यज्ञदानतपःकर्म न त्याज्यमिति चापरे ॥ ३ ॥
18:3.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:3.explanation: 'Tyājyaṃ doṣavad iti eke' — some say action should be given up as defective: this represents the extreme renunciant position. The argument is that all karma is intrinsically tainted — by desire, by ego, by the fundamental duality of subject and object — just as fire is inseparable from its smoke. Under this view, the only true spiritual path is the complete cessation of all voluntary action. This is a philosophically serious position defended by certain Vedāntic and Sāṃkhya traditions. 'Yajña-dāna-tapaḥ-karma na tyājyam iti cāpare' — others say that sacrifice, charity, and austerity should never be abandoned: this represents the engaged-renunciant position. These three types of action — yajña (ritual and social action in service of the whole), dāna (charitable giving), tapas (disciplined self-cultivation) — are the pillars of individual and social flourishing. They cannot be discarded without abandoning one's fundamental obligations. Kṛṣṇa presents both positions with respect before adjudicating in the following verse — a hallmark of the Gītā's epistemological fairness.

18:4.text: O best of the Bhāratas! O tiger among men! Hear my definitive conclusion regarding renunciation. Renunciation has indeed been declared to be of three kinds.
18:4.transliteration:
niścayaṃ śṛṇu me tatra tyāge bharata-sattama |
tyāgo hi puruṣa-vyāghra tri-vidhaḥ samprakīrtitaḥ ||4||
18:4.devanagari:
निश्चयं शृणु मे तत्र त्यागे भरतसत्तम ।
त्यागो हि पुरुषव्याघ्र त्रिविधः सम्प्रकीर्तितः ॥ ४ ॥
18:4.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:4.explanation: 'Niścayaṃ śṛṇu me' — hear my definitive determination: Kṛṣṇa now steps out of the role of objective reporter of traditions and assumes the role of the decisive teacher. The word 'niścaya' (fixed conclusion, certainty) signals that what follows is not one more opinion in a philosophical debate but an authoritative resolution. 'Tatra tyāge' — regarding tyāga: Kṛṣṇa focuses on tyāga rather than sannyāsa because tyāga — the renunciation of fruits while maintaining action — is the Gītā's preferred and more practically viable teaching. The sannyāsa tradition (total cessation of action) remains valid but is not the primary teaching of the Gītā. 'Tri-vidhaḥ samprakīrtitaḥ' — tyāga is of three kinds: the three-guṇa analysis is applied once more — even the act of renunciation is conditioned by one's inner guṇa-state. The type of renunciation one is capable of performing depends on the inner quality of one's consciousness. The two epithets — 'bharata-sattama' (best of Bharatas) and 'puruṣa-vyāghra' (tiger among men) — indicate that this teaching is being given to an exemplary recipient who is worthy of the highest instruction.

18:5.text: Acts of sacrifice, charity, and austerity should not be abandoned — they must indeed be performed. Sacrifice, charity, and austerity are purifying for the wise.
18:5.transliteration:
yajña-dāna-tapaḥ-karma na tyājyaṃ kāryam eva tat |
yajño dānaṃ tapaś caiva pāvanāni manīṣiṇām ||5||
18:5.devanagari:
यज्ञदानतपःकर्म न त्याज्यं कार्यमेव तत् ।
यज्ञो दानं तपश्चैव पावनानि मनीषिणाम् ॥ ५ ॥
18:5.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:5.explanation: 'Na tyājyaṃ kāryam eva tat' — not to be abandoned but rather to be done: this is Kṛṣṇa's unambiguous verdict. Those who advocate abandoning even yajña, dāna, and tapas are mistaken. These three form the irreducible core of ethical and spiritual life. 'Pāvanāni manīṣiṇām' — purifying even for the wise: this is a remarkable statement. It is not only the spiritually immature who need these practices for purification — even the 'manīṣin' (the wise, the discerning) are purified by them. This rules out the idea that once one has attained wisdom, one is beyond these practices. Yajña purifies by cultivating the attitude of offering; dāna purifies by loosening the grip of possessiveness; tapas purifies by strengthening the will's mastery over the impulses. These are not primitive rituals but the fundamental mechanisms of inner transformation — and they remain relevant at all levels of the spiritual path.

18:6.text: But even these actions should be performed, O Pārtha, relinquishing attachment and the fruits. This is my definite and highest conviction.
18:6.transliteration:
etāny api tu karmāṇi saṅgaṃ tyaktvā phalāni ca |
kartavyānīti me pārtha niścitaṃ matam uttamam ||6||
18:6.devanagari:
एतान्यपि तु कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलानि च ।
कर्तव्यानीति मे पार्थ निश्चितं मतमुत्तमम् ॥ ६ ॥
18:6.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:6.explanation: 'Etāny api tu' — even these: Kṛṣṇa now completes the instruction: these three practices (yajña, dāna, tapas) must be performed — but they must be performed in a specific way. 'Saṅgaṃ tyaktvā phalāni ca' — having abandoned attachment and the fruits: 'saṅga' is the attachment to the act itself — the ego's investment in the practice, the pride of the performer, the feeling of 'I am performing a great sacrifice.' 'Phalāni' are the specific rewards expected. Both must be released. 'Kartavyāni' — as duties: the key word. These are not optional extras for the spiritually ambitious — they are duties that belong to one's nature and station. Done as duties, without ego-ownership and without fruit-desire, they become the very vehicle of liberation. 'Niścitaṃ matam uttamam' — my definite and highest view: 'uttamam' (highest, supreme) marks this as the definitive conclusion of the entire discussion. This verse is the practical summary of the Gītā's central teaching of niṣkāma-karma: act, but act without attachment and without desire for results.

18:7.text: The renunciation of one's prescribed duties is not proper. The abandonment of such duties through delusion is declared to be tāmasic renunciation.
18:7.transliteration:
niyatasya tu sannyāsaḥ karmaṇo nopapadyate |
mohāt tasya parityāgas tāmasaḥ parikīrtitaḥ ||7||
18:7.devanagari:
नियतस्य तु संन्यासः कर्मणो नोपपद्यते ।
मोहात्तस्य परित्यागस्तामसः परिकीर्तितः ॥ ७ ॥
18:7.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:7.explanation: 'Niyatasya karmaṇaḥ' — of prescribed duty: 'niyata' karma refers to one's obligatory, appointed duties — those determined by one's svabhāva (inner nature), varṇa, and āśrama. These cannot legitimately be renounced. 'Mohāt tasya parityāgaḥ' — abandoning such duties through delusion (moha): 'moha' is the crucial term — it is the confusion and darkness of mind that leads a person to think 'all action is bondage, I will renounce everything' while in fact evading their actual responsibilities. This is the delusion of mistaking laziness and escapism for spiritual renunciation. 'Tāmasaḥ parikīrtitaḥ' — declared to be tāmasic: the Gītā here makes a stark and uncomfortable pronouncement. What looks like lofty spiritual renunciation can in fact be the most base expression of tamas — inertia, irresponsibility, and moral cowardice. Abandoning one's duties while calling it 'detachment' is not liberation — it is the most refined form of self-deception.

18:8.text: One who abandons an action as painful, out of fear of bodily discomfort, performs a rājasic renunciation and does not obtain the fruit of renunciation.
18:8.transliteration:
duḥkham ity eva yat karma kāya-kleśa-bhayāt tyajet |
sa kṛtvā rājasaṃ tyāgaṃ naiva tyāga-phalaṃ labhet ||8||
18:8.devanagari:
दुःखमित्येव यत्कर्म कायक्लेशभयात्त्यजेत् ।
स कृत्वा राजसं त्यागं नैव त्यागफलं लभेत् ॥ ८ ॥
18:8.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:8.explanation: 'Duḥkham ity eva' — merely thinking 'this is painful': the motive here is purely self-interest — the desire to avoid discomfort. 'Kāya-kleśa-bhayāt' — out of fear of physical hardship: when a person avoids tapas because it is physically demanding, or skips yajña because it is inconvenient, or withholds dāna because giving feels painful — this is rājasic renunciation. The underlying force is not wisdom but desire for comfort and aversion to effort. 'Naiva tyāga-phalaṃ labhet' — does not obtain the fruit of renunciation: because this renunciation is not genuine. The 'fruit of renunciation' is inner purification, freedom from ego, and eventually liberation. None of this can be achieved by an act of avoidance motivated by ego-comfort. Rājasic renunciation is a middle category — not the deluded tamasic abandonment of duty, but not the free sāttvika renunciation of fruits either. It leaves the person spiritually stagnant.

18:9.text: But when prescribed action is performed, O Arjuna, simply as duty — having abandoned attachment and fruit — that renunciation is considered sāttvika.
18:9.transliteration:
kāryam ity eva yat karma niyataṃ kriyate 'rjuna |
saṅgaṃ tyaktvā phalaṃ caiva sa tyāgaḥ sāttvikaḥ mataḥ ||9||
18:9.devanagari:
कार्यमित्येव यत्कर्म नियतं क्रियतेऽर्जुन ।
सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलं चैव स त्यागः सात्त्विको मतः ॥ ९ ॥
18:9.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:9.explanation: 'Kāryam ity eva' — simply 'it is to be done': this three-word phrase captures the entire spirit of sāttvika renunciation. No reward is sought, no acclaim desired, no punishment feared — the action is undertaken purely because it is duty, because it belongs to the situation, because it is what is called for. 'Niyataṃ kriyate' — prescribed action is performed: one does not abandon one's obligatory duties; one performs them fully and completely. 'Saṅgaṃ tyaktvā phalaṃ caiva' — having given up both attachment and fruit: 'saṅga' is the ego's investment in the action — the sense of 'I am the doer, this is mine'; 'phala' is the anticipated result. Both are released. 'Sāttvikaḥ mataḥ' — considered sāttvika: this is the Gītā's model of enlightened action. It is not inaction; it is not the mechanical performance of duties with grim obligation — it is the free, alert, fully engaged performance of duty with complete inner detachment. This verse is the practical definition of karma-yoga.

18:10.text: The renouncer, saturated with sattva, wise and free from all doubt, does not hate the inauspicious action nor cling to the auspicious.
18:10.transliteration:
na dveṣṭy akuśalaṃ karma kuśale nānuṣajjate |
tyāgī sattva-samāviṣṭo medhāvī chinna-saṃśayaḥ ||10||
18:10.devanagari:
न द्वेष्ट्यकुशलं कर्म कुशले नानुषज्जते ।
त्यागी सत्त्वसमाविष्टो मेधावी छिन्नसंशयः ॥ १० ॥
18:10.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:10.explanation: 'Na dveṣṭy akuśalaṃ karma' — does not hate disagreeable action: the sāttvika tyāgī does not base his engagement with action on whether the action is pleasant, rewarding, or successful. A difficult, thankless, painful duty is not avoided because it is unpleasant. 'Kuśale nānuṣajjate' — does not cling to agreeable action: nor does a successful, pleasant, praised action generate attachment. The orientation is entirely duty-based, not pleasure-or-pain-based. 'Sattva-samāviṣṭaḥ' — fully pervaded by sattva: this is the description of a person in whom the sāttvika quality has so thoroughly established itself that the entire psychic apparatus operates from clarity rather than desire. 'Medhāvī' — truly wise: real wisdom is not mere intellectual acuity but the practical ability to act without the distorting filter of personal preference. 'Chinna-saṃśayaḥ' — with all doubts severed: the source of doubt is the pull of competing desires — 'shall I do this or that? which will be better for me?' When action is guided entirely by duty and not by preference, doubt has nothing to feed on.

18:11.text: For an embodied being it is truly not possible to completely abandon all actions. But one who gives up the fruits of action — that person is truly called a renouncer.
18:11.transliteration:
na hi deha-bhṛtā śakyaṃ tyaktuṃ karmāṇy aśeṣataḥ |
yas tu karma-phala-tyāgī sa tyāgīty abhidhīyate ||11||
18:11.devanagari:
न हि देहभृता शक्यं त्यक्तुं कर्माण्यशेषतः ।
यस्तु कर्मफलत्यागी स त्यागीत्यभिधीयते ॥ ११ ॥
18:11.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:11.explanation: 'Na hi deha-bhṛtā śakyaṃ tyaktuṃ karmāṇy aśeṣataḥ' — for an embodied being, the complete relinquishment of all action is simply not possible: this is one of the Gītā's most important pragmatic acknowledgements. As long as there is a body, there is breathing, eating, sleeping, thinking — all of which constitute 'action' at some level. The ideal of total karmaic abstinence is not only philosophically questionable but practically impossible. 'Yas tu karma-phala-tyāgī sa tyāgīty abhidhīyate' — but the one who gives up the fruits of action is the true renouncer: this is the Gītā's definitive redefinition of 'tyāgī.' A renouncer is not someone who lives in a cave having abandoned all outward activity — a renouncer is someone who acts fully and completely in the world while releasing all personal claim to the outcomes. This definition democratises the spiritual ideal: it is available to the householder, the soldier, the merchant, the farmer — anyone who can perform their duties without the ego's grasping at results.

18:12.text: The threefold fruit of action — the undesirable, the desirable, and the mixed — accrues after death to those who have not relinquished; but never at all to those who have renounced.
18:12.transliteration:
aniṣṭam iṣṭaṃ miśraṃ ca tri-vidhaṃ karmaṇaḥ phalam |
bhavaty atyāgināṃ pretya na tu sannyāsinām kvacit ||12||
18:12.devanagari:
अनिष्टमिष्टं मिश्रं च त्रिविधं कर्मणः फलम् ।
भवत्यत्यागिनां प्रेत्य न तु संन्यासिनां क्वचित् ॥ १२ ॥
18:12.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:12.explanation: 'Tri-vidhaṃ karmaṇaḥ phalam' — the threefold fruit of action: aniṣṭa (unpleasant, painful, sinful consequences), iṣṭa (pleasant, meritorious, heaven), and miśra (a mixture of both). These correspond to the three guṇa-qualities of action and their karmic consequences. 'Atyāgināṃ pretya' — accruing after death to the non-relinquishers: those who performed actions with attachment and desire for specific results are bound to experience those results — not only in this life but in future lives. This is the mechanism of saṃsāra (the cycle of rebirth). 'Na tu sannyāsinām kvacit' — but never at all to the renouncer: here 'sannyāsin' means the karma-phala-tyāgī — the one who performed all prescribed duties without attachment to results. Since there was no desire, there is no seed for future fruit. This is the Gītā's doctrine of action-as-liberation: the complete performance of duty without fruit-desire severs the karmic chain entirely, generating no new saṃskāra, no new compulsion, no new rebirth.

18:13.text: O mighty-armed one, learn from me the five causes that have been stated in Sāṃkhya philosophy for the accomplishment of all actions.
18:13.transliteration:
pañcaitāni mahā-bāho kāraṇāni nibodha me |
sāṃkhye kṛtānte proktāni siddhaye sarva-karmaṇām ||13||
18:13.devanagari:
पञ्चैतानि महाबाहो कारणानि निबोध मे ।
साङ्ख्ये कृतान्ते प्रोक्तानि सिद्धये सर्वकर्मणाम् ॥ १३ ॥
18:13.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:13.explanation: 'Pañca kāraṇāni' — five causes: Kṛṣṇa now introduces one of the Gītā's most philosophically sophisticated analyses — the causal structure underlying every act of action. The question being prepared for is: if the ātman (Self) is the eternal witness and not the doer, then what actually does the doing? The five-cause analysis answers this. 'Sāṃkhye kṛtānte' — in the established conclusions of Sāṃkhya: 'kṛtānte' signifies a well-established, authoritative philosophical framework. The Sāṃkhya tradition is the analytical school of Indian philosophy that breaks reality into enumerable components and examines their interactions. 'Siddhaye sarva-karmaṇām' — for the accomplishment of all actions: this is not abstract philosophy — it is the practical metaphysics of action. Understanding the five causes has a direct soteriological (liberation-related) purpose: it dismantles the ego's false claim to sole authorship of action, thereby opening the door to genuine freedom.

18:14.text: The seat of action (body), the agent (ego), the various instruments (sense organs), the many different motions (vital functions), and fifth here — fate (daiva) — these are the five causes.
18:14.transliteration:
adhiṣṭhānaṃ tathā kartā karaṇaṃ ca pṛthag-vidham |
vividhāś ca pṛthak ceṣṭā daivaṃ caivātra pañcamam ||14||
18:14.devanagari:
अधिष्ठानं तथा कर्ता करणं च पृथग्विधम् ।
विविधाश्च पृथक्चेष्टा दैवं चैवात्र पञ्चमम् ॥ १४ ॥
18:14.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:14.explanation: The five causes systematically dismantled: 1. 'Adhiṣṭhānam' — the seat or base of action: the physical body, which is the platform upon which all action takes place. Without a body, no action is possible. 2. 'Kartā' — the agent: the jīva's ahaṃkāra (ego-sense), the functional sense of 'I am the one acting.' Note: this is NOT the eternal ātman, which is the pure witness. The 'kartā' is the ego-instrument, the phenomenal self that identifies with the body-mind complex. 3. 'Karaṇaṃ pṛthag-vidham' — the various instruments: the five sense organs (jñānendriyas), the five motor organs (karmendriyas), and the mind — the full array of cognitive and active instruments. 4. 'Vividhāś ca pṛthak ceṣṭāḥ' — the various separate motions: the five prāṇas (vital life-energies — prāṇa, apāna, vyāna, udāna, samāna) whose activities sustain the body and make coordinated action possible. 5. 'Daivam' — fate, the unseen force: the accumulated results of past karma — the invisible causal force that shapes the conditions, constraints, and outcomes of present action. The profound implication is that no single factor is the 'real' cause of action — all five operate together. The ego's claim to be the sole author of its actions is therefore metaphysically false.

18:15.text: Whatever action a person undertakes with body, speech, or mind — whether proper or improper — these five are its causes.
18:15.transliteration:
śarīra-vāṅ-manobhir yat karma prārabhate naraḥ |
nyāyyaṃ vā viparītaṃ vā pañcaite tasya hetavaḥ ||15||
18:15.devanagari:
शरीरवाङ्मनोभिर्यत्कर्म प्रारभते नरः ।
न्याय्यं वा विपरीतं वा पञ्चैते तस्य हेतवः ॥ १५ ॥
18:15.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:15.explanation: 'Śarīra-vāṅ-manobhiḥ' — by body, speech, and mind: the three channels through which all human action flows — physical acts, verbal acts (speech, thought expressed as words), and mental acts (intention, imagination, planning). Every conceivable human activity operates through one or more of these three. 'Nyāyyaṃ vā viparītaṃ vā' — whether righteous or unrighteous: crucially, the five-cause analysis applies to all action — not just virtue but vice too. This prevents the ego from claiming credit for good actions while deflecting responsibility for bad ones. The mechanism is universal. 'Pañca ete tasya hetavaḥ' — these five are its causes: the philosophical implication is liberating. If the ātman (pure Self) is NOT among the five causes — and indeed it is not, for the ātman is the witnessing consciousness that neither acts nor is affected — then the ātman cannot be the author of karma. The ego (kartā) listed as the second cause is not the ātman but the phenomenal agent. Understanding this frees the wise person from the false sense of absolute personal authorship.

18:16.text: That being so, one who perceives the pure Self alone as the doer — that person, of undeveloped intelligence, is of perverted understanding and does not see truly.
18:16.transliteration:
tatraivaṃ sati kartāram ātmānaṃ kevalaṃ tu yaḥ |
paśyaty akṛta-buddhitvān na sa paśyati durmatiḥ ||16||
18:16.devanagari:
तत्रैवं सति कर्तारमात्मानं केवलं तु यः ।
पश्यत्यकृतबुद्धित्वान्न स पश्यति दुर्मतिः ॥ १६ ॥
18:16.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:16.explanation: 'Tatraivaṃ sati' — that being so (given the five-cause structure): after having established that action arises from five cooperating causes, Kṛṣṇa now identifies the fundamental error of the ego. 'Kartāram ātmānaṃ kevalaṃ tu yaḥ paśyati' — whoever perceives the Self alone as the sole doer: here 'ātmānam' refers not to the pure ātman (which is not a doer) but to the empirical self, the ego. The person who says 'I alone did this, my effort alone produced this result' is picking out only one of the five causal factors (the kartā/ego) and claiming it to be the whole story — ignoring the body, the instruments, the vital functions, and the unseen force of fate. 'Akṛta-buddhitvāt' — due to undeveloped, raw intelligence: 'akṛta' means not fully formed, not mature. This person has not developed the discriminative capacity to see reality in its full complexity. 'Na sa paśyati' — does not truly see: the person thinks they are perceiving reality, but they are constructing a highly selective, ego-flattering fiction. This verse provides the philosophical foundation for egoless action: understanding the five causes dissolves the ego's false claim to absolute authorship.

18:17.text: One whose inner being is free from the sense of 'I am the doer,' whose intelligence is not tainted — even having killed all these worlds, such a person neither kills nor is bound.
18:17.transliteration:
yasya nāhaṅkṛto bhāvo buddhir yasya na lipyate |
hatvāpi sa imāl lokān na hanti na nibadhyate ||17||
18:17.devanagari:
यस्य नाहङ्कृतो भावो बुद्धिर्यस्य न लिप्यते ।
हत्वापि स इमाँल्लोकान्न हन्ति न निबध्यते ॥ १७ ॥
18:17.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:17.explanation: 'Yasya nāhaṅkṛto bhāvaḥ' — whose inner being has no sense of 'I am the doer': 'ahaṅkṛta bhāva' is the ego's appropriation of action — the feeling 'I am doing this, this is my deed.' This is the primary mechanism of karmic bondage. When there is no ego-ownership, there is no karmic residue. 'Buddhir yasya na lipyate' — whose intelligence is not tainted: 'lipyate' means to be smeared, contaminated, adhered to. The person's intellect is not stained by calculation of personal gain or loss, by pride of accomplishment, or by the anticipation of reward. 'Hatvāpi sa imāl lokān na hanti na nibadhyate' — even having slain all these worlds, neither kills nor is bound: this is one of the Gītā's most startling statements and must be understood precisely. It is not a licence for violence — it is a metaphysical statement about the relationship between consciousness and karma. When action arises from the absence of ego-ownership (the ahaṅkāra is not the author) and from non-attachment of intelligence, the action does not generate new karmic seeds. This is directly relevant to Arjuna's situation: if he fights from duty, without personal malice, without the ego's desire for glory or revenge, the action does not bind him.

18:18.text: Knowledge, the object of knowledge, and the knower — these are the threefold impetus to action (karma-codanā). The instrument, the action, and the agent — these are the threefold basis of action (karma-saṃgraha).
18:18.transliteration:
jñānaṃ jñeyaṃ parijñātā tri-vidhā karma-codanā |
karaṇaṃ karma karteti tri-vidhaḥ karma-saṃgrahaḥ ||18||
18:18.devanagari:
ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं परिज्ञाता त्रिविधा कर्मचोदना ।
करणं कर्म कर्तेति त्रिविधः कर्मसङ्ग्रहः ॥ १८ ॥
18:18.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:18.explanation: This verse presents a remarkably compact philosophical framework — two triads that together constitute the complete structure of any act of action. The first triad is epistemological — it concerns the knowledge-side of action: 'jñānam' (the knowledge or awareness that motivates), 'jñeyam' (the object to be known — the 'what' that calls forth action), and 'parijñātā' (the fully knowing subject — the one aware). Together these three form the 'why' of action — every act is motivated by some form of knowing and the recognition of an object. The second triad is ontological — it concerns the doing-side of action: 'karaṇam' (the instruments — senses and organs), 'karma' (the action itself or its object), and 'kartā' (the agent). Together these form the 'how' of action — the machinery through which action is actually executed. These six factors in two triads will now be analysed through the lens of the three guṇas, producing a comprehensive taxonomy of action across all possible modes of human engagement.

18:19.text: Knowledge, action, and the agent — these are also of three kinds each, based on the distinction of guṇas, as has been stated in the Sāṃkhya teaching on guṇas. Hear them rightly as well.
18:19.transliteration:
jñānaṃ karma ca kartā ca tridhaiva guṇa-bhedataḥ |
procyate guṇa-saṃkhyāne yathāvac chṛṇu tāny api ||19||
18:19.devanagari:
ज्ञानं कर्म च कर्ता च त्रिधैव गुणभेदतः ।
प्रोच्यते गुणसङ्ख्याने यथावच्छृणु तान्यपि ॥ १९ ॥
18:19.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:19.explanation: 'Jñānaṃ karma ca kartā ca tridhaiva guṇa-bhedataḥ' — knowledge, action, and agent — all three are themselves threefold according to guṇa: this is a remarkable extension of the guṇa-framework. Not only outward activities like worship, food, sacrifice, and charity are guṇa-conditioned — even the most intimate and apparently internal elements of human activity — the quality of one's knowing (jñāna), the quality of one's acting (karma), and the quality of one's sense of self-as-agent (kartā) — are all conditioned by the three guṇas. 'Guṇa-saṃkhyāne' — in the Sāṃkhya analysis of guṇas: this refers to the Sāṃkhya philosophical tradition's systematic methodology of analysing all of reality through the lens of the three guṇas. 'Yathāvac chṛṇu' — hear them rightly: the adverb 'yathāvat' (as they truly are, exactly) indicates that precision and care are required in the following exposition — these distinctions are subtle and of high practical importance.

18:20.text: Know that knowledge by which one perceives in all beings a single, imperishable existence — undivided in the divided — to be Sāttvika.
18:20.transliteration:
sarva-bhūteṣu yenaikaṃ bhāvam avyayam īkṣate |
avibhaktaṃ vibhakteṣu taj jñānaṃ viddhi sāttvikam ||20||
18:20.devanagari:
सर्वभूतेषु येनैकं भावमव्ययमीक्षते ।
अविभक्तं विभक्तेषु तज्ज्ञानं विद्धि सात्त्विकम् ॥ २० ॥
18:20.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:20.explanation: With verse 20, the exposition of the three types of jñāna (knowledge), karma (action), and kartā (agent) begins — precisely as announced in verse 19. The Sāttvika knowledge is defined by its supreme content: 'sarva-bhūteṣu yenaikaṃ bhāvam avyayam īkṣate' — that knowledge by which one perceives, across the entire field of all beings, one single, imperishable Reality (bhāva: existence, essence, being). This is not merely an intellectual thesis about unity but a direct perceptual realisation — the act of seeing (īkṣate) the One in the many. 'Avibhaktaṃ vibhakteṣu' — undivided within the divided: though all beings appear as separate, distinct, differentiated entities (vibhaktam) to the ordinary eye, the sāttvika knowing sees through this apparent multiplicity to the one indivisible Ātman-Brahman that underlies, pervades, and constitutes all of them. This is the highest form of knowledge in the Vedāntic framework — not knowledge of facts, nor knowledge of distinctions, but the direct, lived recognition of non-dual unity. 'Avyayam' — imperishable: the One Reality does not increase or decrease, is not born and does not die, is not affected by the arising and passing of forms. This knowledge is the antidote to all conflict, hatred, and exploitation — for one who perceives one's own Self in all beings cannot harm any.

18:21.text: But know that knowledge which perceives in all beings separately manifold existences of distinct kinds — through the lens of separateness — to be Rājasa.
18:21.transliteration:
pṛthaktvena tu yaj jñānaṃ nānā-bhāvān pṛthag-vidhān |
vetti sarveṣu bhūteṣu taj jñānaṃ viddhi rājasam ||21||
18:21.devanagari:
पृथक्त्वेन तु यज्ज्ञानं नानाभावान्पृथग्विधान् ।
वेत्ति सर्वेषु भूतेषु तज्ज्ञानं विद्धि राजसम् ॥ २१ ॥
18:21.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:21.explanation: 'Pṛthaktvena' — through the lens of separateness, differentiation: the rājasic knower approaches all beings and things as fundamentally distinct, independent, separate entities. 'Nānā-bhāvān pṛthag-vidhān' — many different kinds of existence (diverse natures, distinct qualities, separate identities): humans differ from animals, animals from plants, one person from another — and the rājasic intellect maps these distinctions with precision and elaboration. 'Vetti sarveṣu bhūteṣu' — it knows this multiplicity across all beings. This knowledge is not wholly false — differentiation and multiplicity are a genuine level of phenomenal reality; the rājasic knower is not deluded in the way the tāmasic knower is. But it is incomplete: it perceives the surface diversity without penetrating to the unifying Ātman beneath. Rājasic knowledge generates the intellect of science, commerce, statecraft, and analysis — indispensable in the world yet insufficient for liberation. Its characteristic limitation is that by always seeing 'the other' as fundamentally other, it breeds comparison, competition, possessiveness, and the inability to realise universal kinship. The rājasic knower can never rest — there is always another distinction to observe, another classification to make.

18:22.text: But that knowledge which clings to one single object as if it were everything, which is without rational ground, without grasp of reality, and trivial — that is declared to be Tāmasa.
18:22.transliteration:
yat tu kṛtsnavad ekasmin kārye saktam ahaitukam |
atattvārthavad alpaṃ ca tat tāmasam udāhṛtam ||22||
18:22.devanagari:
यत्तु कृत्स्नवदेकस्मिन्कार्ये सक्तमहैतुकम् ।
अतत्त्वार्थवदल्पं च तत्तामसमुदाहृतम् ॥ २२ ॥
18:22.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:22.explanation: 'Kṛtsnavad ekasmin kārye saktam' — clinging to a single limited object, phenomenon, or domain as though it were the totality of everything: the tāmasic knower mistakes the part for the whole — like a frog in a well that imagines the well to be the entire ocean. This is the knowledge of fanaticism, of narrow ideology, of parochial worldviews that take one fragment of experience as the complete picture of reality. 'Ahaitukam' — without rational ground, causally ungrounded: this knowledge is not supported by valid evidence, inference, or testimony. It operates through blind belief, superstition, prejudice, and unconsidered assumption. 'Atattvārthavat' — not grasping the true nature of reality (tattva-artha): it is not merely incomplete but actively misleading — it mistakes the non-real for real, the unimportant for important, the impermanent for permanent. 'Alpam ca' — and trivial, small in scope: it has neither breadth nor depth. The three qualifiers together constitute a precise phenomenology of ignorance-mistaken-for-knowledge: narrowness of scope, absence of rational foundation, and distortion of reality. Crucially, tāmasic 'knowledge' is more dangerous than admitted ignorance — for admitted ignorance is open to learning, while tāmasic pseudo-knowledge mistakes itself for wisdom and closes itself to correction.

18:23.text: Action which is ordained (niyata), free from attachment, performed without passion or aversion, by one who does not desire its fruit — that action is called Sāttvika.
18:23.transliteration:
niyataṃ saṅga-rahitam arāga-dveṣataḥ kṛtam |
aphala-prepsunā karma yat tat sāttvikaṃ ucyate ||23||
18:23.devanagari:
नियतं सङ्गरहितमरागद्वेषतः कृतम् ।
अफलप्रेप्सुना कर्म यत्तत्सात्त्विकमुच्यते ॥ २३ ॥
18:23.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:23.explanation: The three-fold typology of karma begins here. Sāttvika action is defined by four interlocking qualities: (1) 'Niyatam' — ordained, prescribed, duty-determined: the action is performed because it is one's dharmic duty (svadharma), as enjoined by scripture and one's station in life — not because of personal whim or desire. It has a legitimate, duty-grounded basis. (2) 'Saṅga-rahitam' — free from attachment: no clinging to the action itself, to the means of performing it, or to any identity derived from being 'the doer' of this action.
