(3) 'Arāga-dveṣataḥ kṛtam' — performed without rāga (liking, attraction toward preferred outcomes) and dveṣa (disliking, aversion toward feared outcomes): the agent is internally equanimous, not pulled by desires toward certain results or pushed by fears away from others. (4) 'Aphala-prepsunā' — by one who does not seek the fruit: this is the Gita's foundational principle of niṣkāma karma — action offered without personal claim on the result. The fruit is surrendered to the Divine, to dharma, to the common good. Together, these four qualities describe action that moves through the world without creating karmic residue — action that serves without binding, gives without grasping.

18:24.text: But action performed by one who desires objects of enjoyment, or performed again with egoism, and with much exertion — that is declared Rājasa.
18:24.transliteration:
yat tu kāmepsunā karma sāhaṃkāreṇa vā punaḥ |
kriyate bahulāyāsaṃ tad rājasam udāhṛtam ||24||
18:24.devanagari:
यत्तु कामेप्सुना कर्म साहंकारेण वा पुनः ।
क्रियते बहुलायासं तद्राजसमुदाहृतम् ॥ २४ ॥
18:24.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:24.explanation: 'Kāmepsunā' — by one who desires fruits of enjoyment (kāma-ipsā: desire for the desired): the action is primarily motivated by personal gain, pleasure, or acquisition of a specific result. This is sakāma karma — action with expectation of reward — the opposite of niṣkāma karma. 'Sāhaṃkāreṇa' — with egoism (sa-ahaṃkāra: accompanied by the I-sense): the performer constantly identifies as 'I am the doer', 'this is my achievement', 'my power, my skill, my effort produced this'. The ego takes ownership of the process and the result. 'Vā punaḥ' — 'or again', meaning either kāmepsutā or ahaṃkāra (or both) are present. 'Bahulāyāsam' — involving great effort, strain, and exertion: because the rājasic actor is attached to the result, every obstacle produces anxiety, every setback produces distress, and the action is coloured by a driven, pressured quality. This excessive straining (āyāsa) is itself a symptom of attachment. Rājasic action is the action of ordinary worldly life — business, ambition, achievement, competition — which sustains civilisation but also perpetuates the cycle of desire-action-bondage. It is not evil but it is binding.

18:25.text: Action that is undertaken out of delusion, without regard for consequences, loss, harm to others, and one's own capacity — that is declared Tāmasa.
18:25.transliteration:
anubandhaṃ kṣayaṃ hiṃsām anapekṣya ca pauruṣam |
mohād ārabhyate karma yat tat tāmasam ucyate ||25||
18:25.devanagari:
अनुबन्धं क्षयं हिंसामनपेक्ष्य च पौरुषम् ।
मोहादारभ्यते कर्म यत्तत्तामसमुच्यते ॥ २५ ॥
18:25.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:25.explanation: Tāmasic action is characterised by the complete absence of four types of consideration: 'Anubandham anapekṣya' — without regard for anubandha (the sequential consequences, the chain of outcomes that will follow from the action): the tāmasic actor never thinks ahead, never traces the downstream effects of what they are about to do. 'Kṣayam anapekṣya' — without regard for kṣaya (loss, depletion, harm to oneself or one's resources): the action may exhaust energy, waste resources, undermine one's own welfare — none of this is considered. 'Hiṃsām anapekṣya' — without regard for hiṃsā (injury, suffering, harm to others): the pain that the action will cause to other beings is simply not factored in. This is moral blindness of the worst order. 'Pauruṣam anapekṣya' — without regard for pauruṣa (one's actual capacity, competence, authority): the tāmasic person undertakes tasks far beyond their ability or mandate, or acts without any self-assessment. 'Mohād ārabhyate' — and all of this springs from moha (delusion, confusion, the fogging of the discriminative faculty): not from considered evil intent but from the complete absence of clarity. Tāmasic action is the action of impulsiveness, recklessness, cruelty, and stupidity — and is destructive both to the actor and to those affected.

18:26.text: The agent who is free from attachment, who does not claim the doership (non-egotistic), who is endowed with fortitude and enthusiasm, and who is unaffected by success or failure — is called Sāttvika.
18:26.transliteration:
mukta-saṅgo 'nahaṃvādī dhṛty-utsāha-samanvitaḥ |
siddhy-asiddhyor nirvikāraḥ kartā sāttvika ucyate ||26||
18:26.devanagari:
मुक्तसङ्गोऽनहंवादी धृत्युत्साहसमन्वितः ।
सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योर्निर्विकारः कर्ता सात्त्विक उच्यते ॥ २६ ॥
18:26.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:26.explanation: The three-fold typology of the kartā (agent, doer) begins here. The sāttvika agent is characterised by five qualities: (1) 'Mukta-saṅgaḥ' — released from attachment: no clinging to the work itself, its means, its results, or one's identity as performer. (2) 'Anahaṃvādī' — not one who asserts 'I am the doer' (a-ahaṃ-vādī: non-declarer of ego): this is the quality of non-doership in action — the sāttvika agent knows that the Ātman is the real witness, that Prakṛti and its guṇas are the true agents, and that the ego's claim of authorship is a presumption. This is the inner recognition that dissolves karmic bondage. (3) 'Dhṛty-utsāha-samanvitaḥ' — endowed with both dhṛti (steady fortitude, unwavering persistence) and utsāha (enthusiastic energy, vitality): this pairing is crucial and balanced. Dhṛti without utsāha becomes inertia; utsāha without dhṛti becomes recklessness. The sāttvika agent brings both: sustained, energetic, calm engagement. (4) 'Siddhy-asiddhyor nirvikāraḥ' — unaffected by success and failure: in victory, no inflation; in defeat, no deflation. This is the samabhāva (equanimity) that the Gita advocates throughout — rooted not in indifference but in the recognition that results belong to a domain beyond the doer's control.

18:27.text: The agent who is passionate, who craves the fruits of action, who is greedy, of a harmful nature, impure, and who is moved by joy and grief — that agent is declared Rājasa.
18:27.transliteration:
rāgī karma-phala-prepsur lubdho hiṃsātmako 'śuciḥ |
harṣa-śokānvitaḥ kartā rājasaḥ parikīrtitaḥ ||27||
18:27.devanagari:
रागी कर्मफलप्रेप्सुर्लुब्धो हिंसात्मकोऽशुचिः ।
हर्षशोकान्वितः कर्ता राजसः परिकीर्तितः ॥ २७ ॥
18:27.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:27.explanation: Six defining characteristics of the rājasic agent: (1) 'Rāgī' — passionate, characterised by intense rāga (attachment, craving, longing): the rājasic agent has deep emotional investment in their work, their people, and their outcomes. (2) 'Karma-phala-prepsuḥ' — eager for the fruits of action: every action is performed with a personal stake in the result. This is the defining feature of sakāma karma — self-interested, result-oriented activity. (3) 'Lubdhaḥ' — greedy, covetous: never satisfied with what has been obtained, always seeking more. The appetite of the rājasic agent is inherently insatiable. (4) 'Hiṃsātmakaḥ' — of a harmful nature: in the pursuit of goals, the rājasic agent does not scruple to cause harm — to competitors, subordinates, or bystanders. Ruthlessness is accepted as a pragmatic necessity. (5) 'Aśuciḥ' — impure, unclean: either inwardly (through vices, dishonesty, mixed motives) or outwardly (through lack of cleanliness in conduct), or both. (6) 'Harṣa-śokānvitaḥ' — accompanied by exultation and grief: emotionally driven, the rājasic agent soars in triumph and collapses in defeat. This reactive oscillation is the direct consequence of attachment to results — the agent has made their emotional wellbeing hostage to outcomes they cannot fully control.

18:28.text: The agent who is undisciplined, vulgar, arrogant, deceitful, malicious toward others, lazy, despondent, and procrastinating — is called Tāmasa.
18:28.transliteration:
ayuktaḥ prākṛtaḥ stabdhaḥ śaṭho naiṣkṛtiko 'lasaḥ |
viṣādī dīrgha-sūtrī ca kartā tāmasa ucyate ||28||
18:28.devanagari:
अयुक्तः प्राकृतः स्तब्धः शठो नैष्कृतिकोऽलसः ।
विषादी दीर्घसूत्री च कर्ता तामस उच्यते ॥ २८ ॥
18:28.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:28.explanation: Eight razor-sharp characteristics of the tāmasic agent: (1) 'Ayuktaḥ' — undisciplined, unsteadied: no inner balance, no self-restraint, no collected attention or yogic composure. (2) 'Prākṛtaḥ' — vulgar, crude, untrained: one who acts purely on raw instinct and sensory impulse, without the refining influence of education, scripture, or discrimination — functioning at the level of animal nature. (3) 'Stabdhaḥ' — arrogant, stiff, obdurate: puffed with conceit, unwilling to listen, learn, or yield — the combination of pride and mental inertia. (4) 'Śaṭhaḥ' — deceitful, dishonest: cunning, crafty, willing to deceive others for personal advantage. (5) 'Naiṣkṛtikaḥ' — one who destroys others' livelihood or means: this is active malice — not just indifference to others' suffering but deliberate harm to their sources of sustenance. (6) 'Alasaḥ' — lazy: deeply averse to effort, seeking always the path of least resistance, unwilling to engage fully with any task. (7) 'Viṣādī' — despondent, chronically sorrowful: the tāmasic agent has no hope, no joy, no vision of possibility — existence is coloured by heaviness and joylessness. (8) 'Dīrgha-sūtrī' — procrastinating (literally 'one who draws the thread long'): endlessly postponing what needs to be done, unable to bring anything to completion. Together, these eight qualities paint the portrait of a consciousness overwhelmed by tamas — inert, dishonest, destructive, and incapable of purposeful action.

18:29.text: Hear now, O Dhanañjaya, the threefold division of intellect (buddhi) and also of fortitude (dhṛti), according to the guṇas — declared in full, distinctly, without remainder.
18:29.transliteration:
buddher bhedaṃ dhṛteś caiva guṇatas tri-vidhaṃ śṛṇu |
procyamānam aśeṣeṇa pṛthaktvena dhanañjaya ||29||
18:29.devanagari:
बुद्धेर्भेदं धृतेश्चैव गुणतस्त्रिविधं श्रृणु ।
प्रोच्यमानमशेषेण पृथक्त्वेन धनञ्जय ॥ २९ ॥
18:29.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:29.explanation: Having completed the three-fold analysis of jñāna (vv.20–22), karma (vv.23–25), and kartā (vv.26–28), Krishna now turns to two further inner faculties: buddhi (discriminative intellect — the power of knowing what is right, what is real) and dhṛti (resolve, fortitude — the power of holding to what is known and willed). 'Buddher bhedaṃ dhṛteś caiva' — the distinctions of buddhi and dhṛti: these two are placed together because they function as a unified pair. Buddhi discerns the correct course; dhṛti sustains the resolve to hold to it against the pull of distraction, temptation, and adversity. Both are of three kinds according to the three guṇas. 'Guṇatas tri-vidham' — the same threefold guṇic framework is applied. 'Procyamānam aśeṣeṇa pṛthaktvena' — aśeṣeṇa: without remainder, exhaustively, completely; pṛthaktvena: distinctly, with each variety clearly separated — a mark of the meticulous precision of this entire exposition. This transitional verse announces the next six verses (30–35).

18:30.text: O Pārtha, the intellect that knows the paths of engagement (pravṛtti) and withdrawal (nivṛtti), what ought to be done and ought not to be done, what is to be feared and what is not, what is bondage and what is liberation — that intellect is Sāttvika.
18:30.transliteration:
pravṛttiṃ ca nivṛttiṃ ca kāryākārye bhayābhaye |
bandhaṃ mokṣaṃ ca yā vetti buddhiḥ sā pārtha sāttvikī ||30||
18:30.devanagari:
प्रवृत्तिं च निवृत्तिं च कार्याकार्ये भयाभये ।
बन्धं मोक्षं च या वेत्ति बुद्धिः सा पार्थ सात्त्विकी ॥ ३० ॥
18:30.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:30.explanation: Sāttvika buddhi is defined by its capacity to correctly discriminate four pairs of complementary realities: (1) 'Pravṛttiṃ ca nivṛttiṃ ca' — pravṛtti (the path of action, engagement, when to move into the world) and nivṛtti (the path of withdrawal, renunciation, when to step back): the sāttvika intellect knows both and knows when each is appropriate — the wisdom of right timing and right context. (2) 'Kāryākārye' — what ought to be done (kārya: action enjoined by dharma and wisdom) and what ought not (akārya: action prohibited by dharma and wisdom): the clear knowledge of right and wrong action without confusion or compromise. (3) 'Bhayābhaye' — what is truly to be feared (adharma, the consequences of wrong action, the cycle of saṃsāra) and what is not truly to be feared (death, worldly censure, temporary discomfort — for the liberated soul): discernment between genuine danger and mere seeming-danger. (4) 'Bandhaṃ mokṣaṃ ca' — bondage and liberation: knowing precisely what binds (attachment, ego, desire, wrong action) and what liberates (knowledge, dispassion, surrender, right action). This is the supreme spiritual discernment — the Vedāntic viveka in its purest form. This buddhi is the product of scriptural study, reflection, meditation, and divine grace working together.

18:31.text: O Pārtha, the intellect which perceives dharma and adharma, and what ought to be done and what ought not, in a distorted way (not as they truly are) — that intellect is Rājasī.
18:31.transliteration:
yayā dharmaṃ adharmaṃ ca kāryaṃ cākāryam eva ca |
ayathāvat prajānāti buddhiḥ sā pārtha rājasī ||31||
18:31.devanagari:
यया धर्ममधर्मं च कार्यं चाकार्यमेव च ।
अयथावत्प्रजानाति बुद्धिः सा पार्थ राजसी ॥ ३१ ॥
18:31.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:31.explanation: 'Dharmaṃ adharmaṃ ca' — dharma and adharma (right and wrong, righteous and unrighteous). 'Kāryaṃ cākāryam eva ca' — what ought to be done and what ought not. 'Ayathāvat prajānāti' — knows them not as they truly are: this is the crucial phrase. The rājasī buddhi is not wholly ignorant — it recognises the categories of dharma/adharma and kārya/akārya — but its perception is distorted, skewed, or incomplete. It sees through the lens of personal interest, desire, ego-investment, or passionate attachment. The characteristic cognitive failure of rājasī buddhi is rationalisation — the process by which the intellect, in service of the ego's desires, constructs seemingly-logical justifications for actions that are in fact self-serving or adharmic. The rājasic mind knows the right thing but finds compelling reasons to do the convenient thing instead. It does not deny dharma openly — that would be too honest — instead, it reframes, reinterprets, and contextualises until dharma conveniently aligns with desire. This is the intellectual corruption of rajovṛtti (rājasic mental tendency), and it is subtler and therefore more dangerous than the frank confusion of tāmasī buddhi.

18:32.text: O Pārtha, the intellect that, enveloped in darkness (tamas), regards adharma as dharma and sees all things in inverted order — that intellect is Tāmasī.
18:32.transliteration:
adharmaṃ dharmam iti yā manyate tamasāvṛtā |
sarvārthān viparītāṃś ca buddhiḥ sā pārtha tāmasī ||32||
18:32.devanagari:
अधर्मं धर्ममिति या मन्यते तमसावृता ।
सर्वार्थान्विपरीतांश्च बुद्धिः सा पार्थ तामसी ॥ ३२ ॥
18:32.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:32.explanation: 'Tamasāvṛtā' — completely veiled, enveloped, overwhelmed by tamas (ignorance, inertia, the darkening principle): unlike the rājasī buddhi which is biased, the tāmasī buddhi is wholly obscured — the natural luminosity of discriminative intelligence is entirely suppressed. 'Adharmaṃ dharmam iti manyate' — it considers adharma to be dharma: this is not merely bias or rationalisation — it is total inversion. It takes the harmful, the wrong, the destructive as right, correct, and sacred. This is the cognitive error of extremism, fanaticism, and blind ideology — where cruelty is called justice, violence is called righteousness, and ignorance is called wisdom. 'Sarvārthān viparītāṃś ca' — it perceives all meanings and values in inverted, reversed form: the eternal is seen as perishable, the perishable as eternal; pleasure is found in what causes suffering; the truth is mistaken for falsehood. This complete reversal of all values is tamas at its most dangerous. If rājasī buddhi is a compass pointing in a skewed direction, tāmasī buddhi is a compass that points exactly backward — and the more confidently and decisively one follows it, the more catastrophically wrong one's path becomes.

18:33.text: O Pārtha, the fortitude (dhṛti) by which — through unwavering yoga — one sustains and directs the activities of the mind, the prāṇa, and the senses — that dhṛti is Sāttvikī.
18:33.transliteration:
dhṛtyā yayā dhārayate manaḥ-prāṇendriya-kriyāḥ |
yogenāvyabhicāriṇyā dhṛtiḥ sā pārtha sāttvikī ||33||
18:33.devanagari:
धृत्या यया धारयते मनःप्राणेन्द्रियक्रियाः ।
योगेनाव्यभिचारिण्या धृतिः सा पार्थ सात्त्विकी ॥ ३३ ॥
18:33.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:33.explanation: 'Dhṛti' — fortitude, resolve, the power of holding firm: the inner faculty that sustains directed attention and steadfast purposefulness across time and adversity. It is the psychological complement of buddhi — if buddhi knows the direction, dhṛti supplies the power to hold to it. 'Avyabhicāriṇyā' — unwavering, non-deviating (a-vi-abhicāra: without going astray): this is the key qualifier. Sāttvikī dhṛti is not merely present — it is unshakeable and consistent across all circumstances, not varying with mood, season, or situation. The same word 'avyabhicāriṇī' is used in chapter 13 to describe the highest bhakti. 'Yogena' — through yoga: this fortitude is not a natural endowment but a cultivated achievement — it is earned through systematic yogic practice (meditation, prāṇāyāma, self-discipline, continuous inner work). 'Manaḥ-prāṇendriya-kriyāḥ dhārayate' — it sustains and governs the activities of the mind (thought, feeling, imagination), the prāṇa (the vital breath and its modifications), and the sense organs (perception and their engagement with objects): the three great levels of inner activity are all held in purposeful, disciplined alignment. This is the architecture of inner mastery.

18:34.text: O Pārtha, but the fortitude by which one holds to dharma, kāma (pleasure), and artha (wealth) — with attachment and with desire for the fruit — that dhṛti is Rājasī.
18:34.transliteration:
yayā tu dharma-kāmārthān dhṛtyā dhārayate 'rjuna |
prasaṅgena phalākāṅkṣī dhṛtiḥ sā pārtha rājasī ||34||
18:34.devanagari:
यया तु धर्मकामार्थान्धृत्या धारयतेऽर्जुन ।
प्रसङ्गेन फलाकाङ्क्षी धृतिः सा पार्थ राजसी ॥ ३४ ॥
18:34.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:34.explanation: 'Dharma-kāmārthān dhārayate' — it holds to dharma, kāma, and artha: the three of the four classical puruṣārthas (aims of life) — duty, pleasure, and material welfare. Note that mokṣa (liberation), the fourth and highest puruṣārtha, is absent here. The rājasī dhṛti is sufficient to sustain engagement with the first three aims of life but lacks the reach to grasp the fourth. 'Prasaṅgena' — through attachment, with deep personal investment: the agent is emotionally and volitionally attached to these three pursuits. 'Phalākāṅkṣī' — desiring the fruit: dharma is observed for the reward (heaven, prestige, merit); kāma is pursued for immediate enjoyment; artha is acquired for security and power. The motivation throughout is personal gain. Rājasī dhṛti is not a moral failing — it drives the entire productive, civilisational life of the world and is indispensable at that level. But it is the fortitude of ambition, not liberation. It binds as surely as it sustains: the agent who holds to dharma-kāma-artha through attachment and fruit-desire remains firmly anchored in saṃsāra, however energetically and reliably they hold their course.

18:35.text: O Pārtha, the fortitude by which a dull-witted person does not release sleep (sloth), fear, grief, despondency, and arrogance — that dhṛti is Tāmasī.
18:35.transliteration:
yayā svapnaṃ bhayaṃ śokaṃ viṣādaṃ madam eva ca |
na vimuñcati durmedhā dhṛtiḥ sā pārtha tāmasī ||35||
18:35.devanagari:
यया स्वप्नं भयं शोकं विषादं मदमेव च ।
न विमुञ्चति दुर्मेधा धृतिः सा पार्थ तामसी ॥ ३५ ॥
18:35.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:35.explanation: 'Durmedhā' — one of dull intelligence (dur-medhā: weak, impaired medhā — the cognitive faculty of grasping, retaining, and applying knowledge): the tāmasic holder is someone whose inner light of discrimination is deeply obscured. What does the tāmasī dhṛti hold? 'Svapnam' — sleep, torpor, excessive unconsciousness (both literal sleep and the metaphorical sleep of spiritual inertia). 'Bhayam' — fear: irrational, groundless anxiety, the grip of existential dread unrelieved by wisdom. 'Śokam' — grief: persistent, unresolved sorrow, wallowing in loss. 'Viṣādam' — despondency, depression, hopelessness — the complete collapse of inner energy and purpose (ironically, viṣāda is what Arjuna experiences in Chapter 1, which initiates the entire Gita). 'Madam eva ca' — and arrogance (mada: the intoxication of ego, the swollen sense of self-importance). 'Na vimuñcati' — does not release, does not let go: this is the defining perversity of tāmasī dhṛti. The tāmasic person is not incapable of holding firm — they hold with great tenacity, but they hold to precisely what needs to be released: inertia, fear, grief, depression, ego. Their 'fortitude' is the stubbornness of darkness — clinging to what should be relinquished, unable to move upward.

18:36.text: O best of Bharatas, hear now from me the threefold nature of pleasure — that in which one rejoices through practice, and which leads to the end of suffering.
18:36.transliteration:
sukhaṃ tv idānīṃ tri-vidhaṃ śṛṇu me bharatarṣabha |
abhyāsād ramate yatra duḥkhāntaṃ ca nigacchati ||36||
18:36.devanagari:
सुखं त्विदानीं त्रिविधं श्रृणु मे भरतर्षभ ।
अभ्यासाद्रमते यत्र दुःखान्तं च निगच्छति ॥ ३६ ॥
18:36.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:36.explanation: With verse 36, the final and perhaps most practically significant of all the three-fold classifications is announced: the three types of sukha (pleasure, happiness, well-being). This is the culmination of the entire guṇic analysis because all human motivation — every action, every choice, every striving — is ultimately directed toward experiencing sukha and escaping duḥkha (suffering). 'Tri-vidhaṃ śṛṇu' — hear the threefold: the same methodical framework is applied. 'Abhyāsād ramate yatra' — in which one rejoices through practice, through cultivation: this is the key discriminator for the highest pleasure. The sāttvika sukha is not immediately gratifying — it must be earned through sustained practice (abhyāsa: repeated, disciplined engagement). The tāmasic pleasure requires no cultivation at all — it is immediately available to the lowest impulse. The rājasic pleasure requires some effort but yields results relatively quickly. The sāttvika pleasure demands the most sustained investment but yields the most lasting and real return. 'Duḥkhāntaṃ ca nigacchati' — and which leads to the end of all suffering: the sāttvika sukha does not merely provide momentary relief from suffering but actually brings one to the end of the very root of suffering — ignorance and identification with the perishable.

18:37.text: That pleasure which, at first, is like poison, but at the end is like nectar — born of the clarity of one's own Ātman and buddhi — is declared Sāttvika.
18:37.transliteration:
yat tad agre viṣam iva pariṇāme 'mṛtopamam |
tat sukhaṃ sāttvikaṃ proktam ātma-buddhi-prasāda-jam ||37||
18:37.devanagari:
यत्तदग्रे विषमिव परिणामेऽमृतोपमम् ।
तत्सुखं सात्त्विकं प्रोक्तमात्मबुद्धिप्रसादजम् ॥ ३७ ॥
18:37.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:37.explanation: 'Agre viṣam iva' — like poison at the beginning: all forms of sāttvika pleasure involve an initial period of difficulty, discomfort, and resistance. Meditation begins with scattered, restless attention. Study demands sustained concentration. Fasting and austerity strain the body. Practice of non-attachment means denying the ego's habitual gratifications. This initial 'poison' is the natural resistance of the inertia-bound mind-body system to the discipline of sattva. 'Pariṇāme 'mṛtopamam' — but in the end, like nectar (amṛta: the immortal substance, the divine drink of the gods): with sustained practice, the very activities that seemed aversive transform into the source of the deepest, most stable, most expansive joy available to a human being. The meditator who perseveres through the initial restlessness discovers the silence that lies beyond — and that silence is the highest pleasure. 'Ātma-buddhi-prasāda-jam' — born of the prasāda (clarity, luminosity, serenity) of the Ātman and buddhi: this pleasure does not originate in any external object or sensory contact but in the inherent luminosity of the purified intellect (buddhi-prasāda) in contact with its own source, the Ātman. Therefore, it cannot be taken away by any external change — it is independent of circumstances. This is the Gita's definition of real happiness: durable, self-sustaining, growing with practice, and ultimately revealing the Ātman itself.

18:38.text: That pleasure arising from the contact of sense organs and sense objects — which at first seems like nectar but at the end is like poison — is remembered as Rājasa.
18:38.transliteration:
viṣayendriya-saṃyogād yat tad agre 'mṛtopamam |
pariṇāme viṣam iva tat sukhaṃ rājasaṃ smṛtam ||38||
18:38.devanagari:
विषयेन्द्रियसंयोगाद्यत्तदग्रेऽमृतोपमम् ।
परिणामे विषमिव तत्सुखं राजसं स्मृतम् ॥ ३८ ॥
18:38.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:38.explanation: This verse is the precise inversion of verse 37 — the same two stages (beginning and end) are reversed. 'Viṣayendriya-saṃyogāt' — from the union (saṃyoga) of sense organs (indriya) and sense objects (viṣaya): this is the origin of all sensory pleasure — the pleasurable contact between a sensory faculty and its corresponding object (eye and beautiful form, tongue and delicious taste, ear and pleasant sound, etc.). 'Agre 'mṛtopamam' — at first, like nectar: this description captures with perfect accuracy the phenomenology of sensory pleasure. The initial taste is intensely appealing — the food smells wonderful before it is eaten, the music enchants at first hearing, the romantic encounter at its beginning glows with promise. The ego rushes toward the experience with full excitement. 'Pariṇāme viṣam iva' — but at the end, like poison: this is the empirically observable trajectory of all purely sensory pleasure. After satiation comes dulling, after dulling comes craving, after craving comes desperate seeking, after desperate seeking comes either frustration or brief re-satiation followed again by dulling. The addiction cycle is the extreme form of this pattern. Even when the pleasure is not addictive, the loss of the pleasant object produces grief proportional to the depth of attachment. The rājasic path thus creates a structural suffering through the very mechanism of its pleasure. This is what the Gita means when it says desires are like fire (3.39) — the fuel of pleasure feeds the flame of want rather than extinguishing it.

18:39.text: That pleasure which — both at the beginning and subsequently — deludes the self, arising from sleep, sloth, and heedlessness — that is declared Tāmasa.
18:39.transliteration:
yad agre cānubandhe ca sukhaṃ mohanam ātmanaḥ |
nidrālasya-pramādotthaṃ tat tāmasam udāhṛtam ||39||
18:39.devanagari:
यदग्रे चानुबन्धे च सुखं मोहनमात्मनः ।
निद्रालस्यप्रमादोत्थं तत्तामसमुदाहृतम् ॥ ३९ ॥
18:39.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:39.explanation: 'Yad agre cānubandhe ca' — both at the beginning and at the end (anubandhe: in the sequel, in the consequence): this is the key structural distinction. Sāttvika pleasure is bitter at first, sweet at last; rājasic pleasure is sweet at first, bitter at last; tāmasic pleasure is neither truly bitter nor truly sweet at any stage — it is 'mohanam' throughout. 'Sukhaṃ mohanam ātmanaḥ' — pleasure that deludes the Self: it does not stimulate, it does not purify, it does not intensify experience — it merely stupefies. It clouds consciousness, reduces awareness, and produces a kind of pleasant numbness rather than genuine joy. This is not the nectar of sattva or even the excitement of rajas — it is the fog of tamas dressed as rest. 'Nidrālasya-pramādottham' — arising from three characteristic tāmasic states: nidrā (sleep, excessive drowsiness — the retreat into unconsciousness), ālasya (laziness, the positive aversion to effort), and pramāda (heedlessness, carelessness, intoxication — the dulling of awareness and moral attention). The pleasure derived from oversleeping, from avoiding all effort, and from being carelessly distracted is tāmasic pleasure. It deluds both at its inception and in its aftermath — there is no 'nectar phase' at all. The tāmasic person considers their stupor to be bliss and their inertia to be peace. This is the deepest form of self-deception.

18:40.text: There is no being on earth, or again in heaven among the gods, that is free from these three guṇas born of Prakṛti.
18:40.transliteration:
na tad asti pṛthivyāṃ vā divi deveṣu vā punaḥ |
sattvaṃ prakṛtijair muktaṃ yad ebhiḥ syāt tribhir guṇaiḥ ||40||
18:40.devanagari:
न तदस्ति पृथिव्यां वा दिवि देवेषु वा पुनः ।
सत्त्वं प्रकृतिजैर्मुक्तं यदेभिः स्यात्त्रिभिर्गुणैः ॥ ४० ॥
18:40.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:40.explanation: This verse is the grand conclusion of the entire guṇa-analysis that has occupied verses 20–39. It is one of the most sweeping metaphysical declarations in the Gita — a statement of absolute universality. 'Na tad asti pṛthivyāṃ' — there is no such thing on earth: among all earthly beings — human, animal, plant — there is no exception. 'Divi deveṣu vā punaḥ' — nor in heaven among the gods: even the celestial beings — Indra, Brahmā, the devas — are not free from the three guṇas. This is a crucial point. The gods are beings of highly sāttvic conditioning but they are still within Prakṛti, still conditioned by guṇas. Even Brahmā (the creator) is a manifestation within Prakṛti and is ultimately transient. 'Sattvaṃ prakṛtijair muktaṃ yad ebhiḥ syāt tribhir guṇaiḥ' — no being (sattva: any existent entity) could be free from these three guṇas born of Prakṛti. The guṇas are the very substance of Prakṛti — wherever Prakṛti extends, the guṇas extend with it. They cannot be escaped while one remains within the domain of Prakṛti. Liberation (mokṣa) means transcending Prakṛti altogether — not by manipulating or balancing the guṇas but by realising the Puruṣa/Ātman that is prior to, and the witness of, all Prakṛtic manifestation. This verse thus simultaneously establishes the universality of the guṇas and the necessity of transcending them.

18:41.text: O Parantapa, the duties of Brāhmaṇas, Kṣatriyas, Vaiśyas, and Śūdras are distributed according to the guṇas born of their own nature (svabhāva).
18:41.transliteration:
brāhmaṇa-kṣatriya-viśāṃ śūdrāṇāṃ ca parantapa |
karmāṇi pravibhaktāni svabhāva-prabhavair guṇaiḥ ||41||
18:41.devanagari:
ब्राह्मणक्षत्रियविशां शूद्राणां च परन्तप ।
कर्माणि प्रविभक्तानि स्वभावप्रभवैर्गुणैः ॥ ४१ ॥
18:41.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:41.explanation: With verse 41, a new and profoundly significant section begins (41–45): the Gita's teaching on the four-fold division of social duty (varṇa-dharma) as grounded in guṇa-psychology rather than hereditary birth alone. 'Svabhāva-prabhavair guṇaiḥ' — by the guṇas arising from svabhāva (one's own nature, the particular combination of guṇas that characterises an individual's psycho-physical constitution, developed over lifetimes of karma and conditioning): this is the key. The Gita's understanding of varṇa (the four-fold division) is explicitly guṇa-based and svabhāva-based — not merely birth-based. This is fully consistent with the earlier declaration (4.13): 'cātur-varṇyaṃ mayā sṛṣṭaṃ guṇa-karma-vibhāgaśaḥ' — the four-fold order was created by the Lord based on the distinctions of guṇa and karma. 'Karmāṇi pravibhaktāni' — duties are distributed distinctly: each social role has its own specific duties appropriate to the guṇic constitution that characterises it. This is a theory of specialisation based on innate tendency — the individual best serves the whole by performing the function that aligns with their deepest nature. The next four verses specify these duties precisely.

18:42.text: Equanimity of mind (śama), self-restraint (dama), austerity (tapas), purity (śauca), patience (kṣānti), uprightness (ārjava), knowledge (jñāna), experiential wisdom (vijñāna), and faith in the Supreme (āstikya) — these are the natural duties of a Brāhmaṇa.
18:42.transliteration:
śamo damas tapaḥ śaucaṃ kṣāntir ārjavam eva ca |
jñānaṃ vijñānam āstikyaṃ brahma-karma svabhāvajam ||42||
18:42.devanagari:
शमो दमस्तपः शौचं क्षान्तिरार्जवमेव च ।
ज्ञानं विज्ञानमास्तिक्यं ब्रह्मकर्म स्वभावजम् ॥ ४२ ॥
18:42.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:42.explanation: Nine defining qualities of the Brāhmaṇa's svabhāva-karma — all predominantly sāttvic, oriented toward knowledge, refinement, and spiritual leadership: (1) 'Śamaḥ' — tranquillity of mind (śama): the capacity to withdraw mental activity from external agitation and sustain inner stillness — the foundational quality of all meditative and contemplative life. (2) 'Damaḥ' — restraint of the senses: the disciplined governance of the sensory faculties, not letting them run uncontrolled after objects. (3) 'Tapaḥ' — austerity: the willing acceptance of physical, verbal, and mental discipline for purification and higher purpose (see 17.14–16 for the three-fold tapas). (4) 'Śaucam' — purity: outer (bodily and behavioural) and inner (of motive and thought) cleanliness. (5) 'Kṣāntiḥ' — patience, forbearance: the capacity to endure difficulty, opposition, and misunderstanding without reactive retaliation. (6) 'Ārjavam' — uprightness, directness: the alignment of thought, speech, and action — no duplicity, no agenda-driven manipulation. (7) 'Jñānam' — knowledge: learning, the study of scripture and the nature of reality — the theoretical dimension of wisdom. (8) 'Vijñānam' — wisdom: realised, experiential knowledge — the direct, living insight that transcends mere learning and becomes identity with the known (see 7.2 where jñāna and vijñāna are paired). (9) 'Āstikyam' — faith: the unwavering conviction in the reality of ātman, the binding power of karma, the authority of the Vedas, and the existence of liberation — the foundational epistemic commitment of the dharmic life.

18:43.text: Heroism (śaurya), radiant vitality (tejas), steadfast resolve (dhṛti), skill (dākṣya), not fleeing from battle, the quality of generosity (dāna), and the quality of sovereign governance (īśvara-bhāva) — these are the natural duties of a Kṣatriya.
18:43.transliteration:
śauryaṃ tejo dhṛtir dākṣyaṃ yuddhe cāpy apalāyanam |
dānam īśvara-bhāvaś ca kṣātraṃ karma svabhāvajam ||43||
18:43.devanagari:
शौर्यं तेजो धृतिर्दाक्ष्यं युद्धे चाप्यपलायनम् ।
दानमीश्वरभावश्च क्षात्रं कर्म स्वभावजम् ॥ ४३ ॥
18:43.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:43.explanation: Seven qualities that define Kṣatriya svabhāva-karma — the natural disposition of the warrior-ruler, predominantly rājasic in energy but elevated and directed by sāttvic purpose (the protection of dharma and the welfare of people): (1) 'Śauryam' — heroism, fearless courage: the capacity to face danger, opposition, and the prospect of death without retreat. (2) 'Tejaḥ' — radiant vitality, commanding presence: the personal aura that naturally inspires confidence in allies and wariness in adversaries — an innate quality of leadership. (3) 'Dhṛtiḥ' — steadfast resolve (the same dhṛti analysed in vv.33–35, here in its sāttvika-rājasic expression): holding one's purpose and command through adversity, setback, and the chaos of conflict. (4) 'Dākṣyam' — skill, expertise, efficiency: technical proficiency in the arts of governance, strategy, and combat — practical excellence. (5) 'Yuddhe cāpy apalāyanam' — not fleeing from battle: specifically relevant to Arjuna, who contemplated exactly this. Withdrawal from one's duty in the field of conflict is the supreme violation of kṣatriya-dharma. (6) 'Dānam' — generosity: the sovereign's duty to give — to sustain subjects, reward loyal service, and support the weak. (7) 'Īśvara-bhāvaḥ' — the quality of lordship, sovereign governance: the natural bearing of command and responsibility — not ego-based domination but the innate capacity and willingness to govern, lead, and take responsibility for the welfare of the collective.

18:44.text: Agriculture, cattle-rearing, and trade are the natural duties of a Vaiśya; and service-oriented action is also the natural duty of a Śūdra.
18:44.transliteration:
kṛṣi-go-rakṣya-vāṇijyaṃ vaiśya-karma svabhāvajam |
paricaryātmakaṃ karma śūdrasyāpi svabhāvajam ||44||
18:44.devanagari:
कृषिगौरक्ष्यवाणिज्यं वैश्यकर्म स्वभावजम् ।
परिचर्यात्मकं कर्म शूद्रस्यापि स्वभावजम् ॥ ४४ ॥
18:44.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:44.explanation: Vaiśya svabhāva-karma — three productive functions: (1) 'Kṛṣi' — agriculture, cultivation of the earth: producing food, the primary basis of all life and civilisation. The Vaiśya's relationship with the earth is productive and nurturing — making the land fertile and fruitful. (2) 'Go-rakṣya' — protection and rearing of cattle: in Vedic culture, the cow represents the entire complex of animal husbandry that sustains the human community — milk, ghee, fertiliser, labour. Cow-protection is the symbol of the Vaiśya's responsibility to the living wealth that sustains human life. (3) 'Vāṇijyam' — trade, commerce: the exchange of goods and services that makes the economy of a community flow. The Vaiśya's svabhāva includes the aptitude for production, preservation, and profitable exchange — oriented toward the material sustenance of society. Śūdra svabhāva-karma: 'paricaryātmakam karma' — action that is service-oriented (paricaryā: attendance, assisting, supporting), the natural disposition and skill of service: supporting and enabling the functioning of the other three varṇas. 'Api' — also, too: the word 'api' is deeply significant — Śūdra's karma is equally natural (svabhāvajam) to their constitution, equally valid, equally honourable in its own domain. The Gita makes no hierarchical judgment here — each varṇa's work is a legitimate expression of its svabhāva. The next verse will make the radical proclamation: through perfection in one's own duty, every person of every varṇa attains the highest perfection.

18:45.text: Engaged (with deep love and dedication) in one's own duty, a person attains the highest perfection. How one devoted to one's own duty finds this perfection — hear that from me.
18:45.transliteration:
sve sve karmaṇy abhirataḥ saṃsiddhiṃ labhate naraḥ |
sva-karma-nirataḥ siddhiṃ yathā vindati tac chṛṇu ||45||
18:45.devanagari:
स्वे स्वे कर्मण्यभिरतः संसिद्धिं लभते नरः ।
स्वकर्मनिरतः सिद्धिं यथा विन्दति तच्छृणु ॥ ४५ ॥
18:45.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:45.explanation: 'Sve sve karmaṇy abhirataḥ' — deeply engaged in one's own respective duty: 'sve sve' (one's own, each one's own — the repetition emphasising personal specificity) and 'abhirataḥ' (deeply engaged, absorbed, finding joy in — not merely compliant with obligation but lovingly immersed). This verse announces the great democratising principle of the Gita's teaching: the path to the highest perfection (saṃsiddhi) is open to everyone, through every legitimate form of duty. It is not the exclusive province of brāhmaṇas who have special access to rites, nor of kṣatriyas who command armies, nor of any single class. 'Saṃsiddhiṃ labhate naraḥ' — a person (naraḥ: any human being) attains the highest, complete perfection (saṃsiddhi: sam + siddhi — thorough, complete, perfect attainment). This is not a class-conditioned, limited success but the universal supreme attainment — mokṣa itself. 'Sva-karma-nirataḥ' — constant, unwavering in one's own duty: the key is not exceptional ability or extraordinary circumstance but sustained, wholehearted dedication to the duty that accords with one's own nature. 'Siddhiṃ yathā vindati' — how one finds this perfection — the next verse (46) will reveal the transforming element: the consecration of all action to the Supreme Being, which is what transforms ordinary svadharma into a path of liberation. This verse is the pivotal bridge between the social ethic (varṇa-dharma) and the spiritual teleology (mokṣa).

18:46.text: By worshipping through his own duty that Being from whom all creatures have their impulse and by whom all this is pervaded — a man attains perfection.
18:46.transliteration:
yataḥ pravṛttir bhūtānāṃ yena sarvam idaṃ tatam |
sva-karmaṇā tam abhyarcya siddhiṃ vindati mānavaḥ ||46||
18:46.devanagari:
यतः प्रवृत्तिर्भूतानां येन सर्वमिदं ततम् ।
स्वकर्मणा तमभ्यर्च्य सिद्धिं विन्दति मानवः ॥ ४६ ॥
18:46.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:46.explanation: This verse answers the question posed at the end of verse 45: 'how does one devoted to their own duty find perfection?' The answer is the key: 'tam abhyarcya' — by worshipping, honouring, consecrating to That. The transforming principle is the inner orientation of the action. 'Yataḥ pravṛttir bhūtānām' — from whom comes the very impulse, the motivating force, the life-energy of all beings. 'Yena sarvam idaṃ tatam' — by whom all of this is pervaded, suffused, permeated — the omnipresent Brahman in whom everything inheres. When the worker recognises that the Being who pervades all creation is also present in their own field of action — in the farm, in the court, in the workshop, in the sickroom — and consecrates every action as an act of worship toward that omnipresent Divine, then svadharma becomes sādhana, ordinary duty becomes sacred offering, and worldly perfection (siddhi) becomes liberation. This verse is the synthesis of karma-yoga's highest principle: not the abandonment of work but its transformation through consecration.

18:47.text: Better is one's own dharma, though imperfectly performed, than the dharma of another well-performed. Performing the duty ordained by one's own nature, one incurs no sin.
18:47.transliteration:
śreyān sva-dharmo viguṇaḥ para-dharmāt sv-anuṣṭhitāt |
svabhāva-niyataṃ karma kurvan nāpnoti kilbiṣam ||47||
18:47.devanagari:
श्रेयान्स्वधर्मो विगुणः परधर्मात्स्वनुष्ठितात् ।
स्वभावनियतं कर्म कुर्वन्नाप्नोति किल्बिषम् ॥ ४७ ॥
18:47.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:47.explanation: This verse repeats the foundational principle of svadharma already declared in 3.35, now enriched by the entire contextual understanding of guṇa, svabhāva, and consecrated action built through Chapter 18. 'Svadharmo viguṇaḥ' — one's own dharma even if lacking in excellence (viguṇa: without the full complement of qualities, imperfectly executed — for it is always harder to perfect one's own native duty than to mimic another's more easily mastered role). 'Para-dharmāt sv-anuṣṭhitāt śreyān' — superior to another's dharma perfectly performed: because authenticity in alignment with one's own nature has intrinsic value that superior performance of a borrowed role cannot replicate. The person singing their own melody imperfectly is more fully alive than one flawlessly performing another's song. 'Svabhāva-niyataṃ karma kurvan nāpnoti kilbiṣam' — performing action determined by one's own natural constitution, one does not incur sin (kilbiṣa): because action aligned with svabhāva, when offered as worship to the Divine, does not accrue karmic bondage. The sin is not in the nature of the action but in the misalignment between the actor's nature and the action performed, and in the ego-driven, self-seeking orientation of that action.

18:48.text: O Kaunteya, one should not abandon the duty born of one's own nature, even if it is flawed; for all undertakings are covered by imperfection as fire is covered by smoke.
18:48.transliteration:
sahajaṃ karma kaunteya sa-doṣam api na tyajet |
sarvārambhā hi doṣeṇa dhūmenāgnir ivāvṛtāḥ ||48||
18:48.devanagari:
सहजं कर्म कौन्तेय सदोषमपि न त्यजेत् ।
सर्वारम्भा हि दोषेण धूमेनाग्निरिवावृताः ॥ ४८ ॥
18:48.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:48.explanation: 'Sahajaṃ karma' — duty born of one's own nature (saha-ja: born with, innate, co-originating with one's svabhāva): the spontaneous, natural activity that one is constitutionally suited for. 'Sa-doṣam api na tyajet' — even with its attendant imperfection (doṣa), it should not be abandoned. Every act of svadharma has some inherent limitation or collateral imperfection: the farmer disturbs insects, the physician causes temporary pain, the judge inflicts punishment, the warrior takes life in defence of order. These are not moral failures — they are the inevitable textures of engagement with a complex world. 'Sarvārambhā hi doṣeṇa dhūmenāgnir ivāvṛtāḥ' — for all undertakings are enveloped in imperfection as fire is inevitably accompanied by smoke. This is one of the Gita's most consoling and realistic teachings: do not seek the impossible standard of perfect, consequence-free action before committing to your duty. Action without any limitation does not exist in the phenomenal world. The choice is not between imperfect action and perfect non-action — it is between the imperfection of engaged, consecrated svadharma and the far greater imperfection of inaction, avoidance, or pretence.

18:49.text: One whose intellect is unattached everywhere, whose self is conquered, from whom all longing has departed — through renunciation, that one attains the supreme perfection of non-action (naiṣkarmya).
18:49.transliteration:
asakta-buddhiḥ sarvatra jitātmā vigata-spṛhaḥ |
naiṣkarmya-siddhiṃ paramāṃ saṃnyāsenādhigacchati ||49||
18:49.devanagari:
असक्तबुद्धिः सर्वत्र जितात्मा विगतस्पृहः ।
नैष्कर्म्यसिद्धिं परमां संन्यासेनाधिगच्छति ॥ ४९ ॥
18:49.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:49.explanation: This verse describes the inner condition that enables the highest realisation — naiṣkarmya-siddhi, the perfection of actionlessness. 'Asakta-buddhiḥ sarvatra' — whose buddhi (discriminative intelligence) remains unattached everywhere: not merely in pleasant circumstances but universally, across all situations — attraction and aversion both dissolved. 'Jitātmā' — whose self is conquered (jita-ātmā: the lower self — the complex of mind, ego, and senses — brought under the sovereignty of wisdom): this is not suppression but genuine transmutation, the mind naturally resting in the Self rather than rushing after objects. 'Vigata-spṛhaḥ' — from whom all spṛhā (longing, the subtle ache of wanting) has departed: not merely gross desire but the subtlest residual pull toward any experience. 'Saṃnyāsenādhigacchati' — through renunciation one attains: here saṃnyāsa is the inner renunciation of all identification with the fruits and the doership of action — not necessarily the external form of the wandering monk. 'Naiṣkarmya-siddhi parāmā' — the supreme perfection of non-action: the state in which action continues (the sage acts, breathes, speaks) but no new karma is generated because there is no ego-actor claiming ownership of the action. This is the state of jīvan-mukti — liberation while living.

18:50.text: O Kaunteya, hear from me briefly how one who has attained perfection reaches Brahman — that is the highest culmination of knowledge.
18:50.transliteration:
siddhiṃ prāpto yathā brahma tathāpnoti nibodha me |
samāsenaiva kaunteya niṣṭhā jñānasya yā parā ||50||
18:50.devanagari:
सिद्धिं प्राप्तो यथा ब्रह्म तथाप्नोति निबोध मे ।
समासेनैव कौन्तेय निष्ठा ज्ञानस्य या परा ॥ ५० ॥
18:50.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:50.explanation: 'Siddhiṃ prāptaḥ' — one who has attained the perfection (siddhi) described in verse 49 — naiṣkarmya-siddhi, the inner non-action of the liberated actor. 'Yathā brahma tathāpnoti' — how such a one attains Brahman: this is a crucial progression. Verse 49 described the attainment of siddhi; verse 50 declares that beyond siddhi lies Brahman-realisation — the ultimate fruit that siddhi opens the door to. 'Niṣṭhā jñānasya yā parā' — that which is the supreme, highest culmination (parā niṣṭhā) of all knowledge (jñāna): not the perfection of any particular knowledge but the apex of knowing itself — the dissolution of the knower-known duality in the recognition of Brahman. 'Samāsenaiva' — in summary, briefly: the following verses (51–53) will describe the qualifying disciplines; verses 54–55 will describe the fruit. 'Nibodha me' — know this from me: the personal transmission from the teacher is emphasised. This transitional verse marks the shift from the social-practical dimension of the teaching (svadharma, varṇa, karma) to the ultimate metaphysical dimension: Brahman-realisation.

18:51.text: Endowed with pure intellect, firmly restraining the self through fortitude, abandoning sound and other objects of the senses, casting aside passion and aversion...
18:51.transliteration:
buddhyā viśuddhayā yukto dhṛtyātmānaṃ niyamya ca |
śabdādīn viṣayāṃs tyaktvā rāga-dveṣau vyudasya ca ||51||
18:51.devanagari:
बुद्ध्या विशुद्धया युक्तो धृत्यात्मानं नियम्य च ।
शब्दादीन्विषयांस्त्यक्त्वा रागद्वेषौ व्युदस्य च ॥ ५१ ॥
18:51.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:51.explanation: Verse 51, 52, and 53 form a single grammatical unit — a sustained list of the inner disciplines that constitute the immediate preparation for Brahman-realisation. 'Buddhyā viśuddhayā yuktaḥ' — endowed with purified buddhi: the discriminative intellect cleansed of all distorting impurities (desire, aversion, ego-identification), like a perfectly polished mirror that reflects reality without distortion. 'Dhṛtyātmānaṃ niyamya ca' — restraining the self (the entire psycho-physical complex: mind, senses, ego) through dhṛti (steady, unwavering resolve — the sāttvikī dhṛti described in 18.33): not by suppression but by the sustained power of wisdom-grounded intention. 'Śabdādīn viṣayāṃs tyaktvā' — having abandoned the sense objects beginning with sound (the five viṣayas corresponding to the five sense organs): not a blanket sensory deprivation but the voluntary, wisdom-motivated withdrawal of investment in sensory gratification as a source of fulfilment. 'Rāga-dveṣau vyudasya ca' — having thoroughly cast aside (vy-ud-asya: pushed completely away) both rāga (attraction-attachment) and dveṣa (repulsion-aversion): the two great distorting forces that colour all experience with personal bias. The verse deliberately breaks mid-sentence, continuing into verses 52–53.

18:52.text: Resorting to solitude, eating lightly, with speech, body, and mind restrained, ever devoted to dhyāna-yoga, established in dispassion...
18:52.transliteration:
vivikta-sevī laghv-āśī yata-vāk-kāya-mānasaḥ |
dhyāna-yoga-paro nityaṃ vairāgyaṃ samupāśritaḥ ||52||
18:52.devanagari:
विविक्तसेवी लघ्वाशी यतवाक्कायमानसः ।
ध्यानयोगपरो नित्यं वैराग्यं समुपाश्रितः ॥ ५२ ॥
18:52.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:52.explanation: The list of qualifications continues. 'Vivikta-sevī' — one who resorts to solitude (vivikta: isolated, clear, separated from distraction): the deliberate cultivation of silence and aloneness — necessary not as social withdrawal but as the space in which the mind can settle and the Self become apparent. 'Laghv-āśī' — eating lightly (laghu: light; āśī: one who eats): the moderate, careful diet that keeps the body and mind alert and free from the dullness that comes from overfeeding (tamasic) or the agitation of excessive stimulants (rājasic). 'Yata-vāk-kāya-mānasaḥ' — with speech, body, and mind all controlled (yata: restrained, disciplined): this is the three-fold tapas described in 17.14–16 — physical, verbal, and mental austerity practised as a unified discipline. 'Dhyāna-yoga-paraḥ nityam' — perpetually absorbed in dhyāna-yoga (the yoga of meditative union): meditation not as an occasional practice but as the continuous orientation of consciousness. 'Vairāgyaṃ samupāśritaḥ' — thoroughly grounded in vairāgya (dispassion, the non-attraction to sense objects): not by force but by the natural withdrawal of interest that comes from having tasted something far more satisfying than sense pleasure.

18:53.text: Having relinquished egoism, force, arrogance, desire, anger, and possessiveness — free from the sense of 'mine', at peace — one becomes fit for the realisation of Brahman.
18:53.transliteration:
ahaṃkāraṃ balaṃ darpaṃ kāmaṃ krodhaṃ parigraham |
vimucya nirmamaḥ śānto brahma-bhūyāya kalpate ||53||
18:53.devanagari:
अहंकारं बलं दर्पं कामं क्रोधं परिग्रहम् ।
विमुच्य निर्ममः शान्तो ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते ॥ ५३ ॥
18:53.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:53.explanation: The three-verse qualification list culminates here with the six inner relinquishments and the declaration of their fruit. The six to be released: (1) 'Ahaṃkāram' — egoism, the sense of being a separate, self-contained 'I', the fundamental error of individual identity taken as absolute; (2) 'Balam' — the pride of power/strength (the ego inflated by capacity — physical, intellectual, social); (3) 'Darpam' — arrogance, insolence, the dismissive self-assertion born of ego-pride; (4) 'Kāmam' — desire, the deep pull of wanting, the root of all agitation; (5) 'Krodham' — anger, the reactive force that arises when desire is obstructed; (6) 'Parigraham' — possessiveness, the compulsive accumulation and clinging ownership of things, people, and positions. These six are the comprehensive list of ego's primary expressions. 'Vimucya' — having thoroughly released (not suppressed) all six. 'Nirmamaḥ' — free from the sense of 'mine' (nir-mama: without mine-ness, without possessive identification). 'Śāntaḥ' — at peace, tranquil, the deep settled stillness that follows genuine release. 'Brahma-bhūyāya kalpate' — becomes fit (kalpate) for brahma-bhūya (becoming Brahman, the realisation of identity with Brahman): not merely approaching Brahman but literally becoming it — the dissolution of the false separate identity into the ocean of pure Consciousness.

18:54.text: Having become Brahman, with a serene self, one neither grieves nor desires. Equal toward all beings, one attains the highest devotion to me.
18:54.transliteration:
brahma-bhūtaḥ prasannātmā na śocati na kāṅkṣati |
samaḥ sarveṣu bhūteṣu mad-bhaktiṃ labhate parām ||54||
18:54.devanagari:
ब्रह्मभूतः प्रसन्नात्मा न शोचति न काङ्क्षति ।
समः सर्वेषु भूतेषु मद्भक्तिं लभते पराम् ॥ ५४ ॥
18:54.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:54.explanation: This is one of the most luminous verses in the entire Gita — the description of the realised state and its spontaneous fruit. 'Brahma-bhūtaḥ' — having become Brahman: not 'having understood Brahman' or 'having approached Brahman' but having become it — the full Vedāntic realisation of identity, the dissolution of the separate-self illusion. This is jīvan-mukti: liberation in the living body. 'Prasannātmā' — with a serene, luminous Self (prasanna: clear, bright, expanded — literally 'spread out', the opposite of contracted): the natural joy of the Self freed from its constriction in ego-identity, now resting in its own boundless, spontaneous luminosity. 'Na śocati na kāṅkṣati' — neither grieves nor desires: the two definitive symptoms of liberation. Grief arises from the loss of what we thought was 'mine'; desire arises from the lack of what we think we need. In brahma-bhūya, the ego's ownership and lack-sense are dissolved — nothing can be lost that belongs to the Self, and the Self lacks nothing. 'Samaḥ sarveṣu bhūteṣu' — equal in all beings: because the realised one sees only Brahman everywhere — in friend and enemy, in human and animal, in the celebrated and the despised. 'Mad-bhaktiṃ labhate parām' — attains the highest devotion to me: this is the Gita's most radical integration — Brahman-knowledge (jñāna) does not replace devotion (bhakti); rather, realisation of Brahman naturally flowers into the purest, highest, most transparent devotion to the personal Divine. The knower of Brahman and the supreme devotee are one.

18:55.text: Through devotion one truly knows me — who I am and what my extent is. Then, having known me in truth, one enters into me immediately thereafter.
18:55.transliteration:
bhaktyā mām abhijānāti yāvān yaś cāsmi tattvataḥ |
tato māṃ tattvato jñātvā viśate tad-anantaram ||55||
18:55.devanagari:
भक्त्या मामभिजानाति यावान्यश्चास्मि तत्त्वतः ।
ततो मां तत्त्वतो ज्ञात्वा विशते तदनन्तरम् ॥ ५५ ॥
18:55.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:55.explanation: 'Bhaktyā mām abhijānāti yāvān yaś cāsmi tattvataḥ' — through bhakti alone one truly, essentially, completely knows me (abhijānāti: to know thoroughly, to know directly from within). 'Yāvān' — my full extent, the boundlessness of my being (reference to the cosmic and transcendent dimensions of the Divine revealed in Ch 10–11). 'Yaś ca asmi' — and who I am, what is my essential nature (the personal, intimate reality of the Divine that is deeper than any philosophical formulation). 'Tattvatah' — in truth, in essence, as I actually am: not a conceptual approximation but the direct, living recognition. And the revolutionary claim: this knowing is possible only 'bhaktyā' — through devotion. Not through intellectual analysis alone, not through debate, not through mere austerity — the most intimate knowing of the Divine is through love. 'Tataḥ māṃ tattvato jñātvā viśate tad-anantaram' — then, having thus known me in truth, one enters into me immediately, without delay (tad-anantaram: right after that, at that very moment). 'Viśate' — enters: the language of entering-into is the language of love's ultimate union — the lover entering the Beloved, the wave returning to the ocean, the separate self dissolving into the universal Self. Knowledge (jñāna) and devotion (bhakti) here achieve their supreme integration: the knowledge that only love enables, and the love that knowledge has purified and perfected.

18:56.text: One who takes refuge in me — even while performing all actions always — through my grace attains the eternal, imperishable abode.
18:56.transliteration:
sarva-karmāṇy api sadā kurvāṇo mad-vyapāśrayaḥ |
mat-prasādād avāpnoti śāśvataṃ padam avyayam ||56||
18:56.devanagari:
सर्वकर्माण्यपि सदा कुर्वाणो मद्व्यपाश्रयः ।
मत्प्रसादादवाप्नोति शाश्वतं पदमव्ययम् ॥ ५६ ॥
18:56.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:56.explanation: 'Sarva-karmāṇy api sadā kurvāṇaḥ' — even while always performing all actions: the emphatic 'api' (even) and 'sadā' (always) together make the most liberating declaration in the Gita's practical teaching. Liberation does not require the cessation of action. Full worldly engagement — the farm, the court, the battlefield, the household — is fully compatible with the highest spiritual attainment when the inner orientation is right. 'Mad-vyapāśrayaḥ' — taking complete refuge in me (vi-apa-āśraya: thorough, comprehensive shelter): not a partial or occasional turning toward the Divine but a wholehearted, central, organising orientation of one's entire life around the Divine reality — Īśvara as the ground, centre, and summit of all activity. 'Mat-prasādāt' — through my grace: this is the Gita's most tender and most important admission. The final attainment is not the mechanical outcome of sufficient spiritual effort — it is received as grace (prasāda: the Lord's gift of self-revelation, the bestowal of liberation). The practitioner's effort purifies the instrument and orients it toward the Divine; but the final opening is grace. 'Śāśvataṃ padam avyayam' — the eternal (śāśvatam: always existing, without temporal beginning or end), imperishable (avyayam: without diminution or decay) abode — the state of mokṣa, the realisation of the changeless, deathless Ātman-Brahman.

18:57.text: Mentally surrendering all actions to me, regarding me as the supreme goal, taking refuge in the yoga of the intellect — keep your consciousness fixed in me always.
18:57.transliteration:
cetasā sarva-karmāṇi mayi saṃnyasya mat-paraḥ |
buddhi-yogam upāśritya mac-cittaḥ satataṃ bhava ||57||
18:57.devanagari:
चेतसा सर्वकर्माणि मयि संन्यस्य मत्परः ।
बुद्धियोगमुपाश्रित्य मच्चित्तः सततं भव ॥ ५७ ॥
18:57.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:57.explanation: 'Cetasā sarva-karmāṇi mayi saṃnyasya' — mentally surrendering (saṃnyasya: laying down, consigning, releasing) all actions into me: this is not the external abandonment of activity but the inner relinquishment of ownership. Every action is offered as it arises — not held as 'mine', not claimed as the product of personal agency, but released into the Divine as a flower is placed on an altar. 'Mat-paraḥ' — taking me as the supreme (para: highest, ultimate): orienting the entire life-compass toward the Divine — every decision, every relationship, every aspiration measured by the single question: does this bring me closer to the Supreme? 'Buddhi-yogam upāśritya' — taking refuge in buddhi-yoga (the yoga of discriminative intelligence aligned with the Divine — the integration of all faculties — mind, intellect, will — in conscious service of spiritual wisdom): this ensures the surrender is not emotional collapse but wisdom-rooted conscious offering. 'Mac-cittaḥ satataṃ bhava' — be always (satatam: uninterruptedly, perpetually) of me-absorbed consciousness (mac-citta: citta established in me): the continuous, unbroken practice of Divine remembrance that the Gita prescribes as the most direct and complete path. 'Satatam' admits no holiday — the transformation of consciousness is total and continuous, not periodic.

18:58.text: Being of mind fixed in me, you will cross over all difficulties by my grace. But if, out of ego, you do not listen — you will perish.
18:58.transliteration:
mac-cittaḥ sarva-durgāṇi mat-prasādāt tariṣyasi |
atha cet tvam ahaṃkārān na śroṣyasi vinaṅkṣyasi ||58||
18:58.devanagari:
मच्चित्तः सर्वदुर्गाणि मत्प्रसादात्तरिष्यसि ।
अथ चेत्त्वमहंकारान्न श्रोष्यसि विनङ्क्ष्यसि ॥ ५८ ॥
18:58.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:58.explanation: 'Mac-cittaḥ sarva-durgāṇi mat-prasādāt tariṣyasi' — being of Divine-absorbed consciousness, you will cross over all durgāṇi (difficulties, impassable places — the word durga means literally 'difficult to pass through', the same root as the name of the Goddess Durgā): all the hard terrain of life — physical danger, emotional devastation, spiritual dryness, material adversity — by my grace (mat-prasādat). The promise is total: not 'some difficulties' but 'all difficulties'. And the mechanism is not personal strength or strategy but Divine grace operating through the surrendered, God-absorbed consciousness. 'Atha cet tvam ahaṃkārān na śroṣyasi' — but if out of ahaṃkāra (ego, the insistence on being the self-sufficient agent) you do not listen (na śroṣyasi: refuse to hear, to receive, to follow this teaching): here is the other side of the choice. Ahaṃkāra is precisely the faculty that makes a being deaf to wisdom — it insists that it already knows, that it can manage alone, that it does not need to surrender. 'Vinaṅkṣyasi' — you will perish, be destroyed: not a divine punishment but the natural consequence of ego-driven action in a world that demands humility and wisdom. This verse brings the entire Gita full circle — to Arjuna's opening refusal to fight, which was ego-confusion dressed as compassion.

18:59.text: If, taking shelter in ego, you think 'I will not fight' — this resolve of yours is false; your own nature will compel you.
18:59.transliteration:
yad ahaṃkāram āśritya na yotsya iti manyase |
mithyaiṣa vyavasāyas te prakṛtis tvāṃ niyokṣyati ||59||
18:59.devanagari:
यदहंकारमाश्रित्य न योत्स्य इति मन्यसे ।
मिथ्यैष व्यवसायस्ते प्रकृतिस्त्वां नियोक्ष्यति ॥ ५९ ॥
18:59.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:59.explanation: 'Yad ahaṃkāram āśritya na yotsya iti manyase' — if, taking refuge in ahaṃkāra (the ego's presumed authority over the situation), you think 'I will not fight': Krishna here diagnoses the final form of Arjuna's resistance as ego-based. The apparent compassion and high-mindedness of Arjuna's refusal in Chapter 1 is, when seen clearly, another form of ahaṃkāra — the ego's insistence on being the sovereign decider, the one who will choose 'not to fight' on its own authority. 'Mithyaiṣa vyavasāyas te' — this resolve of yours is false, illusory, ineffectual (mithyā: unreal, based on false premises): because it is not founded in wisdom but in ego-confusion. 'Prakṛtis tvāṃ niyokṣyati' — your own Prakṛti (your svabhāva, your innate natural constitution as a kṣatriya warrior, shaped by all the guṇas of your accumulated karma) will compel you, will deploy you (niyokṣyati: will put to work, appoint to duty). The teaching is profound: one cannot permanently defy one's own nature through an ego-driven decision. The river of svabhāva will eventually overcome the dam of ego-will. The only escape is the higher freedom of consciously aligning with svadharma through wisdom and Divine surrender — not the pseudo-freedom of ego-driven refusal.

18:60.text: O Kaunteya, bound by your own action born of your own nature — what you do not wish to do out of delusion — you will do even against your will.
18:60.transliteration:
svabhāva-jena kaunteya nibaddhaḥ svena karmaṇā |
kartuṃ necchasi yan mohāt kariṣyasy avaśo 'pi tat ||60||
18:60.devanagari:
स्वभावजेन कौन्तेय निबद्धः स्वेन कर्मणा ।
कर्तुं नेच्छसि यन्मोहात्करिष्यस्यवशोऽपि तत् ॥ ६० ॥
18:60.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:60.explanation: 'Svabhāva-jena svena karmaṇā nibaddhaḥ' — bound by one's own action born of one's own svabhāva: the web of guṇa-conditioned karma and innate tendency in which every embodied being finds itself. This is not a moral judgement but a metaphysical description: the individual's svabhāva (natural constitution accumulated over lifetimes) creates a field of gravitational pull that determines what that person will naturally, inevitably do. 'Yat mohāt kartuṃ necchasi' — what you wish not to do out of moha (delusion, confusion, the fog of grief and misidentification): the emphasis on 'moha' is crucial — Arjuna's refusal is not wisdom but confusion. A genuinely wisdom-born renunciation of violence would be different (as in genuine ahiṃsā practised as dharma). But refusal born of moha is simply avoidance. 'Kariṣyasy avaśo 'pi tat' — you will do it even involuntarily, even helplessly (avaśaḥ: without the capacity to resist, compelled): because one cannot indefinitely resist one's own deepest natural disposition. The choice is only between two modes of doing: either freely, consciously, wisely, as a spiritual act of dedicated svadharma — or helplessly, driven by nature, unconsciously, as a mechanical function of conditioning. The former is liberation; the latter is deeper bondage.

18:61.text: O Arjuna, the Lord dwells in the hearts of all beings, causing all beings to revolve — mounted upon a machine (the body) — by the power of Māyā.
18:61.transliteration:
īśvaraḥ sarva-bhūtānāṃ hṛd-deśe 'rjuna tiṣṭhati |
bhrāmayan sarva-bhūtāni yantrārūḍhāni māyayā ||61||
18:61.devanagari:
ईश्वरः सर्वभूतानां हृद्देशेऽर्जुन तिष्ठति ।
भ्रामयन्सर्वभूतानि यन्त्रारूढानि मायया ॥ ६१ ॥
18:61.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:61.explanation: One of the most philosophically rich and poetically vivid verses in the entire Gita. 'Īśvaraḥ sarva-bhūtānāṃ hṛd-deśe tiṣṭhati' — the Lord stands (tiṣṭhati: is stationed, abides permanently) in the heart-space (hṛd-deśa: the region of the heart, the cave of the heart, the deepest interior) of all beings without exception. This is the doctrine of the antaryāmin (inner controller) — the Lord not as a remote deity but as the most intimate, ever-present witness at the very core of every living being, closer than thought. 'Yantrārūḍhāni' — mounted upon the machine (the body-mind complex as yantra: a mechanical instrument, a device): the jīva (individual being) sits atop and within this exquisitely complex instrument of Prakṛti — the physical body, sense organs, mind, ego — all of which function as an interconnected machine. 'Bhrāmayan sarva-bhūtāni māyayā' — causing all beings to revolve (bhrāmayan: spinning, rotating, moving in patterns) through Māyā: the image is of a potter's wheel (or a great merry-go-round) — the Lord at the centre, Māyā as the spinning force, and all beings revolving within it. The 'revolving' is saṃsāra itself — the cycle of birth, experience, death, rebirth. This verse is the ultimate argument for surrender: if the Lord is the real operator of all this machinery, ego's claim of autonomous authorship is the most fundamental delusion.

18:62.text: O Bhārata, take refuge in that very Being with your entire existence. By his grace you will attain supreme peace and the eternal abode.
18:62.transliteration:
tam eva śaraṇaṃ gaccha sarva-bhāvena bhārata |
tat-prasādāt parāṃ śāntiṃ sthānaṃ prāpsyasi śāśvatam ||62||
18:62.devanagari:
तमेव शरणं गच्छ सर्वभावेन भारत ।
तत्प्रसादात्परां शान्तिं स्थानं प्राप्स्यसि शाश्वतम् ॥ ६२ ॥
18:62.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:62.explanation: The natural, inevitable conclusion that flows from verse 61: 'Tam eva śaraṇaṃ gaccha' — go to that very One as your shelter (śaraṇam: refuge, protection, sanctuary): the Lord who dwells in the heart, who operates all existence through Māyā, who is both the most intimate and the most all-encompassing — to that One alone go for shelter. 'Sarva-bhāvena' — with your entire being (sarva-bhāva: all modes of existence — mind, intellect, heart, vital breath, body, all relationships and roles): this is total surrender, not partial. Hold nothing back. This 'sarva-bhāvena' mirrors the same phrase used in bhakti's culmination (9.34): worship with your entire being. 'Tat-prasādāt' — by his grace (once more the emphasis on prasāda: the Divine's freely given self-disclosure, the grace that the surrendered heart makes itself available to receive): the fruit is not earned but received. 'Parāṃ śāntim' — the supreme peace (parā śānti: the peace that surpasses all understanding — the perfect stillness of the Self established in Brahman, beyond all perturbation). 'Sthānaṃ prāpsyasi śāśvatam' — you will attain the eternal abode: the permanent, non-revisable state of mokṣa. This verse is the practical summary of the entire Gita in two lines: surrender completely to the Divine who dwells within you, and receive supreme peace and eternal freedom.

18:63.text: Thus this knowledge more secret than all secrets has been declared to you by me. Having reflected on it fully and completely — act as you wish.
18:63.transliteration:
iti te jñānam ākhyātaṃ guhyād guhyataraṃ mayā |
vimṛśyaitad aśeṣeṇa yathecchasi tathā kuru ||63||
18:63.devanagari:
इति ते ज्ञानमाख्यातं गुह्याद्गुह्यतरं मया ।
विमृश्यैतदशेषेण यथेच्छसि तथा कुरु ॥ ६३ ॥
18:63.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:63.explanation: 'Guhyād guhyataram' — more secret than the secret, deeper than deep: each successive teaching of the Gita has been declared more guhya (secret/intimate) than what came before. Now, having disclosed the most intimate truth — that the Lord dwells in every heart, that complete surrender brings supreme peace — Krishna calls this the most private, most personal, most interior teaching of all: the direct transmission between teacher and disciple that transcends public doctrine. 'Vimṛśyaitad aśeṣeṇa' — having reflected on this fully, without remainder (vi-mṛśya: examine, consider, turn over in the mind; aśeṣeṇa: completely, exhaustively, leaving nothing out): Krishna does not demand blind acceptance. He demands something rarer and more dignified: complete, thorough, honest reflection. Every word is to be examined, challenged, tested against experience, held up to the light of one's own intelligence. 'Yathecchasi tathā kuru' — do as you wish: this is one of the most extraordinary statements a teacher can make — and it reveals the true nature of the Gita's God. After eighteen chapters of the most comprehensive wisdom ever delivered, the final instruction is: you are free. Reflect, decide, choose. The Divine respect for human freedom is absolute. This is not indifference — it is the highest form of love, which never compels.

18:64.text: Hear once more my supreme word — the most secret of all. Because you are deeply beloved to me, therefore I shall speak what is beneficial to you.
18:64.transliteration:
sarva-guhyatamaṃ bhūyaḥ śṛṇu me paramaṃ vacaḥ |
iṣṭo 'si me dṛḍham iti tato vakṣyāmi te hitam ||64||
18:64.devanagari:
सर्वगुह्यतमं भूयः श्रृणु मे परमं वचः ।
इष्टोऽसि मे दृढमिति ततो वक्ष्यामि ते हितम् ॥ ६४ ॥
18:64.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:64.explanation: After the apparent conclusion at verse 63 ('act as you wish'), this verse opens an unexpected, intimate, final layer — and it does so through love. 'Sarva-guhyatamaṃ' — the most secret of all (the superlative now extends to 'sarva': of everything): what follows surpasses every previous teaching in intimacy and directness. 'Bhūyaḥ śṛṇu' — hear once more (bhūyaḥ: again, additionally): the listener is invited back, drawn closer. 'Me paramaṃ vacaḥ' — my supreme, ultimate word. 'Iṣṭo 'si me dṛḍham iti' — 'you are deeply, firmly beloved to me (iṣṭa: loved, cherished; dṛḍham: firmly, strongly, without wavering)': this is the most astonishing moment in the entire Gita. Having taken the role of cosmic sovereign, metaphysical teacher, and dharmic instructor throughout, Krishna here drops every other role and speaks as pure love — as a friend who deeply loves this person and precisely because of that love cannot hold anything back. The acknowledgement 'you are my beloved' is addressed to Arjuna — and through Arjuna, to every sincere seeker who has engaged with this teaching. 'Tato vakṣyāmi te hitam' — therefore I shall tell you what is for your good (hita: welfare, benefit, what is truly good for you): the motivation of love is to serve genuine wellbeing, not to flatter or please.

18:65.text: Fix your mind on me, be devoted to me, worship me, bow to me — you will come to me alone. I truly promise you — for you are dear to me.
18:65.transliteration:
man-manā bhava mad-bhakto mad-yājī māṃ namaskuru |
mām evaiṣyasi satyaṃ te pratijāne priyo 'si me ||65||
18:65.devanagari:
मन्मना भव मद्भक्तो मद्याजी मां नमस्कुरु ।
मामेवैष्यसि सत्यं ते प्रतिजाने प्रियोऽसि मे ॥ ६५ ॥
18:65.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:65.explanation: This is arguably the most beloved, most recited verse in the entire Gita — the Divine's four-fold instruction in its simplest, most direct and most personal form: (1) 'Man-manā bhava' — be one whose mind is in me (man-manā: having me-absorbed mind, keeping me as the continuous content of mental awareness): the practice of Divine remembrance that is the foundation of all bhakti. (2) 'Mad-bhaktaḥ' — be my devotee, be devoted to me (mad-bhakta: one characterised by devotion to me): not just the action of worship but the inner orientation of love. (3) 'Mad-yājī' — be one who offers yajña to me (worships, serves, dedicates all action as sacred offering). (4) 'Māṃ namaskuru' — bow to me (namaskuru: offer prostration, express humble surrender): the gesture of ego's yielding to the Divine. These four together constitute the complete practice of bhakti-yoga in its most distilled form. 'Mām evaiṣyasi' — you will come to me alone (eva: certainly, only): the absolute assurance of the outcome. 'Satyaṃ te pratijāne' — I truly promise you (satya: truth; pratijāne: I declare as a promise/vow): an extraordinary moment — God takes a vow, speaks a personal pledge. This divine promise is grounded in the personal relationship: 'priyo 'si me' — for you are dear to me. The cosmos's architect declares personal love. This verse is a mirror of 9.34 but here enriched by the intimate personal confession of love that began in verse 64.

18:66.text: Abandoning all dharmas, take refuge in me alone. I shall liberate you from all sins — do not grieve.
18:66.transliteration:
sarva-dharmān parityajya mām ekaṃ śaraṇaṃ vraja |
ahaṃ tvā sarva-pāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ ||66||
18:66.devanagari:
सर्वधर्मान्परित्यज्य मामेकं शरणं व्रज ।
अहं त्वा सर्वपापेभ्यो मोक्षयिष्यामि मा शुचः ॥ ६६ ॥
18:66.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:66.explanation: This verse — universally known as the carama-śloka (the final, ultimate verse) of the Gita — is the most celebrated, most commented upon, most memorised, and most theologically significant single verse in the entire text. It is called the 'gist of the Gita' by the tradition. 'Sarva-dharmān parityajya' — abandoning all dharmas: 'dharma' here carries its broadest possible meaning — not merely religious duties or moral obligations but all supports, all means, all props that the individual ego relies upon for its sense of security and righteousness: personal merit, ritual performance, yogic practice, scriptural compliance, social duty — everything that the separate self might hold up as its claim on liberation. All of it: release. 'Mām ekaṃ śaraṇaṃ vraja' — take refuge in me alone (ekaṃ: one alone, the single, the non-dual): this is the ultimate act of surrender — releasing every other support, every other identity, every other anchor, and falling entirely into the Divine. 'Ahaṃ tvā sarva-pāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi' — I shall liberate you from all sins (sarva-pāpebhyaḥ: from every sin, every wrong, every karmic residue — without exception): the unconditional promise of the Divine. Not 'I shall reduce your sins' or 'I shall forgive some sins' — but total liberation. 'Mā śucaḥ' — do not grieve, do not sorrow: the Gita began with Arjuna in viṣāda (grief) in Chapter 1. It ends with the Divine's direct command: 'grieve no more.' The circle is complete. This verse is the Gita's heart in two lines.

18:67.text: This is never to be told to one without austerity, nor to one without devotion, nor to one unwilling to listen and serve, nor to one who finds fault with me.
18:67.transliteration:
idaṃ te nātapaskāya nābhaktāya kadācana |
na cāśuśrūṣave vācyaṃ na ca māṃ yo 'bhyasūyati ||67||
18:67.devanagari:
इदं ते नातपस्काय नाभक्ताय कदाचन ।
न चाशुश्रूषवे वाच्यं न च मां योऽभ्यसूयति ॥ ६७ ॥
18:67.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:67.explanation: 'Idaṃ te nātapaskāya' — this is not to be given to one without tapas (austerity, discipline, the purificatory practice that makes the inner instrument capable of receiving and holding deep wisdom): without the purifying fire of tapas, the most refined knowledge slips through an unprepared instrument like water through sand. 'Nābhaktāya' — not to one without bhakti (devotion, love, the heart's orientation toward the Divine): without bhakti, this knowledge becomes mere intellectual exercise — the head absorbs the words while the heart remains unmoved and the life unchanged. 'Na cāśuśrūṣave' — not to one without śuśrūṣā (the eager desire to listen, learn, and serve — the student's spirit of humble, active receptivity toward the teacher and the teaching): without śuśrūṣā, the teaching is heard but not received. 'Na ca māṃ yo 'bhyasūyati' — and not to one who finds fault with me, who is resentful of the Divine, who approaches the teaching with an attitude of hostility, cynicism, or contempt: such an attitude creates a closed circuit in which no wisdom can flow. These four disqualifications are not a rigid gatekeeping exercise but a description of the inner conditions without which this teaching cannot fulfil its transformative function. The responsibility of the transmitter of wisdom is discernment (viveka) in choosing the occasion and recipient.

18:68.text: One who will declare this supreme secret among my devotees — having performed the highest devotion to me — shall come to me alone, without doubt.
18:68.transliteration:
ya imaṃ paramaṃ guhyaṃ mad-bhakteṣv abhidhāsyati |
bhaktiṃ mayi parāṃ kṛtvā mām evaiṣyaty asaṃśayaḥ ||68||
18:68.devanagari:
य इमं परमं गुह्यं मद्भक्तेष्वभिधास्यति ।
भक्तिं मयि परां कृत्वा मामेवैष्यत्यसंशयः ॥ ६८ ॥
18:68.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:68.explanation: The complement to verse 67: having named those to whom the teaching must not be given, Krishna now names the fruit for those who give it to the right recipients. 'Ya imaṃ paramaṃ guhyaṃ mad-bhakteṣv abhidhāsyati' — one who will declare (abhidhāsyati: speak forth, transmit, share) this supreme secret among my devotees (mad-bhakteṣu: among those who are devoted to me — those who possess the four qualities that make the teaching receivable: tapas, bhakti, śuśrūṣā, and absence of fault-finding). 'Bhaktiṃ mayi parāṃ kṛtvā' — having performed the highest devotion to me: the transmitter must themselves be established in parā bhakti — they teach not from intellectual knowledge alone but from the lived reality of their own devotion and realisation. The most effective teacher of liberation-wisdom is one who is in the process of being liberated.
