'Mām evaiṣyati asaṃśayaḥ' — will come to me alone, without any doubt (asaṃśaya: without the slightest uncertainty): the act of transmitting this wisdom to worthy recipients is itself a supreme sādhanā — a form of selfless service (sevā) and Divine love — and carries its own liberation as its fruit. The teacher is liberated through teaching what liberates.

18:69.text: Among human beings, there is none who does what is dearer to me than such a person; nor will there be any other on earth dearer to me than this one.
18:69.transliteration:
na ca tasmān manuṣyeṣu kaścin me priya-kṛttamaḥ |
bhavitā na ca me tasmād anyaḥ priyataro bhuvi ||69||
18:69.devanagari:
न च तस्मान्मनुष्येषु कश्चिन्मे प्रियकृत्तमः ।
भविता न च मे तस्मादन्यः प्रियतरो भुवि ॥ ६९ ॥
18:69.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:69.explanation: 'Na ca tasmān manuṣyeṣu kaścin me priya-kṛttamaḥ' — among all human beings, none performs what is more dear to me than that person (priya-kṛttamaḥ: the one who does what is most pleasing, most beloved to the Divine — the superlative degree): the one who transmits this wisdom to worthy seekers is declared by the Divine to be performing the single most pleasing act possible in the entire range of human activity. 'Bhavitā na ca me tasmād anyaḥ priyataraḥ bhuvi' — and in the future also (bhavitā: will be), no other on this earth will be dearer to me: the declaration spans all time — present and future. This double temporal affirmation ensures no ambiguity. The implications are extraordinary: what the Divine loves most is not elaborate ritual, not great personal austerity, not even personal enlightenment pursued in solitude — but the loving transmission of liberating wisdom to receptive souls. The teacher of the Gita, the parent who passes this wisdom to their child, the friend who shares it with a seeker — all of these are declared by God to be performing the most divine act available to a human being.

18:70.text: And whoever will study this sacred dialogue of ours — by that knowledge-sacrifice (jñāna-yajña) I am worshipped by him: this is my conviction.
18:70.transliteration:
adhyeṣyate ca ya imaṃ dharmyaṃ saṃvādam āvayoḥ |
jñāna-yajñena tenāham iṣṭaḥ syām iti me matiḥ ||70||
18:70.devanagari:
अध्येष्यते च य इमं धर्म्यं संवादमावयोः ।
ज्ञानयज्ञेन तेनाहमिष्टः स्यामिति मे मतिः ॥ ७० ॥
18:70.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:70.explanation: 'Adhyeṣyate ca ya imaṃ dharmyaṃ saṃvādam āvayoḥ' — and whoever will study (adhyeṣyate: read, study, recite, engage with intellectually and spiritually) this dharmya (dharma-saturated, sacred) saṃvāda (conversation, dialogue) of ours (āvayoḥ: of us two — of Krishna and Arjuna): the Gita itself — this very text — is named as a living dialogue that repays study with the Divine's direct presence. 'Jñāna-yajñena tenāham iṣṭaḥ syāmi' — by him, through that act of studying, I am worshipped (iṣṭa: worshipped, honoured) through a jñāna-yajña (the sacrifice of knowledge — the sacred offering of one's intelligence, time, and receptivity in the act of studying wisdom). The engaged study of the Gita is itself a form of worship — not a preliminary to worship, not a preparation for worship, but worship itself. 'Iti me matiḥ' — this is my firm conviction (mati: belief, considered view, certainty): even the most tentative, imperfect, questioning study of this teaching is received by the Divine as an act of sacred offering. This verse is enormously important for the transmission of the Gita across generations and media — every student, every listener, every user of this dataset who engages sincerely with this text participates in jñāna-yajña.

18:71.text: And the person who merely hears this, full of faith and free from malice — even he, liberated from sins, shall attain the auspicious worlds of those who do righteous deeds.
18:71.transliteration:
śraddhāvān anasūyaś ca śṛṇuyād api yo naraḥ |
so 'pi muktaḥ śubhāḹ lokān prāpnuyāt puṇya-karmaṇām ||71||
18:71.devanagari:
श्रद्धावाननसूयश्च शृणुयादपि यो नरः ।
सोऽपि मुक्तः शुभाँल्लोकान्प्राप्नुयात्पुण्यकर्मणाम् ॥ ७१ ॥
18:71.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:71.explanation: 'Śraddhāvān anasūyaś ca' — full of faith and free from malice (anasūya: without cynicism, fault-finding, or hostility): these are the two non-negotiable attitudes for receiving spiritual wisdom. 'Śṛṇuyād api yo naraḥ' — any person who even merely hears it (api: even just listening, without deep scholarly study or the capacity to teach it): the sheer vibratory power of this sacred dialogue is transformative. 'So 'pi muktaḥ' — even he, liberated from the immediate karmic bondage of past sins. 'Śubhāḹ lokān prāpnuyāt puṇya-karmaṇām' — shall attain the auspicious, happy worlds of those who perform meritorious deeds. While the deep practitioner attains Brahman (as stated in earlier verses), the sincere listener who merely hears the Gita with an open heart is guaranteed a spiritually elevated future existence. The compassion of the teaching is total — it offers a path of upliftment to every grade of seeker.

18:72.text: O Pārtha, has this been heard by you with a one-pointed mind? O Dhanañjaya, has your delusion born of ignorance been completely destroyed?
18:72.transliteration:
kaccid etac chrutaṃ pārtha tvayaikāgreṇa cetasā |
kaccid ajñāna-sammohaḥ pranaṣṭas te dhanañjaya ||72||
18:72.devanagari:
कच्चिदेतच्छ्रुतं पार्थ त्वयैकाग्रेण चेतसा ।
कच्चिदज्ञानसम्मोहः प्रनष्टस्ते धनञ्जय ॥ ७२ ॥
18:72.speaker: Śrī Bhagavān
18:72.explanation: This is the final, checking question from the ultimate Teacher to His student — a beautifully intimate moment that concludes the long discourse. 'Kaccid etac chrutaṃ pārtha tvayaikāgreṇa cetasā' — has this been heard by you with a one-pointed, fully attentive mind? Krishna underscores that the reception of profound truth requires absolute psychological presence. 'Kaccid ajñāna-sammohaḥ pranaṣṭas te' — has your severe delusion (sammoha), which was born of ignorance (ajñāna), been thoroughly destroyed (pranaṣṭa: utterly vanished)? This takes us right back to Chapter 1 and Chapter 2, where Arjuna was paralysed by grief and delusion about his duty. Krishna's measure of success for this teaching is not whether Arjuna can recite philosophical tenets, but whether his existential paralysis and identity-confusion have been cured. True spiritual teaching must result in the destruction of delusion.

18:73.text: Arjuna said: My delusion is destroyed, and I have regained my memory through Your grace, O Infallible One (Acyuta). I stand firm, with my doubts dispelled. I shall do Your word.
18:73.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca |
naṣṭo mohaḥ smṛtir labdhā tvat-prasādān mayācyuta |
sthito 'smi gata-sandehaḥ kariṣye vacanaṃ tava ||73||
18:73.devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
नष्टो मोहः स्मृतिर्लब्धा त्वत्प्रसादान्मयाच्युत ।
स्थितोऽस्मि गतसन्देहः करिष्ये वचनं तव ॥ ७३ ॥
18:73.speaker: Arjuna
18:73.explanation: This is Arjuna's final statement in the Gita, and it represents the absolute triumph of the dialogue. 'Naṣṭo mohaḥ smṛtir labdhā' — my delusion is destroyed, and my memory is regained. This is the definition of enlightenment in the Vedāntic tradition: it is not the acquisition of something new, but the recovery of a forgotten truth (smṛti: remembrance of one's true nature as the immortal Self, and one's true relationship with the Divine). 'Tvat-prasādān mayācyuta' — through Your grace, O Infallible One: Arjuna humbly acknowledges that this clarity is not merely the result of his own logic, but a gift of Divine grace (prasāda). 'Sthito 'smi gata-sandehaḥ' — I stand firm (steady, grounded), my doubts completely gone. 'Kariṣye vacanaṃ tava' — I shall do Your word (I will act according to Your instruction). The warrior who had collapsed in the chariot in Chapter 1, throwing down his bow and declaring 'I will not fight', now stands up, his will perfectly aligned with the Divine will. True surrender is not inaction; it is taking up one's hardest duty without ego.

18:74.text: Sañjaya said: Thus have I heard this wondrous, hair-raising dialogue between Vāsudeva and the great-souled Pārtha.
18:74.transliteration:
sañjaya uvāca |
ity ahaṃ vāsudevasya pārthasya ca mahātmanaḥ |
saṃvādam imam aśrauṣam adbhutaṃ roma-harṣaṇam ||74||
18:74.devanagari:
सञ्जय उवाच ।
इत्यहं वासुदेवस्य पार्थस्य च महात्मनः ।
संवादमिममश्रौषमद्भुतं रोमहर्षणम् ॥ ७४ ॥
18:74.speaker: Sañjaya
18:74.explanation: The narrative frame now shifts back from the battlefield of Kurukṣetra to the blind King Dhṛtarāṣṭra's palace, closing the structural loop that began with the very first verse of Chapter 1. 'Ity ahaṃ' — Thus I (Sañjaya) have heard. 'Vāsudevasya pārthasya ca mahātmanaḥ' — between Kṛṣṇa and the great-souled Pārtha. By calling Arjuna 'mahātman' (great-souled), Sañjaya validates that Arjuna has indeed undergone a profound transformation. 'Saṃvādam imam aśrauṣam adbhutaṃ roma-harṣaṇam' — I heard this wondrous (adbhutam) and hair-raising (roma-harṣaṇam: causing the hair to stand on end from spiritual ecstasy and awe) dialogue. Sañjaya is not just a neutral reporter; he is profoundly, viscerally affected by the spiritual power of the transmission he has just witnessed. The teaching is so potent that even the one relaying it is thrown into a state of ecstatic reverence.

18:75.text: By the grace of Vyāsa, I have heard this supreme secret, this yoga, directly from Kṛṣṇa, the Lord of Yoga, speaking it Himself.
18:75.transliteration:
vyāsa-prasādāc chrutavān etad guhyam ahaṃ param |
yogaṃ yogeśvarāt kṛṣṇāt sākṣāt kathayataḥ svayam ||75||
18:75.speaker: Sañjaya
18:75.explanation: Sañjaya explains the mechanism of his extraordinary perception and expresses deep gratitude. 'Vyāsa-prasādāc chrutavān' — by the grace of my guru, the sage Vyāsa, I was able to hear this. Vyāsa had granted Sañjaya divine vision (divya dṛṣṭi) so he could narrate the events of the war to the blind king Dhṛtarāṣṭra. 'Etad guhyam ahaṃ param yogam' — this supreme, most secret yoga. The Gita is definitively termed a 'yoga' — an applied science of union with the Divine, not mere theological speculation. 'Yogeśvarāt kṛṣṇāt sākṣāt kathayataḥ svayam' — directly from Kṛṣṇa, the Lord of Yoga (Yogeśvara), speaking it Himself. Sañjaya is overwhelmed by his extraordinary fortune: he did not learn this through a long lineage of teachers, but heard it directly and immediately as it fell from the lips of the Supreme Lord. This verse highlights the indispensability of the Guru's grace (Vyāsa's blessing) in accessing Divine revelation.

18:76.text: O King, remembering again and again this wondrous and holy dialogue between Keśava and Arjuna, I rejoice again and again.
18:76.transliteration:
rājan saṃsmṛtya saṃsmṛtya saṃvādam imam adbhutam |
keśavārjunayoḥ puṇyaṃ hṛṣyāmi ca muhur muhuḥ ||76||
18:76.devanagari:
राजन्संस्मृत्य संस्मृत्य संवादमिममद्भुतम् ।
केशवार्जुनयोः पुण्यं हृष्यामि च मुहुर्मुहुः ॥ ७६ ॥
18:76.speaker: Sañjaya
18:76.explanation: 'Rājan' — O King (addressing Dhṛtarāṣṭra). 'Saṃsmṛtya saṃsmṛtya' — remembering, recalling again and again: this repetition points to the practice of 'manana' (continuous reflection). Spiritual truth is not consumed once; it is tasted and re-tasted in consciousness. 'Saṃvādam imam adbhutam' — this wondrous, extraordinary dialogue. 'Keśavārjunayoḥ puṇyaṃ' — between Keśava and Arjuna, which is puṇyam (holy, purifying, merit-generating). The very memory of this conversation cleanses the mind. 'Hṛṣyāmi ca muhur muhuḥ' — I rejoice, I thrill with joy, again and again (muhur muhuḥ). Sañjaya's inner state is one of cascading waves of spiritual ecstasy. This verse serves as a testimony to the living power of the Gita: it is not a dead text but an inexhaustible fountain of joy for anyone who anchors their mind in its remembrance.

18:77.text: And remembering again and again that most marvellous form of Hari, great is my wonder, O King, and I rejoice again and again.
18:77.transliteration:
tac ca saṃsmṛtya saṃsmṛtya rūpam atyadbhutaṃ hareḥ |
vismayo me mahān rājan hṛṣyāmi ca punaḥ punaḥ ||77||
18:77.devanagari:
तच्च संस्मृत्य संस्मृत्य रूपमत्यद्भुतं हरेः ।
विस्मयो मे महान् राजन्हृष्यामि च पुनः पुनः ॥ ७७ ॥
18:77.speaker: Sañjaya
18:77.explanation: While verse 76 dealt with the auditory memory (the dialogue/wisdom), verse 77 deals with the visual memory (the Cosmic Vision of Chapter 11). 'Tac ca saṃsmṛtya saṃsmṛtya' — and remembering that over and over again. 'Rūpam atyadbhutaṃ hareḥ' — that incredibly marvellous, breathtaking form of Hari (Kṛṣṇa's Viśvarūpa, the Universal Form). 'Vismayo me mahān rājan' — great is my wonder, my astonishment, O King. 'Hṛṣyāmi ca punaḥ punaḥ' — and I rejoice, I am thrilled, over and over again. The combination of supreme philosophical clarity (the dialogue) and overwhelming mystical experience (the vision) leaves Sañjaya in a state of continuous ecstatic wonder. Subtly, Sañjaya is also trying to hint to King Dhṛtarāṣṭra that opposing an entity capable of manifesting such a terrifying and magnificent cosmic form is an exercise in absolute futility.

18:78.text: Wherever there is Kṛṣṇa, the Lord of Yoga, and wherever there is Pārtha, the archer, there are certain to be fortune, victory, prosperity, and unswerving righteousness. This is my conviction.
18:78.transliteration:
yatra yogeśvaraḥ kṛṣṇo yatra pārtho dhanur-dharaḥ |
tatra śrīr vijayo bhūtir dhruvā nītir matir mama ||78||
18:78.devanagari:
यत्र योगेश्वरः कृष्णो यत्र पार्थो धनुर्धरः ।
तत्र श्रीर्विजयो भूतिर्ध्रुवा नीतिर्मतिर्मम ॥ ७८ ॥
18:78.speaker: Sañjaya
18:78.explanation: This is the grand finale, the culminating verse of the entire Bhagavad Gītā. It serves as both a prophecy for the Kurukṣetra war and a universal formula for ultimate human success. 'Yatra yogeśvaraḥ kṛṣṇo' — wherever there is Kṛṣṇa, the master of all Yoga (representing Divine Grace, supreme wisdom, and transcendent vision). 'Yatra pārtho dhanur-dharaḥ' — and wherever there is Arjuna, the supreme archer (representing focused human effort, dedicated action, and dynamic execution of duty). The formula is profound: Grace alone without human action is not the design of this world; human action without alignment to the Divine is doomed to egoic failure. But when Yogeśvara (Vision) and Dhanurdhara (Action) are united in a single chariot, the results are guaranteed. 'Tatra' — there, in that synergy, will invariably be found four things: 'Śrīḥ' (Fortune, wealth, radiant grace), 'Vijayaḥ' (Triumph, the overcoming of all obstacles), 'Bhūtiḥ' (Ever-expanding prosperity and power), and crucially, 'Dhruvā nītiḥ' (Firm, unswerving righteousness, morality, and justice). 'Matir mama' — this is my absolute conviction. Sañjaya tactfully shatters any remaining illusions of King Dhṛtarāṣṭra: victory cannot belong to the Kauravas, for they lack both Dharma and the Divine presence. In the battle of life, success belongs to those who couple their best efforts with complete surrender to the Divine.
